Title: Regulators' pact with Weyerhaeuser allows logging in spotted-owl habitat
Text: Federal wildlife regulators have agreed on a plan assuring Weyerhaeuser Co. that it can log 209,000 of its acres in Oregon's spotted-owl country.    The agreement is the latest of several struck by the government and private landowners to deal with the collision between the federal Endangered Species Act, the nation's most sweeping conservation law, and private property rights.    Under the so-called habitat conservation plan worked out with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, Weyerhaeuser agreed to limit clear-cutting and employ other logging methods considered less damaging to owl habitat on the company's tract near Coos Bay, Ore. The agreement, to be in force for as long as 50 years, is expected to be announced today.    Weyerhaeuser, based in Tacoma, Wash., owns roughly 5.5 million acres of forest.    The company also agreed to design logging patterns in ways that leave corridors of larger, older trees, which owls can use to move from Weyerhaeuser's lands into surrounding federally owned old-growth groves. Spotted owls, protected by federal law since 1990, typically nest in ancient forests of Douglas fir, redwood and other giant trees in the Pacific Northwest.    In return, the company, the largest private timberland owner in the West, said it will be able to log at relatively high volumes, despite the presence of some spotted owls. A spokesman said the agreement ensures supply for the company's mill in Coos Bay, which employs about 300 people, and several other local area mills.    Numerous big timber companies have struck similar agreements, or are negotiating with the government to ensure their ability to log, despite owls or other protected species on their land. Georgia-Pacific Corp., for example, has entered into a habitat conservation plan in the Southeast on land that's home to a protected woodpecker. Plum Creek Timber L.P. is in negotiations with the Fish and Wildlife Service for an extensive plan for its Pacific Northwest lands.    The Clinton administration believes the plans show the act is flexible enough to accommodate economic interests of private landowners while still protecting wildlife. That flexibility is central to the administration's effort to defuse a growing campaign in Congress and the courts to gut the law. Opponents of the law, who gained strength in recent elections, say it exacts too high a toll on commerce and private property rights. The U.S. Supreme Court is expected within a few months to issue a landmark ruling on the act's enforceability on private land.    Environmentalists, divided on the habitat conservation plans, are likely to criticize aspects of Weyerhaeuser's agreement. Environmentalists are concerned about the long terms of the agreements, and the limited ability to modify them if other species in the forests gain federal protection or if new environmental threats arise from logging.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Agreement between federal wildlife regulators and Weyerhaeuser Co. regarding logging practices in spotted owl habitat.
setting: agreement between federal wildlife regulators and private landowners regarding logging practices in spotted owl habitat
characters: Weyerhaeuser Co. [Hero]; federal wildlife regulators [Hero]; spotted owls [Victim]; opponents of the Endangered Species Act [Villain]; environmentalists [Unknown]
plot: Federal wildlife regulators and Weyerhaeuser Co. agree on a habitat conservation plan allowing logging while implementing measures to protect spotted owl habitat, balancing economic interests with wildlife protection.
moral: The Endangered Species Act should be flexible enough to accommodate private property rights while ensuring the protection of endangered species like the spotted owl.

Title: Conservationists' 'Bogeyman'; Congressman Pombo Pushes Environmental Conservatism
Text: WASHINGTON -- Last year, Congressman Richard Pombo and his staff considered selling off 15 national parks, monuments, preserves and historical sites, along with naming rights for visitors' centers and hiking trails, to corporate bidders. The flamboyant idea -- to raise more federal revenues -- was later withdrawn under bipartisan criticism, but it was the latest in a string of maneuvers that have vaulted Mr. Pombo to public enemy No. 1 for many environmental groups. As chairman of the powerful House Resources Committee with its far-reaching oversight of the nation's public lands, Mr. Pombo has used his position as a bully pulpit to push an agenda of environmental conservatism. In just a few short years, the 43-year-old has become a leader in Congress in rolling back environmental regulations -- at a time when like-minded Republican conservatives in the Senate and White House are in power. Apart from contemplating a selloff of national-park sites, Mr. Pombo has recently tried to overhaul the Endangered Species Act, privatize vast government lands in the West and open up protected coasts and wilderness to more drilling. Often sporting a cowboy hat and boots, he openly chastises environmental groups as "radical" and "extreme." In reaction, the Sierra Club calls Mr. Pombo "the most frightening man in Congress" and recently handed out a Halloween mask of the onetime California rancher. The League of Conservation Voters has given Mr. Pombo a 3% rating in terms of how often his votes are aligned with environmentalists, one of the lowest for any politician. "He has been on just an absolute tear to try to tear apart the conservation laws," says Rodger Schlickeisen, president of Defenders of Wildlife, an environmental group in Washington. Mr. Pombo denies he is antienvironment. Rather, he says, environmentalists are trying to use him. "I'm their bogeyman," Mr. Pombo says in an interview. "They need a bogeyman to help raise money." Mr. Pombo isn't in hot water only with environmentalists. Over the past year, he has come under attack for alleged ethics violations. In 2005, his office lobbied the Interior Department to ease up on wind- energy farm guidelines at the same time his parents in California were running a ranch with wind turbines on it, according to the Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington, a nonpartisan government watchdog group. The group also questions Mr. Pombo's ties to Republican lobbyist Jack Abramoff, who recently pleaded guilty to three felonies and agreed to help prosecutors investigating political corruption. After accepting $7,000 in campaign contributions from Mr. Abramoff since 1999, Mr. Pombo declined to have his committee join in other federal investigations into alleged influence-peddling by the lobbyist, officials of the watchdog group say. Mr. Pombo says a staffer wrote the wind turbine letter to Interior officials without his knowledge, and adds the voluntary guidelines were for new windmills -- not existing ones like those on his parents' ranch. He adds that the first time he was aware Mr. Abramoff had contributed to his campaign was when he read about it in a newspaper. A spokesman for the congressman says he has returned the $7,000. Not surprisingly, Mr. Pombo has many defenders in business. During the 2004 Republican National Convention, the American Gas Association hosted a bash for him called the "Pombo-Palooza," featuring a mechanical bull and country-and-western music. "The left wing is all hot and bothered over Pombo . . . due to his effectiveness and a lack of willingness to pander to liberal pet causes," wrote Raymond Keating, chief economist of the Small Business and Entrepreneurship Council, last year in an op-ed piece in Mr. Pombo's California hometown paper, the Tracy Press. Mr. Pombo's political career began in 1990, when he was elected a city councilman in Tracy, a fast-growing suburb east of San Francisco. Two years later, he unseated an incumbent congressman in the district to begin a seven-term run. Mr. Pombo says he entered politics to rein in government regulations, which he believes were harming property rights of landowners. Regarded by many as a protege of then-House Majority Leader Tom DeLay of Texas, Mr. Pombo in 2003 was named chairman of the powerful House Resources Committee, jumping over nine committee members with greater seniority. In protest, Colorado Republican Joel Hefley resigned from the committee. Although critics accused Mr. Pombo of using his friendship with Mr. DeLay to gain the job, he says he had to convince him and other House members he was the most qualified. "I laid out an agenda of what I wanted to do," he says. Much of that agenda has focused on making it easier for businesses and individuals to operate around public lands. Last year, Mr. Pombo co-authored a bill to revise the 1973 Endangered Species Act, so it wouldn't restrict so many land uses in areas near where threatened animals live. The bill passed the House last fall. If passed by the Senate, environmentalists say the bill would strip out a key provision of the landmark law that allows the government to establish large areas of habitat for recovery of endangered creatures. But Mr. Pombo says the act's success rate is so low -- 10 species have recovered out of about 1,300 listed for protection since 1973 -- that the system needs changing. "The law isn't working, and it has caused a huge amount of conflict," says Mr. Pombo. He advocates more local management of wildlife, which he says worked successfully in some African countries he visited. Mr. Pombo has had mixed success on other initiatives. In 2003, he was instrumental in passing the Healthy Forests Restoration Act, with the help of Democratic Sen. Dianne Feinstein of California. The act opens up more forests on federal land to logging, in a way Mr. Pombo and other supporters of the bill say would make it easier to clear forests of dangerous fuel loads. Environmentalists have decried the bill as a giveaway to timber interests. But Mr. Pombo was unable to pass a mining provision he tacked onto a budget bill before the Christmas break, which would have lifted a 1994 moratorium on selling federal land to people who can prove mining claims. Although Mr. Pombo says the provision would have opened only about 360,000 acres of Western wild lands for possible sale out of about 300 million acres, many environmental groups interpreted the provision as being more far-reaching. Environmentalists and many of their Democratic allies say they plan to make Mr. Pombo one of their main Republican targets in the November midterm election. Even some moderate Republicans are talking of taking him on. Mr. Pombo, who has handily won most of his elections in the district, says he isn't worried. "Anybody is vulnerable," he says in an office adorned with a buffalo skull. "But I am just going to keep doing the things I have always done."
event_description: A bill to revise the Endangered Species Act was co-authored by Congressman Pombo to reduce restrictions on land use near habitats of threatened species.
setting: Congressional debate on the Endangered Species Act and public lands management
characters: Congressman Richard Pombo [Villain]; environmental groups (e.g., Sierra Club, Defenders of Wildlife) [Hero]; endangered species [Victim]
plot: Congressman Pombo proposes an overhaul of the Endangered Species Act to reduce restrictions on land use, which environmental groups oppose, arguing it would harm endangered species and undermine conservation efforts.
moral: The Endangered Species Act is failing to protect species effectively, and a revision is necessary to reduce conflict and promote local management of wildlife.

Title: The Evening Wrap: Asian Aftershock
Text: When a 6.7 magnitude quake shook Taiwan late Tuesday, the first fears were that the tremors would trigger a tsunami -- and this on the second anniversary of the wave that killed 230,000 people in South Asia. Nature spared lives this time, but wasn't as kind to technology. The temblor cut several undersea fiber-optic cables that carry communications traffic through a key nexus in Asia, connecting Hong Kong and Southeast Asia with Japan and, ultimately, North America. Phone and Internet service was spotty, slow or just plain unavailable in several countries in the region, marking the largest such outage in years. The interruption also affected traders, as Bloomberg and Reuters platforms were knocked off line, and clients couldn't log on to trade or get real-time data. Asian markets were little affected overall, as most companies did find workarounds. Still, the wide scope of the disruption -- caused by a single natural disaster with limited physical damage -- illustrates how vulnerable the systems are that support global communications. Nearly all the data that travels between continents does so over fiber-optic filaments, tiny threads of glass that are bundled together and covered in insulation and other protective materials. Companies stretch these cables across thousands of miles of ocean, to rest on the sea floor or float above it. These cables are easy to dislodge -- a fishing boat cut what was then Pakistan's only undersea cable line over the summer and disrupted the company's Internet service for 12 days. The cables damaged this week run through an especially dense pathway between Taiwan and Hong Kong. But large disruptions are possible wherever they are clustered together in a handful of key hubs -- for instance, there are key points near New York City and in southern Britain. Part of the problem is that global investment in fiber-optic lines fell off after the 1990s, when telecommunications companies laid huge amounts in anticipation of an explosion in demand. When that growth didn't happen as quickly as expected, companies were hammered financially -- some even went bankrupt. But this week's incident "raises the question of whether there needs to be a new wave of investment" in these cables, said Duncan Clark, chairman of BDA China Ltd., a telecom consultancy based in Beijing. The outage even trickled down to the region's shipping and logistics industry. KL Tam, managing director of Hong Kong-based Kingstar Shipping said he was unable to reach clients in Japan or Korea, and some in Singapore. "We have been trying other ways to communicate -- but nothing much works," he said. "If this continued for several days, our operations overseas would essentially stop," he said. --- Dow Barrels Down Home Stretch The Dow Industrials topped 12500 for the first time, gaining more than 100 points, as the year-end run continues amid a stronger-than- forecast housing report and falling oil prices. More than three stocks advanced for each that declined on the New York Stock Exchange, though trading was thin during the typically slow final week of the year. Fund managers are adding stocks to their portfolios, a trend that often gives stocks a boost in late December. European shares rose, helped by falling oil prices. In Asia, Japan's Nikkei Index hit a new seven-month high, revved by gains in Toyota. The 10-year Treasury fell to yield 4.601%. On the New York Mercantile Exchange, light, sweet crude oil slid 76 cents to $60.34 a barrel. --- Home Improvement The housing slump may be the darkest cloud hanging over the U.S. economy going into the new year, but data out today emitted a ray or two of hope. The Commerce Department reported November new-home sales climbed 3.4%, rising for the third time in six months and beating economists' expectations for a 1.6% increase. The monthly report also showed a decline in inventories and a rise in median price. Home sales are still down 15% from the same period in 2005, and November marks the 11th straight monthly year-over-year decline, but economists still took heart. "Although no doubt this report was boosted by the mild weather in parts of the country, the pickup in sales and decline in the number of homes for sale provide further tentative evidence that the housing market is stabilizing," Bear Stearns economists wrote. --- Remembering President Ford The flag over the White House flew at half-staff in honor of former President Gerald R. Ford, who died last night at 93. Speaking from his ranch in Texas, President Bush said, "Americans will always admire Gerald Ford's unflinching performance of duty and the honorable conduct of his administration and the great rectitude of the man himself." Funeral arrangements will be announced today, and next week, as per tradition, stock exchanges in the U.S. will be closed on the day of his funeral. --- Air Force Looks Outside The Air Force is considering hiring an outside firm to oversee systems integration of its next-generation navigational satellites, according to industry and government officials, in a major policy decision that has multibillion dollar ramifications. Air Force officials already have said they intend to award separate manufacturing contracts for the newest version of Global Positioning System satellites and related ground facilities. But in a break from traditional acquisition strategies, senior Air Force generals also are mulling hiring private experts to supervise the integration of the overall GPS III system.  Hiring a separate project integrator would be a tacit acknowledgement that both Air Force Space Command and the Pentagon's massive weapons-buying bureaucracy lack the necessary expertise to perform the required oversight role. --- Saddam Speaks as Sentence Looms A farewell letter posted on the Internet in the name of Saddam Hussein called on Iraqis not to hate the invaders of their country. "I call on you not to hate because hate does not leave space for a person to be fair and it makes you blind and closes all doors of thinking," said the letter, supposedly written because Mr. Hussein was not given the opportunity to speak after his sentence was delivered. In Amman, Jordan, one of Mr. Hussein's lawyers, Issam Ghazzawi, said that the letter was authentic. Meanwhile, an Iraqi presidential spokesman said an appeals court order yesterday upholding Mr. Hussein's death sentence might not require the Iraqi president's approval. If it doesn't, the former dictator will have no other legal means of avoiding execution. Some Iraqis said Mr. Hussein should be hanged immediately, but others fear Iraq's bloodletting could escalate if the former dictator is executed at a time when sectarian attacks are already on the rise. --- Troops Close In on Somali Capital Ethiopian and Somali government troops drove Islamic movement fighters out of Jowhar, the last major town on the road to the Mogadishu, Somalia's capital. Ethiopian forces crossed the border Sunday to reinforce the internationally recognized Somali government. The militias of the Council of Islamic Courts seized Mogadishu in June and went on to take much of southern Somalia. The militias seemed invincible after capturing the capital, but they are no match for Ethiopia, which has the strongest military in the Horn of Africa. Somali government spokesman Abdirahman Dinari confirmed that talks for the peaceful surrender of Mogadishu were under way. "We will not attack Mogadishu. We hope that Mogadishu will fall to our hands without firing a shot." Unconfirmed reports estimate as many as 1,000 people have died and 3,000 been wounded since the fighting began a few days ago. --- On Thin Ice The U.S. Department of Interior proposed to list polar bears as a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act, saying global warming is causing sea ice -- a primary habitat for the bears -- to melt at dangerous rates. The proposal marks a potentially significant departure for the administration from its cautious rhetoric about the effects of global warming. Interior Department Secretary Dirk Kempthorne said oil and natural gas drilling in the Arctic region is not to blame for the receding ice, but was silent on what is actually causing the warmer temperatures. The Interior Department plans to take comments on the proposal in effort to make a decision within 12 months on whether to list the bears as endangered species. --- More Backdating Fallout Backdating questions bogged down more companies as HCC Insurance Holdings revised several years' worth of financial statements and said its former CEO "retroactively priced options." According to an amended report filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission, Mr. Way, who resigned last month, mispriced the options to retain talent and "provide employees with the best option price." Meanwhile, Nabors Industries said it's initiating a further review of its option granting practices after a Wall Street Journal article stated that CEO Eugene Isenberg is among the highest-paid executives in history. The company said an initial review of its stock option practices going back to 1998 "did not suggest that there was reason to question the propriety" of its option granting practices. --- Retailers' Results May Disappoint A Saturday spree by last-minute shoppers may not be enough to save the Christmas shopping season for retailers. ShopperTrak RCT Corp. reported that retail sales for the week ended Dec. 23 rose 5.2% on a surge of spending that should bring total holiday sales "relatively close" to original projections despite recent shortfalls. Sales on Dec. 23 totaled $8.72 billion, making it the second-biggest holiday shopping day of the year behind Black Friday. But a survey released by the International Council of Shopping Centers, Inc. and UBS Securities LLC put the number much lower. Their figures show U.S. chain store sales for the week ended Dec. 23 rose 1.7% compared to the year-ago period, putting the season's subtotal on  track to hit the low end of expectations. --- Study Points to Safer Catheter Use Commonsense safety and hygiene practices, often overlooked by doctors, can almost wipe out a dangerous type of bloodstream infection that kills thousands of patients each year in U.S. hospitals, according to a new study. The simple steps include making sure doctors and nurses wash their hands and wear protective clothing. The study, published in tomorrow's New England Journal of Medicine, focused on central-line catheters, which deliver drugs and nutrition to patients through a major vein in their neck, chest or groin. Researchers examined Michigan hospitals that agreed to use the safety procedures in their intensive-care units, where catheters are most commonly used. --- Itsy-Bitsy, Spray-On Bikinis That exotic dancer might look topless, but truth is there's a thin layer of latex between you and the finer points of her anatomy. Strippers in Alabama have brokered a deal with the state to comply with one of the strictest anti-nudity laws in the U.S. Under a settlement between 18 adult nightclubs and the Alabama attorney general's office, dancers statewide are spritzing their buttocks and breasts with flesh-colored latex. The law says that any skin that would normally be covered by a modest bikini must be swathed in an opaque covering. But the law doesn't specify the type of material, so, in the legal sense, a nylon swimsuit and spray-on latex are one and the same. The state reluctantly went along with the clubs' virtually invisible latex bikinis rather than having a federal judge follow through on his threat to throw out the entire statute as unconstitutional. "The choice we were faced with was some covering or no covering," said Keith Miller, the chief deputy attorney general. "We decided it was better to have these coverings than nothing at all." --- Write to Megan Ballinger at megan.ballinger@wsj.com
event_description: The U.S. Department of Interior proposed to list polar bears as a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: U.S. Department of Interior proposing species listing under the Endangered Species Act
characters: U.S. Department of Interior [Hero]; global warming [Villain]; polar bears [Victim]
plot: The U.S. Department of Interior proposed to list polar bears as a threatened species due to global warming causing the melting of sea ice, which is critical for their habitat.
moral: The text suggests that urgent action is needed to address global warming and protect polar bears by listing them as a threatened species.

Title: High Court to Examine HMOs' Liability In Cases of Cost Cuts, Patients' Health
Text: WASHINGTON -- Stepping into a debate with huge stakes for managed-care companies, the Supreme Court agreed to determine the legal liability of these providers when they are accused of cutting costs at the expense of patients' health. The case was among nine the justices added to their docket for this year, as they prepare for the term that begins next week. Other cases range from whether Congress exceeded its authority when it passed the 1994 Violence Against Women Act, to whether California may deny a tax deduction to corporations that don't have their headquarters there. The court also will decide whether to limit the activities of certain antiabortion activists outside abortion clinics. Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg, who was operated on for colon cancer over a week ago and returned home yesterday, participated in deciding which cases to take, a court spokesman said. In a statement, the court indicated that Justice Ginsburg's medical prognosis appeared to be good. "All lymph nodes proved negative for cancer and there was no metastasis evident," the statement said. In the managed-care case, Cynthia Herdrich claims that to hold down costs, her doctor at a health-maintenance organization run by Health Alliance Medical Plans Inc. of Urbana, Ill., delayed treating what was appendicitis. Among other legal actions, Ms. Herdrich sued her doctor and the HMO under the Employment Retirement Income Security Act, or Erisa, the 1974 law governing employee pension and health plans. She charged that the delay was motivated by the HMO's desire to save money, rather than to act in the patient's best interest, as mandated by Erisa. The U.S. appeals court in Chicago ruled that the lawsuit under Erisa may go forward, and if the Supreme Court agrees, it will reshape the landscape for HMOs, says a lawyer for the defendants in the case. "The fundamental assumption now is that HMOs will create incentives to save money and provide health care that's affordable," says Carter Phillips, a Washington attorney with Sidley & Austin, who is representing the HMO. "If the appeals court is right, HMOs as we know them will be hard pressed to survive." (Pegram vs. Herdrich) In the Violence Against Women Act case, the justices will review a lawsuit brought by a former student at Virginia Polytechnic Institute who claimed she was raped by two football players. The case gives the high court the opportunity to, once again, examine the scope of the Commerce clause of the Constitution, which over the past half-century has given the federal government authority to extend its reach, from civil rights to the Endangered Species Act. In 1995, the high court for the first time since the 1930s, reversed that extension when it ruled that the U.S. had exceeded its power to regulate interstate commerce with a law banning guns near schools. Now, the court may use the 1994 Violence Against Women Act, which established gender-based violence as a federal crime, to further define its views on how far Congress may go to regulate noneconomic activity under the Commerce clause. (Brzonkala vs. Morrison) The tax case was brought by Hunt-Wesson Inc., a unit of ConAgra Inc., based in Omaha, Neb. Hunt-Wesson claims that California, by denying a tax deduction for interest expense to certain companies that operate but aren't based there, violates the Commerce and Due Process clauses of the Constitution. "If California is upheld, then other states will do this. It amounts to a double tax on income," says Donald Falk, an attorney in the Washington office of Mayer Brown & Platt. (Hunt-Wesson Inc. vs. Franchise Tax Board) The justices also will decide the constitutionality of a Colorado law that makes it a criminal offense to counsel, protest or communicate within eight feet of a person while within 100 feet of an abortion clinic. The case was brought by so-called sidewalk counselors who offer alternative actions to women on their way into abortion clinics. The case, brought by three such counselors, contends that the 1993 state statute violates their free-speech rights because it burdens "would-be speakers with the duty of obtaining consent before communicating with the public." The state's attorney general counters that protestors outside abortion clinics often block the facilities and harass patients. (Hill vs. Colorado) In another case, the Supreme Court will try to spell out when the Internal Revenue Service's three-year statute of limitations begins for income-tax refunds. A lower court denied a request for a $1,175 tax credit from David Baral, a former Washington Post mailroom worker, for overpayments he made in 1988. Mr. Baral didn't file his 1988 tax return until 1993, beyond the three-year statute of limitations for refunds. Mr. Baral argued the statute of limitations should start when he filed the return; the IRS said the clock starts ticking when the tax is paid. (Baral vs. U.S.) Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Supreme Court will examine the scope of the Commerce clause in relation to the Violence Against Women Act.
setting: Supreme Court review of legal cases
characters: Cynthia Herdrich [Hero]; Health Alliance Medical Plans Inc. [Villain]; patients [Victim]
plot: Cynthia Herdrich claims that her doctor delayed treatment for appendicitis to save costs for the HMO, which led her to sue the HMO under ERISA. The U.S. appeals court allowed her lawsuit to proceed, potentially reshaping the legal landscape for HMOs.
moral: The problem of managed-care companies prioritizing cost savings over patient health must be addressed, and the Supreme Court should uphold the appeals court's decision to allow lawsuits against HMOs to ensure patient interests are protected.

Title: E-Business: Cellular Towers Get Static From Environmentalists
Text: WASHINGTON -- In Baghdad, Fla., environmentalists hope to prevent construction of a cellphone tower in a wetland. On Maryland's Eastern Shore, they have targeted a tower slated to rise over the scenic Chesapeake Bay. And in Dog River, Ala., the groups have taken aim at one planned for an eco-sensitive floodplain.    For years, the wireless industry has found itself under assault from community groups or municipal officials when it tries to install new towers. These groups complain about the aesthetics or alleged health risks of the giant steel poles. But now, the industry faces a new enemy: environmental groups complaining about the ecological impact of one of the most ubiquitous symbols of the Information Age.    The Federal Communications Commission already requires wireless companies to submit environmental impact studies with each tower application, examining factors ranging from a tower's electromagnetic emissions to the structure's impact on surrounding wildlife and vegetation. But the groups argue that the reviews don't go far enough.    With hundreds of towers being erected each year, the environmentalists complain, the FCC has yet to study their cumulative effect in ecologically fragile areas. The groups cite government and private-sector studies showing that millions of birds and bats are hurt or killed each year when they collide with the towers -- figures the industry says are exaggerated. The groups also say that vegetation and animal habitats in wetland and floodplain areas can be damaged as the companies fell trees, string power and communications lines, and build access roads and other infrastructure to support the towers.    The groups' bitter, high-stakes lobbying war threatens to damage the phone companies' ability to expand their networks, which they say is necessary to attract new customers and provide better service to existing ones. Nationwide, the industry is racing to build hundreds of additional towers to pave the way for rolling out new wireless services such as high-speed mobile Internet access.    A pair of environmental groups here have filed dozens of petitions in recent months asking the FCC to deny about 40 tower applications until the agency conducts further environmental studies. The groups, Friends of the Earth and the Forest Conservation Council, have also asked the FCC to hold off on awarding any other permits for towers in ecologically sensitive areas until the agency assesses the cumulative enviromental impact of all of the towers that dot the American landscape. Such a review could easily take more than a year.    An FCC spokesman said the agency is reviewing the petitions, but declined to comment further.    An FCC decision to delay approving the 40 towers at the heart of the environmentalists' petition would be costly: each extra month of FCC deliberation, the companies figure, costs them nearly $300,000 per tower in lost revenue. But an FCC decision to impose stricter environmental standards on all future tower applications could be catastrophic because the industry's capacity to carry calls already is stretched to the breaking point because of surging demand.    "This could have a very serious dampening effect on wireless technology deployment in the U.S.," says Rudy Baca, an analyst with the Precursor Group here. "Right now, everyone has to put up with a fairly high level of dropped calls. That can't be fixed without additional spectrum and additional towers. And once you get more spectrum, the towers become even more important."    The environmental assault represents the latest front in the war over cellphone towers. The structures are large -- usually about 250 feet tall -- and highly unpopular in many areas. Other opponents argue that the electromagnetic radiation poses a health risk, even though federal law says that the emissions are not to be considered a factor when deciding where to build a tower. And they contend that the structures are eyesores that depress property values in surrounding neighborhoods.    Wireless companies say that there are no conclusive data proving that cell towers cause health problems, and insist that they have tried to be responsive to each community's aesthetic concerns. One approach has seen the companies build so-called stealth towers designed to look like something else. Churches across the country have cell towers disguised as steeples or bell carillons, while the light poles at many high school football stadiums are actually wireless signal transmitters. Other towers are disguised to look like trees.    Though the grass-roots campaigns against cell towers have had some success, the environmental groups have chosen a different tack. Rather than asking local officials to say no to individual towers, the environmental groups have taken their case directly to the FCC, hoping to get the agency to reject dozens of tower applications in one fell swoop.    "We're trying to nationalize a fight that has raged in individual cities and states," says Brian Dunkiel, a staff attorney with Friends of the Earth. "The FCC's actions are supposed to be governed by various federal environmental laws, like the Clean Water Act and the Endangered Species Act, but it's readily apparent that the laws aren't being followed fully and in some cases aren't being followed at all."    The fight has been quietly raging for years, and has often pitted different branches of the government against each other. In November 1999, Jamie Clark, the then-director of the Fish and Wildlife Service, told then-FCC Chairman William Kennard that the towers killed at least four million birds per year. Ms. Clark asked the agency to track the cumulative ecological impact of the towers, which she said were built "with almost no environmental oversight by FCC," but Mr. Kennard turned down her request. The environmentalists face even steeper odds now that the FCC is under the control of Republican Michael Powell, a staunch believer in deregulation and free markets.    Officials at several wireless companies scoff at the environmentalists' claims. "We already do a fairly stringent environmental assessment," says Bill Clift, an engineer at Cingular Wireless, the joint venture of BellSouth Corp. and SBC Communications Inc. "We take this stuff seriously, and I can assure you that the FCC does too."    Indeed, Mr. Clift says the biggest environmental problem the company has faced is guano from birds nesting on its towers. "Once the birds get comfortable out there," he says, "none of our engineers want to get all that close to the towers."    -- Journal Link: If it meant better service, how close to your home would you allow a tall cellphone tower? Participate in the Question of the Day, in the online Journal at WSJ.com/Question.    (See related article: "That *%&)@*$ Cellphone! --- Carriers Install Better Gear, Use Roving Fix-It Vehicles But Quality Is Still an Issue" -- WSJ July 23, 2001)  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Environmental groups have filed petitions asking the FCC to deny tower applications until further environmental studies are conducted.
setting: FCC review of tower applications and environmental impact studies
characters: environmental groups [Hero]; wireless industry [Villain]; birds and bats [Victim]
plot: Environmental groups are filing petitions with the FCC to deny tower applications until further environmental studies are conducted, arguing that the current reviews are insufficient and that the cumulative ecological impact of the towers is not being assessed, which harms wildlife.
moral: The problem is the insufficient environmental oversight of cellphone tower construction, which threatens wildlife; the text suggests that the FCC should impose stricter environmental standards and conduct comprehensive studies before approving new tower applications.

Title: House panel agrees to end ban on drilling
Text: WASHINGTON -- A key House panel agreed to end a 14-year-old moratorium on oil and gas drilling off most of the U.S. coastline, setting the stage for a floor battle in coming months.    While the provision breezed through the House Appropriations subcommittee with less than five minutes of debate, it drew immediate criticism from both President Clinton and a coalition of more than 20 congressional Democrats. "This action is a mistake, and I will have no part of it," Mr. Clinton said yesterday.    The ban also enjoys strong support among California and Florida Republicans, who are expected to partake in the effort to keep it alive.    A congressional vote to lift the ban, however, would only partially clear the way for oil companies to drill in areas such as Alaska's Bristol Bay, the eastern Gulf of Mexico, the coast of California, and the Florida Panhandle and Keys. Permission for drilling would still require approval from the administration.    While that would be unlikely under the Clinton administration, supporters of the ban argue that a change in party in the White House would make future drilling more likely.    The move to end the drilling ban emerged as one of the most controversial segments of a subcommittee measure to cut natural-resources spending $1.4 billion below last year's level.    As expected, the same subcommittee also agreed yesterday to a broad array of steps cutting spending and eliminating agencies in environmental and natural-resources programs. The bill would eliminate six federal agencies, such as the Office of Indian Education and the Pennsylvania Avenue Development Corp., as well as nearly 50 programs in areas from mining to Indian affairs.    It also would extend a moratorium on listing new wildlife and plants under the Endangered Species Act and cut energy programs by about 27%. On the other hand, the bill would provide a slight increase in operational funds for the National Park Service.    The fact that the lifting of the ban has made it this far illustrates the clout of the oil industry in the Republican-controlled Congress.    The spending bill also shows the momentum of the GOP's effort to cut federal spending. Despite the wide array of cuts and eliminations, Democrats on the committee made only token efforts to restore funding or save programs. One of the few efforts to save an agency came when Washington Republican George Nethercutt offered an amendment to save the Bureau of Mines.    Subcommittee Chairman Ralph Regula of Ohio, however, quickly brushed the effort aside. "If we are going to start downsizing, we have to take agencies that do not have a truly dynamic impact and phase them out," he said.    Republicans also moved a step closer to cutting $1.35 billion from current funding for energy and water programs when the full House Appropriations Committee last evening essentially rubber-stamped a subcommittee's previous recommendation. The energy and water measure, which will now move to the full House for a vote, would cut solar and renewable energy funding by half while preserving funding elsewhere for more basic science research in the Energy Department.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The bill would extend a moratorium on listing new wildlife and plants under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: House Appropriations subcommittee debate
characters: President Clinton [Hero]; oil industry [Villain]; environmental and natural-resources programs [Victim]
plot: The House Appropriations subcommittee agreed to end a 14-year moratorium on oil and gas drilling, which faced criticism from President Clinton and congressional Democrats. The lifting of the ban illustrates the oil industry's influence in Congress, while the bill also proposes cuts to environmental programs and agencies.
moral: The text suggests that the move to lift the drilling ban is a mistake that threatens environmental programs, advocating for the preservation of these programs and the continuation of the moratorium on drilling.

Title: The Race Is On: Bush Assails Clinton And Congress, Vows To Restrain Spending --- He Urges Novel Tax Option To Fight Deficit as Aides Chart Electoral Strategy --- Battle for Midwestern States
Text: HOUSTON -- Calling for new economic initiatives, mocking his opponent and deriding the "gridlock Democratic Congress," President Bush finally came out swinging. In his critical acceptance speech, Mr. Bush -- facing the fight of his life -- rolled out an extraordinary proposal to permit taxpayers to do what Congress won't: Reduce the deficit. Under his plan, taxpayers would check off a box on their income-tax returns specifying how much of their payment, up to 10%, would be put in a trust fund to reduce the national debt. Spending then would, in theory, be cut by an amount equal to the cash in the trust fund. Taking his cue from Harry Truman, the president signaled that he is eager to run against the Democratic Congress and to veto spending bills throughout the fall to underline his new dedication to domestic policy. He pledged to work for what he called "a stronger, safer, more secure America." Mr. Bush called for across-the-board tax cuts next year, to be paid for by spending cuts that he didn't specify, and probably won't until next year. All in all, his plan would force deep spending cuts -- so deep that some budget experts doubt the plan's credibility. The president also called for a one-year moratorium on new regulations, for measures to help small business and for a new job-training program. He contrasted his program with tax and spending increases proposed by Gov. Bill Clinton of Arkansas, calling that program "Elvis Economics," adding: "America will be checking into the Heartbreak Hotel." That was only one of several pejorative remarks about his rival that were sprinkled throughout what amounted to Mr. Bush's campaign kickoff. Mr. Bush also contrasted his performance as commander-in-chief in the Gulf War with Gov. Clinton's alleged equivocation over intervention in the Gulf, belittling his rival as the "leader of the Arkansas National Guard" and a man who changes his positions so often that he's been "spotted in more places than Elvis." He said Mr. Clinton won't be able to overhaul the legal system because his campaign is backed "by practically every trial lawyer who ever wore a tassled loafer." And he portrayed the governor as a member of the "liberal McGovern wing" of the Democratic Party. "We tried this once before, combining the Democratic governor of a small Southern state with a very liberal vice president and a Democratic Congress," Mr. Bush said. "America doesn't need Carter II." The speech provides a rough road map for a campaign that almost everyone expects will turn out to be hotly contested. Most American presidential elections tend to be very lopsided or very close; after a convention celebrating traditional family values, capped by Mr. Bush's speech, this one has all the earmarks of a nail-biter. And that means Mr. Bush is going to need to do a lot of work in a lot of places that he and his campaign strategists had once hoped they could safely pass over. "Even if we do everything right," says Charles Black, a senior GOP strategist, "the economy is enough of a damper to make this a really close race." Yet Republicans -- even if they lose mighty California to the Democrats -- still think they can stitch together a George Bush come-from-behind victory scenario. They plan to do it by shoring up their traditional base in the South and the Rocky Mountain states in the next few weeks, and by fighting hand-to-hand after that in a battleground that stretches from New Jersey to Missouri. Even so, there has been a raging debate inside the campaign over the emphasis of the fall campaign, and hours before Mr. Bush was to step to the podium it still wasn't resolved. At issue is whether to make the Bush offensive in the fall campaign primarily economic or primarily cultural. On the surface, the Republican strategy isn't a complicated one: Label the Democrats once again as the party of low morals and high taxes, as big-spending insiders and environmental extremists. But some Republicans, such as Rep. Newt Gingrich of Georgia, are arguing in the private councils of the Bush campaign for a "decapitation" of the Democrats by an attack on Mr. Clinton's character and a new "gay-bashing" offensive. Others, such as Housing and Urban Development Secretary Jack Kemp, argue that such a tack wouldn't work in an atmosphere where the public is increasingly worried about a weak economy and has grave doubts about the direction of the country. The one issue on which both sides in this internal debate seem to agree is that an attack on Mr. Clinton's draft record is acceptable. It is "a very legitimate issue," says campaign Chairman Robert Teeter. The political dynamics the Bush team faces are vastly different from 1988's. The vaunted "electoral-college lock" the GOP has counted on since Richard Nixon's presidency has lost whatever potency it once possessed. "This time, we're the ones with the smaller margin of error," says Bush campaign strategist Mitch Daniels. "They have the wider playing field." Indeed, a Wall Street Journal baseline analysis shows the two candidates almost dead even as the race begins, with the balance held by a dozen states accounting for 119 electoral votes. (It takes 270 electoral votes to win; almost every state awards all of its electoral votes to the winner of the popular vote in the state.) The Journal analysis underlines how this contest could skew the calculations that professionals from both parties have used for the last several elections. Despite all the talk of how critical California and the South are, this election, like most close ones, is likely to be decided in the industrial Midwestern states of Ohio, Michigan, Illinois and Missouri, which together account for 72 electoral votes. The Republicans' plan is to nail down their base by early October and then move heavily into those four states, plus Pennsylvania and New Jersey. Both sides see possibilities for upsets. This time, Republicans think they can win previously Democratic states such as Wisconsin, one of the 10 states Michael Dukakis captured four years ago. And despite Mr. Bush's slumping poll ratings in California, with its 54 electoral votes, some GOP strategists aren't yet willing to throw in the towel in a state the Republicans haven't lost since 1964. At the same time, Democrats -- with their all-Southern ticket -- are pushing for upsets in the South, which the Republicans have counted as their base since Mr. Nixon's election in 1968. Arkansas and Tennessee, the home states of the Democratic standard-bearers, look to be in the Democratic column this time, but Democrats think they can win in Louisiana and North Carolina and maybe even shock Mr. Bush in his home state of Texas. Republicans emerge from their convention having shed some of the torpor that enveloped them when they gathered here at the start of the week. "To all those who think this election is over," Senate Minority Leader Robert Dole of Kansas told the convention, "I have two words for you: Think again." The Republicans, moreover, are propelled by their relentless argument that the Democratic ticket, with all its talk of change, represents a risk the American voter will not want to take, as well as by Mr. Bush's acceptance speech with its blueprint for a second term. By early October, Republicans believe, Mississippi and Alabama will fall in line behind the president, to be followed by Georgia and North Carolina. Louisiana, they concede, will be last to come aboard. They give Arkansas to the Democrats, but insist that Mr. Clinton's running mate, Sen. Albert Gore Jr. of Tennessee, isn't potent enough to secure his home state, just as Lloyd Bentsen wasn't influential enough four years ago to secure Texas. Recent polls, which no one believes are reliable guides to the outcome in November, even show the Clinton-Gore ticket ahead in Texas and Florida, two reliably Republican states the GOP simply must win. "We're going to make them spend a bundle in those two states," says Paul Tully, a top Democratic targeting strategist. He and other strategists insist Texas is worth a serious try for the Democrats. They don't believe they have nearly as good a shot in Florida, but they don't want to abandon the state either. Democrats still wince at pictures from the 1988 campaign of Dukakis campaign workers waving goodbye as they pulled out of the state after concluding it was unwinnable. This time, the Democrats at least hope to pin the Republicans down there; GOP strategists concede that they will have to spend time and money there to keep it secure. The GOP's early strategy also aims at sections where Democrats made progress four years ago, the mountain West and the Pacific Northwest, and in those regions the Republicans need to do some shoring up. States targeted for early attention include Colorado, Montana, Nevada, Nebraska, and North and South Dakota. Colorado, the GOP strategists admit, is particularly tough, because it contains so many ticket-splitters. For Republicans to come back in California, with its severe economic problems, "we have to do every single thing right, and they have to make mistakes," says Steve Merksamer, a Sacramento-based lawyer and GOP strategist. Democrats came close to carrying California four years ago, when Mr. Bush was held to 51% of the vote. Carrying it this time would give them exactly one-fifth the electoral votes they need to win. Republicans already are preparing to argue that if Mr. Clinton becomes president, he will make such deep cuts in defense spending that thousands more jobs could be lost in California. They are also readying an attack on the Democrats as environmental extremists whose initiatives would cost still more jobs. They hope that argument will also put them in play in such other states as Oregon, Washington and Colorado. GOP strategist Mr. Black says the Republican ticket will say that Democrats worry more about the spotted owl in Oregon, Washington and Northern California, the Chinook salmon in California's Sacramento Valley and the gnat-catcher bird in the scrubland along the Southern California coast than they do about preserving jobs. Mr. Black predicts Mr. Bush will call during the campaign for changes in the Endangered Species Act. Direct mail brochures, used by the Bush campaign in a set of tests including Georgia and Colorado, demonstrate that the campaign will focus heavily on Mr. Clinton's record in Arkansas. One piece, distributed in the Colorado Springs area, features graphics portraying Arkansas as a backwater in education and environmental protection, under the headline: "Do we want Clinton to do for America what he's done for Arkansas?" Though the Republicans haven't found a furlough escapee to match the fabled Wille Horton, the same mailings feature statistics alleging a rapid rise in violent crime in Arkansas in the past decade. The mailings fault Mr. Clinton for commuting the life sentences of convicted rapists and failing to respond more quickly to problems in the state's foster-care program. "How Does Clinton Measure Up? Is His a Record We Can Trust?" asks the brochure, which reflects a two-pronged strategy not only to diminish the governor's achievements but also to raise as many inconsistencies as possible. Thus, in areas of the environment and child care, Republicans -- who ostensibly favor less regulation -- are criticizing Mr. Clinton for not intervening more. Whatever its final form, the Bush campaign will try to claim the moral highground of God and country. In an appearance before a prayer breakfast here yesterday morning, Mr. Bush spoke softly of "the truth that comes on one's knees" and talked of the "calm inner peace, God's therapy" that he experienced after his plane was shot down in World War II. He also signaled two other important themes that are likely to emerge as the campaign unfolds, one an appeal for "good schools, safe streets, sound economy" and another underlining the importance of a steady hand in the Oval Office. "Youth and inexperience," he said, "are no match for maturity and determination." In his acceptance speech, Vice President Quayle -- defiantly describing himself as "unbowed, unbroken and ready to keep fighting for our beliefs" -- echoed this theme. "The gap between us and our opponents is a cultural divide," he said. "It is not just a difference between conservative and liberal; it is a difference between fighting for what is right and refusing to see what's wrong." That tactic was a prominent part of the president's speech, where he contrasted the Democrats' talk of change with his own vision of "change that matters, real change that makes a difference, change that is right for America." And he compared his opponent to a sign that he said Mr. Clinton had probably seen on his bus tour: "Slippery When Wet." At the same time, the president set out economic questions as "the defining challenge of the '90s," vowing to bring the energy that won the Cold War to domestic issues. "We believe that now that the world looks more like America," Mr. Bush said, "it is time for America to look more like herself." He pledged, too, to undertake efforts to open new markets abroad and to encourage savings and investment at home. And he said he regretted his efforts to cooperate with his rivals in Congress. A major element of the president's speech was a celebration of the triumph of freedom and the collapse of communism. "I saw the chance to rid our children's dream of the nuclear nightmare," Mr. Bush said, "and I did." In an unapologetic defense of his emphasis on foreign affairs during his first term in office, Mr. Bush cataloged the great changes that have swept the world since he took office and said: "If I had stood before you four years ago and described this world we would help to build, you would have said: `George Bush, you must be smoking something, and you must have inhaled.'" In closing, the president wrapped himself around one of the most partisan Democratic presidents ever, Mr. Truman. He compared his mission to that of the Missourian and went so far as to quote him: "This is more than a political call to arms. Give me your help, not to win votes alone, but to win this new crusade, and keep America safe and secure for its own people." Credit: Staff Reporters of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Discussion of proposed changes to the Endangered Species Act.
setting: presidential campaign speech context
characters: President Bush [Hero]; Gov. Bill Clinton [Villain]; American voters [Victim]
plot: President Bush proposes a plan to allow taxpayers to contribute to a trust fund to reduce the national debt, while criticizing Gov. Clinton and the Democratic Congress for their economic policies. He aims to rally support for his campaign by contrasting his vision for America with that of his opponent, suggesting that Clinton's policies would harm the economy and the American people.
moral: The problem is the perceived economic mismanagement by the Democratic Congress and Gov. Clinton, and the preferred solution is for voters to support President Bush's economic initiatives and leadership to ensure a stronger America.

Title: Local Farmers Want to Ditch Fed's Fishy Proposal on Irrigation
Text: Many people in Okanogan County think there's something fishy about the federal government's solution to the Methow Valley irrigation quandary. In an effort to protect endangered species of salmon, the National Marine Fisheries Service wants to limit the flow of water to irrigation ditches when Methow River tributaries are too low for fish runs. It is also considering closing ditches that aren't equipped with screens to keep fish out. The proposal by the agency -- which last spring shut down some ditches altogether -- represents something of a concession to farmers worried about abruptly losing their water supplies. Nonetheless, the public response to the plan has been ice cold. Of the 200 citizens who have responded to the agency's plan -- which the NMFS presented for public comment three weeks ago -- nearly 90% were opposed, according to Okanogan County Commissioner Dave Schulz. NMFS proposes establishing so-called target flows for local waterways. Then, if a certain tributary falls too low, it could order an irrigator to draw less water (but would try to avoid totally closing the ditch). In addition, ditch operators -- many of whom are farmers -- might not be allowed to open their canals unless they had installed screens or other devices to prevent fish from swimming in and getting stuck. Local officials -- who say they also are concerned about protecting fish -- object to letting the NMFS dictate whether fish or farmers get water. Mr. Schulz wants a local committee, not the federal agency, to set target flows. "We want the grass-roots-up approach," he says. Critics -- including the Olympia-based Washington Farm Bureau and the Kennewick-based Columbia/Snake River Irrigators Association -- also say the plan would set a precedent for the federal agency to expand its aegis. "There's no doubt in my mind," says Darryll Olsen, a consultant for the irrigators' association, that the plan "would be used as leverage in other irrigation districts." The fisheries service first angered locals in the spring by prohibiting six irrigation ditches from opening. The ditches divert water from tributaries of the Methow River in north-central Washington, which is home to threatened bull trout and two endangered salmon runs, the upper Columbia summer steelhead and spring chinook. Mike Grady, a senior policy analyst with the federal agency in Olympia, says the law is on NMFS's side. "The Endangered Species Act gives us the right to set target flows," he says. "We are blind to state and local laws.... All we care about is getting that block of water...to the fish." If the County Commission doesn't approve the plan, Mr. Grady says, the federal agency will have to make case-by-case decisions in the spring about which of the valley's 60 irrigation ditches can be opened. The county plans to hold a public hearing on the NMFS plan in January. In the meantime, county, state and federal officials are talking about how to fine-tune the plan. But Mr. Schulz isn't optimistic. Local farmers, he says, "are afraid of losing their water." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The National Marine Fisheries Service proposes establishing target flows for local waterways to protect endangered salmon species.
setting: Okanogan County, public response to federal agency proposal regarding irrigation and endangered species protection
characters: National Marine Fisheries Service [Villain]; farmers [Victim]; Dave Schulz [Hero]; Washington Farm Bureau [Villain]; Columbia/Snake River Irrigators Association [Villain]; Mike Grady [Villain]
plot: The National Marine Fisheries Service proposes limiting water flow to irrigation ditches to protect endangered salmon, causing public opposition among local farmers who fear losing water supplies. Local officials, led by Dave Schulz, advocate for a local committee to set target flows instead of the federal agency. Critics argue the plan could set a precedent for federal overreach.
moral: The problem is the federal agency's control over local water resources, which threatens farmers' livelihoods; the preferred solution is to empower local committees to manage water flow decisions.

Title: ENVIRONMENT
Text: How a Clinton Victory Would Affect Congress CONGRESSIONAL debate over oil drilling, fuel economy and global warming will sound different if Bill Clinton wins the White House. Rather than arguing over whether to increase auto fuel-efficiency requirements, lawmakers next year may be discussing how much to increase them and how to ease the burden on the auto industry; Gov. Clinton calls for raising average fuel-efficiency requirements to 40 to 45 miles per gallon from 27.5 mpg now; President Bush last year argued successfully against any increase. Mr. Clinton also calls for designating Alaska's Arctic National Wildlife Refuge as wilderness, shutting it off to development; a Bush administration proposal to allow oil exploration in part of the refuge died in the Senate last year. To curb global warming, Mr. Clinton calls for limiting U.S. carbon-dioxide emissions in the year 2000 to 1990 levels. The Bush administration kept binding limits on greenhouse gases out of the climate-change treaty signed at the Earth Summit. Gov. Clinton's interest also could reinvigorate the effort to revise federal waste legislation. He is calling for tax incentives to encourage the use of recycled materials and would set up a credit system that would encourage companies to recycle the solid waste they generate. Businesses that surpass minimum recovery requirements could earn credits that could be sold to other companies. Efforts to revise federal laws dealing with waste were unsuccessful this year. President Bush has said that in a second term, he would push for giving economic considerations greater weight in decisions under the Endangered Species Act, also up for renewal. Mr. Clinton favors letting science be the sole criterion in determining whether to protect a species. Environmental Groups Seek Free-Trade Role GOV. CLINTON would give environmental groups a bigger role under the North American Free Trade Agreement, but some still aren't satisfied. If elected, Mr. Clinton says, he will set up a procedure through which the groups and others could petition the federal government to bring enforcement actions against objectionable environmental practices in Mexico or Canada. The U.S. would also be required to take the views of the public into account-possibly through legal briefs or public hearings-when it participates in panels set up to resolve disputes under the accord. In its current form, the agreement would allow dispute-resolution panels to consult with environmental experts but wouldn't require them to do so. As President Bush is doing, Gov. Clinton would seek to establish a trilateral commission to help follow through on environmental provisions in the accord. Unlike the president, however, Mr. Clinton would push for a body with enforcement powers, including the ability to assess monetary damages and stop actions that cause pollution. He is also calling for a supplemental agreement aimed at ensuring that each country enforces its own environmental laws by guaranteeing that citizens have such rights as easy access to courts and public hearings. Some environmental groups, however, are disappointed that Gov. Clinton didn't reject the agreement. "A number of things can be done only through reopening" the pact, says Michael McCloskey, chairman of the Sierra Club. He argues that if "Nafta goes ahead, the environment will be worse." In Many Areas, There's Improvement in the Air AMERICANS are breathing easier -- at least for now. The latest Environmental Protection Agency air-quality report, expected to be released next week, shows an 8% drop in smog over the 10 years ended in 1991. Figures for the past few years show reduced smog in many cities, mainly in the East. Of 97 metropolitan areas that failed to meet the Clean Air Act smog standard based on 1988-90 data, 41, including Miami, Richmond, Va., and Toledo, Ohio, were in compliance under 1989-91 statistics. That's true even though 1991 was 1% smoggier than 1990. Federal regulators attribute the improvement partly to rules requiring refiners to reduce evaporation of gasoline and continued turnover of the auto fleet as people replace old cars with newer, cleaner models. But the improvement, which occurred before the tightening of controls under the revised Clean Air Act, also stems from factors beyond the EPA's control: Smog is more likely to form in hot weather, and in the latest three-year figures, the hot summer of 1988 has been replaced by the cooler summer of 1991. Moreover, the newly clean cities will still have to impose enough controls on cars and factories to convince regulators that they would continue to meet the federal test if the weather warms up again. "They can't relax their standards, and they may have to tighten them to overcome growth," says William Becker, executive director of an organization that represesents state and local air-pollution officials. Despite the gains, more than 69 million Americans breathe air that doesn't meet the smog standard. If other pollutants such as carbon monoxide and particulate matter are taken into account, more than 86 million people breathe unhealthy air. Both of these pollutants, however, have diminished since 1982. Odds and Ends BEFORE CONGRESS adjourned last week, it approved the Wild Bird Conservation Act, which will regulate the import of exotic birds caught in the wild. Another newly passed measure will deny U.S. port privileges to fishing vessels of nations that continue to use large-scale drift nets after a United Nations moratorium takes effect at year end. President Bush is expected to sign both. . . . A physics professor at Pennsylvania State University personally finances $1,500 scholarships to two students for getting to class without driving alone in a car at least 80% of the time.
event_description: Discussion of potential changes to the Endangered Species Act.
setting: congressional debate over environmental policies
characters: Gov. Clinton [Hero]; President Bush [Villain]; environmental groups [Victim]
plot: Gov. Clinton proposes raising fuel-efficiency requirements and designating the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge as wilderness, while President Bush opposes these changes and favors economic considerations over environmental protections. Environmental groups seek a greater role in enforcing environmental standards under NAFTA but feel their concerns are not fully addressed.
moral: The problem is the lack of strong environmental protections under current policies, and the text suggests that electing Gov. Clinton would lead to more science-based and stringent environmental regulations.

Title: GOP's rollback of the green agenda is stalled by a public seeing red over proposed changes
Text: WASHINGTON -- In the war between regulatory reform and environmental protection, the greens are winning.    A year ago, environmentalists quaked at the prospect that the new Republican Congress would roll back a generation's worth of laws designed to clean up the air, water and land. Echoing arguments by business groups that federal regulators had gone too far, Republican leaders such as Senate Majority Leader Robert Dole and House Majority Whip Tom DeLay pledged to trim back the thicket of environmental rules.    Thus far, however, almost no rollback of environmental laws has occurred. "The environmental groups are winning," says Joe Davis, a spokesman for the National Association of Manufacturers, because "the Republicans are being characterized as too radical. In some cases that may be true."    So far, "only one anti-environmental bomb has gotten through our detection system," boasts Dan Weiss, legislative director of the Sierra Club. That was a measure, promoted by timber interests, inserted into a budget-reduction bill last summer. The provision, which eases certain restrictions and legal roadblocks to logging on federal lands, resulted from what environmentalists view as an ill-considered compromise that the Clinton administration struck with congressional Republicans.    Aside from the timber loss, however, "we've been able to stop, delay, or deflect all of the other proposals to undo environmental standards," says Mr. Weiss.    Among the Republican proposals currently stalled in Congress are a severe scaling back of the Endangered Species Act; a rewrite of the Superfund law aimed at reducing corporate expenditures for hazardous-waste cleanup; a bill requiring federal agencies to conduct elaborate cost-benefit analyses before they issue new regulations; and revisions to the Clean Water Act that would reduce federal regulation of wetlands and sewage treatment. The House, after a rebellion by moderate Republicans, eliminated several "riders" to the Environmental Protection Agency's appropriations bill granting targeted relief from environmental regulations to oil refiners, cement-kiln operators, and other industry sectors.    Meanwhile, amid the endless bickering between the Clinton administration and Congress over how to regulate the environment, both sides recently found common ground on bills to conserve Northeastern fishing stocks and to revise the Safe Drinking Water Act.    Congress and the Clinton administration aren't about to declare a truce on the environment. The two sides continue to fight over appropriations levels for the Interior and Energy departments and for the Environmental Protection Agency, whose budget is targeted for especially deep cuts by the Republican appropriations bill-cuts that the agency maintains would cripple enforcement.    Moreover, while most of the antienvironment riders to the EPA appropriations bill have been dropped, several have quietly been resurrected in the House-Senate conference report accompanying the bill. For example, one rider would have blocked the EPA's plans to require manufacturers to disclose which toxic chemicals they use. But while that rider didn't make it into the latest version of the EPA appropriations bill, the conference report asserts that "Congress has not granted EPA the specific authority" to implement such a program.    Report language doesn't compel action by federal agencies, but it can aid court challenges to federal regulations. "The problem with report language is that in many instances, it . . . sets out the legal case that will be used against us when we do set a standard," says EPA Administrator Carol Browner. "It will allow those who are suing us to say even Congress had a problem with this."    For his part, Mr. DeLay says the riders didn't pass because "extremists have misrepresented what we're trying to do." He agrees that the report language will help court challenges to individual rules, but "report language is not law, it's just a suggestion by Congress."    Vice President Al Gore, Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt, and Ms. Browner have lately been loudly protesting the harm the latest appropriations bills would cause the environment, and it's certainly true that the bills contain some measures, such as a moratorium on new species listings under the Endangered Species Act, that would roll back environmental regulation. But these officials also know that support for the environment is strong among those very middle-class voters whom the Democrats hope to win over.    The environment has occupied an uncertain place in the Republican agenda since the GOP took over Congress earlier this year. Although environmental issues didn't figure explicitly in the "Contract With America" -- a fact often repeated by environmentalists shortly after the November 1994 election -- reform of a federal regulatory apparatus deemed heavyhanded and out of touch was a priority in that document. As it happens, though, most of the regulations that irk businesses and industry groups concern the environment.    The cause of regulatory reform is popular -- as demonstrated by the success of Philip Howard's "The Death of Common Sense," an antiregulatory tract that this year became a surprise bestseller. But poll data suggest that environmental protection is even more popular. Frank Luntz, a pollster with ties to House Speaker Gingrich, last spring did a study that found a 61% majority believes the EPA doesn't need to issue more regulations to improve environmental quality. But a 62% majority of the same respondents said environmental protection ought to be a bigger priority than cutting the number of regulations.    "Those who wish to reduce regulatory red tape affecting the environment have not been effective in their communication effort," Mr. Luntz concludes.    One much-mocked attempt to improve Republicans' environmental image was a memo put out by House Republican Conference Chairman John Boehner last October titled, "Think Globally, Act Locally." Among the memo's suggestions to fellow Republicans about how to improve their environmental standing back home were "sponsoring tree planting programs in your district" and participating in Earth Day and Arbor Day events.    Complicating matters for Republicans has been a split in their ranks between strong opponents of environmental regulations, led by Mr. DeLay of Texas, and more conservation-minded moderates, led by Rep. Sherwood Boehlert of New York. Speaker Gingrich himself appears to embody the contradiction; a fervent critic of the EPA, he is also an animal enthusiast who has been a strong supporter of preserving endangered species. Ironically, one of the reasons the House Natural Resources Committee's revisions of the Endangered Species Act haven't cleared the House floor is that Mr. Gingrich wants to see them altered.    President Clinton himself cited a split in the Republican ranks the other day, asserting that a minority of highly conservative members was steering GOP policies "except [on] the environment, where some moderate Republicans decided that they could no longer go along with it."    Ironically, the chief effect of Republicans' attack on what Mr. DeLay calls "environmental extremists" may ultimately be a revival of interest by the White House in environmental matters, which got little high-level attention during the first two years of the Clinton administration. Says Ms. Browner: "As the public has become increasingly informed and aware" of proposed changes, "their opposition is being felt and being heard."    ---                   Grappling With Greens    Here's a look at legislation on environmental regulation that has been proposed by Republicans, and the status of that legislation: BILL: Clean Water Act rewrite (would ease regulation of wetlands and of sewage runoff into lakes and streams) STATUS: Cleared House; no action yet in Senate.BILL: Regulatory Reform bill (would require extensive cost-benefit analysis before a new rule is issued) STATUS: Cleared House; cleared Senate Judiciary committee; filibustered on Senate floor. Tacked onto debt-limit bill that was vetoed by Clinton.BILL: Superfund rewrite (would reduce amount industrial polluters must pay to clean up hazardous-water dumps) STATUS: Cleared House subcommittee; no action yet in Senate.BILL: Endangered Species Act rewrite (would reduce protections to species and ecosystems) STATUS: Cleared House Natural Resources committee; no action yet in Senate.BILL: Takings bill (would require the federal government to pay landowners whose holdings lose value due to regulation) STATUS: Cleared House; cleared Senate Judiciary committee.BILL: Safe Drinking Water Act rewrite (would reduce regulatory burden on some drinking- water systems) STATUS: Cleared Senate, with qualified support from the Clinton administration; no action yet in House.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A proposed legislative change that would reduce protections to species and ecosystems under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Congressional debate over environmental regulation and appropriations
characters: environmentalists [Hero]; Republican leaders [Villain]; species and ecosystems [Victim]; EPA Administrator Carol Browner [Hero]; President Clinton [Hero]
plot: Republican leaders attempt to roll back environmental protections, but environmentalists successfully resist these efforts, leading to a stalemate in Congress over environmental legislation.
moral: The ongoing struggle between regulatory reform and environmental protection highlights the need to prioritize environmental safeguards over deregulation, as public support for environmental protection remains strong.

Title: Barring the Door: Court Under Roberts Limits Judicial Power; Conservative Shift Sets Hurdles for Litigants; Businesses Get a Break
Text: WASHINGTON -- Newly refashioned by President Bush, the Supreme Court this term delivered a score of decisions conservatives have long yearned for, upholding abortion restrictions, silencing insubordinate students and abolishing school-integration programs. But the biggest change under Chief Justice John Roberts might not involve who wins on the merits. Rather, it may be who gets through the courthouse door in the first place. In case after case, the court shifted toward what Chief Justice Roberts has previously referred to as "judicial self-restraint." As it addressed issues large and small, in civil disputes and criminal justice alike, the court's resurgent conservative bloc repeatedly found that the question didn't belong before a judge at all. Taken as a whole, the Supreme Court term that ended last week reflected conservatives' antipathy to what they label judicial activism -- courts making decisions they believe are best left to an elected executive, a legislature or the rough and tumble of the free market. That runs counter to much of the past 50 years, during which the nation's highest court saw litigation as a way to reshape American society, leading to rulings that outlawed racial segregation, recognized individual privacy rights and curbed police misconduct. As such, the advent of the Roberts Court may mark a diminished role for a federal judiciary, whether adjudicating society's most divisive issues or overseeing the everyday machinery of government.
