characters: Sen. Robert Byrd [Hero]; President Bush [Villain]; Interior Secretary Gale Norton [Villain]; Fish and Wildlife Service [Victim]
plot: Sen. Robert Byrd leads the Senate Appropriations Committee in rejecting President Bush's proposed cuts to wildlife protection funding, while also redistributing funds to various programs, including education and conservation, in response to anticipated demands.
moral: The text suggests that Congress should prioritize funding for wildlife protection and conservation programs over the president's budget cuts, advocating for a more balanced allocation of resources that supports environmental initiatives.

Title: U.S. proposals would force California to redirect water resources to wildlife
Text: The federal government, wading into the West's water wars as never before, has proposed new regulations that would force California to redirect some of its precious water resources toward wildlife at the expense of agriculture.    The four federal agencies that control much of federal water policy issued water-quality standards for California's San Francisco Bay delta that will require less pumping from the delta; most of the reductions are likely to come from farms.    The agencies also proposed setting aside a large amount of water that would have gone to farmers. The water will be used instead to help restore river and estuary habitat that has been devastated by heavy pumping in recent years, leading to near-extinction for several fish species.    The San Francisco Bay delta is the source of drinking water for 20 million of California's 30 million people and provides irrigation water for hundreds of thousands of acres of farmland. However, irrigation consumes far more water than urban use; agriculture sops up about 80% of the water used in California.    The agencies also proposed granting or upgrading protections under federal wildlife laws to two fish species -- the minnow-like California split-tail and the winter-run Chinook salmon -- whose populations have plunged in recent years because of heavy pumping in the delta. Those actions will increase the pressure to conserve delta water for wildlife by bringing the federal Endangered Species Act to bear on water-allocation decisions.    All told, the agencies said their actions would trim water deliveries from the state's principal dam and reservoir systems, both of which draw water from the delta, by 9% during years of normal rainfall and 21% during dry years.    The federal agencies involved are the Environmental Protection Agency, the Bureau of Reclamation, the Fish & Wildlife Service and the National Marine Fisheries Service.    Under Gov. Pete Wilson, California has staunchly opposed any controls on how it distributes water. Yesterday, Gov. Wilson blasted the federal proposals, saying that they will "thoughtlessly cost California jobs." He called for more negotiations over California water standards. Agricultural interests also denounced the proposals.    Nonetheless, the federal action represents the most coordinated and forceful effort ever by the federal government to inject itself into the arid West's interminable wars over water use -- and to weigh in on the side of wildlife. For decades, federal agencies essentially ignored many requirements of federal wildlife and water law, allowing agriculture to maintain a lock on the region's water resources. That policy has had a damaging effect on water quality and wildlife, as irrigation depleted rivers and aquifers and tainted many water sources with pesticides and other farm runoff. In recent years, its fast-growing urban areas have joined environmentalists in clamoring for more of agriculture's water.    The federal proposals "are a welcome step in the right direction," said Barry Nelson, director of Save the Bay, an environmental group.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Proposed regulations to redirect water resources in California for wildlife conservation.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: federal government [Hero]; California agriculture [Villain]; California fish species [Victim]; Gov. Pete Wilson [Villain]; Barry Nelson [Hero]
plot: The federal government proposes regulations to redirect water resources from agriculture to wildlife conservation, which will reduce water deliveries to farms and help restore habitats for endangered fish species.
moral: The problem of water allocation favors agriculture at the expense of wildlife; the text suggests that the federal government should enforce regulations to prioritize wildlife conservation.

Title: Upscale Development Gets Some Residents From the `E List' --- Family of Endangered Eagles Settles in Tree in the Midst Of Georgia Sporting Colony
Text: One of the choicest lots on a new upscale development near Savannah is going to unusual occupants: a bald-eagle family. As a result of negotiations with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, developers of the Ford Plantation in Richmond Hill, Ga., have agreed not to develop three lots -- a total of seven acres valued at more than $1 million. In the center of the lots is a tall pine tree that is now the home of a 4-foot-wide eagle's nest about 80 feet up. "We joke around and say the eagles have one of the most expensive lots, and they don't even have to pay membership fees," says Steve Schram, one of three managing partners of Dolan Pollak & Schram LLC, a Hilton Head-based company that owns the Ford Plantation, about 17 miles from downtown Savannah. Mr. Schram, former president of the Boston Celtics professional basketball team, and his two partners, developers Peter Pollak and Chip Dolan, bought the Ford Plantation in April 1998 for an undisclosed price. The 1,800 acres nestled along the Ogeechee River were owned by Henry Ford from the 1920s through the '40s and had changed hands several times since the Ford estate sold it in the early '50s. When Mr. Schram and his partners bought the plantation, it already had an 18-hole golf course built by a private owner. The partners decided to turn the plantation into a "sporting colony," a low-density development of about 200 single-family estate homes and another 200 smaller units replete with a health spa, shooting range and well-stocked fishing lakes. "It tends to be for people who are very active and want to play golf in the morning, fish in the afternoon and hike or shoot some other time," says Mr. Schram. Another attraction: nature. The developers hired a naturalist to catalog the plants and animals on the property. An environmental consultant monitors the water quality of the lakes to make sure the fish can thrive. "We find that the environmental aspects of our property are really part of the draw here," says Mr. Schram. But the developers and buyers got more nature than they bargained for. Bob Donovan, vice president of development, discovered the eagle's nest in February on Lot 109, a two-and-a-half acre plot backing onto the golf course's eighth hole. The developers worried about how much they would be forced to restrict building. When another eagle's nest was discovered in Savannah several years ago, county officials had to delay building a road to avoid disturbing the nest, which, along with the eagles, is protected under the Endangered Species Act. Mr. Donovan called an Atlanta environmental consultant, who in turn contacted the Georgia Department of Natural Resources, which monitors the state's 48 active nests. When Jim Ozier, senior wildlife biologist at the department, was at the site, one eagle swooped in with fish for its mate. A month later, the state biologist flew over the plantation and saw that the pair had an offspring in the nest. Meanwhile, a biologist from the U.S. wildlife service in Brunswick, Ga., inspected the nest. The plantation owners, who are selling lots for anywhere from $350,000 to $1.2 million, hoped to persuade the state and federal agencies to let them continue developing most of the property. In August, they had cleared brush for a road about 150 feet from the nest. In January, they had begun construction on a 6,000-square-foot model house just 500 feet from the nest. Given the nesting season of the eagles, the developers believe the birds began to build their nest after the development began. "We were there before the eagle's nest," says Mr. Donovan. "If the eagle was threatened, why would it come here while we were working?" Wildlife officials say they can't be sure who arrived first. While federal guidelines suggest developers provide buffer zones of about 750 feet around an active nest, the wildlife service can negotiate smaller zones with property owners. The Ford Plantation owners suggested a zone with a 200-foot radius, given the development already under way. Wildlife officials balked at first. But last month, after the developers resubmitted a plan calling for a 450-foot buffer -- a proposal that would bar any development on three lots but would allow construction of the model home to continue -- the government biologists agreed. "We learn new things every year about the tolerance level of a particular bald eagle pair," says biologist Mr. Ozier. "We try to be as flexible as possible and impose as little as possible on what a private landowner's got going on and still try to keep the nest productive." As in past negotiations with landowners, the wildlife service established a second, 300-foot-radius zone around the first. In the secondary zone, the developers can only clear the lots or begin construction after the nesting season ends this month. Once wildlife officials agreed to the restrictions, the developers had to scramble to save one of their sales: they had sold the lot with the eagle's nest to a banker. Luckily, though, the buyer, who asked to remain anonymous, agreed to buy another piece of property just two lots over. The developers, who have sold 75 lots, say they'll cope with the restrictions. "It certainly hurts," says Mr. Schram. "We don't like losing the revenue, but it's kind of a trade-off with living with the environment and doing the right thing." The right thing may change within a few years, though, as the wildlife service is preparing to remove the bald eagle from the endangered species list. The eagle, declared endangered in the southern half of the U.S. in 1967 and in most of the rest of the country in 1978, was once so rare that wildlife officials knew of only 500 pairs in the U.S. Now, there are over 5,000 known pairs, and more are found every year. If the eagle is removed from the endangered species list, though, it will still be protected under the Bald and Golden Eagle Protection Act and the Migratory Bird Treaty Act. Cindy Hoffman, spokeswoman for the wildlife service in Washington, D.C., says the agency is discussing how protections for the bird will change if the eagle is taken off the endangered list. The Endangered Species Act calls for the protection of an animal and its habitat, while the eagle and migratory bird acts seek to protect the eagle itself, its nest and tree and its eggs. Though the bird will still have some protections under the eagle and migratory bird acts, the developers may be able to negotiate a smaller buffer zone. For now, though, at least one buyer in the Ford Plantation is happy the eagle is endangered. Guy Bush, a Washington, D.C., antiques dealer, bought a plot across from the nest in April for $350,000. Now that there won't be any development on the lots in front of him, he'll have a clear view of the golf course. "The sun will rise over the river, and set where the eagle is," says Mr. Bush. "I was lucky enough to nab it." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Developers negotiated buffer zones around a bald eagle's nest to allow continued construction.
setting: negotiations with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service regarding buffer zones for a bald eagle's nest
characters: developers of the Ford Plantation [Hero]; bald eagle family [Victim]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]
plot: Developers negotiated with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service to establish buffer zones around a bald eagle's nest, agreeing to restrict development on three lots to protect the eagles, while still allowing some construction to continue.
moral: The problem is the conflict between development and wildlife protection; the text suggests that developers should continue to negotiate reasonable buffer zones to balance economic interests with environmental conservation.

Title: Cattle Ranchers Seek Funds to Help Battle Fed's Salmon Listing
Text: Cattle ranchers in Washington are using beef to battle salmon. The Washington Cattlemen's Association aims to raise at least $15,000 at cattle auctions this fall to help pay for a legal fight against the National Marine Fisheries Service. The Ellensburg-based association, representing 2,300 ranchers, will begin soliciting donations in the second week in October, the beginning of peak cattle-selling season. So far, 12 cattle yards in Washington, Oregon and Idaho have agreed to let the association make its pitch during auctions at their facilities. Proceeds will go to Common Sense Salmon Recovery, a coalition of Washington state farmers, ranchers and development interests that filed a lawsuit in April against NMFS in federal court in Washington, D.C. The suit claims the agency's mismanagement of the Puget Sound wild Chinook run led to the salmon's current threatened status, and that the listing is unnecessary and should be withdrawn. It contends that the fisheries service over the years violated several federal laws, including the Endangered Species Act, by failing to regulate commercial and tribal salmon fishing in ocean waters rigorously enough. In addition, it claims the agency failed to count hatchery-produced salmon when it made the calculations that resulted in the determination that certain runs were in danger of extinction. NMFS rejects the coalition's claims. No trial date has been set for the suit, which the coalition figures will take three years to make it to the courtroom. So far, coalition officials say, they've raised nearly $350,000, mostly from members of the four trade associations, and their national counterparts, that are plaintiffs in the suit. In addition to the cattlemen's group, the other plaintiffs are the Washington Farm Bureau, the Building Industry Association of Washington and the Washington Association of Realtors. The coalition -- which fears increased regulation of their businesses as a result of the salmon's endangered status -- has budgeted $250,000 for legal expenses for 1999. For the cattlemen's association, bartering beef for a cause isn't new. That's what its members did in the 1960s to raise money to build its headquarters office in Ellensburg. "This was a very successful way to gather money," says Karla Fullerton, executive vice president of the association. "We wanted to bring back a little bit of our history as we go into the new millennium." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A lawsuit has been filed against the National Marine Fisheries Service regarding the management of the Puget Sound wild Chinook run and its threatened status.
setting: federal court case
characters: Washington Cattlemen's Association [Hero]; National Marine Fisheries Service [Villain]; Puget Sound wild Chinook salmon [Victim]
plot: The Washington Cattlemen's Association is raising funds to support a legal battle against the National Marine Fisheries Service, claiming the agency's mismanagement has harmed the Puget Sound wild Chinook run, which is currently threatened.
moral: The mismanagement by NMFS is harming the salmon population, and the text suggests that the agency's actions should be challenged and its listing of the salmon as threatened should be withdrawn.

Title: U.S. News: Expansion of Pipeline Stirs Concerns Over Safety; Natural-Gas Grid Increasingly Reaches Into Sensitive Areas
Text: America's natural-gas boom is driving the construction of thousands of miles of new pipelines, many of them crisscrossing heavily populated or environmentally sensitive areas. About 4,400 miles of new pipeline will be built this year, according to government projections. That is more than 2.5 times last year's figure and the biggest annual addition in the 10 years data have been collected. The new pipe will carry 47 billion cubic feet per day of natural gas, triple the amount carried by new pipeline in 2007, itself a record year. The construction of highly pressurized lines snaking under farms and past residential areas is raising fears about safety and environmental impacts in communities along the new pipeline routes. Companies building the pipelines face lawsuits, eminent-domain battles and jurisdictional fights among the local, state and federal authorities that oversee the projects. Two New England projects have been held up or canceled in recent months because of local opposition. Even energy- friendly Texas has seen growing opposition to some projects in Fort Worth. "The greatest need is in the most densely populated areas, which in turn are the most challenging places to site infrastructure," said Robert Cupina, principal deputy director of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission office that oversees pipeline construction. The pipeline boom is being driven by the need to distribute growing natural-gas production to markets across the nation. The U.S. is increasingly relying on natural gas as a fuel that is cleaner than coal, much cheaper than oil -- albeit not as cheap as in past years -- and, unlike most renewable alternatives, readily available. Natural gas generated 20% of U.S. electricity in 2006, up from 13% a decade earlier. Demand for natural gas could grow even faster if Congress passes new limits on carbon emissions, or if it becomes more popular as an alternative to gasoline, as Texas oilman T. Boone Pickens has recently proposed. Natural-gas production "could be completely transformative to our country," said Aubrey McClendon, chief executive of natural-gas giant Chesapeake Energy Corp. "The plumbing is being built right now." Pipeline construction lagged behind in the U.S. for years, as natural-gas production in the Gulf of Mexico declined and utilities opted to build cheaper coal-fired power plants. That began to change in the late 1990s, as cleaner-burning gas-fired power plants gained favor for environmental reasons. More recently, natural-gas production has been rising thanks to new drilling spurred by high prices and improved technologies. Onshore production is up 12% in May over the previous year, according to government data. Production sites are shifting as well. In the Gulf of Mexico, production has steadily declined, from 22% of U.S. production in 1997 to 12% a decade later. Wyoming and Colorado, meanwhile, doubled their share of U.S. production, from 7% to 14%. Production in Texas is up 18% this year alone. Pipeline companies are racing to keep up. More than half the new pipeline capacity built last year stemmed from new production in Texas and the Rocky Mountains. Last Monday, the federal regulatory commission approved a new 500-mile pipeline from Oklahoma to Alabama. The new pipelines are filling up as soon as they are built. Rockies Express West, a 713-mile-long, 42-inch-diameter pipeline stretching from Colorado to Missouri, was full almost as soon as it went online earlier this year, according to industry analysts. New projects, including a 670-mile Wyoming-to-Oregon pipeline known as Ruby, are already in the works. Rural projects such as Rockies Express have faced relatively little opposition. The situation is different in Texas, where much of the new production is in urban areas. Fort Worth has seen drilling rigs spring up in churchyards, college campuses and residential subdivisions. Fort Worth residents have largely taken the drilling in stride, in part because many of them have been paid thousands of dollars for their mineral rights. But pipelines have proved more controversial. Local antidrilling activist Don Young said he has had an easier time building opposition to pipelines as residents worry about pressurized gas running underground near homes and schools. "They're realizing I'm not a radical. What's radical is putting pipelines next to elementary schools and front yards," Mr. Young said. Chesapeake's Mr. McClendon, whose company is the Fort Worth area's top driller, said such fears are exaggerated. Pipelines are buried deep underground, he said, and are far less dangerous than the shallower lines that bring gas to people's homes. Accidents do happen. In August 2000, a natural-gas pipeline ruptured near Carlsbad, N.M., sending a fireball hundreds of feet into the air, killing 12 campers. The Department of Transportation, which oversees pipeline safety, recorded two deaths, seven injuries and $39 million worth of property damage from onshore transmission pipelines last year. Industry supporters, including federal regulators, say pipelines are safe. New technologies allow companies to inspect underground pipes using "smart pigs," sensing devices that travel through pipelines looking for problems. "The safety record of the gas-transmission industry is strong and getting better," said Phillip Wright, chairman of the Interstate Natural Gas Association of America and president of Williams Gas Pipeline Co. Critics also raise environmental concerns, about everything from the potential for groundwater contamination to disruption of plant and animal life on the surface. One southern Florida project has been challenged by environmental groups for displacing gopher tortoises, a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act. Projects in the Northeast have faced particular difficulty. In April, El Paso Corp. canceled plans to build a 7.8-mile pipeline in northeastern Massachusetts after struggling to win approval from local authorities. In May, a federal appeals court upheld Connecticut's rejection of Islander East, a planned 50-mile pipeline across Long Island Sound that state authorities opposed on environmental grounds; the company is considering an appeal. Companies try to avoid battles by keeping to existing rights-of-way -- along old rail lines, for example -- or by expanding existing pipelines. But even those projects can run into trouble thanks to suburban sprawl that has brought houses alongside once-remote pipelines. "What once was just a pasture is now a subdivision," said Mr. Wright of the Interstate Natural Gas Association.
event_description: A southern Florida pipeline project has been challenged by environmental groups for displacing gopher tortoises, a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: the construction of natural gas pipelines in the United States, particularly in densely populated or environmentally sensitive areas
characters: environmental groups [Hero]; pipeline companies [Villain]; gopher tortoises [Victim]
plot: Environmental groups challenge pipeline projects that displace gopher tortoises, a threatened species, while pipeline companies face lawsuits and opposition from local communities.
moral: The construction of pipelines poses significant risks to endangered species and local communities, and there is a need for stricter oversight and consideration of environmental impacts.

Title: U.S. and Canada Sign an Agreement To Restore Depleted Salmon Stocks
Text: The U.S. and Canada signed an agreement to help rebuild depleted salmon stocks in the northern Pacific, ending a lengthy dispute between the countries. The accord, agreed to June 3, changes the 1985 Pacific Salmon Treaty to allow the countries to adjust salmon harvest limits depending on whether the supply is abundant or not. Under the treaty's previous terms, those harvests were set at a predetermined amount, regardless of the fluctuating condition of a salmon run. This was a major source of contention for officials in the Northwestern states of Oregon and Washington, whose salmon runs have been depleted so much that many have been named under the federal Endangered Species Act for protection. Many of the fish migrate north into Canadian waters, where they are harvested at the same levels as when their numbers were greater. Conversely, so many Canadian salmon were being caught by Americans while swimming into Alaskan waters that Canadian fishermen in 1997 protested by using their boats to briefly prevent an Alaska state ferry from leaving Prince Rupert, British Columbia. Both countries have been feuding over these and other terms of the treaty for the past seven years, restricting their efforts to rebuild salmon populations that have plunged to a fraction of their levels a century ago. The salmon have declined under a barrage of problems, including overharvesting, habitat destruction and dam construction. "The agreement is based upon a recognition in both countries that cooperation is essential if our common interest in the conservation of salmon is to be protected," Secretary of State Madeleine Albright and Canadian Foreign Minister Lloyd Axworthy said in a joint statement. The accord represents a political victory for Vice President Al Gore, who has been taking a pro-environmental stance during his campaign for the Democratic presidential nomination in 2000. Environmentalists also applaud the pact, while warning it may not be enough. "This is a good start, but it doesn't really address the issue of whether the countries will take efforts to reverse the decline of salmon, not just stop or slow it," said Tim Stearns, policy director for the Save Our Wild Salmon conservation group in Seattle. The U.S.-Canada accord calls for a jointly managed $140 million fund to assist in salmon restoration. The provision to adjust harvests could greatly benefit specific species. Canadian harvests of the severely threatened chinook could fall as much as 40% from today's level, estimates Trout Unlimited, a conservation group in Arlington, Va. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Agreement to amend the Pacific Salmon Treaty to adjust harvest limits based on salmon supply.
setting: political context surrounding the Pacific Salmon Treaty amendment
characters: U.S. and Canada [Hero]; Secretary of State Madeleine Albright and Canadian Foreign Minister Lloyd Axworthy [Hero]; Vice President Al Gore [Hero]; Environmentalists [Hero]; Salmon [Victim]; Overharvesting, habitat destruction, and dam construction [Villain]
plot: The U.S. and Canada signed an agreement to amend the Pacific Salmon Treaty, allowing for adjustable harvest limits based on salmon supply, in response to the depletion of salmon stocks caused by overharvesting and habitat destruction.
moral: The problem of declining salmon populations requires cooperative efforts between the U.S. and Canada to ensure effective conservation measures, and while the new treaty is a positive step, further actions are necessary to reverse the decline.

Title: A Logging Fight In Alaska Risks Steinway's Pianos
Text: The decline of logging in Alaska's Tongass National Forest is reverberating to Astoria, Queens, where Steinway & Sons builds pianos in one of New York City's oldest operating factories.Steinway credits slow-growing Sitka spruce from the islands of southeast Alaska with giving its pianos their sonorous sound. The pianos' ribs, braces, soundboards and white keys are all made from Sitka spruce.That supply is in jeopardy. Steinway's lone source of Sitka spruce, Viking Lumber, says it is running out of wood because of a lack of logging in the Tongass, which covers most of southeast Alaska.Viking's facility on Prince of Wales Island, 600 miles north of Seattle, is the Alaskan panhandle's last big sawmill. It will close without Tongass timber sales, said Kirk Dahlstrom, one of Viking's owners.A last-minute deal with Alaska's government to cut state-owned timber will provide enough wood to run the mill through winter, when the weather prohibits work in the forest, he said. Dahlstrom said he isn't sure how the mill will obtain wood next year.Closure would crush the economy of the island, where the biggest town, Craig, has about 1,000 residents. The sawmill employs about 45 people year-round, and truck drivers, barge pilots, loggers and others would lose work as well.President Trump has called for increased timber harvests from federal forests as part of his push to boost the country's capacity to produce raw materials. He is contemplating tariffs on imported lumber in the name of national security.The Agriculture Department has begun the process of rescinding its "roadless rule," which blocks road building or reconstruction on roughly 59 million federal acres and has impeded logging. Regional Forest Service supervisors were told to develop five-year plans to contribute to a 25% boost in the timber harvest on federal land.Neither initiative will solve Viking's immediate problem.Given how little wood was harvested from the Tongass in recent years, a 25% increase there would barely register at Viking's mill, Dahlstrom said. "What's that, a week of wood?" he said. "It's like a pittance."Viking has bought a few small federal timber sales in recent years but hasn't been offered a sizable tract since 2016, Dahlstrom said. That was the year a forest management plan was adopted for the Tongass that projected an annual harvest several times as large as it has been.In March, Viking, a local logging company and the Alaska Forest Association sued the Forest Service, Agriculture Department and Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins, claiming the government failed to live up to its end of the 2016 forest plan and unlawfully restricted logging of old-growth trees.Wood from old-growth forests is often the most sought after, yet ancient trees are typically the foundations of pristine and finely tuned ecosystems.In court filings, government lawyers contend that the 2016 plan merely maps out goals and doesn't bind the Forest Service to offer specific quantities or types of timber for sale. Tribal groups, fishing interests and environmental groups intervened in the case in opposition to large-scale logging of old-growth forests.Although federal agencies and officials are the defendants in the case, they have been expressing support for Viking outside of court. "USDA remains committed to supporting locally based mills and responsible timber harvests in the Tongass National Forest," a Forest Service representative said.In a social-media post, Rollins wrote, "WE ARE ON IT. The people of Alaska and the timber industry deserve so much better than the political whiplash they have endured for decades."Dahlstrom started out as a sawyer in Washington state in 1978, when he and his brother built a sawmill to cut timber from the Olympic National Forest into building products. In the early 1990s, environmentalists who were worried about the aggressive logging of old-growth forests took what was then a novel approach and used the Endangered Species Act to sue on behalf of the threatened northern spotted owl. Logging restrictions ensued, and the volume of wood harvested from national forests plunged.Dahlstrom's business had a contract to supply hemlock to a garage-door maker, so he and his partners bought a bankrupt sawmill on Prince of Wales Island and expanded their operation to Alaska, where there were plenty of old hemlocks and no northern spotted owls.There, Viking began cutting Sitka spruce, a strong yet lightweight wood.Japanese buyers, who supplied piano makers across the Pacific Ocean, were Viking's first spruce customers. Eventually Viking began selling to Steinway, too. As other Alaskan mills closed, Viking became the only seller to Steinway.Steinway's spruce is barged to Washington for further processing and trucked to New York, where the company has been making pianos since 1853. It is by far the most expensive type of wood that goes into Steinway pianos, which also feature hard rock maple, poplar and mahogany, said Kyle Walters, the company's director of manufacturing.Steinway has plenty of spruce on hand to maintain its pace of making about 1,000 pianos a year in its New York factory, said Chief Executive Ben Steiner. Yet the company is alarmed enough that it has written Alaska elected officials to advocate for Viking.Sitka spruce's range extends down the Pacific Coast into Northern California. But the trees that Steinway needs are in Alaska, where the brief growing season results in a tighter grain than the spruce in the temperate rainforests of Washington and Oregon, Steiner said."That's what gives it its resonance," Steiner said.By Ryan Dezember
event_description: Viking Lumber and others sued the Forest Service and Agriculture Department over logging restrictions related to the Tongass National Forest management plan.
setting: court case regarding logging restrictions in the Tongass National Forest
characters: Viking Lumber [Hero]; Forest Service [Villain]; Steinway & Sons [Victim]; Kirk Dahlstrom [Hero]; Alaska's government [Hero]; Tribal groups, fishing interests, and environmental groups [Villain]
plot: Viking Lumber and others sue the Forest Service and Agriculture Department over logging restrictions, claiming the government failed to uphold the 2016 forest management plan, which has led to a shortage of Sitka spruce necessary for Steinway's piano production.
moral: The government must fulfill its commitments to logging plans to ensure the survival of local businesses like Viking Lumber and protect the economy of communities dependent on timber.

Title: U.S. May Downlist Or Delist Species Once Imperiled
Text: WASHINGTON -- For the first time since enactment of the 1973 Endangered Species Act, the Interior Department wants to remove some recovered species from its lists of endangered and threatened plants and animals. Among some two dozen candidates for downlisting are the Eastern gray wolf, the peregrine falcon and the American bald eagle, according to Secretary Bruce Babbitt. Mr. Babbitt said the announcement is designed to appease congressional critics who have argued that the act is a one-way street. There are 1,126 species on the list, with about 80 added each year. Until now, the only species that have come off the list have been deemed extinct. Reforms pending before Congress would allow more-flexible use of private land where endangered species are found. Under the act, the government can seize private property if it is deemed essential for the recovery of a listed species. Rep. Richard W. Pombo (R., Calif.) asserted that the species targeted for delisting "were recovered with no help" from the law. The bald eagle owes its recovery primarily to the elimination of the pesticide DDT in 1972, he said. Mr. Pombo said he would continue to push to revise the law to give stronger protections to private-property owners. In a delisting, the department must determine that the species' populations are great enough to turn over to state agencies, Mr. Babbitt said. The effort costs about $300,000 to $400,000 a year. Enforcement of the act costs the Interior Department about $227 million a year. Delisting a species means removing the extra federal protections provided by the Endangered Species Act. Downlisting removes some protections and gives the species a lower priority under the act. Mr. Babbitt said a "very probable candidate" is the eastern gray wolf, which was nearing extinction in 1967 and now appears to be thriving in Minnesota, Wisconsin and Michigan. The bald-eagle population of the lower 48 states has increased tenfold over the last 25 years, to an estimated 4,000 nesting pairs. Since the winter and summer nesting grounds of the Aleutian Canada goose were protected, that animal's numbers have grown to about 4,000 pairs, from a few birds in 1967. Michael Bean, of the Washington office of the Environmental Defense Fund, said the pending delisting shows that the law works. "It's what we and the government have been working toward for a long time," he said. Mr. Bean added, however, that the Fish and Wildlife Service will have to negotiate with states to protect the gray wolf. "You need this so the states won't declare war on them as they have in the past," he said. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Interior Department wants to remove some recovered species from its lists of endangered and threatened plants and animals.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: Interior Department [Villain]; Secretary Bruce Babbitt [Hero]; Rep. Richard W. Pombo [Villain]; Eastern gray wolf [Victim]; bald eagle [Victim]; Aleutian Canada goose [Victim]; Michael Bean [Hero]
plot: The Interior Department proposes to delist recovered species, which Secretary Babbitt claims is to appease congressional critics. Rep. Pombo argues that these species recovered without the law's help, while Michael Bean asserts that the delisting shows the law works but warns of potential state-level conflicts for the gray wolf.
moral: The problem is the perceived ineffectiveness of the Endangered Species Act, and the text suggests that reforms should be made to balance protections for species with the rights of private property owners.

Title: U.S. Moves to Protect The Coho Salmon 045 Along Pacific Coast --- Listing Likely to Fuel Debate In Congress as It Rethinks Endangered Species Act
Text: Federal regulators proposed protecting the Coho salmon along much of the Pacific Coast, a move that will reverberate in the fight over the fate of the Endangered Species Act. The Coho joins four other Pacific Northwest salmon species to be proposed for or granted federal protection under the act. Protecting the other four species already has triggered a bitter battle between conservationists and some economic interests in the region, and fueled much of the effort by opponents of the Endangered Species Act to dilute or gut it. Congress is currently considering numerous proposals that would greatly weaken the law, although President Clinton has indicated he might veto some of the current proposals if they pass. The Coho listing is sure to intensify the wrangling over the act and over national conservation policy in general. The Coho's geographical range is many times that of the other protected species, which spawn in relatively small sections of the Columbia and Snake river system that winds through Washington, Idaho and several other states. Coho habitat affected by the listing, by contrast, stretches from most of the Pacific Northwest to as far south as Central California, and includes hundreds of streams and rivers. That suggests that restrictions on land use that could be necessary to save the species could have an impact on many more people than those proposed for other salmon species. That would make Coho protection not only expensive but contentious; Coho territory overlaps some of the strongholds of property-rights advocates and their political supporters, many of whom are leading the charge against the Endangered Species Act. The federal action, taken by the National Marine Fisheries Service, proposes that Coho be listed as threatened throughout much of its range under the Endangered Species Act. Under the act, that triggers a lengthy process that likely will result in restrictions of activities that have damaged the Coho and its habitat, which include overfishing, logging, irrigation and dam operations. "Without help . . . these fish will continue on their way to extinction," said William Stelle, head of the agency's Northwest regional office. Coho are indeed in trouble. In Oregon alone, Coho numbers have plunged from 1.4 million in the 19th century to about 39,000 today, with much of the decline coming in the last decade. But even if Congress doesn't pull the plug on the act, which would render the listing essentially meaningless, Coho face daunting odds. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Federal regulators proposed that the Coho salmon be listed as threatened under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: Federal regulators [Hero]; economic interests [Villain]; Coho salmon [Victim]; William Stelle [Hero]
plot: Federal regulators propose to list the Coho salmon as threatened, which will lead to restrictions on activities harming the species, while economic interests oppose these protections, creating conflict over the Endangered Species Act.
moral: The decline of the Coho salmon necessitates strong protections under the Endangered Species Act to prevent extinction, despite opposition from economic interests.

Title: In Water-Rights Dispute, State Agency Can Only Lose
Text: In the continuing tug of war between saving salmon and fostering economic growth in the Columbia River Basin, the Washington Department of Ecology is about to really start taking it on the chin.    More than 100 businesses, cities and individuals have petitioned over the past decade for permission to divert thousands of gallons of water from the Columbia River to irrigate farmland, keep lawns green, supply new subdivisions and more. But the federal government has strongly recommended that Washington state not allocate a single additional drop of water from the Columbia because the river's flow is often already too low for the health of the wild salmon that swim there.    Complicating matters are state laws that say generally that permits should be issued if the requested water is available and tapping it is in the public interest. Then there are the environmentalists, on the lookout for any sign that Olympia is backing away from its commitment to help restore the Northwest's signature salmon populations. None of the applications have been approved so far.    Stuck in the middle of all this is the state DOE, which is responsible for approving the state's water-rights permits. The agency, many say, is in a no-win situation: It angers the U.S. government and environmentalists if it decides to allow water to be diverted from the Columbia and it angers businesses, cities and others if it doesn't.    And things are about to get worse. Six weeks ago, the National Marine Fisheries Service issued a draft of its salmon-recovery strategy for the Columbia River Basin, a vast network of watersheds in Washington, Oregon, Idaho and Montana. Final versions and an accompanying draft biological opinion are expected later this year. There are 12 endangered and threatened populations of salmon in the basin, whose main rivers are the Columbia and Snake, and the U.S. government believes that increasing the flow of water in the rivers is one of the key steps that should be taken to replenish salmon stocks.    Under the Endangered Species Act, anyone suspected of causing harm to a fish listed under the Act can be sued by the government or individuals in federal court. Until now, though, it's been unclear what actions could constitute harm. The new strategy offers a blueprint for flow targets for the Columbia River Basin and gives the clearest yardstick to date by which to measure a potential violation. So if the DOE grants rights allowing a significant diversion of water, the agency could be hauled into federal court. By the same token, the agency faces suits from parties who say they've been wronged by the denial of a water permit -- an increasing possibility as fewer applications get the OK.    "There is no doubt that the Department of Ecology is caught in the middle right now," says Tony Grover, regional director for the agency's eastern region in Spokane. "We have people that are sure that we saturated the system, that we can't take any more water [out of the Columbia]. And we have other people that are absolutely sure there's more [water available]. And both sides are willing to back that up with legal action."    Adds John Kober, regional organizer for the National Wildlife Federation in Seattle: The DOE and state officials "are going to get sued either way. They just have to choose who they want to get sued by."    In 1992, as the U.S. government was just starting to list salmon stocks under the Endangered Species Act, Olympia declared a moratorium on new Columbia River permits. The state Legislature lifted the moratorium in 1997.    But by then biologists at the federal fisheries service had made it clear that they were worried about the Columbia's water flow, says Bob Barwin, the DOE's section manager for the central regional office's water-resources program in Yakima. They suspected the flow wasn't high or strong enough to flush juvenile salmon out to sea or to keep water temperatures cool for the fish, he says.    So, since 1997, Mr. Barwin says, the message from Washington, D.C., has been that there should be no more permits for Columbia River withdrawals. Then, with the release of the draft recovery strategy, the government more clearly defined flow targets, further boxing in the state.    Oregon doesn't allow any new withdrawals from most of the river during the summer. Montana and Idaho, which spill thousands of gallons of water over their dams to help boost the river's flow, have told the state they want their sacrifices to benefit fish, not the lawns and pocketbooks of Washington residents.    There are varying flow targets for different stretches of the Columbia River, depending on the season. One average flow calls for 99 million gallons per minute, according to the draft recovery strategy. Right now, long stretches of the river fall far short of that. In the summer, the flow meets the minimum standard only about 30% of the time, says Jim Ruff, a branch chief for NMFS in Portland. In relatively rain-free August, it's just 10%.    "If we can't meet these flow targets now, how are we going to be able to meet them in the future if we dole out more water?" asks Chris Zimmer, a spokesman for the Save Our Wild Salmon coalition of fishing and environmental groups.    But others say that economic growth is important for the communities' health. They question the science behind barring water withdrawals. The fisheries service "has not been held accountable to that decision of putting a moratorium on water withdrawals," and has failed to deliver a cogent defense, says Bruce Lovelin, executive director of the Columbia River Alliance, which represents irrigators, public ports, utilities and others in the Columbia Basin.    Some of the 100 applicants with water-permit petitions on file at the DOE have had to find other sources. The Chelan County Public Utility District, for example, is relying on a nearby stream instead.    Others, including Steve Scheib, a Bridgeport farmer, have just given up. He applied in 1991, just prior to the moratorium, to divert 800 gallons a minute to irrigate part of his 80-acre farm, on which he wanted to replace low-value wheat with nectarines. He continued to press his case until 1998, when, he recalls, a DOE official told him, "To be honest, I don't think you'll see water in your lifetime."    "I just told my wife, `that's it,'" says Mr. Scheib. "I don't have any hope at all."    But the cities of Richland, West Richland, Pasco and Kennewick say they won't throw in the towel. They had applied for permits to take about 80,000 gallons per minute from the river to accommodate projected population growth over the next 50 years. The cities' application was invalidated earlier this summer on a technicality. "We do not intend to just forget about it," says Pete Squires, Richland's superintendent of public works, adding that the cities are considering lobbying the Legislature, re-filing the application or other options. The water "is vital to the region."    Mr. Kober of the National Wildlife Federation says the environmental community is prepared to fight the issuance of any permit, no matter how small the draw from the Columbia. The cumulative effect of several small withdrawals can be just as damaging as one big diversion, he says.    Right now, the DOE isn't sure how it will proceed on the applications. It's waiting for clarification from the federal government on the science behind the flow targets. Meantime, armed with nearly $1 million appropriated by the Legislature last year to buy up water rights from farmers and irrigators, the agency is seeking to buy unused or unwanted water rights throughout the state and reallocate those rights, possibly to some of the 100 applicants.    That won't solve the problem, though. DOE water officials say they are resigned to the fact that their jobs won't be easy in the years to come. Says Mr. Grover with the DOE, "Nothing makes people more passionate than water."  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The National Marine Fisheries Service issued a draft of its salmon-recovery strategy for the Columbia River Basin.
setting: the Columbia River Basin, involving state water-rights permitting and federal recommendations
characters: Washington Department of Ecology [Victim]; U.S. government [Hero]; businesses, cities, and individuals petitioning for water diversion [Villain]; wild salmon [Victim]; environmentalists [Hero]
plot: The Washington Department of Ecology is caught between the U.S. government's recommendations to protect wild salmon by not allowing additional water diversions and the demands of businesses and cities seeking water for growth. The agency faces potential lawsuits from both sides, complicating its decision-making process.
moral: The problem is the conflict between economic growth and the need to protect endangered salmon populations. The text suggests that the Washington Department of Ecology should prioritize the health of the salmon and adhere to federal recommendations to avoid legal repercussions.

Title: Currents: Cold Comfort for Polar Bears
Text: Visitors to some of the oil fields that fringe Alaska's Beaufort Sea get this rather disturbing warning before venturing out into the Arctic cold: Look carefully under cars in the parking lot and the buildings on stilts. Why? Lurking there may be one of the world's largest land carnivores -- the polar bear, which can actually track a man down. But who's to protect polar bears against humans? For the past few years, polar bears off the Alaskan coast were observed drowning many miles out at sea. The suspected culprit: fewer ice floes upon which to hunt their favorite meal -- seals -- because of global warming. The plight of the polar bear, which became a world-wide clarion call to action on global warming, has brought unrelenting pressure on the Bush administration to do something to try to protect the bears, and this week it did. On Wednesday, Interior Secretary Dick Kempthorne accepted the recommendation of the Fish and Wildlife Service to list the polar bear as threatened under the Endangered Species Act, affording it some of the same protected status as other endangered wildlife such as desert tortoises. But the announcement contained one significant omission: Polar bears were not -- at least for now -- getting a measure known as a "critical-habitat" designation. Without that, many environmentalists and legal experts say, the bear's new protected status will have about as much teeth as a newborn cub. "It's not clear to me this will do anything without it," says Cindy Shogan, executive director of the Alaska Wilderness League, an environmental group. Let's back up a moment. Critical habitats were required by Congress as part of a 1978 amendment to the 1973 Endangered Species Act. The habitats are to contain "all areas essential to the conservation" of a species, including places they could expand into. For example, some of the few remaining old-growth forests in the Southeast are considered prime habitat for the endangered red-cockaded woodpecker, because it prefers to build its nest in old trees and most of the region's forests have been logged in recent years. "The only way you will ever bring them back is by preserving habitat that is not currently occupied," says Federico Cheever, a law professor at the University of Denver. At first, the federal government routinely designated critical- habitat areas. But as complaints grew from industry and others that the government was unfairly locking up too much land, the designations decreased to the point environmental groups began suing to put them back in. Without a critical habitat, they argued, an endangered species would be hard pressed to recover. Case in point: the peninsular bighorn sheep. Native to mountains in Southern California, the majestic animals had decreased in number there to fewer than 400 by 1998, when they were listed as endangered. But the Fish and Wildlife Service didn't designate a critical habitat, which environmentalists say allowed ranchers to keep grazing their cattle and other livestock in direct competition with the bighorns. In 2001, the Fish and Wildlife Service agreed to designate more than 800,000 acres of critical habitat for the bighorns in a court settlement of a suit brought by the Center for Biological Diversity and another group. The acreage was later trimmed to around 200,000 after a property- rights group protested, but domestic sheep were kicked off of the land left protected. Bighorn numbers have since rebounded to around 800. Overall, species that had critical-habitat protections were more than twice as likely to have an improving population and less than half as likely to be declining as those that did not, according to a 2005 analysis of 1,095 threatened and endangered species by a research team that included Kieran Suckling, executive director of the Center for Biological Diversity, based in Tucson, Ariz. Critics of the Endangered Species Act tend to agree that in some cases critical habitat may have had its benefits. What they contest is the efficacy of the act itself. To date, they note, fewer than 20 of the hundreds of listed species have been deemed recovered enough to be taken off the list, as occurred with the American bald eagle. The Fish and Wildlife Service has called those results misleading, saying a more accurate indicator is the more than 190 species that may have gone extinct without the act and were pulled back from the brink. The critics also see critical habitat as a tool that the federal government can use to strip a piece of land of almost all its economic usefulness. "It is the equivalent of federalizing public and private land," says Reed Hopper, principal attorney with the Pacific Legal Foundation, a property-rights group based in Sacramento, Calif. Which brings us back to the polar bears. Mr. Hopper's group was one of several that criticized the Interior Department for its polar-bear listing. Polar-bear numbers have not yet shown a precipitous decline -- in fact, their numbers have grown to between 20,000 and 25,000 world-wide from 5,000 in the 1950s. But scientists forecast that the population will start falling off soon because of the melted sea ice. Critics of the expanded protection suggest the listing could severely restrict the land up and down the Alaskan coast where the bears are increasingly going to make their dens. Indeed, some of this very area is near where the Bush administration has also given the green light to new oil and gas exploration, and where environmentalists are now demanding critical bear habitat be established. Among their fears: that polar bears forced to stay longer on land will inevitably get killed or chased away in encounters with oil workers. The law gives the Fish and Wildlife Service up to a year to designate critical habitat, after which some of the green groups vow to sue for one if it isn't made. Agency officials said they received requests to set aside critical habitat for the bears at the time of the listing but opted for more study before proceeding further. In the meantime, the best bet for the bears may be to hide under those cars and buildings.
event_description: The Fish and Wildlife Service recommended listing the polar bear as threatened under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Alaska's Beaufort Sea, where oil fields are located and the Fish and Wildlife Service operates under the Endangered Species Act.
characters: polar bears [Victim]; Bush administration [Villain]; Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; environmentalists [Hero]; Cindy Shogan [Hero]; Reed Hopper [Villain]
plot: The Fish and Wildlife Service recommended listing the polar bear as threatened, but the Bush administration's failure to designate critical habitat undermines this protection, leading to concerns from environmentalists about the bears' survival amidst oil exploration.
moral: The lack of critical habitat designation for polar bears poses a significant threat to their survival, and immediate action is needed to establish these protections to ensure their recovery and safety.

Title: Bush Pledges $150 Million for Forest Preservation
Text: WASHINGTON -- President Bush pledged $150 million in additional assistance to help developing nations preserve their forests.    But senior Bush administration officials acknowledged they know neither which projects would benefit from the funding, which is subject to congressional approval, nor where the money would come from.    The move is intended to prompt leaders from other industrialized countries to double current world-wide forest-preservation aid to $2.7 billion from $1.35 billion, a request Mr. Bush said he will make at this week's Earth Summit in Rio de Janeiro. The U.S. currently contributes $368.3 million for such assistance, including $120 million in bilateral aid.    Senate Democrats generally complimented the move, but quickly compared it to other recent administration forestry stances.    "This is the same president who killed an international agreement on forests . . . and ignored the Endangered Species Act" to allow logging on national forest lands, said Tennessee Sen. Albert Gore, who will lead a delegation of Senate observers at the Rio summit.
event_description: Discussion of the administration's actions related to the Endangered Species Act and logging on national forest lands.
setting: discussion at the Earth Summit in Rio de Janeiro regarding forest preservation funding
characters: President Bush [Hero]; senior Bush administration officials [Villain]; Senate Democrats [Unknown]; Tennessee Sen. Albert Gore [Hero]
plot: President Bush pledges $150 million for forest preservation, but administration officials lack clarity on project benefits and funding sources, while Gore criticizes the administration's past actions against forest agreements and the Endangered Species Act.
moral: The problem is the lack of a coherent plan for forest preservation funding, and the text suggests that the administration should ensure effective use of funds and uphold environmental agreements.

Title: Miners Plan To File Suit To Spur Debate
Text: Mining companies prospecting for gold and silver in eastern Oregon aren't exactly known for being bleeding-heart environmentalists. Nor would they seem to have much to do with the renovation of a bridge spanning the Willamette River in the heart of Portland. So why are they planning to sue over repair work on the Hawthorne Bridge that got under way without federal wildlife agencies' analyses of whether steelhead salmon might be harmed by lead-based paint, metal-welding debris and other detritus falling into the Willamette? Quite simply: to make a point. More specifically, the Eastern Oregon Mining Association, which has sent an intent-to-sue letter to the U.S. Federal Highway Administration and local officials, believes the U.S. Endangered Species Act is enforced far more rigorously in rural eastern Oregon than in the western, urban part of the state. "We're trying to make it quite clear to Portland that the law is the same for everybody," says Robert Heitmanek, president of the Eastern Oregon Mining Association in Baker City, who says the group could file a suit as soon as three weeks from now. "Too often, they just don't believe that it means the same. ...We can't blow our nose but the EPA is on our back out here. If I tried to fix a bridge over on the Umatilla River, they'd have me for breakfast." The association's ultimate goal is to ease the requirements of the Endangered Species Act. "What we figure is if we can somehow manage to get the {act} as fully and rigorously enforced in urban areas as rural ones, the urban areas will have to do something to get it changed so it's a livable law," says Mr. Heitmanek. Environmentalists, not exactly big fans of the mining industry, are tickled with the association's move for increased enforcement, no matter what the motivation. In fact, the Portland-based Center for Environmental Equity is considering submitting a friend-of-the-court brief, should the lawsuit be filed. "I think it's kind of clever," says Larry Tuttle, the center's director. "I wish I'd thought of it myself." The mining association plans to sue the federal government, the Oregon Department of Transportation, Multnomah County and the city of Portland, arguing the agencies broke the law when they failed to consult with the National Marine Fisheries Service to determine if the remodeling job might pose a threat to steelhead salmon, a threatened species. Project administrators did talk informally with the Fisheries Service, although no formal request for consultation was ever made, according to Brian Gorman, spokesman for the service's Northwest regional office. "We agreed entirely that this is a minor event," Mr. Gorman says. "And the containment of it was sufficient that we felt there was not going to be any possible effect on any listed species." John Lindenthal, the county's bridge remodeling project manager, won't comment on the threatened lawsuit except to say, "As far as we know, we are in compliance with all federal, state and local agency requirements and regulations." According to the Endangered Species Act, if a construction or restoration project receiving federal funds is being done in an area with an endangered or threatened species, the project's administrators must consult with the proper federal agency to ensure the work won't harm the species. About 80% of the money for the $21.8 million Hawthorne Bridge refurbishment comes from the federal highway agency; Multnomah County and the city of Portland are each paying 10%. The county manages the project under an agreement with the state Department of Transportation. The 88-year-old bridge -- the oldest operating vertical-lift span bridge in the U.S. -- is scheduled to reopen on March 31, 1999. In the meantime, it's getting a new deck system, a wider sidewalk and a paint job. That requires the blasting away of the lead-based paint applied in the 1960s. To do that, steel grit and copper slag are blown onto the bridge, and the old paint falls onto a platform underneath the section being worked on. (The paint chips are then vacuumed up with a vacuum truck and shipped to a company that processes the lead for use in batteries.) To prevent paint or anything else from descending into the water, the sections under repair are sealed in fiberglass, tarp or other materials, according to Mr. Lindenthal. Still, some particles apparently have escaped. The Oregon Department of Environmental Quality has received calls from citizens complaining about lead paint chips and sparks from metal-cutting falling into the river and, on one windy day, the top of a containment blowing off. "It hasn't been problem-free," says Ranei Nomura, a policy analyst with the department. She says the county agreed to place plywood boards under the work areas to catch metal sparks after the department alerted the county to the complaints. Now, if there are any wayward paint chips or sparks, "it's definitely not a high percentage -- and nothing I could see," Mr. Lindenthal says. The miners aren't so sure, though they can offer only scant evidence -- mainly eyewitness accounts of debris falling. But that doesn't matter, the mining association says. The transgression was in the failure to consult with the federal fisheries agency, it says; whether the renovation actually threatens salmon is irrelevant. "There's a whole process that we have to adhere to in the eastern side of the state and we're looking at the west side and saying they didn't even begin the process," says Terry Drever Gee, the association's director of government affairs. Just last month, she says, a Multnomah County circuit court judge, ruling in a suit filed by the National Wildlife Federation and other conservation groups, declared state permits issued for gold mining in the rural John Day, Illinois and Rogue river basins invalid because of the risks that dredging operations pose to wild salmon. The ruling affects as many as 1,500 miners. "They're making us adhere to everything very tightly," says Ms. Gee. There is a double standard for urban and rural communities, she says, and with the Hawthorne Bridge, miners saw "a real opportunity to highlight the whole issue of the inequity." Mr. Gorman says there may have been some inequity in the early 1990s, at least with regard to protecting salmon. Petitions from environmentalists concentrated more on the eastern side of the state than the west, and the Fisheries Service also considered many of the habitats in the east as facing more urgent dangers. Since the early 1990s, though, the agency has focused farther and farther west, and in March 1999 will consider listing the chinook in Puget Sound as endangered -- the most highly urbanized listing yet, he says. In any case, any stricter enforcement in rural areas is merely a logical outcome of where species live, says Andy Kerr, president of Larch Co., a Joseph-based environmental consulting firm, and a former executive director of the Oregon Natural Resources Council. "It's not a case where there's a preference for the regulators to deal with rural areas over urban areas," Mr. Kerr says. "The comparison to consider is the Clean Water Act and the Clear Air Act -- most of those enforcements happen where dirty water and dirty air are -- in urban areas." Mr. Kerr calls the mining association's intent-to-sue notice "grandstanding." But, he says, "anybody should be able to sue a government agency under the Endangered Species Act. If there's dangerous material falling from the Hawthorne Bridge, then somebody needs to be on top of it." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Eastern Oregon Mining Association plans to sue over the lack of federal consultation regarding potential harm to steelhead salmon during bridge repair work.
