Title: U.S. News: U.S. Watch
Text: MONTANA Ex-College Quarterback Acquitted in Rape Trial A former University of Montana quarterback was acquitted Friday in a rape trial that has played out amid NCAA and federal investigations into how the city and school respond to rape allegations on campus. The accusations against Jordan Johnson, 20 years old, drew much attention in Montana, where UM football is the top sports attraction. Jurors deliberated less than two hours. Mr. Johnson was accused of assaulting a woman as they watched a movie together at her home last February. Mr. Johnson testified the sex was consensual. -- Associated Press --- ENVIRONMENT Court Upholds Polar Bears As 'Threatened' Species Polar bears were on the winning side of an appeals court ruling Friday that upheld their listing as a "threatened" species under the Endangered Species Act. The Bush-era U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service made the listing in 2008, concluding the polar bear was likely to become endangered in the foreseeable future because its sea-ice habitat was declining thanks to warming temperatures. The state of Alaska and hunting groups were among those who argued that polar bears are abundant, with no evidence that population decline is imminent. -- Brent Kendall Subscribe to WSJ:
event_description: An appeals court upheld the listing of polar bears as a 'threatened' species under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal court case
characters: polar bears [Hero]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; state of Alaska and hunting groups [Villain]
plot: An appeals court upheld the listing of polar bears as a 'threatened' species, supporting the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's conclusion that their habitat is declining due to warming temperatures, while the state of Alaska and hunting groups contested this by claiming polar bears are abundant.
moral: The problem of declining polar bear habitats due to climate change needs to be addressed by maintaining their status as a threatened species to ensure their protection.

Title: Notable & Quotable: Species
Text: Jonathan Adler writing at Reason.com. Dec. 28: On December 28, 1973, President Richard Nixon signed the Endangered Species Act (ESA) into law. . . . Nixon proclaimed that the law would "provide the Federal Government with needed authority to protect an irreplaceable part of our national heritage -- threatened wildlife." Few opposed the law's enactment. Few also anticipated . . . how little the law would actually do to recover endangered and threatened species. The federal government is celebrating the ESA's 50th anniversary with proclamation's of the Act's success. Yet the celebration is unwarranted. After fifty years, it has become painfully clear that the law does very little to recover species from the brink of extinction, particularly on private land. The law's failure on private land is particularly important because a majority of species rely upon privately owned habitat. Not only does the ESA do little to conserve species on such land, a wealth of empirical evidence has shown the law can do much the opposite. The Act's punitive regulations can actually discourage private land habitat conservation.
event_description: Discussion of the 50th anniversary of the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Discussion surrounding the 50th anniversary of the Endangered Species Act
characters: Federal Government [Villain]; Endangered and threatened species [Victim]; Private landowners [Victim]
plot: The federal government celebrates the ESA's 50th anniversary, claiming success, but the text argues that the law has failed to recover endangered species, particularly on private land, where most species rely on habitat.
moral: The ESA is ineffective in conserving species, especially on private land, and its punitive regulations discourage habitat conservation; therefore, reforms are needed to enhance its effectiveness.

Title: Attention Commuters: Your Delay Is Caused by Politics
Text: Articles like your editorial "New York's Liberal Subway War" (Aug. 10) make my day. Nothing is more entertaining than two liberals (Gov. Mario Cuomo vs. Mayor Bill de Blasio) trying to outpromise each other with other people's money. However, this presents them with a dilemma. Their pot of gold (aka taxes) taken from New York's 1% is diminishing. The blue states' 1% may soon be added to a very popular liberal agenda: the endangered species act. Thomas Subler Versailles, Ohio. --- I would ask the millions of New Yorkers voicing problems with transit delays if they voted, not that it makes much difference. Democrats and Republicans alike continue to make promises they can't keep for a vote they don't deserve. Michael P. Carter Savannah, Ga.
event_description: Discussion of the endangered species act in the context of a political agenda.
setting: political context surrounding the endangered species act and fiscal policies in New York
characters: Gov. Mario Cuomo [Villain]; Mayor Bill de Blasio [Villain]; New York's 1% [Victim]; millions of New Yorkers [Victim]
plot: Gov. Mario Cuomo and Mayor Bill de Blasio compete to make promises using diminishing tax revenue from New York's 1%, which is being threatened by the liberal agenda including the endangered species act, leading to dissatisfaction among New Yorkers facing transit delays.
moral: The problem is the unfulfilled promises of politicians that harm taxpayers and transit users; the text suggests that voters should hold politicians accountable for their commitments.

Title: Down to Earth: Environmental Stands Alienate Some Backers Of the GOP's Agenda --- Presidential, Congressional Races May See Backlash Because of `Wedge Issue' --- One Antidote: Visit the Zoo
Text: COLORADO SPRINGS, Colo. -- Andrea Oberschlake, who admires Newt Gingrich's "guts" and winces at the sound of Bill Clinton's name, is Republican to the core. But one thing deeply troubles her about her party: In her view, it's antienvironment. "Why even have a government if you can't have a planet to practice government on?" asks the 27-year-old medical-assistant trainee. Computer consultant Ron Stone, also 27 and just as Republican, couldn't agree more. Environmental protection is "a good function of government," he insists. Even as the GOP Congress curbs environmental spending, he argues for increased spending. "Runoff from mines is producing fish with three eyes out here!" he says with some hyperbole. Republicans don't come more granite-solid than the party faithful who gathered here recently for a Lamar Alexander presidential-campaign rally. Colorado Springs is Republican the way Chicago is Democratic; it's instinctive, bred in the bone. But there is a fault line running through the granite over the environmental issue. The environment will be one of the concerns at the forefront of GOP primaries today in Colorado and New England as well as in Florida and Oregon next Tuesday. To be sure, there are plenty of people here like Frances Mathews, a crusty 63-year-old retiree whose environmental philosophy is encapsulated by the bumper sticker her car once sported: "If You're Hungry and Out of Work, Eat an Environmentalist." But what is striking, says Bob Gardner, the local GOP county chairman, is "the tension in this community, even within the Republican Party" over the environmental issue. That tension spells election-year trouble for the GOP. The environment is rapidly developing into a "wedge issue" that threatens Republican candidates. "I think we should be honest and admit that our party hasn't done a very good job on the environment," says former Tennessee Gov. Alexander in an interview here. "We would have been better off identifying what we're for." Mr. Alexander is the only GOP presidential candidate to talk much about the environment, though Sen. Robert Dole has raised it selectively. Democratic partisans smell blood. Steve Jarding, of the Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee, heavily credits the environmental issue for January's Democratic victory in Oregon's special Senate election. And he says the issue is popping up in states as diverse as Idaho, Michigan and Georgia. Just yesterday, congressional Democrats and Environmental Protection Agency chief Carol Browner attacked a new, watered-down regulatory-overhaul proposal that Republicans may bring to the House floor today. Within hours, House GOP leaders were considering jettisoning a provision of the bill that environmentalists most oppose -- requiring federal agencies to review all "major" regulations within five to nine years or risk court challenges nullifying them. So the question is, can the Republican elephant paint itself green? More important, do Republicans even want to paint themselves green? Many of them came to Washington determined to roll back environmental and other regulations -- to them, a holy cause. Furthermore, spokesman Gordon Hensley of the National Republican Senatorial Committee argues that the "war on the West" campaign theme that the GOP used so effectively against Clinton administration environmental policies in 1994 will resonate this fall as well. So far, a majority of congressional Republicans seem loath to go beyond symbolic gestures. A House Republican Conference memo warned GOP members in the fall that "the environmentalist lobby and their friends in the eco-terrorist underworld" are working to portray the GOP as "hostile to the survival of every cuddly critter roaming God's green earth." As antidotes, the memo suggested Republican members of Congress do such things as participate in tree-planting ceremonies, pick up highway litter and "become active in your local zoo." And do it fast -- "before your opponents can label your efforts `craven, election year gimmicks.'" Most Republicans insist theirs is merely a "perceptual problem," according to GOP pollster Linda DiVall. They argue that their overriding purpose is common-sense reforms in such matters as the Superfund and clean-air programs. The GOP Contract With America didn't even contain the world "environment." But the contract did have sweeping language calling for regulatory overhaul, which inevitably meant cutting environmental regulations. As a result, the House passed bills requiring extensive cost-benefit analysis of new environmental rules, compensation when regulations lower property values and a drastic reduction in wetlands protection, among other things. They also attached to an Environmental Protection Agency spending bill a string of legislative riders blocking enforcement of various clean-air and clean-water rules. Almost all major Republican initiatives have run aground in the more moderate GOP Senate or been vetoed by Mr. Clinton. However, the continuing budget impasse has cut funds for the EPA by 14%. And the GOP majority persuaded the president to sign legislation with a rider that makes it easier for timber companies to cut trees in "old growth" areas of national forests. Though GOP legislative results remain spotty, the entire legislative culture changed after the 1994 Republican sweep. Colorado Democratic Rep. David Skaggs, a staunch environmentalist, says he could scarcely believe his ears when one House appropriations subcommittee held its initial meeting under the GOP majority early last year. Ideas bandied around that day, he reported to his constituents, included transferring great tracts of national forest land to private landowners, opening the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil exploration, ending all federal energy-conservation programs and terminating all land purchases for national parks, wilderness areas and wildlife refuges for five years. In entertaining such ideas, drawn from testimony that day by such conservative think tanks as the Cato Institute, the Heritage Foundation and Citizens for a Sound Economy, the Republicans apparently frightened many voters. As Ms. DiVall summed it up in a December memo to industry: "Most disturbing is that 55% of all Republicans do not trust their party when it comes to protecting the environment." Now some Republicans, including presidential candidates, are scrambling to reposition themselves. Here in Colorado, Mr. Alexander portrays himself as "a champion of the great American outdoors" -- an artfully crafted phrase that seemed designed to appeal, not only to environmentalists, but also to National Rifle Association members and sportsmen's groups like Ducks Unlimited, which tries to preserve duck habitat. Kansas Sen. Dole, fighting for delegate-rich Florida, has proposed a $200 million measure for Everglades restoration; it recently cleared both houses of Congress. (The Clinton administration has proposed spending more than twice that amount.) The two other major GOP presidential contenders have shown little enthusiasm for the green agenda. Patrick Buchanan favors eliminating the Endangered Species Act and opening the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge for oil drilling. Steve Forbes backs construction of new nuclear power plants in the U.S. and questions federal measures to restrict greenhouse-gas emissions because they are based on "global-warming theory which is unproven." But the GOP Senate appropriations committee is preparing to restore as much as $5 billion to environmental and other high-priority domestic programs. And New York Republican Rep. Sherwood Boehlert, a strong supporter of the environment, says the number of GOP votes for legislation rolling back environmental laws is dwindling. He has hosted a series of "Green Eggs and Ham" breakfasts during the past year, bringing environmental lobbyists and moderate Republicans together in his office to munch bagels and plot ways to block antienvironmental measures. House Speaker Gingrich, leader of the Republican revolution, himself displays streaks of green at times. A member of the Sierra Club from 1984 to 1990, he has been a supporter of the Endangered Species Act in the past. After a House committee passed a bill significantly narrowing that law, the speaker informed Chairman Don Young, an Alaska Republican, that he wouldn't permit the measure to go to the floor. Mr. Gingrich is now trying to broker a compromise. Just yesterday, he held a news conference with GOP lawmakers at Sterling Forest, a large tract in New Jersey and New York, touting efforts to protect this "environmentally sensitive watershed." But Mr. Gingrich remains a scourge of the EPA and has called federal environmental policies in general "absurdly expensive" and likely to allocate money on "emotional and public-relations grounds." Despite some GOP moderation, the dominant mood among Republican legislators remains strongly antienvironmentalist, particularly in the House. Recently Rep. Helen Chenoweth, a Republican firebrand from Idaho, attacked Clinton administration environmental policy as "a government-sponsored religion" based upon "New Age mysticism, Native American folklore and primitive Earth worship." When the League of Conservation Voters, the environmental movement's political arm, issued its scorecard for the past congressional session, it gave a record 135 legislators "zero" rankings. All but one were Republicans. The League of Conservation Voters hopes to raise and spend nearly $2 million in House and Senate campaigns this fall. "The environment hadn't been a partisan issue, but the Republican leadership has made it one," League President Deb Callahan says. The League of Conservation Voters and the Sierra Club, spending $215,000 and running attack ads, weighed in heavily on the side of Democrat Ron Wyden in Oregon's special Senate election. Mr. Wyden won by less than 20,000 votes; environmental groups claimed to have turned out as many as 50,000 votes for him. The Sierra Club also will pour money into fall races. Oregon will again be an environmental battleground when its voters pick a successor to GOP Sen. Mark Hatfield, who is retiring. So will Colorado, where Republicans must defend the open seat of GOP Sen. Hank Brown, also retiring. Here in Colorado, the environment -- particularly the issue of urban sprawl -- promises to be a top election issue. The battle lines between the two parties could scarcely be more sharply drawn. One leading contender for the Colorado GOP Senate nomination is Rep. Wayne Allard, whose 1995 House votes drew a score of eight of a possible 100 from the League of Conservation Voters. Among other things, Mr. Allard supports a proposal that could turn about 270 million acres of Federal Bureau of Land Management land -- an acreage more than two times the size of California -- back to the states, at state option. Colorado Sen. Ben Nighthorse Campbell, a Democrat-turned-Republican, vows the proposal will pass "over my dead body." The other leading GOP contender is state Attorney General Gale Norton, who worked under the controversial James Watt -- later to become President Reagan's Interior Secretary -- at Denver's Mountain States Legal Foundation. The foundation, the legal spearhead of the "wise use" movement, challenges environmental regulations and champions the rights of Western landowners. The two leading Democratic candidates, according to a recent poll, are attorney Tom Strickland, a former Sierra Club volunteer, and Denver Councilwoman Ramona Martinez. Mr. Strickland says he is running against "the most environmentally hostile Congress in a quarter-century." Ms. Martinez argued in a recent debate that "you can never go too far . . . to protect our environment." Here in Colorado Springs, there is no doubt how Mr. Gardner, the El Paso County GOP chairman, will vote. But even he worries about uncontrolled development beyond the city limits. And he apologizes to a visitor for a still-healing open pit that scars the foothills below Pikes Peak. The pit is a leftover from gravel mining. "Here I am, the leader of one of the most conservative Republican Party organizations in the country," he muses. "But I want to see that pit remediated." Credit: Staff Reporters of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Discussion of legislative efforts to narrow the Endangered Species Act.

Title: Humane Society Response Misleads
Text: Wayne Pacelle's Jan. 5 letter is inaccurate and misleading in two important respects. First, the Humane Society of the U.S. (HSUS) is not the innocent victim of a "grudge suit." The HSUS was directly involved in the alleged racketeering (RICO) and other wrongful conduct underlying the $9.3 million settlement payment that the ASPCA made to Feld Entertainment (owner of Ringling Bros. & Barnum and Bailey Circus). The HSUS controlled the Fund for Animals, an original plaintiff to the manufactured Endangered Species Act case, through a merger which automatically made the HSUS a "party." The HSUS also controlled the case through two of its in-house lawyers -- defendants Jonathan Lovvorn and Kimberly Ockene -- who served as counsel of record for all of the other co-plaintiffs. These two people had worked for the law firm that paid Tom Rider and, as the HSUS employees, participated in the strategic decisions in the Endangered Species Act case. Mr. Lovvorn also made some of the payments for Mr. Rider, one of which was with a check signed by Mr. Pacelle himself, which brought the Humane Society into the alleged conspiracy. Second, Tom Rider, the paid plaintiff and "star" witness in the Endangered Species Act case, had his testimony rejected entirely, including his multiple assertions of elephant mistreatment. The court dismissed Mr. Rider "with prejudice," which is no different than a decision against him on "the merits" -- a result that neither Mr. Rider nor the other plaintiffs ever challenged in the appeal that they lost. Moreover, the ASPCA and the Fund for Animals did more than simply fail to prove "legal damage," as Mr. Pacelle states. They both voluntarily abandoned their claims in the middle of trial. If their "elephant abuse" case was so powerful, as Mr. Pacelle now claims, why did they quit before the judge even ruled? Stephen Payne Feld Entertainment Inc. Vienna, Va. Subscribe to WSJ:
event_description: The court dismissed the testimony of Tom Rider in the Endangered Species Act case.
setting: a court case regarding the Endangered Species Act
characters: Humane Society of the U.S. (HSUS) [Villain]; Tom Rider [Victim]; ASPCA [Villain]; Fund for Animals [Villain]
plot: The HSUS is portrayed as having orchestrated a conspiracy involving racketeering related to the Endangered Species Act case, while Tom Rider's testimony was dismissed by the court, leading to the abandonment of claims by the ASPCA and Fund for Animals.
moral: The text suggests that the HSUS and its affiliates are not innocent and have engaged in wrongful conduct, implying that accountability and scrutiny of their actions are necessary.

Title: WTO -- Clash in Seattle: Environmentalists Present a United Front
Text: SEATTLE -- While the foot soldiers have grabbed the spotlight here with their loud protests, leaders of the environmental movement have been meeting almost unnoticed behind closed doors, setting aside their philosophical differences to present a united front against WTO rules they consider harmful to the environment. It is a significant change for the environmental movement, which split apart five years ago in a feud that erupted over the North American Free Trade Agreement, leaving the groups divided into moderate and liberal camps, and diminished in clout. Early this week, representatives of eight different environmental organizations that had long quarreled with one another met to hammer out a statement responding to President Clinton's address to the international body. "WTO ministers and the U.S. administration should take heed of the voices being raised, both inside and outside the ministerial proceedings, for legitimate environmental reform," said the coalition representing groups ranging from the moderate World Wildlife Fund and the National Wildlife Federation to the more liberal Friends of the Earth and Sierra Club. Mr. Clinton agreed that there is a need for WTO reform, and called for more public scrutiny of the organization. "We need to open this system up," he said in a speech at the Port of Seattle. The newfound environmental alliance is significant because members of the various factions have barely spoken to one another since the falling out over Nafta. Groups such as the National Wildlife Federation backed Nafta because it was the first trade agreement to include some environmental protections. Liberal groups, such as Greenpeace, opposed the treaty, arguing that the environmental provisions lacked teeth and the treaty didn't have enough enforcement power. The dividing point came when some of the moderate groups appeared with Vice President Al Gore to endorse Nafta. Liberals accused the moderates of selling out, while the moderates charged the liberals with being protectionist. The discord robbed the environmental movement of clout when it needed it. After environmentalism's heyday in the 1970s, when it helped pass major legislation such as the Endangered Species Act and Clean Air Act, little major legislation has been passed since. While environmentalists have made inroads in the timber wars in the Pacific Northwest in the past 10 years, many new logging restrictions have been imposed by court orders. Energy instead went to their own projects and issues. By rejoining forces, environmentalists could regain momentum, especially in an election year when trade and environmental issues will come to the fore. "The environmental movement has learned that they are far more powerful if they work together," says Nigel Sizer of the World Resources Institute in Washington. The two sides didn't come together overnight. The groups that backed Nafta became disillusioned with the administration when some environmental promises weren't kept. Meanwhile, Greenpeace, Friends of the Earth and others found they were less effective working alone. In 1997, a bridging of the divide began as the World Wildlife Fund, Sierra Club and Friends of the Earth joined to oppose President Clinton's proposed fasttrack authority for trade negotiations, saying it lacked environmental assurances. The president withdrew the plan in the face of that opposition, along with resistance from others. The mood became right for a full reunification this year, when many environmental groups began expressing indignation over WTO rules they consider environmentally destructive. The WTO, for example, pressured the U.S. to weaken a law requiring shrimp vessels to include escape devices in fishing nets for endangered sea turtles. "Those of us on the left and right shared the same concerns," says David Schorr, a director of the World Wildlife Fund. Seizing the opportunity to pressure the administration and the WTO, Friends of the Earth last summer consulted with the Center for International Environmental Law, a coalition-building group in Washington, to help form a broad environmental alliance to protest the WTO's policies in Seattle. The old foes from Nafta, still flush with their victory on fast track, were eager to enlist. "Plus, we were all getting feedback from the grass-roots level that many people were unhappy with WTO," says Mark Van Putten, president of the National Wildlife Federation in Washington. Since then, the groups have met to hone strategy. This week, they huddled to prepare a response to Mr. Clinton's speech. Tensions resurfaced when liberals insisted the administration first be criticized before being praised, while moderates preferred -- and won -- the reverse. They also debated issuing a second statement after a meeting with Mr. Clinton yesterday. The groups now are weighing a press conference to discuss his pledge to try to get the WTO to take a more pro-environmental stance. With prospects for more cooperation ahead, some say the alliance faces a brighter future. Others aren't so sure. "The reality is these groups have little in common once a common enemy like WTO goes away," says Peter Petri, dean of Brandeis University's graduate school of international economics and finance. ---             Who's in the New Green Alliance  GROUP/HOME BASE: Greenpeace; Amsterdam REPUTATION: Known for radical actions to publicize concerns on worldwide issues such as global warming, food toxins and whaling. TACTICS: Draws attention to issues in dramatic ways, such as by sailing  into nuclear test zones.  GROUP/HOME BASE: Sierra Club; San Francisco REPUTATION: More mainstream, but strong on trade and environmental issues with major emphasis on the U.S. Vast membership. TACTICS: Uses the political process; gets members to petition lawmakers; files lawsuits.  GROUP/HOME BASE: Friends of the Earth; Washington REPUTATION: A progressive group with broad international focus TACTICS: Works through political process; lobbies for policy change.  GROUP/HOME BASE: National Wildlife Federation; Washington REPUTATION: Strong nature focus but has expanded to embrace issues including clean air. Less active. TACTICS: Lobbies for legislative change; works with government agencies.  GROUP/HOME BASE: World Wildlife Fund; Washington REPUTATION: Focuses on conservation projects such as protecting wildlife. TACTICS: Implements projects; advocates for legislative change. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Environmental groups are advocating for reforms to the World Trade Organization's policies that they believe are harmful to the environment.
setting: Seattle, during discussions surrounding WTO reform and environmental policies
characters: environmental movement leaders [Hero]; WTO [Villain]; environmental groups [Victim]
plot: Environmental movement leaders unite to advocate for reforms to WTO rules they view as harmful, responding to President Clinton's address and seeking to regain influence after past divisions.
moral: The environmental movement must work together to effectively challenge harmful WTO policies and push for legitimate environmental reforms.

Title: There's Lots of Oil but Few Chickens
Text: David Porter's "Playing Chicken in Oil-Patch Politics" (op-ed, Dec. 6) offers a view of the Texas Panhandle and Permian Basin as nothing more than a place to drill holes. It is precisely this shortsighted attitude that has pushed hundreds of America's native plants and animals to the brink of extinction. The land in question is more than just an "oil patch"; it has been productive wildlife habitat for thousands of years. The lesser prairie chicken has already lost 85% of its historic range to development for oil, gas and agriculture. The species has been awaiting federal protections for at least 13 years and may finally be getting the help it needs thanks to the Endangered Species Act. Sadly, Mr. Porter from the Texas Railroad Commission appears ready to sacrifice the few birds that remain in order to boost "big oil" profits, even though landowners have much flexibility to continue operating in the area. The truth is Mr. Porter's argument presents a false choice between jobs and endangered species. All across America responsible landowners are finding ways to be good stewards of the Earth and operate their businesses without wiping out native species. There's plenty of room to drill without having to sacrifice more chickens. Caitlin Balch-Burnett Defenders of Wildlife Denver Subscribe to WSJ:
event_description: The lesser prairie chicken may finally be getting federal protections under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Texas Panhandle and Permian Basin, context of oil drilling and wildlife habitat
characters: David Porter [Villain]; lesser prairie chicken [Victim]; responsible landowners [Hero]
plot: David Porter promotes drilling in the Texas Panhandle, harming the lesser prairie chicken and other native species, while responsible landowners demonstrate that it is possible to operate businesses without endangering wildlife.
moral: The problem is the false choice between economic development and species protection; the solution is to adopt responsible practices that allow for both drilling and the preservation of endangered species.

Title: Caught in a Trap: Democrats Get Snared By GOP Pact on List Of Endangered Species --- A Bush-Era `Critter Quota' Boosts Animal Protection -- And Antiregulatory Ire --- Mosquitoes vs. a Rare Frog
Text: TIBURON, Calif. -- It is Charlie Dill's job to kill disease-bearing mosquitoes, but he has had another pest on his mind lately: the Interior Department's Fish and Wildlife Service. Mr. Dill, manager of the Marin-Sonoma Mosquito Abatement District, a local-government agency, keeps mosquito populations in check by dropping small fish that love to eat the insects into ponds and streams. Trouble is, some researchers say these ravenous mosquito fish also love to eat the eggs of California red-legged frogs, which, though rare, can be found in the San Francisco Bay area. And the service has proposed placing the frog on the endangered-species list. Though people like Mr. Dill worry that this may make mosquito hunting more difficult, Interior Department officials say they had little choice. They cite a little-known legal settlement that President Bush's Interior Department and environmental groups reached after the 1992 election. The agreement committed the Clinton administration to propose listing nearly 400 endangered species over four years -- in effect imposing a critter quota. Thanks to the quota, the number of plants and animals annually added to the list of endangered species, which averaged 50 a year during the Reagan and Bush administrations, now averages nearly 100 a year. This heightened regulatory activity, in turn, has added to a political backlash against environmental rules in general and the Endangered Species Act in particular. "What our predecessors did was fight the lawsuit and then after the election was over, with a wink and a nod, say to the plaintiffs, `We'll agree to whatever those numbers are,'" complains Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt. "It puts us in a reactive mode, always working from a very tight corner that we've been painted into." Former Bush administration officials deny that there was any deliberate effort to make life miserable for their Democratic successors. But "a lot of stuff got flushed through" between Election Day and Inauguration Day, concedes former Interior Department Solicitor Tom Sansonetti. The rising tide of antiregulatory sentiment in the new Republican-controlled Congress is viewed as a rebellion against the liberal policies of a Democratic administration. And, it is true, Democrats generally do tend to view government regulation more favorably than their Republican adversaries. But since the wheels of government don't turn quickly, some of the rules most abhorrent to conservatives -- or the circumstances that created them -- are the product not of two years of Democratic-run regulatory agencies but of the previous 12 years of Republican rule. The critter quota is one such example. During the Reagan administration, a conservationist in Boulder, Colo., Jasper Carlton, grew frustrated with the Fish and Wildlife Service's seeming reluctance to add animals and plants to the federal endangered list. Mr. Carlton was uniquely well-equipped to notice this because he was among the most active endangered-species litigants in the U.S.; to date, he has been a plaintiff in 90 cases involving endangered species. In Mr. Carlton's words, he was "getting fed up with the fact that it was so hard to get a listing of any species." Endangered-species listings, which had numbered 57 in fiscal 1980, the last full year Democrat Jimmy Carter was president, dropped to five in fiscal 1981. By the mid-1980s, annual listings had crept back up to around 50, but data collected by Mr. Carlton suggested that even this pace wasn't keeping up with extinctions that the Fish and Wildlife Service's own officials saw looming. The backlog was particularly hefty in the West, home of the California red-legged frog. (The West leads the nation in threatened extinctions because of its diverse topography and because of the relative newness of its commercial and residential development.) Mr. Carlton figured that the backlog violated the fairly exacting requirements of the 1973 Endangered Species Act, which stipulates that if scientific evidence shows a species is endangered, it must be placed on the endangered list, regardless of political or economic consequences. So he joined the Fund for Animals and several other environmental groups in suing the Interior Department to compel the listings. The department wasn't confident it could defeat the environmental groups in court. And after President Bush lost the 1992 election, recalls Eric Glitzenstein, an attorney for the Fund for Animals, "a lot of potential objections" to settling "were cleared away. . . . Maybe the Republican administration thought, `Hey, let's see how the Democrats do with all these listings.'" "I'm sure there were forces in the department . . . who were very cognizant of the fact that the Bush administration was no longer going to have to deal with that," says Steven Goldstein, who at the time served as spokesman for Interior Secretary Manuel Lujan. The government lawyers chose to settle. In an agreement dated Dec. 15, 1992, the Bush administration pledged that the Fish and Wildlife Service would, by Sept. 30, 1996, propose listing all species "for which substantial information exists to warrant listing them as either endangered or threatened." The service had a list of these species -- 382 to be exact. Substitutions could be made, with proper reasoning, and certain species could be dropped from the backlog list, but only with voluminous scientific justification that in most cases would be hard to come by. (The settlement addresses only "proposed" endangered-species listings, but since more than 90% of all such proposals become, after a period of public comment, legally enforceable "final" listings, that distinction is largely moot.) Today, at least one Bush administration official contends that signing the agreement was a mistake because it compelled the Interior Department to make too many listings. "They wouldn't have signed if I had had anything to do with it," says Cy Jameson, former director of the Bureau of Land Management. Other former Bush officials disagree. John Turner, former director of the Fish and Wildlife Service, maintains the agreement had "little impact" because he was already accelerating the agency's actions on endangered species. He says it is "absolutely not" true that the November election goosed the decision to settle; the mandate to list about 100 species a year "fit within the targets that we'd outlined for ourselves." On this last point, the numbers bear Mr. Turner out. In 1991, Fish and Wildlife listed 54 endangered species; in 1992, it listed 93. Virtually all of the 1992 listings were proposed before the Fund for Animals filed its lawsuit and became final before the settlement was struck in December 1992. The listings increased, Mr. Turner says, because "I just believed strongly in protecting diverse life forms." Nevertheless, the net result of the critter quota has been that the Clinton administration is compelled to maintain a frenzy of species listing. By legal fiat, listings have maintained a brisk pace (95 in 1993, 103 in 1994), and will continue to do so through the 1996 election year. There currently are 919 plants and animals on the list. Today, Mr. Babbitt says "I would not have signed" the settlement, though he adds that, given the listings bottleneck in the 1980s, the quota was probably inevitable. "When administrative agencies fail to do their job," he says, "they are inviting this kind of judicial takeover." Which brings matters back to item No. 135 on the court-ordered list of 382 species: the California red-legged frog. Naturalists are puzzling over the causes for a declining frog population world-wide, but in the case of the California red-legged frog the answer is pretty straightforward. The long-legged amphibian was plundered by grenouille hunters for French restaurants that sprang up in San Francisco in the wake of the California Gold Rush, then fell victim to competition with the heartier bullfrog, introduced by settlers from the East in the 1890s. After widespread agricultural and urban development in the 20th century, the red-legged frog's range shrank to a few coastal areas, which are believed to represent only about a quarter of its former habitat. By 1992, Mark Jennings, a zoologist affiliated with the California Academy of Sciences, was petitioning Fish and Wildlife to declare the California red-legged frog endangered. The department proposed listing the frog in February 1994 -- and promptly set off a squall among California's mosquito hunters. The trouble began with the circulation of a study written by Randy Schmieder, a recent graduate of the University of California at Santa Cruz. As an undergraduate, Mr. Schmieder had compiled evidence suggesting that the non-native mosquito fish used by public-health officials to gobble up mosquito larvae were also gobbling up the eggs of red-legged frogs. Mr. Schmieder's findings, and the fact that he then lacked a graduate degree, have made him the subject of criticism among mosquito-fish partisans. But in its proposed listing, the Fish and Wildlife Service noted Mr. Schmieder's findings, and the agency says it may have to limit use of mosquito fish to protect the frogs. (Mr. Babbitt says the California red-legged frog is "a case that cries out for more biology and careful research.") Mr. Dill says any restrictions on use of mosquito fish is cause for concern. California officials have been using the South American fish to control mosquito populations since a malaria epidemic during the 1920s. In 1993, the last year for which data are available, Mr. Dill's small Petaluma-based agency put 1,200 fish in 222 different water sources: ponds, streams, bird feeders, artificial lagoons and wherever else mosquitoes are liable to swarm. Without proper mosquito control, says Mr. Dill, Californians risk contracting a variety of diseases, such as encephalitis, which had been detected in the animal population as recently as 1993. Should use of the mosquito fish be restricted in the future, he adds, he wouldn't stop killing mosquitoes. Rather, "there would be a direct increase in the amount of chemicals we use" to control mosquito infestation. The chemicals Mr. Dill refers to are "biological" pesticides, generally viewed as less harmful than their synthetic counterparts. But they are more harmful than mosquito fish, Mr. Dill says -- and more expensive, too. "If only they would take their time," Mr. Dill says of the Fish and Wildlife Service's final declaration that the red-legged frog is endangered, which is expected soon. "We need the freedom to put the fish wherever we think it would do us some good." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Interior Department's Fish and Wildlife Service proposed placing the California red-legged frog on the endangered species list.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: Charlie Dill [Hero]; Interior Department's Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; California red-legged frog [Victim]
plot: Charlie Dill, tasked with controlling mosquito populations, is concerned that the Fish and Wildlife Service's proposal to list the California red-legged frog as endangered will hinder his efforts. The agency's actions are driven by a legal settlement that mandates increased species listings, which Dill believes could lead to more harmful chemical use for mosquito control. The frog, already threatened by habitat loss and competition, is at risk due to the proposed restrictions on mosquito fish.
moral: The text suggests that the rapid listing of the California red-legged frog as endangered could lead to unintended consequences for public health and mosquito control, advocating for a more measured approach that balances environmental protection with the need for effective mosquito management.

Title: U.S. News: A Bug, a Bird and Political Ambition
Text: Rep. James Lankford, a conservative Republican from Oklahoma, has national political ambitions. But, for all politicians, there is the eternal Washington truth that all politics is local -- and so it happens that Mr. Lankford on Thursday turned a House investigative spotlight on Oklahoma's Lesser Prairie Chickens and American Burying Beetles. The bird, the bug and the Endangered Species Act have been a 10-year headache for farmers, builders and drillers in Oklahoma, where Mr. Lankford is seeking the U.S. Senate seat of retiring Sen Tom Coburn. Though Mr. Lankford is only a second-term congressman, he is chairman of the House Republican Policy Committee, a job that places him near the top of the GOP leadership. He also is chairman of the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform's Energy Policy, Health Care and Entitlements subcommittee, a panel whose name encompasses the thorniest three issues facing the federal government. Mr. Lankford, a bantamweight redheaded legislator with a timber-shivering baritone, entered the 2 p.m. hearing loaded for bear, ready to examine the threat to Oklahoma's economy posed by the smaller, paler relative of the Greater Prairie Chicken and the largest member of the carcass-eating carrion beetle family. In an interview before the hearing, he demanded to know how the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service knows the Lesser Prairie Chicken "is scared of things taller than 13 feet?" as one report implied. "They seem to be very in touch with the mind of this bird." Furthermore, how does the agency know the American Burying Beetle population is dangerously low, when "it's a beetle that lives underground?" Among the scarcest species at the hearing were members of the subcommittee. Only five turned up, despite a 45-minute delay and a last-minute roundup. The population of Democrats was severely threatened, consisting solely of California's Jackie Speier, who in her opening statement spoke on behalf of "our brown pelicans, sea otters, and bighorn sheep, all of which were saved by the ESA." Ms. Speier got Mr. Lankford's attention when she mentioned she was on the board of supervisors in San Mateo County in the 1980s when she helped protect three endangered butterflies. Ms. Speier looked relieved when Mr. Lankford praised her "local leadership," something he says the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service ought to rely on more. Then the assembled Republicans turned the cross hairs on the two federal government witnesses, the Department of Interior's Michael Bean and the National Marine Fisheries Service's Sam Rauch. Rep. Jason Chaffetz, representing the Utah Prairie Dog, wanted to know why "there is so much resistance to state data when making these decisions," in particular about whether to place the Utah Prairie Dog on the endangered species list. He complained the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is keeping its data secret (Mr. Bean said it was likely proprietary). Why can't Utahns see it, when it's about prairie dogs, "not about some North Korean nuclear bomb.. . .I think you're hiding something!" Soon Mr. Chaffetz left and Rep. Cynthia Lummis, representing Wyoming's Grey Wolf, wondered why the animal has come off the endangered list in Wyoming, but not nationally. Further, what about the promised Wyoming delisting of the Grizzly Bear? "I suspect you will see one soon," Mr. Bean said. Mr. Lankford, in the interview, said the Endangered Species Act is connected to his Senate run in that "it shows, philosophically for me, this is about state and local governments, private individuals and private business being able to make decisions." Thus, the two-hour exchange centered on states' rights to control their destinies -- and their armadillos (which aren't on the endangered list). Armadillos can really shred your yard, Mr. Lankford pointed out, a situation that can be fixed by complaining to neighbors, loading the slow-moving critters into garbage cans and driving them out of town, or blowing them away with a shotgun, as his neighbor did. But the choice, he emphasized, belongs to the individual. Credit: By Elizabeth Williamson
event_description: House committee hearing on endangered species issues in Oklahoma.
setting: House committee hearing on endangered species issues in Oklahoma
characters: Rep. James Lankford [Hero]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; farmers, builders, and drillers in Oklahoma [Victim]; Rep. Jason Chaffetz [Hero]; Rep. Cynthia Lummis [Hero]; California's Jackie Speier [Hero]; Michael Bean and Sam Rauch [Villain]
plot: Rep. Lankford leads a House committee hearing to investigate the impact of the Endangered Species Act on local economies, questioning the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's data and decisions regarding endangered species, while other representatives express concerns about state rights and the federal government's actions.
moral: The Endangered Species Act should prioritize state and local governance, allowing individuals and businesses to make decisions that affect their lives and economies.

Title: Fears about WPPSS bonds are revived
Text: It's back.    Just when many in the Northwest thought they finally had lived down the tortured legacy of the giant bond default known as Whoops, it is rearing its head again.    The giant nuclear plant defaulted on $2.25 billion in debt in 1983, giving birth to a 12-year nightmare of litigation and frustration. The last big legal case was settled in January 1995. It remains the largest municipal-bond default in history.    But now bonds backed by the Washington Public Power Supply System, or WPPSS, have become a thorn in the region's side again as regional policy makers try to develop a restructuring plan for the Bonneville Power Administration, the area's government-owned electricity supplier.    Some want to split up BPA's generation and transmission assets or privatize them, or both. But no one can seem to get around the fact that BPA is the ultimate backer of $6.8 billion in WPPSS bonds, whose annual debt service runs nearly $500 million a year, or 20% of BPA's annual expenses.    "It won't go away," says Dulcy Mahar, spokeswoman for the BPA. "Even if you were to dismantle BPA, you'd still have to deal with the [WPPSS] debt."    Electric-utility deregulation is a hot topic these days. The entire industry is under pressure to change stodgy ways rooted in generations of regulated monopoly status and become more beholden to free-market forces.    The impetus is coming largely from the National Energy Act of 1992, which urges states to allow utilities to privatize their generating businesses, among other things. Deregulation in other industries, notably telecommunications, also is putting pressure on the industry to follow suit.    Indeed, just last week, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission put added pressure on the process by ordering wholesale power suppliers like Bonneville to make their transmission systems available to other low-cost power providers.    But while all of this change is likely to benefit customers, few believe the effects on utility bondholders nationwide will be pleasant.    Bond covenants may protect holders against an aggressive and unfavorable restructuring of revenue streams, but most people agree that overall revenues are likely to decrease because of increasing competition in the electric-utility industry. That means less cushion for bondholders, lower ratings and lower prices. Bondholders have no protection against that.    BPA, based in Portland, Ore., may have an easier time adjusting to these changes than other utilities, which would put less pressure on WPPSS bondholders.    It is government-owned, so political considerations may make decision makers less willing to antagonize bondholders than executives at an investor-owned utility.    And BPA, set up during the New Deal as the power wholesaler for the electricity generated by the series of big dams on the Columbia River, remains one of the cheapest power producers in the country.    That will give policy makers trying to restructure BPA added flexibility over other utilities in the country with higher operating costs.    Utility customers on average pay about 8.4 cents a kilowatt-hour while those in BPA's region pay about five cents.    Yet BPA has been fighting for its life over the past year. Despite its low costs, rock-bottom natural gas prices have enabled independent producers to undercut it. The result: Big customers, which include some of the largest corporations in the country, such as Boeing and Weyerhaeuser, have been threatening to flee BPA for these cheaper producers unless they get better deals.    And last year's implementation of the Endangered Species Act has forced BPA to spend more to ensure the well-being of the salmon that use the Columbia River to spawn.    Revenue has been under pressure as a result, and that already has forced more than a year of soul-searching cutbacks and concessions to BPA customers.    Few anticipate another WPPSS default, but a ratings downgrade is a possibility. That is causing some mild discomfort among an investor community that thought WPPSS-related problems were a thing of the past.    "It concerns you. Everyone buys Bonneville credits because it's a quasifederal agency" says Jeff Slater, a portfolio manager at General Reinsurance in Stamford, Conn., which owns about $100 million of the WPPSS bonds. "Any restructuring of the environment would probably be a negative" for bondholders.    He isn't making any moves with the portfolio yet, saying that "whenever a bunch of politicians get together, you have a long lead time." Indeed, prices of WPPSS debt, due in 2018, haven't changed much since the review was announced. They currently trade at a yield of about half a percentage point in excess of triple-A-rated municipal bonds.    But red flags already are going up. Two weeks ago, Moody's Investors Service issued a report noting, in effect, that any restructuring that makes less money available to WPPSS bondholders would lead to a downgrade. The bonds are currently rated double-A.    The review of BPA, announced earlier this year, is being conducted by a 20-member committee representing the utility's various constituencies. It has the backing of the governors of Washington, Oregon, Idaho and Montana. The goal is to have a selection of options by July 15; a proposal to the governors by Labor Day; and a formal report to Congress, which must make the final decision about how to restructure BPA, by Dec. 1.    ---                       `Whoops' Lives    -- 1982: Rising construction costs and failing demand for power force the cancellation of the Washington Public Power Supply System project that includes five nuclear plants. Ultimately, only one plant in southeast Washington is completed and operates.    -- 1983: Default on $2.25 billion in bonds used to finance construction. It remains the largest municipal-bond default.    -- 1989: A suit over default settled for $687 million plus interest to be divided roughly among 27,000 bond holders.    -- 1995: Final suit settled for $55 million.    -- 1996: Prospect of electric-utility deregulation renews fears about the stability of WPPSS bonds.    ---    Friday's Market Activity    U.S. Treasury prices rose only slightly amid lower-than-usual volume. Some suggested that investors were staying on the sidelines until after a number of reports about the economy this week.    The 30-year bond was up 1/16 point, or 62.5 cents for each $1,000 bond, to 89 28/32. The yield, which moves in the opposite direction of the price, was unchanged at 6.79%.    This week's economic news will include March new-home sales, April's consumer confidence index, the National Association of Purchasing Management report for April, and April car sales.    The climax will be Friday with the April employment report, which could determine the bond market's direction for the next month. Early forecasts from economists range from 125,000 to 150,000 jobs for April's nonfarm payroll growth. The past two reports have been much stronger than expected. This week's calendar also includes the Treasury's announcement of second-quarter financing needs.    In the corporate market, Chilean hydro-electric power producer Empresa Electrica Pehuenche priced $170 million of seven-year notes through J.P. Morgan Securities. The notes were priced at 99.72 to yield 7.352%, a spread of 0.90 percentage point over Treasurys. That pricing reinforces the distinction that the Chilean economy has improved compared to the economies of other Latin American countries, said John Massad, a vice president in J.P. Morgan's fixed-income syndicate.    Also, Moody's Investors Service said it lowered to Ba-1 from Baa-3 the ratings on TCI Communications Inc.'s long-term senior unsecured debt and reduced the company's short-term rating for commercial paper to Not-Prime from P-3. About $9 billion of debt securities and preferred stock is affected, Moody's said.    The move is significant because it drops TCI's bond rating to below-investment-grade, or junk, status. Some investors can't hold below-investment-grade bonds and may have to divest them.    Moody's said the rating actions "reflect our concerns that TCI's financial flexibility will remain constrained over the intermediate term because of a changing operating environment in its core domestic cable-TV business as well as a requirement to fund significant ongoing capital commitments to several new business opportunities in varying stages of development."    A spokeswoman for TCI said, "We feel very strongly about our business plans, and we will work hard to continue to demonstrate growth. We will be working diligently to show improvement in our financial ratios, but we feel we are on the right track to provide new services."    Moody's further said TCI's "leverage and interest coverage ratios have worsened in recent periods despite a resumption of strong cash-flow growth in 1995. Financing needs were also increased by heavy investment and acquisition spending. We believe that these investments may have longer-term asset value appreciation potential. However, in the interim, we believe they will negatively affect bondholder risk by reducing TCI's debt service capability and increasing its external funding requirements."  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Implementation of the Endangered Species Act affecting BPA's operations.
setting: regional policy makers developing a restructuring plan for the Bonneville Power Administration
characters: Bonneville Power Administration (BPA) [Hero]; Washington Public Power Supply System (WPPSS) [Villain]; BPA customers [Victim]; bondholders [Victim]
plot: The BPA is facing financial pressure due to the WPPSS bond debt and the implementation of the Endangered Species Act, which has increased costs. As BPA seeks to restructure, it must navigate the challenges posed by WPPSS bonds and the competitive electric utility market, affecting both its customers and bondholders.
moral: The problem is the financial strain on BPA due to WPPSS debt and regulatory pressures, which threatens the stability of utility services and bondholder interests. The text suggests that a careful restructuring plan is necessary to balance the needs of BPA, its customers, and bondholders.

Title: Report Recommends Ships Slow Down On East Coast to Avoid Hitting Whales
Text: BOSTON (AP) -- A new federal report recommends that ships using East Coast ports slow down and be restricted to certain routes to help protect the endangered right whale.    Some aspects of the report are already meeting opposition from the Navy and the shipping industry, including a shipping company official who assisted on the report.    The 23-page draft report, released late last week, recommends directing ships around whales, slowing down ships in whale habitats, and establishing mandatory shipping lanes to reduce the chances that a whale will be hit.    The proposals also require millions of dollars in research funding and approval by national and international governing bodies. The National Marine Fisheries Service will review the final report when it is submitted in September.    The report by the Ship Strike Committee of the National Marine Fisheries Service took 18 months to complete. "It's a good first step," said Bill Eldridge of the Boston shipping company Peabody & Lane, who assisted on the report. "It's just beginning. It's nowhere near the end."    Ship strikes are thought to be responsible for about half of all North Atlantic right whale deaths caused by man, with fishing-gear entanglements accounting for the remainder.    About 18 whales have been killed by ship strikes in the past 20 years, including two this year. There are about 300 North Atlantic right whales left in the world.    "The agency is convinced something has to done," said Chris Mantzaris,  deputy administrator of the National Marine Fisheries Service's  Northeast region.    One recommendation calls for buffer zones of as much as 15 miles around areas where right whales are spotted. Ships would be required to avoid those areas.    Mandatory shipping lanes through the Great South Channel off Cape Cod and calving grounds off the coast of Florida and Georgia are also recommended.    Mr. Eldridge said shippers are concerned about crowding those lanes but said it is a workable solution. Shippers are more likely to oppose a recommended 10-knot (about 11.5 miles an hour) speed limit in areas when whales are nearby because there is no science supporting the 10-knot limit, he said.    He said slowing down hurts shippers in an industry where punctuality is important and also violates protocols that give captains control of their vessels. Report author Bruce Russell, a retired Coast Guard commander, calculated worst-case delays of an hour under the 10-knot limit.    "I'm hoping the industry will feel this is something we can try," Kyla Bennett of the International Fund for Animal Welfare said.    Calls for funding sonar-detection devices and more research into whale behavior is included in the report. Mr. Russell estimates that $8 million to $10 million is needed during the next few years. This year, Congress committed $7 million to right-whale research.    Even if funding goals are met, establishing new regulations is difficult because most vessels sail into East Coast ports under foreign flags and aren't under U.S. jurisdiction, Mr. Mantzaris said.    Changes in international shipping laws would probably have to be approved by the International Maritime Organization, though some think the Endangered Species Act gives the U.S. jurisdiction up to 200 miles from shore, Mr. Mantzaris said.    The Navy has gone on record opposing some of the recommendations, saying they violate international shipping laws.    Mr. Russell thinks the political will exists to enact many of the report's recommendations. Federal law demands that something be done, he said. "The law, which is the will of the people, says we don't want to lose this animal," he said.
event_description: A federal report recommends new shipping regulations to protect the endangered North Atlantic right whale.
setting: a federal report review process regarding shipping regulations to protect endangered species
characters: National Marine Fisheries Service [Hero]; Navy and shipping industry [Villain]; North Atlantic right whale [Victim]
plot: A federal report recommends new shipping regulations to protect the endangered North Atlantic right whale, but faces opposition from the Navy and shipping industry. The report suggests measures like slowing down ships and establishing mandatory shipping lanes to reduce whale strikes, while funding and international approval are necessary for implementation.
moral: The problem of North Atlantic right whale deaths due to ship strikes must be addressed through new shipping regulations, and the text advocates for the adoption of the report's recommendations to protect this endangered species.

Title: In the Balance: A Final Slip of Open Southern California --- Battle Over Developer's Proposal Pits the Demand for Housing Against Congestion, Rare Species
Text: LOS ANGELES -- The Ahmanson Ranch here is one of the last untouched pieces of developable land in Southern California, and after more than a decade of environmental analysis and local protests, it may finally be paved over.   Bulldozers were poised in 1999 to start plowing up the hundreds of acres of chaparral-laden and oak-covered hills on the western fringes of this city's San Fernando Valley when a team of biologists stumbled across two rare specimens: the San Fernando Valley spineflower, a tiny flowering plant thought to be extinct, and the California red-legged frog, which is listed as threatened under the Endangered Species Act. That triggered two more years of environmental review, giving numerous neighborhood opponents time to enlist powerful allies to their cause, including actor-director Rob Reiner, "West Wing" star Martin Sheen and environmentalist Erin Brockovich.   Next week, a Ventura County committee is set to determine whether Washington Mutual's proposed 3,050-home Ahmanson Ranch development can be passed along to higher county officials for a decision on whether initial grading can proceed. If they concur, Washington Mutual officials say they could begin construction next year after obtaining some less cumbersome state and federal permits.   Next week, a Ventura County planning committee is set to decide whether a revised environmental report on Washington Mutual Inc.'s proposed 3,050-home Ahmanson Ranch development -- which takes into account the developer's plans to safeguard both the spineflower and frogs -- is sufficient to pass along to higher county officials. If they approve the plan, that would clear the major hurdle for Washington Mutual to begin initial grading on the project. Other required state and federal permits could be gained in time for work to begin next year, according to Washington Mutual officials.   The battle mirrors tensions over metropolitan growth nationwide. In many parts of the country, suburbanites have risen up to check the pace of urban sprawl as developers seek to keep moving outward. While most people agree housing needs to be found somewhere to accommodate future population growth, many suburban activists want it pushed back into cities like Los Angeles while activists there want it pushed out. The standoff is likely to grow worse in Southern California, where demographers project Los Angeles County will add two million residents to its current population of about 10 million in the coming  20 years.   Few housing developments have aroused local passions as much as Ahmanson Ranch, nestled beneath the Santa Monica Mountains just over the Ventura County line from Los Angeles County. The developer has already dedicated about 10,000 acres of surrounding undeveloped land for conservation. But the celebrities now are warning Seattle-based Washington Mutual that if it doesn't make some kind of deal to conserve all 2,800 acres of the land in question, it faces the prospect of a consumer backlash against its sizable business in Southern California.   "At the end of the day, Washington Mutual is going to realize that bad PR is not good for them," says Mr. Reiner, star of the 1970s sitcom "All in the Family."   Washington Mutual officials call the threat of customer reprisal "rhetoric or hyperbole" that is unlikely to happen. And the company has gotten other big names to endorse its project, most notably former Housing and Urban Development Secretary Henry Cisneros and former Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt. Mr. Babbitt, who was known for favoring environmental policies in his tenure under President Clinton, calls the proposed Ahmanson Ranch one of the more progressive urban communities in the country. Even before the new species were found, the developer agreed to plant 5,600 new oaks and capture most storm runoff to help safeguard creeks that lead to the beaches of Malibu downstream.   "If you are going to have development, this is as good as it gets," says Mr. Babbitt, who has been retained by Washington Mutual as an adviser on the project.   The development, first proposed in 1986 by its former owner, Home Savings of America, which was acquired along with the ranch in 1998 by Washington Mutual, has undergone an exceptional environmental scrutiny even by California's famously rigorous standards. As condition of granting an initial environmental impact report in 1992, for instance, Ventura County ordered the developer to turn over the 10,000 acres of other local wild lands for conservation. The developer did so by assembling several tracts of land, including the nearby Runkle Ranch and Corral Canyon from comedian Bob Hope for $30 million.   Still, many opponents argue that the project would pour thousands of additional cars onto the already clogged Ventura Freeway, a major commuter route into downtown Los Angeles, as well as dump as much as 840 tons more pollution per year into the smoggy air. "Urban sprawl is not something we need any more of," says Mary Wiesbrock, a resident of the nearby city of Agoura Hills.   The developer has countered that the project would be designed with most homes clustered around a village center, to reduce the need for driving, and that many residents could work at office complexes in the San Fernando Valley, where they could commute via back roads instead of the freeway. "This area is so crowded we actually need more than one Ahmanson Ranch," says Guy Gniadek, president of Washington Mutual's Ahmanson Land Co. unit, as he stands atop a knoll of the ranch overlooking a sea of homes and businesses stretching east across the San Fernando Valley.   Washington Mutual has spent the past two years formulating ways to protect the spineflower and frogs. For example, the company has proposed keeping homes off one hillside so as to set up a 330-acre spineflower preserve. The red-legged frogs would be protected, in part, by the company redesigning its storm flow canals to bypass their ponds. Pending a final decision on its plans, Washington Mutual is already paying for frog research on its own. The amount hasn't been disclosed. "I am sometimes embarrassed by what I ask them to do, but they do it," says Lenora Kirby, director of an independent frog-monitoring institute which the county required the developer to help fund.   The opponents remain unimpressed. They have attacked Ventura County's new supplement to the environmental report as inadequate, in part because while it addresses the two new species it doesn't contain an updated traffic analysis. They demand a new one that does. They add that the mitigation efforts to protect the environment pale in comparison to the damage they say would be caused by so many homes being built on the ranch. The Rally to Save Ahmanson Ranch, which is an umbrella group for opponents, has gotten some scientists to criticize the development's environmental and preservation plans, while a number of state agencies have expressed similar concerns. So many elected officials oppose the project in both Ventura and Los Angeles counties that a final outcome is still uncertain.   The campaign gained more money, and star power, after one prominent resident of the San Fernando Valley, HBO Chairman Chris Albrecht, noticed the activists handing out leaflets against the development outside a local supermarket about three years ago. According to Mr. Reiner, Mr. Albrecht helped persuade him to join the campaign. Mr. Reiner and Mr. Sheen drew headlines when they participated in protests of the project at the time of a Washington Mutual shareholders meeting in April.   "This is just the wrong place," says Mr. Reiner, reclining in his trailer on the set of a new romance movie he is filming in the San Fernando Valley, "to put a development like this."
event_description: The proposed development includes plans to establish a preserve for the San Fernando Valley spineflower and protect California red-legged frogs.
