Title: U.S. News: Small Fish, Big Water Fight --- Severe Drought Fuels Dispute Over Protections for Critters Like the Silvery Minnow
Text: As water managers in the Southwest parcel out the scarce commodity to cities and farmers in a prolonged drought, environmentalists are threatening to sue them for not leaving enough for a tiny fish called the Rio Grande silvery minnow. WildEarth Guardians maintains that the minnow's numbers are dramatically shrinking because water users in Colorado and New Mexico are siphoning off too much water before it reaches the fish's main remaining habitat on the Rio Grande. The Santa Fe, N.M., nonprofit group, which focuses on nature preservation in the West, notified federal and state agencies earlier this year that it intends to sue them to ensure better conditions for the minnow, which has been listed as an endangered species since 1994. "Living in the Southwest, everyone knows that drought is imminent," says Jen Pelz, director of the group's wild rivers program. "There hasn't been any planning or foresight of how we make the system resilient for everyone, including the species." Farmers upstream say they don't have a drop to spare, pointing to their idled fields. They fear their crops will suffer even more if water curbs are put in place to protect the fish. The minnow dispute is one of many challenges federal and state officials face as they try to balance the legal rights of critters protected under the Endangered Species Act with the realities of the drought. For decades, the legislation has generated fights about the preservation of animals. A dispute in the 1970s about another small fish, the snail darter, resulted in the delay in construction of a dam in Tennessee. Last month, farmers in California protested a federal appeals court's decision upholding limits on water shipments to the drought-hit Central Valley to protect the delta smelt, a threatened fish under the Endangered Species Act. The limits were adopted after environmental groups sued federal authorities. California farmers are expected to idle 800,000 acres due to the drought and environmental restrictions, according to the California Farm Water Coalition, a trade group in Sacramento. In Texas, the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality is fighting a federal-court ruling that ordered the agency to allocate water for endangered whooping cranes. A group of conservationists and coastal municipalities filed suit, saying state water managers contributed to several bird deaths by not sending enough fresh water downstream. The Endangered Species Act, signed by President Richard Nixon in 1973, requires U.S. agencies to avoid spoiling ecosystems on which listed animals depend. Amid the drought, endangered-species advocates are using the law to ensure overdrawn river systems contain sufficient water for wildlife. "It is nearly the only tool that works in many parts of the West," said Reed Benson, a University of New Mexico professor specializing in water law and the environment. Such conflicts look certain to increase as the drought lingers. Many states don't require setting aside water for the environment, and in some places water rights exceed what rivers carry, meaning even human rights-holders aren't getting their share. "People are loath to lose money to protect some animal or plant they don't care about," said David Cassuto, a law professor at Pace University in New York. Some 70 miles from the Rio Grande's headwaters, upstream from the silvery minnow's habitat, Colorado farmers are feeling the drought. In the past few years, the river's flow has been about two thirds of its longtime average, according to the San Luis Valley Irrigation District. "We've done what we can do to survive without the water we're used to," said Randall Palmgren, a barley and potato farmer and president of the district's board. In February, WildEarth Guardians notified the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and U.S. Bureau of Reclamation it planned to sue them for failing to prevent damage to the silvery minnow's habitat. The group also notified the Colorado Department of Natural Resources the previous month, claiming its water distribution was harming the minnows. The Colorado natural-resources department and the two federal agencies declined to comment. Mike Hamman, a Bureau of Reclamation manager in Albuquerque, said his office has taken steps to protect the fish, including leasing water to increase downstream flows, but can only do so much due to the dry conditions. "We did everything we possibly could for the benefit of the species under the resource constraints," he said. Still, in the last three years there hasn't been sufficient water to trigger mass spawning. In October, service biologists found only three silvery minnows in a 120-mile stretch, down from thousands in previous years. Credit: By Ana Campoy
event_description: WildEarth Guardians intends to sue federal and state agencies for failing to protect the habitat of the Rio Grande silvery minnow.
setting: the Southwest during a prolonged drought, involving water management and endangered species protection
characters: WildEarth Guardians [Hero]; water managers in the Southwest [Villain]; Rio Grande silvery minnow [Victim]; farmers upstream [Victim]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and U.S. Bureau of Reclamation [Villain]
plot: WildEarth Guardians intends to sue federal and state agencies for failing to protect the habitat of the Rio Grande silvery minnow, which is suffering due to excessive water diversion by water managers for agricultural use, leading to a decline in the minnow's population.
moral: The text suggests that immediate action is needed to ensure sufficient water allocation for the endangered Rio Grande silvery minnow, advocating for better planning and resource management to balance human and ecological needs.

Title: U.S. News: Bush Seeks to Limit Species Law; Plan to Help Speed Some Development Draws Activists' Ire
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Bush administration is proposing to reduce the environmental reviews federal agencies must undertake as part of the Endangered Species Act. The proposal could speed up commercial development, but environmentalists said the changes could threaten the protection of wildlife. The draft rules also would attempt to limit the use of the Endangered Species Act as a tool for fighting climate change. They would state that agencies don't have to consult one another in cases where a project would have only an indirect effect on a threatened or endangered species. Bush administration officials described that aspect of the proposal as a way to prevent the act from becoming a "back door" for regulation of greenhouse-gas emissions. Interior Secretary Dirk Kempthorne cited such concerns in May when he announced his decision to list the polar bear as a threatened species under the act, following evidence that the loss of sea ice is threatening polar bears' Arctic habitat. The draft rules would be subject to a 30-day public comment period before being finalized by the Interior Department, giving the administration time to impose them before the fall election. Under current law, all federal agencies must consult the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service or the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration before undertaking any actions that could affect an endangered species. Such consultations occur thousands of times each year and often prompt complaints from home builders, highway developers and other groups that say the regulations unnecessarily delay certain projects. Administration officials said the proposed changes would limit the duration of such consultations and lend greater certainty to the process. The proposals would allow agencies to terminate consultation with the Fish and Wildlife Service if it doesn't act on a request within 60 days. Currently, the Fish and Wildlife Service must tell agencies within 30 days of getting a consultation request whether it needs more information, but it isn't required to give agencies a formal answer on the request within a set time period. Business groups complain that the consultation process can last months or even years. In announcing the proposed changes, Mr. Kempthorne described them as an attempt to respond to the frustrations of Fish and Wildlife Service officials, who he said often have been unable to prioritize as a result of the many consultation requests from other agencies. Administration critics assailed the proposed changes as an 11th-hour effort to undermine the Endangered Species Act. "What the administration is proposing to do is allow agencies to decide for themselves that their actions will not impact endangered species and thereby remove any opportunity for the experts at the [Fish and Wildlife Service] to review those determinations," said Bob Irvin, a senior vice president of the Washington-based Defenders of Wildlife.
event_description: The Bush administration is proposing to reduce environmental reviews required under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: Bush administration [Villain]; environmentalists [Hero]; polar bears [Victim]; Bob Irvin [Hero]
plot: The Bush administration proposes to reduce environmental reviews under the Endangered Species Act, which could speed up commercial development but threatens wildlife protection. Environmentalists and critics argue that this undermines the act and allows agencies to bypass necessary consultations, putting species like polar bears at risk.
moral: The proposed regulatory rollback threatens endangered species and undermines environmental protections; therefore, the administration should prioritize wildlife conservation and maintain rigorous environmental reviews.

Title: How a Little Lizard Gets Palm Springs To Do Its Bidding --- Developers Were Destroying Its Sandy Desert Habitat; It Could Shut Them Down
Text: COACHELLA VALLEY, Calif. -- Attorney Paul Selzer looks out over the desert here and suggests a worst-case scenario: roughly $19 billion in potential real-estate development stopped in its tracks. Jobs lost, companies ruined. All for the sake of a scrawny, seven-inch reptile. Leapin' lizards! Actually, the lizard in question is more apt to dive than to leap. "Uma inornata", better known as the Coachella Valley fringe-toed lizard, is a sand swimmer able to burrow into dunes in a flash. For a time, some people here were calling it "that damned lizard." The reason: the once-ubiquitous reptile, in decline as its habitat is gobbled up by development, is subject to the protection of the federal Endangered Species Act. And for a while, federal authorities, pressured by environmentalists, seemed likely to use that law to shut down development in the valley, which happens to be the location of Palm Springs, Rancho Mirage and other pricey resorts, until a way could be found to save the lizard and to give it a congenial place to live. These days, however, a lizard-rescue plan exists, and it has occasioned a fragile truce between developers and the lizard's protectors. Just a few weeks ago, even former President Gerald Ford, a resident of Rancho Mirage, patted "Uma" on the head at a lizard fund-raiser, lending his support to preservation efforts led by the Nature Conservancy, a national conservation group. The lizard's improved image is largely the result of public perception that a plan to create a 13,000-acre, $25 million preserve 12 miles south of Palm Springs on land once eyed by developers will succeed despite obstacles that had seemed as imposing as the desert itself. Developers, having helped give the lizard a secure home, then would be free to proceed with future projects of their own without the specter of federal intervention should they happen to inconvenience or even squash a lizard or two. Others here are beginning to look beyond the lizard to what may be the greatest virtue of the plan: the preservation of a giant parcel of pristine habitat that will become a kind of Central Park in a desert valley otherwise destined for development. But there are problems, among them the cost of creating what is said to be the most expensive single-species conservation project ever attempted. So far, about $3 million in land has been purchased -- about $1 million by the state of California and $2 million by the Nature Conservancy, whose headquarters is in Arlington, Va. The rest must be raised from sundry sources: roughly $10 million in federal money from the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and another $5 million from the federal Bureau of Land Management through the sale of BLM lands. Valley developers are being called upon to contribute about $7 million in so-called mitigation fees. The money, in the end, may be the easy part. Negotiations over preserve boundaries in the growth-minded valley were as ticklish as a Middle East peace plan, and although an agreement exists, it is tenuous. "What we have here, essentially, is a finely tuned balance of terror," says Steve McCormick, an official of the Nature Conservancy's California office. Developers at the moment are lizard lovers because under the truce, they can continue to build so long as they pay mitigation fees -- now, about $750 for each acre developed -- to help buy lands for the preserve. Environmentalists for their part feel that the current compromise plan is better than any deal the lizard is likely to get from a lawsuit. Time also is a factor, says Allen Muth, who heads a valley environmental coalition concerned about the lizard's future. As much as 70% of the lizard's habitat already has disappeared beneath golf courses, condominiums and subdivisions in a valley grown from only 12,000 residents in 1940 to about 220,000 today. That the fringe-toed lizard is on the Endangered Species List at all is largely the work of Wilbur Mayhew, a tenacious University of California at Riverside researcher who had trailed "Uma" for decades before he began to promote the endangered-species designation a dozen years ago. At first Mr. Mayhew marveled at the creature's adaptation to the valley's wind-blown environment, where dunes are formed by highly localized wind patterns that make them unique. The lizard's chiseled head, sharp jaw and superior neck muscles enable it to make headlong plunges into these dunes in order to escape predators, Mr. Mayhew says. The lizard, once underground, also has adjustable nostril openings that let it breathe the air between sand grains without inhaling sand. In dunes outside the Coachella Valley, where grains of sand are either bigger or smaller and layered differently, the lizard can't survive. So in the early 1970s, when surveys began to indicate a rapid decline in the lizard population, Mr. Mayhew concluded that the animal's future lay in habitat conservation. The lizard was something of an entering wedge: Saving its habitat also would ensure the survival of scores of other animals and plants dependent on the same desert ecosystem. "Listing the lizard was a tool to get at the habitat," Mr. Mayhew says. Several other Coachella Valley species -- including a pugnacious-looking bug called the desert cockroach -- were also getting scarce. "But we settled on the lizard," recalls Mr. Muth, the environmentalist. "Politically, an endangered cockroach would have been hard to sell." Ironically, in 1980, when the lizard finally made the official Endangered Species List, few valley developers thought that event meant much. Just a few thousand lizards remained; sightings were infrequent. Conventional wisdom hereabouts held that developers were exempt from the act if they could show that there were no lizards on their property. Should any lizards be found, developers envisioned modest on-site "mitigation" -- perhaps they would leave a small part of their land undisturbed. But friends of the lizards threatened a showdown over a clause in the Endangered Species Act that prohibits killing or harassing an endangered species even in the course of a lawful act -- during the development of property, for instance. They also contended that the lizard's habitat is its historical range -- the entire valley, not just the desert pockets where it roams today. The matter seemed bound for court until the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, which administers the Endangered Species Act, suggested a compromise. The service would use recent act amendments to permit developers to "incidentally take" lizards in the course of their projects. But permits would be issued only if the lizard's future were assured by a valley-wide habitat plan, says Gail Kobetich of the agency's California office. The most logical plan, the service concluded, was Mr. Mayhew's old idea of a preserve. It might work if all sides were willing to contribute, Mr. Kobetich told the various factions. The scheme got a boost from William Bone, the chairman of Sunrise Cos., a longtime valley resident and its largest real-estate developer. Mr. Bone's company had actually had a project stopped by the wildlife service on the ground that nothing had been done to protect the lizards thought to roam there. Stunned and angry at first, Mr. Bone decided to talk instead of sue. He sent his lawyer, Mr. Selzer, on a mission to Mr. Muth, the lizard's chief spokesman, to see what could be worked out. Their meeting, both men concede, was as amiable as a scorpion convention, but it was a prelude to compromise. At about the same time, the Nature Conservancy, which has experience in pitching conservation to big business, got involved. The conservancy, whose specialty is acquiring endangered habitat, had come to the valley seeking about 2,000 acres of rare desert palm oases. The land it was interested in happened to lie at the heart of a likely lizard preserve. Reluctant at first, the conservancy agreed to take on the more ambitious preserve project when it appeared that a valley-wide coalition was possible. Today, the project has the smell of success. Many who once cursed the lizard now amuse outsiders with tales of its sand-swimming prowess. Lizard fund-raising parties are all the rage in the valley. Membership in the Lizard Club -- an informal gathering of those shepherding the project along -- confers status hereabouts. "People even call me Mr. Lizard," says Mr. Selzer, who, as a member of the club, has been a useful go-between with developers. "I don't even mind," he says. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A plan to create a preserve for the Coachella Valley fringe-toed lizard to ensure its habitat is protected while allowing for continued development.

Title: Environment: Green groups enter a dry season as movement matures
Text: After years of fighting to save whales and spotted owls, the nation's big environmental groups are in agony about another dwindling species -- their supporters.    Consider Elizabeth Mahone.    About a year ago, the 27-year-old organic farmer from Westmoreland, N.H., dropped her memberships in five groups, including the Sierra Club and the National Audubon Society. That's surprising, since she recycles her trash and has a master's in environmental studies. She didn't like the endless pleas for money.    "It felt like getting hit by buckshot," she says.    James Frederick, an art gallery owner from Pittsburgh, now finds other issues more pressing. "I'm putting a higher priority these days on things I see in my community, like the homeless or hungry people on the street," he says.    For environmental groups, such reversals have been devastating. The Sierra Club has racked up deficits of nearly $3 million since 1990; its membership has dropped to 500,000 from just under 630,000 in 1990, according to its executive director. The Wilderness Society shrunk 31% over the period, while the National Audubon Society is laying off workers and trimming programs after recording a $1.7 million deficit in 1993. Greenpeace saw membership plunge by 800,000 since it peaked at 2.5 million in 1990.    "It's clear the environmental movement has lost some of its momentum over recent years," says Tom McGuire, membership director for the World Wildlife Fund, one of the few that isn't shrinking.    And a rebirth isn't likely soon. Many former backers say green awareness is integrated into their lives, and other issues such as crime and health care now take precedence. Most say they're still vigilant about the environment and would balk if businesses took the withering of the movement to mean they can be careless about their pollution.    Part of the problem is money. High-paid white-collar workers, traditionally big backers of the groups, were hit hard by the last recession. Donations never snapped back as most had expected.    But the decline was hastened by what green groups dub "the Gore effect" after Vice President Al Gore, a dedicated environmentalist. "The basic theory is that with Gore and Clinton in power, the environmental shtick is being handled," so people are free to worry about other things, says Susan Carlson, a Washington-based communications consultant and former National Audubon Society staffer.    It doesn't seem to matter that nearly every major environmental initiative, from grazing reform to updating the Superfund toxic-waste cleanup law, has failed to win government approval. Most people figure the groups could have done little about this anyway.    Just having a government that talks favorably about green issues is a huge departure from the past. For example, James Watt, the interior secretary appointed by Ronald Reagan, helped galvanize support for environmental causes by advocating such actions as logging on public lands.    Indeed, green groups view the 1980s as a golden era. The World Wildlife Fund saw membership explode from 172,000 in 1985 to one million in 1991. Many groups moved into tony offices and spent heavily on expanded staffs, lobbying and promotional materials. It also didn't hurt that the decade saw a series of environmental calamities, including the 1989 Exxon Valdez oil spill.    The movement's boom in the 1980s was a natural part of its maturation. It had scored crucial victories -- including laws to protect air and water quality -- and was digging into the stickier problem of enforcement. The groups also got better at arguing their cases. With big budgets, they could invest in scientific and economic studies, which helped them win disputes.    But as they grew more powerful, they also tended to lose touch with members, says Michael Kellett, executive director of Restore: the North Woods, a small group based in Concord, Mass. And as they sought to attract as wide a base of support as possible, "it became harder and harder to tell what they stood for," he adds.    Consider the National Audubon Society. About five years ago, the group known as a haven for bird lovers recast itself as a broader-based environmental action group. That prompted many of the "birders" to quit, while failing to attract large numbers of new members.    Now many groups are going the opposite way. The Sierra Club recently announced it is narrowing its focus to two subjects -- protecting "wild lands" and preventing pollution -- rather than embracing the full panoply of green concerns.    But what most rankles some members is the way the groups spend money. Cindy Gabriel was shocked to learn that Jay Hair, the president of the National Wildlife Federation, makes about $232,000 a year. "I'll probably drop that membership," says the 39-year-old Ms. Gabriel, who recently lost her job at Wang Laboratories.    For their part, the groups say they are cutting costs. Carl Pope, executive director of the Sierra Club, hopes to recruit 75,000 members over the next year and is considering having activists go door to door. Among other things, the club has collapsed what were formerly 63 national committees into six; layoffs for some of the group's 350 employees are inevitable.    Even Greenpeace is moving to run more like an efficient business -- setting budgets based on expected income, for instance, rather than making plans for spending and then trying to raise the money. It hopes to rebuild membership to two million.    But winning back supporters will be tough. Environmental issues have become more complex and, in the process, less likely to evoke strong emotions. "The fact is the Endangered Species Act or Superfund just don't stir the heart and soul the way classic conservation issues such as creating new parks do," says Mr. Kellett of Restore: the North Woods.    Moreover, many people feel that big groups are poorly suited to deal with the problems. Cindy Hill, a 30-year-old lawyer from Middlebury, Vt., was once on the board of her local Sierra Club. But when a debate arose four years ago over endorsing hunting in a local preserve, the board split, riling Ms. Hill and other hunting opponents. Big groups have "become too eager to compromise," says Ms. Hill, who quit the club and joined local groups.    Others such as Ms. Mahone, the organic farmer, contend that fund-raising tactics counter some of the very issues green groups are supposed to champion. She objects to the wads of paper used in mass mailings, and says it dawned on her about a year ago that the letters were aimed at "manipulating, not informing" her.    Yet the fact that many groups are in trouble may help them with some former members. Richard Keeble quit the Sierra Club and National Audubon Society two years ago, after repeatedly asking them to stop sending tons of mail.    "I told them to stop trying to proselytize the converted," says Mr. Keeble, 60, who owns a travel agency in Miami. "But since I've heard they're having troubles," he adds, "I've decided to go back in. I may be annoyed, but somebody needs to be doing what these groups do."  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Discussion of the declining support for environmental groups and the challenges they face in advocating for the Endangered Species Act.

Title: An Endangered Species Olive Branch
Text: It's always a small relief when an environmentalist notes flaws in the Endangered Species Act -- or at least allows it can be improved. I applaud Timothy Male for rising above the cant of other environmentalists ("A Green Olive Branch on Endangered Species," op-ed, Jan. 17). However, we need a more exact statement of the problem. The ESA leaves rights to property and species up to anyone's guess and, therefore, to no one's satisfaction. The Supreme Court doesn't award takings claims for part of a property value, and the court has a similarly all-or-nothing standard for "take" of a species (the word used for a violation of ESA protections). Accordingly, when the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service acts, it takes only margins of property value while also allowing only margins of harm to species. The FWS describes but doesn't measure these damages in its rulings, which are professionally informed but ultimately subjective documents called "Biological Opinions." All concerned feel taken without justice, so they routinely sue to assert their own judgments. The Interior Department inspector general concluded that lawsuits "are driving nearly everything [FWS] does in the ESA arena." There has been no political solution because when rights are at risk, compromise sounds like a crumbling foundation of principle, not a stroke of statesmanship. An old idea can break new ground again: results. Before ESA, starting in the 1930s, wildlife conservation produced results. Sportsmen and sporting-equipment industries joined with government to restore deer, elk and other then-depleted wildlife. This worked politically because it added -- instead of taking -- value. It worked in policy because money improved field work instead of sharpening legal briefs. Something like it can work today. Greg Schildwachter Arlington, Va. --- According to Mr. Male, the goal of the ESA when it was passed a half-century ago was "to protect America's special animals and plants no matter the cost." Stop right there: If "no matter the cost" is the ESA's marching order -- and only in Washington could that be possible -- reasonable people would argue for repeal and replace, not repair and expand. Mr. Male is a former vice president at Defenders of Wildlife, an organization that assigns top priority to securing government initiatives, policies and laws "for dramatically expanding the amount of high-priority habitat managed for ecological integrity." He also worked at the Environmental Defense Fund, an organization that thrives on nuisance litigation. It seems that many in the movement now sense limits to lawsuit effectiveness, so executive orders and agency rules have re-emerged as highest priorities. Mr. Male's final goal is admirable: a new law that yields more recovery species, greater regulatory predictability for businesses and smart ways to lower costs. By "lower costs" he must be calculating new costs relative to those that would be incurred with endless litigation and harassment, incomprehensible and constantly shifting regulations and interminable delays. On an absolute basis, however, costs of habitat protections advocated by Mr. Male would be more burdensome for businesses and private landowners who lack Washington's luxury of proceeding no matter the cost. Kyle Donovan Knoxville, Tenn.
event_description: Discussion of a new law aimed at improving species recovery and regulatory predictability.
setting: legislative chamber or committee debate
characters: Timothy Male [Hero]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; businesses and private landowners [Victim]
plot: Timothy Male acknowledges flaws in the Endangered Species Act and advocates for a new law that improves species recovery and regulatory predictability. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's actions lead to dissatisfaction among property owners and species due to subjective rulings and legal challenges. This dissatisfaction results in lawsuits and a lack of political solutions, as stakeholders feel unjustly treated.
moral: The Endangered Species Act needs reform to balance species protection with the rights of property owners, advocating for a new law that promotes recovery and predictability while reducing litigation costs.

Title: Public Lands, Private Endeavors --- As Administration Rolls Back Safeguards of Wilderness, Preservationists Cry Foul
Text: INDIAN PASS, Calif. -- The desert here is so pristine that artifacts from an ancient tribe lie untouched in the drifting sand, a fact former Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt cited when he denied a permit for a huge gold-mining operation in the area during the waning days of the Clinton administration.   But the sanctity of this place is under renewed assault. President Bush's interior secretary, Gale Norton, reversed Mr. Babbitt's denial, clearing the way for Glamis Gold Ltd. of Reno, Nev., to dig a 1,600-acre open-pit mine that critics say would leave a gaping hole in the ground and skyscraper-tall mounds of toxic mine tailings -- marring one of the few American landscapes that is still largely untouched by modern man.   Leaders of the 3,000-member Native American Quechan tribe, whose ancient foot trails, pottery shards and sacred rock sculptures dot the area, are aghast. "The Bush administration is laughing at us," says tribal president Mike Jackson Sr. "They're saying, `We don't give a damn about your history.'"   Administration officials and their supporters deny they are weakening environmental protections. They say they are merely restoring balance after years of unfair throttling of logging, ranching and mining interests. In any case, Ms. Norton's actions are part of a series of environmental rollbacks that the administration, using its executive powers, has made through this year across the vast public lands of the American West.   Just this week, on the day after Christmas, the U.S. Forest Service gave the go-ahead to another mining proposal long opposed by environmentalists, the Rock Creek mine project in the Cabinet Mountains Wilderness Area of the Kootenai National Forest in northwest Montana.   The Rock Creek mine, sponsored by Sterling Mining Co. of Veradale, Wash., would extract copper and silver ore from shafts dug deep into the Cabinet mountain range. Local environmentalists vehemently oppose the mine, as do many area businesses tied to tourism, fearing it will pollute streams and lakes, harm endangered species and possibly cause parts of the wilderness area to collapse.   But officials with the Kootenai National Forest, which oversees the wilderness area, say their hands are tied in part by the federal Mining Law of 1872, which makes it difficult for federal agencies to thwart a properly filed mining claim on U.S.-owned land. At the close of the Clinton administration, Mr. Babbitt amended the hard-rock mining rules to empower federal officials -- for the first time in 130 years -- to reject mining proposals deemed to pose excessive environmental risks. But his successor, Ms. Norton, rescinded the historic revision this fall, restoring the 1872 law.   Opponents of the Rock Creek mine say they will appeal the Forest Service's decision to federal court if higher officials of the agency fail to rescind the action by local forest managers. Many environmentalists complain they can't get a fair hearing from Republicans in Washington, who are beholden, they claim, to big donors from the mining, timber and oil industries. In the 2000 campaign, Mr. Bush and the Republican Party received $3.5 million from the coal industry, while hard-rock mining companies pitched in $5.8 million in contributions to Mr. Bush and his party, according to the Center for Public Integrity in Washington.   "It's real obvious what the game is: to side with industry and blow everybody else out of the water," says Gloria Flora, a former U.S. Forest Service supervisor who now heads a conservation group in Montana.   White House spokeswoman Claire Buchan disputes that. "The president is committed to promoting a clean environment, and to give local communities input and flexibility to ensure the environmental standards work for their community," she says.   But "flexibility," on the ground, has often meant reopening public land for exploitation. In Oregon's coastal mountains, for example, crews recently have been busy felling giant trees on U.S. Bureau of Land Management property that had been protected under a 1998 listing of the region's coho salmon as threatened under the Endangered Species Act. The loggers went to work in September, after a U.S. district judge in Eugene, Ore., ordered the coho delisted, in a lawsuit filed by fishing groups. Earlier this month, the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals in San Francisco ordered the coho put back on the endangered list, but not before dozens of stately Douglas fir trees, some centuries old, came down.   Environmentalists accuse the Bush administration of helping friends in the timber industry by not filing its own appeal of the delisting decision, and then trying to block Earthjustice and other environmental groups that did manage to intervene on the government's behalf.   "The timber should never have been cut," says Francis Eatherington, a 52-year-old environmental activist who lives near the fresh clearcuts in Oregon's coastal mountains. "It's heartbreaking."   A BLM spokesman says the agency wasn't trying to help the industry, and that any logging it authorizes must follow a strict environmental review.   The administration also failed to appeal another U.S. district judge's ruling last May that blocked implementation of President Clinton's order barring road construction on about 60 million acres of national forest. Environmental groups had to intervene on the federal government's behalf then, too, appealing the ruling by Judge Edward Lodge in Boise, Idaho, to the Ninth Circuit. But, with an appeals ruling still pending, officials of Alaska's Tongass National Forest have gone ahead with timber-sale plans in several roadless areas anyway, threatening one of the world's last preserves of giant, old-growth spruce and hemlock trees.   "This is absolutely the opposite of what the [Clinton] roadless rule intended," says John Schoen, a National Audubon Society biologist in Anchorage.   Tongass officials say they are resuming a permitting process that had already begun before the roadless rule was signed. It is up to Mr. Bush, they say, to decide whether the Tongass area should remain in the plan or not.   Here in the Southern California desert, the gold-mine controversy flared up in October, when the Interior Department's new top lawyer issued an opinion that there was no legal justification behind Mr. Babbitt's denial. That led to Ms. Norton's reversal a few weeks later. She has yet to make a final decision on a permit for the mine, but members of the Quechan tribe are preparing for another day in court.   "You wish you didn't have to refight this battle," says tribe lawyer Courtney Ann Coyle, "but it looks like we're going to have to."   (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: Article Was Sanctified By Overload of Green" -- WSJ Jan. 11, 2002)  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A U.S. district judge ordered the coho salmon to be delisted from the endangered species list.
setting: federal court case regarding environmental regulations and mining permits
characters: Bruce Babbitt [Hero]; Gale Norton [Villain]; Quechan tribe [Victim]; environmentalists [Victim]; timber industry [Villain]; Bush administration [Villain]
plot: Bruce Babbitt denied a mining permit to protect the desert area, but Gale Norton reversed this decision, allowing Glamis Gold Ltd. to proceed with mining, which critics argue will harm the environment and the Quechan tribe's heritage. Environmentalists and the Quechan tribe oppose the mining operations, fearing destruction of their land and resources.
moral: The text suggests that the problem is the Bush administration's rollback of environmental protections, which threatens sacred lands and ecosystems. It advocates for the restoration of these protections to safeguard the environment and respect the rights of the Quechan tribe.

Title: Bush Promises to Give Economic Costs Greater Weight in Environmental Law
Text: President Bush journeyed to the Pacific Northwest to promise help for forest-product industries, with Bill Clinton close on his heels to accuse him of not doing enough.    The theme of the day was the balance between the economy and the environment, with each candidate saying he was right and the other wrong.    The president promised to seek changes in laws protecting endangered species to give more weight to the economic consequences. "It's time to make people more important than owls," he said.    Speaking at a lumberyard in Colville, Wash. -- in an area hit hard by the recession and by judicial mandates to halt timber cutting to protect the threatened spotted owl -- Mr. Bush suggested a variety of legislative strikes aimed at what he called "environmental extremists."    He said he wouldn't sign a renewal of the Endangered Species Act unless it includes language giving greater weight to the adverse economic consequences of species protection. While that is an idle threat for now -- Congress has no plans to take up the measure this year -- Mr. Bush's aides hinted that he might veto legislation funding the agency that administers wildlife and other natural resources programs unless his views are taken into account.    Mr. Bush also told timber workers he would insist on raising the timber harvests on federal land, which have been sharply limited by lawsuits aimed at protecting the environment.    Mr. Bush's message was a sharp departure from the environmental image he tried to cultivate four years ago, when he traveled west to proclaim his desire to be the "environmental president." Although Mr. Bush initially won plaudits from environmentalists for his leadership in seeking renewal of the Clean Air Act, a number of those groups have since accused him of seeking to weaken the measure through regulation. And they have become disillusioned with his increasingly blunt call for economic considerations to guide environmental policy.    In Portland, Ore., Mr. Clinton accused the president of failing to help the troubled timber industry and said that if he were elected president he would aid the industry. He said he could improve the area's economy while upholding his commitment to protecting the environment. "You can be pro-growth and pro-environment," the Arkansas governor said.    "We've got to invest," Mr. Clinton said, especially on worker retraining. "We can do better."    Mr. Clinton finessed the details of how he would accomplish both economic growth and environmental protection. His aides instead pointed to his call for a Pacific Northwest forest summit of business, labor and government officials to hammer out a solution.    Mr. Clinton said he has a plan to help the Pacific Northwest and its staple industry. In addition to the summit, it includes: discouraging exports of raw logs; encouraging better forest management and lumber processing techniques; promoting exports of timber products; and creating tax incentives for the start-up of new companies and the purchase of manufacturing equipment.    Mr. Clinton also said the government ought to find jobs for people who lose work as a result of government policies.    Today, Mr. Bush will speak to a National Guard convention in Salt Lake City, and Mr. Clinton altered his schedule so he could address the meeting after Mr. Bush does. The Clinton camp wants the candidate to be in position to answer expected criticism of Mr. Clinton's draft avoidance.    Separately, the Bush campaign again rejected a series of scheduled debates with Mr. Clinton. Bush campaign manager Robert Teeter insisted that the president would debate only if the 1988 format -- two debates with a panel of reporters asking questions -- is adopted. The bipartisan presidential debates commission has proposed three presidential debates and one vice presidential debate with a single moderator to encourage a livelier and more direct exchange between the candidates.    Last night, Mr. Clinton said he would stick with the original plan and show up for the first scheduled debate next Tuesday, whether Mr. Bush does or not.  Credit: Staff Reporters of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Discussion of proposed changes to the Endangered Species Act to prioritize economic consequences.
setting: political context surrounding the presidential campaign and discussions on the Endangered Species Act
characters: President Bush [Villain]; Bill Clinton [Hero]; timber workers [Victim]; environmental extremists [Villain]; environmentalists [Victim]
plot: President Bush proposes changes to the Endangered Species Act to prioritize economic consequences, suggesting that people should be prioritized over owls, while Bill Clinton accuses him of not doing enough for the timber industry and offers a plan to balance economic growth with environmental protection.
moral: The problem is the conflict between economic needs and environmental protection; the text suggests that a balanced approach that considers both is necessary, advocating for policies that support the timber industry while also protecting the environment.

Title: House freshman is chosen to be Gopac's chairman
Text: WASHINGTON -- The political action committee that helped bring House Speaker Newt Gingrich to power chose a staunchly conservative House freshman to be its new general chairman.    Gopac plans to announce the selection of Republican Rep. John Shadegg of Phoenix today, people familiar with the choice said.    Mr. Gingrich earlier this year relinquished the chairmanship of Gopac, which he used for years to train Republican candidates and boost his own image. The group, which has been searching for a new chairman for months, is at the center of many complaints filed against the speaker with the House Ethics Committee by Democrats, who claim the PAC was improperly intertwined with a nonpartisan and tax-exempt educational foundation affiliated with the speaker. Mr. Gingrich denies any wrongdoing.    Mr. Shadegg's father, Stephen Shadegg, was a close adviser to Barry Goldwater. Rep. Shadegg has advocated dismantling the Endangered Species Act in favor of an incentive-based approach. He opposes raising the debt ceiling until the Clinton administration agrees to a seven-year balanced-budget plan.    As a fund-raiser -- his chief new responsibility -- he appears untested. According to a Common Cause study of contributions to House freshmen in their first six months in office, he raised $78,598 -- about $50,000 less than the average first-termer.
event_description: Rep. Shadegg has advocated dismantling the Endangered Species Act in favor of an incentive-based approach.
setting: political context surrounding the selection of a new chairman for Gopac and the advocacy for dismantling the Endangered Species Act
characters: Rep. John Shadegg [Hero]; House Democrats [Villain]
plot: Rep. John Shadegg is selected as the new chairman of Gopac and advocates for dismantling the Endangered Species Act, while House Democrats file complaints against House Speaker Newt Gingrich, claiming unethical behavior.
moral: The text suggests that dismantling the Endangered Species Act is a necessary step towards a more effective, incentive-based approach, despite opposition from House Democrats.

Title: Environment: A giant logger and a tiny bird test limits of conservation law
Text: About two years ago, some Pacific Lumber Co. employees spotted what looked like a tiny marbled murrelet -- a protected seabird that's snarling the company's logging efforts -- right in executive Thomas Herman's living room.    It turned out to be a picture, affixed to a dart board for a company party hosted by Mr. Herman, who is responsible for Pacific Lumber's logging operations. Employees spent the evening throwing darts at the target.    Mr. Herman says the episode didn't reflect "any hatred of the species." But critics wonder. And they cite it and other disclosures that have emerged from a closely watched case pending in San Francisco federal court that pits Pacific Lumber, the world's largest producer of redwood lumber, against advocates for the murrelet, which is under federal and state protection. At issue is whether Pacific Lumber may log 137 acres of company-owned, ancient forest in Owl Creek, one of the largest intact stands of privately owned virgin redwoods -- and some of the last marbled murrelet habitat, biologists say.    The case is a key legal test of how far regulators can go to enforce the federal Endangered Species Act on private land. Earlier this year, a federal appeals court in Washington, D.C., ruled in a case known as Sweet Home that, contrary to what was assumed, the government has very limited power to enforce restrictions on private property to protect wildlife under the act. Now loggers, miners and other landowners are trying to get the country's courts to apply the ruling in other conservation cases.    Property-rights advocates hail Sweet Home as blessed relief from government regulation, but environmentalists fear it could be a devastating blow to wildlife protection. "If Sweet Home becomes the standard, it would be extremely damaging to our ability to prevent extinctions," says Suellen Lowry, a lawyer for the Sierra Club Legal Defense Fund.    The Pacific Lumber case, expected to be decided this month by Judge Louis C. Bechtle, was filed before the Sweet Home decision. But Pacific Lumber is using it as its principal defense, and the case will be the first test of how courts will apply it in other situations.    The Owl Creek showdown dates to 1990, when Pacific Lumber first proposed logging in the grove. Objections from environmentalists and regulators stalled the plan, but eventually, the state approved it, with conditions. The main one was that Pacific Lumber cooperate with regulators to minimize harm to murrelets.    But over a weekend in June 1992, Pacific Lumber cut down dozens of ancient trees in Owl Creek before regulators found out and stopped it. State and federal regulators say Pacific Lumber then promised not to log there again until the two camps agreed on a murrelet-protection plan. Then Pacific Lumber cut more Owl Creek redwoods over Thanksgiving, when regulators were on vacation.    Pacific Lumber maintains it had every right to log in Owl Creek because it had fulfilled its agreement to "cooperate" with regulators and because its logging plan had been approved by the state. But regulators didn't see it that way, and furious environmentalists sued. They accuse Pacific Lumber of violating the Endangered Species Act by destroying murrelet habitat and seek to block logging in the grove. If logging is permitted, the bird "will be pushed even closer to the brink of extinction," says Mark P. Harris, an attorney for the plaintiffs.    In court, Pacific Lumber attorney Alson Kemp called such arguments "piddly." The company questions whether murrelets should be protected in California, in part because they're so uncommon. "So little is known about murrelets that the government can't say with any certainty whether logging would threaten them or not," Mr. Kemp contends.    Biologists didn't discover until 1974 that the murrelet nests only in coastal forests in California, Oregon, Washington and Alaska. Only 71 nests have been found. The bird flies at up to 60 miles per hour and only at night, making it hard to detect.    The Endangered Species Act prohibits "taking" of protected species, defined two ways: outright killing or "habitat modification" that so disturbs a species's territory that its population is likely to be harmed. The Sweet Home judges said that, on public land, regulators may prevent both types of take. On private land, the judges held, regulators can't restrict habitat modification unless it directly and immediately kills an endangered species -- even if the animal might later be harmed.    Pacific Lumber argues that the Sweet Home ruling means it can log its Owl Creek lands because it doesn't expect to kill any murrelets directly. Although biologists dispute that assertion, some private-property-law experts think the company has some strong legal points.    But its case may have been clouded by disclosures about some of its practices. Pacific Lumber's top logging manager, Ray Miller, conceded on the stand that its murrelet surveyors -- who are supposed to scour proposed logging lands for signs of the birds and report to regulators -- were under orders not to record certain evidence used to determine logging restrictions.    For example, surveyors are supposed to inform regulators if they hear a murrelet's distinctive "keer" call. Mr. Miller and several Pacific Lumber surveyors said the company decided, without consulting regulators, that it wouldn't count keer calls as murrelet evidence. Mr. Miller also testified that he once lost a report in which a company wildlife surveyor recorded having heard murrelet wing beats, which regulators consider prime evidence of the birds. In court, Mr. Miller said he replaced the report with one he filled out himself that didn't mention wing beats.    Mike Penny, a Pacific Lumber surveyor, testified that company managers recently "grilled" him when he reported murrelet sightings. He testified a supervisor warned him that "a lot of money was tied up" in Owl Creek and suggested that surveyors' jobs might be at stake if the sighting was reported. The report wasn't altered.    Mary Bullwinkel, a Pacific Lumber spokeswoman, says the company hasn't violated any laws in Owl Creek and defends its murrelet practices. She says keer calls weren't counted as murrelet evidence because the regulators' prescriptions for what constitutes such evidence aren't binding and that the company's murrelet experts considered the calls an unreliable indicator. She says Mr. Miller replaced the report because he had determined that the surveyor wasn't "absolutely, 100%" sure the sounds he detected were wing beats. And she says managers were merely striving to confirm what murrelet activity Mr. Penny had detected.    Whichever way Judge Bechtle rules, it will immediately affect Pacific Lumber, a unit of Maxxam Inc., which is controlled by Texas financier Charles Hurwitz. It is feuding with regulators over a logging plan for Allen Creek, an ancient redwood grove near Owl Creek. Regulators have pronounced Pacific Lumber's murrelet plan inadequate there, too.    (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: Very Expensive Bird Watching" -- WSJ Nov. 21, 1994)    941020-0117    YY94 MM11  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The case involves a legal challenge regarding logging in marbled murrelet habitat and the enforcement of the Endangered Species Act.
setting: a federal court case in San Francisco regarding logging in marbled murrelet habitat
characters: Pacific Lumber Co. [Villain]; marbled murrelet [Victim]; environmentalists [Hero]; regulators [Hero]
plot: Pacific Lumber Co. is challenging the enforcement of the Endangered Species Act to log in marbled murrelet habitat, while environmentalists and regulators argue that logging will harm the species and seek to block it.
moral: The problem is that logging threatens the marbled murrelet's survival, and the text suggests that strict enforcement of the Endangered Species Act is necessary to protect the species from extinction.

Title: U.S. News: Emperor Penguins to Be Listed As Threatened Species by U.S.
Text: The emperor penguin will soon be considered a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service said. The federal agency said Tuesday it had finalized protections for the species, following a proposal last year. It cited the effects of a warming climate on the sea-ice habitat in Antarctica, where emperor penguins spend the majority of their lives, as the "primary threat to the penguin." "Climate change is having a profound impact on species around the world and addressing it is a priority for the Administration," Fish and Wildlife Service Director Martha Williams said in a statement. "The listing of the emperor penguin serves as an alarm bell but also a call to action." Emperor penguins are considered a bellwether of any future climate changes in Antarctica because their icy habitat is so sensitive to rising temperatures. The current emperor penguin population, estimated to be around 625,000-650,000 individual birds or 270,000-280,000 breeding pairs, appears stable at the moment, according to Fish and Wildlife Service officials. But, they added, "the species is in danger of extinction in the foreseeable future in a significant portion of its range." By 2050, the population is estimated to decrease by 26% to 47%, depending on the level of carbon emissions, Fish and Wildlife Service officials said. "While this estimated decline is concerning, listing the emperor penguin as threatened under the ESA comes while there is still time to prevent the species from extinction," the Fish and Wildlife Service said. The agency's final rule listing the species as threatened will publish Wednesday and go into effect 30 days later. The Endangered Species Act of 1973 can provide benefits to an animal even if it doesn't exist within U.S. borders. The act will usually prohibit the import or export of the animal, as well as its use in interstate commerce and foreign commerce. Listing a species can create increased awareness of the threatened species and provide limited financial assistance to help conservation efforts for species listed in foreign countries. The emperor penguin is the tallest and heaviest of all living penguin species, with adults that can weigh up to 88 pounds. The flightless bird relies on sea ice to forage for food, form breeding colonies and avoid predators. Credit: By Talal Ansari
event_description: The emperor penguin will soon be considered a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; emperor penguin [Victim]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service finalizes protections for the emperor penguin, citing climate change as the primary threat to its survival, which could lead to a significant population decline by 2050.
moral: The problem of climate change threatening the emperor penguin necessitates immediate action to prevent its extinction, highlighting the importance of the Endangered Species Act.

Title: Washington Tries Board System To Handle Water-Rights Issues
Text: When James Holmes, owner of Ceil Du Cheval Vineyard in West Richland, Wash., decided to plant more of his prize-winning merlot grapes and irrigate them with water from a well on the property, he needed permission. So he asked the Benton County Water Conservancy Board for a change in his water-rights permit. Mr. Holmes applied for authorization to irrigate 20 additional acres, pledging to use a new drip method that wouldn't draw any more from the well than the vineyard's outdated sprinkler system had. He received the go-ahead within six months. If Ceil Du Cheval (French for horse heaven) had pressed its case directly with the Washington Department of Ecology, as Mr. Holmes initially planned, rather than through the one-year-old conservancy board, he believes he would still be waiting for an answer. "It would have taken forever," he says. "I'd be dead before it happened." Even department officials don't argue with his assessment. With demand for water growing and supply increasingly restricted by the Endangered Species Act -- and with the agency chronically under-funded -- the queue of candidates for permit adjustments and new water rights is 6,800 applicants long. The notorious backlog prompted the Washington Legislature in 1997 to pass a law authorizing a network of conservancy boards, which lawmakers reckoned would ease the gridlock and give locals some say over the allocation of natural resources. The emergence of the boards -- along with individuals who act as "water brokers" in the state -- underscores a broader trend in water rights: Residents and developers are increasingly seeking alternatives to finding water since getting new rights directly from the state is next to impossible. And it's unlikely to become any easier; with Washington's population projected to increase by about 14% over the next decade, it will no doubt give rise to an ever-greater number of disputes over the water supply. Later this week, on Dec. 18, the Department of Ecology is scheduled to implement the final regulations necessary to set up three-member boards in any of the 39 counties in the state that petitions for one. And by the middle of next year, at least a dozen are expected to be in business. "The future lies in making better use of existing water, because there is less and less opportunity to establish new rights," says Fred Rajala, a water-resource specialist with the ecology agency in Olympia. Not everyone, however, is enthusiastic. Some environmentalists are unhappy with what they view as the inexperience of board members, who are appointed by county commissioners. "We think the boards are packed with a bunch of lay people who have little to no experience with water management and are riddled with conflicts of interest," says Rob Caldwell, director of the Seattle-based Center for Environmental Law & Policy. Mr. Caldwell is worried that as the boards proliferate, they'll be used by developers to bypass stricter state environmental standards. That, he says, violates the Legislature's intent -- and could put at risk the flows of rivers and streams that support threatened and endangered species of fish. Mr. Caldwell says the center believes the Department of Ecology will be overstepping its authority if it implements regulations regarding the boards this week. Tribal leaders also worry about the conservancy boards. Steve Suagee, a lawyer for the Colville Tribes in eastern Washington, says the boards pose "major problems." Mr. Suago says while he supports local control in theory, he is concerned that board members aren't familiar with federal laws that deal with tribal rights and so might "disregard" those legal protections. Rep. Gary Chandler, a Moses Lake Republican who sponsored the 1997 legislation and is now co-chairman of the House Agriculture and Ecology Committee, says he isn't surprised by the objections. "When haven't the environmentalists filed some suit to block something like this?" he says. But when the boards are finally up and running, Rep. Chandler says, "they'll give a lot of relief to the department of ecology. They should have gone forward two years ago, when the bill passed." In Benton County, board members -- Daryll Olsen, an irrigation consultant with a doctorate in applied-energy studies; John Jaksch, who has a doctorate in resource economics; and Matt Berg, an agricultural economist -- take offense at the center's contentions. "Water-conservancy boards are a reflection of legislation that is the most progressive when it comes to water marketing in the western United States," says Mr. Olsen, the board's chairman. He adds: "If the environmentalists had a good case, they would have appealed our decisions, and they didn't." The conservancy-board experiment is new. Only five are fully operational, and together they have so far made just seven water-rights decisions, which must be approved by the Department of Ecology. Decisions can be appealed to the Pollution Control Hearings Board by any interested party. Henry Johnson, a former farmer and property manager who is chairman of the Franklin County Water Conservancy Board in Pasco, concedes that the required training for board members -- 32 hours of schooling by DOE staffers on issues of law and hydrology -- isn't much. "I certainly wouldn't characterize it as rigorous," Mr. Johnson says. But he notes that most members have experience in the field. And, he adds, when compared with the alternative, the conservancy-board system will serve a critical need given the impasse at the state level. "I think that they're going to gain prominence quickly," he says. "When people become familiar with what we're empowered to do and the relative speed with which we can act compared to DOE, people will find a lot of relief in dealing with us." The Department of Ecology has been strapped for cash for since 1994 when the Legislature didn't approve continued funding for the water-resources division, resulting in a 60% budget cut and a total loss of 46 positions. The Legislature approved four new staff positions for ecology in 1999. As the agency has struggled to keep up with applications, the implementation of the Endangered Species Act has begun to curb government's ability to meet the public's demand for water. Fifteen runs of salmon are listed as either endangered or threatened in Washington, along with many other species of fish and wildlife. Federally mandated preservation plans only now being devised -- and legal action by environmentalists -- are certain to make the business of apportioning water resources even more difficult. For that reason, developers and business interests are generally supportive of the conservancy-board system. "It's the only glimmer of hope we have to get through this horrible backlog," says Jody Slavik, legal counsel for the Building Industry Association of Washington in Olympia. "He who controls the water controls everything, particularly growth in our state. That's why the environmentalists are against this. ...They're short-sighted and want to stop everything." The availability of water is definitely a key selling point for real-estate agents, according to Tim Reierson, a former water-rights investigator for the Department of Ecology and now a water consultant in Yakima. "I have my ears open all the time for potential water rights that can be marketed and clients looking for water rights," Mr. Reierson says. "The boards bring on an extra work force to deal with the changes that are out there." Joe Mentor, a Seattle lawyer who has brokered several water-rights sales, agrees, calling transfers "the wave of the future." Mr. Mentor and brokers like him can't change waters rights; only the Department of Ecology and the boards, and in some cases the courts, can do that. But private middlemen can -- and increasingly do -- act as go-betweens for rights-holders eager to sell and developers eager for water. Mr. Mentor, for example, recently purchased from seven farmers and other landowners enough rights to supply most of the water necessary for Trend West, a resort being built near Roslyn. Such transfers don't bother most environmentalists, since existing rights move from those who don't need them to those who do, making for an efficient distribution of a limited resource. What they object to is what happens in cases such as that of Ceil Du Cheval Vineyard, in which the Benton County board allowed Mr. Holmes's right to be altered without, the environmentalists complain, the proper analysis by trained technical personnel of its impact on fish. Locals board don't have paid staff. Moreover, says Mr. Caldwell of the law and policy center in Seattle, Mr. Holmes's needs weren't serious enough for him to enjoy such a swift and positive response. "If you get a water user that just wants to maximize water use, like spreading it further in the case of the winery, there's no direct benefit to society," Mr. Caldwell says. "No benefit isn't good enough to give them a priority to change their water right. They should stand in line with everyone else." Standing in line, of course, is what lawmakers said they wanted to help Washingtonians avoid when they passed the legislation sanctioning local boards. For his part, Mr. Holmes certainly disagrees with Mr. Caldwell's assessment. Mr. Holmes initially applied to the department for permission to modify his rights in early 1997, before the Benton County panel existed, and says he got nowhere. Now, he's a fan of conservancy boards. "When you change water from one place to another, it's a pretty simple thing," he says. "And there's only one question: Does it hurt anyone? It's a very local issue, and it should be decided on a one-page application." Evidence is abundant, most observers say, that the old way of doing water business in Washington simply doesn't work anymore. Just last week, citing its inability to win the water rights it needed, Seattle-based R.D. Merrill Co. threw in the towel after spending some $20 million on plans for Arrowleaf Resort and the Freestone Inn in the Methow Valley, a resort that was to include a golf course, a 100-room lodge, 565 single-family units, 20 miles of hiking trails and an equestrian center. With 40 applicants in the queue ahead of Merrill, and two runs of fish in the area protected by the Endangered Species Act, the company figured there was no end in sight. If there had been a conservancy board in Okanogan County, says Tom Bartholomew, Arrowleaf's project manager, "that absolutely could have helped. But there wasn't one." Mary Getchell, a spokeswoman for the department, says the chances Arrowleaf would have been granted new water rights were slim; developers are better off asking conservancy boards for transfers, or making deals through water brokers, she and others say. "There's just not a lot of water available," Ms. Getchell says. "The game is changing." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Discussion on the process of adjusting water rights permits in relation to the Endangered Species Act.
setting: NA
characters: NA
plot: NA
moral: NA

Title: The Morning Brief: How Long and How Far Will Oil Prices Decline?
Text: The Wall Street Journal Online The Morning Brief, a look at the day's biggest news, is emailed to subscribers by 7 a.m. every business day. Sign up for the e-mail here. Crude futures continue to slide even as war rages near a Caspian pipeline, leaving little doubt that the near-term direction of oil prices is down. But the longer-term outlook is less clear, and depends in part on whether we have permanently altered our energy-consuming habits. Yesterday, benchmark crude oil on the Nymex fell to as low as $112.72 a barrel before settling at $114.45 a barrel, down 75 cents. Since June 30, the price of oil has fallen 18%, The Wall Street Journal reports. The newspaper says the rapid decline reflects pessimism about demand from Europe and Japan as those two economies continue to soften. "Inside the commodity-trading pits and brokerage houses, the conversation isn't whether prices are pulling back in the near term, but how far and for how long," the Journal writes. Other factors contributing to lower oil prices are the stronger dollar, which makes commodities more expensive to purchase with other currencies, and diminished demand from China, the Los Angeles Times reports. In July, Chinese oil imports fell 7% to a seven-month low, according to Beijing, and the Times says that could reflect factory closures as the country tried to cut down on pollution ahead of the Olympics. Americans, meanwhile, are curbing what the Journal calls our "legendary appetite for energy" by buying smaller cars and more efficient appliances, driving less and more slowly, using less packaging, changing distribution networks, and choosing locally made products. Consumers stung by surges in oil and gas prices have resolved not to count on cheap energy any longer, analysts tell BusinessWeek. "The changes we're seeing are permanent," Fadel Gheit, senior oil analyst for Oppenheimer Holdings, tells the magazine. "People are not going to rush to the dealer to buy Hummers again. The energy shock was beneficial in the longer term to our psychology." Not all observers agree. Auto-data tracker Edmunds.com says that, as prices have fallen at the gas pump, interest in smaller vehicles is beginning to level off, and consideration of gas-electric hybrids is down 34% since June, according to the L.A. Times. Still, the Journal notes, lasting declines in oil prices are less likely than they once were, given the rapidly growing economies of China and India. Despite the recent decline in the market, prices remain above the prior inflation-adjusted peak of $106.15, set in April 1980. And this morning in its monthly oil-market report, the International Energy Agency said the tight balance of global supply and demand for oil is easing, but warned that conditions may again tighten in coming months due to healthy consumption in China and geopolitical factors, as Dow Jones Newswires reports. The recent decline in oil prices despite the conflict between oil- producer Russia and what the Journal calls the "oil crossroads of Georgia" reignited debate in some quarters about the extent to which speculation has played a role in recent oil-price volatility. "It speaks volumes about how this market was hijacked by investors who pushed the market up -- and now they are pushing it down," Peter Beutel, president of energy risk-management firm Cameron Hanover, tells the L.A. Times. Indeed, the Washington Post, citing Wall Street brokers and congressional investigators, reports that sovereign-wealth funds "are now among the biggest speculators in the trading of oil and other vital goods like corn and cotton in the United States." The paper writes that the activity is going undetected by the market's regulator, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, because the funds invest through brokers known as "swap dealers" who often operate on unregulated markets. And the Journal writes that noncommercial oil traders such as hedge funds have taken on an increasingly bearish view of oil -- perhaps in part because the credit crunch has made it more difficult to meet margin calls to support bullish positions. --- The Role of Oil in the Georgian Conflict. . . Oil, it seems, is one issue at the heart of the violent conflict between Russia and Georgia. In striking its neighbor hard in the ostensible tussle to control the breakaway Georgian province of South Ossetia, Moscow is signaling that it won't give up control of the oil- rich Caspian region, the Financial Times reports. "Georgia has scant energy resources of its own but hosts pipelines built by international oil majors to carry Caspian oil and gas to western markets," the newspaper writes. "The so-called east-west energy corridor across Azerbaijan and Georgia to Turkey, established with strong political backing from the U.S., has eroded Russia's stranglehold over energy exports from one of the world's few remaining untapped oil provinces." Analysts tell BusinessWeek that Moscow must walk a fine line between cementing its dominance of European energy supplies and not spooking Europeans into backing the construction of yet more non-Russian pipelines, but that any expansion of European or U.S. influence is unlikely since no Caspian leader will want to put his survival on the line by embracing a new Western project. Chris Ruppel, an energy analyst at Greenwich brokerage Execution, tells the magazine, "Russia has demonstrated that its military is a force to reckon with, that it can defeat a Western-trained force, and that the West and NATO will not act to intervene." It remains to be seen whether Europe is wholly without influence. This morning, just before French President Nicolas Sarkozy arrived at the Kremlin to negotiate a European Union-backed truce, Russian President Dmitri Medvedev took to the airwaves to announce that Russia would end its current military operation against Georgian forces because it has achieved its goals, as The Wall Street Journal reports. But Mr. Medvedev stopped short of saying Russia would withdraw its troops from Georgia, and even as his comments were broadcast, Agence France-Presse cited a Georgian official as saying the Russian air force had attacked the key Baku-Tblisi-Ceyhan pipeline. "We don't know yet whether it was damaged. It's a second attempt to bomb this pipeline since Aug. 10," Alexander Lomaia, secretary of Georgia's National Security Council, told AFP. --- . . . And the Conflict's Role in U.S. Policy The flow of oil and gas revenue is also at the heart of a "troubling strategic reality" which will confront either Republican Sen. John McCain or Democratic Sen. Barack Obama when November's election winner assumes the White House, as Wall Street Journal columnist Gerald F. Seib says. Just as funds from the sale of expensive oil have emboldened Moscow to defy the U.S. by attacking Georgia, "The rise of energy prices also is enriching other bad actors, creating a kind of globe-girdling string of well-heeled regional antagonists the U.S. can't ignore," Mr. Seib writes, citing Iran's push to build a nuclear program and bankroll militias like Hamas and Hezbollah, and Venezuela's antagonism toward America. The next president will need to build stronger ties to Mexico, Chile and Brazil to offset Venezuela's influence, Mr. Seib writes, and will want to cultivate ties with China to avoid having similar problems with Beijing that the U.S. will apparently have with Moscow. Leading up to November, the escalation in the Caspian "has put a new focus on the increasingly hard line that Senator John McCain has taken against Russia in recent years, with stances that have often gone well beyond those of the Bush administration and its focus on engagement," the New York Times writes. Sen. McCain has befriended Georgian President Mikheil Saakashvili and embraced the country's efforts to join NATO, and has called for excluding Russia from the Group of Eight leading industrialized nations. In contrast, Sen. Obama has made clear he favors more engagement with Russia, but, the Times notes, this week the two rivals found themselves holding more or less the same position, calling for a U.N. resolution on the violence, for a path to NATO membership for Georgia, and deployment of an international peacekeeping force. --- Also of Note. . . Financial Times: J.P. Morgan Chase provided evidence of more turbulence in financial markets, warning that stormy credit conditions had forced it to take a $1.5 billion write-down on mortgage-backed assets in July. In a regulatory filing, J.P. Morgan said since the beginning of July, trading conditions in the mortgage market "had substantially deteriorated." Wall Street Journal: Securities regulators are widening the list of Wall Street firms that are being told to fix the auction-rate securities mess. Morgan Stanley was the latest to act in response, saying it would buy back about $4.5 billion in securities it sold to retail clients, charities and small businesses. The action came after New York Attorney General Andrew Cuomo's office sent letters to Morgan Stanley, J.P. Morgan Chase and Wachovia, demanding they make their investors whole for auction-rate securities that were sold to them. Dow Jones Newswires: UBS said it will begin separating its troubled investment bank from its money-management arm for the wealthy, as mortgage losses continued to hammer the beleaguered Swiss bank in the second quarter. Bowing to shareholder pressure, the Zurich-based bank said its main units will be separated, backing away from its integrated model, but said it has no specific plans to sell its investment bank, the source of major losses. CNET: Advanced Micro Devices announced its most powerful graphics technology to date, going after Nvidia in the rarified -- and closely watched --enthusiast game segment. This also marks the current performance pinnacle of AMD's strategy to beat Nvidia at the high end by building comparatively smaller chips and then ganging them together for better performance. Associated Press: A roadside bomb hit a Pakistan air force truck in a northwestern city today, killing about 12 people as the military pounded insurgent positions in a nearby tribal region. Interior Ministry chief Rehman Malik said al Qaeda-linked militants were likely behind the attack. Separately, Northwest Pakistan lawmakers have passed a resolution against President Pervez Musharraf, the second major act in the ruling coalition's campaign to impeach and oust the embattled leader. Times-Picayune: Federal investigators collected documents from the shuttered New Orleans Affordable Homeownership Corp., the city- chartered and city-financed nonprofit that ran a home-remediation program in 2006 and 2007. The city nonprofit has been under scrutiny as news reports raised questions about whether its contractors billed taxpayers for work they didn't perform on blighted New Orleans homes. The remediation program, aimed at the poor and elderly, was touted as a salve to sagging neighborhoods by Mayor Ray Nagin in his 2007 budget address. Washington Post: The Bush administration proposed a regulatory overhaul of the Endangered Species Act to allow federal agencies to decide whether protected species would be imperiled by agency projects, eliminating the independent scientific reviews that have been required for more than three decades. The new rules, which will be subject to a 30-day per comment period, would use administrative powers to make broad changes in the law that Congress has resisted for years. CBS: Some people say you can't outrun time, but a new study has found that pounding the pavement might slow down the aging clock. Researchers from Stanford University say older runners have fewer disabilities and a long lifespan compared to their non-running peers.
