They tracked runners for more than 20 years and found elderly runners had a more active lifestyle and were less likely to die early. --- Quote of the Day "I train to win on my worst day," Aaron Peirsol told ESPN after taking a gold medal for the U.S. in the 100-meter backstroke this morning at the Water Cube in Beijing. "Honestly, I don't know what goes on in these other guys' heads. I know what I have to do to get my hand on the wall." --- Write to Elana Beiser at elana.beiser@wsj.com --- Some links in this column are to sites that require a subscription or registration.
event_description: The Bush administration proposed a regulatory overhaul of the Endangered Species Act to allow federal agencies to decide whether protected species would be imperiled by agency projects.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: Bush administration [Villain]; protected species [Victim]
plot: The Bush administration proposed a regulatory overhaul of the Endangered Species Act, allowing federal agencies to determine the impact of their projects on protected species, which could lead to harm for those species.
moral: The proposed changes to the Endangered Species Act threaten the survival of protected species, and there is a need to maintain independent scientific reviews to ensure their protection.

Title: U.S. News: Yellowstone Grizzlies to Lose Protections
Text: The Trump administration said it will removeendangered-species protections for the Yellowstone grizzly bear, a move that was initiated bythe Obama administration after the numbers of the West's largest land predator rebounded sharply over the past four decades. The decision, criticized by environmentalists, paves the way for grizzly-hunting to resume in some places after a 40-year ban. Grizzly bears in and around Yellowstone National Park had plummeted to as few as 136 by 1975, when they were listed as threatened under the Endangered Species Act. Because of aban on hunting and other protections, the number of grizzlies in a Greater Yellowstone ecosystem that includes parts of Idaho, Montana and Wyoming has increased more than fivefold to an estimated 700,according to the Interior Department. The federal government aimed to boost the population to 500 grizzlies. The animal can weigh up to 600 pounds. Interior Secretary Ryan Zinke, a former Montana congressman who announced the delistingThursday, hailed the recovery as one of the nation's greatest conservation success stories. "As a kid who grew up in Montana, I can tell you that this is a long time coming and very good news for many communities and advocates in the Yellowstone region," Mr. Zinke said in a statement. Environmental groups condemned the move and vowed to try to fight it in the courts. Lawsuits filed by environmentalists succeeded in blocking efforts by the Bush administration to delist the grizzly in 2007, when their numbers already were considered recovered by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. Environmentalists saygrizzlies have a slow reproduction rate and would be imperiled as a result of hunting and other dangers. Once the rule by the Fish and Wildlife Service takes effect laterthis year, states will regain their authority to resume trophy hunts that have been banned for 40 years. The grizzlies would remain protected from hunting in Yellowstone and Grand Teton national parks. "This premature decision to remove endangered-species protections could set grizzly recovery back by decades," said Michael Brune, executive director of the Sierra Club, in a statement. "The end result will be fewer bears restricted to an even smaller area," Mr. Brune said. Supporters of the delisting, though, say there are so many Yellowstone grizzlies that conflicts with humans have increased, as well as attacks on livestock. "Grizzly bears have met or exceeded recovery objectives since 2003 and have long warranted delisting," Wyoming Republican Gov. Matt Mead said in a statement. The Wyoming governor, among other Westerners, asked the Obama administration to resume the delisting process. In 2016, the Fish and Wildlife Service issued a draft notice to take the bears off the list, as states including Wyoming gave assurances they would continue to manage the grizzlies so their numbers remained healthy. The debate over grizzlies is reminiscent of the one over the gray wolf, another iconic predator that was reintroduced to Yellowstone in 1995 under endangered-species protection. But as the number of wolves exploded, they were delisted in both Idaho and Montana.   Credit: By Jim Carlton
event_description: The Trump administration announced the removal of endangered-species protections for the Yellowstone grizzly bear.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: Trump administration [Villain]; environmentalists [Hero]; grizzly bears [Victim]; Interior Secretary Ryan Zinke [Hero]; Michael Brune [Hero]; Wyoming Republican Gov. Matt Mead [Hero]
plot: The Trump administration announced the removal of endangered-species protections for the Yellowstone grizzly bear, a decision that allows hunting to resume after a long ban. Environmentalists criticized this move, arguing it could harm the grizzly population, which has only recently recovered. Supporters of the delisting claim that the bear population has exceeded recovery goals and that conflicts with humans have increased.
moral: The problem is the premature removal of protections for the grizzly bear, which could jeopardize its recovery. The text suggests that protections should remain in place to ensure the long-term survival of the species.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial): Burying the Future
Text: A federal judge has plopped another science and technology choice in the lap of the Clinton administration. As with the Von Roll incinerator debate in Ohio, this one concerns disposal, but it directly affects medicine and biotechnology, industries on which the new President can help the United States build a higher-wage economy. In a taste of what awaits other parts of the country, California's biomedical and surgical talent at work in the field of nuclear medicine is running up against a desperate scarcity of sites for discarding the remnants of low-level nuclear technology used in so many cutting-edge fields of research and practice. The situation became pressing this year when two Western U.S. dumps became off-limits to California shipments, forcing them to a remaining destination in South Carolina until that, too, becomes unavailable over the next couple of years. Governor Pete Wilson, fighting a many-front war to keep industry from leaving his reeling state, stepped up efforts to open a California site, as was promised years ago to the affected scientists and entrepreneurs, and as the federal government itself has demanded of it (and other states). He wanted to swap for a federally owned parcel in the Southern California desert, outside the desolate town of Needles. But the state Lands Commission, controlled by Mr. Wilson's partisan foes, blocked the exchange. So he cooked up a deal with departing Interior Secretary Manuel Lujan to sell the site for a sum to be provided by U.S. Ecology, the company that would operate the landfill. The usual environmentalist and NIMBY howls have accompanied the whole process, with objections centering on the potential for radioactive leakage through an aquifer to the Colorado River about 13 miles away. It looked, however, as if the project at least would be moved a big step forward. Then came Tuesday's ruling by District Judge Marilyn Patel in San Francisco. Judge Patel, a familiar name to those who follow bench writs, invoked a different basis: the Endangered Species Act. (Desert tortoises, you see.) The judge's injunction has put over the federal sale to Bruce Babbitt's reign at Interior. Probably Vice President Gore and the greenies will want to weigh in. For the sake of balance, Hazel O'Leary at Energy ought to have a say. She seems to recognize that impeding commerce in the name of all that is good and pure sometimes has adverse effects on real people. Some opponents charge that biotech is a cover for using the site to contain the "hot" remains of decommissioned nuclear plants. Presumably public officials would be held accountable for observing whatever conditions are laid down at the time a site is opened. Moreover, from the intensity with which the scientific-business sector has supported this siting, they would appear not to think their interests can be met in another way. Without Ward Valley, "nuclear waste" is going to have to make a trek of 3,000 miles, or collect on the premises of California's labs and hospitals, most of which are near residential neighborhoods. If the threat posed by these materials is anything like what the alarmists say, those two options would seem to be especially unpleasant alternatives. A final option, and one that those who use nuclear technology will come to eventually in any case, is to move their facilities to wherever disposal can be arranged, or simply shut down operations that promise life-saving medicine as well as high-value occupations. That is not what Governor Wilson wants in California, and it is not what President Clinton must seek for the nation.
event_description: A federal judge issued an injunction based on the Endangered Species Act regarding a proposed site for nuclear waste disposal.
setting: federal court case
characters: Judge Marilyn Patel [Villain]; Governor Pete Wilson [Hero]; California biomedical and surgical talent [Victim]; U.S. Ecology [Unknown]; environmentalists and NIMBYs [Unknown]; Vice President Gore [Unknown]; Hazel O'Leary [Unknown]
plot: A federal judge issued an injunction based on the Endangered Species Act, blocking a proposed nuclear waste disposal site in California, which Governor Wilson was trying to establish to support the state's biotechnology industry. This decision affects the biomedical and surgical talent in California, who face a scarcity of disposal sites for nuclear waste.
moral: The text suggests that the injunction is detrimental to California's economy and the biotechnology sector, advocating for the need to find a solution that allows for the establishment of a nuclear waste disposal site to support life-saving medicine and high-value occupations.

Title: Hydroelectric Dams Generate a Flood of Controversy
Text: POTLATCH, Wash. -- When the two-dam complex known as the Cushman Project took shape 70 years ago, promotion of hydroelectric power was a major federal policy. Before a crowd of admiring school children, President Coolidge flipped a switch in the White House, opening floodgates here to start Cushman's mighty turbines whirling.    Built by the city of Tacoma, Wash., the dams' cheap, clean electricity ended chaotic brownouts. They also meant more jobs and new industries that Tacoma lured to enjoy what it called America's "Electric City."    Those turbines still spin, generating about 20% of the city's power. But for hydroelectric dams, the political world has turned upside down. The Cushman Project is under fire from environmental groups and more than a dozen state and federal agencies over allegations ranging from harming the region's wildlife to damaging the culture of a nearby Indian tribe. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission deliberated the issues for 24 years. Last year, it decreed that Cushman may have a new license to operate but only after adopting expensive fish-protecting measures that the city says would result in an annual $2.5 million loss for the project.    Today's federal policy puts the future of scores of such dams in doubt. In place of President Coolidge, there is Bruce Babbitt, secretary of the Interior Department, who has begun taking a sledgehammer to various dams deemed no longer useable, giving them a televised tap before the wrecking crews arrive. In his dam-busting forays, which often leave the dams replaced by fast-running rivers, Mr. Babbitt has said he senses a "deep, widespread understanding that Americans overshot the mark in our dam-building frenzy." Mr. Babbitt celebrated another dam deconstruction yesterday, saying that PacifiCorp's decision to tear down a small hydroelectric dam on the White Salmon River in Washington state was an "excellent outcome."    The Cushman case, which Tacoma is taking to the U.S. Court of Appeals in Washington, D.C., "is a real concern for our industry," says Linda Church Ciocci, head of the National Hydropower Association, which represents owners of the nation's hydroelectric dams. "They see it as an indication of where things are going." The economic worth of goods produced by the dams, which include clean electricity, flood control and water for irrigation, she says, are being outweighed in the licensing process by environmental claims.    Within the next 15 years, she says, 284 hydro-dam owners, or roughly two-thirds of those not operated by the federal government, are heading for the same rough FERC relicensing gauntlet. She worries that many projects, burdened with new environmental restrictions like Cushman, won't be able to compete against coal and gas-fired plants.    From an environmental point of view, that might seem odd. Plants powered by fossil fuel create tons of air pollutants including carbon dioxide, which many scientists claim is overheating the Earth's climate by creating an atmosphere that traps more of the sun's heat. Dams, which generate about 11% of the nation's power, emit no air pollutants.    But in the current Rodney Dangerfield climate, that doesn't generate much respect. And the big federal power dams, which aren't regulated by the FERC, face an even tougher gauntlet. Under the U.S. Endangered Species Act, federal agencies that protect wildlife can issue "mandates" requiring the dams to change activities deemed harmful to animals.    That the dams don't produce carbon dioxide doesn't factor into this, explains Brian Gorman, a spokesman for the Commerce Department's National Marine Fisheries Service. "Our obligation is purely a biological one. We will look only at the biology of the fish and only at endangered salmon."    Among the opposition to dams is American Rivers, a Washington, D.C., environmental group. Begun in 1973, its specialty is intervening in FERC relicensing cases, such as Cushman, to demand more environmental protection. Its role was enhanced after it lobbied for a 1986 change in the Federal Power Act that requires the FERC to weigh environmental factors on the same footing as power and safety issues.    Rebecca Wodder, president of American Rivers, says the group is working on a scorecard that would measure how dams are protecting the environment. The scorecard doesn't weigh in that dams don't produce air pollution, she says, because it is focused on changes in river environments. "The U.S. is No. 1 in the world in many kinds of aquatic species," she says. But many "are more endangered than birds. It's a significant issue."    The U.S. Department of Energy believes another significant issue is the need for "green" or renewable energy. Its proposed bill for restructuring the utility industry would require utilities to produce 7.5% renewable power by 2010. But while hydroelectric dams operate with a clean, renewable source of energy, they are excluded from the DOE's definition of "green."    A senior DOE official explains that environmental groups lobbied heavily against dams. Only power derived from solar, wind, geothermal and biomass sources qualifies. (Biomass includes power generated from organic wastes.) "These are growing industries, and we wanted to give them a bit of a push," the official says.    For Steven Klein, superintendent of Tacoma Public Utilities, which operates the Cushman Project, that means he may soon have to buy some solar energy to meet his quota for green power that will not be met by the water flowing through the city's turbines. "It is absolutely insane to have hydro projects out there buying credits for renewable technology," argues Ms. Ciocci of the National Hydropower Association.    So far, Interior Secretary Babbitt hasn't shown up here with his sledgehammer, but Mr. Klein wonders whether it is just a matter of time. Since the late 1980s, when Mr. Klein took over the marathon Cushman case to renew its license from the FERC, he has battled claims from the Interior Department's bureaus of Indian Affairs, Reclamation and the Fish and Wildlife Service. "They all came in with their wish lists: flood control, fish, Indian culture. They don't care if they conflict. Nobody cares about anybody else. There's just no compromise." In the midst of all this, Interior's National Park Service declared that Cushman's two elegant, highceilinged powerhouses must be included in the nation's Register of Historic Places and Landmarks.    The Cushman Project's struggle with the Skokomish Tribe, meantime, seems never ending. While the FERC ordered Tacoma to install an expensive fish passageway that may improve the tribe's fishing prospects, it ignored the tribe's larger claim that the two dams have destroyed its 10,000-year-old culture.    Some of Cushman's land was originally bought in the 1920s from tribal members. In the 1930s, the tribe's leaders tried to block completion of the dam, but their suit was thrown out of court. After that the tribe's major business, fishing, shrank when Cushman's engineers redirected most of the north fork of the Skokomish River down three pipes leading to the project's second powerhouse.    Now the tribe is preparing a second suit. They want Tacoma to pay $5.8 billion. "This is not just for the Skokomish people; it's for all people," says Denny Hurtado, chairman of the tribe, which has 800 members. Mr. Hurtado said he might settle if Tacoma simply gave him the dam, which he said the tribe would run to maximize flowing water for salmon.    At times, tribal leaders have called the Cushman dams a "rogue project" and have even claimed it is "evil." The latter definition brings a weary smile to Mr. Klein's face as he burrows through the refrigerator-size pile of documents that has grown in his Tacoma office since the fight over Cushman began.    "Evil? Look, we're municipally owned. We can't afford to be evil."    (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: Energy Policy Needs A Balanced Approach" -- WSJ Oct. 12, 1999)  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Cushman Project is undergoing a relicensing process that includes new environmental restrictions to protect fish.
setting: FERC relicensing process and U.S. Court of Appeals
characters: Cushman Project [Villain]; environmental groups [Hero]; Skokomish Tribe [Victim]; Linda Church Ciocci [Hero]; Bruce Babbitt [Villain]
plot: The Cushman Project, originally built for hydroelectric power, is facing opposition from environmental groups and federal agencies due to its negative impact on wildlife and local culture. The FERC has mandated new environmental measures for the project, which could lead to significant financial losses for Tacoma. The Skokomish Tribe is preparing legal action against Tacoma for cultural damages caused by the dams.
moral: The text suggests that the Cushman Project's operations are harming the environment and local culture, advocating for stronger environmental protections and recognition of tribal rights in the relicensing process.

Title: U.S. Moves to Ease Curbs on Habitats Of Spotted Owl
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Interior Department moved to ease restrictions on some private timberlands inhabited by the northern spotted owl, but it also pledged to enforce logging restrictions on other private acreage more aggressively. The purpose of the new scheme is to coordinate the use of private lands with the forest summit plan announced in July by the Clinton administration. That proposal, which concerned mostly public lands, was geared toward reducing by 80% the timber harvesting on federal lands in the Northwest, while at the same time minimizing federal regulation of private forest land. The new proposal, which is roughly the equivalent of a proposed regulation, would affect individual landowners as well as large companies such as Weyerhaeuser Co. of Tacoma, Wash., and Boise Cascade Corp. of Boise, Idaho. The plan "clarifies and, in some areas, relieves the restrictions on cutting of private timberlands imposed by the Endangered Species Act on account of the spotted owl," said George Frampton, assistant secretary for fish and wildlife and parks. However, considering that the Interior Department previously had what Mr. Frampton termed a policy of "official nonenforcement" toward many of the spotted-owl restrictions for private land, it's unclear whether the change would mean a net increase or decrease in logging. Mr. Frampton said that he didn't know how many acres would be freed up for logging under the plan, but that it would be a "significant amount." The plan isn't intended to recreate habitats for the northern spotted owl on private lands -- most such habitats have disappeared through development or logging. Rather, the plan aims at using private lands to connect federal lands where the owl would be protected. The plan would provide 10 protected areas, or roughly half the previous number. Mark Rey, vice president for forest resources at the American Forest and Paper Association, called the department's plan "a first step in evaluating whether they can make good" on the promise to reduce regulation of private timberlands. The proposal, he said, appears to call for less timber harvesting in Oregon and more harvesting in Washington state. He said he couldn't predict how it would affect harvesting in California. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Interior Department proposed easing logging restrictions on private timberlands inhabited by the northern spotted owl.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: Interior Department [Villain]; northern spotted owl [Victim]; George Frampton [Hero]; Mark Rey [Hero]
plot: The Interior Department proposes to ease logging restrictions on private timberlands inhabited by the northern spotted owl while pledging to enforce logging restrictions more aggressively on other private lands. This plan aims to coordinate private land use with federal land protection efforts, but it remains unclear if it will lead to more or less logging overall.
moral: The problem is the unclear impact of easing logging restrictions on the northern spotted owl's habitat, and the text suggests that a careful evaluation of the new plan is necessary to ensure the owl's protection while balancing timber harvesting needs.

Title: Nonprofit Aims To Raise Funds To Aid Salmon
Text: The mighty chinook salmon has inspired poets, lured tourists and hooked anglers. Can it raise $10 million or so a year? Some wealthy and well-connected Seattleites are betting on it. They've just established a first-of-its kind nonprofit organization, the Puget Sound Salmon Foundation, with the aim of raising $10 million to $15 million annually for salmon-habitat restoration in the Puget Sound area. The plan is to give the money to nonprofit groups and government agencies for the repair, protection and purchase of vital tracts of habitat along salmon-supporting rivers and estuaries. Even though millions of dollars are already being spent on a state and federal level, members of the foundation's board say it isn't enough. They contend that saving the chinook and other salmon populations will take years and hundreds of millions of dollars -- two things government, with its many priorities, isn't equipped to provide. So it's up to the metropolitan area's well-heeled to lend a hand. The foundation will provide a way "for the private sector to engage in this issue," says board member Gerry Johnson, managing partner in the Seattle law firm Preston Gates & Ellis. Last year, the Loomis Forest Fund took the same tack with the threatened lynx, and it paid off. The fund raised $16.5 million in 18 months from more than 5,100 donors -- including Microsoft Corp. co-founder Paul Allen, who kicked in $3.4 million -- for the sole purpose of buying 25,000 acres of the Loomis Forest in north central Washington, one of the last remaining major stomping grounds for the lynx. The purchase from the state, completed in December, put 20% of the forest off limits to logging. Loomis fund leaders, however, caution that the Salmon Foundation shouldn't expect the same smashing results. There are a lot of people in the region with a lot of money, "but that doesn't mean it's easy to get," says Fred Munson, the Loomis fund's campaign coordinator in Seattle. "It's not like people with a lot of money have a shortage of places to give it." Mr. Munson adds that it's a great deal more difficult to solicit contributions year after year than it is to convince people to pony up for a one-time effort, like the Loomis fund did. "The Loomis was a very specific campaign both in terms of time frame and in terms of, `Save this particular piece of land,'" he says. "How does a philanthropist feel about giving $2 million to the Salmon Foundation, and then being asked again next year?" But board members of the Salmon Foundation argue that the salmon is so powerful a symbol for people in Washington, and especially Seattle, that checkbooks will fly open. "Salmon are an icon species, really a talisman of the quality of our environment," says the foundation's Mr. Johnson. Plans are for serious fund raising to begin in the next two months. The foundation's first-year operating budget is $100,000, donated by the Bullitt Foundation, a Seattle philanthropic organization that focuses on environmental causes. The money will be spent in part on setting up an office and hiring staff. The other board members are: Doug Walker, chief executive of Seattle software maker WRQ Inc.; Jerry Grinstein, a director in the Seattle venture-capital firm Madrona Investment Group; Craig Gannett of the Seattle law firm, Davis Wright Tremaine; and Secretary of State Ralph Munro. The federal government listed the Puget Sound chinook as threatened in March 1999 under the Endangered Species Act, the first such listing in a densely populated urban area. Another run in the region, the Hood Canal summer chum, also got a threatened designation last spring. For its part, King County, one of the largest county governments in the Puget Sound area, plans to spend $3.5 million on salmon-habitat restoration this year, not including the $7 million it plans to use to buy roughly 3,500 acres of land or development rights to land to protect salmon. Still, the county welcomes any additional money dedicated to the cause, according to Tim Ceis, chief of staff for King County Executive Ron Sims and director of the county's Endangered Species Act office. Jim Kramer, a Seattle natural-resources consultant who is helping the Salmon Foundation get organized, says it was modeled in part after the 33-year-old Chesapeake Bay Foundation in Annapolis, Md. That foundation's $13 million annual budget, nearly all of which is from private donations, is spent on research, litigation, lobbying and education programs. In Seattle, the Salmon Foundation intends to give funds to a number of projects, probably with some advice from the Salmon Recovery Funding Board, Mr. Kramer says. The funding board, created by the Legislature in 1999, is charged with doling out state and federal funds to various public and private projects around the state that purchase and repair habitats. The idea isn't to let government off the hook, says Mr. Munro, the secretary of state. "If anything, we'll be poking government with a sharp stick," by calling renewed attention to the salmon's uncertain fate, he says. "The problem is immense," says Jacques White, a marine ecologist with People for Puget Sound, a Seattle conservation group. But "there's a lot of new wealth that has no history of giving or getting involved in civic opportunities. Hopefully, this body can provide a mechanism to get some of that money involved in habitat" protection and restoration. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Establishment of the Puget Sound Salmon Foundation to raise funds for salmon-habitat restoration.
setting: Puget Sound area, establishment of the Puget Sound Salmon Foundation
characters: Puget Sound Salmon Foundation [Hero]; federal government [Villain]; chinook salmon [Victim]; King County [Unknown]; Tim Ceis [Unknown]; Fred Munson [Unknown]; Jacques White [Unknown]
plot: The Puget Sound Salmon Foundation is established to raise funds for salmon-habitat restoration, aiming to collect $10 million to $15 million annually. The foundation plans to distribute these funds to nonprofit groups and government agencies to repair and protect salmon habitats, as existing government efforts are deemed insufficient.
moral: The problem of salmon habitat degradation is immense, and the text suggests that private sector involvement through the Puget Sound Salmon Foundation is essential to raise the necessary funds for effective restoration efforts.

Title: A Special Weekly Report From The Wall Street Journal's Capital Bureau
Text: Gingrich plans to spend Earth Day at Atlanta's zoo with schoolchildren. The Environmental Information Center issues a flier which claims that zoos are "the only place you'll see many of those animals" after "phony" environmentalists in Congress gut "the Endangered Species Act." Clinton plans a walk along the C&O Canal in Washington to unveil a parks initiative and attack the GOP. The House GOP threatens to investigate the EPA for allegedly planning Earth Day events in districts of vulnerable GOP freshmen; the EPA denies it. CLINTON GEARS UP to get his presidential campaign off to a fast start. New ads hit the air today in Midwest battleground states, hammering Dole and Gingrich for votes against putting more police on the streets. The campaign expects to publicly name state directors by mid-May. Clinton has $21 million left to spend before the fall campaign, with nearly half planned for advertising. The campaign looks to compensate for the loss of Ron Brown, particularly in California. Brown had planned an affirmative-action debate there, as well as a high-profile event on export financing for companies. The campaign adds strategist Bill Carrick, who will focus on California. Democrats are stunned by Dole's spending and insist he is nearing federal limits. DOLE STEPS UP his attacks on Clinton's "liberal" judicial appointments. The GOP senator, in a speech to newspaper editors today, is expected to blast Clinton's "judicial hall of shame." He won't back down on his criticism of Harold Baer, a New York federal judge, and will cite others. Dole advisers see judges as a prime way to show ideological differences with Clinton. In a test of coordinating messages, the speech will be followed up with charges by GOP state officials against Clinton judges. Dole's bigger challenge is to find a way to finish the budget, both to finance the rest of the year and to soften public perceptions of GOP cuts. Some negotiations with Clinton may still be needed. Beyond cutting spending and government, "there also has to be a message of caring and sensitivity, and that is difficult," says Sen. McCain, a top Dole adviser. Forbes shows signs of wanting to bury bitterness and work for Dole; he pays a visit to Dole campaign headquarters. REPUBLICANS ACCUSE the administration of delaying this year's Medicare report (due April 1) updating when the fund is expected to run out of money. "There are shenanigans by the trustees on Medicare," says Rep. DeLay. But Medicare trustees blame last winter's government shutdowns. HELPING HAND: The Christian Coalition, which has been trying to reach out to minorities, plans to announce Monday a $25,000 reward for information that would lead to an arrest in a spate of firebombings of black churches, many in the South. The coalition will challenge civil-rights groups to match the effort. TAXING DEMANDS: Texas Rep. Archer, who heads the tax-writing Ways and Means Committee, filed for an extension after spending 16 hours on his tax return. He refuses to use an accountant because "I ought to have hands-on experience in dealing with the tax code." PHANTOM VETO: GOP Rep. Ballenger, in a news release, charges that his regulatory-reform bill was vetoed by Clinton due to "election-year politics." Actually, the bill never got out of committee. A Ballenger spokeswoman says the release was in error. DEPARTMENT OF TRADE? The idea revives as Kantor moves to head Commerce. As U.S. Trade Representative, Kantor resisted folding his office into a revamped Commerce Department to create a new agency. Some GOP budget-cutters, as well as the late Commerce Secretary Brown, liked the idea. Proposing a trade agency now "would be a really bold move for the administration," says former acting Commerce Undersecretary David Rothkopf. One argument is that Clinton could take credit for moving to consolidate the federal bureaucracy. Brown had already begun looking into moving the Export-Import Bank into the department. In addition, all of Commerce's economists and trade experts could work more closely with the government's trade-negotiating arm. The drawback: the trade office, with only 164 employees, works well now as a lean and independent agency. MINOR MEMOS: U.S. Ambassador to Japan Walter Mondale says Dole, his vice presidential opponent in 1976, "should be beatable. Even I beat him.". . . Dueling disciples: Democrat Mark Warner, hoping to unseat Virginia GOP Sen. Warner, issues a bumper sticker: "Mark, Not John.". . . A GOP pledge of allegiance by the Gross National Product comedy group ends with "one nation, under Dole, invisible, with no liberal justices at all." EARTH DAY will be filled with a lot of political mudslinging. Environmentalists will crash GOP media events Monday. Some will hand out a zero scorecard for Texas Rep. Stockman's record when he attends a beach cleanup. A Sierra Club cartoon shows an imaginary "Sen. Janus Hoodwink" bashing green laws "every day but Earth Day." The group runs radio ads attacking California Rep. Seastrand and others for their records on wetland protection.
event_description: Claims of the Endangered Species Act being undermined by Congress.
setting: Earth Day events and political activities surrounding endangered species legislation
characters: Environmental Information Center [Hero]; Congressional Republicans [Villain]; Endangered species [Victim]
plot: The Environmental Information Center claims that Congressional Republicans are undermining the Endangered Species Act, which threatens the survival of endangered species, while they engage in political activities on Earth Day.
moral: The text suggests that the problem is the undermining of the Endangered Species Act by Congressional Republicans, and it advocates for protecting the Act to ensure the survival of endangered species.

Title: Cross Country: Wolves Attack Wisconsin With Washington's Help
Text: Milwaukee -- When the Trump administration announced a plan last week to overhaul the Endangered Species Act, the frantic reactions were illustrated with pictures of humpback whales and baby bald eagles. But a much better way to understand what Republicans have in mind is to consider Wisconsin's wolf problem. Paul and Judy Canik raise bighorn and hair sheep near the small town of Butternut. Their herd has grown to about 400, each sheep worth around $1,500. Guarding the prized flock are nine Spanish mastiffs, worth $2,500 apiece. While checking on their animals two years ago, the Caniks found that 17 pregnant ewes and one dog had been killed by wolves. All that remained of the dog was its skull. This was the second mastiff wolves had eaten on their property. Last fall on the nearby farm of Daniel and Kathy Postical, wolves killed three angus beef calves. The Caniks "worry all the time" and count their sheep every day. "There's a place for wolves, but not here on the farms," says Ms. Canik. "In summers like this with the windows open I can't sleep when I hear the dogs barking." Since gray wolves in Wisconsin, Michigan and Minnesota are protected under the Endangered Species Act, farmers are forbidden to shoot the predators that devour their livestock. The penalty for "knowingly violating" the law can reach $50,000 and a year in prison. But after decades of federal protection, the wolves have more than rebounded. In 2011 the Fish and Wildlife Service determined that the wolves had recovered sufficiently in the Great Lakes region and could be delisted. For a short time, states like Wisconsin gained the authority to manage their wolf populations. But in 2014 a federal court put the wolves back under protection. The judge ruled, in the words of one news report, that the FWS had failed to consider properly "whether the animal had recovered in other parts of the country where it could live and whether the Midwestern states had adequate plans in place to protect them." Now the FWS is making another attempt. The agency announced in June that it would analyze data on the wolves' historical range and recovery. Depending on the findings, a new proposal to delist could be ready by the end of the year. Congress is trying to solve the problem by including language to delist the wolves in the Interior Department's 2019 appropriations bill. All this is good news for Wisconsin's farmers. In the short period after 2011 when the state had control over its wolf population, the system worked, according to Laurie Groskopf of the Wisconsin Wolf Facts blog. "By allowing Wisconsin to do the management," she says, "particularly responding to agricultural attacks and harassment, we know from experience in the three years where we did manage them, the number of incidents will decrease." The Great Lakes wolf population continues to rise. Wisconsin's most recent numbers, from 2016-17, show the state had between 905 and 944 wolves. Michigan's count was 662, mostly confined to the state's Upper Peninsula. Minnesota had 2,856 wolves, which now are being blamed for a worrying decline in the moose population. Although the Endangered Species Act is a federal law, some states compensate farmers for the damage wolves cause. Last year Wisconsin paid $256,148, according to the Milwaukee Journal Sentinel, up more than 25% from 2016. The Caniks received $800 a sheep, $1,500 for the first dog, and $2,500 for the second. With the help of the U.S. Agriculture Department, they put in an electric fence that, so far, has kept the wolves away. The state paid the Posticals about $200 per calf, even though they say the beef could have eventually sold for upward of $800. They installed an electric fence, too, but the wolves have learned how to crawl under the wire. The Posticals worry what may happen the next time they have young cattle. Once the wolves are delisted, Wisconsin could allow farmers to shoot the predators coming after their animals. The state could set up hunting seasons, which would help control the population without putting the species back in danger. "Wolves were eliminated in the past because there was a very large bounty on them, and other methods we would never use were being used, such as poison," Ms. Groskopf says. "Simply using trapping and hunting and response to incidents could never ever eliminate them. The main problem will be their numbers continuing to increase in spite of these controls." Delisting the gray wolves in the Great Lakes region would be a significant delegation of authority back to the states, giving Wisconsin, Minnesota and Michigan the power to decide how to control their wolf populations. Since state legislators are closer to the citizens, they're more attuned than the federal government to the needs of farmers in Butternut. That's why delisting would be a win not only for the Caniks and Posticals but also for federalism and property rights. --- Ms. Petersen is a writer and research associate at the Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty. (See related letters: "Letters to the Editor: How Best to Keep Those Wolves From the Door" -- WSJ Aug. 13, 2018)   Credit: By Cori Petersen
event_description: The Fish and Wildlife Service is making another attempt to propose the delisting of gray wolves in the Great Lakes region.
setting: political context surrounding the Endangered Species Act and wolf management in Wisconsin
characters: Paul and Judy Canik [Victim]; Daniel and Kathy Postical [Victim]; gray wolves [Villain]; Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; Congress [Hero]; Laurie Groskopf [Hero]
plot: The Fish and Wildlife Service's proposal to delist gray wolves is driven by the need to address the concerns of farmers like the Caniks and Posticals, who suffer livestock losses due to wolf predation. Congress is attempting to support this delisting through appropriations, which would allow states to manage wolf populations and potentially reduce conflicts with agriculture.
moral: The problem is the ongoing conflict between protected gray wolves and farmers' livelihoods; the text suggests that delisting the wolves would empower states to manage their populations effectively, benefiting both farmers and the principle of federalism.

Title: Plan to Protect Sturgeon In Ala. Faces Extinction
Text: ON THE ALABAMA RIVER -- Just after eight on a recent chilly morning, biologists Phillip Kilpatrick and David Armstrong are hoping to catch a bit of luck. The two employees of the Alabama Game and Fisheries Division are looking for a specimen of the rare Alabama sturgeon -- a bony, brownish bottom-feeder that once was common in the Mobile River Basin but now is almost extinct. As they pilot their boat to the middle of the quiet river near Camden, Ala., Mr. Armstrong, 35, reaches into a cubbyhole to pull out a fish net. But Mr. Kilpatrick, a 46-year-old man whose sun-pinkened face and blistered hands are testament to a lifetime on the water, admonishes him, "If we take it out we're putting a jinx on ourselves." The men need all the luck they can get. For the past two years, Messrs. Kilpatrick and Armstrong, along with three other biologists, have trawled a 132-mile stretch of the river, setting fishing lines three or four nights a week from November to June. But after 3,500 hours of fishing, they've caught a grand total of three sturgeon. "It's one of the hardest projects I have worked on for the least amount of instant gratification," says Mr. Kilpatrick. Indeed, the three fish now swimming in a 450-gallon tank at a state fish hatchery about 90 miles west of Montgomery are the result of a $1.1 million statewide effort to keep the sturgeon off the federal government's list of endangered species. The fishermen's work is part of a voluntary protection plan crafted by an unlikely coalition of business leaders, environmentalists, and federal and state agencies. This spring they were hoping to begin seeing a payoff for all their work: breeding the captive sturgeon and spawning enough offspring to return to Alabama's rivers. Now, though, the coalition itself is endangered. Yesterday, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service issued a proposal to offer the sturgeon protection under the Endangered Species Act. The last time such a listing was proposed -- six years ago -- it triggered a bruising political battle that ended with the federal agency backing down and eventually, the formation of the coalition. The 26-year-old act long has been a political hot potato throughout the U.S. Bitterly opposed by business interests that fear onerous regulation, it is fervently pushed by environmentalists who see it as vital to protecting threatened wildlife from runaway development. When a listing was proposed for the sturgeon in 1993, a group of 34 Alabama business leaders and state agencies commissioned a study that claimed the listing could cause Alabama and Mississippi to lose a total of 20,000 jobs as a result of curtailed dredging, which could ultimately shut down barge traffic on key waterways used by shipping, timber and power industries. Now, the agency is likely to be in for another fight. "We don't think there's anything to gain by the listing re-proposal," Bill Satterfield, a Birmingham attorney who helped lead the business fight against the listing six years ago, said yesterday. "It is likely to throw us all back into the same controversy we were involved in" with the first proposal. For their part, federal officials say a final decision on whether to list the species won't be made for another year. And Alabama's voluntary conservation plan will "weigh heavily on the decision," says wildlife service spokeswoman Cindy Hoffman. But she says the Endangered Species Act gives the agency no choice but to proceed with the listing process on the sturgeon, since only three fish have been caught in two years. Environmental activists believe the sturgeon should have been protected the first time around. "We'll aggressively seek to see the fish listed," says Ned Mudd, a Birmingham attorney who is on the board of the Biodiversity Legal Foundation, a non-profit organization based in Boulder, Colo., that sued the wildlife service in 1995 after it dropped the first listing proposal. Around the turn of the century, many Alabama sturgeon skimmed the bottom of most of the state's rivers. But by the early 1980s, it had become so rare that fishermen would alert the Alabama Department of Conservation when they snagged one. Biologists believe the sturgeon all but disappeared for a variety of reasons: the navigation dams built along the rivers around the middle of the century; the dredging that helped develop navigation channels for barge traffic; and mining near the rivers that has increased the buildup of silt and sediment in the waterways. All of these practices altered the sturgeon's habitat, possibly preventing the fish from getting to its breeding grounds. After many studies, the wildlife service officially proposed an endangered listing for the Alabama sturgeon in 1993. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, which is responsible for dredging the Alabama and Tombigbee rivers, at first announced that the listing would "significantly impact" its activities, but later said that was unlikely. By that time, Alabama's business leaders were already in the midst of a media and lobbying blitz, turning the sturgeon into an emblem of environmental regulation run amok. "It was just the principle of letting somebody from Washington tell us how to . . . take care of our business," says Roy McAuley, technical services manager of a Union Camp Corp. paper mill in Prattville, just north of Montgomery. If the fish is listed as endangered, Mr. McAuley says the mill could face a costly cleanup of effluent it has dumped into the river. U.S. Sen. Richard C. Shelby, a Republican from Tuscaloosa, warned in the media that a listing would "open a Pandora's box that would wreck thousands of lives." In December 1994, the wildlife service withdrew its proposal, saying that it had "insufficient information to justify listing a species that may no longer exist." After that fight, wildlife-service officials worried the ill will would spill over into another effort it was just beginning: developing a recovery plan for 15 other species previously listed as endangered in Alabama. So, in early 1995, officials at the U.S. Fish and Wildlife's field office in Jackson, Miss., asked business leaders, environmentalists and other federal and state officials to discuss how to protect these species without unduly affecting business practices. Thus, the Mobile River Basin Coalition was born. Over time, the business leaders learned to trust their former foes in government. "Four years ago . . . I considered [the federal officials] very, very difficult people," Mr. Satterfield, the Birmingham attorney, said in an interview before yesterday's listing proposal. But "we have figured out how to work positively together." Still, business leaders remained worried about the sturgeon. For one thing, evidence kept cropping up that the sturgeon did indeed exist. Only four months after the wildlife service abandoned its first listing effort, a commercial fisherman caught a sturgeon and handed it over to wildlife officials. A second fish was caught by an agency biologist, but died days after it was tagged. Business leaders fretted that such catches would prompt the agency to again propose an endangered listing. Meanwhile, environmental forces turned up the heat. In August 1995, the Biodiversity Legal Foundation filed a lawsuit in federal district court in Montgomery against the wildlife service, alleging the agency had violated the Endangered Species Act by withdrawing its listing proposal. (The suit is awaiting a ruling, and yesterday Mr. Mudd said the group wouldn't drop the suit until the sturgeon is listed.) Mr. Satterfield realized the issue wasn't likely to die, and asked wildlife officials if they would work together once again -- this time on a voluntary conservation plan for the sturgeon. The idea, says Mr. Satterfield, was to avoid a future listing. "Three or four years down the road we may have already solved the fish's problems," he says, "and then they won't have to list it anyway." Sen. Shelby became an ardent supporter of the coalition's plan, leading the effort that appropriated $1.1 million in federal dollars since 1997. About $600,000 of the funds has been used to support fishing expeditions, breeding efforts and other research into the fish's habitat. Messrs. Kilpatrick, Armstrong and three other biologists began fishing for the sturgeon in early 1997. Luck struck quickly: In April, Messrs. Kilpatrick and Armstrong caught their first sturgeon. Hauling in the line with a sturgeon thrashing at the end of a hook "was as close to a heart attack as I have ever been," recalls Mr. Kilpatrick. The two rushed to grab a net before the fish flipped back into the water. "We were scrambling like the Three Stooges," says Mr. Kilpatrick. When the fish was brought to the hatchery in Marion, workers named it "Serge," then ignorant of its sexual identity. Without a partner, though, the 2.05-pound sturgeon didn't have much chance of saving the species. But in December, the biologists caught a second sturgeon. And in January 1998, hatchery officials performed delicate surgery on both fish and determined they had one male and one female. It was nearly another year before the fishermen snared another sturgeon, which nearly died in the effort. By May, William C. Nichols, hatchery manager for the Alabama Department of Conservation in Marion, hopes the first two fish will be ready for spawning. Biologists will effectively perform artificial insemination, injecting the female with a hormone to induce ovulation and fertilizing the eggs in a dishpan. But breeding so few fish could be the ichthyological equivalent of marrying a cousin. "You have to be careful to maintain the genetic integrity of the population," says Mr. Nichols. None of these efforts much impresses some environmentalists. "It's a nice political tactic," says Mr. Mudd of the Biodiversity Legal Foundation. "Let's assume they do breed them. What are they going to do with them? The habitat is so blown to hell. Who's going to do the dirty work and fix the rivers that the sturgeon live in?" Paul Hartfield, a biologist at the wildlife-service's field office in Jackson, says Alabama's voluntary conservation plan already addresses habitat issues. He and other biologists have been diving the length of the Alabama River, looking at how dredging has affected mussel beds, which the biologists believe are compatible with the sturgeon. Mr. Hartfield believes it could simply be a matter of moving dredging activities a few feet or tinkering with the dredging schedule. But an endangered species listing could provide some protection that a voluntary plan doesn't: with a listing, any federal project -- including a new dam or a proposal to dredge untouched parts of a river -- deemed detrimental to the sturgeon must be modified. Business leaders like Mr. Satterfield say pressing the law again could cause the delicate cooperation between businesses and environmentalists to fall apart. "It would be tremendously counterproductive . . . to be on the verge of success, to have it shut down because of some procedural process," says Mr. Satterfield. The wildlife agency will be conducting a series of public hearings on the proposal. Agency spokeswoman Ms. Hoffman adds: "If something out there [already] is going to protect the species, that is something [we] will take into consideration." Even before the federal agency reaches a decision, though, the voluntary conservation plan could collapse. Yesterday, Sen. Shelby said the listing proposal "undermines the ongoing cooperative efforts to pursue a voluntary conservation plan." An aide to the senator added that funding for the plan was now in jeopardy because federal law bars Congress from appropriating certain funds for a local conservation effort if the species is listed. But wildlife biologist Mr. Hartfield says the species would just become eligible for different funding, and that local efforts could continue. For the moment, deep in the woods of southern Alabama, the fishing biologists are still hoping to catch another break. On a recent excursion, the men navigate toward the gear they put out the night before: four trotlines, each sporting 58 hooks baited with night crawlers. Mr. Armstrong begins pulling up the first line. The first fish to appear is a six-inch blue catfish. Mr. Kilpatrick quickly tosses it back into the river. Over the next two hours, he pitches 23 more blue catfish, five channel catfish, two flathead catfish and one freshwater drum back into the water. As he is pulling up the last line, he begins to count the number of hooks left. When the last one comes up empty, he shrugs, "There's always tomorrow." "Every time we come out I am expecting to catch one," says Mr. Kilpatrick. "We always keep our hopes up. . ." Mr. Armstrong finishes the sentence: ". . . but with the knowledge of low probability." --- 1993: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service proposes listing the sturgeon under the federal Endangered Species Act. 1993-94: Business leaders warn that listing could cause loss of 20,000 jobs in Alabama and Mississippi. 1994: Wildlife service abandons proposal, saying the sturgeon "may no longer exist." 1995: Mobile River Basin Coalition formed. Two sturgeon caught. The Biodiversity Legal Foundation sues the wildlife service for failing to list the sturgeon. 1996: The coalition begins work on a voluntary conservation plan for the sturgeon. 1997: Five biologists begin fishing. Two sturgeon caught. 1998: Third sturgeon caught. 1999: Wildlife service again proposes the sturgeon for listing as an endangered species. Source: Economic Forecasting Center, Georgia State University Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service issued a proposal to offer the Alabama sturgeon protection under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: NA
characters: NA
plot: NA
moral: NA

Title: World-Wide
Text: THE MEXICAN ARMY PUSHED Mayan rebels deeper into jungle strongholds. Troops tightened their grip on the southern state of Chiapas, pursuing guerrilla leaders into remote areas and, with help from neighboring Guatemala, cutting off escape routes. News reports and official statements said that town after town in territory once held by the rebel Zapatistas was falling under military control, with few indications of resistance. The government was strictly controlling information about the military offensive, launched after President Zedillo last week ordered the arrest of rebels. It wasn't clear whether the soldiers intended to follow the retreating guerrillas in pursuit of their main target, Zapatista leader Marcos. A conservative opposition party was favored to win control of the prosperous western state of Jalisco in yesterday's gubernatorial elections there. --- Israel and the PLO pledged to "negotiate promptly" an accord on Palestinian elections and Israeli troop redeployment in the West Bank. The commitment was made by the two sides during a meeting with Secretary of State Christopher in Washington. He said Israel also had promised not to build any new settlements in the West Bank. Clinton presided over a three-hour meeting of the Israeli, Jordanian and Egyptian foreign ministers and a PLO aide. The ministers agreed to set up industrial free-trade zones in the region, and the president said he would clear the way for exports from those zones to enter the U.S. duty-free. --- President Clinton would have to renege on aid to Russia and also pare his plans for government-industry investments in new technologies at home, under spending cuts that are sought by House Republicans. --- Clinton vowed to veto any effort to undermine his program to put 100,000 new police officers on the streets. The House is scheduled to vote tomorrow on the proposal for a $10 billion block grant that would merge funds for the new police program with crime prevention money. --- The White House provided new information about surgeon general nominee Foster's record and attempted to play down the controversial aspects. However, abortion foes increasingly are seizing the controversy as an opportunity to debate abortion practices. --- Democrats stepped up attacks on the House Republicans' welfare overhaul bill, saying it does almost nothing to move welfare recipients into jobs. At the same time, Democrats indicated they could compromise on a key issue: whether programs for the poor should remain open to any needy person who qualifies. --- More than 1,000 Serb fighters crossed into Bosnia from Croatia, according to U.N. reports, signaling an escalation of military activity in northwest Bosnia. Meanwhile, the U.N. commander in Bosnia failed to persuade the government's army chief to return to peace talks. --- Angola's Unita rebels agreed at their national congress to abide by a peace accord signed with the government three months ago. The peace pact had been in doubt because of Unita leader Savimbi's failure to attend a November signing ceremony. --- Rebel Chechnya claimed its forces killed 250 Russian soldiers and destroyed two dozen armored vehicles by blowing up a former Soviet missile site southwest of the Chechen capital, Grozny. --- Wildlife regulators agreed with Weyerhaeuser on a plan under which the company can log 209,000 acres it owns in Oregon's spotted owl country. The accord is the latest of several reached between the government and landowners to deal with conflicts involving the Endangered Species Act and private property. --- Peru has bolstered troop strength along its northern border in advance of a "final assault" on an Ecuadoran outpost in a disputed region, news reports in Lima said. President Fujimori said Peruvian anti-aircraft fire downed two Ecuadoran warplanes on the border yesterday. The countries have been fighting for 18 days over the area. --- Algerian security forces killed 20 Muslim fundamentalists over the weekend amid threats of more antigovernment suicide attacks. Ten of the fundamentalists died during a police search operation in Mascara.
event_description: Wildlife regulators reached an accord with Weyerhaeuser allowing logging on 209,000 acres in Oregon's spotted owl habitat.
setting: the context of wildlife regulation and logging agreements under the Endangered Species Act
characters: wildlife regulators [Hero]; Weyerhaeuser [Villain]
plot: Wildlife regulators reached an agreement with Weyerhaeuser, allowing the company to log 209,000 acres in Oregon's spotted owl habitat, which reflects ongoing conflicts between the Endangered Species Act and private property rights.
moral: The text suggests that while logging may proceed, it highlights the need for careful management of endangered species habitats to balance economic interests with conservation efforts.

Title: White House and GOP Leaders Agree On $35 Billion Medicare-Medicaid Bill
Text: WASHINGTON -- Congress pushed toward adjournment as the White House and Republican leadership agreed to a $35 billion, 10-year Medicare-Medicaid package that would assist financially strapped hospitals and help enroll poor children in government health-care programs.    As part of the same year-end budget deal, Democrats won a partial restoration of an immigration-law provision making it easier for illegal aliens who have overstayed their visas to apply for residency status without leaving the U.S.    House Speaker Dennis Hastert was pressing for a final vote today, but the very size of the cumbersome bill -- which provides billions for education and medical research -- made it a magnet for late-breaking amendments that could keep lawmakers in town longer.    Senate Majority Leader Trent Lott weighed in with a late demand to authorize a brand-new medical-imaging institute at the National Institutes of Health. Amtrak tried -- and failed -- to add a $3.26 billion, 10-year tax-credit intended to help the passenger-rail corporation raise money for high-speed trains and track. The sugar industry was turned back when its Senate allies sought to tighten U.S. trade rules to prevent importers from circumventing sugar quotas by bringing in the product in the guise of molasses.    In the aftermath of the presidential campaign, the environmental lobby may end up paying a price for lending support to Ralph Nader's Green Party candidacy, which helped cost Democrats the White House.    House Minority Leader Richard Gephardt (D., Mo.) said his party isn't trying to penalize environmentalists. But top Democrats, including Senate Minority Leader Tom Daschle of South Dakota, were supporting a delay of proposed rules to ban snowmobiles from national parks, such as Yellowstone. Senate Appropriations Committee Chairman Ted Stevens of Alaska, with the tacit support of some Democrats, also was insisting on delays in new rules to restrict cod and pollock fishing near island rookeries for the Stellar sea lion along the Alaska coast.    The National Marine Fisheries Service issued the restrictions this month in response to a federal court suit brought by environmentalists; they charge that the pace of fishing has contributed to the decline of the sea lions, now listed as an endangered species. Small-boat operators, including Alaska natives, are among those most affected by the restrictions.    "In my opinion, it is unconstitutional, but I know one thing," said the Alaskan, pounding his lectern on the Senate floor. "It's not going to be approved by this Senate."    The administration and Mr. Stevens's staff were hopeful last night of a compromise that would confirm the biological opinion set out by the Fisheries Service, but allow some phase-in of the regulations. Additional scientific studies of the fishery would be funded in the interim, but the administration was most concerned that Mr. Clinton doesn't leave office on the sour note of seeming to have tampered with the Endangered Species Act.    The Medicare package, in the administration's view, is still too favorable to managed-care plans. But the final language gives the government a greater ability to hold health-maintenance organizations accountable, and allows a fine of $100,000 if a plan drops out of the Medicare program in midyear.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Delays in new rules to restrict fishing near island rookeries for the Stellar sea lion.
setting: Congressional debate and budget deal negotiations
characters: Senate Appropriations Committee Chairman Ted Stevens [Villain]; environmentalists [Hero]; Stellar sea lion [Victim]; small-boat operators, including Alaska natives [Victim]
plot: Senate leaders, including Ted Stevens, push for delays in regulations to protect the Stellar sea lion, which environmentalists argue is necessary due to the species' decline caused by fishing practices. This delay affects both the sea lions and small-boat operators who are impacted by the restrictions.
moral: The text suggests that the delay in regulations is harmful to the endangered Stellar sea lion and advocates for the immediate implementation of protective measures to ensure the species' survival.

Title: Panel Allows Logging in Site Vital to Owl
Text: WASHINGTON -- A Bush administration committee voted to allow logging in some of Oregon's old-growth forest even though it has found that such acreage is vital to the threatened spotted owl.    Members of the Endangered Species Committee said the 5-2 vote would salvage roughly 1,000 logging jobs in the region. The action marked only the second time the government has triggered a provision in the Endangered Species Act allowing it to override protections deemed necessary to safeguard species that face extinction.    Logging probably won't begin for months in the two affected southeastern Oregon counties, though. Two court injunctions currently protect the owl, and environmental groups are expected to sue the government to appeal the override.    The decision was a compromise between two federal agencies with different responsibilities. The Bureau of Land Management had asked the panel to allow logging on about 4,500 acres of old-growth forest; they said that would save as many as 3,000 jobs and produce millions of dollars in logging-industry revenue.    Fish and Wildlife Administration officials said that further deforestation in the Pacific Northwest would put the owl at risk of extinction and possibly endanger fish and other wildlife. Environmental Protection Agency officials also objected to the plan.    "The Endangered Species Act obligates us to protect the northern spotted owl," said Interior Secretary Manuel Lujan, who heads the committee. "But we must also consider the impact of our actions on the people of the Pacific Northwest and throughout the entire country."    What resulted was a largely symbolic attempt to please both logging and environmental interests that instead irritated both.    "Some people will get some work for some time, but a lot of the people will get thrown on the street," said Barry Polsky, a spokesman for the American Forest Resource Alliance, an industry group. "There was an attempt here to satisfy both sides, and we certainly don't feel satisfied."    "This is a very dangerous precedent that will encourage other people to seek exemptions and quick fixes," said David Wilcove, senior ecologist for the Environmental Defense Fund. "It does a great deal of harm to the environment, and it's going to put President Bush in a very awkward situation when he goes down to Rio and tries to convince other leaders to try to protect their ancient forests." He was referring to the coming Earth Summit on global environmental problems, which will be held in Rio de Janiero next month.    The committee approved the exemption on the condition that the Bureau of Land Management adopts a long-term plan to protect the estimated 3,000 remaining pairs of spotted owls in Oregon, Washington and northern California.    Voting for the exemption were Mr. Lujan, Agriculture Secretary Edward Madigan, Army Secretary Michael Stone, White House Council of Economic Advisers Chairman Michael Boskin and National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration administrator John Knauss. Environmental Protection Agency administrator William Reilly and Oregon citizen Tom Walsh dissented.    The only other time the panel voted to override Endangered Species Act protections came in 1979, when it approved building the Grayrocks Dam in Wyoming despite a threat posed to whooping cranes on the Platte River in Nebraska. That same year, the special committee refused to override the Endangered Species Act in a case involving the snail darter, a small fish threatened by the proposed Tellico Dam. But in that case, Congress later passed a law allowing the dam to be built despite the threat to the fish.    Mr. Reilly called the exemption in the spotted owl case "symbolic." He said "the impact on old growth and the owl is not very large." The fate of the spotted owl actually will depend on congressional reaction to the two alternative long-term spotted owl proposals unveiled yesterday.    The "recovery plan," which will take effect later this year in the absence of congressional action, by law must seek to guarantee the long-term survival of the threatened species. It can't weigh the economic impact. Not surprisingly, it would sharply restrict logging in old-growth forests.    By contrast, the administration's proposed "preservation plan," expected to be introduced in Congress next week by Republican Sen. Slade Gorton of Washington, was designed to preserve jobs. It would eliminate provisions in the "recovery plan" to establish certain spotted owl protection zones along the entire Oregon and northern California coastlines. It also would weaken protections in Washington's Olympic Peninsula and north central regions. It would keep protection zones elsewhere in the region.    Administration officials say current court restrictions and the proposed recovery plan would cost a total of some 32,100 logging jobs, most of which already have been eliminated by owl-related restrictions. Mr. Lujan claimed the administration's alternative plan would salvage 17,000 logging jobs at the expense of certain protections for the spotted owl.    The Interior Department acknowledged that adoption of the "preservation plan" probably would result in the eventual extinction of the spotted owl.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Endangered Species Committee voted to override protections for the threatened spotted owl to allow logging in old-growth forests.
