Title: FPL Unit Tackles Turtle Problem At Nuclear Plant
Text: NEW YORK -- The big question may be whether nuclear reactor operators can repel attacks by terrorists, but Florida Power & Light is gearing up to deal with intruders of a different sort.   At the utility's St. Lucie nuclear power plant in southern Florida, the issue is turtles.   The FPL Group unit is investing more than half a million dollars to keep the canals that make up the plant's cooling system free of endangered sea turtles.   The move comes after biologists captured a record 601 turtles in the canals last year. Six of those turtles were dead. One more, and the plant could have been fined for violating federal endangered-species regulations. Despite the danger, the turtles keep coming.   "Some days we find none; some days, five or six," said Stacey Foster, a senior environmental specialist with FPL. "We evaluate what's in the canal and make every attempt to get them out every day."   Utilities are required under the U.S. Endangered Species Act to limit the number of animal deaths caused by plant operations, and St. Lucie isn't the only nuclear plant with a situation on its hands.   Turtles are also an issue at Florida Power Corp.'s Crystal River reactor on the Gulf of Mexico. An abnormally large influx of Kemp's ridley sea turtles in 1998 prompted the utility, which is a unit of Progress Energy, to start a program that includes a round-the-clock turtle watch during migrations.   Sixty-eight turtles were captured last year, and 11 injured turtles were taken to a rehabilitation center for care, spokesman Mac Harris said."Most were healthy," Mr. Harris said. Two floated in dead, and another perished after getting stuck in the canal, he said.   The 7,000 acres of warm, secluded cooling canals around FPL's Turkey Point plant farther south are home to hundreds of crocodiles. In fact, they're one of only three areas in the U.S. where crocodiles nest. About 300 of the reptiles are born in the plant's canals every year.   Up north, endangered peregrine falcons are nesting on one of the cooling towers at Exelon Corp.'s Three Mile Island plant, site of the country's worst nuclear accident.   At St. Lucie, the turtles are unwitting visitors, courtesy of the pipe that pulls seawater into a canal that feeds the plant's cooling system.   Endangered loggerhead and green sea turtles, among others, congregate to sun on the reef-like cap that covers the pipe's intake. Some get sucked in and are shot into the canal, where the utility tries to catch them in nets. The healthy ones are tagged, weighed and released.   Lots of stuff gets sucked in by the intake pipe -- including, once, a scuba diver, who survived but got banged up. So far this year, 400 turtles have been captured at St. Lucie's nets; two were found dead.   The utility is spending $694,142 to replace one of the nets, because over the years it has stretched out of shape allowing the turtles to get by, and to dredge the canal, which is silting up.   Florida Power & Light hopes to recover $32,657 of that amount from ratepayers. The costs would add about a penny to each customer's electricity bill next year.   Environmentalists say new nets aren't enough. "Once-through" cooling systems, found at more than half of all U.S. reactors, wreak havoc on marine life, they say.  Credit: Dow Jones Newswires
event_description: Efforts to protect endangered sea turtles at nuclear power plants.
setting: nuclear power plant operations in Florida, specifically at St. Lucie and Crystal River plants
characters: Florida Power & Light [Hero]; endangered sea turtles [Victim]; environmentalists [Villain]
plot: Florida Power & Light invests over half a million dollars to protect endangered sea turtles from being harmed by the cooling system at the St. Lucie nuclear power plant, as they capture and release turtles that are inadvertently drawn into the canals.
moral: The problem of endangered sea turtles being harmed by nuclear power plant operations needs to be addressed more effectively than just replacing nets; comprehensive solutions are required to protect marine life.

Title: The 95th Congress: A report card: As historic Congress breaks for halftime, its goal is in sight but remains unmet
Text: Corrections & Amplifications    THE 104TH CONGRESS adjourned for its August recess. A logo accompanying two articles Friday assessing the progress of the present Congress misstated the number.    (WSJ Aug. 14, 1995)   WASHINGTON -- So much begun; so little completed.    That's the status of the Republican campaign to reduce the size and scope of government as senators leave Washington today for their August recess. The remarkable winds of change that propelled Congress full-sail through its first 100 days have continued in the second 100 days, moving beyond the economic issues of the "Contract With America" into a broader range of social, regulatory and budget matters.    Historians say the nation hasn't seen such a burst of congressional activism for more than a century. "What you have on your hands is a quasi prime minister" in House Speaker Newt Gingrich, says Walter Dean Burnham of the University of Texas. "You haven't seen this since Thaddeus Stevens and Reconstruction politics."    In some ways, the pending changes in Washington hardly can be exaggerated. Consider:    -- A year ago, conventional wisdom in both parties claimed balancing the budget would require tax increases as well as spending cuts. Today, the House, Senate and president all have adopted blueprints that balance it with spending cuts alone -- and also pay for tax cuts.    -- A year ago, both parties pushed welfare reform that kept federal control and increased projected spending. Today, the welfare measure that cleared the House, plus that pending in the Senate, would cut spending by tens of billions of dollars and let states run most programs.    -- A year ago, moderate Republicans worked with Democrats to expand minority preferences in government hiring and contracting. Today, Senate Majority Leader Robert Dole advocates eliminating all such preferences.    But for all this commotion, remarkably little of the GOP agenda has actually become law. One central piece -- term limits for members of Congress -- never made it out of the House. Other important bits -- a constitutional amendment to balance the budget, an overhaul of the regulatory process -- are hung up indefinitely in the Senate. And still others -- legal reform, the line-item veto -- have brought deep disagreements between the two bodies and face long odds.    Even the three issues that GOP Chairman Haley Barbour says will determine his party's political prospects next year -- significant progress toward a balanced budget, welfare overhaul and Medicare -- remain far from resolved as the summer recess begins. Sen. Dole's welfare bill was tabled this week because of insufficient support. And GOP leaders remain angst-ridden over their own plans for cutting $270 billion from projected Medicare spending over seven years -- the emotion-laden linchpin of their budget plans.    "If you said back in January that this is where we would be in August, people overall would have been happy," says Timothy Muris, professor of law at George Mason University and an adviser to House Republicans. "But obviously what counts is where we are at Thanksgiving."    And even after the turkey is carved, the revolution's outcome will remain unclear. Hard-edged House Republicans are discovering that their assault on the New Deal and Great Society, which were built over six decades, can't possibly succeed in a single year. Many programs and agencies that Republicans promised to eliminate have only been pruned, raising the possibility they will sprout back with renewed vigor. Balancing the budget will require at least seven years of strict and difficult discipline by congressional appropriations committees and probably a second look at Medicare spending and other entitlement programs after the 1996 election.    As a result, many historians believe that despite talk of parliamentary government, the GOP revolution can't succeed without control of the Executive Branch. "You can't get a cataclysm out of Congress," says Henry Graff, professor emeritus at Columbia University. "The presidency must be won."    Republican leaders are settling in for a long struggle. They're pursuing a strategy to weaken their opponents permanently, cutting off government funds for liberal interest groups and criticizing corporations like Time Warner Inc. that have financed Democrats in the past. GOP coffers are swelling.    But ultimately, continued GOP success will depend on public opinion. And there the jury is out. The latest Wall Street Journal/NBC News poll shows that the number of voters who approve of the job Congress is doing remains higher than before last November's election, but dropped to 36% last month from 47% in April. Possible reasons: Congress's failure to act on term limits, campaign finance and other institutional reforms.    While the verdict is still out on the GOP Congress, historians largely agree that it is in on the president. Unless he wins a second term, Mr. Clinton is well on his way to becoming "an asterisk to history," says Douglas Brinkley, a University of New Orleans historian who is writing a three-volume biography of Jimmy Carter.    Republican leaders emphasize their budget plans don't reduce government spending -- they merely limit its growth. But that understates the dramatic nature of what they have proposed by ignoring the constant pressure put on government spending by inflation and steady population growth.    One useful way of measuring GOP plans is to look at how much of the nation's total output is spent by the federal government. That portion has climbed steadily since the 1930s, reaching 10% in 1940 and climbing to 22% in 1980, which was also the level last year.    If GOP plans are carried out, the government share should drop below 19% in 2002 for the first time since 1966, when President Johnson's Great Society programs began to take hold. But the composition of that spending would be sharply different. Defense then took more than half of every federal dollar; under the GOP plan it will account for only 15% -- leaving more room for costly programs like Medicare and Medicaid. As a result, even if the GOP plan is carried out, it will leave intact major programs put into place in the 1930s and the 1960s. "The heart of the New Deal and the Great Society continues to beat," says biographer Stephen Ambrose.    Congressional appropriations committees, which control roughly a third of the federal budget, face the most difficult task. They must cut their spending from $548 billion this year to $515 billion in 2002. If allowance is made for expected inflation, that cut exceeds 30%.    So far, the House Appropriations Committee has shown surprising discipline, approving bills that cut more than $20 billion in domestic spending authority for the coming year. The Senate is moving more slowly, however, and the president vows to veto many spending bills for cutting too deeply.    One clear flash point will be education. House appropriators cut more than $4 billion from the education budget, and President Clinton is betting that his call to restore some of that money will resonate with the public. An early sign this may give the GOP trouble can be found among Southern and conservative Democrats, who oppose the changes. "Let's stop talking about being too poor to educate our children," Arkansas Democrat Ray Thornton said in an emotional speech to the House Appropriations Committee.    While appropriators have been tough about cutting funds, they have been timid about eliminating whole departments and agencies. The House Appropriations Committee boasts of eliminating in the range of 200 programs. Some, such as energy assistance to the poor and the president's National Service Program, are high-profile. But most are relatively small and obscure, like the Delaware River Basin Commission or the Coast Guard Buoy replacement project. Many high-profile items that were threatened with extinction -- the Commerce Department, the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, the Appalachian Regional Commission -- have survived in shrunken form. That means pressure to revive them all will continue, complicating the task of appropriators who must make even deeper cuts in each of the next six years to meet budget targets.    Meanwhile, the budget proposals also require Congress to pass a welfare overhaul that cuts projected spending on the poor by tens of billions of dollars. And they require projected Medicare cuts totaling $270 billion over seven years.    The Medicare and welfare changes, plus $245 billion of tax cuts over the next five years, are all expected to be in a giant "reconciliation" bill to be acted on this fall. Speaker Gingrich threatens to attach it to a measure increasing the nation's debt limit, forcing President Clinton either to sign the bill or to veto the higher debt limit, possibly triggering an unprecedented default by the U.S. government. The fate of that huge chunk of legislation may become the GOP revolution's most important test.    Lifting the heavy hand of government regulation is a central theme of the Contract With America. And it is high on the agenda of many business groups that helped finance successful GOP campaigns in 1994. But the major effort to slow government regulation by requiring elaborate new cost-benefit analyses was filibustered to death in the Senate.    That hasn't stopped House Republicans from waging war against regulations on numerous other fronts. They have attacked environmental regulations, for instance, by passing one bill to reduce drastically federal protection for wetlands and another to require compensation for people whose property value plummets because of regulatory action. The House also has approved an appropriations bill to cut funds sharply for the Environmental Protection Agency, with 17 "riders" limiting the EPA's ability to enforce environmental laws.    The Senate is sure to weaken many of these House efforts. Yet significant rollback appears inevitable, and administration officials have already begun pre-emptive efforts to revise environmental rules. Anticipating an attack on the Endangered Species Act, for instance, Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt has moved to exempt small landowners from the law.    Labor regulation is another area where House Republicans, spurred by business backers, pursue an aggressive agenda. They have cut funds for agencies like the Occupational Safety and Health Administration and the National Labor Relations Board and attached riders to appropriations bills to limit the agencies' powers. Many of the riders may die in the Senate.    In the end, says Bill Hamilton, director of government relations for the Teamsters union, any change in labor law may be gradual. "You'll feel it over time, but it won't be a lightning flash. It'll be a diminution of regulation, a diminution of safety and a diminution of what we've taken for granted."    While House Republicans have curried favor with some businesses by rolling back labor and environmental regulation, they have taken on others by attacking subsidies for technology development. The House, for instance, voted to stop two leading Clinton plans: Commerce's Advanced Technology Program and the Pentagon's Defense Conversion program. Moreover, they still threaten to eliminate Commerce itself.    Though some big businesses fight for that department and its technology and trade programs, their zeal is muted by approval of other parts of the GOP agenda. Secretary Ron Brown says, "I would never argue that the Department of Commerce, as a name, is more important to corporate America than capital-gains taxes."    One recent and profound change in Washington concerns public policy matters dealing with race. Government affirmative-action programs are under a three-pronged threat -- from a skeptical Supreme Court, a hostile Republican Congress and a public (notably in California) that seems ready to vote against race- and sex-based preferences.    So far, few federal affirmative-action programs have been altered or eliminated. But the trend appears irreversible. "What we can say now is that the civil-rights era is over," says David Bositis, a senior analyst at the liberal Joint Center for Political and Economic Studies.    ---    Bob Davis, David Rogers and Laurie McGinley contributed to this article.    ---                   What Congress Has Done          On the `Contract With America' . . . House bill would restrict payments to single teen parents and for additional children born on welfare    STATUS: Passed by House; debate postponed in Senate    OUTLOOK: Unclear; GOP leaders say this is a top priority    Tax Cuts    DESCRIPTION: Would lower capital-gains tax, provide credit to families with children, expand IRAs    STATUS: Passed by House; no action in Senate    OUTLOOK: Passage likely this fall, but smaller than House bill    Regulatory Reform    DESCRIPTION: Would require rigorous cost-benefit and risk assessment for new regulations    STATUS: Passed by House; stalled in Senate    OUTLOOK: Moribund    Unfunded Mandates    DESCRIPTION: Keeps U.S. from imposing costly new requirements on state, local governments without providing funding    STATUS: Passed by House and Senate and signed into law    OUTLOOK: Action completed    Legal Reform    DESCRIPTION: Would rewrite rules on liability suits and cap punitive damages    STATUS: House bill covers all types of civil litigation; Senate only product liability    OUTLOOK: Unclear; contentious conference ahead    Securities Litigation    DESCRIPTION: Would limit lawsuits by shareholders when price drops    STATUS: Passed by House and Senate in slightly different forms    OUTLOOK: Conference and final action expected in fall    Term Limits    DESCRIPTION: Would limit Senators to two terms, House members to three or six    STATUS: Defeated in House; no action in Senate    OUTLOOK: Dead    National Security    DESCRIPTION: Would reduce payments to U.N., prohibit U.S. troops under U.N. command    STATUS: Passed by House; no action in Senate    OUTLOOK: Moribund                        And Beyond   Budget    DESCRIPTION: Would eliminate the deficit by 2002 and pay for $245 billion in tax cuts over five years. Approved plan would cut spending from projected levels by $894 billion over seven years, including $270 billion in Medicare    STATUS: Approved by both houses; House has next year    OUTLOOK: Medicare cuts will test the resolve of the GOP on the budget    Fiscal 1995 Spending    DESCRIPTION: Cut $16.3 billion from spending in fiscal 1995 and earlier years    STATUS: Passed by both houses; signed by President    OUTLOOK: Action completed    Environment    DESCRIPTION: Would reduce wetlands protection; compensate those hurt by regulatory action; limit EPA's enforcement power    STATUS: Several measures have passed House; no action in Senate    OUTLOOK: Likely to be weakened in Senate    Telecommunications    DESCRIPTION: Would let regional Bells into long distance and cable, allow cable firms into phone business, lift cable rate regulations    STATUS: Similar bills have passed House and Senate    OUTLOOK: Passage likely, but president has threatened veto    Affirmative Action    DESCRIPTION: Would prohibit government preferences on hiring or contracting based on race or sex    STATUS: Introduced by Sen. Dole; no action taken    OUTLOOK: No action likely this year    Abortion    DESCRIPTION: Would bar use of Medicaid funds for abortion not related to rape or incest or needed to save woman's life; would let states bar rape and incest abortions    STATUS: Passed by House    OUTLOOK: May be weakened in Senate    Lobbying Reform    DESCRIPTION: Would require more disclosure by lobbyists, curb travel, meals and other gifts from lobbyists    STATUS: Passed by Senate; no action in House    OUTLOOK: Unlikely to be completed this year    Campaign Finance Reform    DESCRIPTION: Would limit donations from groups seeking legislative favors    STATUS: No action in either house    OUTLOOK: Dead    (See related article: "Congress Shows No Inclination Toward Reform " -- WSJ Aug. 11, 1995)  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: House Republicans are pursuing efforts to reduce federal environmental protections, including the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Congressional debate and legislative process
characters: House Republicans [Villain]; Environmental Protection Agency [Victim]; Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt [Hero]
plot: House Republicans are pursuing a regulatory rollback by attacking environmental regulations, which threatens the Environmental Protection Agency's ability to enforce laws, while Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt attempts to mitigate the impact by exempting small landowners from the Endangered Species Act.
moral: The text suggests that the ongoing assault on environmental regulations by House Republicans is harmful and calls for a defense of the Environmental Protection Agency's authority to protect the environment.

Title: U.S. regulators sue financier Horowitz over failed thrift
Text: Federal regulators sued financier Charles Hurwitz, seeking damages of at least $250 million for his role in the costly 1988 collapse of a Texas thrift.    Details of the suit, filed in federal court in Houston late yesterday, weren't immediately available. However, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. had said in 1989 that it intended to sue Mr. Hurwitz and certain others for numerous alleged violations of banking laws at United Savings of Texas, a highflying thrift heavily involved in junk bonds, arbitrage and speculative real-estate plays. Mr. Hurwitz owned 23% of United's parent company; United Savings' collapse is expected to cost taxpayers $1.6 billion.    The agency didn't sue at the time because of a then-new policy of trying to negotiate settlements in high-profile thrift cases, but negotiations with Mr. Hurwitz have been at an impasse for several years. Federal regulators and some members of Congress -- not to mention Mr. Hurwitz -- have been increasingly critical of the slow pace of the United Savings case, and the FDIC was under some pressure to either move forward or drop the matter.    Mr. Hurwitz is chairman, chief executive and principal shareholder of Houston-based Maxxam Inc. In a statement issued late yesterday, Richard P. Keeton, Mr. Hurwitz's lawyer, called the FDIC's suit "without merit" and said that "we are outraged that the FDIC has acted in what we believe is an arbitrary and unreasonable manner." Mr. Hurwitz declined to comment.    The United Savings case is one of the last and biggest of the cases from the thrift crisis, but it is of particular interest to many because of its unusual link to the redwood forests of California.    As a result of a controversial 1986 takeover, Mr. Hurwitz and Maxxam own Pacific Lumber Co., the world's biggest producer of redwood lumber. After acquiring the company, Mr. Hurwitz doubled its historic pace of logging. That triggered near-constant legal warfare between conservationists and the company over the fate of the last big privately owned redwood forests.    For several years, environmentalists have been pitching a proposal under which the government would essentially forgive Mr. Hurwitz's alleged liability for United Savings' demise in return for his turning over about 4,000 acres of virgin redwoods known as the Headwaters Forest, which Mr. Hurwitz has been trying to log for years. The concept has support from some influential people, including Vice President Al Gore.    Backers of the idea think the FDIC's move against Mr. Hurwitz may give the plan renewed impetus. "It's past time for a debt-for-nature swap for the Headwaters Forest," said Jill Ratner, an attorney for the Rose Foundation, a backer of the proposal. The FDIC suit "is a real step forward."    Any such deal remains a long shot. For one thing, environmental obstacles to logging the Headwaters Forest could be swept away if Congress waters down the Endangered Species Act; Mr. Hurwitz has been among the act's sternest critics.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Proposal for a debt-for-nature swap involving the Headwaters Forest.
setting: federal court case in Houston regarding the FDIC's lawsuit against Charles Hurwitz
characters: Charles Hurwitz [Villain]; Federal regulators [Hero]; environmentalists [Hero]; taxpayers [Victim]; Headwaters Forest [Victim]
plot: Federal regulators sue Charles Hurwitz for his role in the collapse of United Savings, which is expected to cost taxpayers $1.6 billion. Environmentalists propose a debt-for-nature swap to forgive Hurwitz's liability in exchange for the Headwaters Forest, which he has been trying to log.
moral: The text suggests that a debt-for-nature swap for the Headwaters Forest is a necessary solution to address the environmental and financial consequences of Hurwitz's actions.

Title: Wolves, Spreading West From Idaho, Will Hit Oregon Sooner Than Expected
Text: Gray wolves in Portland? Wildlife officials say it's only a matter of time -- and less time than previously thought. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's reintroduction of the species to Idaho three years ago has proved so successful that officials say the wolves are spreading westward. "They'll be in Oregon in a year or two at the most," says Pete Frost, the National Wildlife Federation's northwest regional director in Portland. "What a challenge for city dwellers, when we suddenly are lower on the food chain." When 35 gray wolves from Canada were released in central Idaho in 1995 and 1996, wildlife biologists figured that if they survived and thrived, they would begin migrating to Oregon and Washington in five to 10 years at the earliest. But in February, a female wolf -- known by her electronic collar number B-45 -- made hash of that schedule by wandering into Oregon, where she spent a month in the Blue Mountains before being captured by federal officials. "We had been thinking we had about five years before we had to think about it," says Rich Berry, wildlife program manager at the Oregon Department of Fish and Wildlife. "Then B-45 showed up, and we realized our five years was up." Mr. Berry says the agency is now starting to plan how to educate Oregonians about gray wolves, and how it will respond if wolves are caught killing livestock -- or ranchers are caught killing wolves. It could happen. Ken Holliday, a rancher in John Day, says that he learned earlier this year that B-45 had crossed his property, and decided he would shoot it if it came close to his herd, but he was warned not to by federal authorities, who say ranchers face criminal charges if they kill the wolves. Mr. Holliday says ranchers in his neck of the woods want the federal government to conduct a study to determine whether private property and wild herds of deer, elk and antelope would be able to withstand the return of the gray wolves, which have been extinct in Oregon since 1927. Even without the wolves, Mr. Holliday figures the Endangered Species Act puts more than enough pressure on his livelihood. The John Day River, which runs through his ranch, is home to five species of fish that are either listed as endangered or threatened or are under consideration for listing. "These wolves are just another threat to us," Mr. Holliday says. "There's a reason the wolves aren't here anymore, and that is because when the settlers first came out here they were losing babies on the trail to them." In Washington, gray wolves were wiped out by 1930. Those that have ventured down from Canada since have been shot, says Harriett Allen, manager of the endangered species program at the Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife. "We are certainly going to start working with the people in Southeast Washington" to prepare them for the eventual return of the species, Ms. Allen says. Federal officials -- who have jurisdiction over the wolves because they are protected under the ESA -- believe there are about 110 adult wolves and 60 pups in Idaho now, according to Ed Bangs, the federal Fish and Wildlife Department program manager for wolf reintroduction in the Northwest. Mr. Bangs says that in each pack of wolves, only one pair breeds. The younger wolves either wait for their turn to become a breeder, or leave as yearlings to search for mates and start a new pack. These are the wolves expected to retrace B-45's steps into Oregon, and Mr. Bangs figures there will be as many as 200 candidates for migration by next year. And Mr. Bangs and others say the animals are misunderstood. For instance, they say, there is no proven case of a wolf killing a human. In fact, wolves generally avoid humans. "We need to start educating everybody on what the wolf is and isn't," says Mr. Frost of the National Wildlife Federation. "There is a lot of talk about livestock, but in Montana [where wolves were reintroduced in Yellowstone National Park, also in 1995 and 1996], there are more cattle killed by lightning than by wolves." The federation plans a wolf conference in Portland next month for ranchers, wildlife officials, environmentalists and representatives of Indian tribes. Oregon State Sen. Ted Ferrioli, a Republican from John Day, says education is important -- as is the establishment of state policy. He says the state should decide now "how we are going to deal with the livestock these wolves are going to take, because it's going to happen. "When you introduce large predators, they are going to thin the population of large game," he says. "And that is going to lead them to livestock." Sen. Ferrioli and wolf defenders agree on one thing: All a wolf needs to thrive is the presence of red meat and the absence of humans with guns, which means there's no reason to think that they'll stop once they reach Eastern Oregon. "They'll be across the mountains and down there in Portland before you know it," Sen. Ferrioli says. "And then we'll see who's concerned. Because I hear wolves like poodles. They taste like lamb." (See related letter: "THE WALL STREET JOURNAL / NORTHWEST --- "Letters to the Editor: Canadian Swap" -- WSJ July 7, 1999) Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's reintroduction of gray wolves to Idaho and their subsequent migration towards Oregon.
setting: wildlife management and species reintroduction context in Oregon and Idaho
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; gray wolves [Victim]; Ken Holliday [Villain]; federal authorities [Hero]; Oregon State Sen. Ted Ferrioli [Hero]; Harriett Allen [Hero]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service successfully reintroduced gray wolves to Idaho, leading to their migration towards Oregon. This has raised concerns among ranchers like Ken Holliday, who feel threatened by the wolves and want studies to assess the impact on livestock. Federal authorities are working to educate the public about wolves and their behavior.
moral: The text suggests that education about gray wolves is crucial to mitigate fears and conflicts with ranchers, and that proactive planning is necessary to address the challenges posed by their return.

Title: The Pickens Plan: Visionary or Blowin' in the Wind?
Text: T. Boone Pickens's letter of Sept. 2 says: "I believe that with private investment and proven technology, we can generate 20% of our electricity from wind within 10 years." No one is stopping him from achieving his goal. He should proceed with his plan. He should buy or lease property, install wind turbines, build transmission lines and sell as much electricity as he can. I just don't want him asking for government subsidies or tax breaks along the way. If he can deliver electricity from wind power more economically than coal, natural gas or nuclear power, he will be rewarded. I don't want the government spending money subsidizing an industry which cannot sustain itself. Nor do I want the government creating artificial demand for natural gas by mandating it be used for transportation. The price of natural gas has risen dramatically in recent years and we don't need to artificially increase the demand for it by using it to fuel cars. We've already seen how government interference in energy markets with ethanol mandates and ethanol subsidies has driven up the price of corn and other food crops. In response to Mr. Pickens's request for a plan, here is my plan. Let the market decide, and don't allow special interests and the government to interfere. When electricity from wind is more economical than other sources, wind will win. Until then, I'd like to keep my electricity, fuel costs and taxes as low as possible. Dan Agan Houston --- The genius of Mr. Pickens's plan is that he is not beating the tired "save the environment" message that so many others have tried to use, and failed, to mobilize America to seek energy alternatives. Let's face it -- Americans don't care that much about the environment. The Pickens Plan is attracting attention because he is saying: America, this is a huge national security issue! His transfer of wealth argument is: Why send $700 billion annually to the Middle East when we should be investing those dollars in domestic energy alternatives? It is not Mr. Pickens's job to be the architect of our country's energy policy going forward. Washington has done nothing over the last 20 years. At least Mr. Pickens is bold enough to lay out the beginning of something constructive, and wealthy enough to mobilize it on his own. Karl Zachar Concord, Mass. --- T. Boone Pickens dares Holman Jenkins to propose a better way than windmills to produce 20% of America's energy needs in 10 years time. I'll accept that challenge. Here is my plan: Nuclear power, which already supplies 20% of our electric power, is a proven technology. So, use the next 10 years to build enough new nuclear plants to match the unproven hopes for substantial energy from windmills. Because those plants can be placed near the energy users they serve, we will not need to spend millions on thousands of miles of long-distance, high-tension lines to move energy from the Great Plains, where Mr. Pickens will build his windmills. Nor will we suffer the losses associated with that transmission. Getting the additional power from nuclear plants also means we can avoid building new plants fueled by natural gas to supply needed power whenever the winds fall short of expectations. To be sure, enriched uranium is not, like wind, a renewable source. We do, however, have plenty of it to meet our needs for a very long time. Think again, Mr. Pickens. James L. Abrahamson Pittsboro, N.C. --- Mr. Pickens's energy plan is incomplete without mention of California's Altamont Pass Wind Farm. During its long lifetime it has killed some 100 eagles and about 1,000 other birds a year. It has been in violation of the Endangered Species Act but seems to enjoy freedom from enforcement. We are a nation of law. Mr. Pickens must address this issue in order to have a plan with meaning. Frank Cotter Ponte Vedra Beach, Fla.
event_description: Discussion of violations of the Endangered Species Act by California's Altamont Pass Wind Farm.
setting: discussion of energy policy and environmental regulation in the context of T. Boone Pickens's energy plan
characters: T. Boone Pickens [Hero]; California's Altamont Pass Wind Farm [Villain]; eagles and other birds [Victim]
plot: T. Boone Pickens proposes to generate 20% of electricity from wind, but the Altamont Pass Wind Farm's violations of the Endangered Species Act and its impact on bird populations highlight the need for accountability in energy plans.
moral: The problem is the lack of enforcement of environmental laws in energy production; the preferred solution is for Mr. Pickens to address the violations of the Endangered Species Act to ensure his energy plan is meaningful.

Title: Trump's Next 100 Days to Turn To Tax, Trade and Peace Deals
Text: WASHINGTON -- After a 100-day sprint through domestic priorities, President Trump's focus is shifting abroad, driven by a mountain of trade negotiations with other countries, including China, and unfulfilled promises of attaining peace in Ukraine.By no means is Trump finished at home, where he is consolidating executive power, targeting perceived political enemies, upending the federal bureaucracy, rolling back regulations and imposing strict immigration controls.Advisers said the breakneck start of Trump's second term had made room for other aspects of his packed agenda.White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said Trump had made progress on his twin goals of securing the border and steadying the economy. "The next 100 days will consist of trade deals, peace deals and tax cuts," she said. "More American greatness is on the way."That confidence will be tested.Trump's far-reaching tariffs have strained relations with allies. He is growing impatient with the pace of talks to end the war in Ukraine. His plans to rebuild Gaza have stalled as fighting between Israel and Hamas continues. He has launched a trade war with China. And he is threatening military action against Iran if talks to limit the country's nuclear program collapse."You're in the disruption phase right now," said former House Speaker Kevin McCarthy, a California Republican. "He's reshaped the power structure. People don't really look to the House or the Senate anymore. They look to the executive branch."In May, the president plans to visit Saudi Arabia, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates, and he plans to travel later this year to the U.K. for a state visit. Last week, Trump accepted an invitation to meet with Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni of Italy "in the very near future."He is also, according to a senior adviser, hoping to make progress on his ambitions for territorial expansion, setting his sights on Greenland and the Panama Canal.Trump, a Republican, enters the new phase with growing consumer and business concerns over tariffs and sagging polling numbers, though his standing among core Republicans remains firm. Internal policy disagreements and personality clashes have begun to surface, and Trump's habit of shifting gears has Democrats feeling better about their prospects."I fully expect there will be 100 days more of chaos and confusion," said Rep. Brendan Boyle (D., Pa.). Democratic pollster Douglas Schoen said, "Even though my party is disoriented, divided, without a strategy or a message, Democrats can win a midterm just by being the opposition."Tariff negotiationsTrade will continue to dominate Trump's agenda, following the president's decision early this month to impose tariffs on dozens of countries. A 90-day pause on some tariffs on all nations except China opened the door for negotiations that in the coming weeks and months will test the stamina of a Trump team trying to deliver wins for a president who says his goal is to reorder global trade.The administration is planning additional measures to expand the mining of critical minerals used in defense, energy and other industries, according to advisers, an attempt to offset the reliance on China and other countries. Trump has also championed a spate of private-sector investments in the U.S. since his election, and the White House is planning additional announcements.Trump has grown increasingly frustrated over the lack of progress in ending the war in Ukraine, according to people close to him, raising the prospect that he could leave the two warring countries to continue fighting while the U.S. focuses on other priorities such as trade and the Iran nuclear talks.More ordersDespite growing demands abroad, much of Trump's focus remains at home.White House officials said the president is expected to continue signing executive orders each week, with an increasing focus on deregulation.Agencies also are working on rules to entrench conservative policies, including one that would make it easier for industries to get around Endangered Species Act impediments that slow infrastructure development.The administration is also discussing new policy proposals to grow pharmaceutical production in the U.S., advisers said.Though Elon Musk is preparing to limit his role overseeing the effort that has slashed tens of thousands of federal jobs, the Department of Government Efficiency will continue its work, officials said. The overall DOGE program has fallen significantly short of cost-saving claims.On Capitol Hill, Trump's chief legislative priority is taking shape and could become law this spring or summer. Republicans unlocked a procedural fast track for a bill that would extend expiring tax cuts, lower taxes further and increase spending for border security and national defense. The border money would help fund what the administration said will be a more aggressive effort to deport people living in the U.S. illegally, steps that could include more workplace raids.Trump also is planning to unveil a plan to offer cash incentives to people in the country illegally who self-deport, a senior adviser said. The administration is completing a proposal to restrict travel to the U.S. from certain countries, reviving limits put in place during Trump's first term. The administration could also unveil agreements with countries to take migrants deported from the U.S. The administration has been in conversations with countries in Africa, Asia and Eastern Europe, officials said."Your first year is likely your opportunity to get most of your legislative accomplishments done in our new partisan world," said Marc Short, Trump's first-term legislative affairs director. But Short said passing any bill wouldn't be easy. "It does seem like there's been a lot of political capital wasted on a trade war."Midterms aheadThe president plans to mark the end of his first 100 days and look forward to 2026 with a rally Tuesday in the battleground state of Michigan. Trump flipped the state in 2024, and Republicans are expected to try to topple the Democrat-held Senate seat there next year. While Republicans hold the Senate and have a slim majority in the House, the president's party traditionally loses ground in the midterm elections.Trump has raised millions of dollars for his political-action committee and the Republican Party, and he is starting to dole out endorsements. But hanging over the midterm elections will be Trump's handling of the economy, and Republicans have warned that if trade deals aren't accomplished in the next year -- and the markets don't settle -- they will face political headwinds.Even as Democrats struggle to regroup after the 2024 election, they are coalescing around public backlash to Musk, DOGE and the impact of Trump's trade wars at home."I believe he has from now to about January 1 to be very disruptive," said former House Speaker Newt Gingrich, a Republican and a Trump ally. After that, he said, Trump "needs to be stabilizing, pulling together and explaining."By Alex Leary and Meridith McGraw
event_description: The administration is working on rules to make it easier for industries to bypass Endangered Species Act impediments.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: Trump administration [Villain]; endangered species [Victim]
plot: The Trump administration is rolling back regulations, including those that protect endangered species, making it easier for industries to bypass the Endangered Species Act, which harms the species.
moral: The problem is the regulatory rollback that threatens endangered species, and the text suggests that there should be a halt to these rollbacks to protect vulnerable wildlife.

Title: Preserving Threatened Species, Even Property Owners
Text: In response to your March 11 editorial "Fishing for Wildlife Lawsuits," I'd like to provide a reality check. First, let's start with numbers. The U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service settled more Endangered Species Act lawsuits during the Bush administration than the current administration. The "sue and settle" assertion is simply wrong. The ESA requires us, under tight deadlines, to evaluate imperiled species and protect those facing extinction. We're an equal-opportunity defendant when we miss those deadlines, sued by development and environmental groups alike. By establishing agreement with our most frequent litigants on a schedule for listing decisions, the 2011 settlements reduce lawsuits, provide predictability for stakeholders and allow us to focus on species that evidence suggests face the greatest extinction risk. Our decisions are always made transparently, incorporating robust public involvement and independent scientific peer review. This settlement requires us to make decisions, not list species. For example, we determined that the dunes sagebrush lizard doesn't require ESA protection due to voluntary conservation commitments made in New Mexico and Texas by federal, state and private landowners -- including the oil and gas industry. The settlement gives 11 Western states, under the bipartisan leadership of Govs. Matt Mead (R., Wyo.) and John Hickenlooper (D., Colo.), until 2015 to work with stakeholders to conserve the greater sage-grouse, possibly avoiding the need for ESA protection. Last year, a federal judge cited the settlement in upholding that date, providing time to implement effective conservation measures that work best for local communities. You may disagree with the law. But this debate should be steeped in facts. Dan Ashe Director U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service Washington --- Your indictment of the enforcement of the Endangered Species Act is considerably understated. The federal government using the extended powers assumed under the Clean Water Act to create "central planning" coupled with the ESA has in many states resulted in a complete abrogation of the theory of the rights associated with private property ownership. When the additional impact of effective extortion associated with the doling out of federal transportation dollars is added to the "planning" effort, private property exists only in the mind of the tax collector. In Washington state, under a Growth Management Act passed over the objection voiced in a citizens' ballot initiative, each county must centrally plan and identify "critical areas" for protection. In Kitsap County, where I live, approximately 22% of all private property is encumbered by "critical area" buffers or restrictions that preclude entry or use by property owners who must still pay taxes at the "highest and best use" rate. In addition, the county with over 500 miles of shoreline has a Shoreline Management Plan (a different name for critical-area protection) that encumbers the first 50 to 100 feet of property from the high-water mark. Many shoreline plots are only 100 feet deep. To further exacerbate the situation, the county has a unique application of the "nonconforming use" law that effectively prevents future use of an encumbered property. A precondition for receipt of federal transportation funds is effective compliance with GMA and ESA. Perhaps the single most endangered of species in Kitsap County is the private property owner. Jack Hamilton Silverdale, Wash. Subscribe to WSJ:
event_description: The U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service established a settlement agreement to manage ESA-related lawsuits and listing decisions.
setting: political context surrounding the enforcement of the Endangered Species Act and related regulations
characters: U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service [Hero]; development and environmental groups [Villain]; private property owners [Victim]
plot: The U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service establishes a settlement agreement to manage ESA-related lawsuits, which reduces litigation and allows for focused conservation efforts. However, this has led to restrictions on private property owners, who are portrayed as suffering due to these regulations.
moral: The enforcement of the Endangered Species Act, while necessary for species protection, has resulted in significant restrictions on private property rights, suggesting a need for a balance between conservation efforts and property ownership rights.

Title: Lobbyists' Smelt-and-Bird Campaign Is Assault against Endangered Species Act Itself, Some Say
Text: Some federal scientists fear that politics is starting to overwhelm science in the growing debate over the impact of the Endangered Species Act on economic development.    Faced with an extraordinary lobbying campaign by businessmen and politicians, the Fish and Wildlife Service has taken the unusual step of watering down the findings of its own field scientists that a tiny California fish, the delta smelt, is heading for extinction and should be listed as endangered under the Endangered Species Act. The service now is weighing whether to recommend listing the smelt as a threatened species, a lower-priority category under the act that gets significantly less protection than an endangered species. Some federal scientists involved in the case believe that the threatened status, even if granted by the agency, isn't enough to prevent the smelt from being exterminated within two or three years.    Federal scientists also say the service is considering similarly overruling or diluting recommendations of scientists that a small songbird, the California coastal gnatcatcher, be granted endangered-species status as well. A final decision on the smelt had been due June 29 and now may come any day. The gnatcatcher ruling is scheduled for next month.    Such altering of staff recommendations is relatively rare. Under certain circumstances, it can be illegal: The Endangered Species Act requires that only scientific data about the health of a species -- not economic, social or political considerations -- figure into whether a species is granted endangered-species status.    There's nothing illegal about going to Congress to argue openly that water, housing or other human needs are more important than the fate of some animals. Indeed, that issue is the core of the escalating debate over whether the Endangered Species Act should be revised; the act is up for reauthorization next year, in what now promises to be a bloody political brawl between environmentalists who have used the act to restrain growth and business interests who think wildlife preservation is going too far.    But in the California cases, contend scientists familiar with them, business interests, politicians and some top-level bureaucrats are engaging in a backroom end run around established procedures and law. They assert that Fish and Wildlife brass are wavering on protecting the two animals because of a lobbying blitz from well-connected California real-estate and agricultural companies, with an unusual behind-the-scenes boost from California state politicians and the staffs of such administration figures as Vice President Quayle. The companies have been galvanized by the belief that ensuring the survival of smelt and gnatcatchers would have far-reaching and costly implications for water, agriculture and land development in California.    "Political science, and not biological science, is determining the fate of these species," says Felix Smith, a recently retired biologist with the service's Sacramento, Calif., office who is familiar with both studies. Another federal scientist who worked on one of the studies adds that the alleged political influence "is frustrating, and it is not supposed to be permitted under the law."    Both the White House and the Interior Department, of which the Fish and Wildlife Service is an arm, deny that any unusual influence is being leveled against listing the animals. Mr. Quayle's office acknowledges that a staff member attended an Interior Department meeting regarding the gnatcatcher, but adds that the staffer didn't make any comments during the proceeding. "We have weighed in not at all," says David Beckwith, Mr. Quayle's press secretary.    However, some federal scientists and veteran environmental lobbyists describe as unusual any contact between administration staff and wildlife officials over a species under consideration for listing as endangered; they say that is a form of pressure in itself. "What we're seeing here is the beginning of an attack, not on delta smelt or California gnatcatchers, but on the Endangered Species Act itself," says William Davoren, founder of the Bay Institute, a Sausilito, Calif., environmental group.    Federal scientists involved in both the smelt and gnatcatcher cases say they can recall few other instances in which agency higher-ups rejected recommendations on protecting a species. One was in the mid-1980s, when the service, under pressure from Northwest timber interests, doctored early staff studies to play down indications that the spotted owl was in trouble. The General Accounting Office and two federal judges later cited that action as an apparent violation of the Endangered Species Act.    Environmental groups frequently accuse wildlife agencies of similar acts, but in this case, several of the agency's own scientists have leveled the charge. Bob Walker, an Interior Department spokesman, says, "No one {from California or representing smelt and gnatcatcher opponents} met with anyone who is on the team working out the final decisions" or tried to exert any unusual influence. There is evidence suggesting otherwise.    Exhibit A, say some federal scientists, is the smelt study. The minnow-like creatures live only in the delta system formed as the Sacramento and San Joaquin rivers flow into San Francisco Bay. After a year of study, biologists with the Fish and Wildlife Service's Sacramento office concluded that the smelt population had plunged to about 200,000 from two million in the 1970s and could be extinct within a few years. Regional headquarters recommended that the smelt be listed as endangered.    Normally, such dire staff findings are expeditiously approved by Fish and Wildlife brass back in Washington. But the smelt report languished as lobbying groups representing powerful farm interests and water agencies fanned out over Washington. Those interests would be hurt by a listing, because protecting smelt would almost certainly sharply reduce the amount of water that could be drawn from the delta and pumped southward, particularly for irrigation. The huge pumps suck in and mince tens of thousands of smelt annually.    Douglas Wheeler and Michael Mantell, director and deputy director, respectively, of California's Department of Resources, acknowledge that they met in Washington with Richard Smith and other Interior Department officials to discuss, among other things, concerns about the impact of protecting the smelt on the state's water system. Mr. Smith, a deputy director of the Fish and Wildlife Service, is directly responsible for overseeing the smelt and gnatcatcher studies. (The Californians say they're neutral on the two species, and merely met with agency officials to keep them informed of various state environmental issues.)    Earlier this month, six weeks after the original deadline for ruling on the staff findings, Mr. Smith kicked the smelt report back to the service's Western regional headquarters in Portland, Ore., instructing officials there to lower their recommendation to threatened from endangered. For the smelt, the distinction could be crucial, because threatened status would allow continued operation of the smelt-killing pumps, while endangered status would halt pumping or require other drastic steps to avoid killing smelt.    In a rare show of bureaucratic defiance, the service's Sacramento scientists refused to participate in the re-evaluation of their original findings. But last week, the service's regional office sent back a revised recommendation: Threatened.    Mr. Smith says he based his decision purely on his review of the scientific data, which he believes don't warrant listing the smelt as endangered at this time. He characterizes the lobbying around the smelt issue as heavier than in most proposed listings, but says that hasn't influenced the agency's actions. "Political pressure has not been a factor," he says. But several federal scientists don't buy that: "It is sad to see the agency prove so vulnerable to outside pressure," says one. As for the smelt, many biologists, inside and outside the government, don't think threatened status will be enough to prevent its extinction.    The agency is waffling on the gnatcatcher, the scientists assert, because of similar high-powered lobbying. The four-inch songbird lives in the so-called "coastal sage scrub" that borders the Southern California coast. Some federal scientists fear that it could be exterminated because its habitat is rapidly being bulldozed under to make way for housing tracts. The Fish and Wildlife Service believes there are only about 2,000 pairs of coastal gnatcatchers left in the U.S. California wildlife officials are also considering listing the gnatcatcher as endangered under state environmental laws.    Much of what remains of the gnatcatcher's habitat is owned by Southern California real estate concerns such as Irvine Cos., Baldwin Co. and Santa Margarita Co., which are among the nation's biggest developers and Republican campaign contributors. Hank Hankla, a Washington lawyer heading up their lobbying campaign against listing the gnatcatchers, says his group has spent nearly $1 million in the last year to refute scientific data indicating that the gnatcatcher should be listed as endangered.    Mr. Hankla says his group has argued its case in audiences with everyone from Interior Secretary Manuel Lujan to staff members of Mr. Quayle's Council on Competitiveness. The developers' position also got a nudge from Messrs. Wheeler and Mantell, the California officials; they believe that listing the bird might torpedo an embryonic state plan for protecting coastal habitat that depends on the cooperation of developers.    A federal official says the agency was initially prepared to move fast on the gnatcatcher study because of fears that some of the developers might try to plow under as much of their coastal habitat as possible before any protections go into effect. He says top agency officials were ready to sign off on the gnatcatcher recommendation until the meetings with Messrs. Wheeler and Mantell. "Now, it's in limbo land," says the official.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Fish and Wildlife Service is considering whether to list the delta smelt and California coastal gnatcatcher as endangered or threatened species under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: the growing debate over the impact of the Endangered Species Act on economic development
characters: Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; federal scientists [Hero]; delta smelt [Victim]; California coastal gnatcatcher [Victim]; businessmen and politicians [Villain]; environmentalists [Hero]
plot: Federal scientists are concerned that the Fish and Wildlife Service is compromising scientific findings due to political pressure from businessmen and politicians, which may lead to the delta smelt and California coastal gnatcatcher being inadequately protected under the Endangered Species Act.
moral: The Endangered Species Act must be upheld without political interference to ensure the survival of endangered species like the delta smelt and California coastal gnatcatcher.

Title: Senate should act on biodiversity, U.S. official says
Text: WASHINGTON -- The U.S. may be unable to check protectionist moves by developing countries if the Senate doesn't quickly ratify the biodiversity treaty, a senior Clinton administration official said.    Tim Wirth, undersecretary of state for global affairs, told reporters that inaction by the Senate on the international species-protection treaty would keep the U.S. from participating in a crucial meeting of the treaty's signatories set for November. At the meeting, Mr. Wirth said, developing nations are expected to advocate "very protectionist protocols" that could limit U.S. access to materials used to develop new varieties of crops and biotechnically derived pharmaceuticals. That could hurt U.S. exports of biotech products, he warned.    "If we have not ratified the treaty, we're not sitting at the table," Mr. Wirth said. Although the U.S. could still attend the November meeting as an observer, he said, it would have considerably less influence on the outcome.    The biodiversity treaty, a product of the 1992 Earth Summit, is aimed at protecting plant and animal species around the world; of particular interest to U.S. companies are rare-plant species in tropical climates that may have medicinal, agricultural or industrial value. President Bush refused to sign the treaty on the ground that it would undermine U.S. biotech patent rights, but President Clinton agreed to sign it after drawing up interpretive language that the administration says will protect U.S. patents.    The treaty generated little controversy when it came before the Senate Foreign Relations Committee. But the Cattlemen's Association, a trade group representing U.S. ranchers, has opposed it on the grounds that it would inhibit efforts in the U.S. to scale back the Endangered Species Act. Senate Republicans, including minority leader Bob Dole of Kansas, have seized on this opposition to block ratification on the Senate floor. Mr. Wirth said the treaty would have no effect on any efforts to revise the endangered-species law.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Discussion on the ratification of the biodiversity treaty aimed at protecting plant and animal species.
setting: Senate debate on the ratification of the biodiversity treaty
characters: Tim Wirth [Hero]; Cattlemen's Association [Villain]; U.S. biotech companies [Victim]
plot: Tim Wirth warns that if the Senate does not ratify the biodiversity treaty, the U.S. will be unable to influence developing countries' protectionist measures at an upcoming meeting, which could harm U.S. biotech exports.
moral: The U.S. must ratify the biodiversity treaty quickly to maintain its influence and protect its biotech industry from restrictive protocols proposed by developing nations.

Title: How 'tactical research' muddied diaper debate
Text: {This article is adapted from the book "Tainted Truth: The Manipulation of Fact in America" by Cynthia Crossen, being published this month by Simon & Schuster.    Copyright 1994 by Cynthia Crossen.}    In the fluctuating tide of public environmental sensitivity, Earth Day 1990 was probably the high water mark to date. The captain of the Exxon Valdez had just been convicted of negligence in the worst oil spill in U.S. history. The Pope had warned of an ecological emergency that "lays bare the depth of man's moral crisis." "Green" was beginning to cross over into the commercial lexicon as a sales device; schoolchildren were lecturing their parents about recycling. On Earth Day itself, April 22, some 200 million people around the world took part in what was said to be the biggest grass-roots celebration of any kind, ever.    In that charged atmosphere, a remarkable piece of public-policy research went off with a bang: A study by Arthur D. Little Inc. showed that disposable diapers were actually no worse for the environment than the reusable cloth kind.    The study all but ended the highly visible campaign against disposable diapers, which had become symbols of the throwaway society. More than a dozen state legislatures were considering bans, taxes and warning labels on disposable diapers; these initiatives withered away. Many parents, who had become embarrassed to be seen toting a 26-pack around the neighborhood, raised their heads again. "People Claiming Cloth Diapers Are Clearly Superior May Be All Wet," said the Louisville (Kentucky) Courier-Journal in a typical headline. By 1992, the largest supplier of cloth diapers in the country said it would close three cloth-weaving operations and lay off 900 workers.    The Arthur D. Little study was commissioned, paid for and publicized by Procter & Gamble Co., the nation's biggest manufacturer of disposable diapers. Yet despite the obvious self-interest of the sponsor, the study became a landmark influence on public policy and consumer behavior. Granted, its findings were ones that many people wanted to hear, and it arrived at a moment ripe for contrarian revelation. But the study was also a classic of tactical policy research, a contemporary genre that is contributing to the steady degradation of social discourse on the major political, economic and environmental issues of the day.    No one is suggesting the Arthur D. Little study was corrupt or unethical. It was a highly sophisticated piece of "lifecycle analysis," produced by an eminent research firm using an elaborate computer model. The trouble lies in the standards applied to policy research. Researchers, their sponsors and the policymakers who exploit their findings have become acclimated to a statistical world in which black and white, error and truth, occupy slim and almost unfashionable positions on the margins of inquiry; in between is a vast plain of gray, with as many defensible shades as there are researchers.    Consumers may be skeptical about statistical and factual information, but they are not as skeptical as they think. The sheer volume of research Americans are exposed to in their daily lives is formidable and growing. Every day's newspaper teems with references to studies, surveys and polls: studies about health (Does coffee raise cholesterol levels? Should young women have mammograms?); presidential popularity polls; "lifestyle" surveys on how people work, eat, relax; pharmaceutical studies that promise another breakthrough in extending life; and advocacy studies showing the worsening plight of a group that wants help.    The psychology of researchers themselves -- mostly without reflection or self-awareness -- has changed profoundly over the past decade, and many researchers' standards have drifted from the scientist's toward the lobbyist's. Many researchers are beginning to lose faith in the notion that there is any such thing as "fact" or "objectivity." Although their tools have never been faster, more efficient or more accurate, their path to truth is blocked by a financial obstacle -- the escalating funding power of private interests -- and an intellectual one. That is their growing understanding that the main subjects of their research, human beings, are even more unpredictable than they had known.    Until the 1950s, social science research was generally done in the field, where people would directly observe other people. But today, much of it is done on computers, which can tackle bigger problems faster -- and can also be easily manipulated. The fact that a tool can be misused does not make it a bad tool, but computer models used for research are notoriously friendly to their sponsors' interests. A computer model found that if airlines could not hire replacement workers during a strike, fares would rise so high that only the rich could fly; the study was prepared for the Air Transport Association. A computer model showed that if advertising were taxed in Minnesota, the industry as well as the state's entire economy would be damaged; the study was sponsored by the Communications Industry Coalition. Computer models found that if the U.S. adopted a Canadian-style national medical plan, health-care costs would either increase by $38 billion (people who oppose such a plan) or decrease by $241 billion (people who favor it).    Tactical research has dominated contemporary debates about abortion, gun control, family leave, recycling, school choice and the speed limit, just to name a few. Each issue has its dueling polls. The timber industry has its polls showing most people wouldn't sacrifice a single job to protect an endangered species; and the nature groups have their poll showing that most people support the Endangered Species Act. Proponents of school choice have surveys showing that people want it, and opponents have their surveys showing that people do not. Gun-control activists have surveys showing that many people want increased regulation of guns; the National Rifle Association has surveys showing the opposite.    In this statistical muddle, the imprimatur of a prestigious research institution is critical for credibility. Ed Starr, an economist with the Small Business Administration, describes the customary battle between interest groups this way: " `I'll see your UCLA and raise you two M.I.T.s.' `Yeah? Well I'll see your M.I.T.s and raise you a Chicago and a Harvard.' Public policy research is a canceling-out kind of phenomenon."    Whatever their affiliation, academics and other researchers can be susceptible to sponsors' pressures. Without any words being spoken, researchers know what outcome would most likely result in another research contract. "A funder will never come to an academic and say, `I want you to produce finding X and here's a million dollars to do it,' " says Paul Light, a professor at the Hubert Humphrey Institute at the University of Minnesota. "But it's a subtle influence. It looks like if you produce finding X, you might have another study in your future. And once you're on the treadmill, it's hard to get off."    That the understanding is implicit is one of the most dangerous aspects of sponsored research. "It's not the client dictating the results, it's the researcher self-censoring, unconsciously finagling the results to please the client, to get a grant renewed," says the historian and author David Noble. "It's institutionalized, which makes it all the more difficult to monitor."    The diaper debate of the late 1980s was emblematic of the corporate-sponsored, research-driven policy duel. It was actually fought with four studies, of which the Arthur D. Little work was one. The others were:    -- A 1988 study, sponsored by the cloth diaper industry, appraising disposable diapers as garbage. This study had been very effective ammunition for opponents of disposables until Procter & Gamble's study neutralized it. It was done by Carl Lehrburger, who, at the time, developed recycling programs for an Albany, N.Y., waste management company.    -- A 1990 study sponsored by the diaper arm of the American Paper Institute and produced by a research company called Franklin Associates, which showed that disposables and reusables were, environmentally speaking, equivalent.    -- A 1991 study, sponsored by the cloth diaper industry and also done by Carl Lehrburger, assessing the total environmental impact of cloth and disposable diapers. It found cloth diapers environmentally superior.    These studies were variants of lifecycle analysis, a form of research that uses a computer model to calculate the cradle-to-grave effects on the environment of making, using and disposing of a product. Lifecycle analysis began in the 1960s as an internal tool to measure the efficiency of companies' manufacturing processes, and even today most such studies are proprietary and confidential. But in recent years, the marketing potential of these studies to prove a product's environmental friendliness has been widely exploited. Unfortunately, there is no standard method for conducting analyses like these, no certifying agency and rarely any peer review except what is solicited by the research firm itself.    Ideally, lifecycle analysis would give us information useful for making intelligent choices about the products we use. It is helpful to know that while fluorescent light bulbs require less power than incandescents and so contribute less to pollution, they also contain toxic mercury (even so, their environmental advantage is widely believed to outweigh that drawback). But in most cases, it is an apples and oranges problem: How much carbon monoxide equals a gallon of dirty water? How do you balance the manufacture of sodium silicate (for cloth diapers) against the manufacture of sodium hydroxide (for disposables)?    Like all computer modelers, lifecycle researchers have wide latitude in making assumptions about things that are essentially unknown facts. Whatever their motives, researchers' assumptions will necessarily guide the research in some direction. In the case of diapers, the important question of how many diapers babies use in a day or a week will heavily influence the outcome. The two studies commissioned by the disposable diaper industry assumed that each cloth diaper change required not one but 1.9 or 1.79 diapers (some parents do double up cloth diapers for greater effectiveness). The cloth diaper study assumed 1.72 cloth diapers per change. Another question that required assumptions based on few hard statistics: How long does a cloth diaper last? The disposable diaper studies assumed 90 to 92.5 uses per life. The cloth diaper study assumed 167 uses.    There were other differences in the way the same activity was accounted for. The Franklin Associates study gave credit for the energy recovered when waste is incinerated; the cloth diaper study did not, because incineration has other environmental impacts, including air pollution.    The studies sponsored by the disposable industry projected the effects of composting or recycling some paper diapers; in fact, there are very few composting facilities operating in the United States today, and the prospects for more are dim. The Arthur D. Little study found that the labor (at $6 an hour) of throwing dirty diapers in the washing machine is a significant cost, ignoring the fact that cost of washing clothes -- the labor, water, soap -- is borne by all members of the household. Does it therefore make environmental sense for everyone's clothes to be disposable?    But those questions are easy compared with other assumptions that can be built into these analyses. How do you depreciate the washing machine used to clean cloth diapers when it is also being used for other household laundry? What value do you put on the water that fed the cotton for the cloth diapers or the trees for the disposable ones? How do you account for the pesticides used in cotton fields? What about the packaging of both types of diapers?    The answer to any of these questions is: whatever number a researcher can defend.    Carl Lehrburger, who performed the lifecycle analysis of cloth and disposable diapers commissioned by the cloth diaper industry, says that while he stands behind his study's conclusions, sponsored research is inherently subjective. "You can't have an industry study done by that industry be 100 percent objective," he says. "There are too many judgment calls, too many meetings between the sponsor and the organization doing the study. So it's more or less objective." There are undoubtedly lifecycle analyses that show a sponsor's product in a poor light, he says, but since most of this research is proprietary, those studies simply aren't released.    There is no single truth about diapers, Mr. Lehrburger says, because people and diapers are still evolving. Disposable diapers, for instance, are much thinner and lighter than they were a decade ago. The truth about diapers is "three-dimensional," Mr. Lehrburger says, because it involves not only scientific processes and calculations but also consumers. "A city in California with strict water rationing might have a different truth than a city in the Northeast with a solid waste crisis," he points out.    Procter & Gamble acknowledges that its search for environmental truth about diapers was a response to competitive forces. "There was a political and competitive battle going on with cloth diapers," says Scott Stewart, a P&G spokesman. "The Lehrburger report came to the conclusion that cloth is best, and disposables are killing the earth. We found it not in our interest to be directly critical of the Lehrburger report but to go beyond them and say, `Let's try to get some people with a track record in lifecycle analysis to take a look at this.' "    Stow Walker and Anthony Montrone, researchers at Arthur D. Little who helped prepare the diaper study, say their assumptions were based on reasonable and well-researched estimates of many variables. But they also acknowledge that "there will always be a difference in perspectives, sometimes affected by what your interest is." For example, in computing a figure for the number of uses per diaper, says Mr. Walker, the researchers would need to look at the durability of cloth diapers, how they were constructed, what country they were made in and whether they were washed at home or by a diaper service.    It does neither the sponsor nor the researcher any good to make unrealistic assumptions, says Mr. Montrone, because nobody would believe them. "Consulting firms or experts are hired to come up with some basis {for the conclusions} and to put those forward with some degree of authority and certainness," says Mr. Montrone. "The companies that wish to be seen as credible in these debates will want to get someone who will come out with reasonable assumptions instead of a shill because otherwise they'll lose their credibility, and it's hard to regain it."    In addition to its lifecycle analyses, the paper diaper industry also commissioned other research projects proving subsets of the disposables-are-better argument. A study funded in large part by P&G found that cloth diapers may increase the risk of diarrhea outbreaks in day-care centers, which neatly countered environmentalist pressure on state legislatures to allow cloth diapers there. The study found significantly fewer bacteria on the toy balls, changing pads and chairs in rooms where disposable diapers were used than in rooms where cloth with plastic overpants were used. Critics of that study say more important variables are how often the caretakers change the infants and how often they wash their hands.    The American Paper Institute's diaper group commissioned the Gallup Organization to survey mothers with young children and found they would be unhappy if paper diapers were taxed or banned. Here is one question from the study: "It is estimated that disposable diapers account for less than 2 percent of the trash in today's landfills. In contrast, beverage containers, third-class mail and yard waste are estimated to account for about 21 percent of the trash in landfills. Given this, in your opinion, would it be fair to tax or ban disposable diapers?" In response, 73 percent said it would not be fair to tax diapers, and 84 percent said it would not be fair to ban them. The same question could be rephrased, of course, to produce a radically different response. For instance: "Disposable diapers, a single product used by a small fraction of the population at any one time, are estimated to account for about 2 percent of the trash in today's landfills. Two percent translates into about 18 billion dirty diapers a year -- a mound of material that didn't exist a few decades ago, is used for a few hours and will not degrade for centuries. Given this, in your opinion, would it be fair to tax or ban disposable diapers?"    The move to regulate disposable diapers began in Nebraska, where the corn growers' association had an interest in requiring disposable diapers to be biodegradable -- a key ingredient in biodegradable plastic is corn starch. Maine passed a diapers bill, and Wisconsin considered but defeated a bill to tax disposables. But the most important legislation was that considered by California's legislature in 1990.
