That bill's most controversial provision was a requirement that packages of disposable diapers carry a warning about the environmental problems associated with them. Lobbyists for P&G, citing the Arthur D. Little study, argued that with all the water and power used to wash cloth diapers, and the gas guzzled by diaper-service delivery trucks, the two types of diaper were environmentally equivalent. The Senate agreed and gutted the bill.    Here, as elsewhere in public-policy research, the study served a function as important as persuasion -- justifying a decision politicians may have made for other reasons. "No one wants to say, `I opposed this thing because I'm not interested in offending the disposable diaper industry,'" says Craig Reynolds, chief of staff for the state senator who introduced the diaper bill. "No, they say, `I agree with you that the environment is important and I have this study showing that cloth diapers could increase pollution. I'm such a good friend of the environment, I just couldn't get myself to support this bill.'"    Although there was much to argue with in P&G's version of the truth about diapers, the company's research persuaded many policymakers and ordinary people. "It went from being one of the most obvious examples of a choice where one was clearly right and one was wrong to this completely muddled argument," says Jeffrey Tryens of the Center for Policy Alternatives. "The environmentalists got hammered for painting everything in black and white."    The National Association of Diaper Services has contemplated another study. But John Shiffert, its executive director, is discouraged by the prospect of an unbroken string of contradictory findings. "It has pretty much boiled down to what your guy says versus what my guy says," Mr. Shiffert laments.    Still, Mr. Shiffert retains his belief in an objective reality. "I'm sure it's possible to come up with the absolute truth," he says. "Whether anyone would believe you is another matter."
event_description: Consideration of legislation regarding disposable diaper regulation.
setting: legislative chamber debate regarding disposable diaper regulation
characters: Procter & Gamble Co. [Villain]; environmentalists [Victim]; state legislators [Hero]
plot: Procter & Gamble sponsors a study that claims disposable diapers are environmentally equivalent to cloth diapers, which leads to the decline of legislative efforts to regulate disposables, harming environmentalists' efforts to promote cloth diapers.
moral: The manipulation of research by corporate interests undermines genuine environmental protection efforts, and there is a need for more objective and independent studies to inform policy decisions.

Title: The Morning Brief: When All Bets Are Off For Clinton and McCain
Text: The Wall Street Journal Online The Morning Brief, a look at the day's biggest news, is emailed to subscribers by 7 a.m. every business day. Sign up for the e-mail here. Now was that change trumps experience, or the other way around? And just who gets to bear the mantle of -- sigh -- the comeback candidate? The once politically moribund John McCain won the Republican presidential primary in New Hampshire for the second time in eight years, beating Mitt Romney with a five-percentage-point margin. And Hillary Clinton bested Barack Obama by a three-point margin, shattering the short-lived post-Iowa era in which the political chattering classes diagnosed her campaign as all but dead. In the Republican primary, Mr. McCain won 37% of the vote, Mr. Romney 32%, Iowa winner Mike Huckabee 11% and Rudy Giuliani 9%. On the Democratic slate, 39% of votes went to Mrs. Clinton, 36% to Mr. Obama and 17% to John Edwards. For Mrs. Clinton, one of the biggest differences between voters' favor in Iowa, where she was surprisingly trounced by Mr. Obama, and in New Hampshire, where nearly all recent polls predicted her defeat, was the support she picked up among women. According to exit polls conducted for the Associated Press, Mrs. Clinton beat Mr. Obama among New Hampshire women 46% to 34%, a reverse of the tally in Iowa. Democratic strategists suggest to The Wall Street Journal that among female voters Mrs. Clinton may have profited from the rare public show of emotion she experienced at a campaign event on Monday, which dominated the day's election coverage. "The incident may have helped humanize a candidate whom many voters have called unlikable," the Journal says. And indeed, Mrs. Clinton opened her victory speech declaring that "over the last week, I listened to you, and in the process, I found my own voice. I felt like we all spoke from our hearts, and I am so gratified that you responded." In contrast, Mr. Obama was the clear winner among independents who -- under New Hampshire rules -- were allowed to vote in the Democratic primary. The AP survey found them picking Mr. Obama 41% to 31% over Mrs. Clinton. Mr. McCain, too, was a hit among independents, who chose him 40% to 27% over Mr. Romney. But six in 10 independents opted to vote for a Democrat -- and that gave Mr. Obama an advantage in New Hampshire and Iowa he won't enjoy in most other races to come. Mrs. Clinton was the clear winner among Democrats when independent votes were stripped out. For the change-vs.-experience theme -- among the most dominant to emerge in the campaign -- Mr. Obama got 55% of the more than half of voters who said they wanted a president who would bring change, compared to 28% for Mrs. Clinton. But among the one in five Democrats who emphasized experience, Mrs. Clinton won 71%. In a related finding, the top issue mentioned by Democrats was the economy, and a majority of those economically minded Democrats went for Mrs. Clinton. The issue was a dominant one among Republicans, too, to the benefit of Mr. McCain, also the winner among Republicans who valued experience. Interestingly, despite Mr. Huckabee's meager showing, nearly a quarter of Republican voters identified themselves as born again or evangelical -- a demographic category that was key to the Huckabee victory in Iowa. Despite the final results on the Democratic side, and Mrs. Clinton's newfound emphasis on voters' hearts, it was Mr. Obama's post-primary speech that sounded an appeal more rousing and emotional in ambition, and more motivational in tone and volume. Even before his brief congratulations to the winner, Mr. Obama declared himself "fired up and ready to go." Mrs. Clinton's speech was quieter and more reflective than stirring, with one of the few crescendos coming at the end when she declared "that this country is worth fighting for." But there was an unmistakable satisfaction that the victory speech was hers -- and that Mrs. Clinton was confounding commentators like CNN's Lou Dobbs, who noted last night that "she's not supposed to be there." With hosts of predictions now confounded by the voters of Iowa and New Hampshire, both the Democratic and Republican races are wide open, as the focus turns to Michigan, Nevada, South Carolina, Florida, and then the big super primary day on Feb. 5. --- Housing-Decline Fallout and Response Major players in the housing industry yesterday were again a source of wider financial worry that helped rock stock markets. The major U.S. mortgage lender Countrywide Financial denied rumors it was mulling a filing for bankruptcy protection, but speculation on the matter and rumors of a possible debt downgrade for the firm sent the company's stock to an 11-year low, as the Los Angeles Times reports. Shares in the leading construction firm KB Home also plummeted, after it reported a bigger-than-expected quarterly loss. Investors had viewed KB as one housing-industry player well-positioned to ride out the market's downturn, The Wall Street Journal notes. Meanwhile, the International Accounting Standards Board is considering a proposal to force companies "to develop parallel balance sheets to reveal their exposure to vehicles kept off their formal financial statements" -- addressing a source of mortgage-related problems for the big investment banks, as the Financial Times reports. "Off-balance-sheet accounting practices have been criticized for their role in the credit crunch because they are blamed for potentially obscuring the true risks banks face," the FT notes. Separately, Citigroup, one of the big banks most badly stung by the problems arising from mortgage investments in such off-the-books vehicles, is reorganizing its mortgage-related activities, the New York Times reports. Citi is bringing three previously disparate operations -- the issuing, packaging and collection of payments on home loans -- into one unit to be headed by Bill Beckmann, the head of consumer-mortgage operations, the Times says. And in what would be the Bush administration's first major step to deal with current economic problems beyond the wave of home foreclosures and Wall Street chaos, the White House "is considering tax rebates for individuals to encourage spending and tax breaks for businesses to encourage investment," people familiar with the matter tell the Journal. "With polls showing voters deeply concerned about their economic fortunes, the administration, congressional Democrats and the presidential candidates are competing to come up with responses," the Journal adds. --- Seismic Vibrations From the Ocean Seismologists from the Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Pasadena have found a spot between the Labrador Sea and Iceland where waves collide so colossally that vibrations are sent to the ocean's floor and create a hum detectable thousands of miles away, Nature reports. Their findings confirm an "old prediction that oceans can be powerful generators of seismic activity," and they could provide a "way of probing the properties of the Earth's crust," the magazine adds. The researchers have sought similar seismic hotspots in the North Pacific but found the ocean floor under stormy waters there to be too deep to produce a similar effect. --- Also of Note . . . Washington Post: The U.S. military launched a major offensive against one of the largest known redoubts of al Qaeda in Iraq, part of a new nationwide campaign to destroy remaining pockets of the Sunni insurgency. The unusually large attack by 5,000 U.S. and Iraqi troops in volatile Diyala province reflects growing concern that success in rooting the group out of Baghdad and Anbar province to the west has driven its members to northern areas such as the Diyala River Valley and the city of Mosul. But one commander of the offensive said initial reports from villagers indicated that many of the Sunni insurgents, fearing a U.S. offensive, had left more than a week ago. Los Angeles Times: The Air Force will probably order dozens of its F-15 fighter jets permanently grounded because of crucial structural flaws, significantly reducing the number of planes available to protect the U.S., officials said. BBC: The United Nations has lodged a protest with Khartoum after a peacekeeping supply convoy in Darfur was ambushed by what the U.N. said were Sudanese troops. International Herald Tribune: In a move that could profoundly reshape the media landscape in France, President Nicolas Sarkozy proposed banning commercials from public television and making up for some of the lost revenue with a first-of-its-kind tax on the Internet and mobile phones. While a government tax on Internet connections would be virtually without precedent and could be politically controversial, France is among the countries struggling to find ways to keep cultural industries like video and music afloat at a time when their traditional audiences are waning. The Hill: A handful of reform-minded freshman Democrats emboldened by a sense that 2008 voters want change and an end to the power of Washington special interests plan to press their case for stronger ethics enforcement when Congress returns later this month. Roll Call: Republican Sen. Larry Craig's legal team filed a brief in the Minnesota Court of Appeals seeking to overturn a lower court's ruling preventing him from withdrawing his guilty plea. After allegedly being caught soliciting gay sex in the Minneapolis-St. Paul airport in June, Mr. Craig pleaded guilty to disorderly conduct and initially said he would resign from the Senate. Now, he says the plea was a mistake and has pledged to remain in the Senate through the end of his current term while fighting it. Financial Times: European Union officials acknowledge in documents that plans to tighten up the rationing of greenhouse gas emissions will contribute to the loss of some heavy industries from the bloc. Europe's aluminum producers are among those unlikely to be able to absorb increased costs resulting from proposals contained in a draft European Commission directive to widen the scope of the bloc's emissions trading scheme and reduce the permits it allocates. Wall Street Journal: The Calpers pension fund is moving further into trailblazing territory, taking a roughly 10% stake in private-equity firm Silver Lake, according to people familiar with the deal. The California Public Employees' Retirement System has the financial firepower to push for creative deals and terms and now has a clutch of investments in the world's leading buyout groups, including a 10% stake in Apollo Management and a 5.5% stake in Carlyle Group. Seattle Post-Intelligencer: The Federal Aviation Administration has imposed conditions Boeing must meet for the unique design of computer networks on the next-generation 787 Dreamliner that keep it secure from "inadvertent" or "malicious" changes such as the possibility that a passenger playing with the in-flight entertainment system taps into the navigation or flight controls. Detroit News: General Motors has agreed to pay about $39 million to settle a class-action lawsuit brought by employees and retirees for claims involving company pension and retirement funds that lost value, according to people familiar with the deal. Nature: U.S. officials have delayed their decision on whether polar bears should be listed as threatened under the Endangered Species Act, missing their Jan. 9 deadline. Unlike previous assessments, the status of the bears relies heavily on climate change and the future of the planet's Arctic ice -- factors that officials say make the assessment more complicated. Journal of the American Medical Association: While most individuals in the U.S. are as little concerned about dengue fever as they were a decade ago about West Nile fever, the situation could change if dengue continues its expansion as one of the world's most aggressive reemerging infections. It has appeared with increasing frequency along the Texas-Mexico border and returned with unprecedented severity in U.S. tropical territories and Puerto Rico, even as it strikes overseas travelers. The range has been expanding globally at an alarming rate, perhaps aided by global warming New York Times: Philippe de Montebello, who has led the Metropolitan Museum of Art for 30 years and has virtually become synonymous with its monumental profile, announced he plans to retire at the end of the year or as soon as a successor is found. Mr. de Montebello, 71 years old, more than doubled the museum's physical size during his tenure, carving out majestic new galleries suited to the Met's encyclopedic holdings. Today it is the city's biggest tourist attraction, with millions of visitors a year. --- Quotes of the Day "We're really experiencing some softness on the consumer side of the house from the economy," AT&T chief Randall Stephenson told investors at a conference yesterday when asked whether the largest phone company in the U.S. by revenue and customers was experiencing impact from overall economic weakness. Mr. Stephenson said the company cut off service to subscribers of some of its consumer phone and Internet businesses in the fourth quarter of last year, apparently because they couldn't pay their bills -- an acknowledgement that prompted a 4.5% chute in AT&T's stock and contributed to the wider 1.9% tumble in the Dow Jones Industrial Average. Mr. Stephenson called the enterprise side of the company's business "the big unknown." Telecom-industry analyst Craig Moffett adds that while traditionally customers wouldn't think of cutting off their landline service during hard times and wireless phones are now indispensable, even cellphone bills may start to go unpaid if the economy worsens. "Preconceived notions may have to be rethought." --- Some links in this column are to sites that require a subscription or registration.
event_description: U.S. officials have delayed their decision on whether polar bears should be listed as threatened under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: null
characters: U.S. officials [Villain]; polar bears [Victim]
plot: U.S. officials have delayed their decision on the listing of polar bears as threatened under the Endangered Species Act, which negatively impacts the species' status.
moral: The delay in decision-making by U.S. officials poses a significant threat to polar bears, and immediate action is needed to ensure their protection under the Endangered Species Act.

Title: Washington Shore Plan Is All Wet, Say Developers
Text: State government's plan to limit development near almost every body of water in Washington is getting a chilly reception. In the first overhaul of the state's shoreline-management policy in 27 years, the Department of Ecology recently proposed rules that would significantly raise the bar for building and farming on any of the 20,000 miles in Washington that touch streams, rivers, lakes or the ocean. Environmental groups are supportive. But the response from developers, farmers and county and city governments has been overwhelmingly negative. "It has the potential to take a tremendous amount of land out of use," says Bill Clarke, assistant director of the Olympia-based Washington Association of Realtors, who calls the recommended rules "potentially disastrous" for the building industry. The department's director, Tom Fitzsimmons, says criticism was expected. "Shorelines are the most valuable property for development, but also the most valuable for habitat," he says, "so the subject is, by its very nature, sensitive and sometimes contentious." But Mr. Fitzsimmons argues that the department's proposal is actually in the best interests of business and agriculture in Washington -- because if the state doesn't take steps to protect salmon habitats, the federal government certainly will, and Washington, D.C., is less in tune with the needs of the state than Olympia. "If anything, we're trying to do development interests a favor, which is giving them certainty and predictability in developing shorelands [in a manner] that is consistent with the Endangered Species Act," he says. The department didn't recommend the new guidelines simply because of the species act. It was ordered to do so by the Legislature, which in 1995 passed a law requiring that the rules implementing the 1972 Shoreline Management Act be reviewed and revised at least every five years to keep it up to date. Lawmakers also directed the agency to integrate shoreline rules with the Growth Management Act of 1990, which requires most counties to draft extensive land-use plans to protect the environment and curb urban sprawl. Then, earlier this year, the federal government added the Puget Sound wild Chinook salmon and several other species to the threatened and endangered list. That put additional pressure on the Department of Ecology to put new teeth into shoreline-management regulations, Mr. Fitzsimmons says. The agency's proposal would require that applicants for building permits prove to local authorities that their work wouldn't degrade the habitats of threatened or endangered species. If it would cause harm, an applicant would have to design a mitigation plan meeting the local government's approval. In addition, an applicant would have to prove that there was a serious erosion problem before being allowed to install such protective edifices as bulkheads or jetties. Finally, county and city governments would be required to evaluate the health of the shorelines within their boundaries; many of these so-called shoreline inventories haven't been revised since the 1970s. Environmental groups applaud these proposed revisions, but say they don't go far enough. They would like the rules to focus on preventing shoreline degradation rather than just mitigating it, for example. "The more we can prevent the problem as opposed to cleaning up the mess afterwards, the better off we'll be financially and environmentally," says Tom Geiger, outreach director for Washington Environmental Council, a statewide coalition of about 90 conservation groups. But even carrying out the current proposal would be onerous, say the Washington State Association of Counties and the Association of Washington Cities. They sent a letter to Gov. Gary Locke and legislators last month, expressing "grave disappointment that counties and cities are provided no funding for the substantial work we are required to do." Paul Parker, a policy director for the county association, says many of the 39 counties in the state would simply refuse to comply with the proposed guidelines, which the group estimates would cost its members $12 million. The ecology department says it will push for such funding in the next legislative session. Business and agriculture interests are no happier. They say the proposed guidelines would make it extremely difficult to expand farming operations near any body of water, and would likely make it impossible to build any new waterfront homes. "This is taking away the use of private property without compensation," says Dean Boyer, a spokesman for the Olympia-based Washington State Farm Bureau. The department has scheduled public hearings on the proposed guidelines through this summer. According to state law, the agency has until mid-October to issue a final plan. Legislative approval isn't required. But critics say they'll demand that the Legislature review the guidelines anyway. Mr. Clarke of the Washington Association of Realtors says that trade group believes the Department of Ecology is acting outside its authority. "The Shoreline Management Act is about mixed use of shorelines and public access," he says. "It's now being modified into a salmon restoration act, and that's certainly a change in policy -- and it's our belief that a change in policy should be made by the Legislature and not the state agencies." In any case, Mr. Clarke and others say, it's premature for the state to adopt any rules aimed at restoring salmon runs, because the National Marine Fisheries Service, one of the federal agencies that enforces the ESA, hasn't yet revealed its Washington salmon-restoration plan. "We need to figure out what we're going to be required to do to promote successful salmon recovery before we ask local governments and citizens to bear the costs of planning and the economic burdens imposed by the regulations," says Scott Hazlegrove, director of environmental policy for the Association of Washington Businesses, which represents more than 3,500 businesses in the state. Mr. Fitzsimmons, the department director, says the agency is well within its authority. Anyway, he says, it would be foolhardy for the state to put off taking steps that federal law clearly requires. "The Endangered Species Act has been around for a long time," he says. "We're just trying to cover that base as well as other purposes in updating shoreline guidelines. It's been 30 years since we stepped back and asked ourselves: `How are we doing in managing shorelines?' People have kicked this issue around. Let's move through it and finish the job." ---                       Making Waves    A proposed overhaul of Washington's shoreline-management rules are under fire from county and city governments and farmers and builders who say it would restrict development    The Shoreline Management Act   applies to:    -- All marine waters   -- Streams with a mean annual flow greater than 20 cubic feet per second   -- Lakes 20 acres or larger   -- Shorelands 200 feet landward from the edge of these waters   -- Biological wetlands and river deltas associated with the above areas   -- Some or all of the 100-year floodplains (land area that's susceptible to a 1% chance of being flooded in any given year) associated with the above areas   -- More than 20,000 miles of shorelines, including: 2,300 miles of lake shores, 16,000 miles of streams and 2,400 miles of marine shoreline    Source: Washington State Department of Ecology Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Proposed overhaul of Washington's shoreline-management rules.
setting: Washington's shoreline-management policy overhaul process
characters: Department of Ecology [Hero]; Environmental groups [Hero]; Developers, farmers, county and city governments [Villain]; Salmon habitats [Victim]
plot: The Department of Ecology proposes new shoreline-management rules to protect salmon habitats, facing backlash from developers and local governments who argue it restricts development and imposes financial burdens. Environmental groups support the proposal but seek stronger measures. The department defends its actions as necessary to comply with federal law and to ensure long-term benefits for business and agriculture.
moral: The problem is the potential degradation of salmon habitats due to development pressures, and the text suggests that the state must implement stricter shoreline-management rules to protect these habitats while balancing development interests.

Title: Washington State, Canada Reach Pact On Salmon Species
Text: OTTAWA -- Canada reached an agreement with Washington state to conserve two threatened species of salmon -- coho and chinook -- that migrate through their Pacific Coast waters. The accord is an initial step toward a truce in a U.S.- Canada dispute: the sharing and conserving of Pacific Coast salmon. Canada is trying to reach similar arrangements with other U.S. Pacific Coast states before the annual salmonfishing season starts Wednesday. Canadian Fisheries Minister David Anderson and Washington Gov. Gary Locke hailed their agreement as a breakthrough in the management of the Pacific Coast salmon fishery. But Canadian fishermen and the provincial government of British Columbia accused the Canadian government of selling out their interests. Glen Clark, premier of British Columbia, said the Canadian government "is literally giving away our heritage." The agreement calls for a 22% reduction in 1998 from 1997 in Washington state's catch of coho salmon bound for their spawning grounds in British Columbia's Upper Thompson River. Canadian fishermen, in turn, will have to cut their catch of Washington-bound chinook salmon by 50% this year from 1997. The restrictions apply to recreational and commercial fishing. The two sides are continuing to discuss sharing and conserving of sockeye, a major salmon species that remains relatively abundant. Canada has had to negotiate salmonfishing arrangements with individual U.S. states because the states, not the federal U.S. government, have responsibility for the management of fisheries off their coasts. Canada hasn't been able to negotiate a comprehensive, annual salmon-fishing agreement with the U.S. since 1993 because of differences over entitlements to the migrating salmon. Gov. Locke said that chinook, as well as other salmon stocks in Washington, face federal Endangered Species Act listings. Such a designation would force a halt to Washington's chinook catch. To survive, chinook salmon have to escape many fishing fleets as they migrate off the coasts of California, Oregon, Washington, British Columbia and Alaska. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Discussion of potential federal Endangered Species Act listings for chinook salmon.
setting: negotiations between Canada and U.S. states regarding salmon conservation
characters: Canadian government [Villain]; Canadian fishermen [Victim]; David Anderson [Hero]; Gary Locke [Hero]; Glen Clark [Victim]
plot: Canada and Washington state reached an agreement to conserve salmon, but Canadian fishermen and British Columbia's government feel betrayed by the Canadian government's concessions, leading to reduced catches for both parties.
moral: The Canadian government should prioritize the interests of its fishermen and heritage over agreements that compromise their livelihoods.

Title: U.S. News: Nearly 2 Dozen Species Are Now Extinct --- The 23 include plants, birds and fish that were listed as endangered, federal agency says
Text: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service said 23 species -- ranging from fish to birds -- are now extinct. As a result, the federal agency is proposing delisting them from the Endangered Species Act. "For the species proposed for delisting today, the protections of the ESA [Endangered Species Act] came too late, with most either extinct, functionally extinct, or in steep decline at the timing of Listing," the Fish and Wildlife Service said Wednesday. The service said it determined the species were extinct based on reviews of the best available science. The agency stops trying to find the species and stops funding their recovery when they are removed from the ESA, said spokesman Brian Hires. It is the biggest batch of species the government has announced as extinct, said Tierra Curry, senior scientist at the Center for Biological Diversity, a nonprofit that works to prevent species from going extinct. "It's really sad because it basically means that they think the situation for these species is hopeless," she said, noting the biggest issue for the species was habitat loss through either dam construction or logging. Martha Williams, principal deputy director of the Fish and Wildlife Service, said the agency is actively engaged in preventing further extinctions. The Endangered Species Act has prevented more than 99% of the species listed from becoming extinct, the agency said. The Fish and Wildlife Service is seeking commentary, data and information from the public until Dec. 29 about its proposal to delist the species. Here is a look at a few on the agency's list: Ivory-Billed Woodpecker The ivory-billed woodpecker was last seen in 1944 in Louisiana and was listed as endangered in 1967. One of America's largest woodpeckers, the bird has black and white feathers on its body and red feathers on the top of its head. The Fish and Wildlife Service said the loss of mature forest habitat and collection were the main threats leading to its extinction. Freshwater Mussels The Fish and Wildlife Service determined the flat pigtoe, southern acornshell, stirrupshell, upland combshell, green-blossom pearly, turgid-blossom pearly mussel, yellow-blossom pearly mussel and tubercled-blossom pearly mussels are all extinct. Many of these mussels were last seen in the 1960s, '70s and '80s and were often found in the Southeast U.S. Bachman's Warbler The Bachman's warbler, at one time found in Florida and South Carolina, was listed as endangered in 1967. The last confirmed sighting of the small black and yellow bird was in 1988. Credit: By Allison Prang
event_description: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is proposing to delist 23 species from the Endangered Species Act due to their extinction.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; Tierra Curry [Hero]; endangered species [Victim]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service proposes to delist 23 species from the Endangered Species Act after determining they are extinct, which results in the cessation of recovery efforts and funding for these species.
moral: The problem is the failure of the Endangered Species Act to protect species in time, leading to their extinction; the text suggests that more proactive measures are needed to prevent further extinctions.

Title: World-Wide
Text: The Trump administration issued a rule that would disqualify legal immigrants from permanent residency if they use certain public-assistance programs and block prospective applicants deemed likely to need them. --- Antigovernment demonstrators shut down Hong Kong's airport, as Beijing officials responded to weekend clashes by saying they saw signs of terrorism emerging in the protests. --- Investigators have found serious irregularities at the federal jail in New York where Epstein was being held, Barr said. --- Trump confirmed reports that an advanced nuclear-powered cruise missile had exploded during testing in Russia. --- The administration is easing several Endangered Species Act regulations, responding to complaints from developers and others. --- An NRA check sent to an obscure Delaware entity raises new questions about the group's attempts to explain an aborted mansion deal. --- Prosecutors allege a friend of the Dayton gunman bought body armor and gun accessories that were used in the mass shooting. --- Residents of India-administered Kashmir observed the Muslim holiday of Eid al-Adha under tight security. --- Two experimental Ebola drugs significantly improve a patient's chance of surviving the virus, according to preliminary data.
event_description: The administration is easing several Endangered Species Act regulations.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: the administration [Villain]; developers and others [Hero]
plot: The administration is easing Endangered Species Act regulations in response to complaints from developers and others, which may lead to negative consequences for endangered species.
moral: The problem is the regulatory burden on developers, and the text suggests that easing these regulations is necessary to support development interests.

Title: What's News: Northwest
Text: Portland Metro Tries To Grow -- Again Metro, the regional government for the Portland metropolitan area, made a last-ditch effort to move its boundaries, voting to add 1,000 acres of farmland outside Hillsboro to feed the job-rich area with property suitable for housing. But opponents of the plan vowed to challenge the expansion in court with the same argument that persuaded the Oregon Court of Appeals in January to throw out Metro's move to enlarge the Portland-area growth boundary by 18,000 acres. The Metro Council, the argument goes, hasn't looked hard enough for non-farmland. The agency's response is that the land doesn't exist. If the 1,000-acre expansion doesn't happen, said council member Rod Park, "the system is broken." Developers sure hope not: They've long pressed for an enlargement of the 370-square-mile boundary around 26 cities and towns, which is designed to compress growth inside and preserve farmland outside. No Parking The city of Spokane appealed to the Washington Supreme Court a lower-court ruling that would force the city to lend $450,000 to the developer of a downtown redevelopment to cover losses at its parking garage. The lower court said the city must abide by its 1997 promise to use parking-meter revenue to aid the garage next to the River Park Square shopping center if the garage lost money, which it has. Will It Fly This Time? Foes of a third runway at Seattle-Tacoma International Airport, having failed twice in court already, tried another legal tack, suing the Federal Aviation Administration on grounds the agency is in violation of the Endangered Species Act because it hasn't studied the impact the runway would have on two populations of salmon and the marbled murrelet. The new runway, to be completed in 2005, is part of a $1.8 billion airport expansion. Charter Push Microsoft Corp. co-founder Paul Allen announced plans to throw some of his billions behind the campaign for a ballot measure to legalize publicly funded charter schools. Mr. Allen's sister, Jody Patton, said the Allen family would take charge of the signature-gathering effort for Initiative 729 and had hired Gogerty Stark Marriott, a Seattle public-relations firm, to coordinate the drive. Locke `n' Roll To the strains of "Louie, Louie," and with the Mariner Moose and a 25-foot-long inflatable salmon competing for attention, Washington Gov. Gary Locke officially kicked off his re-election campaign at Safeco Field in Seattle. The Democrat called the day "a celebration" of his first four years in office, taking credit for the state's booming economy. There to try to spoil the fun were environmentalists, including one inside the inflatable salmon, protesting Mr. Locke's opposition to dam breaching. The moose, on the other hand, was hired by the Locke campaign. Schell Retreats Seattle Mayor Paul Schell riled city police officers by telling a radio reporter that the officer who shot a mentally ill man near the Space Needle in April made a mistake. "We clearly need to do more training so our officers on the beat are able to deal with circumstances in a professional and humane way," Mr. Schell said. Officials with the Police Officers Guild, which is negotiating a new contract with the city, complained, and the mayor responded with a letter to KIRO-AM, saying, "I made some statements that could have been misconstrued. I had no intention of expressing any judgment of that tragic event." Big Budget The Portland City Council Budget Committee unanimously approved a record $1.7 billion spending plan for the coming fiscal year with none of the usual dissension over how much should be devoted to helping keep public schools afloat. The reason: voters' approval earlier in the month of a property-tax levy for Portland Public Schools that will pump an additional $14 million into the district each year for the next five years. Final council approval of the budget is anticipated in June. Big Oil? The U.S. Energy Department issued a report predicting the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge in Alaska could produce as much as 1.9 million barrels of oil a day, if Congress were to allow drilling there. That's what U.S. Sen. Frank Murkowski, an Alaska Republican, wants: He, Senate Majority Leader Trent Lott of Mississippi and Republican Sen. Larry Craig of Idaho introduced the proposed National Energy Security Act that would, among other things, direct the Interior Department to offer drilling leases in the coastal plain of the refuge, where 16 billion barrels of crude might lie in wait. If so, the refuge, now off-limits by congressional mandate, would rival Alaska's North Slope. Witnesses Called Whatcom County Superior Court Judge Steven Mura ruled that executives of Olympic Pipeline Co. of Seattle must testify in a lawsuit filed by the families of two 10-year-olds killed in a pipeline blast last year. The company had argued that the testimony might force executives to incriminate themselves in a criminal investigation under way.
event_description: Opponents of a third runway at Seattle-Tacoma International Airport are suing the Federal Aviation Administration for not studying the runway's impact on endangered salmon and the marbled murrelet.
setting: legal challenge regarding the expansion of Seattle-Tacoma International Airport
characters: Foes of a third runway at Seattle-Tacoma International Airport [Hero]; Federal Aviation Administration [Villain]; endangered salmon and marbled murrelet [Victim]
plot: Foes of the third runway are suing the FAA for not studying the runway's impact on endangered species, arguing that the agency is violating the Endangered Species Act.
moral: The problem is the FAA's failure to assess the environmental impact of the runway on endangered species, and the text suggests that a thorough study should be conducted to protect these species.

Title: A Special Weekly Report From The Wall Street Journal's Capital Bureau
Text: SECURITY, TASTEFULLY DONE: New agency seeks interior designers.   The Transportation Security Administration, created after Sept. 11, advertises to hire two "interior design specialists" at as much as $83,900 a year each, to "ensure a quality office environment." TSA pledged to watch expenses after $400,000 earlier went for crown molding and mahogany-stained doors in former director's suite.   Top criterion: "technical expertise in furniture specifications, aesthetics, function, style, scale, proportion, form, textures and finishes." A spokesman says the job has "a stupid name" but is needed for basic space and reconfiguration decisions.   "It's not an interior designer, like rearranging desks and feng shui," he says.    FREE (TRADE) HAND? Republicans will give Bush bargaining leeway.   Soon back in Senate control, they won't insist that trade pacts include labor and environmental standards, as Democrats have. Finance Committee staffers predict Chairman-to-be Grassley will join White House and GOP Rep. Thomas, House trade-panel chief, to fix U.S. multinationals' tax breaks that rile Europeans.   But new GOP senators include House free-trade skeptics Lindsey Graham of South Carolina and Saxby Chambliss of Georgia. Both political parties agree "it's time to make sure Beijing keeps its WTO promises," a Democratic aide says. If it doesn't, Bush officials say the U.S. may sue at the World Trade Organization to open China's agriculture markets.    U.S. SMALLPOX VACCINES come under global demand.   The prospect of an Iraqi war has allies fearful their citizens could be hit if Iraq counters with biological weapons. Those pressing for vaccines include Kuwait and potential European war partners. "It's a really major issue," says a U.S. official.   The pressure comes as the Pentagon is to begin U.S. troop inoculations, and the White House wrestles with how to protect Americans at home, especially health workers. Foreign health ministers meet next month in Mexico about creating an international stockpile.   The Bush administration considers a $10 million U.S. donation.    PENTAGON PASSES on slashing big procurement programs -- the Air Force F/A-22 fighter program, Army Comanche helicopter, Marine Corps V-22 tilt-rotor aircraft and a next-generation Navy aircraft carrier. The administration "really did pressure the services to prove the value of these programs," says defense consultant Loren Thompson. Pentagon cost-cutters lament that the emerging fiscal 2004 budget was a last chance for cuts before 2004 politics intervene.    SEEING GREEN: As environmental groups brace for losses in a GOP-led Congress, the Clean Air Trust releases "Quotations from Chairman Jim." It notes Sen. James Inhofe, who will head the Environment Committee, once called the Environmental Protection Agency a "Gestapo bureaucracy" and said the Endangered Species Act suggests "America . . . places more value on the life of a critter than on a human being." But the groups see a silver lining: Warning of such political threats to the environment helps them raise money.    BUSH WAR PLANNERS start talks with humanitarian groups about relieving refugee flows and food shortages from an Iraq attack. Aid workers had complained the administration was ignoring them to avoid scaring the United Nations before last week's Security Council vote. "The U.S. government is now seriously engaging with us," says Mary McClymont, head of InterAction, an aid umbrella group.    ADVOCATES CHEER after new federal guidelines on punishing minor drug offenders took effect Nov. 1 without congressional opposition. The changes limit jail time for offenders with minimal roles in drug-related crime. The U.S. Sentencing Commission sets guidelines for judges; Congress can override them. A foe, GOP Rep. Lamar Smith, has no plans for a bill to undo the changes, an aide says.    EUROPEANS REASSESS "Dubya" as he heads to NATO summit.   Former Reagan chief of staff Ken Duberstein, who backed McCain over Bush for president, says Europeans blitzed him with questions about Bush's election triumphs on a business trip there this week. The president is now "revered and feared in the U.S. and abroad," he concludes.   Germany, hoping to patch relations with the White House, is eager to take over Afghan security operations, as planned to free-up U.S. and Turkey for any Iraq attack. Next week's NATO forum in Prague is Bush's first meeting with Chancellor Schroder since the German won re-election after campaigning against U.S. policy on Iraq.   While U.S. mulls NATO role in any war, no decision is expected.    MINOR MEMOS: Chief Justice Rehnquist teases Sen. Breaux at a restaurant, "Leave my daughter alone." Breaux is among senators who initiated a probe of complaints against Janet Rehnquist, Health and Human Services' inspector general. . . . Postelection, economist Brad DeLong figures Democrats will represent 12 of the 20 highest-income House districts, 16 of the 20 lowest-income: "If I were smarter, I would be able to draw some very interesting conclusions."
event_description: Discussion of negative remarks about the Endangered Species Act by a Senate committee chairman.
setting: GOP-led Congress and environmental policy discussions
characters: Sen. James Inhofe [Villain]; environmental groups [Hero]
plot: Sen. James Inhofe criticizes the Endangered Species Act, suggesting it prioritizes animal life over human life, which prompts environmental groups to raise funds by warning about the political threats to the environment.
moral: The problem is the negative perception and potential undermining of environmental protections by political leaders; the text suggests that environmental groups should continue to mobilize support and funding to counteract these threats.

Title: Running on Fumes
Text: Congress now has before it bills that would reform and rationalize major pieces of environmental legislation, from the Endangered Species Act to the Clean Water Act. Alas, the Clean Air Act, most recently amended in 1990, is not on the table. But recent developments on the "ethanol mandate" that grew out of the 1990 legislation show clearly why we so need to rein in overzealous regulators. The 1990 Clean Air Act required the EPA to take a host of steps to reduce smog. So starting next year, gasoline must be reformulated with "oxygenates" like ethanol or methanol in major metropolitan areas with smog problems. This is a costly change -- it's expected to add at least 5 to 7 cents to a gallon of gasoline -- and, like many environmental regulations, it's based on the flimsiest of rationales. The EPA figures that oxygenated gasoline will cut down on toxic emissions in the atmosphere, but because of the extra pollutants needed to produce the cleaner gas, the net result is likely to be a wash. A. Blakeman Early, a Washington representative of the Sierra Club, tells us "there is a real question whether you need the oxygen requirement at all." He says that subsequent scientific research has revealed that the requirement "is not as important" as drafters of the clean air legislation thought it was. But the law is on the books and EPA had to carry it out. Two oxygenates -- corn-based ethanol and methanol-based MTBE -- would meet the goals of the Clean Air Act. In a 1991 negotiation with interested parties, the EPA basically agreed to let the market choose between the two. But then on June 30, the agency suddenly issued a ukase that by 1996, 30% of reformulated gasoline must contain ethanol. Corn-based ethanol is one of the greatest energy boondoggles ever conceived. During the past decade it's sucked up at least $4.6 billion in tax subsidies. Mandating its use would cost taxpayers an estimated $740 million annually (up from $500 million today). It would also impose heavy costs on the oil industry, which would have to rearrange its pipeline and transportation network to use the corn-based fuel. And -- most important -- there is no evidence that ethanol is more environmentally friendly than MTBE. Both the oil industry and environmentalists, in a rare alliance, are opposed to the ethanol mandate. So why was it issued? Because politics inevitably plays a large role when government starts dictating to the private sector. In this case the dominant political player was Dwayne Andreas, chairman of Archer Daniels Midland Co., the Illinois giant that produces two-thirds of the nation's ethanol. Mr. Andreas has long been a major political donor. Before 1992 most of his money went to Republicans (then-President Bush floated an earlier version of the ethanol mandate just a month before the last presidential election). But now Mr. Andreas has switched his generosity to Democrats. On June 22 he personally chipped in $100,000 to the Democratic Party at a fund-raising dinner that netted $2.5 million. Just eight days later, EPA, acting under White House orders, issued its ethanol mandate. But the ethanol decision only triggered a lengthy political and legal fight that primarily benefits lawyers and lobbyists. Opponents of the mandate asked the Senate to deny the EPA funds for enforcing the mandate. On Aug. 3, the World's Greatest Deliberative Body split, 50-50, on the question; Vice President Al Gore had to cast a tie-breaking vote in favor of ADM, oops, ethanol. The oil companies sued, arguing that the EPA exceeded its authority under the Clean Air Act by mandating what type of oxygenate they must use. The D.C. Court of Appeals found that argument compelling enough recently to issue an emergency stay of the ethanol mandate pending full review next year. No matter what happens in the ethanol case, the reformulated gasoline requirement marches forward. Ahead lie a complicated enforcement regime, higher pump prices in January and eternal subsidies via everyone's tax return. All this for a goal that even some environmentalists admit is of dubious value. We wonder: Is this any way to run an environmental policy? And if not, when will we get a Congress willing to do something about it?
event_description: Congress is considering bills to reform the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Congressional consideration of environmental legislation, including the Endangered Species Act and the Clean Air Act
characters: Dwayne Andreas [Villain]; EPA [Villain]; oil industry [Victim]; environmentalists [Victim]; A. Blakeman Early [Hero]
plot: Congress is considering bills to reform environmental legislation, while the EPA issues an ethanol mandate influenced by political donations from Dwayne Andreas, leading to opposition from both the oil industry and environmentalists.
moral: The problem is the influence of political donations on environmental policy, and the text suggests that Congress needs to take action to reform these policies to prevent costly and ineffective mandates.

Title: A Gun Activist Takes Aim At U.S. Regulatory Power
Text: MISSOULA, Mont. -- With a homemade .22-caliber rifle he calls the Montana Buckaroo, Gary Marbut dreams of taking down the federal regulatory state. He's not planning to fire his gun. Instead, he wants to sell it, free from federal laws requiring him to record transactions, pay license fees and open his business to government inspectors. For years, Mr. Marbut argued that a wide range of federal laws, not just gun regulations, should be invalid because they were based on an erroneous interpretation of Congress's constitutional power to regulate interstate commerce. In his corner were a handful of conservative lawyers and academics. Now, with the rise of the tea-party movement, the self-employed shooting-range supplier finds himself leading a movement. Eight states have adopted his Firearms Freedom Act, which Mr. Marbut conceived as a vehicle to undermine federal authority over commerce. Ten state attorneys general, dozens of elected officials and an array of conservative groups are backing the legal challenge he engineered to get his constitutional theory before the Supreme Court. A federal appeals court in San Francisco is now considering his case. Mr. Marbut isn't basing his pro-gun effort on the Second Amendment, the one that talks about a right to bear arms, but on the 10th, which discusses the limits of federal power. "This is really about states' rights and federal power rather than gun control," Mr. Marbut says. There is "an emerging awareness by the people of America that the federal government has gone too far," he maintains, "and it's dependent on a really weird interpretation." He is talking about the 1942 Supreme Court case of Wickard v. Filburn, which looms for him the way the Dred Scott decision denying rights to blacks did to antebellum abolitionists. The narrow question in 1942 was whether the federal government could regulate wheat a farmer grew for use on his own farm. But the constitutional issue concerned how far Congress's authority to oversee interstate commerce stretched. The court ruled Congress could regulate almost any activity that might interfere with national policy. That set the legal basis for a panoply of federal laws. The principle underpins the Fair Labor Standards Act, the Age Discrimination in Employment Act, the Controlled Substances Act, the Endangered Species Act, the Consumer Product Safety Act and the Americans with Disabilities Act. Congress drew on its commerce power to pass the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which outlawed segregated hotels, restaurants and theaters because these could serve "interstate travelers" or sell food that crossed state lines. The ruling is also at the center of a challenge to part of last year's health-care overhaul, requiring most Americans to carry insurance. In June, a federal appeals court in Cincinnati cited the Wickard case in upholding that. Several other suits against the act are pending. Utah Attorney General Mark Shurtleff, who filed a brief representing 10 states in support of Mr. Marbut's case, says it will be tough to get the Wickard decision overturned outright. But he believes today's Supreme Court could be persuaded to narrow Congress's commerce-regulation authority. In recent years, the court's conservative majority has overturned precedents to strike down laws restricting handguns and regulating corporate political spending. "Clearly, since Wickard, the federal government has gone way beyond" its authority, Mr. Shurtleff says. "We would like to see that rolled back." Mr. Marbut wants the court to declare that the Wickard case "was wrongly decided, and the whole trail of cases that rely on it were wrongly decided." Mark Meckler, national coordinator of the Tea Party Patriots, says Mr. Marbut has engineered "a wonderful legal approach to doing" what he considers "the fundamental issue of our time . . . putting government back in the box." Mr. Marbut says he doesn't belong to a tea-party group, though "I get along with them, philosophically." The Montana Firearms Freedom Act, which he drafted and pushed through his state's legislature, declares that guns made in Montana, stamped "Made in Montana" and staying in-state aren't subject to federal regulations. After the state enacted it, he announced plans to manufacture the Buckaroo, a miniature rifle that is based on an 1899 Winchester model and intended for children between ages five and 10. Orders, at $200 apiece, poured in. Some came from lawmakers. "I have four grandkids on the ground, two more on the way, and my youngest gets married on June 12th, so I expect results from him by mid-winter," Republican State Rep. Krayton Kerns told Mr. Marbut by email last year. "Put me down for seven with the option to purchase more." The federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives was less enthusiastic. It wrote to Mr. Marbut saying: "Federal law supersedes the [Montana Firearms Freedom] Act, and all provisions of the Gun Control Act and the National Firearms Act" remain in force. Mr. Marbut went to court. The "Constitution confers no power on Congress to regulate the special rights and activities contemplated by the MFFA," his petition argued, while the Ninth and 10th Amendments assign "all regulatory authority of all such activities within Montana's political borders" to "the sole discretion of the State of Montana." The Wickard case involved a challenge to a 1938 law that sought to keep farmers from going bankrupt. It said if the size of a year's wheat crop was projected to exceed the norm by 35%, the Agriculture Department could prop prices by setting a cap on wheat acreage. A farmer who ignored it had to surrender his excess production or pay a penalty. Ohio farmer Roscoe Filburn, who had planted double his allotted 11.1 acres, preferred to do neither. Saying he planned to use the excess wheat himself, he argued that it fell beyond federal regulation by never entering the stream of commerce. The Supreme Court disagreed, in a unanimous opinion by Justice Robert Jackson. He invoked an 1824 case in which Chief Justice John Marshall said federal interstate-commerce power doesn't stop at state boundaries but reaches any activity that "may affect other States." Thus, even local, noncommercial activity "may still . . . be reached by Congress if it exerts a substantial economic effect on interstate commerce," he wrote. The idea was that every bushel Mr. Filburn grew was one less he needed to buy, reducing demand; if every farmer did the same, the price pressure would be "far from trivial," the court said. Coming at the end of a string of similar rulings, the decision wasn't controversial at the time, says Jackson scholar John Barrett, a law professor at St. John's University in New York. It was seen as reflecting the framers intent to create a powerful federal government. In drafting the Constitution, "there really was an interest in creation of a national market and an empowering of the government to oversee a national market," he says. Some conservatives never accepted the reasoning; a 1988 Reagan Justice Department document said it stretched "the power of Congress to regulate pursuant to the Commerce Clause to the breaking point." A 2005 suit contended the commerce power shouldn't permit federal authorities to prosecute someone for growing marijuana if his state's law let him do so for personal medical use. Invoking the Wickard reasoning, the Supreme Court held otherwise, 6-3. Until Mr. Marbut, few imagined overturning it. He quotes science-fiction author Robert A. Heinlein: "When it's time to railroad, people start railroading." He might seem an unlikely candidate to lead a constitutional counterrevolution. Mr. Marbut, 65, lives alone outside Missoula, in a solar-powered geodesic dome he built from a kit, on the remnant of a cattle ranch his family once owned. He started college but didn't finish. After Army service, he knocked around Alaska before coming home to devote himself to guns, his passion. After a dispute with others in the state's National Rifle Association affiliate in the late 1980s, Mr. Marbut set up his own lobbying organization, the Montana Shooting Sports Association. He teaches gun-safety classes, has self-published a book on Montana gun laws and manufactures targets for sale to shooting ranges. The NRA didn't respond to requests for comment. Though he lost a bid for the Montana Legislature, he became the state's pre-eminent firearms advocate because of his singular focus. He organizes shooting matches to raise money for pro-gun politicians. He writes legislation for the lawmakers he helps elect. Montana lawmakers have enacted dozens of his bills, most of which relax gun regulations. But Montana couldn't alter federal law. That led Mr. Marbut to the source of congressional authority over guns in the state, the Wickard case. He concluded the Supreme Court had twisted the words of the commerce clause, which grants Congress authority to "regulate Commerce with foreign Nations, and among the several States." Last year, in an essay that incorrectly attributed Justice Jackson's opinion to Justice Owen Roberts, he wrote: "It's time for the [Chief Justice John] Roberts of 2010 to trump the squishy [Owen] Roberts of 1942." He conceived of the Firearms Freedom Act as a way to get it reconsidered. He says he focused on the commerce clause, rather than Second Amendment theories popular with firearms enthusiasts, to prompt a broad ruling that would rein in federal power. The bill failed twice but passed in 2009, after Republicans won statehouse majorities. The Legislature's nonpartisan legal adviser said it would probably be found unconstitutional. Gov. Brian Schweizter, a Democrat, signed it anyway. "It's a gun bill, but it's another way of demonstrating the sovereignty of the state of Montana," the governor said at the time. State Rep. Robyn Driscoll, a Democrat who got a grade of F from Mr. Marbut's political-action committee, called the bill "absolutely appalling," saying legislators "would not support funding for education or women's clinics or anything like that, but they'll pass this blatantly unconstitutional bill and pay for the Supreme Court fight." Infusing the dry concept of commercial regulatory authority with the emotional issue of gun rights was political "genius," says Barak Orbach, a University of Arizona law professor who has studied Mr. Marbut's impact on lawmaking. On his website, Mr. Marbut posted a seven-step plan for attacking the federal commerce power, centering on passage of Firearms Freedom Acts. Enthusiasts got them introduced in more than 20 states. Nine legislatures passed them. Only one governor vetoed the bill, Democrat Brad Henry of Oklahoma. Arizona Gov. Jan Brewer, a Republican, signed, even as she vetoed a similar measure applying to light bulbs. "Both bills invite lawsuits that would restore our Founding Fathers' vision of a limited federal government based on the 10th Amendment," said her veto message, but "the Firearms Freedom Act is the more immediate and practical vehicle for achieving the objective." Following a strategy pioneered by Thurgood Marshall and the civil-rights movement, Mr. Marbut rounded up allies to file friend-of-the-court briefs. In addition to gun groups, conservative advocacy organizations such as the Goldwater Institute filed briefs backing the court challenge. Mr. Marbut took an even more restrictive view of federal commerce power than did farmer Filburn. Where the farmer claimed his wheat was beyond congressional regulation because he didn't seek to market it, Mr. Marbut says Congress lacks authority over products even when they're sold, if not sold over state lines. As he predicted, he lost the first round in federal court. Federal District Judge Donald Molloy wrote that the Montana legislature was free "to riddle the statutory code with 'political statements'" but not to invent its own constitutional law. Mr. Marbut expects to fall short at the appeals court, as well. If he gets to the Supreme Court and loses there, he is readying a Plan B. He calls it "Sheriffs First," a bill that would authorize Montana sheriffs to arrest federal lawmen who enter their counties without permission. Credit: By Jess Bravin
event_description: Discussion of the constitutional challenge to federal authority over the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal court case
characters: Gary Marbut [Hero]; federal regulatory state [Villain]; Montana residents [Victim]
plot: Gary Marbut leads a movement to challenge federal authority over commerce through the Firearms Freedom Act, arguing that federal laws are based on a misinterpretation of Congress's power. He faces opposition from the federal regulatory state, which enforces existing laws, while gaining support from conservative groups and state officials. The outcome of his legal challenge could redefine the limits of federal power.
moral: The federal government has overstepped its constitutional authority, and there is a need to restore states' rights and limit federal power through legal challenges like the Firearms Freedom Act.

Title: Cable-TV Industry Wins a Round in Battle with Ascap
Text: The cable-television industry won a victory in its continuing battle with the American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers over the way in which cable companies pay for the music in programs they broadcast.    U.S. Magistrate Michael H. Dolinger in federal court in New York ruled that Ascap must issue so-called direct-to-the-viewer licenses to cable networks. For several years, Ascap has been trying to require two levels of licenses in cable television, one for the cable channel and another for local cable operators that transmit the channels to viewers.    "Everybody understands this to be a test case," said R. Bruce Rich, of Weil, Gotshal & Manges, which represented 16 cable programmers that joined Turner Broadcasting System Inc. in the action against Ascap. "Both Ascap and BMI {Broadcast Music Inc.} have rested their entire cable industry pricing strategy on their ability to license at both levels. This puts the propriety of that strategy at stake."    In his decision, Magistrate Dolinger reasoned that local cable operators shouldn't be subject to separate licenses because "they do not control what music is included in the network programming that they air." The magistrate said that similar logic was applied in the 1950s, when Ascap explored licensing both the broadcast networks and their local affiliates.    Bernard Korman, Ascap's general counsel, said Ascap will ask Magistrate Dolinger for permission to have the ruling reviewed by a federal appeals court. "It's a very important ruling. We think it's wrong and that he made a reversible error," said Mr. Korman.    The fees the cable networks will be required to pay Ascap have not yet been determined.    Not only is the decision viewed as a potentially serious blow to the pricing strategy of Ascap, but it may affect other litigation. An antitrust suit filed by cable operators and cable programmers against BMI, the other major performing-rights society, is pending in Washington.    However, Edward Chapin, BMI's general counsel, said the decision will not affect that group's dual-licensing plan because BMI isn't subject to the same consent decree that applies to Ascap rate disputes. The decision, he said, "isn't a flat prohibition of split licenses." Ascap signed a consent decree with the Justice Department in 1941 that regulates the manner in which it licenses copyrighted music for public performance.    The cable networks had also asked Magistrate Dolinger to approve licensing for specific programs, as opposed to the standard flat fees that cover all 45,000 Ascap composers and publishers. In his decision, the magistrate said the consent degree requires Ascap "to make both of these licenses available to cable program suppliers."    The Ascap attorney, Mr. Korman, said his client also will fight the per-program license requirement. Ascap has argued that the new technology for cable is very different from network broadcasting in ways that weren't foreseen when the original consent decree was drafted. "Just what is a program on MTV or CNN?" said Mr. Korman. "It isn't clear the way it is with the networks."    ---    AUDUBON SOCIETY SUES FDIC over wetlands once held by defunct S&L.    Conservationists and federal savings and loan regulators are tracking the California case, thought to be the first legal challenge to the regulators' handling of environmentally significant property.    The Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. holds 238 properties labeled as "environmental concerns," plus an unknown number of parcels that shelter endangered species, an FDIC representative said. The Resolution Trust Corp. also holds many environmentally sensitive properties seized from failed thrifts.    The suit contends that the FDIC violated the Endangered Species Act by selling its lien on an 85-acre marshland to a private developer without consulting the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. The suit, filed by the Sierra Club Legal Defense Fund on behalf of the Marin County Audubon Society, is pending in federal court in San Francisco.    The city of San Rafael, Calif., had allocated more than $570,000 to buy and conserve the land, which supports the endangered salt marsh harvest mouse and more than 100 species of birds. But the FDIC sold its lien -- acquired when the agency seized the State Federal Savings & Loan in Lubbock, Texas -- to a local developer.    FDIC attorneys say the agency isn't subject to the Endangered Species Act, which requires federal entities to consult the Secretary of the Interior before taking any action that might jeopardize endangered species or their habitats.    The FDIC has warned the local developer, Joe Lemon, that any projects he undertakes on the wetland must comply with environmental laws, said Debra Meyers, managing attorney for the FDIC's office in Irvine, Calif.    ---    COSTA RICAN WORKERS SUE over alleged injuries from pesticides.    The approximately 100 farm workers sued a Castle & Cooke Inc. unit and three pesticide manufacturers, claiming they had been injured by exposure to pesticides that were exported abroad, even after being banned in the U.S.    The plaintiffs say they were rendered sterile by exposure to the pesticides while working at banana plantations operated by Castle & Cooke's Standard Fruit Brands unit. The chemicals, known as nematocides, contained the compound dibromochloropropane, or DBCP.    Also named as defendants are Dow Chemical Co., Shell Oil Co., a unit of Royal Dutch/Shell Group, and Occidental Petroleum Corp.'s Occidental Chemical unit; all three made DBCP-containing products called Fumazone and Nemagon.    The Environmental Protection Agency banned use of DBCP in the U.S. in 1979, not long after workers at an Occidental facility in California were found to be sterile. The compound is suspected to be carcinogenic, according to the suit.    U.S. law permits manufacturers to export products that are banned in this nation.    The suit alleges the workers "suffered serious and permanent mental and physical injuries," and that the defendants were negligent and "knowingly concealed information concerning the observed health effects of DBCP-containing products."    Last year, the Texas Supreme Court ruled that foreign plaintiffs have the right to bring suit in the state's courts for damages they allege were caused by companies doing business in Texas.    Louis Robles, who represents the plaintiffs, says the suit raises the issue of whether "companies in this country, who know that a product is harmful, can exploit the lack of an adequate {legal} system in foreign countries by selling their products there."    In Los Angeles, a Castle & Cooke spokeswoman, saying she hadn't yet seen the suit, declined to comment.    A spokesman for Occidental Chemical said the unit never shipped the pesticide DBCP to Costa Rica and doesn't believe any of its output of the pesticide was sent there. He said the company would argue that it shouldn't be part of the lawsuit.    Shell and Dow Chemical officials couldn't be reached.    ---    SAN FRANCISCO FIRM is paying new associates to delay reporting to work.    One measure of how the recession has hit law firms is Thelen, Marrin, Johnson & Bridges's offer of as much as $20,000 to law school graduates to postpone starting work at the firm. The 15 new lawyers promised jobs by the law firm's San Francisco office have been offered $10,000 to delay work for four months or $20,000 not to work for 10 months. The law firm's Los Angeles office offered 10 newcomers $20,000 to postpone joining the law firm.    Steven Hock, managing partner of the San Francisco office, said seven associates accepted the four-month offer; three accepted the $20,000 offer; and four are reporting to work. Mr. Hock said the law firm needed to cut costs and expects to save a "substantial amount" by staggering the starting dates of the class of 1991.    Mr. Hock said that offers to new associates to delay their law firm careers appears to be a new development. Jackson & Walker, a Dallas law firm, is believed to be the only other firm still in business to have made a similar offer.    Jackson & Walker's managing partner, Michael French, said that 12 of 15 new associates have accepted offers of $21,000 to postpone starting by one year. He said Jackson & Walker isn't suffering a fiscal crunch, but that too many law school graduates accepted job offers, probably as a result of the tight job market.    ---    NOTED: E. Lawrence Barcella Jr., a litigator specializing in white-collar criminal law, has joined the 300-attorney Chicago law firm Katten Muchen & Zavis as a partner. Mr. Barcella, a former assistant U.S. attorney for the District of Columbia, will work in the firm's Washington office.  Credit: Staff Reporters of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Marin County Audubon Society filed a lawsuit against the FDIC for allegedly violating the Endangered Species Act by selling marshland without consulting the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service.
setting: federal court case in San Francisco regarding the Endangered Species Act
characters: Marin County Audubon Society [Hero]; FDIC [Villain]; endangered salt marsh harvest mouse and more than 100 species of birds [Victim]
plot: The Marin County Audubon Society filed a lawsuit against the FDIC for selling marshland without consulting the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, which allegedly violates the Endangered Species Act, putting endangered species at risk.
moral: The FDIC must be held accountable for its actions that jeopardize endangered species, and it should consult the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service before making decisions that affect their habitats.

Title: Farmers Rail Against Plan For Ditches
Text: Okanogan County farmers are charging that the U.S. government is demanding they sacrifice too much to protect endangered and threatened fish, a complaint that could become common in the Northwest. The National Marine Fisheries Service has told the farmers they can't use six irrigation ditches running through the Okanogan National Forest in northern Washington until the agency drafts a plan that will essentially tell ditch operators what steps they must take to protect the fish. The Endangered Species Act gives the agency more than four months to produce the plan. The problem is that irrigation season started this week in the Methow Valley. Alfalfa and apple farmers with fields that rely on the decades-old, miles-long trenches say if they aren't allowed to irrigate soon, they'll see profits squeezed. "If we don't get any water, we're burnt up," says Jerry Sullivan, president of Skyline Ditch Co., the operator of one of the ditches, and a farmer who grows five acres of grass hay irrigated by Skyline. "It'll be devastating to see everything you own just go dry." The six ditches irrigate about 1,000 acres, most of which are farmed by producers who have other jobs to supplement their income. But the farmers and community leaders say there will be an eventual financial impact if the ditches aren't opened soon -- and that the situation in Okanogan County should put others on notice. "What's happening down here is the beginning of what's going to happen throughout the state, as far as restrictions put on people because of endangered species," says Darlene Hajny, a member of the Washington Farm Bureau in the county seat of Okanogan. Mike Grady, a senior policy analyst with NMFS in Lacey, says that while federal officials understand why there's grumbling in Okanogan County, their hands are tied. "Everyone is responsible now for complying with" the act, he says. "Every action we take is under scrutiny as well. We are all now sharing in the pain." The ditches that cross the Okanogan National Forest divert water from tributaries of the Methow River that are home to the endangered upper Columbia summer steelhead and spring Chinook salmon -- listed in August 1997 and March 1999, respectively -- and the threatened bull trout, listed in June 1998. Since the ditches run through national forest land, farmers need forest-service permits in order to draw water from them. And since the three fish are listed under the Endangered Species Act, farmers starting this year must also get the clearance from NMFS to operate the ditches. But that won't happen until the protection plan is completed. Fisheries-service officials -- who say they're working as fast as they can on the plan -- claim the farmers who operate the ditches didn't supply the information the agency needed soon enough for it to have wrapped up the process by now. The farmers -- and the national Forest Service, which is required under the Endangered Species Act to work with operators to gather information -- had to submit an assessment of how the ditches are endangering fish, what measures they are going to take to fix the problems and how they plan to pay for it. The farmers counter by saying NMFS is dragging its feet and endangering their crops. The Farm Bureau even sent a letter last month to the fisheries service saying "this issue has been prolonged to the point that it now jeopardizes the viability of our farmers in Okanogan County." The farmers and Forest Service add that they gave the fisheries service all the information about a year ago. And if NMFS had decided the information wasn't complete, it was required by law to state that in writing within 30 days -- something forestry officials say it never received. NMFS admits it never sent out such a written notice. It was something that slipped through the cracks, says Mr. Grady. But his agency did meet with forestry officials, at the latter agency's request, in November and told them what additional information was required, he says. The operators delivered the missing information last month. "Now, NMFS is in the awkward position of having just received the information, and then they expect {us} to open the ditches overnight," says Mr. Grady. Moreover, Mr. Grady says he thinks the ditch operators, most of whom also are farmers, were in "denial" when the fish-protection-plan process started last year. "They were hoping that it would go away and hoping it would be business as usual this year," he says. The farmers say that absolutely wasn't the case. Mr. Grady says NMFS will know within a few weeks whether it now has all the information it needs to draft the fish-protection plan; it will then have 135 days to complete it. "Hopefully, we'll be well under that timeline," Mr. Grady says. But even when the plan is finally completed, the feud probably won't end, since most involved expect the agency will order expensive fish-friendly improvements to bring the ditches into compliance with the Endangered Species Act. Biologists say the irrigation canals pose several possible threats to the threatened and endangered species. For example, the earthen berms built into the river and streams to divert water might block fish as they navigate the waterways. Some ditches either have inadequate or no screens to keep fish out of the ditches. And some of the trenches might suck in so much water that levels in the river and streams might fall too low to keep the fish alive. If the fisheries service finds that the latter is the case, it might order that the ditches be lined with concrete, which would reduce the amount of water lost through seepage into the soil. Or it might demand that the ditches be abandoned and replaced with pipelines, which would give operators more control over flow. Either option would cost ditch operators -- who are responsible for paying for the improvements -- millions of dollars. That has operators like Mr. Sullivan of Skyline Ditch wringing his hands. He estimates it will cost as much as $3 million for his ditch to put in pipelines and make it comply with the Endangered Species Act. "As I sit here now," he says, "without any money to pay for it, I see no way out of it."
