Indeed, Okanogan County commissioners are so worried that ditch operators won't be able to fund the costly improvements that county staff are helping the farmers apply for state, federal and private grants. The county fears that if the farmers don't get enough money, the irrigation channels can't be used. And that, says County Commissioner Dave Schulz, means a financial hit for the county as land values fall and unemployment rises. Meanwhile, some farmers say it won't matter what the plan mandates, because they might have quit the business by the time it's finally issued. Steve Devin, president of Early Winters Ditch Co., one of the six ditches, and a farmer near Mazama, figures his alfalfa fields will yield about two-thirds less this season if he can't irrigate. That would mean a loss of about $10,000 in alfalfa sales. That won't ruin him since he also raises oats on land irrigated by other ditches, and owns a liquor store and an inn in nearby towns. Still, he says, that's quite a bit of money to lose. And with the Endangered Species Act now an undeniable part of the agricultural business, it might be time to get out. "The hassles with the agencies, with trying to grow crops -- it just isn't worth it," Mr. Devin says. "Life's too short." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The National Marine Fisheries Service is drafting a plan to protect endangered and threatened fish, which affects irrigation operations in Okanogan County.
setting: Okanogan County, Washington, involving the National Marine Fisheries Service and the Endangered Species Act
characters: Okanogan County farmers [Victim]; U.S. government (National Marine Fisheries Service) [Villain]; endangered and threatened fish [Victim]
plot: Farmers in Okanogan County are restricted from using irrigation ditches due to a pending fish protection plan by the National Marine Fisheries Service, which they argue threatens their livelihoods. The farmers claim they provided necessary information to the agency, but delays in the plan's completion could lead to significant financial losses. The agency acknowledges the situation but insists compliance with the Endangered Species Act is necessary.
moral: The text suggests that the U.S. government must expedite the fish protection plan to prevent financial devastation for farmers, while also ensuring the protection of endangered species.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial): Out in the Cold
Text: Oil and gas drillers are usually assumed to be big boys who don't need much public sympathy, but it seems to us they should be entitled to the same legal protections as anyone else. Which is why we're interested in complaints by a group of drillers that a federal judge in Montana decided a lawsuit against the federal government that deprived the drillers of the use of valuable leases without their ever having been notified that the suit was in progress. The federal government had issued 711 exploration leases covering nearly 1.4 million acres in the Flathead and Gallatin national forests in Montana. Leaseholders and seismic companies had invested about $45 million in the properties. But in a suit brought by the Montana Wildlife Federation, U.S. District Judge Paul Hatfield last year found that the Bureau of Land Management and the Forest Service had violated procedural requirements in not drawing up environmental-impact statements on the effects of the oil and gas activities; environmental-impact statements, the judge said, are required by the National Environmental Policy Act and the Endangered Species Act. The judge found nothing wrong with the activities of the private oil and gas interests themselves. The leaseholders, however, say they were never notified of the suit. Mountain States Legal Foundation, based in Denver, has entered the case on behalf of some of the leaseholders. The foundation has asked the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit for an expedited hearing, which it hopes to receive this month, on its request for a new trial. The effect of the judge's decision, MSLF says, "was to revoke and cancel the property interests of the lessees." It says that if the court's decision is executed, the private owners "may lose their oil and gas leases, in violation of the most basic principles of due process." But just as interesting is the kind of mind-set that caused no one involved in the environmental-impact suit to think it important to notify the lessees. The Montana Wildlife Federation was mainly interested in targeting the federal bureaucrats, correctly surmising that they were vulnerable on any issue involving the intricacies of impact statements. The government's lawyers were concerned with fending off the environmentalists. And the judge, so we gather, didn't seem to think it very important that there were people with a $45 million stake who weren't being allowed to defend their interests in his court. In other words, all parties to the case assumed that this was strictly a fight between the environmental movement on the one hand and the federal bureaucracy on the other. Oil and gas drillers are big boys, but when two political giants are at war, mere property rights obviously don't even rate their day in court.
event_description: A lawsuit was filed regarding the lack of environmental-impact statements for oil and gas leases in national forests.
setting: a federal court case
characters: oil and gas drillers [Victim]; Montana Wildlife Federation [Villain]; federal government [Villain]; U.S. District Judge Paul Hatfield [Villain]; Mountain States Legal Foundation [Hero]
plot: The Montana Wildlife Federation filed a lawsuit against the federal government, leading to a judge's decision that deprived oil and gas drillers of their leases without notification. The drillers, represented by the Mountain States Legal Foundation, argue that their property rights were violated and seek a new trial.
moral: The problem is the lack of due process for oil and gas drillers in legal proceedings, and the text suggests that they should be notified and allowed to defend their interests in court.

Title: Ruling Aims to Aid Owl In Timber Sale Guidelines
Text: SAN FRANCISCO -- Federal timber policy has taken another shot from the courts.    Yesterday, the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals here held that the government's Bureau of Land Management must consult with federal wildlife officials on the bureau's overall guidelines for future timber sales in spotted owl territory. The court enjoined the agency from selling any timber for logging until it comes up with new timber sale guidelines in consultation with the Fish and Wildlife Service.    The bureau's current timber sale guidelines, known as the Jamison strategy after the Bureau of Land Management's chief, Cy Jamison, were formulated in 1990 with no input from the Fish and Wildlife Service's owl experts. The court ruled that the failure to consult with the service violated the Endangered Species Act.    The court ruling has no immediate impact because the agency is already enjoined from selling timber in most of the Pacific Northwest under a ruling in a separate case in federal court in Portland. Nonetheless, it adds to a string of court decisions in which federal judges have ruled that the bureau and the Forest Service's timber opertions have broken numerous federal environmental laws.
event_description: The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled that the Bureau of Land Management must consult with federal wildlife officials on timber sale guidelines in spotted owl territory.
setting: federal court case
characters: Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals [Hero]; Bureau of Land Management [Villain]; Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; spotted owl [Victim]
plot: The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled that the Bureau of Land Management must consult with federal wildlife officials on timber sale guidelines in spotted owl territory, enjoining the agency from selling timber until new guidelines are established, due to previous violations of the Endangered Species Act.
moral: The Bureau of Land Management must prioritize consultation with wildlife experts to protect endangered species like the spotted owl and comply with environmental laws.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial): Red-Taped Housing
Text: Owning a home is part of the American dream, but for most people today it has become a mirage. The Census Bureau reports that 57% of American families can't afford a median-priced home near where they live. HUD Secretary Jack Kemp thinks many more people could afford to buy homes if the federal government used its leverage and withheld housing subsidies from communities with exclusionary zoning laws, goldplated construction requirements and exorbitant development fees. A special HUD commission on barriers to affordable housing recently reported that the cost of that kind of bureaucracy and red tape has added $15,000 to $30,000 to the cost of houses in many markets. It shouldn't surprise anyone then that nine out of 10 renters and three-quarters of Hispanic and black families are frozen out of the housing market. The commission's members represented a wide variety of views. They included builders, local government officials and advocates for low-income housing. Yet they all agreed on the need to cut red tape and housing costs. Their recommendations include: more exemptions from federal Davis-Bacon laws that require union-scale labor on federal housing projects, placing time limits on building-permit reviews, and an overhaul of the Endangered Species Act. The Stevens kangaroo rat recently became one of the largest "land-owners" in California when a 30-square-mile stretch of land worth $100 million was declared off-limits to development in order to protect the rat. Nancy Kaufman, a Fish and Wildlife Service official, defended the move by saying that humans have reached the limit on how far they can intrude on the environment. We guess that means a lot of people in the future will have to double up in apartments Soviet-style. Ms. kaufman isn't all that concerned about human habitats: "I'm not required by law to analyze the housing-price aspect for the average Californian." Secretary Kemp says that if local governments "want to preserve the spotted owl at the expense of prospective home buyers, they can. But they should do it without federal subsidies." He wants federal housing money withheld from communities that refuse to come up with a plan to remove all manner of regulatory barriers to affordable housing. Those barriers come in many shapes and sizes. Some Ohio towns require that cul-de-sacs be wide enough to accommodate the most modern fire equipment, even though such trucks are never used in residential areas. Other cities outlaw prefab housing, mobile homes or the renting out of single rooms in houses. Still others charge exorbitant development fees that represent nothing more than a backdoor way of raising taxes. Areas near Chicago and Seattle bar new homes on lots of less than five acres. Many strict zoning laws grow out of a natural concern that new development will lower property values and increase congestion. But property values have held up well in cities that have liberalized their zoning. As for congestion, exclusionary zoning often forces development into outlying regions. The results: suburban sprawl that leads to longer commutes, more traffic and an effective bar to any form of mass transit. The artificially high cost of housing also has broader social consequences. Police officers, firefighters and teachers often can't live in the community in which they work; studies show that can contribute to poor morale. The elderly often can't afford housing near their children. Regulations that add $40,000 to the cost of an average home in Orange County, California, mean that many local workers can't live closer than a 70-mile congested commute from their jobs. Curiously, the commission is largely silent on the impact of rent control on the supply of affordable housing. Rent control, now in effect in more than 200 American cities, has been called the most effective way to destroy cities short of carpet bombing. It discourages both the building of new housing and the rehabilitation of old buildings. The Department of Housing and Urban Development has a task force examining the impact of rent control but it hasn't issued its report, even though it was formed nearly two years ago. Still, the Kemp commission's report is a start toward developing a strategy to sweep away the bureaucratic underbrush that is choking off the supply of decently priced housing. If nothing is done, the children of today will be the first generation of Americans who won't be able to afford to live in the communities they grew up in.
event_description: The text discusses an overhaul of the Endangered Species Act as part of recommendations to cut red tape and housing costs.
setting: federal housing policy discussion
characters: Jack Kemp [Hero]; Nancy Kaufman [Villain]; American families [Victim]
plot: Jack Kemp advocates for withholding federal housing subsidies from communities with exclusionary zoning laws to make housing more affordable, while Nancy Kaufman defends regulations that limit development, negatively impacting American families who cannot afford homes.
moral: The problem of unaffordable housing is exacerbated by bureaucratic regulations and exclusionary zoning laws; the text suggests that the federal government should take action to cut red tape and promote affordable housing solutions.

Title: Courthouse Is the Likely Arena for a Salmon Deal
Text: As uncertain as the fate of Washington's salmon seems, there is one thing about its future that is increasingly likely: A federal judge, not lawmakers, will end up shepherding the effort to save several species from extinction. Environmental and other special-interest groups have expressed their concerns about some of the plans on the table. And environmental groups already are talking about the grounds on which they will sue. Even Gov. Gary Locke's top salmon policy adviser, Curt Smitch, says any salmon-recovery program approved by the Legislature this session is "guaranteed to be in court." But Mr. Smitch doesn't think that is anything to fear. Given the political difficulty of crafting a plan, the federal court system has become a part of the Locke administration's grand salmon plan: Lawsuits, the administration figures, will buy Olympia more time for negotiations with the dozens of interest groups with economic stakes in how soon-to-be-listed threatened species of the fish are to be preserved. "If we have a scientifically credible plan" from the Legislature, Mr. Smitch says, "my view is a judge is going to give us another year" to fine-tune it. Political analysts say the administration's strategy -- to get whatever it can from the Legislature and wait for the lawsuits -- isn't as odd as it might sound to some, given the salmon chaos in Olympia right now. Consider: The centerpiece of Gov. Locke's recovery plan, which would sharply tighten restrictions on the use of water by individuals and businesses throughout the state, was dead on arrival in both the Democrat-controlled Senate and in the House, split evenly between Democrats and Republicans. Furthermore, a deal to limit logging near salmon habitats, worked out by the timber industry, state and federal agencies and some tribes, is under heavy fire from tribes not involved in the negotiations, and from environmentalists who have powerful allies in the Legislature. Meantime, the clock is ticking: The National Marine Fisheries Service has signaled that this month it will list about seven species of trout, Steelhead or salmon as threatened, including the Puget Sound wild Chinook. Under the Endangered Species Act, if the state doesn't design a recovery plan for the fish within 90 days of their listings, the federal government must step in to prescribe one. The state would much rather that didn't happen. Even the federal fisheries service wants Olympia to come up with a blueprint on its own. "Politically, it's much more palatable when the feds don't come in," says Brian Gorman, an agency spokesman in Seattle. "We can't protect species unless we get broad support from the local, state and regional levels." So, because "the Legislature has to act this year or the feds will step in, there's huge pressure," says Jennifer Belcher, the state's commissioner for public lands. But that doesn't make consensus any easier. Indeed, the timber pact is being assailed by a number of groups, including the League of Women Voters, as a back-room sell-out that excluded public input. "Our first priority is to keep this bill [spelling out the timber deal] from moving forward," says Joan Crooks, executive director of the Seattle-based Washington Environmental Council. And that could very well happen in court. Under the pact, the state would limit harvests for 50 years near streams and on steep slopes. It calls for timber landowners to repair logging roads that bleed sediment into streams. In exchange for placing trees off limits, the industry would get a 20% reduction in the excise tax on logging. And state and federal officials have pledged to shield the industry from new environmental regulations. Environmentalists say the plan is inadequate because, among other things, it doesn't allow for flexibility should fish stocks or water quality and supply degenerate. "I'm already doing the research, looking into how to sue," says Toby Thaler, a Seattle lawyer with the environmental council. Lawmakers "don't leave any choice but to go to court if they pass that." Gov. Locke's water plan, meanwhile, is being savaged by business groups, builders and farmers. And there already has been talk that these groups could sue to keep it from moving forward. The water plan, as originally proposed, would restrict water use for lawns and car-washing; crack down on the illegal use of ground and surface water; and require timber harvesters to leave more trees along streams and ranchers to keep cattle away from waters. The plan also would shorten salmon fishing seasons and ban it outright in certain areas; and it would force developers to provide greater protection for wetlands. The plan's most controversial elements -- allowing farmers to "spread" the water they conserve if they return half the saved water to streams, and eliminating the water-right exemption now given to small wells -- have already been eliminated in a much-amended version of the plan heard last week. Despite all the discord, some sort of salmon safekeeping document -- which Gov. Locke also wants to include $200 million in federal and state money -- is likely to be passed out of both houses. But what it will look like, and how comprehensive it will be, is anyone's guess. Ms. Crooks, executive director of the environmental council, views federal court "as a last resort," because lawsuits take time and money -- and while they are making their way through the system, threatened-species populations could continue to decline. But Mr. Smitch, the policy adviser, says that any proposal to safeguard salmon will have "too big of an impact to our way of life" not to be challenged in court by "those who think it does too much or those who think it's not enough." And though the Locke administration might get what it wants -- breathing room to continue negotiating with all sides -- if a federal judge assumes control, there may be unintended consequences as well. "We need some leadership at the state level," says Todd True, an environmental lawyer with Earth Justice in Seattle. "A failure to take the steps to save the salmon is going to be viewed very dimly and the public is not going to like it." ---                  Road to Preservation    Fish likely to be listed this month under the Endangered Species Act:    -- Puget Sound: Chinook Salmon; Hood Canal Summer Chum   -- Washington Coastal Waters: Lake Ozette Sockeye   -- Upper Columbia River: Spring Run Chinook   -- Middle Columbia River: Steelhead   -- Lower Columbia River: Chum; Chinook    Source: Washington State Department of Fish and Wildlife Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Several species of salmon and trout are likely to be listed as threatened under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: political context surrounding salmon recovery efforts in Washington State
characters: Environmental groups [Hero]; Gov. Gary Locke's administration [Villain]; Salmon species [Victim]; Timber industry [Villain]; Public [Victim]
plot: Environmental groups are preparing to sue over inadequate salmon recovery plans, while the Locke administration seeks to delay federal intervention through legal maneuvers. The timber industry is negotiating a deal that is criticized for lacking public input, and the public faces potential harm from both inadequate protections and the political stalemate.
moral: The problem is the failure of state leadership to create an effective salmon recovery plan, which risks federal intervention and further decline of salmon populations. The text suggests that immediate, comprehensive action is necessary to protect salmon and satisfy public and environmental concerns.

Title: Cross Country: NOAA's Plan to Save the Whales Has Maine Lobstermen Boiling
Text: I spent part of my first summer as a former governor working at a coastal Maine restaurant. My customers included lobstermen, and a hot topic of conversation was the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration's attempts to protect the shrinking North Atlantic right whale population. NOAA has ordered Maine to recommend a plan this month to reduce the number of whale deaths in its coastal waters. There's only one problem: No whale deaths due to entanglements or ship strikes have been recorded in Maine waters since 1998. According to NOAA, entanglement in endlines -- the fishing ropes connecting traps on the ocean floor to buoys floating on the surface -- is the "leading cause of known right whale mortality." Facing pressure from environmental groups, the agency wants Maine to reduce the number of endlines deployed in its lobster fishery by 50%. The lobstermen I spoke with worry that meeting this target will gut the lobster industry. I was so concerned about what I heard that I wrote to President Trump, calling his attention to this issue. North Atlantic right whales numbered about 250 in the mid-1990s, but by 2015 had rebounded to an estimated 458. The population has now fallen nearly to 400 due to fewer calves being born and a spike in deaths. The loss of right whales is a problem, but penalizing Maine lobstermen won't save them. The Maine lobster fishery and its supply chain contribute more than $1.7 billion to Maine's economy annually. In 2018 Maine lobstermen hauled in a record 54,000 tons of the red crustacean. Lobster has practically become synonymous with the tourist destination known as "Vacationland." Maine attracts about 37 million visitors each summer and lobster is frequently on their menus as well as on the souvenirs they take home. Tariffs and bait shortages have hurt the industry, but the lobstermen I spoke to this summer told me that NOAA's proposed regulations could force many of them out of business. There is no evidence that making Maine's loberstermen pull their lines out of the water would save even one whale. Twenty years ago the local industry changed the type of ropes used for traps and added special links that whales could easily break. The whale population almost doubled after these and additional innovations were adopted in 2009 and 2014. The new regulations would affect only lobstering by Mainers in the Gulf of Maine. But North Atlantic right whales no longer swim in the Gulf of Maine because the shrimplike plankton they eat have shifted to the cooler waters near the Gulf of St. Lawrence in Northern Canada, where heavy shipping traffic puts them at greater risk. Lobstermen cooperated when the data supported changes, but recent data doesn't support further regulation of Maine's lobster industry. Instead it suggests the need for additional negotiation with Canada for more-effective protections. A June study published in the journal Diseases of Aquatic Organisms reviewed the circumstances surrounding 70 known right whale deaths between 2003 and 2018. Entanglement was identified as the cause of death in 22 cases, of which 14 involved Canadian snow crab fishing gear. One death involved U.S. gear, and the remainder were unidentified. Eight right whales have died so far this year, all in Canadian waters; four died from ship strikes, two died from entanglement. Reducing the number of Maine's endlines won't stop whale deaths from ship strikes in Canadian waters. Studies also indicate ocean noise from ships could be a significant source of whale stress, possibly contributing to the lack of calves being born. Maine's 4,500 commercial lobster fishermen run small businesses. In many cases their families have gone to sea for decades. They care deeply about protecting the environment, including whales, and manage one of the Earth's most sustainable fisheries. Meeting NOAA's proposed endline target will cost these small businesses significant revenue and force many fishermen to quit the industry. Furthermore, the regulations will needlessly increase the danger of what is already one of the most hazardous occupations in America. Halving the number of endlines means doubling the number of traps per line. This will add weight to Maine's small lobster boats, making them unstable and increasing the risk of fishermen getting caught in trap lines, falling overboard and drowning. Instead of implementing ineffective regulations in an area where they are unlikely to make a difference, NOAA should focus on where the whales are dying, which isn't the Gulf of Maine. The Commerce Department should address whale protection in future trade and environmental agreements with Canada. A good first step would be for the Trump administration to place a moratorium on these regulations, while a lawsuit being brought against NOAA by conservation groups under the Endangered Species Act moves forward. Maine's entire lobster industry is in turmoil over this. Thousands of small businesses at the heart of the state's tourist economy can't plan what their next year will look like. Let's use common sense and science to truly protect both the right whales and one of Maine's most valuable industries. --- Mr. LePage, a Republican, was governor of Maine from 2011 to 2019.   Credit: By Paul LePage
event_description: A proposed moratorium on NOAA regulations affecting Maine's lobster fishery.
setting: NOAA's regulatory context regarding Maine's lobster fishery
characters: NOAA [Villain]; Maine lobstermen [Victim]; North Atlantic right whales [Victim]; Paul LePage [Hero]
plot: NOAA pressures Maine to reduce endlines in lobster fishing to protect right whales, but lobstermen fear this will harm their industry without evidence it will save whales.
moral: The problem is that NOAA's proposed regulations are ineffective and harmful to Maine's lobster industry; instead, NOAA should focus on whale protection measures in Canadian waters.

Title: Proposed bill signals battle over guarding endangered species
Text: Staff Reporters of The Wall Street Journal "The coalitions delivered your ESA bill to me on Friday," began the memo, written by an aide to Sen. Gorton. "I know that you are anxious to get the bill introduced; however, it is important that we have a better than adequate understanding of the bill prior to introduction." Sen. Gorton maintains that outside groups, though consulted, didn't write the bill. The measure is considered unlikely to advance because Sen. John Chafee (R., R.I.), chairman of the Senate Environment and Public Works Committee, doesn't support it. Neither do many of Sen. Gorton's conservative allies, because the bill wouldn't compensate private-property owners whose activities are restricted by U.S. actions to protect endangered species. However, the bill does reflect many of industry's concerns about the Endangered Species Act. And its introduction marks the beginning of a high-stakes battle that's likely to result in major changes to the law, which is very unpopular in Congress. The American Forest and Paper Association, which yesterday praised Sen. Gorton's bill, is said to have played a strong role in crafting it. Asked if the group wrote the bill, Kent Jeffries, a conservative private-property rights advocate for the National Center for Policy Analysis, said, "Oh, I'm sure the AFPA had help. There's no question that lobbyists made a number of very strong suggestions." But Barry Polsky, a spokesman for the timber group, said, "We did not write the bill," though it "does reflect input from AFPA." Sen. Gorton's bill came under heavy fire yesterday from Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt and environmental groups. "It will repeal the Endangered Species Act," said Mr. Babbitt. Environmentalists were concerned that the bill would allow private-property owners to alter the habitat of endangered species; as currently interpreted, the law doesn't allow such activity, although this regulation has been challenged in a case now before the Supreme Court. Meanwhile, some less-established industry groups are gearing up to fight for a more drastic overhaul of the endangered-species law. Project Common Sense has raised $125,000 toward its $500,000 goal to drum up support for such a move. Even though his group urged activists to thank Mr. Gorton for introducing his bill, project founder Tom Talbot derided it as "just changing some words around" in current law. Among other things, Mr. Talbot wants to require the government to compensate negatively affected property owners. Mr. Talbot, owner of a Wisconsin lumber company who is one of House Speaker Newt Gingrich's big-money donors, hired Eddie Mahe, a political consultant who is close to the speaker, to run Project Common Sense. Mr. Mahe's firm, which worked on Sen. Gorton's last campaign, is developing geographically tailored pro-overhaul messages for use by allies on and off Capitol Hill. "Whoever has the fuzzies on their side wins, because this country has an addiction to fuzzy animals," said Mr. Mahe. WASHINGTON -- Sen. Slade Gorton (R., Wash.), firing the opening shot in what is sure to become a major legislative battle, introduced a bill that would substantially water down the Endangered Species Act. Even before it was introduced, Mr. Gorton's bill stirred controversy last month when the Seattle Post-Intelligencer published excerpts from a Feb. 28 memo suggesting that the bill had been drafted largely by lobbyists for timber and other industries.
event_description: Sen. Gorton introduced a bill aimed at significantly altering the Endangered Species Act.
setting: legislative chamber debate
characters: Sen. Gorton [Hero]; Sen. John Chafee [Villain]; Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt [Villain]; environmental groups [Victim]; American Forest and Paper Association [Hero]; Tom Talbot [Villain]
plot: Sen. Gorton introduced a bill to reform the Endangered Species Act, which faced opposition from Sen. Chafee and environmental groups, while receiving support from industry groups like the American Forest and Paper Association. The bill is seen as unlikely to advance due to lack of support from key figures and concerns about its impact on endangered species.
moral: The text suggests that the Endangered Species Act needs reform to better accommodate private-property owners, but warns that the current proposal may not adequately protect endangered species, indicating a need for a more balanced approach.

Title: U.S. News: Gray Wolf to Lose Federal Protections Under Proposal
Text: The Trump administration is moving to delist the gray wolf from Endangered Species Act protections in the Lower 48 states, setting the stage for another potential clash over environmental policies with green groups and Democrats in Congress. Acting Interior Secretary David Bernhardt said Wednesday the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service would soon propose a rule to delist the gray wolf, which has enjoyed federal protection since it became one of the first species designated endangered in 1974. Management of the large predator would instead be turned over to states and tribes. A spokesman for the wildlife agency said the action is justified because wolf numbers have rebounded greatly in places where they were nearly exterminated in the last century. "Recovery of the gray wolf under the Endangered Species Act is one of our nation's great conservation successes," said the spokesman. Environmental groups disputed that conclusion, calling the action unwarranted and claiming it was intended to benefit hunters and ranchers. "The Trump administration is dead set on appeasing special interests that want to kill wolves," said Collette Adkins, a senior attorney at the Center for Biological Diversity, an environmental nonprofit in Tucson, Ariz., that has previously sued the Fish and Wildlife Service to restore endangered species listings. Ranchers have argued for years that a growing wolf population has threatened their herds in Western states including Idaho and Montana after being reintroduced in Yellowstone National Park in 1995. Depredation by wolves is reaching critical levels, with some counties in Oregon and Washington seeing dozens of livestock killed by the animals every year, said Ethan Lane, senior executive director with the Public Lands Council, a Western cattlemen's trade group based in Washington. "The gray wolf has exceeded all expectations and benchmarks for recovery across the country," Mr. Lane said in an email. "Activists should be celebrating this as an ESA success story, rather than working to prove that the Act is broken by fighting to keep a fully recovered species on the list." Federal officials estimate there are more than 5,000 gray wolves in the Lower 48 states, compared with a few hundred in the 1970s. That figure doesn't include a subspecies of Mexican gray wolf that would remain under endangered species protection. Alaska is home to as many as 10,000 wolves, which aren't considered endangered. Over the past decade, the gray wolf in the Lower 48 has been delisted from the federal endangered species list in Idaho, Montana, Wyoming and parts of Oregon, Washington and Utah, but remains under the federal protections in the rest of the continental U.S. Other states where gray wolves can be found and are currently protected include California, Michigan, Minnesota and Wisconsin. If the proposed rule to delist the gray wolf goes through, it would likely precipitate a heated court battle with green groups that fought past efforts to roll back protections for species including the greater sage grouse. Once the proposed rule is published in the federal record, there is an opportunity for public comment, after which it may go into effect.   Credit: By Jim Carlton
event_description: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is proposing to delist the gray wolf from Endangered Species Act protections in the Lower 48 states.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: Trump administration [Villain]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; gray wolf [Victim]; environmental groups [Hero]; Collette Adkins [Hero]; ranchers [Victim]; Ethan Lane [Hero]
plot: The Trump administration proposes to delist the gray wolf from ESA protections, claiming recovery success, while environmental groups and advocates argue this action benefits special interests and threatens the species.
moral: The delisting of the gray wolf is unjustified and threatens its recovery; the audience should oppose the administration's decision and support continued protections for the species.

Title: Protected birds prohibit Pacific Lumber from logging in its grove, judge rules
Text: SAN FRANCISCO -- A federal judge barred Pacific Lumber Co. from logging in a company-owned ancient redwood grove because of the presence of protected seabirds.    In a decision that will reverberate in the escalating fight between private property rights and wildlife conservation, Judge Lewis Bechtle rejected arguments from Pacific Lumber that the Endangered Species Act, under which marbled murrelets are protected, applies only narrowly on private land.    The judge also bluntly criticized the company for misleading regulators about the presence of seabirds in the grove in an apparent attempt to win permission for logging.    The case pitted advocates of the marbled murrelet, a secretive bird that nests in coastal old-growth forests, against Pacific Lumber, a unit of Houston-based Maxxam Inc., which is controlled by Texas financier Charles Hurwitz. At issue was Pacific Lumber's effort to log in a grove known as Owl Creek in Northern California, one of the largest tracts of ancient redwoods left in private hands.    Among other strategies, Pacific Lumber had sought to have Judge Bechtle apply the so-called Sweet Home standard in the case. The Sweet Home standard emerged from a controversial decision by a federal appeals court in Washington last year. That court ruled that major provisions of the Endangered Species Act can't be enforced on private land. Pacific Lumber was the first of several companies or groups to cite the Sweet Home standard in cases outside the Washington court's jurisdiction; the company argued that the act couldn't prohibit logging in Owl Creek, even if it leads to murrelet deaths by destroying habitat.    The issue of enforcement of the Endangered Species Act on private land has become a key battlefront in the campaign by private property-rights groups and many members of Congress to weaken the act. And the Sweet Home case has become a touchstone for critics of the act. The U.S. Supreme Court has agreed to review the Sweet Home case.    In the Pacific Lumber case, however, Judge Bechtle Friday rejected Pacific Lumber's private property argument, ruling that the Sweet Home decision doesn't apply outside the Washington court's jurisdiction. In a 60-page opinion, the judge also rejected Pacific Lumber's arguments that its logging wouldn't significantly harm murrelets, writing that the logging would violate the Endangered Species Act by resulting in a "high probability" that the murrelet would become extinct in significant portions of its remaining range.    Pacific Lumber said it would appeal Judge Bechtle's ruling, contending that it was "contrary to law as determined in the Sweet Home case." The company also said the ruling was more evidence of the "depredations being inflicted" by the Endangered Species Act and called for changes in the law.    The case was also noteworthy for the glimpse it offered of the internal workings and attitudes of a big lumber concern grappling with conservation pressures. Judge Bechtle found "clear evidence" that Pacific Lumber's procedures for locating birds on its property and reporting murrelet sightings to regulators "either were designed to fail to find" the birds or otherwise were administered "with the intent to . . . grossly understate" the birds' presence. He also said critical information about murrelet sightings was "deleted or altered" in reports to regulators.    Regulators rely on reports from companies to determine if logging restrictions are warranted. A Pacific Lumber spokesman denied that the company ever misled regulators, and said it "stands by the integrity" of its surveying procedures.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A federal judge ruled against Pacific Lumber Co.'s logging efforts due to the presence of protected seabirds under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal court case
characters: Judge Lewis Bechtle [Hero]; Pacific Lumber Co. [Villain]; marbled murrelet [Victim]
plot: Judge Bechtle ruled against Pacific Lumber Co.'s logging efforts due to the presence of protected marbled murrelets, rejecting the company's arguments and criticizing its misleading actions regarding seabird presence.
moral: The Endangered Species Act must be enforced to protect vulnerable species like the marbled murrelet, and attempts to weaken its provisions should be opposed.

Title: Gingrich, critic of 'business as usual,' helps out special interests like 'any member of Congress'
Text: LITTLE ST. SIMONS ISLAND, Ga. -- Last Thanksgiving weekend, House Speaker-to-be Newt Gingrich headed off with his wife, Marianne, to visit this idyllic, private resort off Georgia's coast, which ordinarily charges as much as $500 a day.    On an island offering seven miles of beach, horseback riding and three gourmet meals a day, Mr. Gingrich had much to be thankful for. So too did his host, Michael Berolzheimer, a California timber executive whose family owns the unspoiled, 10,000-acre isle.    Three months earlier, Mr. Gingrich had written a letter to the U.S. International Trade Commission at the request of Mr. Berolzheimer's company, P&M Cedar Products Inc. and an affiliated group. Although Mr. Gingrich is an avowed free trader, his letter urged the commission to impose anti-dumping duties against pencil makers in Thailand and China. A group of U.S. pencil makers had petitioned for the trade protection, and P&M supported the effort because it is a major supplier of pencil wood. The petition was filed shortly before Thanksgiving 1993, when the Gingriches spent their first weekend on the island as Mr. Berolzheimer's guests.    "I am writing to make you aware of my support for this petition," Mr. Gingrich wrote.    Late last year, the ITC decided to permit anti-dumping tariffs on most pencils from China, but not from Thailand.    Speaker Gingirch came to power promising to change the "business as usual" culture of Washington. The special interest favor bank was to be closed. He railed against the Democratic majority for being "subservient to special interest groups, ineffective and unethical in its behavior."    But a review of Mr. Gingrich's communications with the executive branch over the years, obtained through the Freedom of Information Act, shows that he has used his position, in a series of noteworthy intercessions, to help his own friends with special connections.    Some of his own associates acknowledge privately that the fine lines that separate personal pursuits, political efforts and governmental actions appear to blur at times in Mr. Gingrich's quest for a new political order.    Former Minnesota Republican Rep. Vin Weber says Mr. Gingrich long has defined himself as a "national congressman, and he almost performed his constituent service in a national way. I have never known there to be anything venal about his approach . . . but now he's speaker, and he has to worry about appearances."    Mr. Gingrich declined to comment in detail for this story. "The basis for any of his [official actions] is the merit of the idea, not who's giving him the idea," says Gingrich spokesman Tony Blankley. All of his actions, "are consistent with his political philosophy," including advocating trade sanctions. "This story or others like it could be written on any member of Congress" because Mr. Gingrich's actions represent "the standard appropriate pattern for congressmen and senators in our form of government."    The House Ethics Committee is reviewing a host of complaints against Mr. Gingrich, including some alleged conflicts of interests involving contributors to the congressman's multimillion-dollar fund-raising operations, known collectively as Newt Inc.    In each of several cases disclosed in recent months, Mr. Gingrich has denied any connection between his official actions and contributions or other favors. Yet the timing in some additional cases that have now come to light suggests at least extraordinary coincidences.    On April 29, 1994, for example, the Georgia Power Co. made a $7,500 tax-deductible donation to the Progress and Freedom Foundation to help finance Mr. Gingrich's nationally televised college course. Ten days later, he wrote the Securities and Exchange Commission on behalf of Georgia Power's parent, Southern Co., which had grown impatient with the agency 10 months after filing an application to form a wireless communications subsidiary. "I would, therefore, urge the Commission to act expeditiously, whether favorably or unfavorably," Mr. Gingrich wrote SEC Chairman Arthur Levitt. (The SEC approved the application late last year.) Southern Co. spokesman David Mould says the letter was routine and similar to ones sent on its behalf by other lawmakers.    In another case, on Sept. 25, 1989, Southdown Inc. of Houston gave $10,000 to Gopac, Mr. Gingrich's political action committee. A day later, cement makers from Arizona, Florida, New Mexico and Texas petitioned the ITC and the Commerce Department for anti-dumping tariffs against Mexican competitors.    A month later, Mr. Gingrich, who had been elected GOP whip earlier that year, was one of 35 House members who signed a letter to the ITC on behalf of the U.S. cement makers. The letter urged "a complete and thorough investigation" of the petitioners' complaint. Mr. Gingrich was the only signatory who wasn't from one of the four states or with a petitioner's headquarters in his state. Eventually, the U.S. cement makers, after having failed in five previous tries, won the fight, and the Bush administration imposed high tariffs on imported cement, causing U.S. prices to rise.    Craig Murphy, a spokesman for GOP Rep. Joe Barton of Texas, says that Mr. Barton solicited the donation as Gopac's Texas chairman and gathered signatures for the letter, but that there was no connection between the two. The donation had been solicited and approved months earlier, Mr. Murphy and a Southdown executive say.    At other times, Mr. Gingrich's actions have been at the request of personal friends. On Feb. 3, 1992, Mr. Gingrich signed a page-and-a-half letter to William Riley, the Bush administration's Environmental Protection Agency administrator, "on behalf of my constituent [Administrative Law] Judge Thomas B. Yost of Atlanta, Georgia. He advises me that the EPA is in the process of requiring that he be moved to EPA headquarters in Washington." Mr. Gingrich opposed the move. The Office of Personnel Management ultimately approved the transfer and Mr. Yost retired.    In fact, Mr. Yost did not live in Mr. Gingrich's district. "I don't think he was ever my official congressman," says Mr. Yost. Moreover, EPA officials were motivated in part by the fact that a subordinate had earlier accused Mr. Yost of sexual harassment, and the charge had been substantiated, former officials involved in the matter say. Some hoped he would quit rather than move and face disciplinary action. Mr. Yost admits acting inappropriately, and blames a "manic-depressive" condition.    The Gingrich letter came about because Mr. Yost had a friend who worked for the Richmond, Va., law firm that also employed Jim Tilton, an adviser and contributor of Mr. Gingrich's whom the congressman has called "my best and closest friend since high school days." (Mr. Tilton died in 1993.) Mr. Yost called his friend, who called Mr. Tilton, who contacted the congressman.    "I wrote it, and he signed it," Mr. Yost said. He says he has no reason to believe Mr. Gingrich was aware of the sexual harassment charge.    P&M Cedar, Mr. Berolzheimer's Stockton, Calif., company, employs no one in Georgia, nor does the state produce any incense cedar, the wood used to make most pencils. Mr. Gingrich and Mr. Berolzheimer met in February 1993 at a fundraiser for California Rep. Richard Pombo, according to Jean Wilhoit, P&M's public affairs director and the Incense Cedar Institute's executive director. They got to talking, and Mr. Berolzheimer told Mr. Gingrich about his family's island.    Mr. Berolzheimer then invited Mr. Gingrich and his wife to spend the 1993 Thanksgiving weekend on the island, when it is closed for the winter holidays, says Debbie McIntyre, the resort's manager. The Gingriches stayed for two or three days for free and "had a great time," she says.    Ms. Wilhoit says that the Gingriches and perhaps one other family member were Mr. Berolzheimer's guests again this past Thanksgiving, shortly after the election that catapulted Mr. Gingrich to the speakership. Mr. Berolzheimer declines to be interviewed.    Mr. Gingrich wrote his letter to the ITC on Aug. 16, 1994. "We requested it," says Ms. Wilhoit. Gingrich spokesman Blankley says the letter doesn't conflict with Mr. Gingrich's free-trade philosophy. "It's not protectionist to make sure there are not unfair trading practices against American firms," Mr. Blankley says.    P&M officials have let it be known to some in the lumber industry that Mr. Berolzheimer and Mr. Gingrich were friends and have requested briefing papers on forestry issues facing Congress, says Frank Gladics, vice president of the Independent Forest Products Association.    "People within P&M remind us constantly, on any issue we're working on, to send us paper . . . because their boss knows Gingrich," Mr. Gladics says. He says he and others sent briefing papers to P&M Cedar on everything "from soup to nuts" on timber issues shortly before last Thanksgiving.    The papers discussed the Endangered Species Act, appropriations matters and forest health issues, including the industry's desire to expedite salvaging billions of board feet worth of fire- and disease-damaged trees from federal land, Mr. Gladics says. He adds that the salvage issue is important to every timber company in the west, including P&M Cedar.    Mr. Blankley says Mr. Gingrich has "a vague recollection that someone brought up something about forest issues" during his stay on the island. He told the person to call his staff in Washington.    Last month, the House easily approved the salvage measure backed by the timber and home-building industries as part of a spending-cut bill. As the salvage provision worked its way through the Appropriations Committee, says Chairman Robert Livingston of Louisiana, Mr. Gingrich personally gave it his blessing.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The House approved a salvage measure related to timber and forest health issues.
setting: legislative chamber or committee debate
characters: Newt Gingrich [Villain]; Michael Berolzheimer [Villain]; U.S. pencil makers [Victim]; Timber and home-building industries [Hero]
plot: Newt Gingrich, influenced by his connections with timber executive Michael Berolzheimer, supports anti-dumping duties that benefit P&M Cedar Products, while U.S. pencil makers suffer from these trade protections. The House then approves a salvage measure that benefits the timber industry, which Gingrich endorses.
moral: The problem is the unethical influence of special interests on legislative actions, and the text suggests that Congress should prioritize transparency and fairness in policymaking to prevent conflicts of interest.

Title: U.S. News: U.S. Watch
Text: ATLANTA Man Faces Arson Charges in I-85 Fire The man accused of starting the fire that destroyed part of Interstate 85 north of downtown Atlanta last week faces charges of first-degree arson and property damage. Basil Eleby, who was arrested Friday and charged Saturday, told city and federal investigators that he had discussed smoking crack cocaine with two companions under the highway overpass before deciding to do the drug alone, according to his arrest affidavit. The flames that lighted up Atlanta's skyline Thursday burned so hot the concrete and steel overpass disintegrated. Russell McMurry, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Transportation, said repairs will take at least several months and 350 feet of highway must be replaced in both directions on I-85, which carries about 400,000 cars a day through Atlanta. Mr. Eleby's bond was set at $200,000 pending his next court appearance on April 14. -- Associated Press --- WYOMING Grizzly-Bear Habitat Expected to Expand Grizzly bears continue to expand their range amid an effort to turn over management of the bears from the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service to the states of Wyoming, Montana and Idaho, a federal official said. "We've seen an 11% change in increasing range in just a couple of years," Frank van Manen, head scientist of the Interagency Grizzly Bear Study Team, said at a meeting last week in Jackson, Wyo. Since coming under the protection of the Endangered Species Act, grizzlies have expanded their habitat outward from the population's core in Yellowstone National Park. The population of grizzlies within an area monitored annually has fallen for two consecutive years, from about 750 to 690. But Mr. van Manen said grizzly numbers outside the monitoring area aren't counted and he believes the population is now at the highest point in decades. Center for Biological Diversity attorney Andrea Santarsieri said he was concerned by the decline in grizzly numbers and worried that hunting could soon occur near the Yellowstone and Grand Teton park boundaries if states gain management authority. A final rule to delist the Yellowstone-area grizzly bear as a federally protected endangered species will be released as early as June, federal officials said at the meeting. -- Associated Press --- THE SOUTH Storms Prompt 'High Alert' in Louisiana A storm system with hurricane-force winds crawled across the Deep South, damaging homes and businesses Sunday. Louisiana Gov. John Bel Edwards put the entire state on "high alert" and warned residents to stay off the roads. Parts of Arkansas and Mississippi were also under threat of tornadoes, but the bull's-eye was on Louisiana into Monday morning, with the system bringing unconfirmed tornadoes to the state as well as heavy thunderstorms, large hail and flash flooding. National Weather Service warned that it was a "particularly dangerous situation." Straight line winds could reach upward of 80 miles an hour. Hurricanes have winds of at least 74 mph. -- Associated Press
event_description: A final rule to delist the Yellowstone-area grizzly bear as a federally protected endangered species will be released.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: Frank van Manen [Hero]; Andrea Santarsieri [Victim]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]
plot: Frank van Manen reports on the expanding range of grizzly bears, while Andrea Santarsieri expresses concern over the declining population and potential hunting if states gain management authority, leading to a final rule to delist the Yellowstone-area grizzly bear.
moral: The decline in grizzly bear numbers is a significant problem, and the text suggests that management should remain with federal authorities to prevent hunting and protect the species.

Title: The Week Ahead / Our Take On Coming Events
Text: Japanese Economy: Gloom, if Not Doom, Expected In Survey of Business Confidence ---- By Sebastian Moffett Japan's economy has lately run into a storm of troubles. An indication of how those gales are hindering economic growth will come Tuesday, when the Bank of Japan announces the results of the latest tankan, its quarterly survey of business confidence. Economists expect to see less optimism, accompanied by pared-back investment plans, something that would hurt suppliers and ripple through the economy. The country is heavily dependent on exports for growth. But the yen has jumped 11% this year against the dollar, making Japanese exports less competitive and crimping the value of the revenues they yield in yen. The U.S. economy is slowing, threatening Japan's biggest export market for automobiles. Domestically, confidence is fading. The government, bogged down in parliamentary bickering, hasn't been able to appoint a central-bank governor to succeed the one who left office March 19. Such ineffectiveness is dashing hopes that the government will make moves -- such as free-trade agreements or measures to strengthen financial services -- that might promote more robust long-term growth. Concern over the future makes consumers more likely to save and less likely to spend: Last year, consumption contributed just 0.8 percentage point of Japan's 2.1% growth, even though consumption accounted for 55% of gross domestic product. Trade contributed 1.2 percentage points. Pessimistic investors have dumped some of their Japanese shares, and the stock market has lost a quarter of its value in the past half- year. Japan's economy has probably slowed to an annualized growth rate of just 1% or so in the quarter ending March 31, economists say. A further downturn would raise the possibility of simultaneous recessions in the world's two largest economies, the U.S. and Japan. But just how bad are things beneath the gloom? The tankan asks around 10,000 corporations to rate business conditions as "favorable," "not so favorable" or "unfavorable." It subtracts the number replying "unfavorable" from the number saying "favorable," and turns this into a percentage of the total. Three months ago, the headline index for large manufacturers was 19, but on Tuesday it will slip to around 12, economists forecast. The survey matters because it helps the BOJ forecast future price trends and figure out the appropriate monetary policy. The central bank already targets a very low short-term interest rate of 0.5%, because inflation is just 1%, most of which comes from rising energy prices. But some economists think there's a chance that the bank might lower that soon, because of Japan's slowing economy and world financial problems. Goldman Sachs's Japan economists, for example, last week forecast a cut in the rate target to 0.25%, either at the bank's policy board meeting on April 8 and 9 or at a later one April 30. Prices in financial markets implied a 7% chance of a cut at the next meeting. Still, the bank would cut the rate target only very reluctantly. Its abandonment of a five-year, zero-interest-rate policy in 2006 was taken as a declaration of victory over price falls that started in 1999 and slowed economic activity. Congress: Democrats to Press Oil Executives on Tax Incentives -- and Polar Bears ---- By Stephen Power Big Oil will be making an appearance on Capitol Hill this coming week as congressional Democrats open hearings on two topics important to the industry. Top executives from Exxon Mobil Corp., BP PLC, Royal Dutch Shell PLC, Chevron Corp. and ConocoPhillips are expected to testify Tuesday before the House Select Committee on Energy Independence and Global Warming, in an event designed by Democrats to tap voters' frustration with high gasoline prices. With Exxon Mobil and Chevron recently reporting record profits, House Democrats are pushing legislation that would repeal $18 billion of tax incentives for oil and gas producers and use the savings to finance tax credits and other incentives for wind-power projects, solar panels and more energy-efficient cars. Their measure isn't likely to become law anytime soon, however. A similar proposal died in the Senate in December, and the White House has threatened a veto of the current legislation. Not surprisingly, the American Petroleum Institute, an oil-industry trade group in Washington, is voicing its objections. The group says the bill would drive production overseas and make it harder for U.S. oil companies to compete against global rivals. "We don't have an objection to funding these alternative [energy] sources, but there's no justification for singling out the oil industry" while other manufacturers continue to enjoy tax breaks, says John Felmy, a senior economist with the petroleum group. Meanwhile, in another policy dispute with big implications for oil producers, Senate Democrats plan a hearing Wednesday on the Bush administration's failure to meet a statutory deadline for deciding whether the polar bear should be designated a threatened species. In keeping with timetables set by the Endangered Species Act, the Interior Department was supposed to reach a decision on the matter in January, after announcing back in December 2006 that the bear's Arctic habitat "may literally be melting." Days before the deadline, however, the agency's Fish and Wildlife Service said it needed more time, citing the complexity of the issue and the need to review public comments on new scientific research. Environmental groups have sued the Interior Department in federal court over the delay, arguing that the agency has all of the information it needs, and the agency's inspector general has opened an inquiry into the matter. Oil companies have lobbied against listing the bear as threatened, arguing that such a move isn't warranted based on the bears' current population and that listing the animal as threatened will trigger costly new restrictions on their operations in Alaska. The number of polar bears globally is estimated at 20,000 to 25,000. An Interior Department spokesman Friday declined to offer a timetable for reaching a decision on the bear's status. Commodities: New Grain Report Is Likely to Make Waves ---- By Lauren Etter Investors from Wall Street to the Great Plains will be watching Monday morning when the U.S. Department of Agriculture releases its annual report on how much farmers will be planting of which grains. The report holds big implications for livestock farmers, ethanol plants, food companies and consumers. The USDA report gives the planting season's first official peek at how much corn, wheat, soybeans and other grains farmers intend to plant. Traders make investment decisions based on the expected number of planted acres, while farmers use the report to decide which crops might maximize their profits. Last year, the report stirred intense global interest as the USDA predicted -- correctly -- the largest planting of corn acres since World War II. With grain prices pushing higher, the new report will be just as closely scrutinized for how an expected reduction in corn acres ripples through the U.S. economy. Analysts have been predicting that the report will show that farmers intend to plant as few as 86 million acres of corn -- about seven million fewer acres than last year -- and more soybeans and wheat. Those expectations have been largely folded into current grain prices, so the report isn't likely to spur wild price swings Monday on the Chicago Board of Trade. Corn already is trading near its record-high price of $5.70 a bushel, more than double the price of two years ago. Soybeans are hovering near $13 a bushel, nearly double last year's level. But with stocks of corn and other staple grains lower than they have been in decades, grain markets are anything but stable. Agriculture exports are expected to rise to a record this year as China, India and other foreign nations clamor for grains to eat and to feed livestock. As corn-fueled ethanol production keeps growing, corn used for ethanol will make up more than 30% of the corn crop by next year, up from 14% in 2006. Packaged-food companies are hoping not to have to push further price increases for bread and other staples onto consumers. Already corn stocks at the end of this year's marketing year, which ends the last day of August, are projected to be the lowest in decades at 542 million bushels, says Joe Victor, a grains analyst at brokerage firm Allendale Inc. That is equivalent to about 28 days of corn. In 2005, the grain cupboards had about 70 days of corn. "We're living on the edge of a knife," Mr. Victor recently told a group of investors at the Chicago Board of Trade. "There is no room for error." Drought or flooding could make matters worse, sending corn and other prices higher. So far, reports from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration suggest that drought this year in the Corn Belt is less likely than previously thought. But some grain users have started to ration demand; livestock farmers, for example, are slaughtering parts of their herds -- in some cases large parts -- and sending the meat to market to manage the costs of feed grain. Farmers are doing their best to plant as much grain as possible, in some cases spooking environmentalists who fear row crops will overrun wetlands, duck habitats and wildlife preserves. Politics: McCain Hopes to Use Past as a Springboard ---- By Laura Meckler and Elizabeth Holmes After a quarter century in Congress, Sen. John McCain sets out to reintroduce himself to the American public in the coming week, stopping in a string of places that shaped his family, his values and his career. The campaign hopes to tie the past to the future, using Sen. McCain's history as a springboard to discuss how the man set to claim the Republican presidential nomination would govern. A stop at his high school, for instance, is an opportunity to talk about education. His visit to Mississippi, where the McCains first settled in 1848, offers the chance to discuss family values. Biography as political strategy isn't a new concept. At the same time in his campaign, in April 1996, Sen. Bob Dole began airing TV ads featuring his personal story. With the tour, "McCain's taking it a little further," said Scott Reed, Mr. Dole's campaign manager. There is plenty of material, much of it recounted in Sen. McCain's 1999 memoir, "Faith of My Fathers," co-written with Mark Salter, who remains his speechwriter and will be shaping the week's words. The week begins in Meridian, Miss., home to McCain Field, a naval air station named for his grandfather, Admiral John Sidney McCain. Sen. McCain was stationed there early in his career. Once, when he made the approach to land, he was waved off by the radio tower. "Let me land, or I'll take my field and go home," he recounts in his book. He earned a rebuke for "disrespectfully invoking the family history." On Tuesday, he goes to Alexandria, Va., outside of Washington, where he attended Episcopal High School, a boarding school. He plans to talk about how one of his teachers instilled in him a love of literature, which he says sustained him while held prisoner in Vietnam. From there it is on to the Naval Academy in Annapolis, Md., on Wednesday. As a student there, Sen. McCain routinely climbed over the academy walls "in search of a good time," he said in his memoir. At one point he had so many demerits that he feared expulsion, a point he often jokes about on the campaign trail. After graduation, Sen. McCain attended flight school in Pensacola, Fla., which will be a Wednesday-afternoon campaign stop. It isn't clear if his campaign speech will get into how, at the time, he dated an exotic dancer, known as "the Flame of Florida," who cleaned her fingernails with a switchblade. On Thursday, it's onto Jacksonville, Fla., where his family remained while he was in Vietnam. He winds up in his adopted home state of Arizona, where he will deliver a speech in Prescott, the same place where one of his heroes, Sen. Barry Goldwater, launched his 1964 presidential campaign. Of course, none of these places directly invoke the most notable event in his personal story -- his 5 1/2 years as a prisoner of war in Vietnam. But the campaign is covering that with a new TV ad, airing first in New Mexico.
event_description: Discussion on the polar bear's potential designation as a threatened species.
setting: congressional hearing
characters: House Democrats [Hero]; Oil companies [Villain]; Polar bears [Victim]; Interior Department [Villain]; Environmental groups [Hero]
plot: House Democrats are holding hearings to address the oil industry's tax incentives and the delay in the polar bear's threatened species designation, which is being obstructed by the Interior Department and opposed by oil companies.
moral: The delay in protecting polar bears is a significant problem caused by the Interior Department's inaction and the oil industry's lobbying efforts; immediate action is needed to ensure the polar bear's protection and to address the unfair tax incentives for oil companies.

Title: What's News: Northwest
Text: The Rules Are Out,    And the Suits Are In    The federal government made public its long-awaited salmon-protection regulations, finally opening the door to what observers always figured listings under the Endangered Species Act were all about: lawsuits. Once the so-called 4(d) rule for threatened and endangered salmon actually goes into effect in December, anyone who figures that anyone else isn't obliging by the rule -- which is basically a formal prohibition against hurting a salmon population or its habitat -- will have the right to sue under the ESA.    Of course, just the rule's publication was enough to get the lawyers busy. The Earthjustice Legal Defense Fund filed an intent-to-sue notice, complaining that the rule is too vague, and officials with the National Marine Fisheries Service said they expected to spend a lot of time in court in years to come.    Can't Compute    King County Executive Ron Sims sort of took the blame for a fruitless, $38 million, three-year effort to install new financial software in the county's computers. "This was done on my watch," Mr. Sims said, referring, actually, to what wasn't done: the integration of the county's separate payroll and accounting systems. Mr. Sims asked the King County Council for $7 million to fix the bits of the merger that were completed, and to study what should happen next. And what went wrong? No one is sure. "That's the most frightening thing; they still don't know," said Chris Vance, a council member from Auburn.    River Warning    The Washington departments of Health and Ecology advised pregnant women and young children against eating fish caught in the upper Spokane River, where tests showed higher levels of potentially toxic heavy metals in fish, particularly bottom-feeding suckers. Gov. Gary Locke said the findings add to evidence that some mining practices in Idaho's northern Panhandle have created health and environmental threats to Washington state, and called for a "full and thorough" cleanup of the Spokane River basin.    Rebates, Again    For the second time in two years, some 130,000 employers in Washington will receive refunds on their workers' compensation premiums, this time totaling $200 million. The chief reason for the windfall was the fabulous returns on stock-market investments. In cold cash, that means that eligible employers will get back 30.8% of premiums they were assessed by the state Accident Fund during the fiscal year that ended June 30, 1999.    Channel Challenge    The U.S. House Appropriations Subcommittee on Water and Energy denied a request by 10 representatives from the Northwest for $4 million to begin work on deepening the Columbia River to accommodate the new generation of deep-draft ocean freighters. That doesn't mean the $196 million dredging project is dead, but critics said the rebuff gives opponents even more time to muster support.    In the Chips    Semiconductor giant Intel Corp. of Santa Clara, Calif., tipped its hand about future expansions of its Oregon operations last week when it sought approval from environmental regulators for a proposed network of roads on vacant land it owns in Hillsboro. The company budgeted $6 billion for new investments this year and has announced several plant expansions, but so far hasn't said anything about what it plans for Oregon, where it employs 14,200.    Jolting News    Idaho Power Co. contributed to the dawning awareness that the Northwest is running out of cheap energy when it announced that an affiliate, Ida-West Energy, wants to build a gas-fired generating plant in southwest Idaho. The Boise utility said it must have new sources of energy by 2004 to meet demand.    Up in Smoke    The Anchorage Assembly voted 9-2 to ban smoking in most public places in Alaska's largest city. The law, which takes effect Dec. 31, will prohibit smoking in restaurants, sports arenas and bowling alleys. Bars and bingo halls that provide non-smoking sections won't be affected.    Big Swoop    Between 120,000 and 180,000 people shuffled through Paul Allen's $240 million Experience Music Project on its inaugural weekend, shelling out $19.95 for adult tickets to play with the interactive exhibits and ogle Mr. Allen's collection of rock memorabilia. The notoriously camera-shy Microsoft co-founder was in rare form, at one point smashing a Dale Chihuly glass guitar above his head and pronouncing, "Let the experience begin."
event_description: The publication of the salmon-protection regulations under the ESA has led to the initiation of lawsuits regarding compliance with the new rules.
setting: federal government rulemaking under the Endangered Species Act
characters: Earthjustice Legal Defense Fund [Hero]; National Marine Fisheries Service [Villain]
plot: The federal government published salmon-protection regulations, leading to lawsuits initiated by the Earthjustice Legal Defense Fund against the National Marine Fisheries Service for vague rules, which are expected to result in ongoing legal battles.
moral: The problem is the vagueness of the salmon-protection regulations, and the text suggests that clearer rules are necessary to prevent legal disputes and protect salmon populations effectively.

Title: Wildlife Office Gives Critics Policy Role
Text: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal But the agency's thinking began to shift earlier this year when Fish and Wildlife, under intense pressure from business and political leaders, was forced to back down on a proposal to protect the Alabama sturgeon. The dispute ended when the agency said it wouldn't list the bony bottom feeder as an endangered species, saying it was already extinct. In the wake of that fight, says Mr. Hartfield, "I think we learned you can't go in with the heavy hand of government and tell people what to do without them resenting it." The result is virtually unprecedented: allowing business leaders and environmentalists as well as the service's biologists a voice in drafting a recovery plan for the 15 endangered species in the Mobile Basin -- one of the most ecologically diverse, and troubled, areas in North America. If the coalition is able to agree on terms of a plan, the proposal will undergo the normal approval processes, including a 45-day public-comment period before it takes effect. Fish and Wildlife officials tried such a team approach once before. But that was in Louisiana and was done on a much smaller scale, negotiating with only a handful of landowners over the fate of the black bear. "We have just finished a really bloody battle" in Alabama, Mr. Hartfield says. "But if this project works, I think it will serve as a national example." In Alabama, the 50-member group -- put together with the help of the Alabama Department of Economic & Community Affairs -- is in for some contentiousness. Even after regulators decided not to list the sturgeon, the Wildlife Service's opponents have continued to criticize the agency as being out of control. "What should have been a scientific matter turned into a grudge match over at Fish and Wildlife," says William H. Satterfield, a coalition member and an attorney who led the fight against listing the sturgeon. Counters Chuck Hunter, a Fish and Wildlife biologist who monitors the Southeastern region: "Alabama has raised more opposition than any state in the Southeast. They are just against the Endangered Species Act." The immediate goal of the coalition is to mend the troubled relationship between the Fish and Wildlife Service and corporate leaders in such industries as paper, farming and mining. And while the group has met only twice, some critics of the agency say the meetings are a sign it is willing to listen. "This is certainly a step in the right direction," says John D. Grogan, who manages Alabama Power Co.'s environmental-compliance section. "In the past, those who were affected by the service's decisions felt like we had little input." Despite such conciliatory tones, serious disputes remain over substantive environmental matters, such as how to protect the basin. Fish and Wildlife biologists say the area is experiencing extinction rates unparalleled in the continental U.S. Most of the threatened and endangered species are small fish, freshwater mussels and clams that depend on the rocky gravel bars of the basin's rivers for their survival. Soil erosion from farms and clear-cutting of trees has smothered the natural habitats of the animals, biologists say. Dredging, a process that keeps the channels open to barge traffic, and mining near the rivers also have increased the buildup of silt and sediment in the rivers. Environmentalists warn that damage done to the state's rivers is severe. "We are past the point of being able to tweak one thing and restore the rivers' health," says Randall Haddock, a biologist with the Cahaba River Society, a Birmingham environmental group. "There are no magic bullets." But Jim DeCosmo, a manager at Scott Paper Co. in Mobile, after reading a 30-page draft recovery plan for the basin prepared by Fish and Wildlife biologists as a starting point for debate, says he worries about restrictions on dredging along the Tennessee-Tombigbee Waterway. His company depends on the 234-mile waterway for transporting timber from the more than 300,000 acres Scott Paper owns in Alabama. "If for some reason those rivers aren't dredged for a month, it could shut down the entire waterway and halt business for us," Mr. DeCosmo says. Before the battle over the spotted owl in the Pacific Northwest, public hearings on endangered species drew little notice in the Southeast. "A few people would show up and tell us they didn't believe us when we told them there wouldn't be any jobs lost," says the agency's Mr. Hartfield. "Then we would list the species and everyone would forget about it. The animals were in such isolated areas no one ever noticed." That changed with the battle over the Alabama sturgeon. Opponents complained that listing the species would shut down major barge traffic on the Tenn-Tom Waterway, and cost the state billions of dollars. And this time the critics didn't disappear after the public hearings. A number of politicians, including seven Southern governors and several U.S. senators, intensified the debate in Washington, pressuring Fish and Wildlife officials to back down on the plan to protect the sturgeon. Mr. Hartfield says the concerns that the plan would shut down barge traffic resulted from a misunderstanding, noting that the Army Corps of Engineers reviewed the plan and determined that listing the sturgeon wouldn't affect the movement of barges on the waterways. Still, he acknowledges that his agency's failure to discuss the sturgeon listing with opponents fueled concerns. Though bitterness remains, Mr. Hartfield is optimistic about a compromise. During the most recent meeting of the group, held July 18 in Montgomery, Mr. Hartfield explained the threats to the health of the region's river basins. At the next meeting, on Oct. 10, a facilitator will be brought in to help the agency and its opponents discuss solutions to current problems. Federal environmental regulators, battered by business interests in Alabama, have quietly begun meeting with some of those same critics to craft a strategy for the Mobile basin, covering 75% of Alabama and part of Georgia. The move by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is a major departure for the agency, which hasn't allowed the public to play an active role in the drafting of recovery plans for endangered species. "Basically, in the past, we would take public comments" after the recovery plan was drafted, says Paul Hartfield, a recovery biologist for the service.
