"If they were good suggestions, [we would] incorporate them, and if they were sour grapes, say thanks and move on."
event_description: Drafting a recovery plan for endangered species in the Mobile Basin.
setting: the Mobile Basin, involving the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and various stakeholders including business and environmental leaders
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; business leaders and environmentalists [Hero]; Alabama sturgeon [Victim]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, under pressure from business and political leaders, decided not to list the Alabama sturgeon as endangered, leading to a coalition of business leaders and environmentalists drafting a recovery plan for endangered species in the Mobile Basin.
moral: The problem is the strained relationship between the Fish and Wildlife Service and stakeholders, and the text suggests that a collaborative approach involving all parties is necessary to effectively address the environmental issues in the Mobile Basin.

Title: Lawyer Goes Against the Grain In Logging Fight With Maxxam
Text: EUREKA -- In a downtown Victorian in this depressed North Coast port, the oldest of professional traditions and the cutting edge of legal activism can be found alongside each other in the offices of a firm called Pacific Law. There, attorney Steven Schectman, a transplant from San Francisco, is training six apprentices to master the law the old-fashioned way. Just as in the days before California had its first law school, the aspiring attorneys prepare for the bar by working as a practitioner's clerks. But the subject of their work is as modern as the environmental movement: The entire focus of Pacific Law is suing Pacific Lumber Co., and its much-larger parent company, Maxxam Inc. of Houston. The Scotia-based unit owns 200,000 acres of prime Humboldt County timberlands. Maxxam and its chairman, Charles E. Hurwitz, gained control of the venerable Pacific Lumber in 1985 in a highly leveraged hostile takeover. Since then, waves of protests and environmental lawsuits have tried to make Maxxam, which increased its logging to pay off junk bonds, slow its timber harvesting and spare America's last stands of ancient, old-growth redwood trees. "We don't want to crush Pacific Lumber," Mr. Schectman says, "but we want to bring it into legal compliance." Over the past nine months, Mr. Schectman and Bill Bertain, a local attorney who has long fought Pacific Lumber, have filed a trio of lawsuits against Maxxam in state and federal courts. Two actions in Humboldt County Superior Court accuse Maxxam of violating state unfair business practices and federal racketeering statutes and claim such violations inflicted hardship on local residents. A third suit in U.S. District Court in San Francisco alleges that the company fraudulently siphoned $60 million in excess funds from Pacific Lumber's employee pension fund. (All three allegations are untrue, say officials from Maxxam and Pacific Lumber.) Pacific Lumber President and Chief Executive John Campbell says of the complaints: "I don't know what the purpose is, I don't think what (Mr. Schectman's) doing has a lot of merit. We have to ask the question why he's parachuting in to set up a law office in Eureka to litigate one company." As significant as the cases themselves is the unusual operation that is pursuing them. Mr. Schectman, 46 years old, boasts that he works "on the outside edge" of his profession by specializing in "human dignity and corporate responsibility" cases. To that end, over the past two decades he has sued a succession of landlords, corporations, government agencies and even law firms accused of harming his little-guy clients. In recent years, Mr. Schectman won several major court victories and clinched multimillion-dollar settlements that made him a controversial figure in Bay Area legal circles. Last summer -- bankrolled by a lucrative 1997 settlement of an age-discrimination complaint against the San Francisco law firm, Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro LLP -- Mr. Schectman moved lock, stock and law books up to this logging town to stalk even bigger legal game. Establishing the apprenticeship program was part of Mr. Schectman's self-professed dream of leveraging the law to right social and economic wrongs. Twenty years ago, the future litigator apprenticed under Berkeley civil-rights attorney Len Holt. Now, Mr. Schectman declares, "we're going to produce the most dangerous lawyers in America. They're going to learn by doing it." Six local activists quickly signed up, as did environmental lawyers eager to help in the training. But as Mr. Schectman can personally attest, becoming a lawyer through apprenticeship -- which requires at least four years of preparation -- is no easy feat. In 1980, he was one of only 11 apprentices out of a total of 7,000 students who qualified to take the bar examination. Nine passed. Since then, apprentices have had an even poorer showing. According to the State Bar of California, only two of 25 apprentices who took the exam since 1997 have passed. The long odds, however, don't seem to daunt Mr. Schectman's small cadre of faculty or his students, including Josh Kaufman, a 47-year-old former paleontologist at the Los Angeles County Natural History Museum. "We don't entirely know what we're getting into, and getting by the first year exam is kind of sobering," says Mr. Kaufman, who moved to Humboldt County 12 years ago to join the environmental movement. Studying to become an activist lawyer, he says, is "kind of a natural evolution since I've been working through legal channels since I started doing (environmental) work here." Another apprentice, Jamie Romeo, 31, says she opted to take up the law as a means of increasing her commitment to fight Pacific Lumber after a fellow activist died during a protest. In addition to spending at least 17 hours a week studying and clerking for Mr. Schectman, Ms. Romeo is taking classes in police investigative techniques and paralegal procedures at a community college. "This is going to make me a really killer activist," she says. The arrival of Mr. Schectman and his "law school" has provided welcome reinforcement for Mr. Bertain, the Eureka attorney who has been battling Pacific Lumber since the 1985 takeover. Mr. Bertain, a solo practitioner who graduated from the University of San Francisco Law School, grew up around Scotia, where his family ran a Pacific Lumber contract laundry from 1905 until 1992. The affiliation with Mr. Schectman, he says, "could very well turn out to be a good match" even though the two men come from totally different backgrounds. "He has a sense of the need for social and economic justice," says Mr. Bertain. Not all are mesmerized by Mr. Schectman's endeavor, however. The San Francisco lawyer's decision to head into redwood country and wage an all-out legal assault on Pacific Lumber is probably more "about money" than environmental zeal, says Jared Carter, whose Ukiah-based firm is defending the company in the two Superior Court cases, which have been consolidated by a judge. "He seems to be a competent lawyer, but I'm not swept off my feet by his legal theories," adds Mr. Carter, a long-time practitioner of timber and resource law. But both Mr. Carter and Mr. Schectman agree that Pacific Law's state-court cases have injected some "novel arguments" in the continuing legal wrangling between activists and Pacific Lumber. The two complaints allege that Pacific Lumber's logging practices have caused severe economic damages to two communities downslope from timber harvesting. A case filed on May 4 of last year for residents of the hamlet of Stafford claims that logging crews from Pacific Lumber and a second lumber company caused massive mudslides in late December 1996 and early January 1997 that destroyed seven houses and severely damaged seven others. The lawsuit charges the two companies with violating state laws on unfair business practices and the federal Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organization Act by conspiring to go against their state-approved timber harvesting plans and improperly logging on a hillside. The second suit, filed last Aug. 31 on behalf of another group of residents along the Elk River, accuses Maxxam and Pacific Lumber of knowingly employing similarly unsound hillside logging practices that caused soil erosion. The shifting soil made river water undrinkable and unusable for household and agricultural purposes, the suit says. Both complaints seek compensatory, general and punitive damages and compensation for emotional distress; dollar amounts weren't specified. And because of the federal racketeering allegations, Mr. Schectman's clients could collect triple damages if successful in court. The cases, Mr. Schectman notes, depart from previous legal efforts against Pacific Lumber that alleged violations of the federal Endangered Species Act, such as logging operations that damaged the habitat of the marbled murrelet bird and assorted other fauna. "I'm not interested in doing litigation that's already been done. I don't have an expertise in environmental law," Mr. Schectman says. The new cases, he says, are about "poor people going against powerful entities" and are designed to hurt Maxxam's bottom line by making it pay large damage awards. "He's doing something for people who had little or no voice up here," says Michael R. O'Neal, a Stafford resident whose home suffered severe damage in the 1996-97 slide. "We personally don't have the money to fight, ourselves." Kristi Wrigley, whose family has grown apples along the north fork of the Elk River for 96 years, accuses Pacific Lumber of denuding the watershed, causing her water supply to be hopelessly silted. Those problems, she stresses, became noticeable shortly after Maxxam grabbed control of Pacific Lumber and boosted its logging. "The old Pacific Lumber used to treat us like a neighbor; our needs mattered to them," she says. "Maxxam Corporation does not have the same consideration for us." Mr. Carter, Pacific Lumber's attorney, concedes that, at least in the Stafford slide case, residents did suffer severe damage. But blame, he says, can't necessarily be tied to his client's logging practices. "The question zeroes in on whether or not the slides were caused by extraordinary rainfall and would have occurred whether or not there had been any logging," he says. At Elk River, he adds, the legal question is "whether the logging which occurred and admittedly occurred over a period of years was done in an inappropriate manner and resulted in degrading the stream more so than if it had been done correctly." The damages in both cases, Mr. Carter says, aren't the fault of Pacific Lumber. Moreover, he notes that a 1982 state law specifically protects timber companies from being targeted by neighbors' nuisance suits if the logging is being conducted according to state regulations. Mr. Schectman's enlistment in the North Coast redwoods wars and the onslaught of lawsuits, undoubtedly, are caused by Pacific Lumber's notoriety, claims Mr. Carter. "These fellows, Schectman and his co-counsel Bill Bertain, are bringing fairly novel unfair-business practices and RICO claims partially because the company is in the news a lot and appears to be a possible source of attractive litigation," he says. Indeed, Pacific Lumber's already high profile is even higher this month, as a Feb. 28 deadline nears for accepting a joint federal- and state-government offer to pay the company $480 million for the 3,000-acre Headwaters Forest grove of ancient redwoods it owns, along with 7,000 more acres of scenic timberlands. The company, however, must agree to comply with tighter federal and possibly state regulations over how it logs all its Humboldt County holdings. The impending deal has heightened tensions among all participants in the redwoods negotiations. And those tensions will likely increase next week, when Mr. Schectman says he'll file a wrongful-death lawsuit in U.S. District Court in San Francisco -- accusing Pacific Lumber of causing the Sept. 17 death of anti-logging protester David Chain of Texas, who was crushed by tree. Such a move, while not unexpected, would be lamentable, says Mr. Carter, the Pacific Lumber attorney. "I would be very sorry if that matter was not allowed to just go away as a tragic accident that happened to an apparently nice young man," he says. (Humboldt County District Attorney Terry Farmer has found that the death was accidental and said that the logger who cut the tree that fell on Mr. Chain wasn't aware of protesters in the area.) Mr. Schectman, who says he will file the suit on behalf of the victim's mother, Cindy Allsbrooks, of Coldsprings, Texas, says the death of Mr. Chain galvanized him into putting together his single-focus law office and in-house law school to take on Maxxam and Pacific Lumber. "This is a case," he says, "crying out for punitive damages." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A series of lawsuits filed against Pacific Lumber Co. and its parent company, Maxxam Inc., alleging violations of state and federal laws related to logging practices.
setting: NA
characters: NA
plot: NA
moral: NA

Title: Groups Seek `Lid' Exemption In Washington
Text: Life under the Lid may get worse. The constraints of Washington's five-year-old voter-approved cap on state spending -- derisively called the Lid by some but officially known as Initiative 601 -- were bad enough before last week's election. With the passage of a transportation-funding measure, Referendum 49, the fiscal future appears so bleak to educators and environmentalists that they're contemplating fighting back with initiatives of their own. "You can knit at the edges for a long time, but at a certain point someone's going to have to bring some money to the table," says Chuck Collins, a retired chairman of the state Higher Education Coordinating Board, who is advocating a public vote on exempting all levels of education from the spending limit. Adds Ed Zuckerman, executive director of Washington Conservation Voters, "There's just not enough money in state government to fund the things people want funded." The Seattle group is one of several considering a ballot measure to fund salmon recovery and related programs, taking them out from under the Lid. Over the next two years, Referendum 49 will provide a tax cut for motorists and also redirect $156 million that would have gone into the state's general fund to a separate transportation pot, to pay for issuing bonds that will raise $2.4 billion for road improvements. The problem, critics say, is that by reducing the state's car-tag registration fee by about $30 per motorist, the referendum cuts the amount of money earmarked for general state services. This comes as the state economy shows signs of slowing down -- which could further crimp the general fund. And that would make competition for already scarce resources fiercer. One group gearing up for the funding battle is concerned about education. Mr. Collins recently has begun characterizing himself as a "potential sponsor" of a measure exempting public education from the Lid. Talks have taken place in elite higher-education circles -- most importantly in meetings of the 20/20 Commission, a big-picture-thinking task force created by Gov. Gary Locke in 1997 to come up with a vision for the future of education. Mr. Collins stresses that the talk "isn't idle. This is not just the boys chatting over drinks at the beer hall." Environmentalists are fed up, too. Josh Baldi, state policy representative for the Washington Environmental Council, says after a meeting with Gov. Locke last week, he learned that the two-year budget the governor plans to submit to the Legislature doesn't include as much funding as Mr. Baldi contends is necessary to support salmon-recovery efforts required by the U.S. Endangered Species Act. Mr. Baldi wouldn't say precisely what amount he thinks is appropriate, nor would the governor's office release details of his budget. Nevertheless, Mr. Baldi says, momentum is building among environmentalists for a ballot measure that would increase the salmon-recovery budget -- funded at $26.1 million this year -- and remove the program from under the Lid. Narrowly approved by voters in 1993, Initiative 601 created a formula that decides how much money the Legislature can spend each year. The formula takes into account a three-year average of the statewide inflation rate, as well as changes in the state's population during the previous 12 months. If revenue flows into the general fund above the limit prescribed by the formula, the extra money goes into a special emergency reserve fund, which can't be spent unless a two-thirds majority of the Legislature approves it. So far this year, $333 million above the $19.16 billion spending limit for 1997-99 has gone to the reserve fund. If Referendum 49 had failed, an additional $34 million would have gone into reserves this year and an extra $170 million the following year. But the state Budget Office says an all-out recession next year could cause the reserve fund to sink even further. In theory, if the money wasn't replaced and lawmakers spent up to the 601 limit, it would result in a deficit of $1.14 billion by 1999-01. If the economy merely slows over the next two years and then continues at an average pace after that, reserves could drop to $451 million in 1999-01, and then rise to $1.48 billion in 2003-05, the office predicts. Sen. Dan McDonald, a Yarrow Point Republican and outgoing majority leader, says 601 critics are getting ahead of themselves. He advises waiting until this week's new revenue forecast before floating doomsday scenarios. And, he says, if the newly elected Democratic majority shows some discipline, there will be enough money for every interest. But the uncertainty has special interests on edge. Evoking what he calls the coming "crisis" in higher education, Mr. Collins says he fears there won't be enough money to pay for professors, courses and student services for what he figures to be 105,000 new high-school graduates, out-of-state students and continuing-education students expected to enter the public university system by 2020. Mr. Collins puts the cost to the state at around $650 million. Bob Gogerty, a Seattle political consultant who rarely latches onto an issue unless it's destined to become a full-fledged campaign, says he's been testing the waters and raising the question with political operatives to see "if there's a will or a way" to exclude state-funded education programs from the Lid. Polls show his idea has some support. In February, Seattle pollster Stuart Elway asked 400 registered voters if they would favor exempting public education from the Lid, and 68% said they would. Support for salmon recovery, however, was less clear. In another poll of 400 registered voters in the Puget Sound region last August, 75% said they would pay $1 more a month in taxes to save endangered salmon, while 60% said they would oppose a 10%-to-15% increase in their electric bills to fund recovery efforts. "I think people are willing to spend money for a solution to the salmon problem, but they won't support any old proposal that comes along," Mr. Elway says. Right now, educators and environmentalists say they want to give the newly elected Democrats in the Legislature a chance to find new funding, whether from new taxes, fees or the federal government before they begin an all-out fight. But they heard Gov. Locke assert in a post-election news conference that, "None of the math changes" -- or, in other words, that the spending constraints are going to stay in place. "The governor and the new majorities would be ill advised to revisit Initiative 601," says Sen. McDonald. "Education can be dealt with well within the limits, and the Legislature should busy itself doing that rather than figuring out a way to circumvent it." Sensitive to a political backlash if Democrats were to vote to do away with the Lid, anti-601 forces would rather voters be asked to make the decision. "I'd like to see this Legislature around for a while," Mr. Zuckerman says. "So I don't want them forced to do something that's going to end up in their opponents' campaign brochure." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Discussion of a ballot measure to increase funding for salmon recovery efforts.
setting: discussion of a ballot measure in the context of state spending constraints and funding for education and environmental initiatives
characters: Chuck Collins [Hero]; Ed Zuckerman [Hero]; Josh Baldi [Hero]; Gov. Gary Locke [Villain]; Sen. Dan McDonald [Villain]; Washington educators and environmentalists [Victim]
plot: The passage of Referendum 49 reduces funding for general state services, prompting educators and environmentalists to consider initiatives to exempt education and salmon recovery funding from state spending limits. Critics argue that the spending constraints will lead to a funding crisis for education and salmon recovery efforts.
moral: The text suggests that the problem is the inadequate funding for education and salmon recovery due to spending limits, and advocates for initiatives that would exempt these areas from the constraints of Initiative 601.

Title: Timber Giant Cuts Rare Deal On Rare Birds
Text: BAINBRIDGE, Ga. -- Three years ago, Craig Hedman could drive to the far north corner of International Paper Co.'s Southlands Experiment Forest near the Georgia-Florida line and count on one hand the nests he would find for red-cockaded woodpeckers, a Southeastern bird on the federal endangered-species list. These days, as he winds his pickup truck through 5,300 acres of pine stands in land dedicated as habitat for relocated or newly hatched woodpeckers, Dr. Hedman, the company's forest-ecology manager, can point to 16 nests high in the cavities of old pine trees. There are so many birds, in fact, that International Paper sees a rare business opportunity in its rare birds. Under a novel agreement with the federal government, the Purchase, N.Y., pulp-and-paper mill operator can sell red-cockaded woodpecker "credits" to other firms whose logging or real-estate development would destroy woodpecker habitat. (As part of the deal, the buyer of the credits must also try to relocate the birds it displaces.) "It used to be the woodpeckers were seen as a problem," Dr. Hedman says, since the birds' endangered status restricted land use. "Now having a red-cockaded woodpecker is actually an asset." The company may one day even find it more lucrative to sell woodpecker credits on some pine tracts than to harvest them. Currently, the company uses only land designated for research purposes for its woodpecker-credit program. Company officials believe just one credit could sell for as much as $250,000 to a "motivated buyer," such as a real-estate developer in a hurry to move ahead with a resort project. International Paper can also use the credits to get at some of its other valuable timberland that the birds have made off-limits for harvesting. "This is not a game for small private landowners," notes Ralph Costa, recovery coordinator for the red-cockaded woodpecker at the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and principal architect of the government's plan to save the bird. Because of the expense -- either of buying a credit, or, for a seller, of maintaining a scientific staff -- he says, "this system is going to work primarily on the development end, for the golf courses and the big corporations." Though some environmentalists object to the arrangement, the Fish and Wildlife Service says it could help stop the decline of the nation's total population of red-cockaded woodpeckers -- now estimated at about 12,400. Under the terms that International Paper inked last year with the Fish and Wildlife Service, the company is required to maintain 18 woodpecker families -- with each family averaging 2u birds -- on its land throughout the Southeast. (The wildlife service sets a different baseline for other timber companies, according to field surveys.) When it exceeds this baseline, International Paper can begin assuming responsibility for birds on behalf of another developer or logging company that wants to clear-cut land that, because it hosts woodpeckers, is protected by the federal Endangered Species Act. So far, International Paper hasn't had any takers for credits it has been offering since last fall for two woodpecker families above its minimum 18. Sharon Haines, manager of sustainable forestry and policy for International Paper, says the company has had some inquiries, but declines to identify prospective buyers. While many timber companies have struck woodpecker-conservation deals, only one other -- Champion International Corp. -- has sold bird credits. In 1998, Champion, Stamford, Conn., sold a future woodpecker credit for $50,000 to a Texas subdivision developer. The government prohibited the developer from proceeding with plans for four years while Champion attempts to ensure that new birds hatch, mature and mate on its land. Whatever its first credit fetches, the income will be like birdseed to International Paper, a company that in 1999 saw $24.6 billion in sales and $183 million in profit. Although the nascent woodpecker-credit trade has been lauded by environmental groups such as New York-based Environmental Defense, it has been criticized by others. Detractors say cultivating woodpeckers in one area to cancel them out elsewhere doesn't add up to an overall strengthening of the species. "Conservationists tend to be suspicious of anything that takes a species away from a place," says Eric Draper, conservation director for the National Audubon Society in Florida, which is skeptical of the credit program. But International Paper and government officials argue the birds that survive relocation to a protective habitat end up faring better because they get to live and mate with others in a carefully managed forest rather than struggling in a more isolated environment. However, an estimated 90% of adults and 50% of juvenile birds die from the trauma of being moved. International Paper's arrangement with the red-cockaded woodpecker, which was designated as endangered in 1970, developed out of the federal government's efforts to save the bird in the early '90s. The government wanted to avoid the kind of bitter standoff between industry and conservationists that beset the Pacific Northwest in the late 1980s, when logging companies were banned from operating in the northern spotted owl's habitat. After watching a flurry of woodpecker-conservation deals in the early to late '90s -- including the first one in 1993 under which Atlanta-based Georgia-Pacific Corp. agreed to set up woodpecker-protection measures in Arkansas and northern Louisiana -- International Paper opened talks with the Fish and Wildlife Service about its own plan. At first, the company was mainly interested in using woodpecker credits to better exploit logging land of its own that held a red-cockaded woodpecker group, whose territory can include between 60 and 300 acres of timber that has often been valued at between $2,000 and $3,000 an acre. But, in part to test how much the woodpecker-credit market will bear, International Paper decided to try to sell the rights first. Dr. Hedman and his colleagues have tried to encourage mating by simulating the bird's natural habitat: They have installed nearly four-dozen artificial cavities in clusters of 80-plus-year-old pine trees. Last year, the team had their first sign their plan was working. A woodpecker native to the site bred with a young female bird that was imported from the federal government's Apalachicola National Forest in Florida's Panhandle. The female bore three eggs, one of which hatched. Although the baby bird didn't survive, researchers were encouraged and hope to see at least five breeding pairs by this spring. Credit: Staff Reporters of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: International Paper's agreement to sell red-cockaded woodpecker credits to offset habitat destruction.
setting: BAINBRIDGE, Ga. -- International Paper Co.'s Southlands Experiment Forest, under a novel agreement with the federal government regarding endangered species.
characters: International Paper [Hero]; red-cockaded woodpecker [Victim]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; environmentalists [Villain]; Eric Draper [Villain]
plot: International Paper, under a government agreement, sells red-cockaded woodpecker credits to offset habitat destruction, allowing them to profit while maintaining bird populations. The company has increased the number of nests from a few to 16, indicating a successful conservation effort. However, environmentalists criticize the credit system, arguing it does not strengthen the species overall.
moral: The problem is the endangered status of the red-cockaded woodpecker, which restricts land use. The text suggests that the credit system, while controversial, could provide a financial incentive for conservation and help stabilize the woodpecker population.

Title: Canada, U.S. Agree To Limit Catches Of Salmon Species
Text: OTTAWA -- Canada and the U.S. have settled their protracted Pacific Coast salmon-fishing dispute, agreeing to limit catches based on the abundance of salmon species. The agreement, which will be in force for 10 years for most species and 12 years for British Columbia Fraser River sockeye and pink, also calls on the U.S. to spend $140 million on habitat restoration and species growth. The accord aims to restore cooperation after eight years of recurring disputes among fishermen in Alaska, British Columbia, Washington state and Oregon over what portion of the shrinking salmon fishery each was permitted to take. The differences came to a head two years ago when British Columbia fishermen barracaded an Alaskan ferryboat in Prince Rupert and held it for three days to protest alleged overfishing by Alaska. Vice President Al Gore said in a statement that the agreement would benefit the environment and the economic interests of both nations. "Today's agreement is absolutely integral to our long-term strategy to bring the salmon back," he said. Both countries in recent years have taken measures to rebuild dwindling fish stocks, which have been damaged by overfishing, habitat destruction in rivers and streams that salmon use to spawn and poor ocean-survival rates. In March, the U.S. placed nine salmon types under the Endangered Species Act in an effort to restore the species, particularly the once-abundant chinook salmon. Canada last year announced a $270 million program to reduce British Columbia's commercial fleet, assist hard-hit communities and improve fish habitat. "This new agreement with the United States builds on the same approach," said David Anderson, Canada's fisheries minister. "Instead of fighting over a shrinking pie, we will now be working together to conserve stocks." Since no firm quotas were announced for the salmon-fishing season that begins early July, it isn't clear whether the agreement will be embraced by fishermen from either nation. British Columbia fishermen claim Alaskan fishermen have intercepted hundreds of millions of dollars of Canadian-bound salmon during the past decade. Alaska denies the charges. Dennis Streifel, the province's fisheries minister, said he hadn't seen the agreement, but that British Columbia would only support a deal that prevents interception of its fish stocks. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The U.S. placed nine salmon types under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: international agreement context regarding salmon fishing and conservation
characters: Vice President Al Gore [Hero]; British Columbia fishermen [Victim]; Alaskan fishermen [Villain]; David Anderson, Canada's fisheries minister [Hero]; Dennis Streifel, province's fisheries minister [Unknown]
plot: Canada and the U.S. reached an agreement to limit salmon catches and invest in habitat restoration, aiming to resolve disputes over fishing rights and restore salmon populations.
moral: The ongoing decline of salmon stocks due to overfishing and habitat destruction necessitates cooperation between Canada and the U.S. to effectively conserve and restore salmon populations.

Title: The Evening Wrap: Asian Aftershock
Text: When a 6.7 magnitude quake shook Taiwan late Tuesday, the first fears were that the tremors would trigger a tsunami -- and this on the second anniversary of the wave that killed 230,000 people in South Asia. Nature spared lives this time, but wasn't as kind to technology. The temblor cut several undersea fiber-optic cables that carry communications traffic through a key nexus in Asia, connecting Hong Kong and Southeast Asia with Japan and, ultimately, North America. Phone and Internet service was spotty, slow or just plain unavailable in several countries in the region, marking the largest such outage in years. The interruption also affected traders, as Bloomberg and Reuters platforms were knocked off line, and clients couldn't log on to trade or get real-time data. Asian markets were little affected overall, as most companies did find workarounds. Still, the wide scope of the disruption -- caused by a single natural disaster with limited physical damage -- illustrates how vulnerable the systems are that support global communications. Nearly all the data that travels between continents does so over fiber-optic filaments, tiny threads of glass that are bundled together and covered in insulation and other protective materials. Companies stretch these cables across thousands of miles of ocean, to rest on the sea floor or float above it. These cables are easy to dislodge -- a fishing boat cut what was then Pakistan's only undersea cable line over the summer and disrupted the company's Internet service for 12 days. The cables damaged this week run through an especially dense pathway between Taiwan and Hong Kong. But large disruptions are possible wherever they are clustered together in a handful of key hubs -- for instance, there are key points near New York City and in southern Britain. Part of the problem is that global investment in fiber-optic lines fell off after the 1990s, when telecommunications companies laid huge amounts in anticipation of an explosion in demand. When that growth didn't happen as quickly as expected, companies were hammered financially -- some even went bankrupt. But this week's incident "raises the question of whether there needs to be a new wave of investment" in these cables, said Duncan Clark, chairman of BDA China Ltd., a telecom consultancy based in Beijing. The outage even trickled down to the region's shipping and logistics industry. KL Tam, managing director of Hong Kong-based Kingstar Shipping said he was unable to reach clients in Japan or Korea, and some in Singapore. "We have been trying other ways to communicate -- but nothing much works," he said. "If this continued for several days, our operations overseas would essentially stop," he said. --- Dow Barrels Down Home Stretch The Dow Industrials topped 12500 for the first time, gaining more than 100 points, as the year-end run continues amid a stronger-than- forecast housing report and falling oil prices. More than three stocks advanced for each that declined on the New York Stock Exchange, though trading was thin during the typically slow final week of the year. Fund managers are adding stocks to their portfolios, a trend that often gives stocks a boost in late December. European shares rose, helped by falling oil prices. In Asia, Japan's Nikkei Index hit a new seven-month high, revved by gains in Toyota. The 10-year Treasury fell to yield 4.601%. On the New York Mercantile Exchange, light, sweet crude oil slid 76 cents to $60.34 a barrel. --- Home Improvement The housing slump may be the darkest cloud hanging over the U.S. economy going into the new year, but data out today emitted a ray or two of hope. The Commerce Department reported November new-home sales climbed 3.4%, rising for the third time in six months and beating economists' expectations for a 1.6% increase. The monthly report also showed a decline in inventories and a rise in median price. Home sales are still down 15% from the same period in 2005, and November marks the 11th straight monthly year-over-year decline, but economists still took heart. "Although no doubt this report was boosted by the mild weather in parts of the country, the pickup in sales and decline in the number of homes for sale provide further tentative evidence that the housing market is stabilizing," Bear Stearns economists wrote. --- Remembering President Ford The flag over the White House flew at half-staff in honor of former President Gerald R. Ford, who died last night at 93. Speaking from his ranch in Texas, President Bush said, "Americans will always admire Gerald Ford's unflinching performance of duty and the honorable conduct of his administration and the great rectitude of the man himself." Funeral arrangements will be announced today, and next week, as per tradition, stock exchanges in the U.S. will be closed on the day of his funeral. --- Air Force Looks Outside The Air Force is considering hiring an outside firm to oversee systems integration of its next-generation navigational satellites, according to industry and government officials, in a major policy decision that has multibillion dollar ramifications. Air Force officials already have said they intend to award separate manufacturing contracts for the newest version of Global Positioning System satellites and related ground facilities. But in a break from traditional acquisition strategies, senior Air Force generals also are mulling hiring private experts to supervise the integration of the overall GPS III system. Hiring a separate project integrator would be a tacit acknowledgement that both Air Force Space Command and the Pentagon's massive weapons-buying bureaucracy lack the necessary expertise to perform the required oversight role. --- Saddam Speaks as Sentence Looms A farewell letter posted on the Internet in the name of Saddam Hussein called on Iraqis not to hate the invaders of their country. "I call on you not to hate because hate does not leave space for a person to be fair and it makes you blind and closes all doors of thinking," said the letter, supposedly written because Mr. Hussein was not given the opportunity to speak after his sentence was delivered. In Amman, Jordan, one of Mr. Hussein's lawyers, Issam Ghazzawi, said that the letter was authentic. Meanwhile, an Iraqi presidential spokesman said an appeals court order yesterday upholding Mr. Hussein's death sentence might not require the Iraqi president's approval. If it doesn't, the former dictator will have no other legal means of avoiding execution. Some Iraqis said Mr. Hussein should be hanged immediately, but others fear Iraq's bloodletting could escalate if the former dictator is executed at a time when sectarian attacks are already on the rise. --- Troops Close In on Somali Capital Ethiopian and Somali government troops drove Islamic movement fighters out of Jowhar, the last major town on the road to the Mogadishu, Somalia's capital. Ethiopian forces crossed the border Sunday to reinforce the internationally recognized Somali government. The militias of the Council of Islamic Courts seized Mogadishu in June and went on to take much of southern Somalia. The militias seemed invincible after capturing the capital, but they are no match for Ethiopia, which has the strongest military in the Horn of Africa. Somali government spokesman Abdirahman Dinari confirmed that talks for the peaceful surrender of Mogadishu were under way. "We will not attack Mogadishu. We hope that Mogadishu will fall to our hands without firing a shot." Unconfirmed reports estimate as many as 1,000 people have died and 3,000 been wounded since the fighting began a few days ago. --- On Thin Ice The U.S. Department of Interior proposed to list polar bears as a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act, saying global warming is causing sea ice -- a primary habitat for the bears -- to melt at dangerous rates. The proposal marks a potentially significant departure for the administration from its cautious rhetoric about the effects of global warming. Interior Department Secretary Dirk Kempthorne said oil and natural gas drilling in the Arctic region is not to blame for the receding ice, but was silent on what is actually causing the warmer temperatures. The Interior Department plans to take comments on the proposal in effort to make a decision within 12 months on whether to list the bears as endangered species. --- More Backdating Fallout Backdating questions bogged down more companies as HCC Insurance Holdings revised several years' worth of financial statements and said its former CEO "retroactively priced options." According to an amended report filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission, Mr. Way, who resigned last month, mispriced the options to retain talent and "provide employees with the best option price." Meanwhile, Nabors Industries said it's initiating a further review of its option granting practices after a Wall Street Journal article stated that CEO Eugene Isenberg is among the highest-paid executives in history. The company said an initial review of its stock option practices going back to 1998 "did not suggest that there was reason to question the propriety" of its option granting practices. --- Retailers' Results May Disappoint A Saturday spree by last-minute shoppers may not be enough to save the Christmas shopping season for retailers. ShopperTrak RCT Corp. reported that retail sales for the week ended Dec. 23 rose 5.2% on a surge of spending that should bring total holiday sales "relatively close" to original projections despite recent shortfalls. Sales on Dec. 23 totaled $8.72 billion, making it the second-biggest holiday shopping day of the year behind Black Friday. But a survey released by the International Council of Shopping Centers, Inc. and UBS Securities LLC put the number much lower. Their figures show U.S. chain store sales for the week ended Dec. 23 rose 1.7% compared to the year-ago period, putting the season's subtotal on  track to hit the low end of expectations. --- Study Points to Safer Catheter Use Commonsense safety and hygiene practices, often overlooked by doctors, can almost wipe out a dangerous type of bloodstream infection that kills thousands of patients each year in U.S. hospitals, according to a new study. The simple steps include making sure doctors and nurses wash their hands and wear protective clothing. The study, published in tomorrow's New England Journal of Medicine, focused on central-line catheters, which deliver drugs and nutrition to patients through a major vein in their neck, chest or groin. Researchers examined Michigan hospitals that agreed to use the safety procedures in their intensive-care units, where catheters are most commonly used. --- Itsy-Bitsy, Spray-On Bikinis That exotic dancer might look topless, but truth is there's a thin layer of latex between you and the finer points of her anatomy. Strippers in Alabama have brokered a deal with the state to comply with one of the strictest anti-nudity laws in the U.S. Under a settlement between 18 adult nightclubs and the Alabama attorney general's office, dancers statewide are spritzing their buttocks and breasts with flesh-colored latex. The law says that any skin that would normally be covered by a modest bikini must be swathed in an opaque covering. But the law doesn't specify the type of material, so, in the legal sense, a nylon swimsuit and spray-on latex are one and the same. The state reluctantly went along with the clubs' virtually invisible latex bikinis rather than having a federal judge follow through on his threat to throw out the entire statue as unconstitutional. "The choice we were faced with was some covering or no covering," said Keith Miller, the chief deputy attorney general. "We decided it was better to have these coverings than nothing at all." --- Write to Megan Ballinger at megan.ballinger@wsj.com
event_description: The U.S. Department of Interior proposed to list polar bears as a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: U.S. Department of Interior [Hero]; polar bears [Victim]; oil and natural gas drilling [Villain]
plot: The U.S. Department of Interior proposed to list polar bears as a threatened species due to global warming causing sea ice to melt, which threatens their habitat. This proposal indicates a shift in the administration's stance on global warming, as it acknowledges the impact of climate change on the species.
moral: The problem is the rapid melting of sea ice due to global warming, which endangers polar bears. The text suggests that there needs to be a recognition of this issue and a potential new wave of investment in conservation efforts to protect the species.

Title: Alien Invasion: Plague of Asian Eels Highlights Damage From Foreign Species --- Latest of Many Interlopers, These Creatures Could Threaten the Everglades --- Gender-Benders Won't Die
Text: MIRAMAR, Fla. -- An aluminum boat carrying federal biologists roars down Canal C-9, a 20-mile-long waterway that passes a stone's throw from Pro Player Stadium, home of the Miami Dolphins.    The boat, which is equipped with an "electro-fishing unit," slows to a stop, and one of the men lowers two chandeliers of thick wires into the water. With the press of a pedal, he unleashes up to 800 volts into the murky depths. The scientists are stalking one of the newest, most indestructible aquatic predators in the U.S.: the Asian swamp eel.    The more scientists learn about the eel, Monopterus albus, the more discouraged they become about stopping it. Here is what is known so far: It has a bottomless appetite for any aquatic life in its path -- fish, frogs, shrimp, crayfish and insects. A nocturnal hunter, it is rarely seen by humans. The three-foot-long adults have no known natural enemies, with the possible exception of alligators.    The eel's most alarming trait, though, is its uncanny ability to survive extreme conditions. In one study by a Harvard zoologist, an Asian swamp eel lived seven months in a damp towel without food or water. The olive-brown creature prefers tropical waters, yet it can flourish in sub-zero temperatures. It prefers fresh water but can tolerate high salinity. It breathes under water like a fish, but can slither across dry land, sometimes in packs of 50 or more, sucking air through a two-holed snout. It breeds year-round, with one eel laying as many as 1,000 eggs at a time. No mates nearby? No problem: Almost magically -- scientists still don't know how -- the eel can change into a female from a male. Even more of a riddle is how to kill the eel: It thus far appears almost immune to poisons and dynamite.    "We can't find a chink in its armor," says Leo Nico, a biologist with the U.S. Geological Survey, the nation's largest water, earth and biological science agency.    As if this weren't cause enough for concern, the eel is now within a mile of the ecologically fragile Everglades National Park, a 1.5 million-acre oasis of rare plants, animals and fish, and the subtropical jewel of America's park system. If the eels reach the park, there's a chance they could start gobbling their way through one of America's most-threatened ecosystems.    The tale of the eel's discovery in the U.S. offers a window into one of the most vexing problems facing the environment in America today, rivaling wildfires, logging, flooding, earthquakes or tornadoes in sheer economic damage -- the vast and silent explosion of invasive species. In a trend accelerated by the globalization of the world economy, about 50,000 introduced and invasive nonnative species have entered the U.S. to date. Some, such as corn, wheat, rice, cattle and poultry, proved their value to the economy and culture long ago.    But the problems caused by other newer invaders is taking its toll. Introduced and invasive species cost the U.S. an estimated $138 billion annually according to a study last year by four researchers at Cornell University.    In Lousiana, for instance, Formosan termites chew up historic homes in the French quarter of New Orleans and a sheriff's SWAT team shoots beaver-size, nonnative swamp rats from South America overtaking drainage canals. Suppressing a 1996 infestation of Asian long-horned beetles in New York City cost the state and federal governments more than $4 million. In the Great Lakes, Zebra mussels introduced by Russian freighters in 1986 now cause an estimated $5 billion in damage annually to pipes, boats and other structures. Exotic weeds and plants -- a particular nuisance to Western ranchers -- are expanding their range at a rate of 4,600 acres a day.    The environmental cost, meanwhile, is incalculable. Nonindigenous species disrupt delicate food webs and upset rare breeding sites. About 42% of the species listed as endangered or threatened under the Endangered Species Act are there because of competition or predation by foreign species. And there's no end in sight: Every minute, 40,000 gallons of ballast water are dumped into U.S. harbors, some of it containing exotic organisms that could alter or destroy marine ecosystems, according to the Department of Commerce. Even baby's breath, the tiny white flowers from Eurasia that float on a bride's veil, is a menace to unique habitats in the West.    Concerned about the federal and state governments' scattershot and overlapping response to the problem, President Clinton 18 months ago signed an executive order creating the National Invasive Species Council, for the first time coordinating the efforts of 20 federal agencies. In a detailed "management report" to be released next week for public comment, the council outlines ambitious plans to create an early-detection team, educate the public, strengthen import controls and design an online database of species where scientists around the world can share information.    "America's landscape is being transformed by swamp eels, cheatgrass and a host of other creatures and weeds that don't belong," Secretary of the Interior Bruce Babbitt said in a written response to questions. "The national invasive species plan now being proposed is a big, first step in turning back this unwelcome tide."    The Asian swamp eel would still be in hiding today were it not for the abiding curiosity of a Smithsonian Institution scientist named Wayne Starnes, who discovered the eel on the North American continent. Along the way, a hurricane, a part-time snail specialist, a student in Jakarta, and a spontaneous roadside stop by a piranha expert all played a crucial role in understanding what scientists know so far about the species.    In late September 1994, Mr. Starnes, an ichthyologist with a Ph.D. in ecology, flew to Atlanta from his Washington lab for a conference sponsored by the American Zoological Association, where he was eager to pitch a book project on freshwater fish. One evening, on a lark, he joined his co-author, a professional nature photographer named Dick Bryant, in a 20-minute drive north of Atlanta to judge a nature photography contest at the Chatahoochee Nature Center, a log-cabin style museum surrounded by ponds and winding trails. While touring the exhibits at night with a staffer, he noticed a dish on a table that held a three-inch worm as thin "as a thick pencil lead."    "Where did you get this?" he asked the staffer. Earlier in the day, children were collecting bugs and crawfish and "these worms" to study in the center, the man replied. Mr. Starnes lifted the dish to his eyes. It looked like a tiny eel, he said to himself. But American eels wouldn't be this far inland -- they spawn in the ocean. Was it a siren, the eel-shaped salamander with no hind legs, or perhaps a Southern Brook Lamprey, which is native to the Chatahoochee River?    Dr. Starnes slipped the eel in a small vial with formaldehyde and carried it in his briefcase back to the lab at the Smithsonian in Washington. Adjusting the focus of the microscope, Mr. Starnes stared at the eel and whispered to himself, "Oh man, I've seen this face before." It was the same family of eels he had seen on research trips to South America and Central America. This particular family of eels is also found in India, African swamps and flooded rice fields in Asia. The eels had been spotted in Hawaii years ago.    To further educate himself, Mr. Starnes relied on several early eel studies, including one by a Harvard professor named Karel Liem, whose research reached back to the 1960s. Prof. Liem's own first encounter with the eels was memorable: As a graduate student at the University of Indonesia in Jakarta, he traveled one night in 1961 to a swamp to collect frogs for his thesis project. Suddenly, his headlamp lit up a herd of eels moving across land. Shocked and intrigued, "I collected 87 live specimens in maybe less than an hour," he said. He's studied them ever since, focusing on their amazing resilience. In one landmark study, he starved 40 eels for as long as seven months to gauge their ability to survive prolonged drought and food deprivation.    Still curious about the eels, Mr. Starnes flew back to the nature center in Georgia in August 1996, where he met with two men from the Georgia Department of Natural Resources. They were armed with backpack electro-fishing devices, which momentarily stun fish. At the first crackle of electricity, Mr. Starnes quickly saw the orange bellies of three-foot adult eels as they turned rigid in the murky water. "They were thick as hair on a dog's back in there," he recalls. After two hours, they collected 24 eels from three ponds. Mr. Starnes found sunfish and bass in the eels' bellies, and the ponds were depleted of smaller fish.    In the fall of 1997, Mr. Starnes returned to the site with the Georgia DNR, and shocked and scooped up 19 eels. More troubling to Mr. Starnes, though, was the fact that the tropically inclined eels had survived a bitter winter in the Atlanta area that paved ponds with a foot of ice. Mr. Starnes, who by then had taken a job as research curator of fishes at the North Carolina Science Museum, put the eels in a large tank to observe. All of Mr. Starnes's notes jibed with Mr. Liem's observation 40 years earlier. "The limits of their gluttony are hard to establish," he wrote in a paper with Mr. Bryant and others. In captivity, they will eat "minnow after minnow." Particularly eerie was to "walk into a room and have five ominous heads rise to the surface, orient your presence, and follow you about the room with almost demanding expectancy."    A second population wasn't revealed until three years later, in June 1997, nearly 700 miles southeast of Atlanta. A Florida International University student named Carol Curtis was studying electric knifefish, another exotic species. She netted several small baby swamp eels in a pond that drains into a waterway known as canal C-9, north of Miami. She was collecting samples of water hyacinth, an invasive flower species, for her aquarium. The eel found its way to FIU Prof. Joel Trexler, who has a doctorate in biology. "The minute I saw it, I knew it was not from here," he said.    Yet another group of eels emerged around the same time. While driving home to Gainesville, Fla., from a boating seminar in St. Petersburg, Mr. Nico, the federal biologist, wondered if the roadside ditches held any Oriental weatherfish -- a peculiar specimen he was researching whose behavior allegedly can predict weather. In a rural area southeast of Tampa Bay, he eased onto the shoulder of the road and pulled out his pole-nets.    Mr. Nico, who wrote his thesis on piranhas in South America, knew the area held promise. South Florida is a mecca for more than 100 uninvited, or nonnative, species. Fire ants from South America, for example, harass homeowners, even killing an elderly woman there earlier this year. Melaleuca trees from Australia dry up the Everglades, lowering the water table and creating a fire hazard. In the ditch, Mr. Nico scooped up several Oriental weatherfish and two wriggling eels, each about a foot long. Fascinated, he took them back to the lab at the Florida Caribbean Science Center, where he correctly identified them as Asian swamp eels, a species he studied as a student in South America.    Mr. Nico's boss, USGS branch chief Jim Williams, made the connection with the Georgia eels immediately -- Mr. Starnes was a buddy from years back. News of the eels spread slowly on the scientific grapevine, and a network of scientists formed. Mr. Starnes flew down to Florida to study specimens in the FIU lab. Meanwhile, Prof. Trexler asked his students visiting home in Southern China -- where eels are sold in baskets of grass as food items -- to bring back specimens for comparison. And Mr. Nico brought back some tissue samples of baby eels from Venezuela.    That spring, Mr. Nico sampled the C-9 canal in North Miami, a virtual freeway for exotic fish in Florida. Mr. Nico and his team found "dozens of eels of all different sizes" -- the biggest infestation yet. Mr. Nico began surveying every month with the electro-fishing boat, which stuns fish in a 10-foot radius long enough to net them.    Even with all the technology, the eels are extremely difficult to grab. On a recent return to the site, the boat driver yelled directions to the two men manning the nets. "There he goes! Behind that rock! On the bank!" On a small table on the bank, Jeffrey Herod, clad in a long blue plastic apron, called out the weight and length of each eel. Using a small pair of shears, he cut off the heads, which are helpful in gauging the eels' age. "Watch out for these boys," Mr. Herod said to his staff. "They're bleeders."    Soon several agencies pitched in with research and sampling, including the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission, the South Florida Water Management District, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, the National Park Service, as well as the USGS offices in the Caribbean Science Center and the Everglades.    Despite the number of experts involved, though, basic questions remained unanswered. How did the eel get here? How fast and how far can it spread? How do we get rid of it? Theories on their entry are many. The eels could be unwanted aquarium pets dumped into the wild -- pet stores sell baby eels nearby -- or escapees from an aquarium-fish farm, say, during a flood. Another theory is the eels were intentionally introduced as food by Asian immigrants. Considered a delicacy, the eels are cut up in sections, either grilled or stir fried, and served over rice, usually with a sauce.    On Sept. 23, 1998, the Florida scientists flew to Washington to inform executives at the Department of the Interior. Afterwards, the eels were listed by the Aquatic Nuisance Species Task Force, a group of seven federal agencies dedicated to control aquatic nuisances. "The question everybody wanted answered is this: Is there any way to keep this from getting into the park? There was a real desire to contain it or even eradicate it," says Prof. Trexler, who attended the meeting. Among the ideas: a network of underwater electronic fences in strategic locations, similar to the ones used in the Great Lakes region to contain the spread of lamprey eels. The interior department "took it seriously from the beginning," says Prof. Trexler. "They said they'd do everything they can to keep it out of the park. I was amazed at the high level of concern." But the meeting didn't change Mr. Nico's mind. "I just didn't think it could be contained."    Hurricane Irene slammed the Florida keys in the fall of 1999, dumping more than a foot of rain in the Miami area. In Homestead, pastures turned into lakes. Canals flooded over banks. One alarmed owner of a nursery reported several "snakes" around his business, where large potted palms are sold. Bill Loftas, a senior USGS scientist in the Everglades, investigated. He found his first swamp eel run over in the nursery. The next day, with Mr. Trexler, Mr. Loftas found three dead eels and a live one. More questions demanded attention: Had the eel moved across land? Were these eels from the same family in Miami or in Tampa or even Georgia? Or were they a new population?    This past winter, concerned that the eel had already breached the park, Mr. Loftas took the electro-fishing unit in a marsh boat and searched for hours with an FIU postdoctoral intern. They found nothing. "But that that doesn't mean they're not there." Like many of the scientists studying the eel, he believes quick action is critical. "This is not a lot of arm waving," said Mr. Trexler. "This is a real threat."    Over Christmas, Mr. Trexler kept the eel tissue samples from the nursery in his freezer and called Tim Collins, snail specialist and a molecular systematist at FIU. "Could you throw this into your works?" Mr. Trexler asked him. Mr. Collins conducted DNA tests through the Christmas break. "When we found the answer, we were floored," said Mr. Trexler. The eels were a "separate introduction" from previously discovered populations in Georgia, North Miami, and Tampa. After extensive DNA testing, they concluded four different introductions of eels had occured in the U.S.    After a flurry of exchanged e-mails, phone calls and faxes, state and federal biologists agreed to gather in January at FIU to draw up a plan to deal with the eel. Meetings continued through the winter and spring. Today, USGS scientists, working with researchers at FIU and biologists from the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission, are actively and regularly searching waters where the eels were previously found. The USGS is preparing a rapid response team to find and "contain" the eel.    But killing the eels, which are estimated to number in the tens of thousands in the U.S. now, may prove tricky. Dynamite, a crude but effective way to kill fish, doesn't work well on eels because they lack the large air bladder that makes fish susceptible to concussion blasts, and they can retreat to their burrows. Poisoning the entire length of the infested canals still wouldn't kill all eels. In tests using rotenone, a poison that makes it impossible for fish to use oxygen, the eels simply raise their snouts above water and breathe air.    Any such efforts would certainly incite protest over the Everglades. Just two years ago in California, the state Game and Fish Commission poisoned Lake Davis -- the entire lake -- to rid it of northern pike, worried they'd become a threat to native trout. Protests, loud and long, continue today. "Which is worse?" asks Mr. Trexler. "The spread of the invader or the risk associated with containing it?"  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The National Invasive Species Council is proposing a management report to address the issue of invasive species in the U.S.
setting: Canal C-9, Florida, where federal biologists are conducting research on invasive species
characters: federal biologists [Hero]; Asian swamp eel [Villain]; native aquatic species [Victim]
plot: Federal biologists are using electro-fishing to study the invasive Asian swamp eel, which poses a significant threat to native aquatic life. As they gather data, they discover the eel's resilience and potential to disrupt ecosystems, leading to concerns about its spread into the Everglades.
moral: The invasive Asian swamp eel represents a serious environmental threat, and immediate coordinated action is necessary to contain and manage its spread to protect native ecosystems.

Title: Environmental Fight Over Spotted Owl Headed to Congress --- Latest Federal Court Decision Upholding Owl's Habitat Shakes Timber Interests
Text: The fight over the spotted owl is heading for a new arena: Congress. The owl's remarkable ability to cripple U.S. timber policy was reaffirmed last week by a federal court decision in Seattle banning logging on 66,000 acres of prime redwoods and Douglas firs -- "old-growth" forests the owl calls home. The ban will last at least 11 months and probably much longer. In a blunt 35-page decision, Judge William Dwyer blasted the U.S. Forest Service for "deliberate and systematic" violations of environmental and wildlife laws. He ripped the service for bowing to pressure from "higher authorities in the executive branch of government." Noting that the service had dragged its feet for years on owl protection, he ordered it to produce an owl plan by April 1992. Meanwhile, cutting will be prohibited on acreage that spreads over parts of Oregon, Washington and Northern California. The ruling, one of several recent setbacks for timber interests, seemed to leave timber policy in disarray. While the Forest Service ponders coming up with a plan for owls, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is grappling with a separate court-mandated effort to work out details of its own recent owl plan. That plan will set logging guidelines for the vast 11.6 million acre swatch of northwest forests that the service recently designated as prime spotted owl habitat. And in Portland, environmentalists are about to re-file another federal lawsuit attacking timber plans of the Bureau of Land Management. "Right now, the federal courts are dictating national timber policy," said Ralph Sapperstein, legal committee representative of the Northwest Forest Resource Council, a logging group. "It's insane." Judge Dwyer's decision will force timber interests to seek their salvation in Congress. ". . . . unless Congress acts quickly, we'll see the economic fabric of the Pacific Northwest unravel so quickly that it will be impossible to turn the light back on," said Mark Rey, head lobbyist for the American Forest Resource Alliance in Washington. Environmentalists say such predictions of economic doom are overwrought. And in his decision, Judge Dwyer said that while painful, much of the economic dislocation from logging curbs was inevitable because of changes in timber practices and markets unrelated to owls. Nonetheless, timber interests are putting their fading hopes on a bill introduced the same day as Judge Dwyer's decision by a group of congressmen led by Sen. Robert Packwood of Oregon. While setting aside some old-growth forests for owls, the bill would limit environmentalists' ability to challenge Forest Service decisions and would provide economic aid to displaced loggers. However, Congressman George Miller, head of the influential House Interior Commiittee, has pronounced the bill "dead on arrival." Some timber backers say they may push for the "snail darter defense," a reference to Congress's decision in the 1970s to exempt the tiny fish from protection under the Endangered Species Act when snail darter protections threatened to stop construction of the huge Telleco Dam. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A federal court decision banning logging on 66,000 acres of spotted owl habitat.
setting: a federal court case
characters: Judge William Dwyer [Hero]; U.S. Forest Service [Villain]; spotted owl [Victim]; timber interests [Victim]; environmentalists [Hero]; Congress [Unknown]
plot: Judge Dwyer issues a ruling banning logging on spotted owl habitat, criticizing the U.S. Forest Service for its failures, which leads to timber interests seeking legislative relief from Congress while environmentalists challenge timber plans.
moral: The text suggests that Congress must act quickly to address the economic challenges posed by logging bans, while also recognizing the need for environmental protections.

Title: U.S. News: Green Battle Rages in Desert --- Mojave Protection Bill Would Put Prime Solar-Power Sites Off Limits
Text: BARSTOW, Calif. -- A California senator's move Monday to put more than one million acres of the Mojave Desert off limits to development is spotlighting a clash between two prime goals of environmentalists. Before Democratic Sen. Dianne Feinstein introduced legislation to create the Mojave Trails National Monument and other protected wilderness areas, solar-power developers had submitted nearly two dozen proposals since 2006 for projects that would make the Southern California desert the biggest solar farm on Earth. California has set an ambitious target of garnering one-third of its electricity from renewable sources by 2020. That has sparked a renewable-development boom as utilities sign contracts for everything from solar farms to geothermal plants. It also has made it easier for developers to secure financing. But some of the land solar developers consider prime real estate also is prized by conservationists who want to preserve unspoiled stretches of unique desert, such as the Sleeping Beauty Valley and Marble Mountains in the Mojave. The conflicting visions for the Mojave are part of a broader struggle playing out as the nation embarks on a renewable-energy push. In Kansas, some of the last tall-grass prairie habitat could be threatened by wind farms in the Flint Hills area. More than 96% of tall-grass prairie has been destroyed. Critics say wind turbines not only have a visual impact on the landscape, but also require extensive road construction. Mrs. Feinstein's Mojave bill would protect 1.7 million acres of desert, while still allowing current recreational uses. The biggest piece would form the Mojave Trails National Monument, at 941,000 acres, east of Los Angeles along a 105-mile stretch of historic Route 66. It also would create the Sand to Snow National Monument on 134,000 acres of federal land near Palm Springs, and would put additional acreage under wilderness protection, including important animal-migration corridors. Mrs. Feinstein is a longtime supporter of desert preservation who sponsored the 1994 California Desert Protection Act that turned the nearby Death Valley and Joshua Tree wilderness areas into national parks. Once word spread that she was trying to protect another stretch of desert in the Mojave, some developers began to rethink their plans. Tessera Solar, a Houston developer, dropped plans to develop a 5,000-acre site in the Mojave, "knowing Sen. Feinstein was moving forward with her bill," a company spokeswoman said. BrightSource Energy signed power-purchase agreements with Southern California Edison, a unit of Edison International, and Pacific Gas & Electric Co., a unit of PG&E Corp., for 2,600 megawatts of power it intends to furnish from numerous desert sites beginning in 2013. John Woolard, chief executive of BrightSource, said Mrs. Feinstein "got quite upset" when she learned development was proposed on some pristine tracts under federal control. His company decided to forgo plans to build a solar project in the Broadwell Dry Lake area that would be within the proposed monument boundaries. However, he warned that putting parts of the Mojave off limits "would push solar farms out of state." Others have also complained that California's aggressive renewable-energy target, combined with tough land-protection laws, could end up sparking a renewable-energy boom in neighboring Nevada or Arizona. Mrs. Feinstein's bill attempts to find common ground between developers and those who support renewable energy. Although it prohibits energy development within the monument area, the bill includes provisions that would allow faster and cheaper development of private lands. Instead of taking seven to nine years to do endangered-species act reviews on private land, renewable-energy developers would qualify for reviews taking 18 months to three years. California's own analysis shows it needs 128,000 acres of desert terrain to fulfill the state mandate for a big boost in utility-scale solar projects. But there currently are projects proposed that would utilize nearly a million acres. David Myers, the executive director of Wildlands Conservancy, the chief critic of Mojave development, said the legislation is "fantastic" because it redirects activity from sensitive areas to land that already has been degraded by prior use, such as cattle grazing or alfalfa cultivation. His organization donated 600,000 acres in the Mojave to the federal government in stages from 1999 to 2003, with the understanding that it would be permanently protected. Mr. Myers was furious when applications began pouring into the Bureau of Land Management seeking permission to develop it into renewable-energy parks -- as though there were no prior understanding. The Mojave is particularly attractive because it not only offers nearly uninterrupted days of bright sunshine in a sparsely populated area, but lies near a major electric-transmission corridor from California to Nevada. "We don't have to sacrifice our national treasures for renewable energy," Mr. Myers said. "We need both."  Credit: By Rebecca Smith
event_description: Legislation to create the Mojave Trails National Monument and other protected wilderness areas.
setting: legislative chamber debate
characters: Senator Dianne Feinstein [Hero]; solar-power developers [Villain]; David Myers [Victim]
plot: Senator Feinstein introduces legislation to protect the Mojave Desert, countering solar developers' plans for large-scale solar farms, which threaten sensitive areas. As a result, some developers reconsider their projects, while conservationists advocate for the preservation of unique desert landscapes.
moral: The problem is the conflict between renewable energy development and conservation of sensitive desert areas. The text suggests that legislation should protect these areas while still allowing for renewable energy development on less sensitive lands.

Title: U.S. News: After Voting No, Republicans Tout Funds
Text: WASHINGTON -- Republicans railed against the Democrats' massive economic-stimulus and spending bills as fiscally irresponsible, but some GOP lawmakers are taking credit for projects in their own districts funded by the measures. "Washington needs to stop spending money that it doesn't have," Michigan Republican Rep. Pete Hoekstra said in attacking the $410 billion omnibus-spending bill, which funds the government through September. But once it passed, he touted its benefits for his district, which stretches along Lake Michigan. "Safe and navigable harbors are economic engines that drive the communities that surround them," Mr. Hoekstra declared, announcing $3 million for harbor improvements. Facing difficult economic times and looking ahead to 2010 elections, lawmakers are under pressure to show they are helping constituents. That is leading some Republicans, and even a handful of Democrats, to highlight funds in bills they voted against. "There is a political game going on here," said Leslie Paige, spokeswoman for Citizens Against Government Waste, a watchdog group. "On the national stage, you want to look like a good-government guy or gal. But at home, you want to get patted on the back and get a photo op." A number of lawmakers disputed this, saying it isn't surprising that a bad bill would contain some good elements. Even if a spending bill is wasteful, they said, that doesn't mean items for their district can't be worthwhile. "Not to be rude, but it's one of the dumbest things," Mr. Hoekstra said of the notion that there is a contradiction. "The only people who are supposed to get money in an omnibus bill are the ones that vote for it? . . . I don't see any inconsistency at all." Democratic Rep. Dennis Cardoza of California, who brands himself a "fiscally responsible legislator who delivers results for the Central Valley," opposed the spending bill. But when President Barack Obama signed it, Mr. Cardoza said he was "pleased to have been able to secure" nearly $25 million worth of projects. A staffer for Mr. Cardoza said the lawmaker voted against the bill not because of the spending, but because it gave the administration authority to overturn a rule that relaxed the requirements of the Endangered Species Act. GOP leaders took great pride in the fact that every House Republican voted against the $787 billion economic-stimulus bill and that all but 16 opposed the spending bill. They battered Mr. Obama and other Democrats, saying the spending bill increased outlays by 8% over the 2008 fiscal year. They also criticized its numerous earmarks, the special items inserted by lawmakers for their districts. Now many of the Republicans who opposed the bills are highlighting earmarks they inserted or other benefits the bills bring to their states. Rep. Mary Bono Mack (R., Calif.), who denounced the stimulus bill as wasteful, soon announced that it provided a $4.2 million grant for her district to prevent families from becoming homeless. "This funding will provide much-needed assistance," she said. Spokeswoman Jennifer May said the congresswoman considered the bill "misguided" and "bloated," but that Ms. Bono Mack's district was especially hard-hit by the housing crisis and the funding was crucial to keep families in their homes. Rep. Cliff Stearns (R., Fla.) voted against the spending bill. When it passed, he announced that he had "secured" $1.7 million in the legislation for a citrus-research project and a mental-health program. Questioned about this, Mr. Stearns issued a statement saying he opposed the bill because of its cost but would be shortchanging his constituents if he didn't seek money for them. Rep. Mario Diaz-Balart (R., Fla.) also opposed the omnibus bill. After it passed, he announced that it included $570,000 for hybrid-fuel trolleys in Miami Lakes. "I am proud to have secured these federal funds to ensure that all residents of Miami Lakes can have easy access to parks, schools, shops and businesses," Mr. Diaz-Balart said. In an interview, Mr. Diaz-Balart said, "The omnibus was too much money, too much spending, too much borrowing, too much debt, and no accountability. Now, I have stuff in that bill, but I still voted against it. But what I have in there, I am very proud of." Rep. Howard Coble (R., N.C.) issued a news release on March 11 boasting that "six Coble earmarks" were in the omnibus bill, including $855,000 to extend an airport runway. Mr. Coble acknowledged he had opposed the spending bill. "I would be in favor of earmark reform," he said in the statement. "But as long as earmarks remain part of the legislative funding process, I would be doing a disservice to the people of the 6th District by not seeking funding for worthwhile projects." Credit: By Naftali Bendavid
event_description: The spending bill gave the administration authority to overturn a rule that relaxed the requirements of the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Washington, D.C. legislative context surrounding spending bills
characters: Rep. Pete Hoekstra [Hero]; Leslie Paige [Villain]; Rep. Dennis Cardoza [Hero]; Rep. Mary Bono Mack [Hero]; Rep. Cliff Stearns [Hero]; Rep. Mario Diaz-Balart [Hero]; Rep. Howard Coble [Hero]
plot: Republican lawmakers criticize spending bills as fiscally irresponsible but later take credit for funding projects in their districts, highlighting a contradiction in their actions. Some Democrats also secure funding despite opposing the bills, leading to a narrative of political opportunism.
moral: The problem is the inconsistency of lawmakers who oppose spending bills yet benefit from them. The text suggests that lawmakers should prioritize their constituents' needs while advocating for responsible spending.
