Title: U.S. News: Exotic Pursuit's Last Stand --- Texas Ranchers Mount Legal Bid to Stop Tighter Curbs on Antelope Hunting
Text: Texas ranchers and state officials are fighting the federal government to save a flourishing regional pastime: the hunting of rare African antelopes on private preserves. The ranchers are waging a last-ditch legal effort to block new federal rules, due to take effect next week, which would make it more difficult to hunt three antelope species in America that are either endangered or possibly extinct in their native Africa. The antelope -- the Scimitar-Horned Oryx, Addax and Dama Gazelle -- are anything but rare in Texas, where exotic-game ranchers charge hunters as much as $10,000 to kill one. Ranchers have spent millions developing a growing antelope population in what has become an expanding but controversial business. They are asking Washington, D.C., federal Judge Beryl Howell to preserve a federal exception, started in 2005, that allows for the selective hunting of the animals in America despite their presence on a federal list of endangered and threatened wildlife. The exception was intended to be limited in scope, allowing ranchers to manage the size and health of existing herds. The government is now stiffening the law on antelope hunting following litigation from animal-rights groups successfully challenging the previous state of the law. Animal-rights advocates say they oppose allowing hunting of an endangered species in the name of conservation. The value of the antelope as hunters' prey "is what keeps the ranchers able to feed them and take care of them," said Charly Seale, executive director of the Texas-based Exotic Wildlife Association, one of the parties in the case, who adds that Texas ranchers have spent considerable sums to repatriate antelope to their native terrain. "We use hunting as a conservation tool." The ranchers are joined by Texas' attorney general and agriculture commissioner, who have voiced concern about losing an industry that advocates estimate generates $1.3 billion in economic activity annually in the state. Texas has largely cornered the U.S. market for antelope hunting, experts say. "Bureaucrats in Washington are imposing ill-considered regulations that trample private property rights while doing little or nothing to help endangered species," Texas Attorney General Greg Abbott said in a statement. The population of the three antelope species in Texas grew by about 400% from 2004 to 2010, reaching a combined total of about 17,000, according to the Exotic Wildlife Association, which says it is the only group to keep a comprehensive population count. But ranchers say that will plummet if the antelope exception disappears, because they will be forced to obtain federal permits to maintain their hunting operations -- a process many see as cumbersome and time-consuming. Many ranchers won't bother to keep and maintain the antelope if they can't readily earn hunting revenue from them, they say. Vanessa Kauffman, a spokeswoman for the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, said the agency would grant ranchers permits to engage in limited hunting if it is "consistent with the requirements of the Endangered Species Act." Mr. Seale, who owns an exotic-game ranch in south-central Texas, said he recently sold his herd of 25 Scimitar-Horned Oryx, but would get back in the business if he isn't forced to deal with "government red tape." The possibility that antelope hunts could be curtailed upsets aficionados such as Tom Shields, a 65-year-old San Antonio dentist who shot an Oryx at a Texas ranch in 2006 and now has it mounted in his living room. He worries that ranchers will "quit fooling" with the antelope if they are forced to obtain permits. Animal-rights advocates, however, applaud the looming demise of the antelope exception. "The hunting ranches obviously do not welcome the permit process, but it is in the antelope's best interests," said Lee Hall, a vice president of Friends of Animals, a Connecticut-based nonprofit. "Hunting these antelope is no way to save them or treat them with dignity." Credit: By Nathan Koppel
event_description: Legal efforts to block new federal rules on hunting endangered antelope species.
setting: legal efforts to block new federal rules on hunting endangered antelope species
characters: Texas ranchers [Hero]; federal government [Villain]; endangered antelope species [Victim]; animal-rights advocates [Villain]
plot: Texas ranchers are fighting against the federal government to block new regulations that would restrict hunting of endangered antelope species, arguing that these regulations threaten their business and the antelope population they have cultivated.
moral: The problem is the federal government's regulations that hinder ranchers' ability to manage antelope populations through hunting, and the text suggests that these regulations should be reconsidered to protect both the ranchers' livelihoods and the antelope species.

Title: U.S. News: Environment Law Gutted In California
Text: California lawmakers on Monday night rolled back one of the most stringent environmental laws in the country, after Gov. Gavin Newsom muscled through the effort in a dramatic move to combat the state's affordability crisis.The Democratic governor -- widely viewed as a 2028 presidential contender -- made passage of two bills addressing an acute housing shortage a condition of his signing the 2025-2026 budget. A cornerstone of the legislation reins in the California Environmental Quality Act, which for more than a half-century has been used by opponents to block almost any kind of development project.The abuses of the law have spread so widely that opponents used it to block some bicycle-lane expansions when Newsom served as San Francisco's mayor, he said during a signing ceremony at the Sacramento capital. Democratic leaders of the Assembly and Senate, who had steered the bills to bipartisan passage earlier Monday, flanked him."We have seen this abuse over and over and over again," the governor said. "We have fallen prey to a strategy of delay. As a result of that, we have too much demand chasing too little supply. This is not complicated, it is Econ 101."Some environmentalists and other defenders of the longstanding law were furious, and warned that developers will now go unchecked. "Who needs Trump when we have a wolf in sheep clothing negotiating backroom deals while he and his oligarch donors score big," one critic wrote on X.The lack of affordable housing has climbed to the top of voter concerns in other coastal blue strongholds. New York City's brutal rental market became a flashpoint in the city's recent Democratic primary for mayor, and helped propel Zohran Mamdani to victory.But California sits at the center of America's home shortage. The state has faced a homelessness crisis. Nine of the 10 least affordable cities in the country are located there, according to a May 20 report by WalletHub, a personal-finance company. And the heart of the problem, say housing experts, are the regulatory barriers to construction. The state needs 3.5 million units, but only about 100,000 are built annually."We have turned off the spigot on housing for the last 20 to 30 years," said Michael Lens, a professor of urban planning and policy at the University of California, Los Angeles.The California Environmental Quality Act was signed into law in 1970 by then-Gov. Ronald Reagan, at a time when Republicans were at the forefront of the nation's burgeoning green movement. President Richard Nixon also signed groundbreaking protections, including the Endangered Species Act.CEQA, as it is known, requires state and local agencies to review environmental impacts of planned projects and to take action to avoid or lower any negative effects. Opponents of projects have used the law to delay them by years.Over time, CEQA became such a thorn in the side of developers that governors, beginning in 1983, kept talking about overhauling it.Newsom has made housing affordability one of his top issues, and during his tenure he has signed numerous bills aimed at getting more homes built. None, though, promise to be more consequential than these two, which streamline the regulatory process for housing, most notably by exempting many projects altogether from the law's review -- particularly ones within densely built cities."We are living in a crisis that has priced out working families, made it harder for people to be able to live where they work," Senate President Pro Tem Mike McGuire said.By Jim Carlton
event_description: California lawmakers rolled back the California Environmental Quality Act to streamline housing development.
setting: California legislative context regarding housing development and environmental regulation
characters: Gov. Gavin Newsom [Hero]; California lawmakers [Hero]; environmentalists [Victim]; developers [Villain]
plot: California lawmakers, led by Gov. Gavin Newsom, rolled back the California Environmental Quality Act to address the housing affordability crisis, allowing developers to bypass environmental reviews, which environmentalists argue will lead to unchecked development.
moral: The problem of housing affordability necessitates regulatory changes to streamline development processes, even at the cost of environmental protections, to meet the urgent demand for housing.

Title: Feds, Citing Salmon, Nix I-405 Plan
Text: Federal fisheries officials have vetoed Olympia's ideas for alleviating gridlock on Interstate 405.    In a recent letter to the Washington State Department of Transportation, the National Marine Fisheries Service declared unacceptable four proposals for accommodating a projected 250% increase in traffic over the next 20 years on the already jam-packed Puget Sound area expressway. The reason: The proposals might cause harm to threatened populations of wild salmon.    Without NMFS's approval, the state can't proceed with the I-405 project, because it wouldn't be able to get the permits it needs to start construction, and would find it difficult to obtain federal funding.    In a process governed by rules established by the Federal Highway Administration, the state and the fisheries agency will enter a conflict resolution process this week to try to iron out a compromise. It won't be without acrimony.    "I'm disgusted," says Sen. Jim Horn, a Republican from Mercer Island who sits on both the I-405 committee created by the Legislature last year and the Senate Transportation Committee. "Fisheries officials are overextending their authorities and clearly threatening that it's going to be their way or no way. Well, their way does absolutely nothing for people and businesses. It's just about fish."    I-405 is a 36-mile stretch of highway from Tukwila to Lynwood that feeds traffic in and out of such cities as Bellevue and Redmond on the technology-rich east side of Lake Washington. It's the state's second-busiest thoroughfare, handling an average 284,000 motorists a day and most of the parts that shuttle between Boeing Co.'s massive assembly plants in Renton and Everett.    The state transportation agency has spent nearly $6 million developing proposals for making traffic flow more swiftly on the freeway in coming decades, when population growth is expected to put extreme pressure on the roadway. The 26-member I-405 planning committee gave the nod in May to three options, which would cost between $2 billion and $6 billion each:    -- Build a light-rail or monorail system along the I-405 corridor, add an extra lane in each direction and complete limited improvements on feeder roads.    -- Expand bus service, build two extra lanes in each direction and improve feeder roads.    -- Add one lane in each direction and build a new four-lane toll expressway from Renton to Lynwood that would be elevated in some portions.    According to Michael Cummings, the DOT's I-405 corridor planning supervisor in Seattle, the response to the three plans from NMFS -- and from the Washington Department of Ecology and Washington State Department of Fish and Wildlife -- was a request for another alternative focusing more on high-capacity public transit. The committee complied and in June voted in favor of a fourth option: Build a transit system but add no new lanes.    The addition of that alternative appeased all but the fisheries service, which in turn sent its Sept. 8 letter to the DOT. In the letter, NMFS said it disapproved of the four options because each would facilitate more residential and commercial growth in the area, which would imperil the habitat of Puget Sound Chinook, a salmon run listed by the U.S. government as threatened under the Endangered Species Act.    The NMFS position raised the ire of many on the committee, including Sen. Horn. "They are trying to take on transportation and growth-planning policy instead of sticking with environmental concerns," he says. "It's not right."    The fisheries service disagrees. "We think our concerns are entirely appropriate," says Brian Gorman, an agency spokesman in Seattle. "We simply cannot concur with the DOT's proposals because more roadways permit more growth, and that's something that isn't in the best interest of fish."    Mr. Gorman says that NMFS officials do believe the congestion needs to be relieved, and that they think both fish and people can be provided for. He points out that the agency in July proposed its own solution: Close one lane of I-405 in each direction to general use and devote the space to a high-occupancy transit system.    "We're advocating a very prudent and cautious approach," Mr. Gorman says.    Mr. Cummings, the state I-405 supervisor, calls NMFS's suggestion "unrealistic." He says the planning committee considered the idea but decided it couldn't possibly be included as an option. DOT consultants, he says, concluded it would make congestion worse than doing nothing at all. The freeway is only four lanes wide at some points. (It's 10 lanes across at its widest.)    NMFS's opposition to the proposals has exposed a rift in the committee. Some members, including Bellevue Deputy Mayor Connie Marshall and King County Councilman Rob McKenna, have joined Sen. Horn in voicing frustration with the fisheries agency. Others, such as Redmond Mayor Rosemarie Ives and Kirkland Councilwoman Joan McBride, have loudly defended NMFS as doing what Congress ordered it to do.    "This faulting [of NFMS] is unfounded," Ms. Ives says. "Their reluctance to sign off on the identified alternatives isn't going to be the nail in this project's coffin. It's going to force us all to do what's responsible."    Meanwhile, there are cheers from environmental groups that have in the past criticized the fisheries agency's efforts to protect salmon as lackluster at best. "It's heartening that the National Marine Fisheries Service is finally starting to do its job," says Eric Espenhorst, a policy associate at Seattle-based Friends of the Earth.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: National Marine Fisheries Service disapproves transportation proposals due to potential harm to threatened salmon habitat.
setting: a conflict resolution process governed by rules established by the Federal Highway Administration regarding the I-405 project
characters: National Marine Fisheries Service [Hero]; Sen. Jim Horn [Villain]; threatened populations of wild salmon [Victim]
plot: The National Marine Fisheries Service disapproves transportation proposals due to potential harm to threatened salmon habitat, leading to a conflict with state officials who seek to alleviate traffic congestion on I-405.
moral: The problem is the potential harm to salmon habitat from transportation proposals, and the text suggests that a more environmentally responsible approach should be taken to address both traffic congestion and the protection of endangered species.

Title: U.S. Unveils Plan to Save Spotted Owl, But Some See Strategy as Short-Sighted
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Bush administration announced some steps designed to preserve the endangered northern spotted owl, but it put off issuing its promised long-term strategy for saving the bird without seriously damaging the timber industry. The administration said its plan would cost 1,000 logging jobs in the Pacific Northwest. But environmentalists contended it would allow so much logging in the next two years that little room would be left to carry out any long-term plan ultimately agreed upon. "This is the most short-sighted approach to saving the owl and saving the timber industry that the White House could come up with," said Rindy O'Brien, chief lobbyist for the Wilderness Society. In announcing the forest-management steps, administration officials also said they support pending legislation that would ban the export of raw logs from state lands. The Forest Service estimates that the change would save about 6,000 sawmill jobs in the Pacific Northwest by the year 2000. In addition, the administration said it will ask Congress to expand the role of a cabinet-level committee that rules on whether proposed timber sales would jeopardize an endangered species. Under the administration's plan, the panel also would be allowed to develop long-term forest-management plans. Environmentalists objected strenuously to this proposal, saying, among other things, that it would politicize the forestry decisions. The administration also plans to seek legislation that would allow it to carry out the spotted owl forest-management plans without being subject to court challenge. On Friday, the administration declared the owl threatened and put it under the protection of the Endangered Species Act. That decision brought dismay to the industry, but timber groups were more hopeful after yesterday's announcement. "It is encouraging that the federal agencies are responding to President Bush's call for balance as it applies to protecting northern spotted owls and timber families," said Mark Rey, executive director of the American Forest Resource Alliance, an industry group. Under the administration's plan, there will be little, if any, change in the amount of logging on federal lands in the Pacific Northwest for the current fiscal year, which ends Sept. 30. In the following year, logging on Interior Department land will be reduced to 750 million-to-800 million board feet from about 950 million board feet this year. The plan was outlined by Agriculture Secretary Clayton Yeutter and Interior Secretary Manuel Lujan. Mr. Lujan said the steps would save 450 pairs of owls, 125 more than under a plan proposed by an inter-agency scientific panel last spring. There are about 2,000 pairs of owls left, mostly in old-growth forests in Oregon and Washington. The 1,000 jobs to be lost as a result of the administration's plan compares with 7,600 under the earlier proposal. Environmentalists, however, contend that the reduced employment loss would be achieved only because the administration would allow excessive cutting in areas that aren't designated as spotted owl habitats. The Agriculture Department, which controls land accounting for 75% of the annual timber harvest, hasn't presented its plan for future fiscal years. The administration is setting up an inter-agency task force to devise a forest management plan for fiscal 1991 that it says will be designed to protect the owl while mitigating economic dislocation. The panel, which will be headed by Mr. Yeutter and will consult with lawmakers and governors from affected states, will complete its work by Sept. 1 so that any legislative changes recommended can be acted upon in the current congressional session. "The administration is deeply concerned both about endangered species and the livelihood of families in the Pacific Northwest who depend on timber production," Mr. Yeutter told reporters. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The administration declared the northern spotted owl threatened and put it under the protection of the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Washington, D.C. political context surrounding the Endangered Species Act and timber industry regulations
characters: Bush administration [Hero]; timber industry [Victim]; environmentalists [Villain]; northern spotted owl [Victim]
plot: The Bush administration announced steps to protect the northern spotted owl while attempting to mitigate job losses in the timber industry. Environmentalists criticized the plan, arguing it would allow excessive logging that could harm the owl's habitat. The administration's actions aim to balance the needs of the owl's protection with the economic interests of timber families.
moral: The problem is the conflict between protecting the northern spotted owl and preserving timber jobs; the text suggests that a balanced approach is necessary to protect both the species and the livelihoods of those dependent on the timber industry.

Title: The Afternoon Report: Existential Angst
Text: Grim housing data have become about as routine as the sunrise, but the latest disappointing report on existing-home sales distinguished itself by also revealing a huge build-up in the number of homes lingering on the market. The National Association of Realtors said existing-home sales skidded by 0.3% during May to a 5.99 million annual pace, the lowest level in almost four years. Given the long downdraft in the housing market, gruesome data from the sector have been piling up like sheets of plywood at a construction site, and the fall in the headline reading on today's report was generally within expectations and even tame by recent standards. But economists said what stood out this time were worrisome readings on prices and supply. According to the trade group, the national median price for an existing home was $223,700, lower by 2.1% compared to the same time a year ago. Given the falloff in demand, lower prices should be no surprise, but the prices decline has proven long and deep. Steven Wood of consulting service Insight Economics wrote in a note that May marked the 10th consecutive month in which median prices declined in annual terms, "the longest period since data collection began in 1968." And critically, the prolonged descent in prices doesn't yet seem to be wooing bargain hunters. Prices are poised to come under even greater pressure over the months to come. That's because the inventory of homes on the market is continuing to surge; in May it advanced to a level that would take nearly nine months to work off at the current pace of sales. Economists were nearly universal in their assessment that the swollen backlog of homes was a sign of continued danger. Just a year ago, inventories were running at slightly more than a six months' supply, while two years ago inventories were less than half of current levels. Joshua Shapiro, chief U.S. economist at MFR Inc., spotted the huge increase in inventories coming. He reasoned that homeowners who kept their properties off the market during the winter in hopes of fetching a higher price come the spring selling season put up for-sale signs in droves last month. He noted that the stockpile of homes for sale had been as lean as 3.7 months in January 2005. Inventories could be winnowed down as sellers realize the crush of competition that they are facing in the market and pull their listings back. Others could slash their asking prices, putting further downward pressure on prices overall. All that means that the housing market remains some distance from its nadir. If buyers pull their existing homes off the market, that means they themselves are unlikely to relocate anytime soon, drying up one possible fount of demand. The tightening of lending standards after the subprime mortgage fiasco also appears likely to restrain demand. And that's trouble not just for homeowners seeking to sell, but also for builders, who could be forced to take even greater steps to rein in still-enormous inventories. "Construction will continue to shrink until much of the inventory overhang is eliminated," Mr. Wood of Insight Economics said. The Commerce Department will unleash the latest findings on new-homes sales tomorrow morning, and builders including Lennar and KB Home are scheduled to report earnings this week. Observers don't think any of those reports will deliver much cause for optimism. "Demand is soft, prices are weak, and the prospects of a turnaround anytime soon are slim," said economist Joel L. Naroff. --- Despite Housing News, Stocks Climb The big inventory increase in the housing market didn't seem to spook markets, as all three major stock indexes rose. The Dow Jones Industrial Average was higher by about 100 points at 13460, the Nasdaq Compsite Index climbed about a dozen points to 2600 and the Standard & Poor's 500 Index gained about eight points to 1510. A decline in crude-oil prices helped sentiment; futures slid by more than $1 a barrel to around $67 after a general strike in Nigeria, Africa's largest crude producer, was resolved. Treasury yields also dipped, helping ease some concern over higher interest rates. The yield on the key 10-year note was about 5.09%. The dollar was mixed. Markets in Europe and Asia were weaker. --- Supreme Court Loosens Campaign-Ad Curbs The Supreme Court today loosened restrictions on corporate- and union-funded political advertisements that air close to an election, weakening a key provision of the McCain-Feingold campaign-finance reforms. The court, split 5-4, upheld an appeals court decision that said that an anti-abortion group should have been allowed to air ads during the final two months before the 2004 elections. The ads asked voters to contact Wisconsin's two senators, Democrats Russ Feingold and Herb Kohl, and urge them not to block President Bush's judicial nominees. Sen. Feingold, co-author of the campaign-finance law, was up for re-election in 2004. In another political-speech case decided today, the justices again split by 5-4 in upholding the right of an Alaskan school principal to confiscate a banner that read "Bong Hits 4 Jesus" from a student who unfurled it off school grounds at an Olympic torch event. "We hold that schools may take steps to safeguard those entrusted to their care from speech that can reasonably be regarded as encouraging illegal drug use," Chief Justice John Roberts wrote. In other cases, the high court threw out -- in another 5-4 split -- an atheist group's challenge to President Bush's faith-based initiatives office. And the court ruled 5-4 that the Endangered Species Act takes a backseat to the Clean Water Act when it comes to the EPA's handing authority to a state to issue water pollution permits. --- Hamas Airs Audio of Captured Israeli Soldier Hamas militants posted an audio recording of Israeli Cpl. Gilad Shalit, who was captured a year ago from an army post near the Gaza Strip. It was the first sign that he is still alive since his capture. "I have spent a full year in prison, and my health is still deteriorating, and I need a prolonged hospitalization. I regret the lack of interest on the part of the Israeli government and the Israel Defense Forces in my case," Cpl. Shalit says in the recording. He was captured June 25, 2006, by militants who tunneled into Israel from Gaza. Though it was uncertain when the tape was made, it appeared authentic; Cpl. Shalit's father said that the statement sounded coerced. Human-rights group B'tselem accused the captors of war crimes, noting their refusal to let Red Cross workers to visit Cpl. Shalit. Separately, a video showed the BBC journalist Alan Johnston wearing a suicide bomber's belt packed with explosives, with a message from his captors warning that the charges will be detonated if any effort is made to free him by force. Mr. Johnston was captured in Gaza March 12; a group called Army of Islam claims responsibility. Deposed Palestinian Prime Minister Ismail Haniyeh denounced the kidnapping in a speech yesterday. Al Qaeda's Ayman al-Zawahri called on Muslims to support Hamas and urged the party to unite with al Qaeda. --- Iran Looks to Clear Up Nuclear Questions The International Atomic Energy Agency said that Iran has invited the nuclear watchdog to send a team to Tehran to draw up a plan to resolve questions over the country's contested nuclear program. Iran claims that it wants to use nuclear energy solely for producing electricity, but Washington and its allies worry that Tehran is developing nuclear weapons. The U.N. Security Council has imposed two round of sanctions against the Islamic regime, and is said to be considering a third unless Iran suspends enrichment of uranium. Iran hid past research from the IAEA and has long stonewalled international inspectors. The new invitation followed discussions between Iranian nuclear negotiator Ali Larijani and IAEA director Mohamed ElBaradei. The IAEA said it will send a team to Tehran "as early as practicable." Separately, Iranian lawmakers said the country will begin rationing gasoline next week amid fuel-supply concerns. Though the nation has plentiful crude reserves, its lacks refining capacity and must import about 40% of its gasoline. The threat of rationing has led some Iranians to stockpile fuel. --- Iraqi Cabinet Backs Draft Oil Law Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki's cabinet has approved a U.S.-backed draft oil law, a measure that along with several other pieces of benchmark legislation is seen as a critical step in the Iraqi reconciliation progress. The legislation is still facing a difficult road; Iraq's fractious parliament is expected to take up the bill next week. Violence was meanwhile continuing, after a suicide bomber walked into the lobby of Baghdad's Mansour Hotel and detonated a blast that killed at least seven, including four Sunni Arab tribal leaders gathered at the hotel for a summit. "It was a great breach of security because there are three checkpoints, one outside and two inside," said Saif al-Rubaie, a hotel worker who witnessed the attack. The sheiks were associated with the Anbar Salvation Council, which has been fighting al Qaeda forces in Anbar province. The attack was just one of several that claimed at least 45 lives around Iraq today. Suicide bombers killed at least 13 Iraqi police officers in an attack on a joint U.S.-Iraqi security station in Beiji, north of Baghdad. A U.S. soldier was killed in southern Baghdad. --- Tyson Strikes Fuels Deal Tyson Foods and synthetic-fuel maker Syntroleum confirmed plans to build a $150 million plant that will take fat from the food company's processing plants and turn it into liquid fuel. A newly formed company, Dynamic Fuels, will turn out fuels for the renewable diesel, jet, and military markets. The first facility is expected to produce 78 million gallons a year of renewable synthetic fuels from 569 million pounds of feedstock, starting in 2010. Tyson reached a similar deal with oil giant ConocoPhillips several months ago. Though emblematic of the drive toward alternative fuels, Tyson and Syntroleum's facility would produce only about 5% of the fuel that comes out of a typical U.S. oil refinery annually. --- Prescription Sales Boost Walgreen Drug retailer Walgreen's earnings surged 20% on strong prescription- drug sales and increased market share in most of its major product categories. Prescription sales, which accounted for two-thirds of Walgreen's revenue during the quarter, increased by 14% and same-store prescription sales jumped 9%. The company said its gross margins improved by 0.84 percentage point to 28.3%. Drugstore chains are facing a legal settlement that may lower a key pricing benchmark for prescription drugs, a potential hit to profits. Rite Aid reports profits Thursday. --- Dry Cleaner Wins Pants Suit A judge ruled in favor of a dry cleaner who had been sued for $54 million over a missing pair of pants, ruling that the owners of Custom Cleaners didn't violate Washington D.C.'s consumer protection law by failing to live up to Roy L. Pearson's expectations of a "Satisfaction Guaranteed" sign placed in the store's window. Mr. Pearson, an administrative law judge, had originally sought $67 million after he claimed that the cleaners lost the suit pants and then tried to replace them with a pair of pants that weren't his. --- Write to Tim Annett at tim.annett@wsj.com
event_description: The Supreme Court ruled that the Endangered Species Act takes a backseat to the Clean Water Act regarding the EPA's authority to issue water pollution permits.
setting: federal court case
characters: Supreme Court [Villain]; Endangered Species Act [Victim]; Clean Water Act [Hero]
plot: The Supreme Court ruled that the Endangered Species Act is subordinate to the Clean Water Act, allowing the EPA to prioritize water pollution permits over species protection.
moral: The ruling undermines the protections afforded by the Endangered Species Act, suggesting a need to reevaluate the balance between environmental regulations and water management.

Title: What's News: Northwest
Text: Gov. Locke May Be Getting Some Bad News in Olympia Gary Locke, Washington's self-styled education governor, may not get the chance to prove it this session. Most bills must be reported out of legislative committees by Friday to make it to the floor of either house, and Senate Education Committee Chairwoman Rosemary McAuliffe, a Bothell Democrat, says she probably won't advance a $1 billion Locke plan to reduce public-school class sizes. Why? She says the Legislature can't afford to pay for that and the $300 million-a-year property-tax-cut measure that Democrats, Mr. Locke included, are backing in the wake of Initiative 695. The initiative reduced fiscal-year 2000 revenue by $750 million, and also set off a tax-cutting craze in Olympia. But Ed Penhale, a Locke spokesman, says the governor isn't crying uncle: "We consider the governor's education proposals very much alive." Katz, 52; Clinton, 89 Portland's mayor, Vera Katz, spoke for 37 fewer minutes than the president did in the annual state-of speech, but tendered nearly as many proposals, including several that observers noted have little to do with City Hall. Ms. Katz allowed as how it would be great if Portland State University added a new engineering school, something to which the Legislature would have to give the nod. She also said that Max, the metro-area light-rail system, should be extended to Vancouver, Wash., perhaps forgetting that Clark County voters said no thanks to that idea in 1995. But the mayor, announcing plans for a run for a third term, also came close to home: She told developers that if they fail to come up with designs that make Portland look prettier, rather than denser, they had better be ready to "build it somewhere else." And she said she supported the establishment of a domestic-partnership registry in which gay couples could officially log their relationships. Crude Salvation Big Oil, in the form of BP Amoco PLC's BP Exploration Alaska Inc. and Atlantic Richfield Co.'s Arco Alaska Inc., promised to spend about $1 billion on projects in the North Slope, Prudhoe Bay and Kuparuk, heartening the oil-revenue-dependent state, which has fretted over the output decline of recent years. And Speaking of Oil Sure, crude oil prices recently hit a nine-year high, but Idaho U.S. Attorney Betty Richardson nonetheless demanded that the U.S. Department of Justice investigate the unfairness of the rising cost of gasoline in the state. "At first blush," she wrote in a letter, "there doesn't seem to be an adequate explanation." The letter was in line with a widespread belief in Idaho, which is that suppliers in Salt Lake City -- not members of OPEC, who make no bones about cutting back on output to push oil values up -- might have somehow colluded to pump up gasoline prices in the state. According to the AAA, Idaho has the eighth-highest pump prices in the nation, while Oregon has the fourth highest. Narrow Decision A Thurston County Superior Court judge dismissed a lawsuit by Gig Harbor-area residents that challenged the state's plan to contract with the private sector to build and operate a toll bridge over the Tacoma Narrows. The plaintiffs plan to appeal. Beaming Bosses The Washington House passed a bill to give employers a $580 million break on unemployment-insurance taxes over the next five years, and to earmark $130 million for the retraining of laid-off workers in the aerospace, fishing and timber industries. Agency's Woes A federal judge slammed the National Marine Fisheries Service, calling its evaluation of the impact of commercial fish trawling on sea lions in the Bering Sea "limited in scope, heavy on general background information and deficient in focused and meaningful discussion and analysis." Not only that, said U.S. District Court Judge Thomas Zilly in Seattle, but NMFS "entirely ignored relevant factors and admittedly failed to analyze and develop projections" based on available information. The agency was ordered back to the drawing board in a lawsuit filed by environmentalists who claim that NMFS has ignored the Endangered Species Act in its management of Bering Sea fisheries, the most lucrative in the nation. The agency said it will get back to work. ---                         The Buzz    The number of stories mentioning the following words in a representative selection of newspapers in the region and the Associated Press:    Jan. 24-Jan. 31  Internet               259 Taxes                  143  Salmon                 86 Initiative 695          68 Rain                    65 Skiing                  30 Ferries                 15 Asian economy           15 Ken Griffey Jr.          6 Snowboarding             4
event_description: A federal judge criticized the National Marine Fisheries Service for its inadequate evaluation of the impact of commercial fish trawling on sea lions, stating it failed to comply with the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal court case
characters: National Marine Fisheries Service [Villain]; sea lions [Victim]; environmentalists [Hero]
plot: A federal judge criticized the National Marine Fisheries Service for its inadequate evaluation of the impact of commercial fish trawling on sea lions, which led to a lawsuit filed by environmentalists claiming NMFS ignored the Endangered Species Act.
moral: The National Marine Fisheries Service must improve its evaluation processes to protect sea lions and comply with the Endangered Species Act.

Title: Congressional campaign to ease curbs on logging business gets under way
Text: The much-anticipated congressional campaign to reduce environmental obstacles to logging has begun.    Aides to Idaho Sen. Larry Craig said he introduced legislation that would exempt so-called salvage logging and certain other timber harvesting techniques from many environmental regulations.    The Idaho Republican said the bill is necessary to allow speedy thinning of densely packed timberlands that are vulnerable to wildfires like last summer's costly infernos, which torched tens of thousands of acres in the Western forests. The bill would also speed logging in burned-out areas, where many trees have been damaged but still retain some commercial value.    Sen. Craig calls the fire danger in federal forests a "crisis," and indeed many environmentalists agree that some forests are perilously overgrown and unhealthy, mainly because of decades of a federal policy, supported by timber companies, to suppress fire to preserve more trees for logging.    The bill is opposed by environmentalists, however, because it takes aim at many procedures they cherish. For example, it would eliminate the right of citizens to challenge some emergency logging with administrative appeals, and would override some provisions of the Endangered Species Act and the National Environmental Policy Act, two of the laws environmentalists have used to win their spotted-owl cases and other legal victories blocking logging. As a result of those court victories, logging on public lands in the Western U.S. has been reduced to less than a quarter of the peak levels of the 1980s.    Moreover, the bill would also promote new logging in untrammeled roadless areas, which environmentalists argue should be essentially left alone because so much other federal forest habitat has been degraded by past logging. "The bill's sole purpose is to increase the cut level on public lands already severely degraded by overlogging" and other practices, said Samantha Mace of the Western Ancient Forest Campaign.    Sen. Craig's proposal is the first of many that are expected in the new Congress to roll back restrictions on logging, and is part of a promised broad review of environmental regulations. For example, Sen. Robert Packwood (R., Ore.) has said he will introduce legislation that would double or triple logging in federal forests in the Northwest.    The effort comes as a new firefight in the West's timber wars has erupted in Sen. Craig's state, triggered by an unusually broad federal-court injunction issued last month that barred logging, road building, mining and grazing in much of six national forests in Idaho. The judge ruled that the activities might damage endangered salmon habitat. The injunction infuriated loggers and others who work the forests, and has been suspended until March -- which has triggered a rush by some companies to push ahead with logging and other projects before the injunction takes effect again.    In recent days, dozens of forest protesters have staged sit-ins in an area known as Cove Mallard, where Shearer Lumber Co. has begun logging in oldgrowth groves. Two protesters were arrested earlier this week for sitting on logging roads and were charged with felonies, the first such charges under a controversial new state law that makes certain logging protests felonies.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Legislation proposed to exempt logging from Endangered Species Act provisions.
setting: congressional campaign to reduce environmental obstacles to logging
characters: Sen. Larry Craig [Hero]; environmentalists [Villain]; citizens [Victim]; Samantha Mace [Villain]; loggers [Victim]; forest protesters [Victim]
plot: Sen. Larry Craig introduces legislation to exempt logging from environmental regulations, claiming it is necessary to reduce wildfire risks. Environmentalists oppose the bill, arguing it undermines protections and harms ecosystems, while loggers and citizens face restrictions due to environmental policies. Protests arise against logging activities, leading to arrests under new laws.
moral: The problem is the excessive environmental regulations that hinder necessary logging practices to prevent wildfires, and the text suggests that legislation should be passed to roll back these restrictions to protect both forests and the logging industry.

Title: Water Saga Illuminates Rove's Methods; Bush Strategist Works Agencies in Bid to Make Policy Decisions Jibe With Political Goals
Text: Washington -- In a darkened conference room, White House political strategist Karl Rove was making an unusual address to 50 top managers at the U.S. Interior Department. Flashing color slides, he spoke of poll results, critical constituencies -- and water levels in the Klamath River basin. At the time of the meeting, in January 2002, Mr. Rove had just returned from accompanying President Bush on a trip to Oregon, where they visited with a Republican senator facing re-election. Republican leaders there wanted to support their agricultural base by diverting water from the river basin to nearby farms, and Mr. Rove signaled that the administration did, too. Three months later, Interior Secretary Gale Norton stood with Sen. Gordon Smith in Klamath Falls and opened the irrigation-system head gates that increased the water supply to 220,000 acres of farmland -- a policy shift that continues to stir bitter criticism from environmentalists and Indian tribes. Though Mr. Rove's clout within the administration often is celebrated, this episode offers a rare window into how he works behind the scenes to get things done. One of them is with periodic visits to cabinet departments. Over the past two years Mr. Rove or his top aide, Kenneth Mehlman -- now manager of Mr. Bush's re-election campaign -- have visited nearly every agency to outline White House campaign priorities, review polling data and, on occasion, call attention to tight House, Senate and gubernatorial races that could be affected by regulatory action. Every administration has used cabinet resources to promote its election interests. But some presidential scholars and former federal and White House officials say the systematic presentation of polling data and campaign strategy goes beyond what Mr. Rove's predecessors have done. "We met together and talked a lot about issues of the day, but never in relation to polling results, specific campaigns or the president's popularity," says Lisa Guide, a political appointee at Interior during the Clinton administration. Frank Donatelli, political director in the Reagan White House, says "we were circumspect about discussing specific administration rulings that had yet to be made." Mr. Rove declined to comment. White House spokeswoman Ashley Snee says the agency visits simply were designed to keep political appointees apprised of the president's accomplishments and priorities. Klamath River water levels were an issue at least as far back as the 2000 presidential campaign. During the unusually dry summer of 2001, angry farmers stormed the head gates to forcibly release water, but the Bush administration generally resisted their demands. In 2002, the issue continued to loom large as Mr. Smith faced a potentially difficult re-election challenge. On Jan. 5, Mr. Rove accompanied the president to an appearance in Portland with Mr. Smith. The president signaled his desire to accommodate agricultural interests, saying "We'll do everything we can to make sure water is available for those who farm." The next day, Mr. Rove made sure that commitment didn't fall through the cracks. He visited the 50 Interior managers attending a department retreat at a Fish and Wildlife Service conference center in Shepherdstown, W.Va. In a PowerPoint presentation Mr. Rove also uses when soliciting Republican donors, he brought up the Klamath and made clear that the administration was siding with agricultural interests. His remarks weren't entirely welcome -- especially by officials grappling with the competing arguments made by environmentalists, who wanted river levels high to protect endangered salmon, and Indian tribes, who depend on the salmon for their livelihoods. Neil McCaleb, then an assistant Interior secretary, recalls the "chilling effect" of Mr. Rove's remarks. Wayne Smith, then with the department's Bureau of Indian Affairs, says Mr. Rove reminded the managers of the need to "support our base." Both men since have left the department. An Interior spokesman, Mark Pfeifle, says Mr. Rove spoke in general terms about the Klamath conflict in the course of a broader discussion. Without directing a policy outcome, Mr. Pfeifle says, Mr. Rove simply "indicated the need to help the basin's farmers." In the end, that is what happened when Interior reversed its previous stance and released more water. Mr. Rove's intervention wasn't the only reason. Mr. McCaleb himself says the biggest factor was a report from the independent National Research Council, which questioned the basis on which Interior scientists had made earlier Klamath flow decisions. But Mr. Rove didn't let the matter drop after the Shepherdstown meeting. Weeks later, he returned to Oregon and met with a half-dozen or so farmers and ranchers. Thereafter, the White House formed a cabinet-level task force on Klamath issues. The results became clear on March 29, when the water was released to parched farms. That hasn't ended the controversy. Environmentalists blame the change in water levels for the subsequent death of more than 30,000 salmon, calling it the largest fish kill in the history of the West. A National Marine Fisheries Service biologist, Michael Kelly, has asked for protection under federal "whistle-blower" laws, saying he was subjected to political pressure to go along with the low-water plan and ordered to ignore scientific evidence casting doubt on the plan. This month, a federal judge ruled the administration violated the Endangered Species Act in the way it justified the water diversion. Administration officials note that the judge found fault only with a narrow portion of the biological opinion, and didn't order changes in water flow. Interior is investigating the cause of the fish kill, Mr. Pfeifle says. Oregon farmers point to other factors in the salmon kill, including water temperature and the presence of an infectious disease during salmon-spawning season. And they haven't stopped pressing to keep the irrigation water coming. A few weeks ago, the federal Bureau of Reclamation in Klamath Falls warned farmers that the department would curtail the irrigation flow. Irate, Republican Rep. Greg Walden began making calls to protest. His first one went to Mr. Rove's office. Within hours, the idea was dropped. Interior officials say managers from two cabinet departments agreed on a way to avoid it. ---                 A River Runs Through Him   A look at Karl Rove's involvement in the Klamath River dispute:   -- Jan. 5, 2002: Rove accompanies Bush, who lost Oregon by less than 1% in 2000, to Portland, Ore.; Bush voices support for Klamath Basin farmers.   -- Jan. 6: Rove gives presentation to Interior Department officials connecting regulatory actions in key states, including Oregon's Klamath issue, to Republican prospects in the coming elections.   -- March 2: Rove meets with farmers in Oregon.   -- March 29: Bush administration sides with farmers and diverts Klamath water for agricultural use.   -- Sept. 21: Thousands of salmon die in the shallower Klamath River.   -- June 25, 2003: Regional officials tell Klamath farmers the flow of irrigation water needs to be curtailed; worried congressmen call Rove's office for help. The decision is reversed later in the day.
event_description: The Bush administration's decision to divert water from the Klamath River basin for agricultural use.
setting: a conference room at the U.S. Interior Department during a meeting in January 2002
characters: Karl Rove [Villain]; U.S. Interior Department [Victim]; environmentalists [Victim]; Indian tribes [Victim]; Oregon farmers [Hero]
plot: Karl Rove influences the U.S. Interior Department to divert water from the Klamath River basin to support agricultural interests, leading to increased water supply for farmers but resulting in environmental harm and the death of salmon.
moral: The diversion of water for agricultural use is prioritized over environmental concerns, suggesting that the administration should reconsider its approach to balance agricultural needs with ecological preservation.

Title: Court Strikes Down Rules Loosening Habitat Protections
Text: A U.S. appeals court has struck down a federal regulation used by the government to loosen habitat protections for the endangered Gulf sturgeon, in a ruling that could make it easier for environmentalists to block development in many other areas where endangered species are listed.    The opinion Thursday by the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals in New Orleans invalidated a regulation under which the Fish and Wildlife Service and National Marine Fisheries Service looked only at the survival of an endangered species in determining whether to set aside so-called critical habitat zones.    The agencies used the rule in 1998 to deny environmentalists' requests to designate critical habitat for the Gulf sturgeon, a salmon-like ocean fish whose population has declined precipitously amid pollution and damming on its river spawning grounds along the Gulf Coast. Such a designation could have curbed construction and navigation activities on as many as a dozen rivers from Florida to Louisiana, including the Pascagoula River which runs past the Pascagoula, Miss., hometown of Senate Majority Leader Trent Lott.    Lawyers for the Sierra Club, which filed a suit challenging that decision, argued that the 1973 Endangered Species Act required that critical habitats be designated to assist a species primarily in its recovery -- not just a smaller area tied to its immediate survival. The U.S. District Court in New Orleans sided with the government, but that decision was reversed on appeal to the Fifth Circuit. The court remanded the case to the lower court, with instructions to disregard the survivor-oriented regulation. If left unchallenged before the U.S. Supreme Court, legal observers say the government will have little choice but to apply a far more rigorous standard over lands it protects for endangered species.    "What this means is that the agencies will have to take a much harder look at critical habitat than they have in the past," said Robert Wiygul, staff attorney for the Earthjustice Legal Defense Fund, a San Francisco environmental group that assisted the Sierra Club in its case.    Lawyers for the government weren't available to comment.    Other environmental groups said the case establishes an important legal precedent that can be used in other habitat battles around the country, including some in which they say habitats aren't being protected. In the San Diego area, for example, environmentalists have notified the government that they intend to file suit for permitting a toll road to go through critical habitat either established or proposed for the threatened California gnatcatcher and Quino checkerspot butterfly.    "Loss of habitat is the No. 1 cause of species endangerment in the United States," said David Hogan, a wildlands coordinator for the Center for Biological Diversity group in Berkeley, Calif.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A U.S. appeals court struck down a federal regulation affecting critical habitat designations for endangered species.
setting: a U.S. appeals court case regarding federal regulations on critical habitat designations
characters: U.S. appeals court [Hero]; Fish and Wildlife Service and National Marine Fisheries Service [Villain]; Gulf sturgeon [Victim]; Sierra Club [Hero]; Earthjustice Legal Defense Fund [Hero]; environmental groups [Hero]; David Hogan [Hero]
plot: The U.S. appeals court struck down a regulation that limited critical habitat designations, allowing environmentalists to potentially block development that harms endangered species like the Gulf sturgeon. This ruling could lead to more rigorous habitat protections across the country.
moral: The problem of inadequate habitat protection for endangered species must be addressed by enforcing stricter regulations that prioritize the recovery of species, not just their immediate survival.

Title: Following Timber's Lead, Farmers In Wash. Are Crafting Habitat Plan
Text: Farmers in Washington, taking a page from the timber industry's book, are working with the state and federal governments on a plan that would protect both salmon and profits. The talks with officials from the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, the Washington Department of Agriculture and others began in December. The goal: to set land- and water-use guidelines that would abide by the U.S. Endangered Species and Clean Water acts but also keep farming economically viable. "Fish and farms . . . we can do both. We have to do both," says Jim Hazen, executive director of the Washington State Horticultural Association, Wenatchee, which represents 3,700 tree-fruit farmers. Curt Smitch, special assistant to Gov. Gary Locke for natural resources, says he hopes an accord can be ironed out by early next year, but adds that is "optimistic," considering all the players at the table. The negotiators aim to craft a blueprint similar to the so-called forests-and-fish pact approved last year by a number of federal and state agencies and endorsed by the timber industry, though not by many major environmental groups in the state. It allows for the harvesting of trees on eight million acres of privately owned land around the state, in exchange for landowners' pledge to meet certain standards to protect habitat for threatened and endangered species. The agriculture plan would cover more than 30,000 farms around the state, both those that rely on irrigated water and those that don't. The idea would be to reward farmers who voluntarily make habitat-protection improvements -- such as erecting buffer zones around streams -- with a "get out of jail free card" protecting them from some of the stiff civil and criminal penalties the Endangered Species Act, or ESA, requires for the destruction of endangered species or their habitats, according to Jim Jesernig, director of the Washington Agriculture Department. Both the possible agriculture plan and its forests-and-fish model are similar in design to the habitat-conservation plans authorized by Congress in an amendment to the ESA. These are designed to protect fish and wildlife, though often to a lesser degree than the act itself would, while relaxing land-use restrictions. HCPs, as the plans are called, have become a popular method of forestry management, viewed by the Clinton administration and others as successful because they allow economic activity to continue but also satisfy many environmental concerns. In Washington, several timber companies have received or are seeking federal and state approval of HCPs. The fate of the latest such timber plan in Washington, for Simpson Timber Co. of Shelton, could be determined within a few months. Mr. Smitch, Gov. Locke's special assistant, says the governor likes HCPs and comparable plans because they offer long-term certainties for both industry and fish and wildlife. They can also make regulators' jobs easier, he says: Instead of "chasing individual species around," regulators can take an ecosystem-based approach. Still, not everyone favors HCPs and their derivatives. Some tribal governments complain that the plans don't go far enough in protecting fish, and many environmentalists argue that insufficient research goes into dictating guidelines, so that the results are often based in politics, not science. While such plans are "a substantial improvement on current practice," they are "substantially less than what the fish need," says David Bayles, conservation director for the Pacific Rivers Council in Eugene. "The conceptual idea . . . is probably fine, but it's really only as good as the conservation plan that results. And, so far, they're not that good." The Seattle-based Washington Environmental Council pulled out of the fish-and-forest talks last year, objecting to what the council complained was a focus on landowners' needs. David Mann, the council's president, says it hasn't decided yet whether to participate in the agriculture negotiations. In any event, even without environmental opposition, striking a deal with farmers might prove more difficult than it was with timber interests. "The trust level's below zero," says Mr. Jesernig of the Washington Agriculture Department. "We have to find a way to work back up to zero." Tensions between farmers and regulators have been high since the National Marine Fisheries Service last year closed several irrigation ditches in the Methow Valley, claiming their operation could be harmful to fish. Not only that, but the agriculture business is a patchwork of different interests in Washington, where more than 200 crops, from peas to potatoes, are produced. Mr. Jesernig, however, says the Locke administration is committed to forging an agreement, in large part because the alternative is so unattractive. "I think we're on the verge of a litigation Armageddon," he says. "I think the courts are a pathetic place to work out natural-resource issues." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Negotiations for an agriculture plan to create habitat conservation plans that protect endangered species while allowing farming.
setting: NA
characters: NA
plot: NA
moral: NA

Title: Timber trade group blames wildfires on less logging, calls for more cutting
Text: The timber industry's main trade group blamed the West's roaring wildfires on insufficient logging and called on Congress to allow more cutting on public lands.    Henson Moore, president of the American Forest & Paper Association, a timber trade group, said the West's fire season, one of the worst in years, results from a "lack of management of these federal forest lands." In the timber industry, "management" means logging. He said that reducing logging levels to protect endangered species has increased the "fuel load and that increases the likelihood for forest fires."    The statements were immediately attacked by the U.S. Forest Service and environmentalists, and further a continuing debate about the role of fire in natural ecosystems and the cause of this year's fires, which have blackened more than 490,000 acres so far. As of yesterday, fires were burning in California, Idaho, Oregon and most other Western states.    While there is little argument that fuel loads are high in public forests, most forestry scientists say that has little to do with the logging limits. Instead, it is the result of decades of a policy of heroically fighting fires in federal forests to preserve more timber for timber companies to cut.    Suppressing fire for so long has eliminated fire's healthy role as a thinner of overcrowded forests and led to the dense packing of trees and brush that fuels many of the current fires. A recent several-year, federally sponsored study by 155 scientists of California's Sierra Nevada area, for example, concluded that "timber harvest, through its effect on forest structure, local microclimates and fuel accumulation, has increased fire severity more than any other human activity."    Moreover, another major reason for this year's fires is simply weather. Much of the West has been hit by record-high temperatures this year, often accompanied by strong winds and dry weather.    Jack Ward Thomas, chief of the Forest Service, called the timber industry position an oversimplification. "This has been a long time in coming," he said. "It didn't come about from a simple situation that we didn't do forest management." Added Sami Yassa, staff scientist with the environmental group Natural Resources Defense Council: "The industry is using fire as a scare tactic. . . . Its position is antiscience, and its motivation is solely to cut more trees, not forest health."    Congress has already opened up formerly restricted areas to so-called salvage logging, a controversial practice that has allowed companies to fell many old-growth trees, in addition to dead and dying trees. President Clinton infuriated environmentalists by signing the bill, and has since said it was a mistake.    Mr. Moore, the trade-group official, said the salvage-logging bill didn't go far enough to prevent fires. He called on Congress to limit citizens' right to challenge planned logging in high-fire danger areas. He also said 120-day limits should be placed on consultations between the Forest Service and federal wildlife agencies required by the Endangered Species Act.    (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: Loggers' Scare Tactics Focus on Wildfires" -- WSJ Oct. 1, 1996)  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Proposal to limit consultations between the Forest Service and federal wildlife agencies under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Congressional debate on logging practices and wildfire management in federal forests
characters: Henson Moore [Hero]; U.S. Forest Service [Villain]; environmentalists [Villain]; timber industry [Hero]; endangered species [Victim]
plot: Henson Moore blames wildfires on insufficient logging and calls for more cutting on public lands, while the U.S. Forest Service and environmentalists attack this position, leading to a debate about fire management and its impact on forest health.
moral: The timber industry argues that increased logging is necessary to prevent wildfires and protect forest health, while the opposition claims this perspective oversimplifies the issue and threatens endangered species.

Title: U.S. News: Seeing Red Over Green Shrub
Text: SAN FRANCISCO -- In order to protect and restore an endangered species, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service sets aside "critical habitats" in places such as the wilds of Alaska or the pine forests of Louisiana. Now, the agency has targeted a few hundred acres in the heart of this city -- including the backyards of some homeowners. That is drawing fire from some residents who, despite government assurances, fear the proposed protections could constrain land use. Under the proposal, about 270 acres of land mostly in parks would be designated as critical habitat for the Franciscan manzanita, a low-growing shrub thought to have been extinct here since the 1940s until a single example was found in 2009. According to Jennifer Norris, supervisor of the agency's field office in Sacramento, a critical-habitat designation often comes with minimal restrictions on land's use, except perhaps to temporarily fence off a particular area. When it involves private property, she said, cooperation is voluntary -- unless plans by a landowner require federal approval. She said an activity that would raise that concern, such as filling in wetlands, would be rare in a San Francisco backyard. Still, many residents have expressed concern that creating the habitat may curtail recreational activities. "We don't have enough open space for all the recreation needs of city residents as it is," Mary McAllister, a retired-medical school administrator, wrote in a comment regarding the proposal. "We cannot afford to lose huge swaths of this precious resource to become a manzanita garden." The habitat would include about one-third of the 800 acres of undeveloped parklands managed by the city. San Francisco has about 3,470 acres of city-owned parkland in total, according to the Trust for Public Land, a conservation group. Similar debates are playing out across the U.S. The amount of land and water covered by the Fish and Wildlife Service's critical-habitat listings has jumped 46% since 2003 to an area as big as Texas and Tennessee combined. That growth has come in tandem with increased listings under the Endangered Species Act, according to the service. In St. Tammany Parish, La., five landowners in February filed suits against Fish and Wildlife for designating 1,544 acres of a timber farm, which sits on federally supervised wetlands, as critical habitat for the dusky gopher frog. The government estimated the designation could cost them as much as $33.9 million, in part, landowners say, because they wouldn't be able to develop the property. The cases were consolidated into one suit, which is pending in U.S. District Court in New Orleans. "When your land is designated as critical habitat, they do something worse than take your land -- they turn it into a federal preserve," said Reed Hopper, an attorney with the Pacific Legal Foundation, a property-rights group representing plaintiffs in the Louisiana case and others. Fish and Wildlife officials wouldn't comment on the Louisiana case and say most of the opposition stems from a misunderstanding of what the critical habitats mean. Just a designation as a critical habitat can be more significant than any restrictions, said Dave Owen, professor of law at the University of Maine. "There could be economic impacts on properties where someone decides not to develop because of critical habitat," said Mr. Owen, whose analysis found few restrictions occurred on land use, public or private. After the discovery of the single Franciscan manzanita, the Wild Equity Institute -- a group that helps organize environmental causes -- helped get the plant listed as endangered, and in September 2012, Fish and Wildlife proposed the habitat area. In June, the agency added 3.2 acres of private property that would abut as many as 22 homes. The city's Recreation and Park Department supports the habitat. Fish and Wildlife's Ms. Norris said specific plans for its restoration would be made after the habitat is finalized. But affected homeowners remain on edge. "If I wanted to terrace my yard completely, I could potentially run into some resistance," said Owen Randall, a 38-year-old legal-services project manager. The objections have gotten the attention of some city officials. "My hope," said Supervisor Norman Yee, "is that the wildlife folks will work with the local jurisdiction and property owners to come up with an amenable solution." Credit: By Jim Carlton
event_description: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service proposed designating about 270 acres in San Francisco as critical habitat for the Franciscan manzanita.
setting: critical habitat designation process in San Francisco
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; homeowners/residents [Victim]; Jennifer Norris [Hero]; Mary McAllister [Victim]; Reed Hopper [Villain]; Supervisor Norman Yee [Hero]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service proposes to designate 270 acres in San Francisco as critical habitat for the Franciscan manzanita, which raises concerns among local homeowners about potential restrictions on land use. Despite assurances from the agency, residents fear that the designation could limit their recreational activities and property rights, leading to opposition and calls for collaboration between the agency and local officials.
moral: The problem is the fear and misunderstanding among residents regarding the implications of critical habitat designation, and the text suggests that the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service should work collaboratively with local property owners to address their concerns and find a mutually agreeable solution.

Title: Salmon Recipes Won't Be Running on This Program
Text: "Who Wants to Be a Millionaire"? Who cares? Now there's something more riveting to watch: Salmon TV.    Seattle, Bellevue and King County governments are sponsoring a monthly half-hour program focusing on the latest government efforts to help save the salmon and spotlighting ways the public can join in.    The first program aired Friday night and featured the executives for King, Snohomish and Pierce counties and Seattle City Council member Margaret Pageler. The program will repeat throughout the month at various times on 20 noncommercial municipal, educational and tribal cable channels to allow as wide a viewership as possible, says Dan Klusman, a spokesman for the King County Endangered Species Act office.    The channels, dubbed the Salmon Information Television Network, can reach 600,000 households from Tacoma to Marysville, Mr. Klusman says, although no one has figures yet on how many households actually chose to tune in for broadcasts so far. Regardless of ratings, the sponsors hope the television programs will drive people to seek out additional information on their Web site, www.salmoninfo.org, or by calling 1-877-SALMON-9.    Future episodes include ways the public can help save salmon as well as a feature on watersheds.    The programs, produced by volunteer efforts with money stitched together from local governments and other sponsors, are meant to be entertaining as well as educational, Mr. Klusman says, and is hosted by aptly named former Northwest comedy "Almost Live" cast member Nancy Guppy.
event_description: A television program series aimed at educating the public about efforts to save salmon.
setting: Seattle, Bellevue and King County governments' public awareness campaign
characters: Seattle, Bellevue and King County governments [Hero]; public [Victim]
plot: The Seattle, Bellevue and King County governments sponsor a television program to educate the public about efforts to save salmon, aiming to engage viewers and encourage them to seek more information.
moral: The problem is the lack of public awareness and engagement in salmon conservation efforts, and the preferred solution is to promote educational television programs that inform and involve the community.

Title: Judge Bars Land Transfer For Nuclear Waste Dump
Text: SAN FRANCISCO -- A federal court here effectively doomed an attempt by the Interior Department to push through a land transfer for a controversial nuclear-waste dump in the California desert. Judge Marilyn Patel extended a temporary restraining order blocking the transfer of a patch of federal land known as Ward Valley to the state. The state supports an effort to build a low-level nuclear waste dump there, but the proposal has met fierce public resistance. Earlier this month, Interior Secretary Manuel Lujan said he planned to transfer the land to the state, a key requirement for advancing the dump project. Critics of the proposed dump accused Mr. Lujan, who leaves office today, of circumventing normal procedures to ram the transfer through before leaving his post. Dump opponents sued, alleging that the land transfer didn't comply with the Endangered Species Act, among other laws. Judge Patel's decision means the thorny issue of low-level nuclear-waste disposal will fall into the lap of the Clinton administration. That's where dump opponents wanted it, because they believe Mr. Clinton's administration will be more sympathetic to their cause. A federal law passed in 1980 required states to set up low-level nuclear dumps, but not one has yet been built, largely because of public opposition.
event_description: A federal court ruling blocks the transfer of federal land for a proposed nuclear waste dump due to compliance issues with the Endangered Species Act.
setting: a federal court case
characters: Judge Marilyn Patel [Hero]; Interior Secretary Manuel Lujan [Villain]; dump opponents [Victim]
plot: Judge Marilyn Patel blocks the land transfer for a nuclear waste dump, which was being pushed by Interior Secretary Manuel Lujan, due to compliance issues with the Endangered Species Act, thereby shifting the issue to the Clinton administration.
moral: The problem is the rushed land transfer for a nuclear waste dump that circumvents normal procedures; the preferred solution is to have the Clinton administration address the issue more sympathetically towards dump opponents.

Title: The Evening Wrap: Opening the Ad Gates
Text: The nation's highest court ruled that an important package of campaign-finance measures trampled free-speech rights of labor unions and issue advocates, in a decision that likely will have ad-weary voters hitting the mute button often during the coming presidential campaign. The Supreme Court, in a 5-4 decision, loosened restrictions on corporate-and union-funded political advertisements that run close to election day, weakening a key provision of the McCain-Feingold campaign-finance legislation. The law had blocked companies, unions or other interest groups from running broadcast advertisements that reference a candidate for federal office  two months before a general election or a month before a primary. The justices upheld a lower court ruling that Wisconsin Right to Life, an antiabortion group, should have been allowed to broadcast advertisements in the months leading up to the 2004 elections. The ads asked voters to contact Wisconsin's two senators, Democrats Russ Feingold and Herb Kohl, and urge them not to block President Bush's judicial nominees. Sen. Feingold, as circumstance would have it the co-author of the campaign- finance law, was then up for re-election. "Discussion of issues cannot be suppressed simply because the issues may also be pertinent in an election," wrote Chief Justice John Roberts in the majority opinion. "Where the First Amendment is implicated, the tie goes to the speaker, not the censor." The court had previously upheld most of McCain-Feingold from a broad attack brought by Sen. Mitch McConnell in 2003, and while the majority has stopped short of overturning that decision, at least three justices who joined the majority -- Justices Antonin Scalia, Clarence Thomas and Anthony Kennedy -- indicated they would have gone that extra step. Still, the ruling would appear to throw open the ad floodgates in time for the 2008 race. The ACLU, NRA and others that had linked up in an unlikely coalition to get the provision tossed out are almost certain to be blasting at each other's preferred candidates as often as their pocketbooks will allow. Indeed, the effect of today's ruling may be seen as soon as December, as a number of states have moved primaries forward into January. Observers said that the ruling will make an indelible mark in the 2008 race and beyond. "There is no doubt today's decision reflects a constitutional sea change that is likely to have dramatic effects on upcoming elections," said Richard H. Pilbes, a professor at New York University School of Law, on the Supreme Court blog of law firm Akin, Gump. "Some will celebrate that change, others will bemoan it, but that the change is dramatic cannot be doubted." In another speech case before the high court, the justices split 5-4 in upholding the right of an Alaskan school principal to confiscate a 14-foot banner reading "Bong Hits 4 Jesus" from a student who unfurled it away from school grounds at an Olympic torch-relay event in 2002. "We hold that schools may take steps to safeguard those entrusted to their care from speech that can reasonably be regarded as encouraging illegal drug use," Chief Justice Roberts wrote for the majority. Joseph Fredrick, the student who had set up the banner, said that the message was meant to be nonsensical and was intended as an illustration of his right to say anything at all. Also today, the high court threw out, in a 5-4 split, an atheist group's challenge to President Bush's office of faith-based initiatives. And finally the justices ruled -- in yet another decision that broke 5-4 -- that the Endangered Species Act takes a backseat to the Clean Water Act when it comes to the EPA's handing authority to a state to issue water pollution permits. --- Existing-Homes Sales Weaken The National Association of Realtors said existing-home sales skidded by 0.3% during May to a 5.99 million annual pace, the lowest level in almost four years. Given the long downdraft in the housing market, gruesome data from the sector have been piling up like sheets of plywood at a construction site, and the fall in the headline reading was generally within expectations and even tame by recent standards. But economists said that what stood out were worrisome readings on prices and supply. According to the trade group, the national median price was $223,700, 2.1% lower than the same time last year. Steven Wood of consulting service Insight Economics wrote that May marked the 10th consecutive month in which median prices declined in annual terms, "the longest period since data collection began in 1968." And critically, the prolonged prices skid isn't wooing bargain hunters. Prices are poised to come under even greater pressure over the months to come as the inventory of homes on the market continues to surge; in May inventories rose to a level that would take nearly nine months to work off at the current pace of sales. But if prices are heading lower, many homeowners seem to be convinced it isn't going to happen to them. According to the Boston Consulting Group, Americans are confident that their homes are retaining and even climbing in value.
