And 75% believe they could sell their house within the next six months at a price they think that it's worth. --- Stocks Flatten Out A large inventory increase in the housing market didn't spook markets too much, although by the end of the session the major benchmarks had flattened out. The Dow Jones Industrial Average declined 8.21 points to end at 13352.05, the Nasdaq Composite Index slipped 11.88 points to 2577.08, and the Standard & Poor's 500 Index slid 4.82 points to 1497.74. Slumping crude-oil prices helped stocks early on, but futures resumed their climb late in the day as relief over the end of a general strike in large African oil producer Nigeria was outweighed by reports of more refinery outages, keeping traders' supply concerns alive. The front-month crude-oil futures contract on the New York Mercantile Exchange climbed four cents a barrel to settle at $69.18. Treasury yields dipped, helping ease concern over higher interest rates. The yield on the key 10-year note ended at 5.079%. The dollar was mixed. Markets in Europe and Asia were weaker. --- Hamas Airs Audio of Captured Israeli Soldier Hamas militants posted an audio recording of Israeli Cpl. Gilad Shalit, who was captured a year ago from an army post near the Gaza Strip. It was the first sign that he is still alive since his capture. "I have spent a full year in prison, and my health is still deteriorating, and I need a prolonged hospitalization. I regret the lack of interest on the part of the Israeli government and the Israel Defense Forces in my case," Cpl. Shalit says in the recording. He was captured June 25, 2006, by militants who tunneled into Israel from Gaza. Though it was uncertain when the tape was made, it appeared authentic; Cpl. Shalit's father said that the statement sounded coerced. Human-rights group B'tselem accused the captors of war crimes, noting their refusal to let Red Cross workers to visit Cpl. Shalit. Separately, a video showed the BBC journalist Alan Johnston wearing a suicide bomber's belt packed with explosives, with a message from his captors warning that the charges will be detonated if any effort is made to free him by force. Mr. Johnston was captured in Gaza March 12; a group called Army of Islam claims responsibility. Deposed Palestinian Prime Minister Ismail Haniyeh denounced the kidnapping in a speech yesterday. Al Qaeda's Ayman al-Zawahri called on Muslims to support Hamas and urged the party to unite with al Qaeda. Meanwhile, Israeli Prime Minister Ehud Olmert promised that he'll release 250 Fatah members from Israeli prisons as a gesture of goodwill aimed at bolstering Palestinian President Mahmoud Abbas following his party's break with Hamas. Mr. Olmert announced the prisoner release at a summit with Mr. Abbas and the leaders of Egypt and Jordan. "I will bring before the Israeli cabinet a proposal to free 250 Fatah prisoners who do not have blood on their hands, after they sign a commitment not to return to violence," Mr. Olmert said at the gathering. --- Accident Raises Questions on Chinese Tires A lawsuit stemming from a fatal car accident in Pennsylvania is pointing out the potential dangers of tires imported from China. The accident occurred last August, when a steel-belted radial on a cargo van carrying four passengers allegedly experienced a tread separation -- the type of problem that prompted the big Firestone recall of 2000 -- causing the driver to lose control and crash. Two passengers were killed, the two others injured. The tire's U.S. distributor said that it discovered that a safety feature that it had specifically requested the manufacturer to include was omitted from up to 450,000 of the tires imported from China's Hangzhou Zhongce Rubber Co. since 2002. The problem comes amid the discovery of issues with several other types of Chinese imports, such as toys that were discovered to be painted with lead paint and toothpaste that contained hazardous materials, as well as contaminated ingredients that ended up in several brands of pet food. --- Iran Looks to Clear Up Nuclear Questions The International Atomic Energy Agency said that Iran has invited the nuclear watchdog to send a team to Tehran to draw up a plan to resolve questions over the country's contested nuclear program. Iran claims that it wants to use nuclear energy solely for producing electricity, but Washington and its allies worry that Tehran is developing nuclear weapons. The U.N. Security Council has imposed two round of sanctions against the Islamic regime, and is said to be considering a third unless Iran suspends enrichment of uranium. Iran hid past research from the IAEA and has long stonewalled international inspectors. The new invitation followed discussions between Iranian nuclear negotiator Ali Larijani and IAEA director Mohamed ElBaradei. The IAEA said it will send a team to Tehran "as early as practicable." Separately, Iranian lawmakers said the country will begin rationing gasoline next week amid fuel-supply concerns. Though the nation has plentiful crude reserves, its lacks refining capacity and must import about 40% of its gasoline. The threat of rationing has led some Iranians to stockpile fuel. --- Iraqi Cabinet Backs Draft Oil Law Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki's cabinet has approved a U.S.-backed draft oil law, a measure that along with several other pieces of benchmark legislation is seen as a critical step in the Iraqi reconciliation progress. The legislation is still facing a difficult road; Iraq's fractious parliament is expected to take up the bill next week. Violence was meanwhile continuing, after a suicide bomber walked into the lobby of Baghdad's Mansour Hotel and detonated a blast that killed at least seven, including four Sunni Arab tribal leaders gathered at the hotel for a summit. "It was a great breach of security because there are three checkpoints, one outside and two inside," said Saif al-Rubaie, a hotel worker who witnessed the attack. The sheiks were associated with the Anbar Salvation Council, which has been fighting al Qaeda forces in Anbar province. The attack was just one of several that claimed at least 45 lives around Iraq today. Suicide bombers killed at least 13 Iraqi police officers in an attack on a joint U.S.-Iraqi security station in Beiji, north of Baghdad. A U.S. soldier was killed in southern Baghdad. --- Tyson Strikes Fuels Deal Tyson Foods and synthetic-fuel maker Syntroleum confirmed plans to build a $150 million plant that will take fat from the food company's processing plants and turn it into liquid fuel. A newly formed company, Dynamic Fuels, will turn out fuels for the renewable diesel, jet, and military markets. The first facility is expected to produce 78 million gallons a year of renewable synthetic fuels from 569 million pounds of feedstock, starting in 2010. Tyson reached a similar deal with oil giant ConocoPhillips several months ago. Though emblematic of the drive toward alternative fuels, Tyson and Syntroleum's facility would produce only about 5% of the fuel that comes out of a typical U.S. oil refinery annually. --- Prescription Sales Boost Walgreen Drug retailer Walgreen's earnings surged 20% on strong prescription- drug sales and increased market share in most of its major product categories. Prescription sales, which accounted for two-thirds of Walgreen's revenue during the quarter, increased by 14% and same-store prescription sales jumped 9%. The company said its gross margins improved by 0.84 percentage point to 28.3%. Drugstore chains are facing a legal settlement that may lower a key pricing benchmark for prescription drugs, a potential hit to profits. Rite Aid reports profits Thursday. --- Lost-Pants Suit Lost Sure, the Supreme Court is winding down its term by unveiling a collection of important decisions on matters of great constitutional import. But the justices' august perch was only the second most closely watched court in Washington this morning. Enjoying top billing was the District of Columbia Superior Court, where Judge Judith Bartnoff ruled in favor of a dry cleaner who had been sued for $54 million over a missing pair of pants. Roy L. Pearson, an administrative law judge, brought the lawsuit after Custom Cleaners allegedly lost a pair of his suitpants and attempted to replace them with some other pair that didn't belong to him. Mr. Pearson, reduced to tears during trial when speaking of his missing trousers, claimed that the cleaners violated Washington's consumer-protection law by failing to live up to his expectations of "Satisfaction Guaranteed" -- as guaranteed in sign placed in the store's window. Mr. Pearson originally sought $67 million, a number that he says he arrived at by tallying up years of alleged violations and almost $2 million in common law fraud claims. "A reasonable consumer would not interpret 'Satisfaction Guaranteed' to mean that a merchant is required to satisfy a customer's unreasonable demands" or agree to demands that the merchant would have reasonable grounds for disputing," the decision in the case read. Judge Bartnoff ordered the plaintiff to pony up the court costs of defendants Soo Chung, Jin Nam Chung and Ki Y. Chung. --- Write to Tim Annett at tim.annett@wsj.com
event_description: The Supreme Court ruled that the Endangered Species Act takes a backseat to the Clean Water Act regarding the EPA's authority to issue water pollution permits.
setting: Supreme Court ruling on campaign finance measures
characters: Supreme Court [Hero]; labor unions and issue advocates [Victim]; corporate and union-funded political advertisements [Villain]; Wisconsin Right to Life [Hero]; Sen. Russ Feingold [Victim]
plot: The Supreme Court ruled that campaign-finance measures infringe on free speech, allowing corporate and union-funded political advertisements close to elections, which harms labor unions and issue advocates while benefiting groups like Wisconsin Right to Life.
moral: The ruling highlights the need to protect free speech rights in political advertising, suggesting that restrictions on such speech should be lifted to ensure a more open electoral process.

Title: U.S. News: U.S. Watch
Text: EDUCATION New Student Loans Set at Higher Rate The cost of borrowing for college is about to get more expensive. New 10-year U.S. Treasurys were sold at auction Wednesday with a 4.483% yield. Interest rates on new federal undergraduate student loans are determined each year by adding the yield on the 10-year Treasury note in the May auction to a fixed premium of 2.05 percentage points. That means that the interest rate on a new student loan should rise to around 6.5% in July, the highest level since 2008, from 5.5% currently. That 10-year Treasury yield has climbed a lot since a year ago. But it could have been worse for borrowers. In secondary-market trading, the 10-year yield reached as high as roughly 5% in October. It was above 4.6% last week, before data showed cooling in the labor market, causing investors to increase bets on interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. -- Sam Goldfarb --- GEORGIA Election Interference Case Likely Delayed A Georgia appeals court on Wednesday agreed to review a lower-court ruling allowing Fulton County District Attorney Fani Willis to continue to prosecute the election interference case against Donald Trump. The move seems likely to delay the case and is the second time in as many days that the former president has gotten a favorable ruling that could push any future trials beyond the November election, when he is expected to be the Republican nominee for president. A day earlier, the judge in his Florida classified-documents case indefinitely postponed that trial date. Trump and some other defendants in Georgia had tried to get Willis and her office removed from the case, saying her romantic relationship with special prosecutor Nathan Wade created a conflict of interest. Superior Court Judge Scott McAfee in March found that no conflict of interest existed that should force Willis off the case. -- Associated Press --- CALIFORNIA Two Children Swept Away by Creek Die Two young siblings died after being swept away by a rapidly flowing creek in Southern California's San Bernardino Mountains, authorities said. The tragedy occurred on Tuesday when a mother took her 4-year-old daughter and 2-year-old son to spend the afternoon at Thurman Flat picnic area on Highway 38 near Mill Creek, the San Bernardino County Sheriff's Department said. They were playing along the creek when at one point the mother was attending to her son and her daughter was taken downstream, the department said. "The mother searched for her but could not locate her," the department said. "When she returned, her son was missing and assumed to be down river as well." Rescue crews eventually found both children. The siblings were taken to local hospitals and pronounced dead after extensive efforts to save their lives, the department said. -- Associated Press --- OBITUARY Pete McCloskey, 96, Ran Against Nixon Pete McCloskey, a pro-environment, antiwar California congressman who co-wrote the Endangered Species Act and co-founded Earth Day, has died. He was 96. A Republican "in the mold of Teddy Roosevelt," as he often put it, McCloskey also ran for president against an incumbent Richard Nixon in 1972. He battled party leaders while serving seven terms in Congress and went on to publicly disavow the GOP in his later years. He died at home Wednesday, according to Lee Houskeeper, a family friend. McCloskey cited disillusionment from influence peddling and ethics scandals under the George W. Bush administration as reasons why he switched parties in 2007 at the age of 79. His enduring legacy is the Endangered Species Act, which protects species designated as endangered or threatened and conserves the ecosystems on which they depend. -- Associated Press
event_description: Discussion of the legacy of the Endangered Species Act.
setting: discussion of the legacy of the Endangered Species Act
characters: Pete McCloskey [Hero]
plot: Pete McCloskey, a pro-environment congressman, co-wrote the Endangered Species Act, which protects endangered species and their ecosystems, and his legacy is highlighted following his death.
moral: The problem of endangered species requires continued advocacy and protection, as exemplified by McCloskey's legacy, suggesting that we should honor and build upon his efforts to safeguard the environment.

Title: U.S. News: White House Considers Ways to Cut Emissions
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Bush administration -- under pressure from Congress, some big businesses and overseas allies -- is weighing proposals to curb U.S. greenhouse-gas emissions. The administration continues to oppose a bill to be debated soon in Congress that would impose mandatory caps on carbon-dioxide emissions, White House press secretary Dana Perino said. "But our views on how to do this [control greenhouse-gas emissions] the right way are things that we are talking about," she said. The White House said President Bush might make a statement in the next few days. Ms. Perino was responding to a report in the Washington Times that Mr. Bush was poised to call on Congress this week to pass legislation to combat global warming. The administration is worried that the current regulatory and legislative framework is poorly designed to deal with climate change and could have harsh unintended effects on many businesses and economic sectors, Ms. Perino said. "You have . . . a regulatory train wreck," Ms. Perino said Monday, citing the Clean Air Act, Endangered Species Act and National Environmental Policy Act, among others, as laws that potentially could be used to rein in greenhouse-gas emissions. Those laws are creating "regulatory paths on climate change that they were never meant to deal with," she added. One option administration officials have discussed, according to individuals close to the administration, is a statement endorsing caps on carbon-dioxide emissions on the condition that such caps wouldn't take effect until technology capable of capturing and storing carbon- dioxide emissions at large power plants is proven commercially viable. Ms. Perino said White House officials internall are engaging in a "very robust discussion" on the issue. The prospect of a Bush shift on climate change stirred unease Monday in the business community, prompting a series of calls from executives and industry lobbyists to the White House, a person familiar with the calls said. Many experts say the technology is at least a decade from being commercially viable on a large scale. The Energy Department withdrew its support for a project to test such technology in January after huge cost overruns. The administration's signal that it might make new proposals on reducing greenhouse gases comes as the president is under increasing pressure on the issue from congressional Democrats and foreign countries, especially in Europe. A statement on the issue also could help the White House firm up a national goal for reductions that it wants to offer to other countries involved in international negotiations over climate change. The leading bill in Congress that attempts to achieve that goal is a measure sponsored by Sens. Joseph Lieberman (I., Conn.) and John Warner (R., Va.) that would cap greenhouse-gas emissions from power plants and other polluters, and allow companies that exceeded the limits to buy credits from other companies that met them with room to spare. The administration has opposed the bill. The Lieberman-Warner bill has been approved by one committee, but faces an uphill fight on the Senate floor, where 60 votes would be needed to move it forward. --- Greg Hitt and Elizabeth Williamson contributed to this article.
event_description: Discussion of potential changes to the regulatory framework involving the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Congressional debate on climate change legislation
characters: Bush administration [Villain]; Congress [Hero]; business community [Victim]
plot: The Bush administration is weighing proposals to curb greenhouse-gas emissions while opposing a bill in Congress that would impose mandatory caps. The administration's concerns about the regulatory framework create unease in the business community, which fears negative impacts from potential regulations.
moral: The problem is the poorly designed regulatory framework that could harm businesses, and the text suggests that Congress should pass legislation that effectively addresses greenhouse-gas emissions while considering the viability of technology.

Title: Hydro-Plant License Process Is Challenged
Text: A coalition of environmentalists and government agencies is objecting to the time-honored process for licensing hydropower projects in the Northwest, and their legal challenge could have an impact on the fates of dozens of dams and turbines in the region. A three-judge panel of the Ninth U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals, based in San Francisco, will hear arguments in Portland today over a challenge to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission's decision to issue a new license to a pair of McKenzie River power-generating plants that some say threaten the fish that call the waterway home. The focus of the legal fight is narrow: whether two small turbines on the river in Oregon that generate just 23 megawatts of electricity should be allowed to continue to operate as they have for more than 50 years. But the impact of the appeals court's decision could be far-reaching. Lawyers for the environmentalists and government agencies mounting the challenge say their aim is to force sweeping changes in the way the FERC evaluates applications for the re-licensing of the facilities that generate most of the electricity in the region. The appellants' argument is that the way rivers flowed in the Northwest before the Industrial Revolution -- not the way power-generating dams and canals have since forced them to move -- should serve as the baseline for assessing the merits of hydropower projects, including the more than 300 hydropower dams in the Northwest. Environmentalists favor the longer-range assessment because it takes into account how decades of hydroelectric projects and other developments have affected what they call the "natural" state of the environment. The FERC's approach, on the other hand, looks only at the environmental impact since the previous license was granted. If the appeal is successful, lawyers say, federal energy regulators will be required to put the natural state of the environment on a equal footing with the needs of electricity consumers in the Northwest, the majority of whom buy their power from facilities that rely on water, rather than natural gas or coal or fuel oil, to generate electricity. "No court has ever ruled on how this baseline for decision-making should be set," says Kristen Boyles of Seattle, the lead attorney for the environmental groups. "Now, a court will decide at what point FERC must begin its evaluation." The case "squarely presents the issue of what environmental analysis is required for dam re-licensing," says Myron Marlin, spokesman for the U.S. Justice Department. The effects of hydropower dams on American rivers "can't be assumed away just because the dams were first licensed 50 years ago." In April 1997, the commission approved the Eugene Water and Electric Board's application for a new license for the Leaburg and Walterville power plants, built in 1930 and 1911, respectively. The re-licensing allowed the utility to continue diverting nearly half of the flow of the river into a canal to drive turbines at the two plants. The canal begins at the Leaburg Dam, 25 miles east of Eugene, stretches for six miles along the wall of the McKenzie River Valley and then plunges down the hillside through the Leaburg Powerhouse. About four miles downstream, the water is diverted again and makes a similar trip to the Walterville Powerhouse. After powering the turbines, the water -- 2,575 cubic feet a second at peak flow -- returns to the river near Thurston, 18 miles from Leaburg. Environmentalists and biologists claim the diversion harms several species of fish swimming in the river, including the McKenzie River bull trout -- listed as a threatened species under the U.S. Endangered Species Act in June 1998 -- and the wild spring Chinook salmon, which has been proposed for listing as threatened. So, in January 1998, five environmental groups, led by the Pacific Rivers Council of Eugene and American Rivers of Washington, D.C., appealed the re-licensing to the San Francisco-based court, claiming that the commission violated both the Federal Power and National Environmental Policy acts by considering the present rather than natural condition of the river in making its decision. A group of state and federal agencies also challenged the ruling, and the two appeals were consolidated. In their appeal, the agencies -- the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, the Environmental Protection Agency, the National Marine Fisheries Service and the Oregon Department of Fish and Wildlife -- made the same points as the environmentalists. They also argued that the FERC had failed to properly consider the scientific evidence presented by the agencies during the application process. Fisheries managers' own research showed that less water should be diverted into the power canals, especially at times of low water. But the commission disregarded the opinion, "substituting its judgment" and, in so doing, violated the Federal Power Act, says Jas Adams, a deputy Oregon attorney general representing the state's Department of Fish and Wildlife in the appeal. The appellants want the court to order the FERC to start the re-licensing process over again, and to require the commission and the Eugene utility to focus on the way the McKenzie flowed before 1911 in making and considering arguments for renewal. But FERC officials say that the commission fully complied with all federal laws in the Eugene case, and that it would be unreasonable to review a license pretending that a decades-old power plant doesn't exist. Officials of the Eugene Water and Electric Board are "frustrated by accusations regarding harm our projects are doing to the river," says Laurie Power, the utility's environmental manager. The utility will close its Walterville powerhouse -- at a cost of $140,000 annually -- during critical spring weeks when juvenile salmon are flushed to sea. And the utility pledges to spend $12 million on new fish ladders and other protective measures. As for the claim that the plants take too much water, Ms. Power says the utility asked for data justifying the claim, "yet that data was never brought forward." Over the next 10 years, the FERC will be asked to re-license 229 hydroelectric projects around the U.S., including 38 in the Northwest. There are already 12 such power plants in the region in the re-licensing application process. "We want to define the way they should be doing this new business," says Mr. Adams, the deputy Oregon attorney general. "How many fish is FERC willing to kill?" Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A legal challenge is being made against the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission's decision to issue a new license for hydropower plants that may threaten local fish species.
setting: a legal challenge in the Ninth U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals regarding hydropower project licensing
characters: environmentalists and government agencies [Hero]; Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) [Villain]; fish species (including McKenzie River bull trout and wild spring Chinook salmon) [Victim]; Eugene Water and Electric Board [Villain]
plot: Environmentalists and government agencies are challenging the FERC's decision to issue a new license for hydropower plants, arguing that the environmental impact assessment is inadequate and that the natural state of the river should be considered. The appeal could lead to a reevaluation of how hydropower projects are licensed, potentially affecting numerous dams in the region.
moral: The problem is that the current licensing process disregards the natural state of the environment, which harms fish species. The text suggests that the FERC should reevaluate its licensing process to prioritize environmental considerations over electricity generation needs.

Title: Salmon Battle Could Spawn Much Bitterness
Text: Federal officials are expected to propose adding more Northwestern salmon to the list of endangered species, escalating a preservation battle that could exceed the conflict over saving the spotted owl.    The National Marine Fisheries Service, an arm of the Commerce Department, is scheduled to announce Friday whether four species of salmon in the Columbia and Snake Rivers merit protection under the Endangered Species Act.    Wildlife officials, environmentalists and even industry representatives expect the service to propose endangered status for at least one of the species, the fall chinook salmon. The other species may also get protection, although they're considered less likely to be listed as endangered, in part because some may already be extinct. The fish will join another salmon species, the Snake River sockeye, which was recommended for protection in April.    Saving the salmon has momentous implications for the Northwestern economy and will affect other parts of the West Coast as well. Although depleted by habitat destruction and fishing practices, the main culprit in pushing the fish to the brink of extinction is a series of eight huge federal dams built mainly in the 1940s and 1950s in the Columbia and Snake River systems.    The dams produce the cheap hydroelectric power and irrigation water that has fueled the region's development, as well as enabling barges and ships to make their way down the rivers to ports. But they kill salmon in prodigious numbers when baby fish are sucked into the turbine blades, and protecting the fish will almost certainly require allowing more water to flow down the rivers, reducing water available for power generation, irrigation and navigation.    Utility operators, farmers and shippers predict economic calamity, as electricity rates rise, crop production decreases and barge traffic dries up. Fish advocates say that they're exaggerating and that the economic impact will be manageable. The battle seems headed inexorably for the courts and Congress. Many involved predict the salmon fight will eventually become more bitter than the four-year fight over the spotted owl, which has altered U.S. timber policy in the Northwest.    "This is a unique preservation battle, and I fear it will be nasty," said Ed Chaney, president of the Northwest Resource Information Center, an environmental and resource protection group based in Eagle, Idaho.    Many observers believe the salmon battle could be used by opponents of the Endangered Species Act to promote revisions in the law and the way it is applied. In the wake of the owl battle, some Northwestern congressmen, including Sen. Slade Gorton, (R., Wash.), and Sen. Robert Packwood (R., Ore.) have expressed concern about the economic impact of the act. Environmentalists fear the salmon dispute may be used in an effort to gut the law.    No one disputes that Northwestern salmon are in trouble. Salmon runs that totaled some 11 million fish in the early part of the century are down to about two million now. The parties to the salmon dispute convened a series of "salmon summits" this year in hopes of forging some consensus or compromise. But the meetings produced no agreement and subsequent talks haven't either.    Fish supporters now say they're being excluded from negotiations taking place between federal agencies and industry groups. The Sierra Club Legal Defense Fund, which had tremendous court success on the owl issue, has threatened to sue the National Marine Fisheries Service if it doesn't grant the Snake River sockeye special emergency protection.    Under Commerce Department procedures, it usually takes a year between proposing a species for protection and formally granting it protection. The legal defense fund lawyers say the sockeye may be extinct before that period is up.    The big obstacle to solving the salmon problem is the number of water users and interested parties, all of whom seem to have a plan that calls for everybody else to sacrifice a little more than they do. A plan put together by Idaho Gov. Cecil Andrus has the general support of the fish and environmental groups. It would require dam operators to allow more water to flow through the rivers for about two months each spring to aid baby salmon in their drive for the sea.    That plan is opposed by most of the other groups involved in the debate, including shippers and power producers. The Northwest Grain and Feed Association, a trade group of commercial grain producers that use the waterways to ship wheat and other crops to ports, says the plan would force producers to ship grain by rail or truck and cost millions of dollars. The association proposed that flows be augmented for only two weeks in the spring.    "We're saying we won't use our barges during that period," said Jonathan Schleuter, executive vice president of the association. "That's a considerable sacrifice for us. But if we don't see other parties making commensurate contributions, forget it, we're not going to do it."  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Proposal to add more Northwestern salmon species to the endangered species list.
setting: NA

Title: U.S. Should Help the Muses, Revise Idiotic Ivory Policy
Text: Regarding the letters of Dec. 8 on the import of animal trophies: It is paradoxical that the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service would consider lifting a ban on trophy hunting while at the same time keeping an import ban on antiques made with African elephant ivory, even those which are over 100 years old. Although the Endangered Species Act allows antique ivory items to be traded in interstate commerce in all 50 states, objects of historical, artistic or cultural importance are prohibited from entering the U.S. since President Obama's executive order was signed in 2014. As a member of the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species, the U.S. ought to first consider lifting a ban on ivory antiques, which has no nexus with the poaching crisis. Scott Defrin New York
event_description: Discussion on lifting the import ban on antique ivory.
setting: discussion on lifting the import ban on antique ivory
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; Scott Defrin [Hero]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is considering lifting a ban on trophy hunting while maintaining an import ban on antique ivory, which is seen as contradictory. Scott Defrin argues that the ban on ivory antiques should be lifted as it does not relate to the poaching crisis.
moral: The problem is the inconsistency in policy regarding trophy hunting and antique ivory imports; the text suggests that the U.S. should lift the ban on ivory antiques to align with its commitments and address the issue more effectively.

Title: Bush Gets Cautious About Killing Clinton Rules
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Bush administration, in a sign that it has begun to tread carefully on environmental and energy issues, has delayed acting on Clinton regulations on reporting lead emissions and raising efficiency standards for air conditioners.    Decisions on the two rules were widely expected this week, the latest in a series of reviews being made on environmental and energy policy decisions made before Mr. Clinton left office -- decisions that conservatives and business interests hope will be reversed.    "I think it's the fear factor," said Mohammad Akhter, director of the American Public Health Association, of the delays. "After they came up with the ruling on arsenic [levels in water] and got beaten up, they're rethinking things." The group has been pressing for wider reporting of the environmental releases of lead, which is easily ingested by the human body and can cause subnormal intelligence and violent behavior in children.    The White House has been criticized -- and Mr. Bush has been pounded in public-opinion polls -- for blocking several Clinton environmental and energy regulations that administration officials say need further study or are based on insufficient science.    Spokesmen for Environmental Protection Agency and Energy Department said they couldn't comment on the politics surrounding the lead and air-conditioner rules, but noted that reviews of both are still under way, and that no decisions have been made.    The lead rule would require companies that use more than 100 pounds of lead a year to make an annual report of their wastes and emissions. Current reporting regulations apply only to companies that use at least 10,000 pounds of lead.    Some 67 trade groups representing chemical, metal and other manufacturers have sued to block the EPA from expanding the reporting requirements. They charge that the rules wrongly apply standards for dangerous organic chemicals to metals. "If this rule is approved, you will have a methodology in place that is being misused," said Jane Luxton, a lawyer who represents the so-called Ad Hoc Metals Coalition.    Mr. Akhter and others, however, say the rules are needed. Although the level of lead in the environment has been declining, new studies show that it is more dangerous at lower levels than previously believed, Mr. Akhter said.    Meanwhile, the Energy Department announced it was proceeding with some new, higher energy-efficiency standards that the Clinton administration proposed for many household appliances, but didn't include the rules on air conditioners.    The regulations that were approved require washing machines to be 22% more efficient by 2004 and 35% more efficient by 2007. By 2004, hot-water-heater manufacturers must increase efficiency for gas units by 8% and electric units by 4%.    Rumors flew earlier this week that the White House was about to heed industry complaints and lower the proposed air-conditioner standards, which would have required manufacturers to improve energy efficiency by 30%. Then Houston-based Goodman Manufacturing Co., the nation's second-largest air-conditioner maker, announced that it would have no trouble meeting that level, undermining the criticism of other manufacturers.    Steven Nadel, head of the American Council for an Energy Efficient Economy, estimated the standards would save the equivalent energy of about 170 small-to-medium power plants. "We're talking billions of dollars here," he said.    But Edward W. Dooley, spokesman for the Air Conditioning and Refrigeration Institute, said the Energy Department overestimated electricity savings. The rules will make new air conditioners larger, harder to install and more expensive, he said, and "people will live with their existing units until they die."    The Bush administration took another hit on Wednesday when the Sierra Club claimed that a proposal in the Interior Department 2002 budget would gut the Endangered Species Act. The provision would eliminate funds for protecting new species deemed endangered by court actions. The Interior Department said it wants to use some of the money to figure out how to prioritize a backlog of some 200 plants and animals claimed to be endangered.    ---                       Pending Business    In January the Bush administration suspended many Clinton environmental policies and regulations. More than 20 initiatives remain on hold, including:    Interior Department   -- Regulations limiting snowmobiles in Grand Teton and Yellowstone National Parks   -- Regulations simplifying Native American land ownership.   -- Policy on maintaining diversity and environmental health of National Wildlife Refuge System    Energy Department   -- Efficiency standards for residential and central air conditioners    Environmental Protection Agency   -- Regulations requiring more companies to report lead emissions.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Proposal to eliminate funds for protecting new species deemed endangered.
setting: Washington, D.C. political context surrounding environmental and energy policy decisions
characters: Bush administration [Villain]; Mohammad Akhter [Hero]; children affected by lead exposure [Victim]; Sierra Club [Hero]; Interior Department [Villain]; trade groups representing manufacturers [Villain]
plot: The Bush administration delays action on Clinton-era environmental regulations, which leads to criticism from health advocates and environmental groups. The Sierra Club claims that proposed budget cuts will harm endangered species protections, while trade groups oppose expanded lead reporting requirements.
moral: The text suggests that the Bush administration's delays and proposed funding cuts threaten public health and environmental protections, advocating for the continuation and strengthening of regulations to protect vulnerable populations and species.

Title: Roberts Opposition Struggles for Focus; Liberal Groups' Message Gets Muddled as White House Allies Launch TV Ad Campaign
Text: Washington -- THE SUPREME COURT nomination of Judge John Roberts, a likable man with a miniature public record on the bench, risks diluting the strength of a liberal coalition that has been organizing for more than a year for this battle. With momentum building strongly in the judge's favor on Capitol Hill yesterday, the potential opposition was forced to scramble. The National Organization for Women and NARAL Pro-Choice America were seeking to mount letter-writing drives and protests; the Sierra Club told activists it was researching his record. People for the American Way simply urged its 750,000 members to ask senators to reserve judgment until Senate Judiciary Committee hearings in September. "It's a very clever, very smart nomination," said Kate Michelman, president emeritus of NARAL Pro-Choice America. And that has already had an impact, muddling the left's message as it searches for a promising line of attack. White House allies have been able to use their substantial treasuries to introduce the 50-year-old, District of Columbia appeals- court judge to the general public in a positive way. Progress for America, which has pledged to spend nearly $20 million to support Judge Roberts, launched a one-week, $1 million cable television advertising campaign with a biographical theme that was titled: "Brilliant." On Capitol Hill, the Roberts charm offensive has been so effective that the so-called Gang of 14, the bipartisan bloc that ended a showdown over appellate judges, emerged from its first meeting predicting that a filibuster is unlikely. Indeed, momentum appeared to be moving so quickly in Judge Roberts's favor that one veteran Republican urged his colleagues to slow down and take their responsibilities more seriously. "This is a very important confirmation process, not a coronation," cautioned Sen. John Warner of Virginia. Liberal groups could yet find their voices and conduct a formidable opposition campaign. MoveOn.org, an online activists organization, already is building grass-roots opposition. "He is a stealth candidate without a substantial public record," said Wade Henderson, executive director of the Leadership Conference on Civil Rights. "He is strongly embraced by the ultraright, which suggests they know something about his views on important issues that is not apparent to the public at large." A team of attorneys representing civil rights, women, environmentalists and other groups is searching for ammunition. Senate Democratic staffers are also scrubbing his record. Judge Roberts's public file is thin. He didn't write many opinions in his two years on the bench. His most controversial decision was joining a majority opinion allowing Guantanamo prisoners to be prosecuted before military commissions without the constitutional protections that defendants in U.S. courts receive. But two other periods of his government service have emerged as potentially target-rich environments: His service in the Reagan administration from 1981 to 1986, during which the administration sought changes in the Voting Rights Act, and his role in the first Bush administration from 1989 to 1993, when the government joined in abortion cases. In one of those abortion cases, Judge Roberts argued in a brief, "we continue to believe that Roe was wrongly decided and should be overruled," because the court's finding of a fundamental right to an abortion has "no support in the text, structure, or history of the Constitution." Judge Roberts tried to play down the importance of that brief during his confirmation hearings for the appellate court two years ago. He suggested then that he was simply serving as a lawyer representing his client, in that case President George H. W. Bush. In any case, as an appellate-court judge, Judge Roberts said he would be bound by Supreme Court precedents, including the Roe decision. But Democrats and legal veterans of previous administrations are now skeptical of that response, particularly since Judge Roberts at the time was a high-ranking, political appointee. As a Supreme Court justice, he would no longer be bound by the court's previous rulings. "It's the answer you expect a lawyer to give," said Robert Reinstein, dean of Temple University Law School and a former Justice Department attorney in the Carter administration. "He was a political appointee. Presumably, he was working at high levels in the Justice Department for an administration in which he thought there would not be a lot of conflict between his own views of what the law should be and the positions the government was taking." Senate Democrats and their allies have signaled that they intend to request documents from the Justice Department that could shed light on Judge Roberts's role and advice on both the abortion cases and the civil-rights actions. "We want the relevant documents," said Ralph Neas, president of People for the American Way. But the Bush administration has a history of resisting such requests and likely would do so again, which could cause some Democrats to reconsider their early inclination to support Judge Roberts. An Associated Press poll released yesterday found that 52% of Americans -- and a solid majority of women -- think Judge Roberts should explain his views on abortion before he is confirmed. Such findings embolden liberal women's groups to plow ahead, expecting that allies will follow later. Nearly alone among liberal activists, they had material to rally supporters as soon as Mr. Bush announced his choice. In the two-hour period after the president unveiled Judge Roberts as his nominee, NARAL had 500 supporters sign up to become activists and organize against him, said President Nancy Keenan. Their appeals gained added urgency since the Supreme Court agreed in the next term to rule on a broadly written New Hampshire law requiring minors to receive their parents' permission before ending pregnancies. "I think whether or not he's affable doesn't have anything to with what his views will be as a member of the Supreme Court," said Ellen Malcolm, founder of Emily's List, an organization that helps elect pro-abortion-rights Democratic women. "Even though everybody in Washington refers to him as a nice person, that doesn't get to the bottom line, which is: Where does he stand on the issues? ---                        Seeking Ammunition   Bush allies have seized the early high ground in John Roberts's SupremeCourt nomination with this ad from Progress for America . . . But liberalgroups are considering several potential lines of attack.  -- Abortion: Stance against Roe v. Wade decision as Republicanadministration lawyer   -- Environment: Dissent as appeals-court judge to an application ofEndangered Species Act.   -- Civil Rights: Role in Reagan administration policy as associate WhiteHouse counsel.   -- Disclosure: Press the White House to turn over legal memos JudgeRoberts wrote in Justice Department.
event_description: Dissent as appeals-court judge to an application of Endangered Species Act.
setting: Capitol Hill during Supreme Court nomination hearings
characters: Judge John Roberts [Villain]; liberal groups (NARAL, National Organization for Women, Sierra Club, etc.) [Hero]; women and civil rights activists [Victim]
plot: Judge John Roberts is nominated to the Supreme Court, prompting liberal groups to mobilize against him due to concerns over his past positions on abortion and civil rights, while his supporters launch a campaign to present him positively.
moral: The nomination of Judge Roberts poses a significant threat to women's rights and civil rights, necessitating a vigorous opposition campaign from liberal groups to ensure transparency and accountability regarding his judicial philosophy.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial): Oil's Troubles
Text: Now the Mother Hubbards in the White House propose to comfort the domestic oil industry, which is in the throes of another price collapse. But the cushion isn't much: a pledge to study various technological, regulatory and export dispensations. Department of Energy studies of help for the oil industry have gathered dust for years. Congress is who disposes, and it has not been inclined to relax the grip on energy producers. Apart from President Reagan's price deregulation in the 1980s, in fact, the trend has been just the opposite. And state lawmakers have piled on for good measure. Add these burdens to what is already a comparative disadvantage for domestic drillers vis a vis most of the big international producers, and you have a recipe for contraction. When prices started their steep descent in the early '80s, employment in the extraction and refining stages of the business began a decline that has halved the work force from its peak of 920,000. That's as bad as any of the more widely publicized Rust Belt downsizings. Ours is not a brief for protection of American oil. Though valid cautions are raised about the security of major world suppliers -- indeed, we fought a war to defend them not three years ago -- we find the market (OPEC and all) has served consumers pretty well since the U.S. shucked the interventionary nonsense of the 1970s. But the home-grown industry that remains is not for wasting. That's been the effect, however, of too many misdirected public policies. North Slope oil can't be exported from Alaska, so it is dumped on the California market, driving crude prices there to $8 a barrel last week. Energy Secretary Hazel O'Leary says consideration of ending this trade prohibition will be part of her "studies." How about suspension of the Jones Act, which requires that Alaskan shipments be under American flag and thereby increases their transport costs and cuts the return at the barrel head by a like amount? Don't count on opening up the Alaskan National Wildlife Refuge and the rest of the vast "wetlands" up there. The Interior Department is seeking to rout energy production from public lands generally. The Endangered Species Act haunts California's central valley, which yields oil as well as crops. And good luck anywhere with the hazardous waste we used to call drilling mud. New offshore oil? Maybe in the Gulf of Mexico, but certainly not along the Pacific Coast. Chevron has had a hard enough time getting approval of a means for delivering the fruits of its existing Point Arguello strike off Santa Barbara, after $2 billion in sunk costs. At the refining point, oil companies in the U.S. confront a welter of federal and state environmental rules made all the more vexing by the Clean Air Act amendments of 1990. In a state like California, the obligation to reformulate gasoline is confounded by other air-quality regs that hamper the retrofitting of refineries. Then there are the ethanol and other "alternative fuels" campaigns that bedevil the domestic market with subsidies. Ironically, the Energy Department on the one hand had been saying sensibly that it would work to eradicate this favoritism at the state level. Then along came heavier hands at the White House with the news this week that EPA will require 30% of reformulated gasoline to come from "renewable sources," meaning ethanol. It is less likely the states will be asked to give up powers most of them have to force oil companies to set aside a fraction of production for politically determined distribution in time of "need." And finally, who can forget that the administration that now wants to intercede -- make that study interceding -- on behalf of oil producers is the same one that proposed a stiff BTU tax last spring (and eventually settled for a hike in the gasoline levy)? Just because your industry is on the intensive care list today doesn't mean it won't be accused of crimes against humanity tomorrow if the administration needs a corporate punching bag. No doubt, technology can help in the location and raising of oil, as well as in improving the efficiency (and thus the cleanliness) with which it is burned. If perchance the Clinton team has some market-friendly ideas for encouraging that, great. But to the degree U.S. oil has declined more than the market would demand, the reasons are mainly legalistic. Any "studied" response by this administration ought to reflect that reality.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial): Enraging Species Act
Text: The Third Amendment to the Bill of Rights states that no soldier can "be quartered in any home, without the consent of the owner." Somehow, though, it apparently never occurred to the Founding Fathers that we might someday need an amendment against the arbitrary "quartering" of endangered species on private land. Good thing the Founders didn't live to see the day, ours, when property owners all over America would be told to idle their land and effectively use it only as a wildlife refuge. Ambitious government "ecosystem management" plans are locking up millions of acres of private land -- without compensation. Take the spotted owl, which has effectively halted most timber production in the Pacific Northwest. The Anderson & Middleton Logging Co. has been enjoined from harvesting any timber on 72 acres of its land in Washington state. No spotted owls live on its land, but two have been seen nesting on government land 1.6 miles away. Residential owners are also affected: Marj and Roger Krueger spent $53,000 on a lot for their dream house in the Texas Hill Country. But they and other owners have been barred from building because the golden-cheeked warbler has been found in "the canyons adjacent" to their land. The government justifies these restrictions by noting that the Endangered Species Act bars actions that "harm" critters protected under it. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service interprets that to include any action that results in "significant habitat modification" that could affect the "breeding, feeding or sheltering" patterns of a species. It has issued countless edicts barring even such ordinary uses of property as clearing brush or harvesting trees because they might "modify" nearby habitat. Even some environmentalists have questioned if Congress intended such restrictions. Michael Bean, chairman of the Environmental Defense Fund's Wildlife Program, has noted "there are forceful arguments that such a broad interpration" is "improper." Last month, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia agreed. It invalidated the U.S. Fish and Wildlife regulation that prevented habitat modification on private land. The court ruled that the regulation "was neither clearly authorized by Congress nor a `reasonable interpretation' of the statute." The court's decision enraged radical environmentalists. They support a provision to the Marine Mammal Protection Act now before Congress that would explicitly allow the regulation of private land use and would bolster the Clinton Administration in its attempt, which it announced yesterday, to overturn the D.C. Court of Appeals decision. The Senate refused to include such language in its version of the bill, but it may wind up adding it in conference if the House insists. Today, Rep. Gerry Studds will demand a House vote to insist the regulatory language be retained. Environmental groups, agitated by recent property rights victories in Congress, are determined to prevail this time. The dispute over endangered species isn't over whether or not society should protect them. It's between a policy that refuses to set priorities and insists on preservation no matter what the costs to the human species or, alternatively, a more balanced approach. We are hard put to see how the species act can itself survive politically operating as an environmentalist land grab of other people's property. The seriousness of the claims for these various species might be better tested if the government had to compensate landowners for their losses. That approach isn't just our idea; it is the "takings" clause of the Fifth Amendment to the Bill of Rights. (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: Tucker Act Enforces Landowners' Rights" -- WSJ May 6, 1994) (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: If Ecosystem Is Harmed, We're All Endangered" -- WSJ May 12, 1994) 940419-0041 YY94 MM05
event_description: The U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia invalidated a U.S. Fish and Wildlife regulation that prevented habitat modification on private land.
setting: federal court case
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; property owners [Victim]; U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia [Hero]; radical environmentalists [Villain]; Rep. Gerry Studds [Hero]
plot: The U.S. Court of Appeals invalidated a regulation by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service that restricted habitat modification on private land, which had negatively impacted property owners. This decision angered radical environmentalists who support further regulation of private land use, while Rep. Gerry Studds is pushing for a House vote to retain such regulatory language.
moral: The text suggests that the current approach to endangered species protection is overly burdensome on property owners and advocates for a more balanced policy that compensates landowners for losses, aligning with the 'takings' clause of the Fifth Amendment.

Title: REAL ESTATE
Text: Lumber Slump Undercuts Complaints by Builders LUMBER prices, which soared to records in March, have retreated to late-1992 levels, taking ammunition from the housing and timber industries in their war with environmentalists over logging restrictions in the Northwest. The cost of framing lumber, based on a composite of different sizes, more than doubled to $506 per 1,000 board feet March 11 from $249 in October, according to Random Lengths, a Eugene, Ore., timber publication. That prompted the National Association of Home Builders to run ads in national newspapers blaming higher house prices on the "Endangered Species Act, other environmental regulations, political gridlock over timber-management laws and an endless stream of litigation." But environmental restrictions were just one of many factors that caused prices to jump, according to David Evans, associate editor of Random Lengths, and other lumber specialists. Heavy snow in the Northwest and rain in the Southeast reduced output, while demand was stimulated by forecasts of a housing market recovery. A buying frenzy ensued, artificially inflating prices. By May, however, the price had plunged back to $302 per 1,000 board feet. Last week it was at $316. "The home builders and timber industry tried to pull a fast one on the American public, blaming lumber price increases on environmental laws," says Kevin Kirchner, a lawyer at the Sierra Club Legal Defense Fund. "They hid the truth about the real causes." David Seiders, chief economist for the home builders group, concedes that many factors contributed to rising prices. But he still singles out environmental restrictions, saying they are creating a "long-term collision between supply and demand" that will push prices higher. Says Mr. Evans of Random Lengths: "Whenever the prices move rapidly, they tend to become a political football." Sweden Plays Big Part In European Slump THE RECESSION in commercial real estate has rippled through Western Europe, hitting London, Frankfurt, Madrid and Paris in succession. But Sweden has been hit the hardest. Many commercial properties in Stockholm have lost half their market value. Moreover, Swedish institutional investors made huge investments elsewhere in Europe, adding to overbuilding that left investors in those markets with big losses. "Sweden is seen as the most unfortunate country in terms of timing," says Mark Benson, director of international markets for Chesterton International, a real-estate advisory firm in London. "They went in to real estate so strongly at the peak of the market." The problem began with massive commercial development in Sweden during the 1980s. For most of the decade, Swedish banks, insurance companies and pension funds were prohibited from investing in foreign real estate. So, dozens of buildings went up at home. Kjell Carlsson, a real-estate consultant in Stockholm, says the boom "gave us about 30% too much capacity." In the late '80s, Swedish banks, insurance companies and pension funds were freed to lend offshore. Swedish money flooded into London, Amsterdam and other cities just as those markets were peaking. In Brussels, Swedish investors bought or built about 10% of the city's current office stock. Then Sweden's economy hit the skids. Office vacancy rates jumped to nearly 12% today from about 2% in 1990, and rents fell more than 30%. The foreign markets crashed, too. Swedish institutions are now sitting on as much bad real-estate debt as their far larger counterparts in England, says Mr. Benson. The Swedish government has taken over two of the nation's five largest banks and is working on a Swedish version of the Resolution Trust Corp. Apartment Vacancies Take Unexpected Turn AN INCREASE in apartment vacancy rates in the U.S. has taken the industry by surprise. The vacancy rate in buildings with five or more apartments jumped to 10.7% in the first quarter from 9.7% in last year's fourth quarter. The West's vacancy rate jumped two percentage points, the Northeast's 1.3 points, the Census Bureau says. The apartment market has been considered the healthiest sector of commercial real estate, because households have been forming far faster than apartments have been built in recent years. The vacancy rate had fallen to 9.7% in the fourth quarter from 10.7% in the 1991 third quarter. "The trend was definitely downward," says Jonathan Kempner, president of the National Multi Housing Council, a trade group in Washington. "I was quite surprised that it rose that much." Industry experts say low mortgage rates are drawing many apartment dwellers to homeownership. And economic weakness in California and the Northeast is causing "discretionary" renters to double up or move back in with their parents. Another factor, says Todd Zimmerman, a housing analyst in Clinton, N.J., is a "mismatch" between the apartment stock and renters' needs. With more baby boomers having families, he says, there are too many studio and one-bedroom apartments and not enough two- and three-bedroom units. This reinforces the move toward ownership, he says. But many apartment owners say vacancy rates will continue their long-term decline, particularly in higher-quality units, which tend to be bigger and more suited to families. Odds and Ends JAPANESE developer Minoru Isutani's sale of the Pebble Beach golf resort for $500 million in March 1992 was more than five times the size of last year's next largest hotel sale, the Waikiki Beachcomber, according to Hospitality Valuation Services. . . . Corcoran Group, a New York brokerage firm, has opened what it bills as the first "residential real-estate store." Apartment hunters can browse for property on their own, using video monitors and a store computer that carries more than 6,000 listings.
event_description: Discussion of the impact of environmental restrictions on lumber prices.
setting: discussion of the impact of environmental restrictions on lumber prices
characters: National Association of Home Builders [Villain]; environmentalists [Hero]; American public [Victim]
plot: The National Association of Home Builders blames rising lumber prices on environmental restrictions, but experts argue that multiple factors contributed to the price increase, misleading the American public.
moral: The problem is the misleading narrative by the home builders blaming environmental laws for price increases; the text suggests that the truth about the real causes should be acknowledged.

Title: U.S. News: Polar Bear Is Declared a Threatened Species
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Bush administration declared the polar bear a threatened species, acknowledging that the loss of sea ice caused by global warming was jeopardizing the animal's future. The decision, after months of delay and legal challenges, was greeted by environmental groups as a watershed moment, and sharply criticized by conservatives who fear its ramifications for the U.S economy. Opponents of listing the bear as threatened have long questioned the science behind such a move and predicted that it will lead to new restrictions on drilling for oil and gas in Alaska, as well as limits on a range of activities in the rest of the U.S. that contribute to the emission of global-warming gases. In announcing the decision Wednesday, Interior Secretary Dirk Kempthorne said he was compelled to act both by the requirements of the federal Endangered Special Act and scientific evidence showing the bear's habitat is melting. Although the global population of polar bears has grown from a low of about 12,000 in the late 1960s to approximately 25,000 today, Mr. Kempthorne said government scientists had advised him that computer modeling projects "a significant population decline" by the year 2050. "This decision may not be a popular decision, but I believe it is the right decision," Mr. Kempthorne said. In announcing the decision, the Bush administration took steps to limit its impact on drilling activities in Alaska, a move that was criticized by environmental groups. Mr. Kempthorne said the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service would promulgate a rule stating that if an activity is permissible under the Marine Mammal Protection Act -- another federal law covering the bear and with which oil companies in Alaska must already comply -- it is also permissible under the Endangered Species Act. Interior officials also said the listing would lead to few new additional regulations on oil and gas drilling in Alaska. "The administration's decision is riddled with loopholes, caveats, and backhanded language that could actually undermine protections for the polar bear and other species," Carl Pope, executive director of the Sierra Club, said in a written statement. It is unclear how the Interior Department's move will affect the broader debate in Washington over how to fight climate change. Mr. Kempthorne said the administration would instruct Fish and Wildlife staff that "the best scientific data available today cannot make a causal connection between harm to" the bear "and greenhouse-gas emissions from a specific facility, or resource development project, or government action." But some legal experts said the decision could still lead to the government applying the Endangered Species Act to a range of business activities in the lower contiguous 48 states that contribute to greenhouse-gas emissions. "It may have a significant impact across myriad industries," said Roger Martella, a former general counsel of the Environmental Protection Agency. A spokeswoman for the American Petroleum Institute, which represents the oil-and-gas industry, said the organization was still reviewing the administration's announcement, but it commended Mr. Kempthorne for stating that the Endangered Species Act shouldn't be "abused to make global-warming policies." In other quarters, reaction to the administration's decision was more critical. Some conservatives accused Mr. Kempthorne of kowtowing to environmentalists and said he had relied on questionable projections about bear populations in midcentury. The debate over the bear's status is unfolding as Democrats and Republicans in Congress continue to quarrel over which party deserves more blame for high oil prices and the U.S.'s heavy reliance on foreign oil. Earlier this week, the Senate rejected a package of measures that called for opening the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge in Alaska and some offshore waters that are now off limits. "This is not sound science, it's a computer game," Rep. Don Young (R., Alaska) said of the administration's decision.
event_description: The Bush administration declared the polar bear a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act.

Title: Foresters Fight Feds Over Salamander
Text: Tree farmers in Florida, Georgia and South Carolina have got the shivers over the five-inch-long flatwoods salamander. Their fear: If the federal government protects the little-known, chocolate-brown amphibian under the Endangered Species Act, the farmers' livelihood could be more endangered than the salamander. Farmers argue that proposed curbs on their most common timbering practices -- which have destroyed the salamander's habitat or altered it so it can no longer prosper there -- will kill their business. The government's proposal "would essentially mean the end of forestry practices within those zones" where salamanders are thought to live, says Phil Gornicki, who handles environmental issues for the Tallahassee-based Florida Forestry Association, an industry group. This is no high-profile battle between well-heeled industry titans and TV-savvy environmentalists staging demonstrations on behalf of the likes of the spotted owl. But as both federal regulators and the region's big timber companies try to ease tensions with conciliatory statements, to some tree growers and environmentalists, the salamander has become a big deal. Consider a hearing in Tallahassee last month on a U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service proposal to list the salamander as a "threatened" species -- a status that is less severe than "endangered." "What you are proposing to do is going to completely destroy the present state of our lifestyle as we know it," Bob Roquemore, a timber manager on 6,000 acres in Lakeland, Ga., told officials. "It's this kind of mindless foolishness that makes people want to blow up federal buildings, send pipe bombs through the mail to judges, [and] be scared of what the government is going to do to them next week." But Bruce Means, president of the Coastal Plains Institute, a Tallahassee nonprofit environmental research group, says, "I don't know what they are crying about. It is patently untrue that this particular animal, when it gets listed, is going to affect very many people at all." The salamander was first discovered in Jasper County, S.C., in 1867. Females lay eggs for about two months of the year in ponds or wetlands found in open flatwood pine forests. For the rest of the year, the critters hide underground, sometimes borrowing crayfish dens. The Fish and Wildlife Service first took notice earlier this decade when biologists discovered that the creature's numbers had significantly declined in the Southeast. In 1992, the Biodiversity Legal Foundation, a Boulder, Colo., conservation group, filed a petition to list the salamander under the Endangered Species Act. The 25-year-old act, which has been used to protect nearly 1,150 plants and animals, makes it illegal to harass, harm or kill listed animals, collect protected plants or conduct land-use activities that could result in death or harm to the species. When the federal agency denied Biodiversity's request, the conservation group sued. In 1994, the Fish and Wildlife Service settled and agreed to accelerate a decision on whether to list the species, based on currently available information. Published information indicated that about 88% of the salamander's habitat has disappeared, replaced by agricultural operations, urban development and the activities of the timber industry, which converted naturally growing forests to dense, intensively cultivated tree plantations and began using herbicides to control unwanted growth. By 1997, studies showed, only 47 clumps of salamanders -- or evidence of their existence -- could be found in Florida, Georgia and South Carolina. So last December, the Fish and Wildlife Service proposed that the forestry industry curtail practices such as dense planting, clear-cut harvesting and intensive use of herbicides within a one-mile radius buffer zone around breeding ponds. And that sparked the ruckus among small timber farmers. "Why should I personally have to finance [the protection of the species] because I'm one of the unlucky few" with land that harbors the species, asks Joe Hopkins, president of family-owned Toledo Manufacturing Co., which grows pine trees on about 46,000 acres in Folkston, Ga. The federal agency's recommendations, landowners say, could be devastating to small farmers. Take, for example, a farmer who may have planted trees to finance his children's college tuition, says Chris Barneycastle, executive director of the Georgia Forestry Association, Norcross, Ga. If evidence of a salamander is found in the area, the farmer might be "unable to harvest any of the timber." But Sidney Maddock, a Buxton, N.C., environmental analyst who works for the Biodiversity Legal Foundation, argues that the forecasts of "economic doom and horrible impacts . . . are made up." Moreover, he says, the federal agency goes out of its way to accommodate industry concerns with habitat-conservation plans, which allow land owners to offset potentially harmful land uses with other protective measures for listed species. According to the Fish and Wildlife Service, the agency has signed 225 such plans across the country and has 200 more in the pipeline. To Fish and Wildlife Service officials, the fervor of the salamander debate came as a surprise. They extended a deadline for comments from the end of February to June 1 and are working with timber-industry representatives to come up with changes to the proposed listing. One change could be a reduction in the size of the buffer zone, says Linda LaClaire, wildlife biologist at the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's field office in Jackson, Miss., which is administering the salamander listing. Ms. LaClaire adds that the agency isn't currently trying to bring back the salamander to areas that "have already been altered" by urban development or forestry practices. The bottom line, she says: "We can protect the salamander and people can do what they need to do to make money." Indeed, representatives from 10 of the largest timber companies with Southeastern land holdings say the salamander needs some protection. "We want to work with the [Fish and Wildlife] Service on developing guidelines that we feel would protect the species," says Jim Sweeney, chairman of the timber group, "but also give some management flexibility" for timber companies. Mr. Sweeney also is manager of wildlife issues at Stamford, Conn.-based Champion International Corp., which has 1.07 million acres of forest land in Florida, Georgia and South Carolina. In Florida, meanwhile, state officials are considering a compromise. Because nearly 80% of the critters in the state have been found on public lands like national wildlife refuges and military bases, Florida wants to sign an agreement between land managers on the public property and the Fish and Wildlife Service. It would impose mandatory protection measures on public lands but make the provisions voluntary for private landowners. But to Elwood Geiger, a tree farmer and consulting forester with 600 acres near Jacksonville, Fla., such compromises miss the point. The Fish and Wildlife Service, he says, "is making this salamander more important than the people who have worked a lifetime protecting their land." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
