Title: Private Property Is Itself Endangered
Text: Regarding Stephen Moore's "Using 'Sue and Settle' to Thwart Oil and Gas Drillers" (Cross Country, Oct. 5): This method of continual lawsuits alleging violations of the Endangered Species Act and National Environmental Policy Act against private developers and public land agencies has been extremely effective. It has destroyed rural economies, communities and schools, and especially the ecological health of our national forests and public lands. In 1993, when President Clinton brokered a comprehensive Northwest Forest Plan to save the Northern spotted owl and rural communities, these nonprofit organizations continued routinely to sue. Two decades later they're moving east, using the ESA to list more endangered species, and then new lawsuits which will restrict how you may use your land. Meanwhile, they recoup their legal fees using the federal Equal Access to Justice Act courtesy of taxpayers. Spotted owl populations have continued to decline, and there are no effective implementation plans to improve its habitat, just to actively kill their competition, the barred owl. Only a comprehensive review and reform of both the ESA, NEPA and new laws requiring healthy rural communities and our forests will allow effective solutions. The courtroom isn't the place. Christopher Brong Skamania County Commissioner, District 1 Stevenson, Wash.
event_description: Continual lawsuits alleging violations of the Endangered Species Act against private developers and public land agencies.
setting: public discourse surrounding the Endangered Species Act and National Environmental Policy Act
characters: nonprofit organizations [Villain]; rural economies, communities, and schools [Victim]; taxpayers [Victim]; spotted owl populations [Victim]; Christopher Brong [Hero]
plot: Nonprofit organizations sue private developers and public land agencies under the ESA and NEPA, leading to economic destruction and ecological harm, while taxpayers fund their legal fees. This results in declining spotted owl populations and ineffective habitat improvement plans.
moral: The problem is the detrimental impact of lawsuits on rural communities and ecological health; a comprehensive review and reform of the ESA and NEPA is necessary to find effective solutions outside the courtroom.

Title: Food: For Caviar Lovers, It's Time to Gulp; Beluga Sturgeon Is Labeled 'Threatened,' but Full Ban On Delicacy Seems Unlikely
Text: Beluga caviar, a delicacy that retails for as much as $135 an ounce, just got more exclusive. Beginning today, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is listing beluga sturgeon, the fish that spawns the expensive eggs, as a threatened species. There's no need to stockpile beluga just yet, though; the designation isn't triggering an immediate ban. Often, a species designated as threatened under the U.S. Endangered Species Act is immediately forbidden from import and export. However, there may be no change in the way beluga caviar is imported or sold in the U.S. The Fish and Wildlife Service says it is probably going to issue a special rule in the next two to four weeks that will propose measures for how to deal with the species' new "threatened" status. Possible measures range from making no change in how beluga caviar is regulated to an outright ban. Whatever is decided, any new rules won't take effect for six months. Wild beluga sturgeon, which once were abundant in Eastern Europe and Central Asia, today are found only in the Caspian and Black seas. After the Soviet Union disbanded in 1991, the once carefully controlled beluga fishery fell into disarray. More than 10 years of rampant poaching, overfishing and habitat loss have decimated populations. The Fish and Wildlife Service says that international law-enforcement agencies estimate the black market for Caspian Sea sturgeon is six to 10 times as great as the legal market. Ken Stansell, the Fish and Wildlife Service's assistant director for international affairs, indicated the most likely next regulatory step would be "something in between" the status quo and a full ban. Possible approaches include a partial ban that would restrict imports from countries that don't have good track records of policing inappropriate fishing activity. Sturgeon-fish farmers in the U.S. could also be excluded from a ban on beluga caviar. The agency says it is opting not to ban beluga caviar outright because the U.S. imports about 80% of the world's caviar and can therefore use its influence in the market to help manage the species. The beluga-caviar trade is governed by the Convention of International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora, a 165 nation treaty. The U.S. agency says it wants to make sure its policies on the fish dovetail with those of the international community. Caviar connoisseurs, tradesmen and conservationists are nervously waiting for the agency to clarify how the threatened status will play out. Caviar Emptor, a nonprofit organization dedicated to protecting the beluga sturgeon, says it wants the agency to implement a complete ban on beluga caviar in order to save the species. Some critics say the agency should have categorized beluga sturgeon as endangered, not  threatened. Endangered indicates  a species is "on its way to extinction," whereas threatened means the species "will become endangered in the near future unless some action is taken," says Mr. Stansell. The agency partially relies on fish-stock assessments from Caspian Sea countries, such as Kazakhstan and Azerbaijan, that are notoriously unreliable, and yet the only numbers available. Frank Chapman, a professor of aquatic science and specialist in sturgeon conservation at the University of Florida in Gainsville, says a total ban on caviar would halt his efforts to protect wild-sturgeon populations by promoting the fish-farming industry. Last year, one company, Sturgeon AquaFarms in Miami, began raising beluga sturgeon on a fish farm in Florida, with the hope of selling beluga caviar that tastes like the imported variety in six or seven years. An outright ban on beluga caviar would put such companies out of business, says Mr. Chapman. Alternatively, if the agency decides to ban imports but allow farm-raised beluga, "it would mean wonderful news for the aquaculture industry in Florida," says Mr. Chapman.
event_description: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is listing beluga sturgeon as a threatened species.
setting: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's regulatory process regarding endangered species
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; beluga sturgeon [Victim]; Caviar Emptor [Hero]; Frank Chapman [Hero]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service lists beluga sturgeon as a threatened species, which may lead to regulatory changes regarding beluga caviar. The agency is considering various measures, including a potential ban, while stakeholders express concerns about the implications for conservation and the caviar market.
moral: The problem is the declining population of beluga sturgeon due to poaching and habitat loss, and the preferred solution is to implement a complete ban on beluga caviar to protect the species.

Title: World-Wide
Text: CLINTON NOMINATED George Tenet, acting CIA director, to head the agency. The nominee is well-known on Capitol Hill, where he once worked, and isn't expected to face the bitter confrontation with Senate Republicans that sank Anthony Lake's nomination. Lake's nemesis, Sen. Shelby, has already signaled support. Meanwhile, the Senate voted 55-44 for a nonbinding resolution calling for an independent counsel to investigate White House fund-raising abuses. The president moved quickly on the nomination before meeting Yeltsin today. Russia said NATO expansion would be the biggest mistake since the Cold War. --- House Republicans tried to quell a revolt by conservatives over suggestions that tax cuts be delayed, while Clinton held budget talks with congressional leaders. The chairman of the Senate Budget panel said after the meeting that "serious" differences with the president remain. Further talks are set for next week. --- The Supreme Court heard arguments in a challenge to a new law attempting to restrict indecent material on the Internet. Separately, justices ruled property owners can sue under the Endangered Species Act to limit government conservation efforts. The ruling is a victory for business and may spur a litigation surge. --- U.N. health officials announced what they called a major breakthrough in tuberculosis treatment, a four-pill, six-month regimen officials predict will save 10 million lives world-wide over 10 years and prevent development of drug-resistant strains. The World Health Organization also warned of a surge in cases in former East Bloc nations. --- A GOP revamping of overtime laws faces a Clinton veto, the White House said as the House approved the measure 222-210. The bill would let employers offer compensatory time off instead of overtime pay. Clinton, who backed the idea in the campaign, now says more protections against employer abuses are needed. --- Palestinian leaders rejected a proposal by Israel's Netanyahu to conclude a final peace agreement in six months. Meanwhile, Israel's police chief said the start of work on a Jerusalem housing project has hurt security cooperation with the PLO and raised the likelihood of guerrilla attacks. Israel's top court refused to order the project halted. --- Mexican officials let a drug suspect make $168 million in transfers the U.S. wanted blocked weeks before Clinton certified Mexico a drug-war ally. Meanwhile, Senate backers of a move to overturn certification sought a deal with the White House, with a vote possible today. --- Cellular-phone researchers have found a flaw in encryption technology used in advanced digital models, casting doubt on industry claims that the new system ensures privacy. Researchers at Berkeley and a consulting firm found eavesdroppers using digital scanners could break the code. --- Italy declared a state of emergency amid a flood of refugees from Albania, with 12,000 arriving in the past week. Rome said it sent back 289 "undesirables," and other refugees can stay only three months. Meanwhile, police tried to reopen Tirana's airport. --- The Pentagon sent military specialists to Zaire to scout escape routes should an evacuation of Americans be ordered. President Mobutu left a Monaco hospital following cancer surgery and aides said he plans to go home to make a stand against rebels. --- Possible Oklahoma bombing jurors met with the judge and attorneys and filled out questionnaires at a fairgrounds near Denver. Out of the assembled 500, 12 jurors and six alternates will be chosen for the trial of Timothy McVeigh, set to start March 31. --- Taiwan resumed missile tests in the Taiwan Strait for the first time since its confrontation with China last March. The launching of the two U.S. Hawk missiles delayed some local commercial flights. --- An Afghan arms dump exploded near the eastern city of Jalalabad, killing at least 16 people and wounding 200 as debris rained on a residential area. Authorities were unsure if the blast was an accident or sabotage. --- Died: Willem de Kooning, 92, Dutch-born U.S. painter who dominated the Abstract Expressionist movement, in East Hampton, N.Y., after battling Alzheimer's disease.
event_description: The Supreme Court ruled that property owners can sue under the Endangered Species Act to limit government conservation efforts.
setting: federal court case
characters: property owners [Hero]; government conservation efforts [Villain]
plot: The Supreme Court ruled that property owners can sue under the Endangered Species Act to limit government conservation efforts, which is seen as a victory for business.
moral: The ruling highlights the need to prioritize property owners' rights over government conservation efforts, suggesting that further legal actions may be necessary to protect these rights.

Title: Government Dries Up California's Water Supply
Text: Many parts of the country, notably California and Texas, are experiencing intense drought. California rainfall in the Northern Sierra this year was 28.8 inches, 60% the normal amount. Most of Texas is experiencing moderate to exceptional drought conditions. Voluntary or mandatory cutbacks in residential water usage have become common. Yet weather isn't the only problem: Government-dictated prices, coupled with restrictions on the transfer of water, have made a bad situation much worse. Shortages are generally caused when governments place ceilings on prices or when they prevent markets from operating freely in some other way, like restricting trade. Gasoline is a case in point. Thanks to the 1970s oil shocks, gas became less abundant and prices rose. The U.S. government's response was to put ceilings on gasoline prices, which caused shortages and long lines at gas stations. The current water situation resembles oil in the mid-1970s. Prices are determined in large part by state and federal government dictates rather than by the market. When drought hits, the price to some users, most notably agriculture, is too low to clear the market and shortages result. Ironically, in addition to eliminating shortages, allowing the market to work would result in prices that are lower than those currently paid by most residential users. Markets would encourage farmers to sell water to urban users, thereby increasing residential supply and driving residential water prices down, as the University of California's Howard Chong and David Sunding showed in a 2006 study published by the Annual Review of Environment and Resources. The price farmers pay for water differs from that paid by urban users, sometimes dramatically. For example, San Francisco Bay Area residents obtain much of their water from the Hetch Hetchy reservoir. The pipes from Hetch Hetchy to the Bay Area intersect the California Aqueduct, which supplies water both to agriculture and urban areas. Residential users of aqueduct water in San Diego pay rates that can be more than five times as high as those paid by the farmers in Kern County. So if some people, businesses or localities have rights to water and others would be willing to pay more for those rights, why not trade? Answer: government controls and lawsuits. In 2012, the Public Policy Institute of California reported on the morass of regulations that continue to restrict the exporting of local water to other communities. Permits are required, which necessitate environmental studies and a lengthy assessment procedure. Statewide water transfers require approval by the State Water Resources Control Board, which also requires proof of compliance with the California Environmental Quality Act and additional proof that the transfer will not injure another legal user. Finally, even approved transfers are subject to reversal by courts. Two months ago, the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled that the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation violated the Endangered Species Act when it failed to inform the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service of plans to renew 41 long-term contracts with California agricultural irrigation districts that could harm the delta smelt, a fish. To solve California's water problem, the first step is to let all owners of water sell their rights with minimal government limitations. This would ensure that water goes to its highest valued use. Second, federal and state agencies that redirect water to environmental use should pay the market price for it. Although there may be good reasons to ensure that some fish and wildlife be protected, we should not pretend that this protection is costless. Agencies that divert water for environmental purposes should be required to budget explicitly for the lost revenue associated with the decision to divert it for this purpose, rather than allowing it to be sold at the market price for urban or agricultural use. Third, farmers who now receive water at below-market prices should be compensated for having to pay higher prices by giving them the rights during a transition period to use or sell the water that they typically use. This would honor existing property rights and help ensure that agricultural lobbies and lawsuits do not block the transfer of water. Rather than praying for rain, we should get the government out of the water-allocation business. --- Mr. Lazear, former chairman of the President's Council of Economic Advisers (2006-09), is a professor at Stanford University's Graduate School of Business and a Hoover Institution fellow. (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: Would You Want Farmers' Water?" -- WSJ July 10, 2014) Credit: By Edward P. Lazear
event_description: The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled that the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation violated the Endangered Species Act.
setting: political context surrounding water allocation and environmental regulations in California
characters: U.S. Bureau of Reclamation [Villain]; delta smelt [Victim]; federal and state agencies [Villain]; farmers [Victim]; residential users [Victim]
plot: The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled that the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation violated the Endangered Species Act by failing to inform the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service about plans that could harm the delta smelt. This ruling highlights the negative impact of government controls on water allocation, leading to shortages and inequitable pricing for farmers and residential users.
moral: To address California's water crisis, the government should minimize restrictions on water rights trading, ensure agencies pay market prices for environmental water use, and compensate farmers for higher prices, thereby allowing water to be allocated to its highest valued use.

Title: Farmers Label Themselves Environmentally Friendly
Text: The Washington State Farm Bureau wants to beat environmentalists at their own game. The Olympia-based organization, which represents about 18,000 family farms in the state, is working on a "Farm Friendly" label that it hopes will soon be slapped on everything from apples to safety goggles. The idea: Shore up farmers' sagging fortunes by positioning them as environmentally conscious producers. "Agriculture and farmers have been painted in a negative light" on environmental issues, says Patrick Chestnut, the bureau's director of marketing for member services. "The reality is that farmers are the true stewards of the land." That may be so, but the bureau has hurdles to scale to get that message across with its green tag. Consumers are already assaulted in stores with dozens of stickers -- from the time-honored Good Housekeeping Seal of Approval to the more recent emblems being churned out by environmental organizations, including the Food Alliance Approved, Salmon Safe, Turtle Safe Certified and Eco-O.K.: Rainforest Alliance Approved. "In the past three to four years -- somewhere between mad-cow disease and E. coli and God knows what -- this stuff has really come on," says Mark Ritchie, president of the Institute for Agriculture and Trade Policy, a nonprofit research center in Minneapolis. What's more, there is no guarantee the labels will attract more consumers to a product. Research by the Portland-based Food Alliance, a nonprofit that certifies farms that meet its standards for environmentally and socially sound practices, shows that so far the effectiveness of green labeling is uncertain. "It's too early to say that any of the labels will bring a price premium," says Deborah Kane, the alliance's executive director. Still, she says, "Consumers are looking for a way to support environmental issues in the marketplace. To the extent that eco-labels make it easy for them to do that, they will choose to vote with their dollars." The Farm Bureau certainly hopes so. It plans to spend as much as $20,000 on the Farm Friendly program, designing and producing the labels, soliciting products and businesses to carry them and educating consumers. The bureau also is gauging the interest of businesses and merchants that may want to declare their goods or services friendly to farms and pay $70 annually for the privilege. The label lets consumers know these businesses recognize the importance of agriculture in the state and don't donate to organizations that are antagonistic toward farmers. Ultimately, the bureau will publish a directory of Farm Friendly businesses. About 25 -- including pesticide suppliers and office-supply stores -- have already signed up, according to Mr. Chestnut. The program won't be a moneymaker. But in the best-case scenario, it will encourage consumers to support the state's agriculture industry. The label on produce will alert shoppers to products that are grown in the state and meet local, state and federal environmental and safety standards, Mr. Chestnut says. "We want a way to communicate, as farmers, to the general public that, `Hey, this is a good product, we are good stewards of the land, we are meeting all these regulations for your safety, and we're working hard to do that.'" Indeed, Washington farmers need the good publicity. In recent years, they've taken a lot of heat from the environmental community -- for burning wheat stubble, fighting state efforts to limit the amount of waste from dairy cows that end up in streams and rivers and challenging enforcement of the U.S. Endangered Species Act. "Everyone paints farmers as wanting to rape the land, but we are the original environmentalists because we want to protect our soil," says Randy Suess, a wheat farmer in Colfax. "It's our livelihood. I think the label is a good way to get that idea across." Environmentalists don't begrudge the bureau for playing the label game. But they question its chances for success. "The Farm Bureau can make a powerful statement in the marketplace by promoting the Washington state origin of these products," says Dan Kent, who heads the Salmon Safe program at the Pacific Rivers Council in Eugene. "But any environmental claim must be backed by independent third-party certification to ensure that the label has meaning in the marketplace." The group introduced its Salmon Safe label 18 months ago. The label -- which shows products have been grown with techniques that keep rivers clean enough for salmon to thrive -- is now seen on about 200 products from 50 farms, including wine and rice. Mr. Chestnut acknowledges the bureau will have to take pains to educate people as to just what Farm Friendly means, and why they should care. Julie Ruth, assistant professor of marketing and international business at the University of Washington in Seattle, says that "in today's cluttered environment, if it's not clear upon first reading, you might not have an opportunity to educate and use it to your advantage." The Washington Farm Bureau is the first in the nation to take a stab at environmental labeling. Meantime, the American Farm Bureau is watching -- and wondering. "Personally, I think it will be confusing to pick up a can of tomatoes and see `Farm Friendly,'" says Scott Rawlins, a fruit and vegetable marketing specialist with the Park Ridge, Ill.-based American Farm Bureau. "What does that mean? It's hard to explain on a can." There are other problems. Mr. Suess, the wheat farmer, notes that if he were to stick the label on his bushels, the only people who would see it would be wholesale buyers, and maybe bakers. "We grow the wheat," he says, "and that gets lost in the bread." County bureaus are discussing whether to place labels on products made from wheat. And at bureau headquarters in Olympia, Mr. Chestnut and his team are still struggling to design a label that will make all members happy. Right now, it's rectangular, which is fine for, say, a carton of milk. But, "of course, the tomato guys don't want a rectangular label, {they want} a circular one," he says. "Our label right now will fit on bananas -- but we don't grow bananas." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Discussion of challenges faced by farmers regarding enforcement of the U.S. Endangered Species Act.
setting: discussion of challenges faced by farmers regarding enforcement of the U.S. Endangered Species Act
characters: Washington State Farm Bureau [Hero]; environmentalists [Villain]; farmers [Victim]
plot: The Washington State Farm Bureau is developing a 'Farm Friendly' label to improve farmers' public image and support their economic viability, while facing skepticism from environmentalists and challenges in consumer recognition.
moral: The problem is the negative perception of farmers as environmental offenders; the preferred solution is to promote the 'Farm Friendly' label to communicate farmers' commitment to environmental stewardship and gain consumer support.

Title: California Rejects Bid for Protection of Coastal Songbird, Stirring Debate
Text: California state officials' rejection of recommendations that they grant protection to a small songbird sets up another bitter court fight over the fate of a troubled species.    Late Thursday, the California Fish and Game Commission declined to grant the California coastal gnatcatcher protection under the Endangered Species Act.    The commission, which consists of four members appointed by the state's governor, rejected the recommendation of the California Fish and Game Department, which after a year of review of the gnatcatcher's condition had strongly recommended listing the bird as endangered in order to avert its possible extinction. The commission has rarely gone against state biologists' findings on a species in the past, but the panel, by a vote of 3 to 1, held that it wasn't convinced that the bird's condition is dire enough to warrant protections.    Environmentalists, led by the Natural Resources Defense Council, attacked the commission's decision and vowed to challenge it in court as early as this week.    Federal officials are also considering whether to grant the gnatcatcher protection under the federal Endangered Species Act. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's California field scientists have recommended that it be listed as endangered under the act. The federal agency's decision on the bird is due later this month.    The gnatcatcher dispute is shaping up as an important test case in the increasingly bitter clash over how far to go to protect wildlife. The gnatcatcher lives in the low scrub that dots the southern California coast; much of its habitat has been plowed under to make way for housing tracts in fast-growing counties such as Orange, Riverside and Los Angeles. State and federal scientists believe there are only about 2,000 pairs of gnatcatchers left in the U.S.    Thousands of acres of the remaining coastal scrub are owned by big developers, such as Irvine Cos., Baldwin Co. and Santa Margarita Co. The companies, which are among the biggest Republican campaign contributors in the nation, have spent millions of dollars on a lobbying blitz aimed at blocking any listing of the bird as endangered under either state or federal wildlife laws. The developers contend that they're already voluntarily setting aside enough gnatcatcher habitat to ensure the bird's survival, and that listing it as endangered would paralyze housing construction and road projects, costing billions of dollars and thousands of jobs.    The commission in early August had delayed its decision on the gnatcatcher, after developers produced a new study they maintained refuted scientific findings that the gnatcatcher was headed for extinction. But at Thursday's commission meeting in Long Beach, Calif., Pete Bonadelli, head of the state Fish and Game Department, reported that the new information in some ways only strengthened his agency's belief that the gnatcatcher is imperiled and should be listed under the state's endangered species statute. As is often the case with California environmental laws, the state's endangered species statutes are in some respects tougher than federal laws.    The commission still voted against listing the gnatcatcher. One reason may have been a last-minute appeal from Michael Mantell, deputy director of the state's Department of Resources, which is trying to persuade developers to cooperate voluntarily with its own plan for preserving coastal habitat. Many environmentalists support the Department of Resources plan, but they contend the plan is many months, if not years, away from bearing fruit and say the gnatcatcher can't wait that long.    Environmentalists accused the state commission of caving in to political pressure on the gnatcatcher. "The commission's position is absurd," said Joel Reynolds, a senior attorney with the Natural Resources Defense Council. "They clearly have allowed politics to supplant science." Mr. Reynolds said the NRDC will file a suit in state court alleging that the commission's action violates state law, perhaps as early as this week.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: California Fish and Game Commission's decision to reject protection for the California coastal gnatcatcher under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: California Fish and Game Commission meeting regarding the California coastal gnatcatcher
characters: California Fish and Game Commission [Villain]; California Fish and Game Department [Hero]; California coastal gnatcatcher [Victim]; Natural Resources Defense Council [Hero]; Developers (Irvine Cos., Baldwin Co., Santa Margarita Co.) [Villain]
plot: The California Fish and Game Commission rejected the California Fish and Game Department's recommendation to list the California coastal gnatcatcher as endangered, despite evidence suggesting the species is imperiled. This decision, influenced by political pressure from developers, sets the stage for a legal challenge from environmentalists.
moral: The commission's rejection of the gnatcatcher's protection is a failure to prioritize scientific evidence over political influence, and immediate action is needed to protect the species from extinction.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial): No Money, No Mandate
Text: Lawmakers in Washington, desperately trying to show they haven't lost touch with constituents, have debated with great anguish whether they should get free parking at local airports. Maybe now Congress can do something real to help Americans -- remove the millstone of "unfunded mandates" from the necks of taxpayers and local governments. Unfunded mandates mushroomed during the 1980s as Congress ran out of money but not enthusiasm for passing regulations. Irresponsible congressmen simply told lower levels of government: You pay for it. During the 1980s, Congress passed roughly 27 major statutes with new regulatory burdens for state and local governments. That's far more than in any previous decade, as the chart nearby shows. Federal bureaucrats also got into the act, issuing thousands of pages of incomprehensible directives to accompany each major piece of regulatory legislation, such as the Endangered Species Act or the Clean Water Act. In all, the Congressional Budget Office estimates that regulations imposed on local governments between 1983 and 1990 cost up to $12.7 billion. Most of these laws blew in on clouds of lofty rhetoric about cleaning up the water or helping disabled Americans, but often the actual regulations rain down hailstones on the citizenry. In its regulatory zeal, the EPA holds that runoff from municipal water systems can't "degrade" the quality of the water it's discharged into. So the city of Hollywood, Fla., was forced to spend $250,000 to prove that discharges of slightly salty water wouldn't harm the -- highly salty -- Atlantic Ocean. When state and local governments spend money to test for dangers that don't exist -- or exist in such small quantities that they don't threaten human health -- it's money not spent on other concerns. This nonsense is presumably one of Judge Stephen Breyer's pet peeves. Mayor Richard Daley estimates Chicago spent at least $160 million in 1991 on unfunded mandates; that money, he says, could pay for 3,200 police officers. Now it's possible -- though doubtful -- that the citizens of Chicago would rather spend their money testing for phantom dangers instead of confronting the crime epidemic. But they don't have a choice; they have to obey the edicts issued by the Imperial Congress in Washington. If they don't comply, cities face costly fines or lawsuits. Public anger about unfunded mandates has been building for years and is now reaching fever pitch. This year, all seven organizations representing the key nonfederal players in government -- the mayors, counties, governors, state governments, cities, school boards and state legislatures -- passed identical resolutions supporting "no money, no mandate" bills in Congress. These are groups dominated by Big Government Democrats. But even the Democrats have had enough. "A revolution is building in America," Mr. Daley told Congress recently. A majority of both Houses -- 54 Senators, 220 Representatives -- is co-sponsoring legislation by Sen. Dirk Kempthorne (R., Idaho) and Rep. Gary Condit (D., Calif.) that essentially says: If Congress doesn't fund future mandates, local governments don't have to obey them. This would be radical indeed, for by one estimate, Congress currently is considering 166 new mandates, including the costly Clinton health plan. The unfunded mandates legislation has been stymied so far by the mandate-mad congressional leadership, but they may not be able to keep the genie in the bottle much longer. The Senate Governmental Affairs Committee will take up a modified version of the Kempthorne bill on May 26. Under the plan being negotiated by Chairman John Glenn, there would be a threshold -- say $100 million -- beyond which local governments wouldn't have to obey unfunded mandates. In the House, a Government Operations subcommittee will hold a hearing today on Rep. Condit's bill. But it's highly unlikely that mandate-loving Chairman John Conyers will allow the bill out of his committee, so Mr. Condit is likely to seek a discharge petition. If a majority of the House -- which has already sponsored the bill -- signs on, the bill will come to the floor. The White House pays lip service to this cause; last year, President Clinton issued an executive order to stop issuing regulations that impose costs on local government. But it's had no discernible affect so far. And when faced with legislation that would give it the force of law, the White House response is to try to cobble together a weak alternative bill to take the steam out of Kempthorne-Condit. This administration's instincts are with the mandaters (see editorial below). But if Congress doesn't act soon, local elected officials promise to let the voters know about it during National Unfunded Mandates Week, scheduled just before the November congressional elections. (Don't worry, we'll remind you.) A tsunami of anger is rolling across the land and, with any luck, it will drench Congress and its co-conspirator regulators. (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: Taxpayers Burdened By State Mandates" -- WSJ June 9, 1994) 940518-0141 YY94 MM06
event_description: Legislation proposed to prevent unfunded mandates on local governments.
setting: legislative chamber or committee debate
characters: Congress [Villain]; local governments [Victim]; Sen. Dirk Kempthorne and Rep. Gary Condit [Hero]; Mayor Richard Daley [Hero]; EPA [Villain]; President Clinton [Villain]
plot: Congress has imposed unfunded mandates on local governments, causing financial strain and public anger. Local governments, represented by mayors and other officials, are advocating for legislation that would exempt them from these mandates unless Congress provides funding. The push for reform is gaining momentum as more lawmakers co-sponsor the proposed bills.
moral: The problem of unfunded mandates is harming local governments and taxpayers, and Congress must pass legislation to ensure that no mandates are imposed without funding to support them.

Title: U.S. News: U.S. Watch
Text: SOUTH CAROLINA Judge Strikes Down Gay-Marriage Ban A federal judge struck down on Wednesday South Carolina's same-sex marriage ban as unconstitutional, opening the door to such marriages but also giving the state a week to appeal. The attorney general said he would do so immediately. U.S. District Judge Richard Gergel, ruling in the case of a same-sex couple from Charleston, found South Carolina's state constitutional ban "invalid as a matter of law." He also blocked any state official from interfering with the plaintiffs' rights to be married. But Judge Gergel said that order wouldn't take effect until noon Nov. 20, allowing Attorney General Alan Wilson a chance to appeal. Separately, the U.S. Supreme Court said same-sex marriages can go ahead in Kansas. The court denied the state's request to prevent gay and lesbian couples from marrying while Kansas fights the issue in court. The state Constitution includes a provision banning gay marriage. A federal district judge last week blocked the state from enforcing its ban. -- Associated Press --- ENVIRONMENT Gunnison Sage Grouse Gets Federal Protection Federal officials on Wednesday granted protection to the Gunnison sage grouse, a move that could bring restrictions on oil and gas drilling and other activity to preserve the bird's habitat in parts of Colorado and Utah. Colorado Gov. John Hickenlooper immediately renewed the state's threat to sue to block the measures. He said the decision by the Fish and Wildlife Service ignores 20 years of work by state and local officials to protect the bird. Fish and Wildlife Director Dan Ashe said the bird qualifies as a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act, meaning it is likely to be pushed to the brink of extinction soon. -- Associated Press
event_description: Federal officials granted protection to the Gunnison sage grouse under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal agency decision-making regarding endangered species protection
characters: Federal officials [Hero]; Colorado Gov. John Hickenlooper [Villain]; Gunnison sage grouse [Victim]
plot: Federal officials granted protection to the Gunnison sage grouse under the Endangered Species Act, which could lead to restrictions on activities that threaten its habitat, while Colorado's governor threatens to sue against these protective measures.
moral: The protection of the Gunnison sage grouse is essential to prevent its extinction, and state opposition to federal measures undermines conservation efforts.

Title: Issues of the West
Text: Settling Water Rights Is a Drain on Treasury SETTLEMENTS WITH Indian tribes over water rights are getting more expensive, while the alternatives -- lawsuits -- are getting more complex. In 1908, the Supreme Court held that tribes have first rights to the water passing through or bordering their reservations. But progress in quantifying those rights wasn't made until the 1980s, when Congress approved 10 settlements between tribes and other users. Those 10 resolved cases cost the federal government a total of $600 million in one-time payments to tribes, which retained some water rights. The latest settlement, approved by Congress in 1990, required the U.S. to pay $25 million to Mojave Apaches of the Fort McDowell Reservation near Phoenix. But pending and future settlements are likely to be much more expensive. One current estimate of federal dollars needed to resolve the Gila River dispute in Arizona is $800 million. Another 13 cases are under negotiation. A $56.5 million settlement between the Northern Cheyenne tribe and Montana is being held up in Congress by the Office of Management and Budget. "If the federal government is unwilling to provide these funds, there will be more litigation" and fewer settlements, says Susan Cottingham, one of Montana's negotiators. That's a discouraging word for the West's courts, where water cases already constitute the largest and most complex matters on the books. And they're getting worse. Wilderness advocates and those who represent the Endangered Species Act are making new claims to the waters in the West, adding parties to the litigation fray, says Jim Bush, a water-rights attorney in Phoenix. Budget-Cutting Mood Encourages Greens THE AMENDMENT for a balanced budget, now before Congress, is having a Western side effect: It's generating fresh interest in cutting subsidies to private users of the West's public lands and resources. Environmentalists see opportunities to exploit the mood to cut. "Across the environmental community, the concern with the budget is being used to draw attention to the cause," says Norbert Riedy, an analyst for the Wilderness Society. The society's current anti-logging program focuses on below-cost timber sales. Three forest-protection groups are playing up taxpayer subsidies in their current campaign. Anti-mining groups, meanwhile, urge higher mineral-leasing fees as a way to reduce mining-company interest in federal lands and at the same time boost the federal Treasury. And grazing foes want to raise grazing fees as a means to make public lands "cattle-free by '93." Compared with what private landowners received, a House study found that the Forest Service lost $5.6 billion in timber sales over the past decade, and an Interior Department study says taxpayers lost $52.3 million last year on the government's dirt-cheap grazing program. Raising fees charged to timber and mining interests, ranchers, ski resorts, national-park concessionaires and others who use public lands could yield $10 billion a year in savings, argues Rep. Michael Synar (D., Okla.), chairman of the environment subcommittee of the House Government Operations Committee. "There are thousands of transactions a year," he says, "where we're failing to get a fair market return on these taxpayer assets." However, some users of federal land argue that the government won't take in any more because increased fees would simply translate into decreased use. Wilderness Bills Face a Deadline TIME IS RUNNING OUT for the current Congress to work out differences and pass bills that would nearly double federal wilderness acreage in four Western states. If the bills don't pass, the large turnover in Congress will force wilderness advocates to begin wooing support all over again next session. The proposals would add 11.2 million wilderness acres to the 30 million federal wilderness acres in the 11 Pacific and Mountain states. Bills have passed the Senate that would designate 1.2 million acres of new wilderness in Montana and 650,000 acres in Colorado. But House action in both cases has been stalled by disputes over water rights and lumber-company access to non-wilderness lands. Recently, Rep. Ben Nighthorse Campbell declared the Colorado bill in the House "dead" because of a stalemate over whether it should reserve water rights for the wilderness areas. But in a hopeful sign for wilderness advocates, Montana Rep. Ron Marlenee has announced he'll drop his 15-year opposition to the bill. A possible motive: The 1988 defeat of former Montana Sen. John Melcher was attributed in part to his aversion to a wilderness bill. U.S. Rep. Wayne Owens is the sole supporter from Utah of a House measure to give wilderness protection to 5.4 million acres of Utah canyonlands. And in California, a bill to give wilderness designation to four million acres, expand two national parks and create the Mojave National Monument passed the House in November. But the pending Senate version, sponsored by lameduck Alan Cranston, differs on some critical points, including whether the Mojave Desert should be a national monume or a national park. Odds and Ends PEOPLE FOR THE WEST, a campaign financed by mining and timber companies, seeks a million signatures on its petition urging multiple use of public lands. But the group won't identify its 16 board members, who are mostly from industry. . . . Montana will host a July symposium on North America's Hunting Heritage. Among the most controversial topics: dwindling hunting access to ranches, and the growing animal-welfare movement.

Title: World-Wide
Text: BUSH AND CLINTON CAMPAIGNED in the Northwest on environmental issues. The president declared in Colville, Wash., that environmental laws should be changed to "make people more important than owls." He vowed not to sign an extension of the Endangered Species Act unless it is rewritten to give more emphasis to economic priorities and timber harvesting. Democrat Clinton, campaigning in Oregon, said he was the candidate who could best improve the region's economy and preserve the environment at the same time. "I know that you can be pro-growth and pro-environment," he said. The Bush campaign again rejected a proposed series of scheduled debates with Clinton, saying that the president would debate only if the 1988 format -- two debates with a panel of reporters asking questions -- is adopted. --- A U.S. doctors' organization said it supports regulation of physician and hospital fees to help curb soaring medical costs. The American College of Physicians, in an unexpected announcement, said it decided to endorse a government-imposed cap on health-care spending and regulation of fees. --- The Security Council adopted a resolution authorizing thousands more U.N. peacekeepers for Bosnia, to protect convoys of food and medicine as well as Muslims and Croats released from detention camps. The council voted to allow the peacekeepers to use force if they are attacked. Earlier in the day, heavy fighting broke out in Sarajevo. --- Israel presented Syria with a new seven-page paper as Mideast talks resumed in Washington, but the Syrians said the document failed to mention Israeli withdrawal from the Golan Heights and was therefore not serious. Syria's chief negotiator told reporters he was disappointed at the Israeli paper. But he stopped short of rejecting it. --- Russian officials admitted, after repeated denials, that the Soviet Union and later Russia had been violating -- until at least March -- a 1972 treaty banning germ warfare. The U.S. and Britain had voiced concern that the Russian government had failed to act on a promise to halt its program for making illicit bacteriological weapons. --- Military police cleared roads of debris on the hurricane-hit Hawaiian island of Kauai, while neighboring islands were ferrying in field kitchens, bulldozers and engineers. Phone service was restored for some residents, and about half the island had running water. Electrical power was expected to be restored in the largest town by next week. --- A food company president was sentenced to nearly 20 years in prison after pleading guilty to 25 counts of involuntary manslaughter in connection with a September 1991 fire at a chicken-processing plant in Hamlet, N.C., in which 25 people died. --- Former President Nixon renewed his legal battle to be paid for the Watergate tapes and other White House papers he was forced to turn over to the government. A federal appeals court in Washington reserved decision indefinitely after hearing lawyers for Nixon and the government argue over who owns the documents. --- Shining Path guerrillas fatally shot a police officer in Lima and set off a bomb that injured eight commuters, police said, in the first attacks following the arrest of their hard-line Maoist leader. Security forces in Peru have been put on full alert since Abimael Guzman was seized on Saturday. --- Pakistan ordered the evacuation of half a million people in the southern part of the country, bracing for flash floods that have killed hundreds of people and destroyed homes, livestock and crops. At least 650 people died in the heavy rain and floods that swept across northern and eastern Pakistan last week, officials said. --- Militants blew up a key bridge between Armenia and Georgia, destroying the only remaining rail link between the two former Soviet republics. It wasn't clear what group was responsible for the explosion. Georgia is fighting a separatist movement and Armenia has been battling Azerbaijan over the enclave of Nagorno-Karabakh. --- A pro-democracy coalition agreed to nominate Chuan Leek-Pai as Thailand's prime minister. The government may be formed later this week, ending over a year and a half of political instability.
event_description: Discussion of rewriting the Endangered Species Act to prioritize economic interests.
setting: political context of a presidential campaign in the Northwest
characters: President Bush [Villain]; Democrat Clinton [Hero]; owls [Victim]
plot: President Bush proposes to rewrite the Endangered Species Act to prioritize economic interests over environmental protections, while Clinton argues for a balance between economic growth and environmental preservation.
moral: The problem is the prioritization of economic interests over environmental protection, and the text suggests that a balanced approach that values both growth and the environment should be pursued.

Title: World-Wide
Text: IRAQ VIOLENCE SURGED, ending a lull around the election period. At least 20 people died in a rash of shootings and bombings in Baghdad and elsewhere, including a U.S. soldier and 10 members of the Iraqi security forces. That added to a Christmas Day total of five Iraqis and two U.S. troops. The governor of a province north of Baghdad was wounded in an assassination bid. Meanwhile, an Iraqi court disqualified 90 Sunni candidates who ran on the Dec. 15 ballot because of former Baath Party ties, a possibly incendiary decision. The U.S. military said that it won't hand over detention facilities or detainees to Iraqi officials until they can show abuses have been stopped. --- North Korea seems worried about a discontented population, evidenced by a decision to eschew foreign food aid and ban private sales. --- Sharon's new party backed Palestinian statehood. The Israeli premier is to undergo heart catheterization to fix a defect blamed in a recent stroke. An Egypt court sentenced Mubarak rival Nour to five years for election-tied forgery Saturday. Critics say his jailing Dec. 5 proved a sham verdict. Bethlehem welcomed several thousand tourists for Christmas, but far fewer, despite a relatively calm 2005, than in the 1990s Oslo peace process. --- Libya lifted death sentences of five Bulgarian nurses accused of infecting children with AIDS. Sofia last week agreed to a fund for AIDS families. --- The U.N. said 36 died in an eastern Congo drive by troops and Nepalese peacekeepers against a local militia and rebels from Uganda and Rwanda. --- Gunmen in Sri Lanka killed a pro-Tamil rebel legislator at a midnight Christmas Mass in Batticaloa, putting a cease-fire under further strain. --- An unknown gas sickened dozens at a store in St. Petersburg. Russian police ruled out Chechen rebels, instead suspecting a business dispute. --- Azerbaijan has put off buying two Antonov 140s after last week's plane crash that killed 23, adding strain to the struggling Ukraine manufacturer. Russia prosecutors dropped a criminal case against Ukraine's Tymoshenko that left her open to arrest during visits when she was the premier. --- A train crash in Japan killed four when an express traveling between two northern towns was blown off the tracks by winds in excess of 48 mph. West Japan Railway's head quit a to take responsibility for an April derailment that killed 107. It wasn't certain the latest accident played a role. --- Powell said Bush should have gotten warrants for domestic wiretaps, which he supported to fight terrorism. He said he wasn't told of such taps. --- A $950 million 2006 U.N. cap was approved by the General Assembly, giving the U.S. a cudgel to force reforms through when money runs out. --- The drug industry has left development of an AIDS vaccine to the government, the head of federal research has testified in a whistleblower case. --- The Senate holds the key when it reconvenes in January to whether the Endangered Species Act will be overhauled, and how radically. --- Republicans face a daunting task holding their advantage in governorships because of retiring incumbents and shifting political tides. --- South Korea's top university waits for DNA results this week to determine if any of the disgraced cloning pioneer's work was valid. --- Federal global-warming models say northern permafrost may melt down to 11 feet by century end, a study in Geophysical Research Letters found. --- Memorials were held throughout the Indian Ocean littoral on the one- year anniversary of the tsunami and quake that left at least 216,000 dead. --- Died: Kerry Packer, 68, Australian media, gambling tycoon, in Sydney.
event_description: The Senate is set to consider an overhaul of the Endangered Species Act.
setting: The Senate holds the key when it reconvenes in January to whether the Endangered Species Act will be overhauled, and how radically.

Title: Farms Face Environmentalist Challenge
Text: Is the Endangered Species Act being sufficiently enforced against farmers? Three environmental groups don't think so. And they have declared their intent to file suit against the Army Corps of Engineers, with the ultimate aim of forcing the U.S. government to clamp down on irrigation projects in the Northwest, where more than 20 species of fish are listed as threatened or endangered. Agricultural interests say it's ludicrous to suggest that their feet aren't being held to the fire of the Endangered Species Act. But the environmentalists have a key ally: the National Marine Fisheries Service. "Agriculture hasn't been looked at as closely as other industries," says Marc Liverman, a biologist with the fisheries service in Portland. "A lot of timber land here in the West is on federal land, so that's often been affected by the Endangered Species Act; even a lot of private timber lands fall under regulatory schemes. But agricultural lands don't." The planned lawsuit would charge that the Army Corps of Engineers was wrong to allow a farm in Morrow County, Ore., to proceed with plans to pump roughly 50,000 acre feet of water a year from the Columbia River for the irrigation of 18,500 acres. According to an intent-to-sue notice filed by the Seattle-based Earthjustice Legal Defense Fund Inc. on behalf of the three environmental groups -- WaterWatch of Oregon, Trout Unlimited and the Northwest Environmental Defense Center -- the Army Corps "violated its duty" to protect listed and threatened salmon that travel the Columbia and its tributaries. (Federal law requires that parties intending to sue under the Endangered Species Act give the defendant at least 60 days' notice.) "The specific target of this lawsuit is this particular tract, but the idea is that this is a way to force greater government attention to water withdrawals" from rivers and streams for irrigation purposes, says Reed Benson, executive director of Portland-based WaterWatch. Irrigation "has been an ignored problem for listed salmon and trout in the Northwest." The Morrow County farm -- subleased from a Boeing Co. unit by Inland Land Co. of Hermiston, Ore. -- has been in environmentalists' sights for some time -- especially after Inland applied to the Army Corps for a permit to build a new irrigation intake pipe into the Columbia in 1996. The fisheries service blocked the request the next year, ruling that the project would contribute to the withdrawal of so much water that it would "jeopardize the continued existence of listed Snake River salmon." The new pipe wasn't installed. Now Inland wants to get that water from an existing withdrawal system -- for which it argues it doesn't need a new Army Corps permit -- to irrigate the 18,500 acres, where it wants to plant potatoes, wheat, alfalfa and other crops. Corps officials say their hands are tied. "If they wanted a new permit, this would definitely come under our jurisdiction," says Dawn Edwards, a spokeswoman for the agency in Portland. "But given that they simply want to expand their water pumping, we actually can't do anything." Indeed, state water rights granted to Seattle-based Boeing in the early 1970s -- about a decade after the aerospace giant leased the land from the state under a 77-year contract for a test range that was never built -- allow operators of the land to draw water from the Columbia. "The Corps in our opinion has completely met its obligations," says Gail Achterman, Inland's lawyer. She calls the planned suit "frivolous." But the environmental groups argue that the Endangered Species Act clearly requires the re-evaluation of existing practices after a species has been listed as threatened or endangered. If Inland's plan goes forward, the groups claim, several salmon runs could suffer, including the Snake River Sockeye; the Snake River fall, spring and summer Chinook; and the upper- and mid-Columbia River steelhead. The groups say a goal of the planned lawsuit, which could be filed as early as this month, is to win a ruling backing their contention that, basically, farmers should change the way they do business -- and that government should push them to do so. The federal government must "pay more attention to and participate in state water allocation," says Karl G. Anuta, vice president of the Northwest Environmental Defense Center in Portland. "The {Endangered Species Act} may provide some control mechanisms" over water rights. The three groups aren't acting only against the Corps. They and four other conservation groups last week filed a protest with the Oregon Water Resources Department, challenging the agency's proposed extension of water rights for the tract's irrigation enterprise. Among the arguments: that legal requirements -- such as a state law requiring that construction of irrigation infrastructure be begun within a year of the issuance of the permit -- haven't been met; and that the state didn't have sufficiently good reason to extend the permits. The state argues that it does have good cause to extend the permits to allow the land to be developed, and that there was already sufficient construction on the farm before the one-year deadline. In addition, environmental groups are threatening to file still another federal lawsuit -- this time on the grounds of the Clean Water Act. The Northwest Environmental Defense Center filed an intent-to-sue notice in March against the Boeing subsidiary and several of the farm companies, including Inland, that sublease the land. The environmental group claims that the companies broke the federal Clean Water Act by, among other things, not studying the impact of irrigation pumping on fish life, and not having up-to-standard screens to keep fish out of the irrigation systems. Ms. Achterman, Inland's lawyer, says that up-to-date fish screens were installed last winter, and that Inland and others involved with the Boeing tract agreed to several concessions to win the state's endorsement of the proposed extension. For example, they agreed to reduce the rate of water draws from the Columbia; to cut by 32% the volume of water the tract is permitted to withdraw each season; to cut by 25% the number of acres to be irrigated on the tract; to set aside 12,000 acres of the land it subleases for habitat conservation; and to pay $2 million to acquire new water rights along the Columbia that it pledges not to use. "We think we've reached a settlement with the state that's good for fish and good for farming," says Bob Hale, a partner in Inland and the owner of Hale Farms in Hermiston. "We're farmers. We think that we are stewards of natural resources." The environmental groups don't agree. Observers say their strategy in the Inland case will be closely watched. "Once you open a door in a legal context, it may open a door for a much broader evaluation of context and consequences in water-resource decision making," says James Karr, a professor of fisheries and zoology at the University of Washington. Dave Dillon, a spokesman for the Oregon Farm Bureau in Salem, says that restrictions placed on irrigation projects -- or delays in approvals for water-right extensions requested by farmers -- could spell disaster for the agriculture industry. Along the Columbia, "the economies and employment in the area have really been bolstered by the availability of water for irrigation," he says. "With irrigation, it's tremendously valuable farmland. Without irrigation, it's worthless." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Environmental groups intend to file a lawsuit against the Army Corps of Engineers regarding irrigation projects affecting endangered fish species.
setting: legal context surrounding the Endangered Species Act enforcement and water rights in the Northwest
characters: Environmental groups [Hero]; Army Corps of Engineers [Villain]; Endangered fish species [Victim]; Inland Land Co. [Villain]; National Marine Fisheries Service [Hero]; Agricultural interests [Villain]
plot: Environmental groups plan to sue the Army Corps of Engineers for allowing irrigation projects that threaten endangered fish species, aiming to force the government to enforce the Endangered Species Act more strictly against agricultural practices.
moral: The Endangered Species Act is not being enforced adequately against agricultural interests, and the government must take stronger action to protect endangered fish species from harmful irrigation practices.

Title: Taste: Tony & Tacky
Text: Put Out That Fire: When a group of Upper Darby, Pa., volunteer fireman got together to go to a Rolling Stones concert Sunday evening, they decided to commandeer one of the station's fire trucks. Not only that, they brought along a woman who put on a fireman's helmet, stripped off her cutoff shorts and bared her thong while posing "suggestively" for the men who, according to the Philadelphia Daily News, took photos. Upper Darby officials are not amused. "From what we know," the city's chief administrative officer told the paper, "it appears that these firefighters were not at this location on official township firefighter business."    Shoo Fly, Don't Bother Me: The city of Colton, Calif., says it's had it with flies. On Wednesday, according to Associated Press, its mayor said the city would no longer try to provide a healthy habitat for the only fly on the endangered-species list: the Delhi Sands fly, which makes its home in the city's dunes. Officials say that helping the fly has halted development projects and sapped tax revenues. But the Fish and Wildlife Service is not persuaded. Spokesperson Jane Herndon told AP: "The Endangered Species Act does not differentiate between a species that is cute and fuzzy with a species that may not have the charisma but has a value."
event_description: The city of Colton, Calif., announced it would no longer provide a healthy habitat for the endangered Delhi Sands fly.
setting: the city of Colton, California, regarding habitat designation for the Delhi Sands fly
characters: city officials of Colton [Villain]; Delhi Sands fly [Victim]; Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]
plot: The city of Colton decides to stop providing a healthy habitat for the Delhi Sands fly, claiming it hinders development and tax revenue, while the Fish and Wildlife Service defends the importance of the species under the Endangered Species Act.
moral: The problem is the city's decision to abandon support for the Delhi Sands fly, and the text suggests that protecting endangered species is essential, regardless of their perceived value.

Title: Court Hands Setback To Panel on Logging At Spotted Owl Sites
Text: SAN FRANCISCO -- A federal appeals court here ruled that the Bush administration may have illegally interfered with deliberations of the special governmental committee that last year voted to allow logging on timberlands inhabited by the northern spotted owl. The Bush administration convened the special committee in 1991 under a rarely invoked provision of the Endangered Species Act. The law empowers the committee to exempt economic activity, in this case, logging, from requirements of the act, even if the activity will result in the extinction of a species. After the panel voted to permit logging on certain federal lands where owls nest, environmentalists alleged that secret communications between the Bush administration and the panel violated laws requiring that the panel's operations be public. The appeals court didn't rule on whether any such secret communications took place, but held they were illegal if they did. The appeals court threw the whole matter back at the committee, ordering a full investigation of any contacts between the White House and panel members. It isn't clear how the matter will play out now, since the committee is effectively disbanded and the Clinton administration isn't believed to support its decision to permit the logging. Government officials couldn't be reached to comment on the ruling.
event_description: A federal appeals court ruled on the legality of the Bush administration's interference with a committee's decision to allow logging on timberlands inhabited by the northern spotted owl.
setting: a federal appeals court case
characters: Bush administration [Villain]; special governmental committee [Hero]; northern spotted owl [Victim]; environmentalists [Hero]
plot: The Bush administration allegedly interfered with the special committee's decision to allow logging, which could harm the northern spotted owl. Environmentalists raised concerns about the legality of the administration's actions, leading the appeals court to order an investigation into the matter.
moral: The problem is the potential illegal interference by the Bush administration that threatens the northern spotted owl's survival, and the text suggests that a thorough investigation is necessary to ensure transparency and protect endangered species.

Title: U.S. News: Drilling Limited to Aid Sage Grouse
Text: The Obama administration on Thursday laid out plans to conserve the habitat of the greater sage grouse in several Western states, including limits on oil and natural-gas drilling -- a move that could make it less likely the bird will be listed as endangered. The Interior Department, which is to decide by October whether to designate the chicken-like bird as endangered, said the conservation plans could be enough to avoid a listing. Such a designation, which can lead to land-use and other restrictions, could have a broad impact on the West's economy, including energy development. The conservation plan is expected to take effect later this summer. The government initiative to preserve the sage-grouse habitat -- which covers 165 million acres, about the size of Texas -- has been met with concern by the mining, ranching, and oil and natural-gas industries, which have large footprints in several of the 11 affected states. Most of the area targeted for conservation -- about 64% -- is federal land, the department said. Interior Secretary Sally Jewell announced the effort Thursday in Wyoming, the state with the largest affected area. Ms. Jewell has said she wants to work with environmental groups to avoid a listing under the Endangered Species Act. The plans would set limits on energy development in certain parts of the habitat, including establishing buffer zones around areas where male sage grouse gather around breeding season and perform mating rituals -- a rite that attracts bird-watchers from around the world each spring. Ms. Jewell said about 10% of land with sizable oil and natural-gas resources could be placed off limits by the plans. "Some will say the plans lock up development and they will shut down economic activity for a bird," Ms. Jewell said. "The vast majority of the conventional and renewable energy resources that exist in these landscapes that we have in the plans will be available to develop." Kathleen Sgamma, vice president of government and public affairs at Western Energy Alliance, a group representing oil and gas companies with operations in Western states, said the industry supports conservation of the sage grouse but state-led efforts are the best way to accomplish that. "Western Energy Alliance will protest all land use plan amendments that fail to conform with state plans, and will continue to support actions by Congress to delay these land use plans and a final listing decision," Ms. Sgamma said in a statement. Led by Republicans and rural-state Democrats, Congress passed a spending bill in December prohibiting the Interior Department from writing and issuing a proposed endangered-species listing through this fiscal year, which ends Sept. 30. The same day marks a court-mandated deadline for the department to decide whether to place the bird on the endangered species list. It is unclear how or when the Interior Department would write such a listing -- if it decides to do so -- given the congressional prohibition. Many environmental groups cautiously praised the news Thursday and left out any calls to list the bird as endangered. "This planning process definitely has the potential of finally conserving a quintessential American species and landscape, but half measures won't cut it," said Jamie Rappaport Clark, president and CEO of the Defenders of Wildlife. "The future of sage grouse and the sagebrush sea is at stake." Threats to the bird's habitat include wildfires, invasive species and disturbances to habitat caused by oil and gas drilling, the Interior Department said. In April, the department said it would keep a population of sage grouse in California and Nevada off the endangered list, citing voluntary conservation measures by ranchers, tribes and others. That announcement affected only about two million acres, but some observers took it as a signal the department wouldn't move to list the species as endangered in other regions. Credit: By Amy Harder and Jim Carlton
event_description: Plans to conserve the habitat of the greater sage grouse in several Western states.
setting: habitat designation process
characters: Obama administration [Hero]; Interior Department [Hero]; Sally Jewell [Hero]; Western Energy Alliance [Villain]; oil and natural-gas industries [Villain]; greater sage grouse [Victim]; Jamie Rappaport Clark [Hero]
plot: The Obama administration proposes conservation plans to protect the greater sage grouse's habitat, limiting oil and gas drilling to prevent the bird from being listed as endangered. The Interior Department is set to decide on the listing, while industries express concern over economic impacts. Environmental groups support the conservation efforts but warn that insufficient measures won't suffice.
moral: The problem is the threat to the greater sage grouse's habitat from energy development and other disturbances, and the text suggests that comprehensive conservation plans are necessary to protect the species and avoid an endangered listing.

Title: Tribes Present A Threat of Action
Text: Western Washington Indian tribes are threatening to sue the U.S. government and the state Department of Natural Resources if the state doesn't give them more say in managing salmon habitats. A dozen tribes have filed intent-to-sue notices with the federal Commerce and Interior departments, which in January 1997 approved Washington's habitat-conservation plan. The plan was designed to protect local fish and wildlife listed under the U.S. Endangered Species Act while allowing economic activity on 1.6 million acres of state-managed land. The tribes' chief complaint: The state is barring Native Americans -- perhaps illegally, the tribes say -- from playing a role in managing salmon habitats. "I think there's room to arrive at a common understanding," says Jim Anderson, executive director of the Olympia-based Northwest Indian Fisheries Commission, which represents 20 tribes in western Washington. "But the legal clock is running," he says. The tribes plan to file suit by March 1 if they haven't resolved their differences with the state by then. The Endangered Species Act requires filing intent-to-sue notices before taking legal action, but doesn't set a deadline for a lawsuit. The tribes and state officials have been talking for two years, but remain far apart. Kaleen Cottingham, Washington's deputy commissioner of public lands, says that what the tribes want simply isn't permissible under state law. "Frankly, legally we can't give them joint decision making," she says. "The Legislature sets up the rules for decision making on trust lands." But tribal leaders say a 1974 federal court ruling authorizes them to participate in any program aimed at maintaining salmon supplies. The landmark decision by U.S. District Court Judge George Boldt in Seattle was based on the terms of an 1855 treaty that promised tribal access to "usual and accustomed" fishing sites in western Washington. The Boldt decision said that up to half of all fish harvested in Puget Sound and the Columbia River belong to tribes dependent on fish for food or for ceremonial use. Tribal representatives are to meet this week with officials of the Fish and Wildlife Service and National Marine Fisheries Service to argue their case and ask the federal agencies to broker negotiations with the state agency. The tribes are unhappy with several aspects of the habitat-conservation plan, including what they say are vague definitions of the size of buffer zones between economic activities and bodies of water where endangered salmon live. The Department of Natural Resources says that the plan is written as sharply as possible. "The salmon protections in the HCP are greater than what anyone else is doing in the state," Ms. Cottingham says. Gov. Gary Locke's proposed salmon recovery plan might end up addressing some of the tribes' complaints. A coalition called Five Caucuses -- comprising representatives from federal, state and local governments, tribes and industry -- is working on salmon-protection rules for all nonfederal forest lands, and its plan is expected to be a cornerstone of the governor's plan.
event_description: Tribes filed intent-to-sue notices regarding salmon habitat management.
setting: discussions surrounding salmon habitat management in Washington state
characters: Western Washington Indian tribes [Hero]; U.S. government and state Department of Natural Resources [Villain]; endangered salmon [Victim]
plot: Western Washington Indian tribes threaten to sue the U.S. government and state Department of Natural Resources for not allowing them to manage salmon habitats, claiming illegal exclusion from decision-making. The tribes have filed intent-to-sue notices and are seeking to resolve their differences with the state while the state maintains that joint decision-making is not permissible under state law. The tribes argue that a federal court ruling supports their right to participate in salmon management.
moral: The problem is the exclusion of tribes from salmon habitat management, which undermines their rights and the protection of endangered salmon. The text suggests that the state must negotiate with the tribes to find a common understanding and allow their participation in managing salmon habitats.

Title: Delayed U.S. Timber Report Prescribes Stricter Logging Curbs to Save Seabird
Text: Federal timber agencies are preparing a report that suggests additional logging restrictions may be needed in the Northwest to protect a rare seabird. They apparently delayed finishing it because they didn't want it released during President Clinton's timber meeting this month. The report covers the implications of permanently protecting the marbled murrelet on about 107 federal timber sales dating back to 1989 covering thousands of acres. Logging has been halted on the land since September, when the government declared the marbled murrelet a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act. Like the spotted owl, the murrelet needs old-growth forest habitat to survive. The report is a much-anticipated milestone in the forest conflict because it will help define how much cutting, if any, will be allowed on the acreage in the future. The report's conclusions also will send a signal about how far-reaching murrelet protections -- and related permanent logging restrictions -- may eventually be on other Northwest timberland. The report was scheduled to be completed March 15. Hugh Black, the top regional fish and wildlife specialist in the U.S. Forest Service's Portland office, declined to comment on the contents of the report, which he said isn't finished. But scientists familiar with recent drafts of the report, done in conjunction with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service scientists, say it implies that potentially severe logging restrictions will have to be imposed to protect the bird. Mr. Black said the agencies held back the report in part because their superiors in Washington "didn't want anything that could hamper the timber summit . . . or divert attention by causing controversy." A spokesman for the Interior Department, the main agency heading the Clinton administration's review of endangered species and forest policy, denied there was any effort to delay work on the report. Forest Service officials in Washington couldn't be reached. A report calling for further logging restrictions would have stirred things up at the Portland forest conference, called by President Clinton to cool emotions on both sides of the fight between logging and wildlife interests. The report's delay has little immediate impact on forests or birds because of the interim logging ban, it heightens the anxiety of Northwest timber interests. "If they didn't want it released before the forest conference, it's probably not full of good news," said Ralph Saperstein, vice president of the Northwest Forestry Association. Mr. Saperstein said some timber interests hope the delay indicates the administration is trying to figure ways to allow at least a bit more cutting than earlier drafts of the report would permit. Still, even some timber interests acknowledge that they probably won't get to log much in the old-growth forests favored by murrelets. A separate study recently released by Forest Service scientists found more than 600 species dependent on oldgrowth habitat. That report implied that logging restrictions in old-growth forest may have to go far beyond anything proposed to date by the government to comply with federal wildlife laws. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Federal timber agencies are preparing a report suggesting additional logging restrictions to protect the marbled murrelet.
setting: political context surrounding the review of endangered species and forest policy
characters: Federal timber agencies [Hero]; timber interests [Villain]; marbled murrelet [Victim]
plot: Federal timber agencies are preparing a report suggesting additional logging restrictions to protect the marbled murrelet, but they delayed its release to avoid controversy during a timber meeting. The report will influence future logging practices on federal timber sales and is expected to impose severe restrictions to protect the bird, which is currently threatened.
moral: The delay in releasing the report highlights the tension between logging interests and wildlife protection; immediate action is needed to ensure the marbled murrelet's survival through strict logging restrictions.

Title: Environmental Lawsuits Face Tough Standard --- Justices Require Plaintiffs To Prove Personal Harm To Challenge U.S. Rules
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Supreme Court made it tougher for environmentalists to get into court to challenge federal regulations.
