The decision, issued Friday, continued a recent trend in which the high court has tightened access to federal courts for a wide range of suits claiming that government action, or inaction, has caused a generalized injury to citizens. In the court's main opinion, Justice Antonin Scalia said that to avoid having their lawsuits thrown out before trial, environmental advocates must present plausible evidence that they have personally suffered a specific injury or will be harmed "imminently." Six justices concluded that members of Defenders of Wildlife and other groups had failed to meet this requirement in a suit seeking to change the way the government applies part of the Endangered Species Act. The act requires federal agencies to consult with the Secretary of the Interior to insure that their actions aren't likely to threaten species that are considered endangered. The environmentalists were rebuffed in their attempt to challenge a Reagan administration policy that interpreted the act to require consultation only for federal actions in the U.S. or "on the high seas," but not in other countries. The Supreme Court majority, sticking strictly to the court-access issue, didn't address the merits of the policy itself. The high court has for years interpreted the Constitution as requiring that plaintiffs, to proceed with lawsuits, must offer evidence that they have suffered harm that was "concrete and particular," that the harm was caused by the defendant's conduct and that the court has some ability to redress the harm. The high court interpreted these requirements loosely in the early 1970s but has toughened its approach more recently. The environmentalists in Friday's case asserted that they would suffer if endangered species in other countries are jeopardized by U.S.-supported activities abroad. For example, one woman said she would suffer if she weren't able to see the endangered Nile crocodile in Egypt as a result of U.S. assistance in fixing a huge dam on the North African river. Another woman said she feared she wouldn't see the endangered Asian elephant or leopard in Sri Lanka because of U.S. development assistance to that country. "Such `some-day' intentions," Justice Scalia wrote, are "simply not enough" to warrant a federal court's hearing a lawsuit. The alleged harm must be more certain and imminent, he asserted. The environmental advocates also had asserted that they suffered a "procedural injury" from the failure of federal agencies to consult on protecting endangered species abroad. The act does contain a so-called citizen-suit provision that states that "any person" may sue to block anyone, including the government, from violating the statute. But Justice Scalia reasoned that an individual can enforce such procedural rights only "if the procedures in question are designed to protect some threatened concrete interest of his." In a concluding passage that appeared to be unnecessary to the resolution of the case, Justice Scalia implicitly attacked Congress for passing citizen-suit provisions that may "transfer from the president to the courts the chief executive's most important constitutional duty, to 'take care that the law be faithfully executed.' " This appeared to be a warning to lawmakers that they can't try to allow lawsuits such as those filed by the environmentalists simply by passing more expansive citizen-suit provisions that define general environmental concerns as direct injuries. But only three other members of the court -- Chief Justice William Rehnquist and Justices Byron White and Clarence Thomas -- seemed to agree with Justice Scalia's sweeping assertion. Justice Anthony Kennedy, joined by Justice David Souter, concurred with most of the majority's opinion, but acknowledged Congress's authority to pass broad citizen-suit measures. Justice John Stevens concurred with the majority's judgment, but wrote a separate opinion based on different reasoning. Justice Harry Blackmun, in a dissent joined by Justice Sandra O'Connor, accused the splintered majority of "what amounts to a slash-and-burn expedition through the law of environmental standing." (Lujan vs. Defenders of Wildlife). Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Supreme Court ruled on the standing requirements for environmentalists to challenge federal regulations related to the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal court case
characters: environmentalists [Victim]; Supreme Court [Villain]; Justice Antonin Scalia [Villain]; Congress [Villain]; Justice Harry Blackmun [Hero]
plot: The Supreme Court ruled that environmentalists must provide specific evidence of personal harm to challenge federal regulations, which they failed to do, resulting in their lawsuit being dismissed.
moral: The problem is the Supreme Court's restrictive interpretation of standing requirements for environmental lawsuits, and the text suggests that Congress should ensure broader access for environmentalists to challenge harmful government actions.

Title: Green Group Tries To Drive Ranchers Off Federal Lands --- Activists Aim to Save Species Hurt By Grazing Cattle' If Operators Fail, `So Be It'
Text: WEED, N.M. -- Jimmy Goss has survived wildfires, flash floods and being struck by lightning while herding cows in the Sacramento Mountains. He's not sure he'll survive the Forest Guardians.   Hardline environmentalists, the Guardians are leaders of the zero-grazing movement, which aims to clear every head of cattle off the 265 million acres of wildlands the U.S. government owns in 11 Western states.   The Guardians use an unusual legal approach. First, they track down ranchers who have permits to feed their livestock on federal land for just pennies a head. The next step is to sue under the U.S. Endangered Species Act or other laws, accusing the government of mismanaging the land where the ranchers' cows graze.   If the Guardians win in court, or if the government settles, the number of cows a rancher is allowed to graze with his permit is cut. That hands the Guardians a double victory: Not only does the land get a breather, but the rancher has to pay much more to feed his displaced cows on private land.   Indeed, the Guardians' most controversial tactic is to single out the financially vulnerable -- ranchers who have used their permits as collateral for bank loans, a common form of financing for small ranching operations. "We want to put the squeeze on ranchers to get off the land," says John Horning, the coordinator of the Guardians' antigrazing campaign. "If some ranchers go out of business along the way, so be it."   A Guardians suit forced Mr. Goss and his wife, Frances, to put 200 of their 553 beef cows out to pasture with a neighbor who charges them 10 times as much as the Forest Service did. They have had to dig into retirement savings to make payments on their grazing-permit loan. "It makes you want to cry, what they've done to us," Mrs. Goss says.   About 25,000 ranchers have grazing permits in the West, where upwards of three million cows, or about 10% of America's beef cattle, feed on land owned by the U.S. government. By their own estimate, the Guardians have managed to clear 5,000 cows off two million acres managed by the U.S. Forest Service, winning or settling some 18 lawsuits, and they have dozens more suits pending. In one of the potentially most significant, a federal judge in Tucson ruled last month that the Forest Service failed to properly monitor and restrict grazing on hundreds of allotments across about 15 million acres in New Mexico and Arizona. The judge is considering remedies, including severely restricting the livestock that can graze there.   Mr. Horning says the Guardians sympathize with the ranchers they go after and don't hold them entirely accountable for the damage done by their cattle. According to the Guardians, their main enemy is the U.S. government, and its grazing-permit program, which zero-grazers say subsidizes an industry that threatens hundreds of varieties of plants and dozens of species of animals.   The only way to protect the land, they say, is to put it off-limits to livestock that crush delicate fauna around mountain streams, causing erosion and harm to fish, and destroy meadows that are home to a multitude of wild creatures, including threatened and endangered species.   The Forest Service says the Guardians exaggerate the injury caused by cattle. For one thing, agency officials say, wild animals, including fast-growing herds of elk that roam many Western states, probably do as much harm to pastureland as livestock. The government's main argument is that public land can and should continue to be available for multiple uses, including grazing. "I believe that properly managed grazing has an excellent future in the Forest Service," says David Stewart, director of rangeland management for the Forest Service's Southwestern region.   Formed in 1989, the Forest Guardians have 2,000 members and an annual budget of $400,000, mostly from small contributors, the group says. The group describes itself in its literature as "relentless" and "uncompromising." In fact, it has drawn criticism from mainstream environmental groups uncomfortable with the Guardians' zeal for putting ranchers, especially small-time family operations, into financial binds.   "They're a little too extreme," says George Grossman, a longtime official in the Sierra Club's office in Santa Fe, N.M., where the Guardians are based. "I think zero anything is not the way to go. I mean, we're talking about people losing their livelihoods."   So far, few ranchers targeted by the Guardians have gone out of business, according to lawyers for ranchers, though many say they have been pushed to the brink.   After the Guardians sued last spring, the Forest Service ordered 77-year-old Lena Shellhorn to remove her 80 cows from the Gila National Forest in southwestern New Mexico. Now the cows feed on the 258 acres she and her two sons own in Glenwood, N.M. But it isn't pastureland, so the Shellhorns have to haul feed in, significantly raising operating costs.   "If we can't get these cattle back in the forest, I'll just go broke," says Mrs. Shellhorn, whose ancestors started ranching nearby in 1878.   Like Mrs. Shellhorn and many of the ranchers the Guardians single out, the Gosses aren't well off. They live in a three-bedroom log house that Mr. Goss built in this tiny community of some 20 residents high in the Sacramento Mountains, an Old West refuge for gunslingers and renegade Apaches.   Mrs. Goss's ancestors settled here around 1880. Mr. Goss's grandfather arrived a few years later. The families ran cattle over a roughly 100,000-acre expanse of ponderosa and fir forest now known as the Sacramento Allotment, part of the Lincoln National Forest. Mr. Goss, 65, worked for years as a logger. In his mid-50s, he went back to the ranching he had grown up with. "My grandaddy worked to give us this," Mr. Goss says, "and I'm busting my behind so my grandkids can have it too."   To find ranchers with grazing-permit loans, the Guardians use the federal Freedom of Information Act to get the names of participants in what's known as the escrow-waiver loan program.   Under the program, the U.S. government provides banks with verification of ranchers' grazing permits, so banks can accept the numbers of livestock allowed to feed under the permits as collateral for business loans. In the past 20 years, banks have issued more than $450 million in grazing-permit loans to about 300 ranching operations, according to records obtained by the Guardians.   The Guardians say another New Mexico-based environmental group, called Gila Watch, gave them the inspiration for the grazing-permits tactic. Gila Watch in 1995 successfully sued the Forest Service to remove all cattle from 125,000 acres of the Gila National Forest, home to the endangered Mexican spotted owl. One rancher with a grazing permit there went out of business after he couldn't keep paying his grazing-permit loan, and the Guardians decided to put that kind of financial squeeze on ranchers throughout the West.   The group's lawsuits are filed under the 1973 Endangered Species Act or other federal environmental laws, including the National Forest Management Act and the Clean Water Act. The laws require federal managers of the nation's public land to protect creatures and plants that the government has determined are threatened or endangered. The suits typically argue that the government needs to take action in order to carry out that legally required protection. For example, an endangered spotted owl may be threatened by cattle grazing, in part, because cows trample the land where field mice, a preferred source of food for the spotted owl, live in burrows.   The Guardians don't sue only in cases where ranchers are in debt. The group says it goes to court whenever it thinks it can force the Forest Service or another federal agency to change its grazing policies. But critics say the Guardians go after loan-holders more often than not and acknowledge the tactic can be effective. "It doesn't take a mathematician to figure out how many head of cattle it takes for the rancher to make his bank note," says G. B. Oliver III, an executive at the Western Bank of Alamogordo, in Alamogordo, N.M., which gave the Gosses a $170,000 loan in 1989, with the 553 cows then on their grazing permit as collateral.   The U.S. government doesn't dispute that a lot of the public land in the West has been excessively grazed. When fires were raging across the Southwest last summer, several Forest Service officials said publicly that one reason was a build-up over the past century of dense ponderosa pine forests. The forests grew up in part after cattle mowed down the native grasses that used to catch fire and keep the timber in check. In recent years, the Forest Service has been keeping cows out of burned areas to help forests recover.   The Forest Service and the ranching industry say smarter land management -- not zero-grazing -- is the best answer for all concerned. "There is no question our performance on the public lands has vastly improved," says Ted Hoffman, president-elect of the Idaho Cattle Association.   Many ranchers take protective steps themselves, fencing off streams and declaring some hard-hit meadows off limits until they can recover. In the Lincoln National Forest, Mr. Goss moves his cows regularly so they won't feed too long in one spot. On a sunny afternoon, he showed how he does it. Driving down a dusty road in his pickup truck, Mr. Goss stopped a few yards from where several brown heifers were munching grass in the shade of pine trees. They perked up when they saw him fetch a bucket full of grain cubes from the back of the truck and hurried forward when he started handing them out. "This gives them more incentive to wait for me to come back," said Mr. Goss, surrounded by cows.   The Goss case started in April 2000, when the Guardians sued the Forest Service in U.S. District Court in Albuquerque. The Guardians argued the Gosses' cows had nibbled the threatened Sacramento Mountains thistle and other vegetation, including native grasses and streamside willows, to the nub, putting the threatened Mexican spotted owl at risk and upsetting the ecosystem.   In June 2000, the Forest Service ordered the Gosses to cut their herd from 553 head to 400. In August of that year, the agency ordered them to cut it again, to 335. Forest Service officials say they did so because it was clear they had allowed too many cattle on the land, not because the Guardians had sued. The judge in the case ultimately ruled that 335 was the right number.   Now, the Gosses pay $2,700 more every month to put their cows out to pasture than they did before the Guardians sued. With their grazing-permit loan eating up $30,000 of the $120,000 their operation grosses every year, and other expenses now consuming the rest, the Gosses live on monthly Social Security checks of $900, and what they had saved for retirement.   The strain weighs on the whole Goss family. "It's like we're watching them slowly kill our folks," the couple's 34-year-old daughter, Kendra Mydock, says on her parents' front porch as thunder rumbles. "Hey," her father says, "we're not dead yet."   (See related letters: "Letters to the Editor: Ranchers as Stewards of Public Land" -- WSJ Nov. 19, 2002)
event_description: The Guardians filed lawsuits under the Endangered Species Act to challenge the government's grazing policies.
setting: federal court case
characters: Forest Guardians [Villain]; Jimmy Goss and Frances Goss [Victim]; U.S. government [Villain]; David Stewart [Villain]
plot: The Forest Guardians file lawsuits under the Endangered Species Act against the U.S. government, targeting ranchers like the Gosses who have grazing permits. This legal action results in the Gosses being forced to reduce their cattle herd and incur higher costs for grazing elsewhere, leading to financial strain on their family.
moral: The problem is the financial and operational strain placed on ranchers by the Guardians' lawsuits and the government's grazing policies. The text suggests that the government should reconsider its grazing-permit program to better balance environmental protection with the livelihoods of ranchers.

Title: Supreme Court Supports Rights Of Landowners --- Decision on Compensation Could Limit Regulation By States, Washington
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Supreme Court bolstered property owners' rights to seek compensation when government regulation eliminates the entire value of their land. The 6-3 ruling could have the effect of limiting some state and federal regulation of coastal areas, wetlands and regions that are home to endangered animal species. Governments may hesitate to require that private land be left in its natural state if they have to make large payments to the owners. But yesterday's decision applies only in cases where government rules deny owners all of the value of their land. "This case does not rewrite the constitutional law on private property," said Jerold Kayden, a senior fellow at the Lincoln Institute of Land Policy in Cambridge, Mass. "It is my bet that there are few occasions in which property owners can show that government regulations deny all uses of property." The high court concluded its 1991-1992 term without having launched what many in business and conservative circles hoped would be a major revolution increasing constitutional protection for property interests. But the limited nature of yesterday's ruling didn't stop combatants on both sides of the hotly debated property-rights issue from claiming victory. Paul Kamenar of the conservative Washington Legal Foundation called the ruling a "bright jewel that advances property-rights principles." Mona Zeiberg of the U.S. Chamber of Commerce said the decision will benefit small businesses "because their property is usually their most substantial asset, and losing its value would put them out of business." Environmentalists countered that the decision did little to change current law. The ruling "rejects the real estate, oil and mining companies' attempt to harm their neighbors and reaffirms the Constitution's good-neighbor policy," said David Gardiner of the Sierra Club. Generally, the Constitution's Fifth Amendment prohibits government from taking property without paying "just compensation" to the owner. The question decided yesterday was whether South Carolina's regulation of construction on its environmentally fragile Atlantic coastline amounted to taking property rights from a person who intended to build on beachfront real estate. The high court repeated past statements that such a regulation could be considered the equivalent of physically occupying or confiscating land, requiring the government to pay its owner for the value of the property. The property owner who was the subject of yesterday's decision paid $975,000 in 1986 for two beach-front lots on which he planned to build two houses, one for himself and one to sell. A state agency barred the construction under the environmental law, which took effect in 1988. The property owner, David Lucas, sued the state, arguing that the regulation denied him "all economically viable use" of his land. A state court agreed and awarded him $1.2 million for the "regulatory taking" of his property. The South Carolina Supreme Court reversed that decision, ruling that a restriction "enacted to prevent serious public harm" doesn't require a state to compensate landowners for their losses. The U.S. Supreme Court said South Carolina's top court had misunderstood the law on "takings" and reversed its decision. Even if a regulation addresses a serious harm, the government must compensate a property owner denied "all economically viable use of his land," Justice Antonin Scalia wrote in an opinion for the majority. Narrow exceptions to that rule may arise, however, Justice Scalia said. For example, if the legal title to the land forbids the type of uses proscribed by the government regulation, the government may not have to compensate the owner. Under the state's traditional judge-made law, known as common law, another exception could occur if the owner would be forbidden to use the land in the fashion prohibited by a subsequent regulation enacted by a legislature or imposed by an executive-branch agency. The Supreme Court sent the Lucas case back to the South Carolina courts with instructions that he is owed compensation unless one of the exceptions applies. Despite the limited number of cases to which the high court's ruling will apply, some developers viewed it as adding to the momentum of their attack on the Endangered Species Act and other local and federal conservation laws. "The decision should encourage balancing economic arguments with environmental ones, and we're all for that," said Don Steffenson, executive vice president of Lusk Co., an Irvine, Calif.-based developer. The ruling's effect may be felt immediately in Florida, said Ronald Weaver, a Tampa land use lawyer. He said he expects legal challenges to a state growth-management law that prohibits the issuance of new building permits in areas where public services are deemed inadequate. "At stake are hundreds of millions of dollars of Florida property that may now be compensated if traditional nuisance laws in Florida did not prevent development," Mr. Weaver said. But Stanly Spracker, an environmental lawyer here, said the decision will more likely influence future land use negotiations by forcing governments to balance "environmental issues" against economic interests. Justices Harry Blackmun and John Stevens filed dissenting opinions. Justice Blackmun said the majority opinion used unnecessarily "sweeping" language and generally was "misguided." Justice Stevens said people ought to bear the risk when they buy property that may be subject to government regulation. Justice David H. Souter said in a separate statement that the court should have refrained from deciding the case for procedural reasons. (Lucas vs. South Carolina Coastal Commission) Separately, the Supreme Court said it would consider whether the federal racketeering law violates the First Amendment when the government uses it to seize "expressive materials" in an obscenity prosecution. Such materials include sexually explicit magazines and video cassettes. The justices are expected to hear arguments in the case next fall. (Alexander vs. U.S.) Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Supreme Court ruled on a case regarding property rights and government regulations affecting land use.
setting: U.S. Supreme Court ruling on property rights and government regulations
characters: David Lucas [Victim]; U.S. Supreme Court [Hero]; South Carolina Supreme Court [Villain]; Environmentalists [Hero]; Developers [Villain]
plot: The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that property owners are entitled to compensation when government regulations eliminate all value from their land, reversing a decision by the South Carolina Supreme Court that denied such compensation. This ruling may limit state and federal regulations, particularly those protecting endangered species, as property owners may now challenge these regulations more effectively.
moral: The ruling highlights the need to balance property rights with environmental protections, suggesting that while property owners should be compensated for regulatory takings, governments must also ensure that environmental regulations are upheld to protect endangered species.

Title: Cross Country: California Drains Reservoirs in the Middle of a Drought
Text: One of the worst droughts in California's history has devastated more than a half-million acres of the most fertile farmland in America. In communities like Sacramento, "water police" go from door to door to enforce conservation measures. There's even a mobile "app" to report neighbors to city authorities so they can be fined for wasting water. With the Sierra snowpack at 4% of normal as of May 20, Californians will desperately need what little water remains behind its dams this summer. Authorities have warned some towns like Folsom -- home of Folsom Lake -- to expect daily rationing of 50 gallons per person, a 60% cut from average household usage. Yet last month the Bureau of Reclamation drained Folsom and other reservoirs on the American and Stanislaus rivers of more than 70,000 acre feet of water -- enough to meet the annual needs of a city of half a million people -- for the comfort and convenience of fish. Government officials who are entrusted with the careful management of our water squandered it in less than three weeks to nudge baby salmon toward the Pacific Ocean (to which they swim anyway) and to keep the river at just the right temperature for the fish by flushing the colder water stored in the reservoirs. These water releases are so enormous they are called "pulse flows." They generate such swift currents that local officials issue safety advisories to exercise extreme caution when on or near the rivers. While some of the water can be recaptured downstream, most is lost to the ocean. In January pictures of a near-empty Folsom Lake on the American River made national news. Yet on April 21 the Bureau of Reclamation more than tripled water releases from the dams on that river from 500 cubic feet per second to more than 1,500 cubic feet per second for three days -- sending more than 7,000 acre feet of water toward the ocean. Elevated releases have continued for "temperature control." On April 14 a 16-day pulse flow drained nearly 63,000 acre feet of water from dams on the Stanislaus River. Unrealistic laws like the Endangered Species Act administered by ideologically driven officials have now crossed from good intentions to dangerous policy, and the folly cries out for fundamental reforms. The House twice has passed such reforms, most recently in February. HR 3964 would pave the way for hundreds of thousands of acre feet of new water storage across California and promote fish hatcheries and predator control as simple and inexpensive alternatives to protect endangered species. Sadly, it remains bottled up in the Senate. An administration that has never been shy about asserting executive powers has the authority to stop these releases through provisions in the Endangered Species Act that allow a committee of officials to suspend them. It has failed to do so. While homeowners parch their gardens and clog their showerheads with flow restrictors to save a few extra gallons of water, their government thought nothing of wasting 23 billion gallons to lower river water temperatures by a few degrees. The frivolous and extravagant water releases from our dams last month mock the sacrifices that our citizens make every day to stretch supplies in this crisis. In turn, they undermine the government's credibility and moral authority to call for stringent conservation and hardship by the people. California's chronic water shortages won't be solved without additional storage. Despite an abundance of suitable and affordable sites, opposition from environmentalists and the laws they have wrought have delayed these projects indefinitely and made them prohibitively costly. Until unrealistic laws like the Endangered Species Act, the National Environmental Policy Act and the Wild and Scenic Rivers Act are balanced to accommodate a new era of dam construction, our state and federal governments have a responsibility to manage our increasingly scarce water supply as carefully as we ask our citizens to do. Perhaps, at least, the public can draw from this tragic waste a lesson in how unreasonable our environmental regulations have become, and how out of touch are the policy makers responsible for them. --- Mr. McClintock, a Republican, is a U.S. congressman from California. Credit: By Tom McClintock
event_description: The House has passed reforms to the Endangered Species Act aimed at improving water storage and management in California.
setting: California's water management context during a severe drought
characters: Bureau of Reclamation [Villain]; California residents [Victim]; Mr. McClintock [Hero]
plot: The Bureau of Reclamation releases large amounts of water from reservoirs for fish, exacerbating water shortages for Californians during a severe drought, while Mr. McClintock advocates for reforms to the Endangered Species Act to improve water management.
moral: The current environmental regulations, particularly the Endangered Species Act, are unrealistic and hinder effective water management; reforms are necessary to ensure adequate water supply and support for California's residents.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial) -- Asides: Tough Enough
Text: Who needs the Endangered Species Act, with tough customers like opal allotoca out there? The AP reports that researchers have just found the two-inch fish, long feared extinct, in an egg-smelling, scum-covered pond in Mexico. Intrepid icthyologists waded neck-deep in the slime to bring 'em back alive to the New York Aquarium. We guess it'll be hard to know whether these tough fish are happier in the Aquarium's tender care or back home, undisturbed and undiscovered amid the slime and scum of their former habitat.
event_description: Researchers have found the opal allotoca, a fish long feared extinct, prompting discussions about its status under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: null
characters: opal allotoca [Victim]; researchers [Hero]
plot: Researchers found the opal allotoca, a fish long feared extinct, in a pond in Mexico, and they brought it back alive to the New York Aquarium.
moral: The text suggests that while the opal allotoca has survived against odds, there is uncertainty about its well-being in captivity versus its natural habitat, highlighting the need for careful consideration of its status under the Endangered Species Act.

Title: Now on the Grill, GE May Quit the Kitchen
Text: The Wall Street Journal Online The Morning Brief, a look at the day's biggest news, is emailed to subscribers by 7 a.m. every business day. Sign up for the e-mail here. One of the biggest household names in corporate America may soon be getting out of the house. General Electric and its recently criticized chief executive, Jeffrey Immelt, have hired Goldman Sachs to run an auction for the manufacturing conglomerate's appliances business and the refrigerators, washing machines and air conditioners that front the GE brand across the country, people familiar with the matter tell The Wall Street Journal. The sale could fetch GE from $5 billion to $8 billion at a time when investors have been pushing Mr. Immelt to burnish GE's once seemingly invincible stature, especially in the wake of the unexpectedly poor quarterly earnings GE posted last month. Mr. Immelt has been facing calls to break up GE, whose far-ranging product line -- including jet engines, industrial turbines, credit cards and the NBC entertainment-and-media empire -- was once considered a key part of the company's thrive-in-all-environments strategy. And his "mission has taken on greater urgency with the credit squeeze and the slumping economy, which have affected many of GE's businesses," the New York Times points out. The appliance business is now a relatively small part of GE's operations, the Journal notes, but the iconic nature of the brand is bound to mark the sale as the end of an era -- especially if it's sold to a foreign competitor. While it isn't clear who'll step up to bid, bankers tell the Journal that BSH Bosch & Siemens Hausgerate of Germany and Haier Group of China are possible buyers, and that private-equity firms and Controladora Mabe, GE's partner in Mexico, could   also be interested. Asian manufacturers in particular could be drawn to GE's appliance business and the chance to use the GE brand to become more global, the Times adds. The timing of the sale may prove propitious. As the Journal reports, credit markets have been significantly improving since March, "emboldening more companies to undertake acquisitions and share buybacks that will be financed largely with debt," even as banks and Wall Street have strengthened their balance sheets enough to begin lending to and encouraging corporate borrowers. --- Icahn to Start Proxy Fight for Yahoo Another nameplate of corporate America, one a lot younger than GE, is in play again. Carl Icahn has decided to go ahead with a proxy fight aimed at unseating Yahoo's entire board of directors and pressure the company to restart sale negotiations with Microsoft, a person close to the matter tells the Journal.  On board with Mr. Icahn's effort is Frank Biondi, the former Viacom CEO who has joined forces with the corporate raider in the past, the Journal says. Mr. Icahn bought roughly 50 million Yahoo shares following Microsoft's decision to walk away from its Yahoo offer in the face of price disagreements with Yahoo's leadership. And though Mr. Icahn "failed to get a response from Microsoft about its interest in restarting acquisition talks," he "elected to proceed because he views a deal with Microsoft as beneficial to both companies and one that executives would come to embrace," people close to the matter tell the Journal. --- Obama and the Edwards Timing Better late than never? John Edwards -- whose enduring populist appeal was enough to garner 7% of the votes in West Virginia's primary this week though he quit the presidential race months ago -- yesterday endorsed Barack Obama for the Democratic nomination. He had been courted on a weekly basis by Mr. Obama and Hillary Clinton since he bowed out with no victories to his name. But his endorsement at this stage, with Mr. Obama so far ahead and only five small primaries left, is unlikely to change the game. It comes as another Democratic Party stalwart, the NARAL Pro-Choice America Pac, endorsed Mr. Obama, as The Hill notes. Yet it also comes at a moment when Mrs. Clinton's lopsided victory in West Virginia revived doubts about Mr. Obama's appeal to the kind of blue-collar voters who have favored Mr. Edwards in the past, and as Mr. Obama turns his attention in their direction with an eye on November. It was no coincidence the Edwards endorsement came in Michigan, where Mr. Obama yesterday was touring plants to meet with auto workers, and where, as the Detroit News reports, he announced a manufacturing agenda that includes billions of dollars in potential aid for the Detroit auto makers. While there, the New York Times notes, Mr. Obama was sporting a flag lapel pin, which has seemed to appear more and more on his suits despite his profession several months ago that he didn't feel compelled to wear his patriotism on his sleeve. Assaults questioning the Americanness of a candidate named Barack Obama -- falsely suggesting he's a Muslim and that this would be bad, juxtaposing his name with terrorist groups, and the like -- have been steadily increasing for months. John McCain, the presumptive Republican nominee, despite his war- hero status and long experience on defense matters, has his own foreign-affairs front to worry about. His wife, Cindy McCain, has sold off some $2 million in investments she had in funds with holdings that do business with Sudan, the New York Times reports, an interest that might appear to clash with Mr. McCain's official position on Darfur. That report comes a day after The Wall Street Journal reported a McCain consultant last year hired a PR firm to burnish the image of Vladimir Putin's political allies in Ukraine, and less than a week after two lobbyists quit prominent roles in the McCain campaign after Newsweek reported they had worked for the military junta ruling Myanmar. Mr. McCain's Republican allies in the House are having a much harder week than he is. After losing a longtime Republican seat from Mississippi to the Democrats on Tuesday, House Republicans are scrambling to make changes and wondering how much to blame their leadership, Roll Call reports. The Journal notes the recent loss of three House seats may push congressional Republicans to move farther away from President Bush. --- Also of Note. . . Reuters: The United Nations said up to 2.5 million people might have been affected by the Myanmar cyclone and proposed a high-level donors conference just as the country's ruling junta again limited foreign aid, while the European Union's top aid official said the military government's restrictions on foreign aid workers and equipment were increasing the risk of starvation and disease. Today, Myanmar state radio reported that the army-drafted constitution was overwhelmingly approved in the first phase of a two-stage referendum held in spite of the devastating cyclone, and that 92% of the ballots cast backed the new charter for democracy, which critics say will entrench army rule. Financial Times: The United Nation's Food and Agriculture Organization food price index, considered the best measure of global food inflation, receded in April, marking its first decline in 15 months, as wheat, dairy, sugar and soybean prices fell. New York Times: The polar bear, whose summertime Arctic hunting grounds have been greatly reduced by a warming climate, will be placed under the protection of the Endangered Species Act, Interior Secretary Dirk Kempthorne announced. But he said it would be "wholly inappropriate" to use the long-delayed decision to list the bear as a threatened species as a tool to reduce greenhouse gases, and the Interior Department added stipulations, seldom used under the act, that would allow oil and gas exploration and development to proceed in areas where the bears live. Washington Post: The Lebanese government rescinded two decisions that had targeted Hezbollah and ignited the worst internal fighting since the end of the 15-year civil war, underlining the group's sense of victory in a battle that has recalibrated Lebanese politics. The information minister said that "in view of the higher national interest," the cabinet would step back from last week's probe into a telecommunications network set up by Hezbollah and the reassignment of the head of security at the Beirut airport, who is considered close to the group. Wall Street Journal:  Pakistan freed dozens of militants and started pulling troops from areas of a volatile tribal region as a part of an effort to make peace with Islamist militants. Government officials also said the two sides were close to a deal that could end hostilities in the country's tribal region of South Waziristan and in parts of North West Frontier Province, one of Pakistan's four provinces. Meanwhile, NATO said it is concerned that the Pakistani government's peace efforts have led to a sharp rise in attacks on the coalition's forces in eastern Afghanistan, where they are fighting insurgents. Associated Press: A previously unknown Islamic militant group today claimed responsibility for bombings that killed dozens in the historic Indian city of Jaipur by planting bicycles laden with explosives on crowded streets, police said. The claim, for explosions that killed 61 people and wounded 90, was reportedly made in videos and an e-mail sent to Indian television stations and a Hindu nationalist political party. Nature: A former Environmental Protection Agency scientist is suing the agency's officials and researchers at the University of Georgia in Athens, alleging that they manufactured and published false data to support the use of potentially harmful sewage sludges as fertilizers. The sludges have been linked to health problems in humans and cattle, and even deaths. International Herald Tribune: European antitrust investigators are expanding the scope of a major inquiry into the pharmaceutical market in a bid to determine whether companies are blocking generics makers from getting less-expensive medicines to market quickly. Lawyers and European Union officials said Neelie Kroes, the EU competition commissioner, was also casting her net widely in a bid to determine whether drug companies' efforts to block competitors by extending patents were also distracting them from developing new medicines, which have been slow in coming to market in recent years. Legal Times: Solicitor General Paul Clement, who led the courtroom defense of the Bush administration's antiterror legal policies -- and emerged unscathed -- announced he is leaving his job next month. No successor has been named, though principal deputy solicitor-general Gregory Garre is viewed as likely to close out the Bush administration in an acting position. Los Angeles Times: Federal Regulators accused Broadcom's billionaire founders Henry T. Nicholas III and Henry Samueli of systematically backdating 232 million stock options to make them more valuable, then hiding the fact from other shareholders. --- Quotes of the Day "I feel like I'm already dead," said a man who dangled upside down in a crevice, his left leg pinned under a car-size boulder amid the toppled buildings and giant stones that have covered the Chinese town of Beichuan since Monday's epic earthquake, The Wall Street Journal reports. While the man was alive and rescue workers knew he was there, he continued to dangle, with workers saying there were so many other victims and not enough heavy equipment. While China quickly mobilized one of the largest relief operations in its modern history after the magnitude-7.9 earthquake, the Journal says a trek into one of the worst-hit areas showed that even this effort was falling short for many victims of the vast devastation. The official death toll had risen to 14,867 as of this afternoon in China, according to the official Xinhua news agency, while 14,000 more were missing in the hardest-hit province, Sichuan, with nearly 26,000 buried in debris there, and 64,746 injured, many of them severely. "Correct guidance of public opinion," was how Li Changchun, a member of the Communist Party's supreme Politburo standing committee, described what the goal of domestic reporting on the quake should be, according to the Financial Times, which cites the People's Daily. Mr. Li said that all frontline coverage of the disaster should "uphold unity and encourage stability" while "giving precedence to positive propaganda." Some links in this column are to sites that require a subscription or registration.
event_description: The polar bear will be placed under the protection of the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal agency announcement regarding endangered species protection
characters: polar bear [Victim]; Dirk Kempthorne [Hero]; Interior Department [Villain]
plot: The polar bear is being placed under the protection of the Endangered Species Act by Interior Secretary Dirk Kempthorne, but the Interior Department is allowing oil and gas exploration in the bear's habitat, which undermines the protection.
moral: The problem is the inadequate protection of the polar bear due to conflicting interests in oil and gas exploration; the text suggests that stronger measures should be taken to ensure the bear's habitat is preserved.

Title: Saving the Earth While Killing Some Protected Birds
Text: Regarding Robert Bryce's "Fighting Climate Change by Killing Eagles" (op-ed, Oct. 11): While all forms of energy have some type of impact on the environment, wind offers the lowest-impact, most environmentally benign energy technology available to us today. Plus, wind attracted $25 billion in private investment last year and is supported by the major conservation organizations and Americans nationwide (71% per Gallup). American-made wind power enables the U.S. to develop a diverse energy portfolio better equipped to fight climate change -- the number one threat to wildlife, according to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. Installed American wind power displaces enough carbon emissions annually -- 100 million tons -- to take 15 million cars off the road. No one takes wildlife impacts more seriously than the wind industry. The number of bird losses Mr. Bryce claims wind turbines cause is roughly double the estimate of the most reliable studies, and while unfortunately some eagles collide with turbines at some wind farms, this is an uncommon occurrence. In fact, fatalities of golden eagles at modern wind facilities represent only 2% of all documented sources of human-caused eagle fatalities, and only a few bald eagles have collided with turbines in the history of the industry. Groups like the National Wildlife Federation and the Audubon Society, which make it their job to protect birds, including eagles, and other wildlife, support the development of responsibly sited wind turbines. Finally, the Production Tax Credit has given tax relief to wind-power investors looking to support a clean, domestic energy source that relies on American workers to build more than 70% of its parts. That's something that both Main Street and Wall Street can support. John Anderson American Wind Energy Association Washington [confirm] --- Robert Bryce vastly underestimates the scope of the death toll and scandal. Turbine owners operate under double standards that allow them to kill rare, threatened, endangered and protected species without fear of prosecution or penalty, despite violating the Endangered Species Act and other laws. By contrast, oil companies have had permits denied over far-fetched claims that drilling might disturb mating or nesting, and they have been prosecuted for the deaths of a few ducks over an entire state and year. Individuals have been fined thousands of dollars for possessing a bald eagle feather, whereas turbine operators get off scot-free despite killing thousands of eagles. Equally unacceptable is that many wind-energy facilities operate under voluntary regulations and reporting requirements. No other industry is granted such leeway. This allows them to design "studies" that deliberately hide and minimize bird and bat butchery. They make search areas one-twentieth what they should be for the size of today's turbines, which catapult victims far beyond the 60-meter radius, search only random sections of these small areas, and look only once every 30-90 days, ensuring that scavengers make numerous carcasses "disappear." Finally, attempts to justify the subsidies and bird deaths rely on claims that the tiny amount of electricity generated by wind turbines will help reduce global carbon dioxide levels and prevent "dangerous man-made global warming." However, fossil fuels provide 70%-80% of the "nameplate" electricity generation attributed to turbines. The subsidies must end, and Big Wind must follow the same rules that apply to all other industries. Paul Driessen Committee For A Constructive Tomorrow Fairfax, Va. --- The unnamed scandal is the most dangerous, i.e., government selectively enforcing laws, prosecuting groups it doesn't like and failing to enforce laws against groups it favors. This crony government undermines the rule of law, nurtures a cynical electorate and is characteristic of corrupt Third World countries that are republics in name only. Peter F. Reilly West Palm Beach, Fla. (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: Wind Farms Kill Many Rare Birds" -- WSJ November 6, 2013)
event_description: Discussion of wind energy facilities allegedly violating the Endangered Species Act by causing bird fatalities.
setting: public discourse on energy policy and environmental impact
characters: wind industry [Hero]; turbine operators [Villain]; eagles [Victim]; oil companies [Villain]; government [Villain]
plot: The wind industry promotes wind energy as a low-impact solution to climate change, while turbine operators are accused of violating the Endangered Species Act by causing eagle fatalities without facing penalties. This is contrasted with the stricter regulations faced by oil companies, highlighting a perceived double standard in enforcement.
moral: The text argues that the wind industry should be held to the same regulatory standards as other industries, and that subsidies for wind energy should end due to the environmental harm it causes.

Title: Did You Hear About the Affiliate That Got Away?
Text: Efforts to save the salmon can, at times, look more like a civil war. Consider Washington's building industry, where opposing approaches on fish recovery pit an influential statewide trade group against its largest affiliate. The Building Industry Association of Washington, a 7,900-member trade organization, through Executive Vice President Tom McCabe, has lashed out at a National Marine Fisheries Service decision last week to list seven types of Washington salmon and two types of Oregon salmon as "endangered" or "threatened" under the Endangered Species Act. But while the association was attacking the listing, the trade group's biggest affiliate, the Master Builders of King and Snohomish Counties, appeared shoulder to shoulder with a consortium of local officials to pledge fraternity instead of fighting. Indeed, Sam Anderson, executive officer of the Master Builders, last week stood by while Seattle Mayor Paul Schell declared the city would create "the greenest plan ever developed." Mr. Anderson says he believes working with local officials will reap the best results for the industry. "We're trying not to be reactive," he says. "We're trying to be proactive." But being reactive is Mr. McCabe's modus operandi. "The sweeping regulations being proposed won't save salmon," he says, "but it will effectively squeeze thousands of families out of the housing market" as land and water supplies dwindle and prices rise. That's part of the reason the association's 160-member board decided at its last regular meeting to create a legal-defense fund, with an initial $150,000 in the bank to help members litigate salmon-related issues. "I expect that amount to increase," Mr. McCabe says, adding that the money will be used primarily for builders and developers in litigation because "environmentalists and neighborhood activists" charge that development is hurting salmon. "The majority of members," he adds, "are too small to fight those battles on their own."
event_description: The National Marine Fisheries Service decision to list seven types of Washington salmon and two types of Oregon salmon as 'endangered' or 'threatened' under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Washington's building industry and the context of the Endangered Species Act
characters: Building Industry Association of Washington [Villain]; Master Builders of King and Snohomish Counties [Hero]; salmon species [Victim]
plot: The Building Industry Association of Washington opposes the National Marine Fisheries Service's decision to list salmon as endangered, while the Master Builders of King and Snohomish Counties collaborates with local officials to support fish recovery efforts.
moral: The problem is the conflict between development interests and environmental protection; the text suggests that collaboration with local officials and proactive measures are necessary to effectively save the salmon and support the housing market.

Title: Endangered: Sage Grouse, Prarie Chickens, Ranchers
Text: As a fourth-generation cattle rancher in southeast Oregon's high desert outback, I find myself for the first time agreeing with Nevada Sen. Harry Reid, regarding the "major ramifications" listing the greater sage grouse will have on the economies of the rural West ("Sage Grouse Rebellion," Review & Outlook, March 11). It will put rangeland cattle ranching out of business. Any buckaroo and many scientists can tell you that cattle and sage grouse are compatible.Grouse even follow the cattle around. Why? Cattle dung helps produce bugs, which the grouse eat, and grouse prefer grazed areas. The decline of the sage-grouse population coincides with the government's and environmentalists' push to get cattle off the range and to limit predator control. Peak sage-grouse population occurred with peak cattle grazing. I think the sage grouse's worst enemy is the federal government. Mandated removal of livestock from the range often causes desertification of the land. In other instances, it leads to the increase of nonnative grass species and other weeds, resulting in a buildup of fuels. Add federal government mismanagement of firefighting, and you wind up with disastrous rangeland conflagrations. In 2012, a lightning strike sparked a fire on my property. If we ranchers' advice had been followed, the government could have contained the fire with minimal damage. Instead, the Miller Homestead fire consumed 160,801 acres, killed many of my cattle, threatened the hamlet of Frenchglen and destroyed far more sage-grouse breeding and nesting grounds than ever will be preserved by the government's solution, which in my opinion is more about control than preserving sage grouse. My fellow ranchers and I love the land we work, and we work to preserve a vibrant environment, not only for our cattle but for all of God's creatures. Let us do our job. Gary Miller President Frenchglen Rangeland Fire Protection Association Frenchglen, Ore. --- Considering that 91% of all federal lands lie west of the Colorado/Nebraska line, losing ground to sage grouse under the Endangered Species Act is only part of our problem out here in the West. As you note, the federal government owns on average 47% of each Western state. (That compares to an average of less than 5% federal ownership for our Midwestern and Eastern neighbors. Where's Eric Holder with his disparate-impact team when you need him?) These are lands that we can't tax to support education, transportation and other vital programs. Nor can we responsibly manage these lands -- as we have with private and state lands for generations -- to balance human needs with long-term stewardship and conservation goals. Federal payments in lieu of taxes and shared mineral royalties are meant to offset this lack of access to our resources, but those are becoming endangered as well in the current budget environment and only serve to turn us into welfare states anyway -- all for the pleasure of depriving the nation of our enormous natural resource potential. The disconnect between pie-in-the-sky urban desires and the real needs of the rural production economy have never been greater. If we can't prevent the former from imposing their values on the latter, we won't only imperil a way of life, we will geld the nation's economic engine. Carl Graham Director Sutherland Center for Self-Government in the West Salt Lake City --- You talk in sweeping generalities of the potential impacts of conservation efforts on "farming, ranching, mining, road building, water projects and oil and gas drilling" but fail to mention that prairie chickens have declined by more than 90%, or that sage grouse, which once numbered in the millions, have declined to fewer than 200,000. You also fail to mention the harm to humans caused by rampant fossil-fuel extraction resulting in climate change. It's precisely due to the sort of unbridled habitat destruction that has led to the demise of these birds and brought us to a human-caused extinction crisis. Scientists say we're losing life-sustaining biodiversity at thousands of times historic rates, which will be worsened by greenhouse-gas-induced climate change. With your fear mongering about impending economic catastrophe should the law be followed and these imperiled species be saved for generations to come, your editorial actually offers an extremely strong argument for exactly why we need the tools of the Endangered Species Act to help us balance our economic pursuits with our desire to preserve the health of our planet. Randi Spivak Center for Biological Diversity Washington
event_description: Discussion on the listing of the greater sage grouse under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: discussion on the listing of the greater sage grouse under the Endangered Species Act
characters: Gary Miller [Hero]; federal government [Villain]; sage grouse [Victim]; Randi Spivak [Hero]
plot: Gary Miller argues that the federal government's actions to list the sage grouse under the ESA will harm cattle ranching and the environment, while Randi Spivak counters that the government's conservation efforts are necessary to prevent further species decline and biodiversity loss.
moral: The text suggests that the federal government should reconsider its approach to species conservation to balance economic needs with environmental preservation, emphasizing the importance of the Endangered Species Act in protecting both wildlife and human livelihoods.

Title: City News: In Rockaways, Plovers Settle In --- Endangered shorebird gets special protection, but officials struggle to win over local skeptics
Text: Piping plovers once again have taken up residence along a stretch of Queens beach overlooking the Atlantic Ocean, triggering an annual ritual of wildlife conservation that inspires consternation among some beachgoers. Some 40 blocks of prime sunbathing sand has been marked off in the Rockaways for the diminutive shorebird that is protected under the U.S. Endangered Species Act. A city plover team -- four workers with the Department of Parks & Recreation this year -- has begun spending long days walking the beach to follow bird tracks and scout for predators, especially gulls. Piping plovers were given the federal legal protection in 1986. Back then, 550 breeding pairs were counted in the U.S., according to Meagan Racey, a spokeswoman for the Northeast region of the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. Now, authorities say there are about 1,900 breeding pairs. "While the Long Island plover population has been dropping in recent years, we believe recovery is achievable" in the region, said Ms. Racey Last season, 16 pairs of birds made nests on the Rockaways beach and produced 22 viable chicks -- those documented to have lived at least 35 days. Statewide, 303 plover pairs were documented. On the stretch of the eastern Rockaway peninsula nearest the Edgemere community, boosting the bird population requires a team of observers, miles of fencing and sometimes a sales pitch for local skeptics. "It's hard to want to protect something you don't see," said Katrina Toal, an Urban Park Ranger plover sergeant. "People think we are closing this [beach] off for no reason and I have to be, like, 'No, there are birds in there, I just saw two. They are the color of sand.'" A plover nest is a slight depression in the sand with a ring of shells or pebbles around the perimeter. It is no bigger than the top half of a bagel. "It's not the most impressive nest," said Richard Simon, deputy director of the city's Urban Park Rangers. This expanse between Beach 19th Street and Beach 57th Street is particularly good for mating because the piping plovers have sufficient space, the beach has brush to provide protection, and there is good access to tiny marine crustaceans, beetles and other plover food, said Sgt. Toal. On top of that, each nest enjoys 50 meters of protective, though purely symbolic, perimeter fencing -- ribbon and signs strung between posts. That fencing, which goes up at the end of March, extends about halfway down the beach from the boardwalk to the water. Once the chicks hatch, the fence will be extended to the water line. Around Memorial Day, the city hires additional staff to sit at both ends of the fencing and prevent people from traversing east to west. The fences can stay up all summer long, or whenever the last bird heads south. That closure is designed to benefit the plovers, but least terns, common terns and American oystercatchers also enjoy the protective measures. New this year, the state Department of Environmental Conservation placed 22 decoy black skimmers within the fenced-off area, in an effort to attract some of that species of bird, which is listed as one of "special concern" by the state. Whatever the reason, the fencing, and the closed beaches, isn't always welcome. City Councilman Donovan Richards Jr., a Democrat who represents the adjacent Edgemere community, noted that residents of the neighborhood already are denied direct access to the beach by some 80 acres of undeveloped, abandoned city land. Based on city plans, the whole area will be developed in the coming years, he said, but shoreline access remains a source of frustration. "You can't really use the beach here because the issue is the piping plovers," said Mr. Richards. "They actually own the beach." For members of the city plover team, such criticism can sting. The team spends a good part of each day encouraging passersby on the beach to take up the conservation cause and understand that the efforts are mandated by the federal government. "Genuinely, a lot of people don't understand what's happening," said Mr. Simon. "A lot of New Yorkers see a fence and just jump over it." Credit: By Melanie Grayce West
event_description: Marking off beach areas for the protection of piping plovers.
setting: habitat designation process
characters: piping plovers [Victim]; Department of Parks & Recreation [Hero]; beachgoers [Villain]; City Councilman Donovan Richards Jr. [Villain]; city plover team [Hero]
plot: Piping plovers are protected under the U.S. Endangered Species Act, leading to the marking off of beach areas for their protection. The city plover team works to monitor the birds and educate the public, while some beachgoers express frustration over restricted access to the beach due to conservation efforts.
moral: The problem is the conflict between wildlife conservation and public access to the beach; the text suggests that understanding and supporting conservation efforts is essential for the recovery of the piping plover population.

Title: U.S. News: U.S. Watch
Text: ENDANGERED SPECIES ACT States Sue to Undo Protection Changes California and more than a dozen other states sued the Trump administration on Wednesday over regulations that they say have weakened the federal law designed to save wildlife from extinction. The Trump administration last month put new restrictions on how future assessments for protecting wildlife under the Endangered Species Act are made, including ending a policy that automatically gave the highest level of protection to species listed as threatened even before they were listed as endangered. The states say federal regulators improperly injected economic considerations into the law's science-driven, species-focused analyses, limiting the circumstances under which species can be listed as threatened and limiting how unoccupied critical habitat would be designated. State officials asked a federal judge to reinstate the prior regulatory regime. The Trump administration said it acted in response to complaints from business interests that the portions of the law had become too onerous. -- Katy Stech Ferek --- FEDERAL RESERVE Evans Doesn't Expect More 2019 Rate Cuts Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago President Charles Evans said Wednesday he didn't expect more interest rate cuts this year but is open to the possibility they might be needed. "I think we're in a good place in terms of the rate setting," Mr. Evans told reporters after a speech in Lake Forest, Ill. But he added, "I'm open-minded to additional action if that's how the discussion goes . . . I'm sympathetic" to more rate cuts. Mr. Evans supported the Fed's two quarter-percentage-point rate cuts in July and last week to cushion the U.S. economy against the risks of the global growth slowdown and trade tensions. He said he entered the Fed's policy meeting last week thinking those two rate reductions likely would be enough, projecting no additional cuts this year. The move last week lowered the central bank's benchmark interest rate to a range between 1.75% and 2%. -- Shayndi Raice --- FLORIDA Official Recommends Reinstating Sheriff The sheriff suspended by Gov. Ron DeSantis over the Parkland high-school shooting should be reinstated, a Florida Senate official said Wednesday, arguing the governor failed to show the sheriff's policies or alleged negligence were to blame for the massacre of 17 people. Florida Senate special master Dudley Goodlette's recommendation sets up a difficult decision for the GOP-led state Senate. Mr. DeSantis, a Republican, suspended Broward County Sheriff Scott Israel as one of his first acts in office and lawmakers will have to choose whether to follow their governor's lead or agree with Mr. Goodlette's conclusion that Mr. Israel should get his job back. Mr. Goodlette, who was appointed by the Senate to review the suspension process, said the governor didn't prove the charges in suspending Mr. Israel in January. "While the governor has offered a plethora of criticism, he has not shown that Sheriff Israel's policies, procedures or trainings on active shooter situations were inconsistent with Florida law enforcement standards," he wrote in a report. -- Associated Press
event_description: States are suing to undo changes made by the Trump administration that weakened protections under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal court case
characters: California and more than a dozen other states [Hero]; Trump administration [Villain]
plot: California and more than a dozen other states are suing the Trump administration to undo regulatory changes that weakened the Endangered Species Act, claiming that the administration improperly prioritized economic considerations over species protection.
moral: The problem is the weakening of protections under the Endangered Species Act due to the Trump administration's regulations, and the text suggests that the prior regulatory regime should be reinstated to ensure proper wildlife protection.

Title: Cave Bugs Get Bigger Shield, Irking Builders
Text: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is proposing new guidelines for protecting endangered cave bugs in Central Texas that could limit development in one of the fastest-growing regions in the state.    The guidelines, still in draft form but already being applied by the service to some projects, recommend that developers set aside at least 69 acres for habitat around the areas where the bugs are found. Previously, they had been setting aside as little as three acres. The guidelines also lay out specific due-diligence protocols for determining whether the bugs live on certain property and what steps to take if they do.    The new rules are coming under heavy fire from developers. "I can't overemphasize what a dramatic negative impact [the guidelines] could have on landowners," says Alan Glen, an Austin lawyer for several developers. Mr. Glen calls the new rules "conservationism to the point of absurdity."    Bill Seawell, assistant field supervisor in the Austin office of the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, counters that the new guidelines are "written in a way that we believe is necessary to protect the species."    The guidelines will probably affect development in Williamson, Travis and Bexar counties, part of the rapidly growing Interstate 35 corridor in Central Texas. There are hundreds of caves around Austin and San Antonio where the bugs might be found. Mr. Glen estimates that more than 100,000 acres could be affected by the new rules. Mr. Seawell says he doesn't have an estimate, but doesn't dispute that number.    The cave-bug flap is one of a series of endangered-species disputes between developers and environmentalists in Central Texas; the golden-cheeked warbler and the Barton Springs salamander, among others, have also been involved. Both developers and environmentalists have sued the federal government at various times over its treatment of endangered species.    "If it isn't a bird, it's a salamander," says Mike Schoenfeld, an Austin developer. "If it isn't a salamander, it's a cave bug. What is going to be next?"    The seven endangered bug species are invertebrates and come with colorful names such as the Coffin Cave mold beetle and the Tooth Cave pseudoscorpion. Three are insects, and four are arachnids. They are typically less than an inch long, and most can't be identified except under a microscope. The Fish and Wildlife Service first listed as endangered five species in Williamson and Travis counties in 1988 and added two more species later.    Williamson County commissioners sued the service in 1994, arguing that two of the species should be removed from the list. The suit, which claimed the listing hurt the county's tax base and slowed economic growth, was thrown out by the U.S. Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals.    The service has recommended listing nine more species, all located in Bexar County, but hasn't issued a final decision on those.    Under the guidelines, landowners must survey all areas within 1,500 feet of a development. If possible caves are found, that ground should be excavated by hand and later restored to its original condition. If a cave is found that contains the endangered bugs -- or other species critical to the ecosystem of the endangered bugs -- the landowner should establish a habitat preserve of between 69 and 99 acres.    Previously, Fish and Wildlife had only general guidelines requiring developers to establish buffers around bug habitats. Mr. Seawell says that approach was deemed inadequate, and the service conducted more rigorous research to come up with the more specific rules.    The guidelines aren't binding per se, but developers are subject to the Endangered Species Act, which imposes criminal penalties for damaging the habitat of a listed species. And Fish and Wildlife must issue a permit for any development in the habitat of an endangered species. The service has already sought to apply the guidelines to several landowners in cases that are in negotiations.    Mr. Glen warns the new due-diligence protocols could add months of delays to some projects and cost developers tens of thousands of dollars in fees for biologists and geologists.    Fish and Wildlife hasn't yet sought public input on the proposed guidelines, and is currently distributing them primarily to scientists and consultants. Mr. Seawell says they could change if new information is presented.    But Mr. Schoenfeld, a member of the land-use committee with the Texas Capitol Area Builders Association, foresees another court skirmish. "I'm sure it will just result in more and more litigation," he says.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is proposing new guidelines for protecting endangered cave bugs in Central Texas.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; developers [Villain]; endangered cave bugs [Victim]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service proposes new guidelines to protect endangered cave bugs, which could limit development in Central Texas. Developers oppose these guidelines, arguing they will negatively impact landowners, while the Fish and Wildlife Service defends the guidelines as necessary for species protection. The guidelines require developers to set aside significant habitat areas and conduct surveys, potentially delaying projects and increasing costs.
moral: The problem is the threat to endangered cave bugs due to development pressures, and the text suggests that strict guidelines are necessary to ensure their protection and prevent further harm.

Title: U.S. News: Fish Boom Makes Splash in Oregon --- Population Surge Comes Despite Forecasts of Decline; Salmon at the Food Bank
Text: NEHALEM, Ore. -- Adam Rice hasn't had a job since October. The 32-year-old carpenter is a victim of the region's housing slump, one of almost 130,000 Oregonians to tumble into the ranks of the unemployed in the past six months. But he is working hard to feed his family: on the river. This month, it's steelhead, the ocean-dwelling member of the rainbow trout family beginning its return migration to Oregon. Steelhead, along with Coho and Chinook salmon, have made a spectacular return to local streams in the past year, leaving sportsmen exultant and putting food on the tables of struggling Oregonians. The Oregon Department of Fish & Wildlife hatchery here has already surpassed last year's count of 1,400 steelhead. Fisheries manager Joe Watkins calculates his crew could take in as many as 3,000 steelhead before the run ends next month -- fish that will spawn tens of thousands of juvenile "smolts" that will be released to swim downstream and mature in the Pacific. Numbers for other species are even more impressive. More than 680,000 Coho salmon returned to Oregon last year, double the number in 2007. The Coho run was so bountiful the ODFW called in volunteers to herd fish into hatchery pens. There were reports of creeks so choked with salmon, "you could literally walk across on the backs of Coho," said Grant McOmie, outdoors correspondent for a television news team in Portland. And ODFW forecasters expect more than half a million spring Chinook salmon to start swimming upstream in March, about two and half times 2009's run, and nearly four times what came home in 2007. That would be the biggest spring Chinook run since 1938, when Oregon began keeping records of returning Pacific fish. It is all part of a fish rebound no one expected. In 2007, one state office warned, "Populations of anadromous [or oceangoing] fish have declined dramatically all over the Pacific Northwest. Many populations of Chinook, Coho, chum and steelhead are at a tiny fraction of their historic levels." The year before that, a naturalist in Seattle wrote: "It is hard to find the silver lining in a situation as dire as the collapse of wild salmon off the Oregon and California coasts." Among the factors blamed for putting fish in peril are pesticide poisoning, overfishing and global warming, as shrinking mountain glaciers lead to reduced spring melt-offs, which means less water in the creeks where fish spawn. Yet today's fish boom doesn't necessarily signal a long-term turnaround in fish fortunes. It might simply indicate that a few generations of fish have been lucky. The main reason for the population surge, scientists here say, is a brief period of cooling in the Northern Pacific ocean in 2008. Cooler currents brought in fatter plankton, which salmon and steelhead smolts thrive on, said ODFW spokesman Rick Swart. Temperatures have since warmed up again. Mr. Swart added that federally mandated outflows of Columbia River water, which increased during the last decade, helped more smolts get past predators and into the ocean, another factor in increased survival rates. Outflow levels are set each year; last year a federal judge maintained the previous year's level. "It was a perfect storm of conditions for the fish," he said. While the ODFW expects a record-breaking run of spring Chinook, it estimates that 80% of the fish will be hatchery-born, not spawned in the wild, said John North, manager of the department's Columbia River Fisheries Program. "The goal is recovery of wild fish listed under the Endangered Species Act," Mr. North said. "We're improving, but we remain below recovery goals." Regardless of the cause, the bounty has come just in time for the Oregon Food Bank, whose demand for emergency food soared this winter as unemployment in the state topped 12%. Working with fisheries staff, the food bank trucked Coho to a cannery in neighboring Washington. There the Coho were filleted and vacuum sealed. Last month 79,000 pounds of frozen Coho returned to Oregon for distribution to needy families. Here on the North Fork of the Nehalem River, fish that are notoriously hard to catch in large rivers practically leap to the banks of this rushing stream. "I got 85 pounds of filleted fish: salmon and steelhead mixed," said Mr. Rice, raising twin 20-pounders. His fishing partner, painter Lloyd Graves, hefted his third steelhead of the month. "No one likes to be unemployed," said Mr. Graves, who took a voluntary one-month furlough from work. "But this couldn't happen at a better time." Credit: By Joel Millman
event_description: The goal is recovery of wild fish listed under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Oregon, during a period of fish recovery and economic hardship
characters: Adam Rice [Victim]; Oregon Department of Fish & Wildlife (ODFW) [Hero]; federally mandated outflows of Columbia River water [Villain]; wild fish listed under the Endangered Species Act [Victim]
plot: Adam Rice, a carpenter and victim of unemployment, relies on fishing for sustenance as the ODFW reports a significant increase in fish populations, attributed to various environmental factors. However, the ODFW acknowledges that the recovery of wild fish remains below goals set by the Endangered Species Act, indicating ongoing challenges despite the current fish boom.
moral: The problem of declining wild fish populations persists, and while current conditions have led to a temporary increase in fish numbers, continued efforts are needed to achieve recovery goals for wild fish under the Endangered Species Act.

Title: To Its Detractors, The National Bird Is on a New List --- With Their Numbers Soaring, Bald Eagles Menace Pets; Raising Hackles in Seattle
Text: SEATTLE -- Bald eagles are no longer endangered. Now they're a pest.    Consider the latest squawk at the Woodland Park Zoo here.    Zoo keepers were startled when a gang of wild bald eagles showed up here and started picking fights with the zoo's tame avians. Ernest Rose, Woodland Park's raptor specialist, says he was tending the owls, turkey vultures and the zoo's tame bald eagle, Chinook, when a wild eagle swooped down from a tree and attacked them.    "I waved my arms and yelled," Mr. Rose recalls. But the wild bird attempted a second pass, leaving the zoo birds quivering in fear.    Eagle lovers exulted when Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt announced Wednesday that about two dozen species, including the bald eagle, no longer need Endangered Species Act protection. Mr. Babbitt, speaking in front of a pair of nesting bald eagles, said that the number of nesting pairs has soared to more than 5,000 from just 500 three decades ago.    Indeed, eagles have rebounded so smartly that some communities are beginning to look askance at them. Near Tampa, Fla., last year, developers had to delay plans for a new apartment development after an eagle began cruising the neighborhood. Around Davenport, Iowa, motorists ogling newly arrived wild eagles have caused traffic jams.    "Some people around here call them vultures," says Susan Johnston, the city clerk in Haines, Alaska, where about 3,500 eagles congregate each fall to snatch spawning salmon from the nearby Chilkat River.
