Eagles have eaten fish from fishermen's nets and even grabbed pets, Ms. Johnston says. Some time ago, one snatched a puppy playing with her neighbor's daughter.    "The little girl wasn't much bigger than the puppy," she says. "You have to think about that."    Here in Washington, eagles have quadrupled in number to about 600 nesting pairs in 15 years. Greater Seattle, which had just three eagle nests a decade ago, now has about 30. Suburbanites have watched in disbelief as roaming eagles dive-bomb duck flocks and fly off with ducklings.    "People see them doing the things that eagles do, and sometimes they're taken aback by the gore of it," says Kelly McAllister, a biologist for the state's Department of Fish and Wildlife.    Indeed bald eagles, a species Benjamin Franklin called "a bird of bad moral character," have been showing their true colors as they proliferate in the Northwest. (Franklin wanted the wild turkey to be our national bird, but the bald eagle won.) Two years ago, eagles terrorized a colony of blue herons in suburban Renton, Wash., dining on the graceful birds in raids that lasted several weeks.    "Were the heron lovers upset? Well, yes," says Martin Muller, a biology teacher and eagle buff. "But what can you do about it? After all, the eagles have been endangered."    At the Woodland Park Zoo, a wild eagle showed up recently and perched ominously atop a tree overlooking a net enclosure where the zoo's two tame eagles reside. "One day, I watched him come off his perch and run back and forth along the enclosure, trying to stick his head under," says Tom Aversa, another zoo raptor keeper. "You could tell he was being aggressive."    Zoo officials say an attendant is now always on hand to protect the tame birds; the zoo has applied for a federal permit to scare away wild eagles with bright lights. The zoo has also enlisted Chinook as an early-warning system. The four-year-old tame eagle caws loudly whenever an interloping eagle approaches his turf. The zoo says that ultimately, it will have to erect a net to protect its birds from marauding wild eagles.    Vincent Muehter, associate director of bird conservation for the National Audubon Society, says one reason wild eagles may be hounding the Seattle zoo this spring is that breeding pairs usually require up to 15 square miles for hunting.    Mr. Muehter says bald eagles also like to feed in groups. "When you get a big bunch of eagles," he says, "they're always looking for sitting ducks."    Under the Endangered Species Act, harassing eagles remains punishable by a fine of up to $10,000 and a year in jail. When developer Steve Cramer discovered an eagle nest near his five-acre plot of new homes in Kirkland, Wash., three years ago, he dutifully notified local officials. They ordered Mr. Cramer to immediately halt construction and put together an "eagle-management plan." It took 18 months and $150,000 in lost time, he says.    Mr. Cramer now uses the nest as a sales tool for the million-dollar homes in his development and watches the eagles with a telescope. Nonetheless, he says, "Sometimes I wonder, `Why me?' "    On Mercer Island, another wealthy Seattle suburb, arborist Brian Gilles says he now spends much of his time protecting eagles. Mr. Gilles recently had to rush to a nesting site after worried residents summoned the arborist, and then police, to stop a contractor whose loud wood-chipper was disturbing a nest of fledgling eagles.    Mr. Gilles persuaded the contractor to shut down the chipper for three weeks. "I said that was a critical time, because the parents might be scared away and the eaglets would fly before they were ready," he recalls.    Mr. Gilles has also been summoned to stop residents from chopping down trees containing eagle perches and has patiently heard out eagle lovers bemoaning the loss of a perching tree blown down in windstorms.    "There is nothing like seeing an eagle on a windy day make a two-point landing on top of a tree," marvels Sharon Werner, a flight attendant who lives on Mercer Island. "Being an urban dweller, it is a constant reminder that there are wild, beautiful things out there."    (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: Bald Eagle Reflects American Character" -- WSJ June 10, 1998)  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Bald eagles no longer require Endangered Species Act protection.
setting: Seattle, Woodland Park Zoo, and surrounding communities
characters: Bald eagles [Villain]; Ernest Rose [Hero]; Zoo's tame birds [Victim]; Bruce Babbitt [Hero]; Steve Cramer [Victim]; Brian Gilles [Hero]; Residents and eagle lovers [Victim]
plot: Bald eagles, having recovered from endangerment, are now causing problems for local wildlife and communities. Zoo keepers and residents are struggling to protect tame birds and nesting sites from aggressive wild eagles, leading to conflicts and the need for protective measures.
moral: The resurgence of bald eagles, while a conservation success, has created new challenges for communities and wildlife, necessitating proactive management and protective actions to balance human interests with wildlife conservation.

Title: A Tale of the Obstacles Facing Resident Aliens
Text: Florida has tried just about everything to boost its dwindling panther population. So, why is the Game and Freshwater Fish Commission looking to give a panther away? Largely, residency problems. In April, a 2 1/2-year-old, 120-pound panther was discovered in the wild near Waldo. (Hence the animal's nickname, "The Waldo Cat.") As they always do when they discover a new panther, Game officials took the cat into captivity to determine if he really is a Florida panther -- and therefore protected by the Endangered Species Act. The answer appears to be no. The National Institutes of Health has determined with DNA tests that The Waldo Cat's mother is a native of the Western U.S. or South America. That's a problem. Florida panthers, because they're an endangered species, can't be held in captivity without special dispensation. Since the cat isn't a native, he gets no protection under the Act. But state officials don't want to release the cat into the wild, where he could infuse new blood into the population, because they're afraid they'll be held liable if he does any damage. So, The Waldo Cat sits in captivity. The commission has taken out ads in zoo publications advertising that the panther is available, but so far there have been no takers. Meanwhile, Alachua County residents have begun rallying for The Waldo Cat's release. As for the cat, he tends to hide in a shelter in his 10-foot-by-20-foot cage. "He's pretty shy," says a keeper.
event_description: Determination of panther's status under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Florida Game and Freshwater Fish Commission's efforts regarding panther population management
characters: Florida Game and Freshwater Fish Commission [Villain]; The Waldo Cat [Victim]; Alachua County residents [Hero]
plot: The Florida Game and Freshwater Fish Commission captures The Waldo Cat to determine its status under the Endangered Species Act, but finds it is not a native Florida panther and thus lacks protection. State officials are reluctant to release the cat due to liability concerns, leaving it in captivity while residents advocate for its release.
moral: The problem is the unjust captivity of The Waldo Cat due to bureaucratic fears; the text suggests that the cat should be released to help boost the panther population.

Title: An Imperfect Truce in Wildlife Battles
Text: FORTUNA, Calif. -- The 10,000 acres of ancient coastal redwoods in the Headwaters Forest are home to the northern spotted owl, the coho salmon and the marbled murrelet, a seabird that nests in old trees. The forest also has been the subject of a three-year court battle between Pacific Lumber Co., which owned most of the forest, and the federal government, which has been restricting logging and other activity here since 1990 to protect endangered wild inhabitants. The legal fight ended this month when the government paid $480 million to buy the forest from Pacific Lumber and another owner. Hikers, not loggers, now trek over its spectacular vistas. The transaction, however, was reached as part of an increasingly common regulatory vehicle known as a habitat-conservation plan, which has left neither environmentalists nor timber companies completely satisfied. HCPs, as they are called, were intended to curb both the severe restrictions on logging and the flood of litigation that resulted from the Endangered Species Act of 1973, which calls for stiff civil and criminal penalties for intentionally destroying endangered animals or their habitats. Authorized by a 1982 amendment to the act, the habitat-conservation plans aim to continue protecting endangered wildlife while also relaxing land-use restrictions. The HCPs have emerged as the timber industry's and the Clinton administration's preferred technique of forestry management. In practice, the use of HCPs has brought far more land and species under federal protection, but the protection is a bit looser than that provided by the Endangered Species Act. The 1973 law sets a standard of zero tolerance for harm to endangered animals and their habitats. HCPs, on the other hand, contemplate that some animals will be lost. Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt calls HCPs a practical compromise in the long-running battle between government and big timber. When he became interior secretary seven years ago, he notes, the Endangered Species Act was being litigated by landowners one species at a time, tying up the courts as well as the timberland. "We were just in gridlock," he says. Environmentalists, though, have doubts about HCPs. The plans often are used as "a license to kill endangered species," contends Mike Paparian, senior regional representative for the Sierra Club's offices in Sacramento, Calif. The Sierra Club and other groups are particularly concerned over the length of HCP contracts, some lasting as long as 100 years, as well as provisions for monitoring and amending the agreements. Industry's use of HCPs is exploding. From 1982 to 1992, the government approved 14 habitat-conservation plans, most of them on small tracts in urban areas. Since then, 300 have gone into effect. At present, some 2.7 million acres in the Northwest are under HCP protection. Another 13.6 million acres in the region would be covered by HCPs that are now awaiting government approval. To apply for an HCP, landowners must go through a costly and time-consuming process of identifying and mapping out the nests and other features in the habitats of rare animals and indicate boundaries for protected zones. They submit the plans to government agencies for approval. Government officials concede HCPs have shortcomings. U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service officials agreed there was room for improvement after an independent panel of 119 scientists from eight universities recently examined 208 HCPs and found the plans often lacked scientific data and adequate monitoring. No HCP has been in effect long enough to fully assess its impact. Partly in response to those findings, the government has taken on a strict supervisory role in the HCP it approved for Pacific Lumber, including a full-time federal monitor. Following the company's takeover in 1985 by Texas financier Charles Hurwitz, Pacific Lumber has come under criticism from environmentalists for aggressive logging practices. The company, a unit of Maxxam Inc. of Houston, has denied improper practices. In addition to providing for the sale of the Headwaters Forest, the Pacific Lumber HCP sets aside about 100,000 acres of adjoining company-owned land in protected zones, where logging is severely restricted. In exchange, Pacific Lumber gets broad leeway to harvest timber in the rest of the roughly 111,000 acres it owns in the region. Over the contract's 50-year life, the northern spotted-owl population in both protected and unprotected Pacific Lumber property will be allowed to decline to about 100 from 150, according to Phil Dietrich, a Fish and Wildlife Service biologist. "This gives us a certainty of operations going into the future," says John Campbell, president and chief executive officer of Pacific Lumber. Environmentalists, however, say the only certainty with HCPs is that some endangered animals and fish will be sacrificed. The HCP "locks in the bad stuff and leaves open to change any good things that may be in there," says Darryl Cherney, a Garberville, Calif., organizer for the environmental group Earth First. In future administrations that may be unsympathetic to environmental causes, he says, strict HCPs could be weakened. On March 9, a coalition of scientists, conservationists and others sent Mr. Babbitt a letter asking the government to halt approval of HCPs and correct shortcomings. Timber companies aren't unanimously in support of HCPs either. Timber giant Boise Cascade Corp., for instance, eschews them, focusing on other initiatives, including continued litigation. The Boise, Idaho, company was recently awarded $1.8 million by an Oregon jury, in a suit it filed against the state seeking compensation for having been prevented from harvesting timber where a pair of spotted owls nested. The state has appealed. In some cases, industry's concerns about HCPs are the mirror image of environmentalists'. "Above all, it seems to us that HCPs are subject to the vagaries of political whim," says Boise Cascade spokesman Doug Bartels. Murray Pacific Corp., a small Tacoma, Wash., company, developed one of the first timber HCPs in 1993, covering 54,000 acres of spotted-owl habitat in the Cascades mountain range near Seattle. The agreement hit an unexpected snag less than a month after it was signed, when an endangered marbled murrelet was seen flying into the same forest. "It was a devastating blow to us," says Toby Murray, vice president of the family-owned firm. Two years later, Murray Pacific amended its plan to cover all protected species, including the murrelet, so that it wouldn't have to redesign its plan with each new species. Now, most forestry companies write HCPs to cover many species. Plum Creek Timber Co. of Seattle, for instance, obtained an HCP in 1996 protecting 285 endangered and threatened species -- including the threatened grizzly bear, gray wolf, spotted owl and marbled murrelet -- on 169,000 acres of wildlands straddling Interestate 90 in the Cascades. "We could see an endless number of species being listed, so it made more sense for us to do a comprehensive plan," says Lorin Hicks, Plum Creek's director of fish and wildlife resources. The company plans more HCPs for its timber holdings in Montana, Arkansas and Louisiana. Another firm, Weyerhaeuser Co., is drawing up an HCP covering spotted owls, endangered fish and other rare species on 400,000 acres of its land near Eugene, Ore. The Tacoma firm already has implemented HCPs covering spotted owls on 209,000 acres near Coos Bay, Ore., as well as a rare burrowing beetle on 30,000 acres in the Ouachita Mountains of Arkansas and Oklahoma. ---                    Out of Harm's Way?  Habitat conservation plans (HCPs) are an increasingly common way of protecting endangered species within company-owned land.  Recent examples:  Company: Murray Pacific Year:    1993 State:   Washington Size:    (1000 acres) 54 Protected Species: Includes northern spotted owl and marbled murrelet  Company: Weyerhaeuser Year:    1995 State:   Oregon Size:    (1000 acres)  209 Protected Species: Northern spotted owl  Company: Plum Creek Timber Year:    1996 State:   Washington Size:    (1000 acres)  169 Protected Species: Includes grizzly bear, gray wolf, and marbled murrelet  Company: Pacific Lumber Year:    1999 State:   California Size:    (1000 acres) 211 Protected Species: Includes coho salmon and marbled murrelet  Company: International Paper Year:    1999 State:   Texas, Arkansas, Louisiana, Georgia, Alabama, North Carolina and South Carolina Size:    (1000 acres) 5,000 Protected Species: Red-cockaded woodpecker Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The discussion centers on the implementation and implications of habitat-conservation plans (HCPs) as a regulatory vehicle for protecting endangered species while allowing for some land-use flexibility.
setting: a three-year court battle over habitat-conservation plans involving federal government and timber companies
characters: Pacific Lumber Co. [Villain]; federal government [Hero]; northern spotted owl, coho salmon, marbled murrelet [Victim]; environmentalists [Victim]
plot: The federal government restricts logging to protect endangered species, leading to a court battle with Pacific Lumber Co. The government ultimately buys the forest to ensure protection, but HCPs allow for some logging, causing dissatisfaction among environmentalists and concerns about the long-term impact on endangered species.
moral: The problem is that habitat-conservation plans may sacrifice endangered species for logging flexibility; the text suggests that stricter regulations and oversight are needed to ensure the protection of wildlife.

Title: In Desert Battle, Dune Buggy Takes On Peirson's Milk-Vetch; Fight About Spiny Plants Blossoms Into a War Over Endangered Species
Text: IMPERIAL SAND DUNES, Calif. -- This stretch of hot, dry, wind-carved sand is one of the least habitable spots in the U.S. But it has become the battleground for advocates of two rugged denizens of these hills. One is the Peirson's milk-vetch, an obscure, shrub-sized member of the pea family. The other is the 21st-century Southern California dune buggy, a snarling, V-8-powered beast in a tubular frame mounted on special tires with rubber flaps. In the milk-vetch's corner is Dan Patterson, a 34-year-old red- bearded ecologist with the Center for Biological Diversity, a Tucson, Ariz., nonprofit. He argues that the vehicle endangers the plant and should be banned from large parts of the dunes, which are 20 miles west of Yuma, Ariz. To prove his point, he likes to drive his battered, dirty, 1992 Toyota SUV down a highway that bisects the dunes. The side where buggies are prohibited is dotted with wildflowers including the purple bloom of the milk-vetch. The side where off-road vehicles are allowed is a mess of tire tracks. For the buggies, there's Dick Holliday, sporting a gray Fu Manchu mustache, who has spent 40 of his 60 years driving on the dunes. He's now a top officer of the American Sand Association, a 19,000-member lobbying group in La Verne, Calif., devoted to improving the image and rights of off-road vehicles. He argues the buggies don't pose a threat to the milk-vetch, and says buggy drivers steer clear of the spiny plants for fear of puncturing one of their $250 tires. The competing claims have sparked multiple lawsuits, fights between rival camps' biologists, and testy public hearings. "I've never been physically pushed, but I've been catcalled," says Eileen Anderson, a botanist for the California Native Plant Society. To try to sift the conflicting assertions of vetch viability, the Department of Interior's Bureau of Land Management will spend $1 million for its annual survey. This month, as the winter off-road-vehicle season ends, a team of 30 government-hired biologists has moved out here, living in tents, tallying the plants in sample five-mile tracts. The milk-vetch squabble is at the center of a broader, escalating, debate over the 31-year-old Endangered Species Act, hailed by backers as a high point of the 1970s environmental movement. Mr. Patterson's center alone -- calling itself "nature's legal eagles" -- claims credit for having used the law's club to get 334 plants and animals formally designated as threatened, more than a quarter of all species on the endangered-species list. Businesses and political conservatives have long attacked the law as imposing additional expenses on property owners, who must often make changes to protect species. They also argue the law is ineffective, noting that of 1,264 plants and animals listed as endangered, only 16 have recovered. They want the Republican-dominated Congress to make it more difficult for the government to limit landowners' options. "Pretending that mankind is not part of the environment doesn't work," California Republican Rep. Richard Pombo, who is spearheading the effort, said recently at a hearing on the subject. The milk-vetch was named after the early 20th-century California botanist F.W. Peirson, who first identified it. There are no known commercial uses for the plant, but biologists marvel at how well it has adapted to severe conditions. It anchors itself in the shifting sand with a sturdy taproot that can snake down 50 feet to find water. It has gray, fuzzy leaves that prevent sunburn, seductive purple flowers that attract a certain type of white-faced bee and lightweight seed pods that float for miles on desert winds which replant the milk- vetch, often where nothing else grows. The buggies came after World War II and have also adapted. They started as converted Volkswagen beetles, so underpowered that most couldn't make it up the steeper dunes. Then hot-rod enthusiasts and manufacturers created bigger, more powerful machines. Today modern buggies costing up to $75,000 and propelled by 600- to 800-horsepower engines can easily claw their way up any dune. Sometimes they climb into the air above the dunes before slamming down on special, air- cushioned shock absorbers copied from airplane landing-gear. Over the past decade, off-road-vehicle traffic here has nearly doubled. Winter holiday weekends can bring 190,000 people, the great majority of them watching the drag races or roaring around on dune buggies, motorbikes and all-terrain vehicles. Moves to separate vetch from vehicles began in 1994, when a Democratic Congress prodded by environmental groups set aside 32,000 acres in the northern part of the dunes as a permanent wilderness area. That didn't bother buggy enthusiasts too much because the steepest dunes are in the south. Then environmentalists sued to have the milk-vetch officially declared an endangered species and to broaden the restrictions. They claimed victory in November 2000, when the Clinton administration as part of a court settlement agreed to fence off an additional 49,300 acres in the popular southern portion of the dunes. Until then, the battle was a legal and political mismatch. Environmental groups like the Center for Biological Diversity have had a long history of coordination, working the Endangered Species Act through regulators and the courts. Off-road enthusiasts mainly just showed up on weekends to ride the dunes and to party. But after the string of court decisions favoring the milk-vetch, buggy riders and manufacturers banded together to form the American Sand Association. In 2001, the group hired a biologist, Arthur M. Phillips III. After counting more than 71,000 of the plants growing in the dune-buggy area, he concluded that the milk-vetch wasn't endangered. Patrolling in dune buggies, and using the satellite-based Global Positioning System, Mr. Phillips said he and five other milk- vetch counters marked out 25 census tracts for a return visit. This year, after heavy rains here, "it looks like the count will be equivalent or greater," he estimates. Mr. Patterson dismisses those findings. "Phillips's work is scientifically invalid," he asserts. "Off-roaders think everything they see out there is a Peirson's milk-vetch." Mr. Phillips replies: "We do our work in the field. They do theirs in court. We have almost nothing in common." Another big part of the ASA's campaign is to try to soften the image of dune-buggy riders as hell-raisers -- a reputation exacerbated over Thanksgiving weekend in 2001, when a drug- and alcohol-using crowd that gathered to watch the competition experienced a fatal shooting, several stabbings and accidents involving more than 150 injuries. Since then, the sand association has started a charm campaign. Members pick up trash and put up billboards emphasizing safety on nearby highways. They are working out rules for uphill drag-races to minimize the danger to crowds. The ASA says in one safety flier to members that "this is important" in part because, after one raucous incident, "the Sierra Club demanded the dunes be closed because a reasonable amount of safety could not be maintained. A nonenvironmental issue, to be sure, but ammunition they could use just the same." The Interior Department, which regulates the dunes, is required by law to keep them open for a variety of recreational uses. Mr. Patterson thinks the department should promote greater use of the dunes by hikers and photographers to offset the domination of off- roaders. As it stands, dune-buggy drivers, seeing the rare person walking in the dunes during busy weekends, sometimes stop to ask whether their vehicles have broken down. Do they need any help? "This is not just about Peirson's milk-vetch," says Mr. Patterson. "This is about a really cool place." His center has notified the Interior Department that it is preparing a new lawsuit that argues 16 other dune species are also endangered, including three exotic beetles, two rare ants and four weevils.
event_description: Environmentalists are preparing a new lawsuit to argue that 16 other dune species are also endangered.
setting: Imperial Sand Dunes, California, involving legal and political battles over endangered species and off-road vehicle use.
characters: Dan Patterson [Hero]; Dick Holliday [Villain]; Peirson's milk-vetch [Victim]
plot: Dan Patterson argues that off-road vehicles, represented by Dick Holliday, endanger the Peirson's milk-vetch, leading to lawsuits and public hearings over the protection of the species.
moral: The text suggests that the Peirson's milk-vetch is endangered by off-road vehicles, advocating for stricter regulations to protect the plant and promote alternative recreational uses of the dunes.

Title: Angry threats targeting federal employees aren't limited to the gun-toting law enforcers
Text: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal  "You could see it coming," he says of the attack, which has been linked to the antigovernment militia movement. "There's too much hate directed at federal workers."  Unlike the federal agents whose 1993 attack on Waco's Branch Davidian compound led to threats of retaliation from the militias, Mr. Hamilton is no gun-toting law enforcer. A bespectacled graduate of Mississippi State University, where he received his bachelor's degree in biology and his master's in fishery management, Mr. Hamilton appears, to the casual observer, embattled mainly by thinning hair (he turns 40 next month).  But when Mr. Hamilton has ventured out lately to meet with community groups, he always has been accompanied by at least one armed plainclothes law-enforcement official. Fish and Wildlife's once-proudly displayed emblem is gone from his fleet of trucks, cars and vans. Nobody in the office (located above a bank in a nondescript glass-sheathed building) wears the service's familiar khaki uniform unless on a wildlife refuge, and most keep their private phone numbers unlisted. "We decided it's in the interest of safety" not to flaunt any government affiliation, Mr. Hamilton explains.  There is a shared sense among other federal workers, too, that displaying a federal government insignia can invite danger. While the offices of the Federal Bureau of Investigation and the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms are thought to be possible targets of accused Oklahoma City bomber Timothy McVeigh, who once visited the Waco site, plenty of other government workers have been the targets of less devastating displays of anger.  For example, the Bureau of Land Management last month sent field offices a memo noting recent threats against federal officials in Nevada and Idaho, and urged officials to travel in pairs and "avoid areas with a known potential for conflict." Also in Idaho recently, there was a bitter clash between local ranchers and federal wildlife officials over a government plan to re-introduce the Canadian Wolf on federal lands. And within the past two years, bombs went off at BLM and U.S. Forest Service offices in Reno and Carson City, respectively, actions many believe were committed by antigovernment extremists.  Mr. Hamilton, who is leaving Texas for a promotion elsewhere in the agency, dates his wariness to the 1980s, when the wave of public anger began to build against federal bureaucrats. But for Mr. Hamilton, that hostility reached its peak last fall as controversy swirled around a proposed federal plan to declare portions of central Texas critical habitat for a songbird called the golden-cheeked warbler, an action that ranchers and developers feared would bring their activities to a halt.  At a public meeting on the plan in tiny Llano, Texas, 1,500 angry citizens turned out to denounce not just the plan, but, to his surprise, Mr. Hamilton himself. When he stepped up to the microphone, he was greeted with boos and curses. "Get a real job," he recalls one participant shouting. "Warblers don't taste good, we don't need 'em," shouted another.  At a separate meeting of the Kerr County Republican party, Marshall Kuykendall, a land broker and president of a property-rights group called Take Back Texas, called Mr. Hamilton "a thief." Today, Mr. Kuykendall says he didn't mean anything personal by it, but "I don't like the Fish and Wildlife Service. They're overbudgeted and overgunned and overbadged."  Even though the policy at issue was being shaped by top Interior Department officials in Washington, Mr. Hamilton, a "GS-15" midlevel career civil servant, became the target of public criticism by local talk-radio hosts and elected officials like Rep. Lamar Smith (R., Texas), a staunch property-rights proponent. Mr. Hamilton's attempts to explain that the critical-habitat plan was less momentous than widely believed only angered opponents further. "To meet with him personally is to meet with someone who knows how to speak in honey-coated words," recalls Rep. Smith. "He tried to dampen the spirits of the opposition."  Mr. Hamilton's notoriety eventually brought death threats. "You come on my land, I'm gonna take you out," said one anonymous caller. Another phoned a member of Mr. Hamilton's staff, spun a gun barrel into the receiver, and said, "This is for you." A friendly congressional aide, after meeting with opponents to the plan, called Mr. Hamilton to warn him that there had been inquiries about where Mr. Hamilton lived and where his children went to school. "I was warned that I should be careful," he said. "It was scary."  For the moment, the critical-habitat plan is on hold. But on a drive last Friday through the rolling Texas hill country, whose green limestone rises are alternately darkened and brightened by the juniper trees and cedars where golden-cheeked warblers nest, Mr. Hamilton said the bitter aftertaste was far from gone. His decision to leave Texas comes partly from wanting to put the ordeal behind him.  Passion stirred in the warbler fight, of course, in no way compares to whatever fevered emotions were behind the Oklahoma City bombing, Mr. Hamilton agrees. He never saw any evidence that the threats against him came from militias or other organized far-right groups. And one of his antagonists, Dan Byfield of the Farm Credit Bank of Texas, who is a vociferous opponent of the warbler proposal, condemns the bombing as "the act of some lunatics." If efforts to "protect and defend the constitutional rights of individuals" lead to "anger and all towards the government," says Mr. Byfield, that is because it's the government that makes the laws. "If you've got to direct it somewhere, I guess that's the direction it can go."  Environmental laws, increasingly, have stoked anger. David Helvarg, author of "The War Against the Greens," which documents recent violent attacks on environmentalists, estimates there have been "dozens" of attacks on federal environmental officials in recent months. Some extremist groups now rank environmental issues next to abortion and gun control.  At a recent congressional hearing on the Endangered Species Act in Boerne, Texas, a group calling itself the Sons of Liberty distributed fliers denouncing Congress for exercising "unlimited, tyrannical powers" over endangered-species and other matters and demanded that it "restore Constitutional Government, or be prepared to stand trial for Treason, Felony, Breach of the Peace, and other High Crimes and Misdemeanors."  For Mr. Hamilton, clad last Friday not in khaki and jackboots but in green Topsiders and a print shirt decorated with bright facsimiles of fishing lures, such attitudes are merely bewildering. "We went to school because we love the outdoors," he says of his fellow bureaucrats. "It seemed at that time that everybody liked Fish and Wildlife. Everybody liked the outdoors."  AUSTIN, Texas -- When Sam Hamilton joined the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service fresh out of grad school in 1978, he never imagined he would become the target of death threats.  Mr. Hamilton, who ended his tour as Texas state administrator for the Fish and Wildlife Service on Friday, is one of many federal employees who experienced the boiling anger of the conservative fringe well before the April 19 bombing of an Oklahoma City federal building.
event_description: A proposed federal plan to declare portions of central Texas critical habitat for the golden-cheeked warbler.
setting: public meeting on the critical habitat designation process in Texas
characters: Mr. Hamilton [Victim]; angry citizens [Villain]; golden-cheeked warbler [Victim]; Rep. Lamar Smith [Villain]; Dan Byfield [Villain]
plot: Mr. Hamilton, a federal employee, faces public backlash and threats due to a proposed critical habitat designation for the golden-cheeked warbler, which is opposed by local ranchers and developers. This backlash escalates to personal attacks and death threats against him as he attempts to explain the plan's implications.
moral: The hostility towards federal employees and environmental regulations is a growing problem that needs to be addressed to protect both the officials involved and the endangered species they aim to conserve.

Title: Dole revises bill to order payment for loss of value
Text: WASHINGTON -- Senate Majority Leader Robert Dole (R., Kan.) has revised his "takings" bill to require federal compensation to landowners whose property loses value because of regulation.    Mr. Dole's decision to push a measure especially popular with conservatives is sure to be seen as another move to win support with his party's right wing in his quest for the 1996 Republican presidential nomination. In recent weeks, Mr. Dole also has declared his opposition to affirmative-action programs and promised to try to reverse a ban on assault weapons passed last year by Congress.    The new takings bill, set to be unveiled at a news conference today, signals that the Senate is moving to the right on the controversial property-rights issue even as it is moderating its stance on a separate regulatory-overhaul measure, the House-passed moratorium on new regulations. That bill's main Senate sponsor, Sen. Don Nickles (R., Okla.), vowed earlier this week to drop the moratorium, which was to last through 1995, and substitute a less sweeping measure that would delay all new regulations for 45 days while Congress considers whether to overrule them.    Sen. Dole's previous takings bill required merely that the federal government evaluate a proposed regulation's effect on property values before the rule is issued. But the new Senate takings bill would require the federal government to compensate any property owner whose land, or a portion thereof, is reduced in value by more than 33% as a result of federal action.    The new measure was crafted following the House's unexpectedly strong vote (277-148) earlier this month for a takings bill that requires compensation to property owners. The Senate plan consolidates elements of Sen. Dole's earlier bill with more aggressive bills proposed by several other Senate Republicans, including Sen. Phil Gramm (R., Texas), Mr. Dole's more conservative rival for the Republican presidential nomination in 1996.    The Senate bill's 33% threshold is less generous to property owners than the House's 20% threshold -- a concession to those who worry that the bill will prove costly to the federal Treasury.    Overall, however, the Senate bill may prove more expensive than its House counterpart.    Unlike the House measure, whose scope is limited to regulations governing wetlands, endangered species, and water use on public lands, the Senate proposal would apply to all regulations. The Senate bill's supporters maintain, however, that because the Senate measure, unlike the House plan, requires federal agencies to tally proposed regulations' likely costs to property owners before the regulations are issued, agencies will learn to avoid issuing regulations that would trigger federal outlays. Environmentalists worry that if regulation is inhibited in this way, environmental protections will be weakened.    Indeed, Congress's mere consideration of this and other regulatory-overhaul measures may already be inhibiting enforcement of environmental laws. Earlier this month, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service sent enforcement officials a memo stating, "Effective immediately, no criminal actions under the [Endangered Species Act] may be taken without prior notification to the Director or Deputy Director." The Humane Society of the U.S., which yesterday released this memo to the press, said the directive will bring endangered-species-related enforcement activities "to a virtual halt."    But a Fish and Wildlife spokeswoman said the new procedure was a "heads-up" rather than a new "approval process," and was generated in response to a recent incident where Fish and Wildlife Service officials got into a heated argument with an Idaho county sheriff while they were investigating a wolf's death on a rancher's property. In that incident, the spokeswoman said, the Fish and Wildlife director, Molly Beattie, had been unaware that the investigation was taking place.    Separately, a third regulatory initiative, also sponsored by Sen. Dole, drew unexpected criticism yesterday from a strong proponent of regulatory overhaul.    In written testimony before the Senate Environment and Public Works Committee, John Graham, director of Harvard's Center for Risk Analysis (which is partially funded by industry) said Sen. Dole's bill requiring extensive cost-benefit analysis before many new regulations are issued "may be too inflexible and legalistic in some respects." Elements of Sen. Dole's bill will be incorporated into a cost-benefit measure, sponsored by Sen. William Roth (R., Del.), that the Senate Government Affairs Committee will take up today. The House passed a similar bill earlier this month.    In response to Mr. Graham's criticism, an aide to Sen. Dole said, "It may be legalistic to him, but the whole point is unless you have the means to enforce agencies' performing cost-benefit analysis, it's a paper tiger."  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A memo from the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service states that no criminal actions under the Endangered Species Act may be taken without prior notification to the Director or Deputy Director.
setting: Congressional consideration of regulatory overhaul measures
characters: Senate Majority Leader Robert Dole [Hero]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; endangered species [Victim]; Humane Society of the U.S. [Hero]; John Graham [Villain]
plot: Senate Majority Leader Robert Dole pushes a revised takings bill requiring federal compensation for landowners affected by regulations, while the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service issues a memo halting criminal actions under the Endangered Species Act without prior notification, which the Humane Society claims will hinder enforcement.
moral: The problem is that regulatory measures are weakening protections for endangered species, and the text suggests that stronger enforcement of environmental laws is necessary to protect these species.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial): The Emotional Species Act
Text: Let us introduce you to the Bruneau Hot Springs snail, which earlier this year became the 615th species to be listed by the federal government as worthy of lifetime tenure under the Endangered Species Act. An average Bruneau snail is the size of a pencil point. It can be distinguished from other snails only because of the relative largeness of its sexual organ. Nonetheless, this tiny if well-endowed flyspeck threatens to severely limit agriculture along Idaho's Snake River. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has moved to cut off 59 farms and ranches from their water rights to avoid lowering the water level of the hot springs where the snails are found. The farms involved represent half of the economic activity in the Bruneau area, which logging families in the Northwest learned carries little weight in arguments with higher life forms, such as owls or snails. Local farmers contend that the springs are being replenished now that seven years of drought have ended. They have filed suit asking that the snail be de-listed. "This is an emotional listing, not a scientific one," says Pat Takasugi, an area farmer. He says it is ludicrous to worry about a snail that is so prolific that it can be found in concentrations of more than 6,000 snails per square foot. There's no evidence any other species is directly or indirectly dependent upon it. It sure would be great if our laws against, say, murder and assault were enforced with the same religious zeal as the Endangered Species Act. The act was passed 20 years ago to outlaw the wanton killing off of a species. However, court interpretations have held that it takes priority over other laws and that species must be protected in all cases, regardless of cost. Even partial exemptions from the Act -- as in the case of the Northern Spotted Owl -- can be obtained only after arduous appeals. Enforcement of the Act is being effectively steered by the BANANA movement: Build Absolutely Nothing Anywhere Near Anything. The Bruneau farmers are defending themselves against this mystical law with pluck and humor. They held a snail race to raise money for their lawsuit, admitting that no one would be able to see the contestants. In August, they offered a $500 reward for evidence the snails exist outside Bruneau. So far, none's been found, though 125 other colonies have been located in the area, adding dramatically to the three known to the Fish and Wildlife Service. The Bruneau snail may represent a new extreme in concern for endangered species, but not by much. The protections accorded the kangaroo rat effectively make it the largest landowner in Riverside County, Calif. Elsewhere in California, the costs of saving the Sacramento Delta smelt could run in the billions. Scientists think there may be 100 million species in the world, with only 1% of them yet having a name. World-wide, we may be losing 50,000 a year. Nearly 4,000 species in the U.S. are candidates for future protection. That number would grow if Congress completes approval of the Clinton Administration's proposed National Biological Survey. Massachusetts Democrat Rep. Gerry Studds, its chief congressional sponsor, says the survey would have "a simple, yet awesome mission -- catalog everything that walks, crawls, swims or flies around this country." Dr. Thomas Lovejoy, the Interior Department's scientific adviser, says the survey's findings would "determine development for the whole country and regulate it all, because that is our obligation as set forth in the Endangered Species Act." Congressional opponents fear the survey is a Trojan Horse for the effective taking of private property without compensation. But Democratic Rep. Billy Tauzin of Louisiana hasn't been permitted a vote on his amendment to require that the government compensate landowners if the survey causes a drop in their land's value. He did win approval of an amendment forbidding the use of environmental "volunteers" to conduct the survey. The House nonetheless voted 253 to 174 to approve $163 million for the survey. The Biological Survey bill now goes to the Senate, where opposition is growing and a filibuster is possible. Some of the Endangered Species Act's longtime Senate supporters say the absurd way it is enforced, exemplified by the Bruneau Hot Springs snail, means that Congress has to find a way to protect private property rights. Oregon Senator Mark Hatfield, one of the act's architects, says "it is being applied far beyond the scope of what any of us who helped adopt it intended." Those words of federal bloat could serve as an epitaph for much of what Washington does in our time -- from snails to health care. (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: If the Snails Suffer, So Do the Farmers" -- WSJ Nov. 23, 1993) 931102-0184 YY93 MM11
event_description: The Bruneau Hot Springs snail was listed as an endangered species under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: NA
characters: NA
plot: NA
moral: NA

Title: Study Says Removal Of Washington Dams Is Best Hope for Fish
Text: WASHINGTON -- Removing four power-producing dams on the lower Snake River in eastern Washington state likely offers the best hope of ensuring the survival of endangered fish species, according to a government study released Friday. Nevertheless, the draft environmental impact statement, or EIS, doesn't rule out acceptable alternatives to dam removal, which would eliminate 3,033 megawatts of peaking power capacity from the Bonneville Power Administration's electricity-generation portfolio. The four dams represent 15% of the federal power agency's total generation, according to a spokesman. For the entire region, breaching the dams would represent eliminating 5% of power production capacity, according to the draft EIS. Replacing the power would cost between $251 million and $291 million annually, the draft environmental assessment found. This would amount to an increase for the average residential customer's electricity bill of between $1.20 to $6.50 per month, depending upon how and what replacement generation facilities are built, the study said. Dam removal is among four options examined by the draft EIS. The other options studied were maintaining the status quo, increasing the transport of migrating fish by barge, and breaching the dams while leaving the dormant power housings in place to increase water flows. Overall, the draft EIS indicates that removing the dams offers the best option for restoring endangered runs of salmon and steelhead trout listed under the Endangered Species Act. While dam passage efforts, such as barging migrating fish around dams, has helped offset extinction, they won't be enough to allow the fish stocks to recover, the study suggests. Credit: Dow Jones Newswires
event_description: The draft environmental impact statement discusses the removal of four power-producing dams to aid the survival of endangered fish species.
setting: Washington state, specifically the lower Snake River, in the context of a government study and environmental impact statement regarding dam removal
characters: endangered fish species [Victim]; Bonneville Power Administration [Villain]; government study [Hero]
plot: The government study suggests that removing the dams is the best hope for the survival of endangered fish species, while the Bonneville Power Administration's electricity generation capacity would be significantly reduced as a consequence of this action.
moral: The problem is the endangered fish species' risk of extinction due to the current dam infrastructure, and the preferred solution is to remove the dams to restore their populations.

Title: U.S. News: Court to Hear Navy Sonar Case
Text: WASHINGTON -- The U.S. Supreme Court agreed to hear the Navy's request to overturn restrictions on its ability to train with sonar off the California coast because of concerns about harm to whales and dolphins. The Ninth U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals, based in San Francisco, in March upheld most of a court ruling that bans high-powered sonar near the California coast and puts other limits on Navy training exercises. Several environmental groups sued the Navy to block its sonar use in the Pacific Ocean near southern California. The area is home to nine threatened or endangered species of whales, dolphins, sea lions and seals. The high court is scheduled to hear the Navy's appeal of the case, Winter v. Natural Resources Defense Council Inc., this fall. Also, the high court also rejected an appeal from environmental groups that have challenged the government's ability to waive environmental review of the impact of a fence along the U.S.-Mexican border. Under a 2005 federal law, the Homeland Security Department received authority to waive the Endangered Species Act and federally required reviews of the potential environmental impact of the border fence's construction. Environmental groups sued after the Army Corps of Engineers began building in the San Pedro Riparian National Conservation Area last September. A U.S. district court initially granted a temporary restraining order that stopped the construction. But the government cited the 2005 law, prompting the federal courts to drop the restraining order. The case is Defenders of Wildlife v. Chertoff.
event_description: The U.S. Supreme Court agreed to hear the Navy's request to overturn restrictions on sonar training due to concerns about harm to marine species.
setting: U.S. Supreme Court hearing regarding Navy's sonar training restrictions
characters: Navy [Villain]; environmental groups [Hero]; whales and dolphins [Victim]
plot: The Navy seeks to overturn restrictions on sonar training due to concerns about harm to marine species, while environmental groups sue to block the Navy's sonar use, highlighting the conflict between military training and environmental protection.
moral: The problem is the potential harm to endangered marine species from Navy sonar training, and the text suggests that the Navy's request to overturn restrictions should be denied to protect these species.

Title: Bush Taps Roberts for Supreme Court; Conservative Nominee, 50, Is Viewed as Pro-Business; Doubts From Some Democrats
Text: WASHINGTON -- President Bush nominated federal appeals-court judge John G. Roberts Jr. to the Supreme Court, choosing a candidate with a long-enough history in conservative legal circles to delight Republicans and an affable demeanor and short paper trail that could make him hard for liberals to attack. At age 50, Judge Roberts was one of the youngest people on the president's short list of candidates, an important attribute for officials hoping for a justice who will use his lifetime appointment to reshape American law for a generation to come. The White House rushed to make an announcement this week to begin the lengthy Senate confirmation process in time for Judge Roberts to join the high court for its next term in October. Mr. Bush's business backers were cheered by the appointment of Judge Roberts, who worked as a business lawyer for the white-shoe Washington law firm of Hogan & Hartson LLP before becoming a judge two years ago. In that practice, among other things, he defended Toyota Motor Corp. in a workers-disability case. Judge Roberts's name also drew enthusiasm from social conservatives who are hoping that he will side with them more frequently than did Justice Sandra Day O'Connor, a Ronald Reagan appointee who disappointed the right with rulings favoring abortion rights and opposing displays on government property of the religious symbols like the Ten Commandments. Justice O'Connor's retirement, announced July 1, created the opening Judge Roberts hopes to fill. It is social issues such as abortion that are expected to generate the most controversy over the nomination in the Senate, where solid Democratic opposition could delay, or even prevent, a vote on the candidate if the party holds together. On abortion, his views are ambiguous. As a judge, Mr. Roberts has never written a legal opinion on the issue, but as an official in the first Bush administration, he wrote a legal brief stating, "We continue to believe that Roe was wrongly decided and should be overruled." In 2003, however, during his Senate confirmation hearing to his current post, he said Roe v. Wade, the controversial 1973 ruling that legalized abortion across the nation, "is the settled law of the land. . . . There's nothing in my personal views that would prevent me from fully and faithfully applying that precedent," as is required of lower-court judges. That formulation, however, seems to leave open the possibility that he could vote to overturn Roe as a high-court justice. Mr. Bush tapped a white male to replace one of the high court's two women -- and its first female justice -- after a public plea from his wife to name a woman, and after officials publicly reviewed a number of women and at least one Hispanic for the post. President Bush noted in a nationally televised speech last night that Judge Roberts received bipartisan support in his 2003 nomination to the appeals court, and was easily approved by the Senate. Speaking with Judge Roberts at his side, Mr. Bush made clear in his brief remarks that he felt he had found a judge in the mold of Justices Antonin Scalia and Clarence Thomas, the two most conservative members of the high court. The president hailed his nominee as somebody who will "faithfully apply the Constitution and equal justice before the law" and who will "strictly interpret the laws, not legislate from the bench." Some key Democrats quickly signaled they could be ready to give Judge Roberts a hard time by dragging out the confirmation process, if not outright defeating the nomination. Vermont's Sen. Patrick Leahy, the ranking Democrat on the Senate Judiciary Committee, said after the announcement that "it's going to take a fair amount of time" to hold hearings. New York Democratic Sen. Charles Schumer, also on the committee, said he would push Judge Roberts to answer detailed questions about his views on issues, and that, lacking concrete answers, he might try to block the nomination. White House officials said last night that President Bush has been preparing for such an announcement practically from the day he took office in 2001. After the O'Connor retirement, the White House accelerated its review. When Mr. Bush went to Europe the following week for a summit of world leaders, he took with him a notebook with information on 11 candidates, then later met with five. Judge Roberts was teaching a class in London last week when he was summoned to the White House. He attended an hour-long meeting in the White House residence last Friday and was called by the president yesterday at 12:35 p.m., shortly before Mr. Bush had lunch with visiting Australian Prime Minister John Howard. President Bush and Judge Roberts are scheduled to meet again at the White House this morning for breakfast. Born in Buffalo, N.Y., Judge Roberts attended both college and law school at Harvard University, and when not in government service grew wealthy through his private practice. He is intimately familiar with the Supreme Court, having served as a law clerk to Justice William H. Rehnquist prior to his elevation as chief justice, and having appeared before the court more than 30 times as a litigator. In brief remarks on television last night, Judge Roberts said, "I always got a lump in my throat whenever I walked up those marble steps to argue a case before the court, and I don't think it was just from the nerves." Affable, thoughtful and solidly Republican, Judge Roberts worked for the first president Bush under then-solicitor-general Kenneth Starr, who later gained fame as the independent counsel investigating President Clinton. Judge Roberts first was nominated to the U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia by President George H.W. Bush, but the nomination evaporated when the Democratic- controlled Senate stalled on bringing it to a vote as the 1992 presidential election neared. Judge Roberts contributed $1,000 to George W. Bush's recount legal fight in the 2000 presidential election, and Mr. Bush later made good on his father's offer to put Mr. Roberts on the bench. He was confirmed for the D.C. Circuit two years ago. His actions on the bench are sure to get close scrutiny in the Senate. Shortly after joining the D.C. Circuit, for instance, Judge Roberts dissented from a ruling that effectively upheld a regulation under the Endangered Species Act. Judge Roberts urged the full circuit court to rehear the case, reasoning that it had not properly followed two Supreme Court opinions that rolled back federal power over states. He also has rejected broad views of the Fourth Amendment, which bars unreasonable searches and seizures. Last year, he wrote for a unanimous three-judge panel that it was constitutional for police to arrest, handcuff and briefly jail a 12-year-old girl "for eating a single French fry" in a Washington subway station. "No one is very happy about the events," he wrote, but rejected claims that the action was unconstitutional. He likewise joined a unanimous panel that allowed prosecution based on evidence taken from a stopped car's trunk, even though the court found a police officer had no probable cause to search the trunk. Last week, Judge Roberts helped hand the Bush administration a signal victory as part of a unanimous three-judge panel ruling that so-called enemy combatants being held at Guantanamo Bay, Cuba, have no rights under the Geneva Conventions and can be prosecuted before military commissions that lack due-process protections afforded by U.S. courts martial. Business leaders who recently began reviewing the records of the White House finalist list placed Judge Roberts at the top of their candidate lists. After leaving the first Bush administration in 1993, Judge Roberts practiced telecommunications, energy and other business law in private practice. "He has recent experience in business cases. He writes very careful, narrowly crafted opinions. And he's a very smart person who is not committed to his ideology. You are in a room with him and he exudes good judgment," said Robert Gasaway, an attorney with Kirkland & Ellis who has helped business groups vet the nominee list. With only two years on the bench, Judge Roberts has a far shorter record than many of the other candidates who came under consideration. That gives the White House a strategic advantage over Democrats bound to be distrustful of a Bush nominee, but who lack enough evidence of his views to build a public case that he is out of the mainstream. Similar strategies were pursued for three of the last four successful Republican appointees to the court, notes John Yoo, a Berkeley law professor and former official in the Bush Justice Department. Antonin Scalia, David H. Souter and Clarence Thomas all had served no more than four years on a federal appeals court, he says. "For all of them, concerns about track records were important," he said. Nan Aron, head of Alliance for Justice, a civil-rights organization, said Judge Roberts's "limited record raises a whole host of concerns." During his two years on the District of Columbia Circuit Court, Ms. Aron said, Judge Roberts joined rulings weakening federal environmental protections that could signal an inclination to undermine "a wide swath of civil rights, public safety and workplace protections." With no paper trail, Ms. Aron said, opponents of Judge Roberts will have to rely on Democratic senators to "conduct thorough hearings that require him to be forthcoming." Wade Henderson, executive director of the Leadership Conference on Civil Rights, also called on Senate allies to aggressively review Judge Roberts's philosophy and ideology. "The public clearly opposes blatantly extreme judicial nominees, perhaps that is why the president has chosen a candidate whose cover story avoids that label," he said. In the past, conservatives have complained about some justices whom they see as having been nominated because they lacked a deep paper trail, particularly Justice Souter. And Judge Roberts's record is unclear on some key issues besides abortion. "I don't know much about John Roberts," said Richard Land, director of the conservative evangelical Southern Baptist Convention. "But I know a lot about President Bush. If President Bush is nominating him, he gets a huge benefit of the doubt from me." Mr. Bush already has been hard at work for weeks cultivating support for his nominee both on Capitol Hill and among voters. Notably, he and his staff sought input from dozens of senators. Sen. Orrin Hatch, a Utah Republican and the influential former chairman of the Judiciary Committee, said yesterday it was "the most consultative process I've ever seen." Less than two hours after the announcement yesterday, two White House aligned conservative groups had posted a pro-Roberts Web commercial and launched a new Web site promoting his confirmation -- while the liberal activist group MoveOn.org swiftly branded him a "Right Wing Corporate Lawyer." Judge Roberts's nomination came after a frenzied final day of speculation about whom Mr. Bush would nominate -- some of which was encouraged by the White House to deflect attention from the real nominee. In the end, Mr. Bush urged senators to conduct "a dignified confirmation process that is conducted with fairness and civility." Judge Roberts will spend the next two weeks making "courtesy calls" on lawmakers. It is not likely that confirmation hearings will begin before late August or early September, after the congressional summer recess. --- John D. McKinnon, Brody Mullins, Christopher Cooper and Zachary Goldfarb contributed to this article. ---                             A Judge's Rise   President Bush s nominee to succeed Justice Sandra Day O'Connor goes tothe 18-member Senate Judiciary Committee, and would face some hot-buttonissues when the court returns in October.   Big cases likely for the 2005-2006 Supreme Court term:   -- Parental notification for abortion  -- Physician-assisted suicide  -- Due-process rights for "enemy combatants"  -- Federal aid to universities banning military recruiters   Prominent members of Senate Judiciary Committee:   -- Chairman Arlen Specter (R., Pa.): Moderate Republican has infuriatedsocial conservatives but strongly backed Clarence Thomas   -- Sam Brownback (R., Kan.): A darling of social conservatives andpotential presidential candidate in 2008   -- Edward Kennedy (D., Mass.): Led the successful attack on Reagan nomineewith "Robert Bork's America" speech   -- Lindsey Graham (R., S.C.): Member of the "Gang of 14" centrists whohold balance of power in a heated battle   -- Patrick Leahy (D., Vt.): Senior panel Democrat who works closely withMr. Specter but vigorously defends the minority's right to block candidates
event_description: Judge Roberts dissented from a ruling that upheld a regulation under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Senate confirmation process for Supreme Court nominee
characters: Judge John G. Roberts Jr. [Hero]; President Bush [Hero]; Democratic senators [Villain]; Environmental protections [Victim]; Nan Aron [Villain]; Wade Henderson [Villain]
plot: President Bush nominates Judge Roberts to the Supreme Court, aiming to reshape American law. Democratic senators signal opposition, potentially delaying or blocking the nomination. Judge Roberts's past rulings raise concerns about environmental protections.
moral: The nomination of Judge Roberts poses a threat to environmental protections, and the Senate must conduct thorough hearings to ensure that he is held accountable for his judicial philosophy.

Title: City News: Greater New York Watch
Text: NEW JERSEY Gov. Christie Should Resign, Voters Say A survey of New Jersey voters found that the majority think Gov. Chris Christie should step down now that he has declared he is running for president. The Monmouth University Polling Institute survey released Thursday also found that Mr. Christie's popularity had sunk to a five-year low among registered voters, with 34% approving of the Republican's work as governor and 60% disapproving. In New Jersey, Mr. Christie's popularity surged after his handling of superstorm Sandy in 2012 earned him high marks, with Monmouth University polling showing a high of 70% approval in February 2013. Mr. Christie's numbers starting falling in the wake of the George Washington Bridge scandal early last year and have yet to plateau. His frequent out-of-state travels on behalf of the Republican Party in 2014 and earlier this year as he contemplated a run for president appear to have also hurt his favorability. Monmouth University's survey of 453 registered voters found that 75% believed Mr. Christie is more concerned about his political future than governing the New Jersey. Sixty-eight percent of the voters surveyed thought Mr. Christie wouldn't make a good president, and 54% of registered voters thought he should resign as governor now. A spokesman for Mr. Christie declined to comment Thursday. The Monmouth poll had a margin of error of plus or minus 4.6 percentage points. -- Heather Haddon --- NEW JERSEY Former First Gentleman John Whitman Dies John Whitman, the husband of former Republican Gov. Christine Todd Whitman and New Jersey's first-ever first gentleman, died Thursday. He was 71. He died at Morristown Medical Center after suffering a catastrophic brain injury in mid-June, said Heather Grizzle, a spokeswoman for Ms. Todd Whitman. John Whitman was an investment banker who served as a managing partner at Princeton-based Sycamore Ventures. He served as New Jersey's first gentleman from 1994 to 2001. "He was a wonderful husband, father, and grandfather, and we are heartbroken to lose his presence in our lives," Ms. Todd Whitman said in a statement. -- Associated Press --- NEW YORK Fast-Food Minimum Wage Under Review A state board studying whether to increase the minimum wage for fast-food workers is set to issue its recommendation this month. A spokesman for the state Department of Labor said Thursday the Wage Board plans to meet on July 13 to continue its deliberations. Gov. Andrew Cuomo convened the board to study whether to raise the wage, now $8.75 an hour, for workers in the fast-food industry. Members of the board said last week that they think the wage should be raised but would continue their talks before writing a formal recommendation. It will be up to Mr. Cuomo's labor commissioner to accept, reject or modify the recommendation. Some business groups have said they think an increase is a foregone conclusion. -- Associated Press --- NEW YORK City to Review Trump Contracts City officials are reviewing the city's contracts with Donald Trump after statements the GOP presidential hopeful made about Mexican immigrants. A spokeswoman for Mayor Bill de Blasio said Wednesday that the review is ongoing. It follows controversial statements Mr. Trump made at his presidential kickoff event last week. Mr. de Blasio's spokeswoman didn't provide any further details about the review. The Trump Organization operates several city concessions, including a golf course, two ice skating rinks and a carousel. The Trump Golf Links at Ferry Point Park opened in April in the Bronx and the Trump Organization has a 20-year concession agreement to operate the facility. On Thursday, City Councilman Mark Levine, chairman of the council's parks committee, said the city should exercise "terminate-at-will clauses" that are written into the contracts for Trump's concessions. "All of Mr. Trump's parks concessions include terminate-at-will clauses," he said in a statement. "The Parks Department should exercise this option to sever all ties with Mr. Trump immediately." Ron Lieberman, Trump's executive vice president, said Thursday that he was surprised by Mr. de Blasio's call for a review and believes the city has no legal ground to terminate them. He said Mr. Trump has had a "great public-private partnership" with the city after revitalizing the concessions. "There's no right to terminate these contracts because of something that was said," Mr. Lieberman said. "These contracts are binding." -- Associated Press --- NEW JERSEY Prosecutors Seek Imported Tiger Pelt Federal prosecutors are seeking to seize a taxidermic tiger that was shipped to New Jersey as part of a household move from France. Prosecutors say the fully mounted animal skin was imported into the U.S. in violation of the Endangered Species Act. It was seized by wildlife inspectors at Port Newark in December. Authorities say the shipment included a certificate that permitted its transport only within the European Union. The owner challenged the seizure, which prompted prosecutors to seek its forfeiture. Authorities say the tiger is a captive-bred female that was born in 2000. -- Associated Press --- CONNECTICUT State Police Seize Fireworks in Danbury Connecticut state police say illegal fireworks worth about $250,000 have been seized from a home and storage barn in Danbury. State police said Thursday that drugs were also found during the search and they expect several people will be arrested on felony charges. The investigation began after a complaint was made about the illegal use and sale of fireworks. -- Associated Press
event_description: Federal prosecutors are seeking to seize a taxidermic tiger imported in violation of the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal prosecutors' legal action regarding wildlife importation
characters: Federal prosecutors [Hero]; Owner of the taxidermic tiger [Villain]; Taxidermic tiger [Victim]
plot: Federal prosecutors seek to seize a taxidermic tiger imported in violation of the Endangered Species Act, as the owner challenges the seizure, prompting prosecutors to pursue forfeiture.
moral: The illegal importation of endangered species must be addressed, and the law should be enforced to protect wildlife.

Title: Some waste cleanups exempted by Senate from its reform bill
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Senate agreed to exempt from the regulatory-reform bill hazardous-waste cleanups conducted under the Superfund law.    Environmentalists had voiced strong concerns about disruptions to Superfund cleanups that might occur as a result of the bill, which requires elaborate cost-benefit analysis before a federal agency issues a regulation.    In the end, proponents of the bill were willing to drop earlier language applying the bill's provision to Superfund cleanups, leaving the matter to be decided by House and Senate members currently drafting a rewrite of the Superfund law. The language was struck from the bill without a recorded Senate vote.    But the Senate narrowly rejected a separate attempt by Sen. Frank Lautenberg (D., N.J.) to strip from the bill language likely to reduce the number of chemicals on the Environmental Protection Agency's toxic release inventory. The program requires large manufacturers to release data about the quantity of potentially hazardous chemicals they release into the air and water.    Separately, Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt said that he would be willing to consider granting estate-tax credits in exchange for conservation agreements by private property holders under the Endangered Species Act.    Mr. Babbitt was apparently responding to pressure from property-rights advocates who favor another regulatory reform bill, now awaiting action in the Senate Judiciary Committee. That measure would require federal compensation to individuals and businesses whose property loses value due to regulations. Currently the Endangered Species Act doesn't grant tax breaks of any kind in exchange for species protection, although an Internal Revenue Service rule does allow income-tax deductions for certain land easements.
event_description: Consideration of estate-tax credits for conservation agreements under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: congressional debate on regulatory reform and Superfund law
characters: Environmentalists [Hero]; Proponents of the bill [Villain]; Private property holders [Victim]; Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt [Unknown]; Sen. Frank Lautenberg [Unknown]
plot: Environmentalists raised concerns about potential disruptions to Superfund cleanups due to a regulatory-reform bill, leading to the Senate's decision to exempt these cleanups from the bill. Meanwhile, Babbitt indicated a willingness to consider estate-tax credits for conservation agreements, responding to property-rights advocates who are pushing for regulatory reforms that would compensate property owners affected by regulations.
moral: The text suggests that the current regulatory framework is problematic for property holders and advocates for the need to consider tax incentives for conservation to protect both property rights and endangered species.

Title: EPA Proposals Worry Industry And Agencies
Text: Northwest timber officials fear that clean-water rules recently proposed by the Environmental Protection Agency could prove too tough to meet. They may not have anything to worry about, though -- state environmental officials are complaining that the laws may be too tough to enforce. The rules, proposed by the EPA in August, would have states establish cleanup plans, and prioritize them, for those bodies of water within their borders that they've identified as too polluted for fishing and swimming. And states would be beholden to reduce contaminants from a broader range of sources than ever before. These would include logging activities -- the felling of trees, the building of roads -- that discharge sediments into nearby streams, rivers or lakes and might imperil the habitats of fish and other animals. The rules, timber-industry leaders say, place so many restrictions on where and how trees could be harvested that it's all but certain that jobs would be lost. "It would have a drastic, negative effect," says Dale Riddle, president of Seneca Sawmills in Eugene, Ore. "It's basically Big Brother appropriating our land and telling us when it's OK to harvest it." At the same time, the state agencies that would be in charge of implementing the rules say they don't have the manpower or money to enforce them. "At a time when we're struggling to find resources to carry out the existing rules, it's going to be hard to be able to say we're going to be able to do this," says Dave Peeler, watershed management supervisor for Washington's Department of Ecology. For more than 20 years, the focus of the Clean Water Act has been on stemming contaminants coming from specific, easily identifiable sources such as factory discharge pipes or sewage plants. Now the Clinton administration has decided it's time to expand the focus of the act. "Having largely succeeded with one part of the problem, we now realize we need to deal with the rest of it," says Tim Hamlin, the EPA's regional water quality unit manager in Seattle. If the rules are adopted, "the long-term effect could be to place many, many acres off-limits to forestry," says Jim Geisinger, president of the Northwest Forestry Association, a Portland-based trade group with 65 members. State water-quality rules governing the timber business are already strict enough, Mr. Geisinger adds. "To have the feds step in now and say they're going to get their hunk of flesh is pretty frightening," he says. In addition, timber officials say, the industry is reeling from federally imposed limits on harvests. In Washington and Oregon, the number of board feet cut has decreased 52% over the past 10 years, to 7,501,807 from 15,660,427, according to Timber Data Co. in Eugene, and the number of timber jobs in the two states has dropped by some 16% to around 119,000 since 1988. State officials in Washington and Oregon say they support the proposed rules in concept, but worry that without additional resources, enforcing them might be close to impossible. Mr. Peeler, with Washington's DOE, says the department is having "a hard enough time" contending with existing clean-water decrees. Finding the money to do the job in Oregon "would be a stretch in that all the resources we have are allocated to current programs," says Dick Pedersen, manager of the standards and assessments section with the Oregon Department of Environmental Quality. For example, spending cuts enacted by the Legislature in 1994 slashed about 35 positions in Washington's DOE. Of those, four have since been filled. Oregon's DEQ can hire only 52 full-time staffers to look into water permits, down from 60 five years ago, and four fewer than the department had budgeted for. There simply wasn't enough revenue to pay for all the positions. And the EPA seems to be in a rush, state officials say. The federal agency set Oct. 22 as the end of the public-comment period, and expects the new rules to be in place by the end of the year. "There's just no way that can happen," Mr. Peeler says. "It's a very complicated set of rules. The states are very concerned the EPA put us in a situation where we're bound to fail." Mr. Hamlin of the EPA says the timber industry's dire predictions about the rules are unfounded. "That environmental regulations increase costs is a legitimate concern," he says. "But to say this is going to destroy the industry is unconvincing. Industries that fall under environmental regulations have called wolf a few too many times for this claim to be convincing on its face." As for state agencies' concerns, Mr. Hamlin says that finding funds to enforce the rules is Congress and state legislators' problem. But there are more concerns among some state officials. In Washington, officials fear that the EPA proposal is at odds with the forest-and-fish plan the state and the timber industry signed off on earlier this year. The plan, which is designed to help Washington comply with the Endangered Species Act, allows continued logging on private land in return for a promise from the timber industry that it will take certain steps to protect aquatic species on 60,000 miles of forested stream habitat. As part of the agreement, the industry and state gave themselves 10 years to establish something called total maximum daily loads -- plans to limit the amount of contaminants a given body of water can take -- and to determine what needs to be done to bring the quality of polluted bodies of water up to standards. The EPA's proposed regulations strongly recommend that states come up with maximum loads for high-priority bodies of water within five years. High-priority waters include those that are home to species listed as endangered or threatened -- about half the bodies of water currently considered impaired under the Clean Water Act in Washington and Oregon. "It's counter to the agreement," says Chris West, a spokesman for the Northwest Forestry Association. Department of Ecology officials agree. "It's important for the industry to get stability," says Dick Wallace, a policy analyst with DOE. "That assurance came from the fact that we wouldn't change the rules on them through TMDL. The assurance was they knew how and when changes would occur. But now it's changing." State officials and timber-industry leaders have asked the EPA to extend the deadline for comments.
