The penalty functioned like a tax -- and other taxes, such as those on cigarettes, are enacted principally to create incentives rather than raise revenue, he said. Although the Obama administration always asserted the penalty was valid under the federal taxing power, until Thursday no court had fully accepted that. It was a day of surprises at the court, including the role of Chief Justice Roberts as the deciding vote instead of the usual swing voter, Justice Kennedy. Reading a joint dissent from the bench, Justice Kennedy said the majority engaged in "vast judicial overreaching" and decided "to save a statute Congress did not write." Before the decision was read, the packed courtroom was silent. The chief began by rejecting Congress's power to legislate the mandate under the Commerce Clause. Many in the audience thought he was en route to striking down the law. Then, about halfway through his 20-minute recitation, Chief Justice Roberts turned to Congress's taxing power. Some in the audience expressed surprise as listeners realized the law would survive. --- Anna Wilde Mathews, Evan Perez and Carol E. Lee contributed to this article. Credit: By Jess Bravin and Louise Radnofsky
event_description: The Supreme Court ruling limited Congress's authority under the Commerce Clause.
setting: Supreme Court ruling on health care legislation
characters: Chief Justice John Roberts [Hero]; President Barack Obama [Hero]; Republican-led states [Villain]; Americans without health insurance [Victim]; Republican presidential nominee Mitt Romney [Villain]; Justices Antonin Scalia, Anthony Kennedy, Clarence Thomas, and Samuel Alito [Villain]; Justices Ruth Bader Ginsburg, Stephen Breyer, Sonia Sotomayor, and Elena Kagan [Hero]
plot: The Supreme Court upheld the health care law's insurance mandate under Congress's taxing power, while limiting Congress's authority under the Commerce Clause, leading to a political battle over the law's future and implications for Americans without health insurance.
moral: The ruling highlights the need for continued support of health care reform to protect vulnerable Americans, while also cautioning against the expansion of federal power under the Commerce Clause.

Title: Bay Area: Proposal to Restore Delta Bogs Down --- Delay in Overhauling How Water From the Sacramento-San Joaquin Area Is Used Threatens Delicate Natural Habitat
Text: Fixing one of the Bay Area's crucial hydrological resources will take more time -- again. Government officials, environmental groups and water agencies say they don't expect to release a complete blueprint for improving California's Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta this year, a missed deadline that could threaten an ambitious plan to revamp the region's fragile water-supply hub and ecosystem. Participants in the group drafting the proposal, called the Bay Delta Conservation Plan, said in interviews that an environmental study and other plan components won't be completed by a key Nov. 18 meeting. That publication deadline was set after the group missed an August target. As a result, a complete draft of the plan won't be available for a 90-day public review, required by state and federal law, until late spring or early summer, says Lester Snow, secretary of California's Natural Resources Agency. "There has been natural slippage in the schedule," Mr. Snow says. He adds the group plans to release as much of the report as it can online next month to give an overview of progress it has made. An area roughly 740,000 acres fed by the Sacramento and San Joaquin rivers, the delta supports a diverse ecosystem and supplies much of the state with water for agriculture and human consumption. Decades of drawing water from the delta has severely damaged the area as a natural habitat. To alleviate the strain, planning officials and environmental groups in 2006 started the BDCP project to better manage the area. Operating under state and federal environmental laws, the plan would create a new permit system that would allow water agencies to pump water from the delta but also require them to pay for restoring the area's habitat. But the recent delay adds to the risks facing the delta. Any further holdups of the project, now four years running, could destabilize a fragile coalition of government, environmental and other groups behind the effort, BDCP committee members say. Meanwhile, the project faces growing costs. California water agencies already have spent some $140 million to study the delta project. An additional $100 million will be needed to fund the next step of the planning. Without a clear sense soon of progress, those agencies might turn off the money tap. "It's at a very delicate stage," says Tim Quinn, executive director of the Association of California Water Agencies. The plan's framers also say they need to have a draft or some progress report complete in time for a new governor and legislators arriving in Sacramento following the November elections. For the Bay Area, changes in the way water is distributed could affect the drinking-water supply in Santa Clara and Alameda counties, as well as water for agriculture, fishing and recreation throughout the region. The BCDP is run by a steering committee that includes state agencies, such as California's Department of Water Resources, and federal agencies including the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation. Environmental groups involved include the Nature Conservancy and the Environmental Defense Fund, among others. From the start, the project's proponents have struggled with how to balance the often-conflicting needs of an area that is both the hub of California's water system and one of North America's most important natural habitats. The area has long been a contested battlefield for farmers, water agencies, environmental groups and local citizens, which each see different value in the estuary. Years of drawing water from the delta have cut fish populations, while decades-old levees and other frail infrastructure increase chances that an earthquake could imperil a water source for 25 million people and a huge swath of California agriculture. By August, the protracted BDCP process led Mr. Snow and the U.S. Department of the Interior to hold a series of meetings with a select group of participants in hopes of making faster progress. Miffed that they weren't included, delta-area legislators, in a letter made public, criticized the "closed-door" meetings as an approach that "serves to further frustrate and anger those within the Delta community." Mr. Snow countered in a Sept. 23 statement that the meetings were a necessary step to crafting the draft plan. The criticism fed a belief among some critics that the BDCP will put certain interests above others. The project includes a proposal for a new canal or tunnel for controlling water flowing through the delta. Water agencies want it, but some environmentalists have long opposed the idea, fearing the project would allow water to be diverted to human needs at the expense of the habitat. BCDP committee members say that isn't the case and are hoping such concerns will be put to rest when the draft plan is released. While much of the draft -- which will likely be more than 1,000 pages and detail major aspects of managing the delta for the next 50 years -- is complete, unfinished sections include a chapter on funding and details of whether water agencies will be included in deciding how permits are awarded, say committee members. The group also needs more time to complete an analysis required by the Endangered Species Act, which will assess the project's impact on fish, wildlife and plant species in the delta. Richard Roos-Collins, a lawyer and water expert who represents the nonprofit American Rivers in the project, says he remains optimistic the draft will get done sooner rather than later. "The risk of failure is real but manageable," he says. The group has to "create some confidence that a better future is in sight." Credit: By Robert A. Guth
event_description: Completion of an environmental study required by the Endangered Species Act.
setting: California's Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta planning process
characters: California's Natural Resources Agency [Hero]; water agencies [Villain]; environmental groups [Hero]; Delta community [Victim]
plot: The completion of an environmental study and other components of the Bay Delta Conservation Plan is delayed, threatening the fragile ecosystem and water supply for millions. This delay risks destabilizing the coalition of stakeholders involved in the project, while water agencies may withdraw funding due to uncertainty.
moral: The ongoing delays in the BDCP process threaten the delicate balance of California's water system and its ecosystems, necessitating immediate action to ensure the plan is completed and to restore confidence among stakeholders.

Title: U.S. News: Fur Flies Over Canceled Grizzly Hunt
Text: A federal judge this week canceled what was to be the first grizzly bear hunt in 44 years in the lower 48 states, dealing a last-minute setback to those who have argued one of the best-known wild animals of the American West has not only recovered from near extinction, but poses a growing threat to humans and livestock. An estimated 50,000 grizzly bears once roamed across the western U.S., but hunting, dwindling food supplies, and other conflicts with encroaching human development nearly wiped the species out in the 1970s. By 1975, when the grizzly was declared threatened in the lower 48 states under the Endangered Species Act, the bears' population in and around Yellowstone National Park was estimated as low as 136. After the grizzly was put on the endangered species list, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service did everything from banning hunting to moving away tasty trash in an effort to revive the animals. Their population rebounded to more than 700 in the region around Yellowstone, which biologists say provides an ideal habitat for the bears because of its remoteness and abundant food supply. The giant predators were removed from protection in the park last year by the Trump administration's U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. Yellowstone is one of two remaining strongholds for grizzlies in the continental U.S., along with Montana's Glacier National Park, where they remain on the endangered species list. This year, Wyoming planned the first grizzly hunt since 1974 and Idaho its first since 1946. Yellowstone spans both states, along with Montana. The two hunts were set to begin Sept. 1, but were delayed by a federal judge. Environmental and tribal groups sued, arguing that Fish and Wildlife used a flawed analysis in delisting grizzlies from the endangered species list. After U.S. District Judge Dana Christensen in Missoula, Mont., ruled in their favor Monday, backers of the hunt were incensed. "The decision to return grizzly bears to the list of threatened and endangered species is further evidence that the [Endangered Species Act] is not working as its drafters intended," Wyoming Gov. Matt Mead said in a statement. Wyoming was in the process of issuing licenses for up to 22 grizzlies to be hunted and killed, while Idaho, with a lower population of the bears, had issued just one. The hunts were so controversial that many grizzly enthusiasts applied for the tags to help protect the bears. A wildlife photographer was awarded one of the Wyoming licenses and pledged to shoot the animals only with his camera. A healthier, larger population of grizzlies led to calls to take them off the endangered species list, beginning around 15 years ago. One of the reasons cited by advocates for lifting their protected status has been an increase in grizzly attacks on livestock in the Yellowstone region. At least 30 grizzly deaths in the area in 2017 were at the hands of humans, nine of which were over attacks on cattle, according to the U.S. Geological Survey. There has also been rising concern over the safety of humans amid the grizzly resurgence. Five of the eight known fatal maulings of humans by grizzlies in the 148-year history of Yellowstone National Park have taken place since 1980, while an additional 38 people have been injured in run-ins with the bears since that time, according to National Park Service records.   Credit: By Jim Carlton and Taylor Umlauf
event_description: A federal judge canceled the first grizzly bear hunt in 44 years, ruling in favor of environmental and tribal groups who argued against the delisting of grizzlies from the endangered species list.
setting: federal court case
characters: U.S. District Judge Dana Christensen [Hero]; environmental and tribal groups [Hero]; backers of the hunt [Villain]; grizzly bears [Victim]
plot: A federal judge canceled the first grizzly bear hunt in 44 years after environmental and tribal groups sued against the delisting of grizzlies from the endangered species list, which was supported by backers of the hunt who argued the bears posed a threat to humans and livestock.
moral: The Endangered Species Act needs to be upheld to protect grizzly bears, as their recovery is threatened by hunting and human conflict.

Title: World-Wide
Text: CONGRESS PASSED a spending bill that cuts rural housing, spares farm export aid. Lawmakers approved the $63.2 billion measure that cuts funding for rural housing and water programs while largely protecting agriculture export subsidies that had been targeted in GOP budget plans last spring. The measure is the first of the major domestic spending bills this year to go to Clinton, who is expected to sign it. The measure passed easily on a 288-132 vote. Southern poultry interests won a provision that bars U.S. Agriculture Department officials from enforcing new labeling rules to distinguish between fresh and frozen chicken. --- Fighting ebbed in much of Bosnia as a cease-fire took hold, but Muslim-Serb clashes were continuing in the northwest. U.S. mediator Holbrooke said he intends to keep Serbia, Croatia and Bosnia engaged in peace talks "until we get results." The talks are to begin Oct. 31. --- President Zedillo said in an interview that Mexico's economy will return to growth in the fourth quarter, and he called the recent volatility in Mexican markets "strictly temporary." Finance Minister Ortiz said Mexican banks will be required to adopt U.S. accounting standards by 1997. --- China is failing to crack down on copyright and trademark piracy, U.S. companies and officials charged. Seven months after China signed an accord promising action, the U.S. officials say the situation hasn't improved and may have worsened, with the number of illicitly copied compact disks soaring. --- PLO leader Arafat complained about Israel's delays in implementing a West Bank peace accord. Arafat, in a letter to President Rabin, demanded that the Israeli government comply with the timetable for withdrawing troops. Arafat plans to meet with Israeli Foreign Minister Peres on Sunday to discuss the issue. Libya's Gadhafi will stop expelling Palestinians, and the hundreds of Palestinians stranded on the Egyptian border will leave within two weeks, Egypt's foreign minister said. Gadhafi ordered the deportations to show that Israel-PLO peace accords don't resolve the plight of Palestinians living in refugee camps in Arab countries. --- A plastic surgeon was allowed to proceed with a suit against silicone breast implant makers to recoup alleged damage to his practice because of the controversy over the devices. The decision by a New York judge appears to be the first to recognize that surgeons, not just patients, may have viable claims for damages. --- The FBI broadened its search for clues in Monday's sabotage of an Amtrak train in Arizona, checking tire tracks in the desert several miles away, visiting homes in the area and speaking with railroad employees. About 40 of the 90 agents working near the site of the crash fanned out to interview residents and others. --- Clinton attacked a Medicare agreement reached between the GOP-led Congress and the American Medical Association, saying it would impose "another hidden tax" on seniors. The AMA decided to support the plan this week after House Republicans offered financial sweeteners for doctors. --- A House panel voted 27-17 to overhaul the Endangered Species Act and give increased financial protection to landowners. The committee bill represents the first step in rewriting the law that protects endangered and threatened plants and animals. --- Scientists showed a new way to insert genes into cells, which could lead to preventive vaccines for AIDS or other sexually transmitted diseases. The researchers used bacteria to carry genes into animal cells -- something that hadn't been done before. --- Austria's governing coalition fell apart, paving the way for mid-December elections that could bring to power a right-wing populist, Joerg Haider, who campaigns heavily on antiforeigner sentiment. --- Russia's top prosecutor warned that at least 100 people with criminal records are running for Parliament. In the absence of constitutional safeguards, Oleg Gaidanov said, some organized-crime figures might get elected on Dec. 17. --- A U.N. conference on land mines failed to agree on new international restrictions on the use of the weapons. Delegates from 42 nations had hoped to conclude their three-week Vienna meeting today by issuing a new protocol to reduce the risk that the mines pose to civilians.
event_description: A House panel voted to overhaul the Endangered Species Act and provide increased financial protection to landowners.
setting: a House panel debate on the Endangered Species Act
characters: House panel [Hero]; landowners [Victim]
plot: The House panel voted to overhaul the Endangered Species Act, which is intended to provide increased financial protection to landowners who may be affected by the law.
moral: The current Endangered Species Act is problematic for landowners, and the proposed overhaul is necessary to protect their financial interests.

Title: House GOP hopes to cut funding used to enforce dozens of U.S. regulations
Text: WASHINGTON -- House Republicans, opening another front in their effort to roll back federal regulations, hope to cut money used for enforcing dozens of federal health, safety and other standards.    The GOP leaders are targeting 62 federal mandates about topics ranging from fuel efficiency to wetlands. Their plan is to delete from the budget all money needed by the relevant government agencies to apply these regulations.    The effort in particular targets regulations labeled as onerous by small businesses, but doesn't seek to repeal any of them. Yet it takes a less direct route to the same end.    "The laws would remain on the books, but there would be no money to carry them out," says Rep. David McIntosh, an Indiana Republican who helped compile the list. "It's a signal to the agencies to stop wasting time on these regulations."    Targeted regulations and standards include ones that create a national registry for problem drivers, monitor air quality in the workplace, regulate water quality in eight Midwestern states, monitor air pollution, prohibit teenagers from loading paper compactors and regulate the sale of cigarettes to minors.    Republicans also seek to roll back the Motor Voter Act, a Clinton administration priority to increase voter registration.    While deregulation is a main element in the GOP's effort to reduce the scope of federal government, it so far has met limited success. Although unfunded-mandates legislation passed earlier this year, a moratorium on new regulation was defeated in the Senate. In addition, a bill requiring cost-benefit analyses of new regulations is in danger of stalling.    The new approach takes a less direct route, working through a less visible and more arcane part of the budget processappropriations.    Administration officials and deregulation opponents, however, concede this method could leave them powerless to prevent a regulation rollback. "At least in the House, this is foregone conclusion," said Gary Bass of OMB Watch, an advocacy group. "It's very much behind the scenes and extremely effective."    As line-by-line details of the fiscal 1996 federal budget are written over the next few months, appropriating subcommittees will try to include provisions restricting use of any federal money to enforce certain regulations.    For example, the appropriators would try to forbid the Environmental Protection Agency from using any funds to list new endangered species. Although the regulation allowing the EPA to do so would technically remain in effect, it would be rendered ineffective without funding.    "We have now seeded the process," said House Budget Committee Chairman John Kasich of Ohio. "We intend to enact as much of this as we can."    Though this effort is still young, House Republicans signaled their seriousness by including a specific list of targeted regulations in their recent budget proposal. The ones listed "are the most expensive and onerous and appear ripe for termination or reform," the House Budget Committee report said.    This tactic isn't new, but has been used sparingly in the past.    Though these regulations could be abolished by legislation, many Republicans consider the backdoor defunding route to be more effective. If they were repealed outright, President Clinton could veto the bill. But by removing enforcement funds for federal agencies, Congress would force President Clinton to veto the entire spending bill and thereby cut off all funds for the agencies.    "This route isn't only faster, but less incendiary," said Scott Hodge, a budget expert at the conservative Heritage Foundation. "It's as good as repealing them altogether."    ---                   Restricting Regulations    House Republicans are moving to cut off funding for, or otherwise restrict, enforcement of 62 federal regulations including:    -- National Maximum Speed Limit Mandate    -- Motorcycle Helmets Mandate    -- Endangered Species Act Mandates    -- Corporate Average Fuel Efficiency Standards    -- New Listings of Endangered Species    -- Section 404 Wetlands Program    -- Indoor Air Quality Regulations    -- Radon Action Program    -- Motor Voter Act Mandate    -- Enforcement of laws regarding cigarette sales to minors    -- Proposed Mega Rule of Meat and Poultry Inspection    -- Sunglasses Labeling    Source: House Budget Committee  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: House Republicans are moving to cut off funding for the enforcement of regulations related to the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Washington, D.C. legislative context regarding federal budget appropriations
characters: House Republicans [Villain]; relevant government agencies [Victim]; Gary Bass of OMB Watch [Hero]; President Clinton [Unknown]; Scott Hodge of the Heritage Foundation [Unknown]
plot: House Republicans are attempting to cut funding for the enforcement of 62 federal regulations, including those related to the Endangered Species Act, which would render these regulations ineffective without funding, thereby signaling to agencies to stop enforcing them.
moral: The problem is the House Republicans' effort to defund regulations, which undermines enforcement of important standards; the text suggests that Congress should resist these funding cuts to protect regulatory enforcement.

Title: Branching Out: New Zealanders Now Shear Trees Instead of Sheep --- Environmentalist Groups Let Weyerhaeuser Cut Timber In Ways Banned in U.S. --- `It's a Bit Like a Wheat Field'
Text: Corrections & Amplifications      SOPER-WHEELER Co. of California recently announced that it was contracting to grow cloned redwoods in New Zealand. A page-one article Thursday incorrectly said the redwoods were being genetically engineered.     (WSJ June 3, 2003)   NELSON, New Zealand -- This country is known for its natural beauty, and it has a fiercely protective environmental movement to match. But that didn't stop a forestry planner for a U.S. company from standing on a hill here, looking down over a majestic pine forest and cheerily talking of mowing it down as far as the eye can see.   "All that is going to go, mate," said Garry Tait, waving at a stand of pines on the South Island one afternoon in March. Mr. Tait, who helps oversee tree cutting for Weyerhaeuser Co., then pointed to another forest the next ridge over. "All that, too," he said.   The doomed trees were about to join hundreds of other acres of pines that Weyerhaeuser has recently felled as part of a cutting regimen more intense than anything it does in the U.S. But the harvest has aroused none of the anti-logging passions that the forest-products giant has come to expect back home in the American Northwest.   Weyerhaeuser has discovered a loggers' nirvana in New Zealand. Forced to look overseas for new timberlands, it has found a land where pines grow twice as fast as in many places in the U.S. thanks largely to rich soil and a favorable climate. It's also a place where environmentalists have cut a pragmatic deal that lets loggers chop down vast swaths of these pines as long as they stay out of virgin forests. Because Weyerhaeuser cuts nonnative trees growing on once-denuded sheep ranches, "it's a bit like a wheat field, you know," says Guy Salmon, head of a local environmental group called Ecologic.   This is a rare case in which globalization appears to have satisfied just about everyone. Felling and replanting these forests not only shields New Zealand's old-growth trees, it also has turned forestry into a fast-growing part of the nation's economy: Forestry exports hit $2 billion last year, exceeded only by dairy and meat exports. Much of this timber would otherwise have come from North American forests, so it makes U.S. environmentalists happy, too.   New Zealand's reliable wood supply has been compelling enough to draw a host of U.S. companies across the Pacific. International Paper Co., Weyerhaeuser's big U.S. rival, manages almost a million acres here. Rayonier Inc., of Jacksonville, Fla., manages roughly 300,000 acres.   Tree farms also flourish in the U.S., but they don't have the same kind of advantages as those in New Zealand. For one thing, New Zealand has lower labor costs and is closer to expanding markets for wood products in Asia.   Environmentalists in New Zealand also are more accepting of tree farms than are their counterparts in the U.S. That's partly because the New Zealand tree farms are on denuded land where sheep grazed for long periods. In the U.S., by contrast, environmentalists say many tree farms reduce habitat for forest animals. Another factor is that many U.S. tree farms are right next to natural forests, and environmentalists fret that the farmed trees will encroach on the natural ones.   The New Zealand tree farms aren't completely trouble-free. Local environmental groups quibble with the industry over such things as stream-side protections and erosion controls. There's concern that, with so many trees of the same species, a devastating tree bug could wipe many out and threaten indigenous plants. (Government officials say the trees are so well pruned that any diseases likely could be checked.) And the industry is still so new that timber companies face logistical problems such as a dearth of wood-processing plants and a lack of proper logging roads.   Still, the lessons learned in New Zealand are being applied elsewhere. Timber companies are finding that denuded farmland could be suitable for planting tree farms in other deforested places around the world. Weyerhaeuser recently started planting a 300,000-acre tree farm on such land in Uruguay. Other companies are planting forests on barren ground in Chile and South Africa.   Weyerhaeuser's road to New Zealand began with troubles at home. In 1990, environmentalists pressured the U.S. into listing the Northern spotted owl as threatened under the Endangered Species Act. That listing protected millions of acres of Northwest forests from logging. Meanwhile, the U.S. wasn't producing new tree farms fast enough to make up the difference. That left companies such as Weyerhaeuser, based in Federal Way, Wash., scrambling to find new sources.   Weyerhaeuser formed a department to find new growing areas overseas and deployed a Briton named Conor Boyd to begin scouting the Pacific Rim. Among Mr. Boyd's first stops was the Russian Far East. He quickly found that he hadn't shaken the anti-logging groups. Activists from a California environmental group followed him in 1992 to cities such as Khabarovsk, where he had been talking to officials about logging, and showed locals photographs of the aftermath of Weyerhaeuser clear-cutting -- a logging technique in which almost every tree is felled in one great swath.   Russia's political volatility made matters worse. After three of Weyerhaeuser's local contacts were found shot dead, one stuffed in a car trunk, Mr. Boyd abandoned his Russian probe and turned his full attention to New Zealand. In 1994, he took the 18-hour flight from Seattle to Auckland, journeying by helicopter and in a pickup truck from there to tour a 500,000-acre tree farm beneath the 9,090-foot Mount Ruapehu volcano. "The trees had been very well managed and were really good-looking," recalls Mr. Boyd, who retired last year.   Cutting good-looking trees like these in the U.S. often means facing anti-logging activists first, and Mr. Boyd was wary of their New Zealand counterparts. Before he would commit Weyerhaeuser money, he met local activists at their homes and offices to find out how they might respond to the U.S. company's moving in to cut on tree farms. He was amazed to find they had almost no concerns.   What Mr. Boyd saw was the product of a unique history. New Zealand's islands were once covered with trees. But as much as four-fifths of these forests were denuded, first by the Maoris who canoed here a millennium ago and then by English settlers in the 1800s. Sheep farms went where trees once stood, as wool and meat exports became New Zealand's main sources of wealth. Later, decades of tumbling wool and mutton prices led to abandoned ranchland. Needing jobs for soldiers returning from World War I, New Zealand in the 1920s set them to planting trees on the more marginal sheep spreads.   New Zealand officials, hoping the postwar planting program would reduce erosion on denuded slopes, tried dozens of species. They found that Monterey pines, which often grow bent and scraggly in their native California coast, thrived more than other trees in the rich soil and moist and moderate climate -- growing up to 40 inches in diameter and 40 feet tall in 30 years. "Lo and behold, they grew like triffids," says Jim Anderton, New Zealand's minister of regional development, referring to the man-eating plants from a 1951 science-fiction novel, "The Day of the Triffids."   It wasn't until the 1980s that New Zealanders realized how profitable it could be to harvest those farm-grown Monterey pines, as well as a smaller number of North American Douglas firs, both originally planted largely to stem erosion. By then, New Zealand's environmentalists had already battled the nation's timber industry over the felling of dwindling natural forests, such as the ones depicted in scenes of "The Lord of the Rings" trilogy filmed here. In 1991, the environmental groups made a deal with the industry: If the loggers left native forests alone, the environmentalists would drop most objections to cutting on tree farms.   The pragmatic approach taken by New Zealand's environmentalists stems largely from economic necessity. With a population of about four million in a land the size of Oregon, the country by the late 1980s was suffering a downturn in its mainstay industry of meat and dairy exports.   The New Zealand forestry accord -- combined with a government move to sell state-owned tree farms to the private sector -- ushered in a tree-farming boom. Since the mid-1980s, the volume of wood harvested off the nearly five million acres of tree farms has more than doubled, to about 8.5 billion board feet a year. New Zealand began wooing the foreign companies, government officials say, because it needed outside investment to harvest so much wood.   Weyerhaeuser moved into the market in 1997 by paying $200 million for a local company's logging rights and operations on about 200,000 acres. That represents only about 0.5% of the company's approximately 43 million acres in global timber holdings, mostly in the U.S. and Canada, but Weyerhaeuser executives say the New Zealand acreage is likely to grow. A unit of Switzerland's UBS AG also invested in the operation as Weyerhaeuser's minority partner.   Mr. Boyd agreed to abide by terms of the forestry accord. It calls for the company to leave the native forests alone, while also treading lightly around streams and other sensitive areas. In exchange, Weyerhaeuser gets to engage in some practices it couldn't elsewhere.   In the U.S., for example, Weyerhaeuser must leave trees standing in large buffers between clearcuts of a certain maximum size. In New Zealand, it doesn't have to leave any buffers -- a much more economical way to cut trees. The results are a jarring shaved look, but it leaves the hillsides in better shape than the sheep did when the ground was denuded ranchland. Weyerhaeuser then replants the forests to be harvested decades later.   As Weyerhaeuser and other loggers began building a track record of turning forests into wealth, local farmers began throwing their lot in with the foreigners, providing even more land for tree farms. One such farmer was Gary Hewetson, who sold his family's 200-acre sheep farm to another timber company and joined Weyerhaeuser as a truck dispatcher two years ago. He could no longer make any money raising sheep, he explains. Like many farmers here, he had a tough time adjusting after New Zealand dropped its big sheep subsidies in the mid-1980s.   "I must admit I hate to see some good farmland being planted in trees," says the 44-year-old Mr. Hewetson, wiping his brow during a break in a lumberyard. "But it boils down to how much money can you make off your land."   In New Zealand, loggers such as Weyerhaeuser increasingly benefit from timberlands, soon coming into maturity, that other farmers planted decades ago. Tom Rogers, a retired forestry manager who lives in the North Island town of Rotorua, planted 25 acres of Monterey pine (or Radiata, as it is known here) on his family's 40-acre farm two decades ago as a hedge against the future. With many of the trees at or near harvest age, now it's about to pay off. "That is basically a superfund for the family," says Mr. Rogers, who plans to sell the harvesting rights.   With trees like these and many more on national lands nearing maturity, local executives talk of a "Wall of Wood" becoming available over the next few years that could increase New Zealand's wood exports as much as six times the current level by 2025. Other multinationals from Japan, South Korea and elsewhere are moving in, while some companies are experimenting with new trees. California-based Soper-Wheeler Co., for instance, recently announced that it was contracting to grow genetically engineered redwoods here.   So much transformation is remarkable in a land not known for rapid change. "My father's generation and his father's generation spent a lot of time clearing the land," says Andrew Karalus, a farmer's son now working as a Weyerhaeuser forestry manager. "Here we are planting it again."
event_description: Listing of the Northern spotted owl as threatened under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: New Zealand's forestry industry and environmental context
characters: Weyerhaeuser Co. [Hero]; New Zealand environmental movement [Hero]; Northern spotted owl [Victim]; U.S. environmentalists [Villain]; Local farmers [Victim]
plot: Weyerhaeuser Co. seeks new timber sources due to U.S. environmental regulations protecting the Northern spotted owl, leading them to New Zealand where they engage in logging practices that are accepted by local environmentalists, benefiting both the economy and the environment.
moral: The problem of limited timber resources in the U.S. due to environmental protections can be addressed by utilizing denuded lands in New Zealand for sustainable forestry, which satisfies economic needs while protecting native forests.

Title: High Court to Decide English-Only Case
Text: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal The justices are expected to hear oral arguments in the fall, with a decision likely in 1997. Buffeted by fear of cultural fragmentation and, in some areas, alarm over immigration, nearly half the states and some cities have enacted legislation declaring English as their official language, according to Arizonans for Official English, the group pressing to revive the Arizona amendment. Arizona voters approved a ballot initiative in 1988 that went further than most other states. In addition to the symbolic gesture of labeling English as the official language, the Arizona amendment bars public employees from using other languages for official duties, with only a few exceptions. The exceptions include teaching English or foreign languages and police communication with suspects or victims. Other states with similarly strongly worded English-only measures include New Hampshire, South Carolina, South Dakota and Nebraska, according to U.S. English, a Washington, D.C.-based organization. Congress is considering legislation that would have roughly the same effect at the national level. The Arizona amendment was challenged in 1988 by a state employee who sometimes used Spanish while processing medical-malpractice claims against the state. A federal judge struck down the amendment, finding that it violated the free-speech guarantee of the First Amendment of the U.S. Constitution. Arizona's former Gov. Rose Mofford, a critic of the state measure, declined to appeal, but Arizonans for Official English was permitted to intervene and filed an appeal. A federal appeals court based in San Francisco last year agreed with the trial judge that the amendment should be struck down. The appeals court reasoned that choice of language is equivalent to speech itself and that the Arizona amendment infringed the rights of both residents who don't speak English but need information and the civil servants who want to help them. In its appeal to the Supreme Court, Arizonans for Official English attacked the notion that public employees have a right to use a particular language and that residents have a right to be served in a tongue other than English. (Arizonans for Official English vs. Arizona) --- Endangered Species Separately, the justices said yesterday they would decide whether property owners may sue under the Endangered Species Act to curb, rather than promote, government conservation efforts. The San Francisco federal appeals court ruled last year that the controversial 1973 species-protection act can't be used by ranchers and others in Oregon who object to federal restrictions on how much water can be drawn from reservoirs in southern Oregon and northern California for irrigation. The limits are designed to protect the habitat of two kinds of endangered fish: the Lost River sucker and the shortnose sucker. The ranchers' suit alleged that federal officials failed to follow a provision of the Endangered Species Act that requires consideration of the "economic impact" of preservation efforts. The act permits privately filed suits by "any person," but the federal appeals court interpreted that seemingly broad language to mean any person seeking to protect endangered species. (Bennett vs. Plenert) --- Taxes on Damages In another action, the justices said they would clarify whether people who win punitive damages in personal-injury lawsuits have to pay federal income taxes on them. Punitive damages are designed to punish and deter wrongdoing, in contrast to compensatory damages, which reimburse losses. The high court will consider a case stemming from the 1983 death of a Kansas woman who suffered from toxic shock syndrome allegedly caused by a tampon made by International Playtex Inc. Her family sued the company and won $10 million in punitive damages, as well as $1.5 million in compensatory damages that aren't at issue in the Supreme Court case. The Tax Code provision at issue exempts from taxation "damages received . . . on account of personal injuries or sickness." The federal appeals court based in Denver said the provision was vague but ruled for the Internal Revenue Service. (O'Gilvie vs. U.S.) WASHINGTON -- The Supreme Court, taking a tentative step into the controversy over language and immigration, agreed to decide whether states may require civil servants to use English in carrying out their duties. The case concerns an invalidated "English only" amendment to Arizona's state constitution. It could get sidetracked, however, on procedural issues having to do with whether the dispute has been litigated by the proper sort of parties. One of the procedural questions is whether private backers of the measure can seek its reinstatement after the state of Arizona itself dropped out of the case.
event_description: The Supreme Court will decide whether property owners can sue under the Endangered Species Act to challenge government conservation efforts.
setting: federal court case
characters: property owners [Villain]; federal officials [Hero]; endangered fish species [Victim]
plot: The Supreme Court will decide if property owners can sue under the Endangered Species Act to challenge government conservation efforts, following a federal appeals court ruling that upheld restrictions on water usage to protect endangered fish habitats.
moral: The text suggests that the Endangered Species Act should be upheld to protect endangered species, emphasizing the importance of government conservation efforts over property owners' objections.

Title: U.S. to Propose Listing Rare Smelt as 'Threatened'
Text: Federal officials will recommend that the California delta smelt be listed as a threatened species, a move that is likely to trigger a fierce legal battle.    An Interior Department official said the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service will make the recommendation as early as today in Portland, Ore. Under the Endangered Species Act, granting a species threatened status requires steps to save it from possible extinction. However, it is a lower-priority classification than "endangered," which requires tougher and swifter action to ensure a species' survival.    The smelt's plight has been closely watched as a test case in the growing confrontation between conservation and economics. The three-inch fish lives in the delta formed by the Sacramento and San Joaquin Rivers as they flow into the San Francisco Bay. It is in trouble in large measure because of heavy pumping of water from the delta, most of which goes to irrigate desert farmland in Southern California.    Fish and Wildlife Service field scientists originally recommended that the smelt be granted endangered status because it was rapidly headed for extinction. However, after an unusually heavy lobbying blitz from politically potent agricultural interests, California congressmen and state government officials concerned that tough smelt protections might disrupt the state's water system, the service's top Washington officials rejected the field scientists' findings and ordered that they recommend no more than threatened status for the fish. The scientists allege that their superiors caved in to political influence, but Interior Department officials heatedly deny that.    The proposal to list the smelt as threatened isn't likely to satisfy environmentalists and probably will be challenged in court. The Sierra Club Legal Defense Fund already has threatened to sue the Fish and Wildlife Service over the smelt issue. Lawyers there couldn't be reached to comment on how they intend to proceed.    David Behar, executive director of the Bay Institute of San Francisco, an environmental group, said listing the smelt as threatened would be a "grave disappointment." Mr. Behar said: "You have politics deciding the future of a species and an entire ecosystem, and that is a tragedy."    The proposed listing triggers a review and public comment period of up to one year.    ---    Barbara Rosewicz contributed to this article.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Federal officials will recommend that the California delta smelt be listed as a threatened species.
setting: Portland, Ore., under the Endangered Species Act
characters: California delta smelt [Victim]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; agricultural interests [Villain]; David Behar [Hero]; Sierra Club Legal Defense Fund [Hero]
plot: Federal officials will recommend the California delta smelt be listed as a threatened species, which may lead to legal challenges from environmental groups due to political influence affecting the decision.
moral: The problem is the political influence undermining the protection of the delta smelt, and the text suggests that stronger actions should be taken to ensure the species' survival and resist agricultural pressures.

Title: Fairy Shrimp Tale: Prawns in a Battle With Developers --- One Man's Mud Puddle May Be Another's Vernal Pool -- Ditch or Natural Habitat?
Text: SACRAMENTO, Calif. -- This is a fairy-shrimp tale that came true. Once upon a time -- up until 1990 -- three species of fairy shrimp were so obscure that they dwelt on this planet unnoticed by humankind. But in just four years, the vernal pool, longhorn and conservancy fairy shrimp have gone from undetected crustaceans to prawns in a bigger battle between environmentalists and developers. These tiny creatures aren't the stuff that shrimp cocktails are made of. Biological cousins of the Sea Monkeys sold through ads in comic books, fairy shrimp measure about an inch fully grown. They swim upside down in mud puddles throughout California's Central Valley, where they feed on plankton. But the most remarkable attribute of the three newly discovered species is their ability to turn the faces of Sacramento County developers red. The fairy shrimp and the developers unwittingly lived together in peace until May 1990, when the three new species were first written up in the Journal of Crustacean Biology. In itself, the discovery was hardly earthshaking. Scientists identify 7,000-odd new plant and animal species somewhere in the world each year. Harvard entomologist Edward O. Wilson, a leading scientific advocate of biodiversity, estimates that the 1.4 million known species logged thus far represent less than one-tenth the total currently inhabiting the earth. "It is a myth that scientists break out champagne when a new species is discovered," Mr. Wilson has written. "Our museums are glutted with new species." What happened next, however, was somewhat more unusual. Few creatures (the snail darter comes to mind) have gone so rapidly from discovery to the threat of extinction. In November 1990 -- six months after the journal article appeared -- Roxanne Bittman, a botanist with California's Fish and Game Department, petitioned the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service to list the three new fairy shrimp species as endangered. The agency formally proposed the listing in 1992. A final decision is pending. If the drive to declare the species endangered succeeds, developers say many of their projects here will be dead in the water. So, rival biologists have been mud wrestling. The developers have sent a team of environmental consultants up and down the state dipping canvas nets into muddy ditches to show that California has no shortage of fairy shrimp. They take to the skies to videotape the fairy shrimp's natural habitat -- geological creases in the dry Sierra foothills that fill with rainwater in the winter months, becoming vernal pools come springtime. From the co-pilot's seat of a droning prop plane, Paul Sugnet, a biologist working for developers led by Angelo Tsakopoulos, the owner of AKT Development Corp., points to streaks of glinting blue in the greenish-brown landscape below. Conservationists say these vernal pools are being bulldozed out of existence. Mr. Sugnet disagrees: "Vernal pools everywhere you look," he says, peering through sunglasses. That sort of talk enrages Robert Holland, a lean, grizzled biologist and sheep farmer. Wading ankle-deep in a muddy pool in search of fairy shrimp, he mutters about "land rapers." Mr. Holland denies he is referring to Mr. Sugnet, who disputes Mr. Holland's claim that at least two-thirds, and possibly as many as 90%, of California's vernal pools have vanished since Europeans first settled here. Mr. Holland wrote a letter in support of the proposed endangered-species listing, taking aim at "esurient land developers, their sniveling attorneys and their `accepta pecunaria quisque advocata' consultants." (A brief glossary: "Esurient" means "greedy," and the italicized phrase, Mr. Holland says, is "bad Latin" for "any position for a price.") Oh, yeah, well, the conservationists and regulators are "zealots" who wrongly view developers as "bad people who only take care of themselves," counters Mr. Tsakopoulos, a wealthy home builder. If the proposed designation is approved, the developers contend, it could cost them up to $500 million during the next decade in foregone construction. Officials at the Interior Department's Fish and Wildlife Service answer that the 1973 Endangered Species Act doesn't permit them to take economics into account when considering a listing. Besides, such action is far less likely to stop building projects than to make developers "plan and think," says Cay Goude, an assistant field supervisor at the agency's Sacramento office. Suspicions abound that in the ongoing debate the fairy shrimp are actually a red herring. People on the developers' side whisper that Ms. Bittman, the botanist, is using the new species as a pretext for protecting various exotic plant species, such as Downingia pusilla and Orcuttia viscida, that are native to the Central Valley's vernal pools. Ms. Bittman denies that she is just out to preserve plants. But she agrees that "I am concerned about plants in the habitat." She adds that "plant species don't receive the same level of protection as animal species" under the Endangered Species Act. So why not petition the federal government to protect the habitat, not merely the shrimp? Because "there's no endangered natural community law," she explains. The federal government can move to limit development in a "critical habitat," but only after a species within that habitat has been declared endangered. On the other hand, evidence that a habitat is disappearing can be used as a basis for declaring species found in the habitat to be endangered. (Vernal pools destroyed by bulldozers might do the trick.) In the regulatory kingdom of endangered species, such a ruling then means that the federal government can move to protect the habitat. But developers claim that the fairy shrimp -- whose Sea Monkey relatives are so hardy their eggs can be shipped in little packets by mail -- aren't necessarily all that partial to vernal pools. Peter Balfour, a biologist working for Mr. Sugnet, makes this point by scooping out one of the fairy shrimp proposed for listing as endangered from a narrow, rain-filled ditch. Conservationists argue that when fairy shrimp are found in such places, it usually means the spot is a vestigial vernal pool. But this one runs between a highway called Industrial Boulevard and a gravelly railroad bed. Mr. Balfour says the ditch was probably created by bulldozers when they laid the railroad tracks. Despite their herculean efforts to prove that the newly discovered species are here to stay, some developers confess they can't see much difference between one fairy shrimp and another. "We're talking about invertebrates," says Chris Vrame, project manager for AKT Development. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Petition to list three fairy shrimp species as endangered.
setting: California's Central Valley, involving the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and developers
characters: Roxanne Bittman [Hero]; developers [Villain]; fairy shrimp [Victim]; Robert Holland [Hero]; Cay Goude [Hero]
plot: Roxanne Bittman petitions to list three fairy shrimp species as endangered, prompting developers to argue against the listing, claiming it will harm their projects. Robert Holland supports the listing, while developers attempt to disprove the shrimp's existence and importance.
moral: The text suggests that the endangered listing of the fairy shrimp is necessary to protect them from harmful development, despite the economic implications for developers. It advocates for prioritizing species protection over development interests.

Title: World-Wide
Text: Democrats hurtled toward a deadline for passing a roughly $1 trillion infrastructure plan in the House, with the bill's fate in jeopardy as they struggled to mend intraparty rifts threatening to derail Biden's domestic agenda. --- Doctors and pharmacies are rapidly signing up patients for Covid-19 booster shots, many without requiring proof of eligibility under standards that federal officials set last week. --- A House Select Committee has issued 11 subpoenas for documents and testimony from people allegedly involved in planning the Jan. 6 rally at Washington's Ellipse before Trump supporters attacked the U.S. Capitol. --- The FTC is considering strengthening online privacy protections, including for children, in an effort to bypass legislative logjams in Congress. --- The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service said 23 species ranging from fish to birds are now extinct and proposed delisting them from the Endangered Species Act. --- Britney Spears's father was suspended as conservator of her estate, as a court moves toward unwinding a legal arrangement the pop star has called abusive.
event_description: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service proposed delisting 23 species from the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; 23 species [Victim]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service proposed delisting 23 species from the Endangered Species Act, indicating that these species are now extinct.
moral: The extinction of these species highlights the failure of conservation efforts, and there is a need for stronger protections to prevent further loss of biodiversity.

Title: U.S. News: High Court Orders Review of Frog Habitat
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Supreme Court gave Weyerhaeuser Co. another chance Tuesday to possibly develop a Louisiana timber farm, which could doom an endangered species of frog, telling a lower court to review the government's decision to designate the land as critical habitat. The unanimous opinion, written by Chief Justice John Roberts, suggested a compromise on a case heard by an eight-member court before the confirmation of Justice Brett Kavanaugh. At arguments Oct. 1, conservative justices voiced concerns with the income loss Weyerhaeuser could suffer if construction were limited, and liberals argued the Endangered Species Act is broad and intended to mitigate the damage human encroachment inflicts on the environment. If the court split evenly, the dusky gopher frog's lower-court win would have been upheld. The decision delays the ultimate fate of the rural tract in St. Tammany Parish, a New Orleans suburb. "While we're disappointed, the ruling doesn't weaken the mandate to protect habitat for endangered wildlife," said Collette Adkins, an attorney who argued for the protections before the Supreme Court. Weyerhaeuser said it supported species conservation but in this case, the "designation was applied to a tract of land where the species has not been present for decades and where the specific habitat conditions it requires do not exist."   Credit: By Jess Bravin
event_description: The Supreme Court is reviewing the government's decision to designate land as critical habitat for an endangered species of frog.
setting: a Supreme Court case reviewing the government's decision on critical habitat designation
characters: Weyerhaeuser Co. [Villain]; dusky gopher frog [Victim]; Collette Adkins [Hero]
plot: The Supreme Court allows Weyerhaeuser Co. to potentially develop land, which threatens the dusky gopher frog, while suggesting a review of the government's critical habitat designation.
moral: The problem is the potential harm to the dusky gopher frog due to development, and the text advocates for maintaining protections for endangered species and their habitats.

Title: Timber Giant Cuts Rare Deal On Rare Birds
Text: BAINBRIDGE, Ga. -- Three years ago, Craig Hedman could drive to the far north corner of International Paper Co.'s Southlands Experiment Forest near the Georgia-Florida line and count on one hand the nests he'd find for red-cockaded woodpeckers, a Southeastern bird on the federal endangered-species list. These days, as he winds his pickup truck through 5,300 acres of pine stands in land dedicated as habitat for relocated or newly hatched woodpeckers, Dr. Hedman, the company's forest-ecology manager, can point to 16 nests high in the cavities of old pine trees. There are so many birds, in fact, that International Paper sees a rare business opportunity in its rare birds. Under a novel agreement with the federal government, the Purchase, N.Y., pulp-and-paper mill operator can sell red-cockaded woodpecker "credits" to other firms whose logging or real-estate development would destroy woodpecker habitat. (As part of the deal, the buyer of the credits must also try to relocate the birds it displaces.) "It used to be the woodpeckers were seen as a problem," Dr. Hedman says, since the birds' endangered status restricted land use. "Now having a red-cockaded woodpecker is actually an asset." The company may one day even find it more lucrative to sell woodpecker credits on some pine tracts than to harvest them. Currently, the company uses only land designated for research purposes for its woodpecker-credit program. Company officials believe just one credit could sell for as much as $250,000 to a "motivated buyer," such as a real-estate developer in a hurry to move ahead with a resort project. International Paper can also use the credits to get at some of its other valuable timberland that the birds have made off-limits for harvesting. "This is not a game for small private landowners," notes Ralph Costa, recovery coordinator for the red-cockaded woodpecker at the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and principal architect of the government's plan to save the bird. Because of the expense -- either of buying a credit, or, for a seller, of maintaining a scientific staff -- he says, "this system is going to work primarily on the development end, for the golf courses and the big corporations." Though some environmentalists question the arrangement, the Fish and Wildlife Service says it could help stop the decline of the nation's total population of red-cockaded woodpeckers -- now estimated at about 12,400. Under the terms that International Paper inked last year with the Fish and Wildlife Service, the company is required to maintain 18 woodpecker families -- with each family averaging 2 1/2 birds -- on its land throughout the Southeast. (The wildlife service sets a different baseline for other timber companies, according to field surveys.) When it exceeds this baseline, International Paper can begin assuming responsibility for birds on behalf of another developer or logging company that wants to clear-cut land that, because it hosts woodpeckers, is protected by the federal Endangered Species Act. So far, International Paper hasn't had any takers for credits it's been offering since last fall for two woodpecker families above its minimum 18. Sharon Haines, manager of sustainable forestry and policy for International Paper, says the company has had some inquiries, but declines to identify prospective buyers. While many timber companies have struck woodpecker-conservation deals, only one other -- Champion International Corp. -- has sold bird credits. In 1998, Champion, Stamford, Conn., sold a future woodpecker credit for $50,000 to a Texas subdivision developer. The government prohibited the developer from proceeding with plans for four years while Champion attempts to ensure that new birds hatch, mature and mate on its land. Whatever its first credit fetches, the income will be like birdseed to International Paper, a company that in 1999 saw $24.6 billion in sales and $183 million in profit. Although the nascent woodpecker-credit trade has been lauded by environmental groups such as New York-based Environmental Defense, it has been criticized by others. Detractors say cultivating woodpeckers in one area to cancel them out elsewhere doesn't add up to an overall strengthening of the species. "Conservationists tend to be suspicious of anything that takes a species away from a place," says Eric Draper, conservation director for the National Audubon Society in Florida, which is skeptical of the credit program. But International Paper and government officials argue the birds that survive relocation to a protective habitat end up faring better because they get to live and mate with others in a carefully managed forest rather than struggling in a more isolated environment. However, an estimated 90% of adults and 50% of juvenile birds die from the trauma of being moved. International Paper's arrangement with the red-cockaded woodpecker, which was designated as endangered in 1970, developed out of the federal government's efforts to save the bird in the early '90s. The government wanted to avoid the kind of bitter standoff between industry and conservationists that beset the Pacific Northwest in the late 1980s, when logging companies were banned from operating in the northern spotted owl's habitat. After watching a flurry of woodpecker-conservation deals in the early to late '90s -- including the first one in 1993 under which Atlanta-based Georgia-Pacific Corp. agreed to set up woodpecker-protection measures in Arkansas and northern Louisiana -- International Paper opened talks with the Fish and Wildlife Service about its own plan. At first, the company was mainly interested in using woodpecker credits to better exploit logging land of its own that held a red-cockaded woodpecker group, whose territory can include between 60 and 300 acres of timber that has often been valued at between $2,000 and $3,000 per acre. But, in part to test how much the woodpecker-credit market will bear, International Paper decided to try to sell the rights first. Dr. Hedman and his colleagues have tried to encourage mating by simulating the bird's natural habitat: They have installed nearly four dozen artificial cavities in clusters of 80-plus-year-old pine trees. Last year, the team had its first sign the plan was working. A woodpecker native to the site bred with a young female bird that was imported from the federal government's Apalachicola National Forest in Florida's panhandle. The female bore three eggs, one of which hatched. Although the baby bird didn't survive, researchers were encouraged and hope to see at least five breeding pairs by this spring. Credit: Staff Reporters of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: International Paper's agreement with the federal government to sell red-cockaded woodpecker credits.
setting: International Paper's Southlands Experiment Forest near the Georgia-Florida line, under a novel agreement with the federal government
characters: International Paper [Hero]; red-cockaded woodpecker [Victim]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; environmentalists [Villain]; Eric Draper [Villain]
plot: International Paper enters an agreement with the federal government to sell red-cockaded woodpecker credits, allowing them to profit while ensuring the protection and relocation of the woodpeckers, which were previously seen as a hindrance to land use.
moral: The text suggests that the woodpecker credit program is a viable solution to balance economic interests and conservation efforts, advocating for the continuation and expansion of such programs to protect endangered species.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial): Spotted Democrats
Text: No word yet on whether the New Democrats will be named soon as deserving protection under the Endangered Species Act. We note, for instance, that New Democrat John Breaux was spotted recently scampering away from the prospect of replacing the Horn-Rimmed Mitchell Liberal as Senate Majority Leader. Now, reports from this past Tuesday's Democratic primaries in Texas suggest that while a rich man can survive getting squashed by the party's core interest groups, a centrist minority candidate probably can't. First take the case of Richard Fisher, the former Wall Street financier who this week easily won the Democratic U.S. Senate nomination in Texas. Mr. Fisher has always been a Democrat, served in Jimmy Carter's Treasury Department and helped Bill Clinton found the moderate Democratic Leadership Council. Nonetheless, Mr. Fisher was attacked by Texas liberals for having sometimes given money to Republicans and even for having married one. In 1992, he supported Ross Perot along with many other Texas Democrats who had doubts about Mr. Clinton. He won 54% of the vote against Jim Mattox, a veteran political brawler who was backed by party liberals. Significantly, Mr. Fisher won the Hispanic vote in the Rio Grande Valley 56% to 44%, aided by his fluency in Spanish and pro-Nafta views. He now faces an uphill battle with GOP Senator Kay Bailey Hutchison. Mr. Fisher prevailed because he could spend $2 million of his own money getting his moderate message out. New Democrats without such personal financial clout find themselves isolated and ostracized. Beverley Clark is a businesswoman and former Houston city council member. This year she ran for a vacant Congressional seat in Houston that was 27% black but had never before been held by an African-American. However, Ms. Clark wasn't welcomed by the party establishment. She favors school vouchers and term limits and opposes abortion and gun control. Polls show many ordinary minority voters agree with her, and even though she spent only $9,000 in the March primary her army of grass-roots volunteers helped her win 37% of the vote and place first. Ms. Clark won in black areas but also easily carried suburban Fort Bend County. Democratic leaders struck back. For the runoff, they rallied around Ken Bentsen, who took second place with 26%. Mr. Bentsen, a nephew of Treasury Secretary Lloyd Bentsen, raised buckets of money from business interests and from liberal groups terrified at the prospect of Ms. Clark joining the unified Congressional Black Caucus. "They did everything to defeat me," she says. Politically connected black ministers helped Mr. Bentsen. A flood of street money turned out liberal voters. Mr. Bentsen spent $250,000 to Ms. Clark's $25,000. Rain and the traditional lower turnout for runoffs led to a third fewer votes cast. Mr. Bentsen won with 64% of the vote. Ms. Clark says she's fed up with the stranglehold that liberals exercise over the Democratic Party. "They preach diversity, but they practice philosophical conformity," she told us. In the fall, she will support Gene Fontenot, a physician who won the Republican nomination. She may find it a less hostile environment.
