Title: Group seeks changes for logging in Canada
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Natural Resources Defense Council submitted a blistering report on Canadian logging practices to the North American Commission on Environmental Cooperation.    The U.S. environmental group's report represents the first substantial request for action submitted to the commission, which was created under a side agreement to the North American Free Trade Agreement; the commission opened its doors last fall. After the NRDC submits additional data, the commission may issue recommendations on forestry practices in North America, or, if it finds Canada in violation of its own environmental laws, it may issue a fine of as much as $20 million.    The NRDC has thus far been vague about what it expects the commission to do. But the report's submission is clearly aimed at increasing pressure on Canada to improve its environmental practices with respect to logging in British Columbia. According to the report, the province allows "clear-cuts," or elimination of forest across a broad swath of land, that are four to six times larger than the maximum allowed across the border in Washington state; it requires no species protection, unlike Washington state, where the northern spotted owl and other species are protected under the Endangered Species Act; and it provides substantially weaker protection to salmon-bearing streams than that in the U.S.
event_description: The report highlights the lack of species protection in Canadian logging practices compared to U.S. standards under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: North American Commission on Environmental Cooperation
characters: Natural Resources Defense Council [Hero]; Canada [Villain]; northern spotted owl and other species [Victim]
plot: The Natural Resources Defense Council submits a report to the North American Commission on Environmental Cooperation, highlighting Canada's inadequate logging practices, which may lead to recommendations or fines against Canada, thereby increasing pressure for improved environmental practices.
moral: The problem is Canada's harmful logging practices that lack species protection, and the text suggests that the commission should take action to enforce stricter environmental standards.

Title: Republicans Rethink Autumn Game Plan; Hurricanes, DeLay Indictment Have Shaken the Political Landscape
Text: Washington -- AFTER A BRUISING opening to their fall political season, President Bush and his Republican allies in Congress now face the prospect of scaling back their agenda for the rest of the year to something more modest than they once envisioned. A day after House Republican leader Tom DeLay was indicted, congressional Republicans and President Bush met in a show of unity at the White House, where officials insisted that the indictment wouldn't affect their legislative ambitions. They ticked off a long list of measures, including hurricane relief and new energy legislation, that they hope to push through Congress by year end. But the new to-do list reflects the drastic changes in the political landscape since last November, when President Bush was re-elected by a margin of three million votes. Then, Mr. Bush laid out aggressive plans for a Social Security overhaul that would add private accounts while paring guaranteed benefits, potentially far-reaching changes in the tax code, a permanent repeal of the estate tax and the extension of high-profile first-term tax breaks, including those on dividends. Now, many of the initiatives, some of which were already in deep trouble, have been all but killed off by the recent crop of disasters and mistakes -- the loss of Mr. DeLay, the hurricane debacle, Iraq war worries, high energy prices and continuing investigations into White House aides and friendly lobbyists. Left in a defensive stance, the administration and congressional Republicans are scrambling to come up with priorities suited to a rougher political terrain. As outlined by White House and congressional officials yesterday, those new goals include the rebuilding of New Orleans, with its multibillion-dollar price tag; the crafting of new conservative- influenced measures to help alleviate the Gulf Coast poverty exposed by Hurricane Katrina; and a new energy bill designed to address surging energy prices. The new measures also are likely to include cuts in spending elsewhere to help offset the mammoth costs of the hurricanes and mollify an increasingly restive conservative base. Republicans insist they remain hopeful, particularly on the tax front. And whatever their problems, they still control both ends of Pennsylvania Avenue, which is a huge advantage. They also face a Democratic Party that so far has shown itself to be incapable of taking advantage of Bush soft spots. While House Democratic leader Nancy Pelosi vowed yesterday to pound away at what she called the "culture of corruption," it isn't clear her party can capitalize enough on its opponents' missteps to make inroads in the 2006 election. It is hardly unusual for presidents to hit rough patches and dips in their popularity in second terms. Sometimes they overreach; sometimes they run short on energy or political capital. Ronald Reagan endured both the loss of Republican control of the Senate and the Iran-Contra scandal in his second term, and Bill Clinton endured the Monica Lewinsky scandal and impeachment. House Republicans say the latest political drama is being overblown. "I think the [Republican] conference will rise to the occasion," said Rep. Tom Reynolds of New York. They note that Congress has already cleared some important, if lesser-known, elements of Mr. Bush's agenda, including legislation dealing with energy, tort reform and free trade with Central America. It is also possible that Republicans' luck could improve quickly -- especially if there are some successes in Iraq or an easing of gasoline prices. "If they [Republicans] pivot and get the policy right in a way that gives the base a sense of ownership and pride, they can come out strengthened and unified," said Michael Franc, vice president for government relations at the conservative Heritage Foundation. "The next 60 to 90 days are very crucial." Even playing defense won't be easy for the Republicans. Votes to trim spending, for example, are likely to prove especially tough, given that Republicans are trying to squeeze some of the savings out of popular entitlement programs like Medicaid and Medicare. The political dynamic gives new clout to Republican moderates, who may hold the balance on some issues, including the Medicaid cuts and a two-year extension of Mr. Bush's 15% tax rates on capital gains and dividends. The tax-and-spend issues are included in a budget- reconciliation package that members will take up at the end of October. Those facing tough re-election races next year may break with Mr. Bush in order to demonstrate their political independence. So far, though, moderate Republicans have shown few signs of disloyalty. Yesterday, 34 House Republicans voted against a sweeping Endangered Species Act overhaul backed by Republican leaders, but the bill still passed, 229-193, after 36 Democrats crossed the aisle to support it. A bigger test could come next week, when Republicans hope to approve legislation that would permit more energy production in the Gulf of Mexico and encourage the production of more oil refineries by easing environmental laws and other regulations. Republicans hope the legislation's prospects have gotten a boost from the energy-supply problems highlighted by the hurricanes. Moderate Republicans will hold the key to those votes. "When I am elected, my instructions are not to turn my voting card over to the leadership," said Rep. Sherwood Boehlert, a Republican centrist from upstate New York. "We are not going to do anything different." Adding to those tensions, the stage has been set for a nasty battle over succession in the House, not just for Mr. DeLay's post but for the job of speaker, which Rep. Dennis Hastert of Illinois is considered likely to give up after the next Congress, in 2008. Moments after Mr. Hastert tapped Rep. Roy Blunt of Missouri to replace Mr. DeLay on Wednesday, low-ranking Tennessee Rep. Zack Wamp launched a campaign for a leadership post. "I clearly am more committed to running for leadership . . .  than I have ever been," Mr. Wamp said in a telephone message for his political supporters. Mr. Wamp, who once flirted with challenging the leadership of Mr. Hastert himself, said he would be willing to run against Rep. Eric Cantor, the No. 5 official in the House Republican power structure. Ohio Republican Rep. John Boehner, who was forced from the Republican leadership a few years ago, is also weighing a campaign for one of the top jobs. Though Mr. Wamp isn't considered a serious threat, filling the vacancies could be a distraction. Yesterday, as Republicans rallied support for the Endangered Species Act overhaul, Mr. Wamp was surrounded by reporters asking about his political ambitions. "It will be difficult for Republicans to focus on the agenda," he said, "but we can do it." ---                                  A New Agenda   Since President Bush's re-election a year ago, changing political windshave forced a new Republican to-do list:   Then...   Social Security private accounts  Tax simplification  Permanent repeal of estate tax   Now   Katrina reconstruction  New initiatives for Gulf Coast poverty  A new energy bill to respond to gas prices, with incentives for newrefineries
event_description: Legislation aimed at overhauling the Endangered Species Act.
setting: legislative chamber or committee debate
characters: President Bush [Hero]; House Republicans [Hero]; Tom DeLay [Villain]; Democratic Party [Villain]; Gulf Coast residents [Victim]
plot: After the indictment of Tom DeLay, President Bush and House Republicans attempt to maintain their legislative agenda despite political challenges, focusing on hurricane relief and energy legislation while facing opposition from the Democratic Party.
moral: The problem is the political turmoil and challenges facing the Republican agenda, and the text suggests that Republicans must adapt their priorities to address pressing issues like Gulf Coast poverty and energy prices to regain strength and unity.

Title: U.S. Seeks to Extend Salmon Protection, In Sweeping Bid to Rebuild Fish Stocks
Text: WASHINGTON -- In a sweeping effort to rebuild dwindling fish stocks, the federal government proposed extending Endangered Species Act protection to 13 salmon and steelhead populations in Washington state, Oregon and California. The National Marine Fisheries Service warned that the fish are headed toward extinction unless steps are taken quickly to restore their numbers. "The message we're sending to everyone is that the status quo isn't acceptable," said Terry Garcia, assistant secretary of Commerce for Oceans and Atmosphere. "Extinction isn't an option." He encouraged the states to develop conservation plans rather than waiting for federal directives. Under the Endangered Species Act, the fisheries service, which is part of the Commerce Department, now has 12 to 18 months to decide whether the species should be listed as threatened or endangered, and thus subject to federal protections. Once a species is officially listed, it is illegal to kill it or adversely affect its habitat. In addition, the fisheries service develops a recovery plan that can include restrictions on fishing, roadbuilding, mining, agriculture and other activities. Mr. Garcia said the federal government might not end up listing the fish as endangered if state, local and tribal governments move quickly to counter hazards to the fish. "The choice is up to them," he said, adding that officials should examine potential threats caused by overfishing, water pollution and excessive development. The proposed listing is notable for its geographic sweep: from south of San Francisco to the Canadian border. It is also a rare instance of the government trying to use the controversial act's protections in a heavily urbanized area -- in this case, Puget Sound, home both to chinook salmon and the city of Seattle. Ron Sims, county executive for King County, which includes Seattle, said that protecting the fish without disrupting the vibrant local economy will be "daunting," but the region has little choice. "We want to send out the message that we are a great place to live, that we protect the environment while having a good economy," he said. "So it isn't just about the restoration of chinook salmon, it's also a quality-of-life issue." He said that the county has already spent millions of dollars on steps to restore the fish and that more action will be needed. Pam Bissonnette, director of the county's department of natural resources, said that the fishing industry already is restricted because of the decline in the fish, which has been going on for more than a year. She said that more restrictions are likely necessary, but that it will be complicated because wild salmon -- the fish that are in decline and proposed for listing -- are mixed with those from hatcheries. "You can't just put a net out and say, `I'm only going to take hatchery fish.'" Melinda Pierce, a spokeswoman for the Sierra Club, applauded the proposed listing, saying it was sorely needed. But she expressed concern that some imperiled populations, such as stocks of chinook salmon in the middle and upper Columbia River, weren't included. However, a spokesman for the fisheries service said the long-term trends for those populations were "positive." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The federal government proposed extending Endangered Species Act protection to 13 salmon and steelhead populations.

Title: `Butterfly Bank' May Save Insects -- and Developers
Text: California's newest bank, which opened earlier this month in Riverside County, doesn't offer checking accounts or automated teller machines. It's the state's only "butterfly bank." Launched by Escondido developer Greg Reden, it provides what proponents say is a market-based way to end the battle between developers -- eager to build on prime Southern California land -- and environmentalists, determined to protect one of the region's most fragile creatures: the quino checkerspot butterfly. For Mr. Reden -- who with his partners at Johnston, Poutre & Reden Inc. owns 2,000 acres of butterfly habitat east of Temecula -- the butterfly bank offers a chance for financial redemption. "All my money is on this project," says Mr. Reden, 48. "I'm praying." The pivotal figure in the project is the quino checkerspot, which once ranged throughout Southern California but is now confined to just two locations: western Riverside County and the Otay Mesa area of San Diego. Since it was declared an endangered species in 1997, developers have dreaded discovering the red, yellow and black butterfly on their property. The federal Endangered Species Act restricts destruction of the habitat of any species so designated. Or, as Borre Winckel, president of the Southern California Building Industry Association, puts it: "If you find quino, it sends a red flag, and you're screwed." That is what happened to Mr. Reden. In 1987, his firm bought 2,800 acres called Silverado Ranch near the unincorporated town of Anza, planning to turn it into "ranchettes" of five to 20 acres each. Some 800 acres had already been developed when endangered species started turning up: the kangaroo rat three years ago, followed two years later by the quino checkerspot. Mr. Reden saw one hope of rescue: Under a 1981 amendment to the Endangered Species Act, property owners may destroy some habitat, provided they come up with an acceptable plan to preserve equivalent habitat elsewhere. The Fish and Wildlife Service, which administers the act, must approve the plan. As a result, the government may allow builders to destroy a large amount of low-quality habitat if the builders also agree to preserve a smaller amount of higher-quality habitat. What makes it palatable for developers is that land that's generally the best for the endangered species is less valuable to builders, since it's often isolated from other development. So they get to develop more-valuable parcels, while preserving parcels that are less valuable. "All habitat is not created equal," explains Dennis Murphy, a University of Nevada-Reno entomologist who helped write the portion of the Endangered Species Act covering the quino checkerspot. "You cannot necessarily take this incredibly small remnant distribution of butterfly, carve its habitat up into pieces and still ensure species survival." Mr. Reden's land happened to be prime territory for the butterfly, far superior to other areas where it also lived. He proposed selling portions of his property to developers who own that less-prime butterfly land. Those developers would deed the Silverado Ranch land as a butterfly preserve in perpetuity, in exchange for the right to develop their other property. The Fish and Wildlife Service had opened similar conservation banks for other species, or authorized their establishment by other public agencies; indeed, Riverside County runs such a preserve for the kangaroo rat. But Mr. Reden's butterfly bank is the first privately owned preserve in the county, and the first preserve of any kind devoted to the checkerspot. There proved to be plenty of demand: In its first week, which ended last Friday, the bank sold off a tenth of its land -- at $10,000 per acre. There were two buyers, one preserving butterfly habitat and the other preserving kangaroo rat habitat. So hot is the property that Mr. Reden says future acres will cost $15,000; his firm figures to make as much as $20 million in all -- about what he thinks it would have made if the original development plans had gone ahead unimpeded. Local developers are eager to buy the credits because they are poised to exploit one of the nation's hottest housing markets. The Building Industry Association projects that southwestern Riverside County will add 1.4 million new residents in the next two decades. And as developers rev up their bulldozers, politicians, scientists and environmentalists are looking at the bank as a way out of future ecological gridlock. "It's the wave of the future," says Rick Hoffman, an aide to Riverside County Supervisor Jim Venable, whose district includes Silverado Ranch. "The previous thought process was that if you had occupied habitat on your property, it was valueless. Well, that is no longer the case." Adds Prof. Murphy: "If you can put together a defensible banking scheme for this species, you can do conservation anywhere." To establish his bank, Mr. Reden had to negotiate an intricate agreement with federal officials specifying how the habitat would be maintained. For example, Mr. Reden's firm must burn away certain kinds of vegetation each year to guard against wildfires. And it had to stipulate that the land would be preserved "in perpetuity." (To guard against the possibility that developers won't want to buy all 2,000 acres, Mr. Reden and his partners are adding only one-third of their land at a time to the conservation bank). Mr. Reden's firm and the Fish and Wildlife Service also had to agree on the distribution, density and size of the quino checkerspot population. There lies one of the potential pitfalls for the Silverado Ranch plan: No checkerspot has yet been seen there this year -- and unless one is, the first sale for butterfly habitat will be canceled, and the buyer will have to go shopping elsewhere. There are other pitfalls as well. Among the biggest right now is that the Fish and Wildlife Service is "overwhelmed" with requests from developers for butterfly surveys and permits to buy conservation credits, according to field supervisor Jim Bartel. And an agreement between the service, county planning officials and developers to streamline that process is not expected to be completed for at least two years. Credit: Special to The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Establishment of a privately owned butterfly conservation bank.
setting: California's establishment of a privately owned butterfly conservation bank in Riverside County
characters: Greg Reden [Hero]; quino checkerspot butterfly [Victim]; developers [Villain]; Fish and Wildlife Service [Unknown]; Borre Winckel [Unknown]; Dennis Murphy [Unknown]; Jim Bartel [Unknown]; Rick Hoffman [Unknown]
plot: Greg Reden establishes a butterfly conservation bank to provide a market-based solution for developers to build on land while preserving butterfly habitat, addressing the conflict between development and environmental protection.
moral: The problem of habitat destruction for the quino checkerspot butterfly can be mitigated through innovative conservation banking, allowing for development while ensuring species survival.

Title: Turning the Tables: Businesses Are Battling Environmentalism With Its Own Laws --- They Claim Restrictions Call For `Impact Statement' Or Run Afoul of Statutes --- A Few Judges Sympathize
Text: FRESNO, Calif. -- Jim Ganulin, general counsel of the Westlands Water District, knows that the big irrigation agency's environmental record isn't entirely clean. "We've done some damage," he says. And he acknowledges that Westlands and its big agribusiness customers have tried for years to dilute environmental laws through court challenges and lobbying campaigns. So Westlands raised some eyebrows hereabouts when it rolled out a feared new weapon in its long-running battle with environmentalists and regulators over irrigation and pesticide practices. The new weapon: environmental laws. In a case in U.S. district court here, Westlands alleges that legislation passed by Congress in 1992 to end decades of California agribusiness's damaging environmental practices itself violates environmental laws. Westlands has asked Judge Oliver W. Wanger to block implementation of the new safeguards while requiring the government to study their environmental impact. During such a study, which typically takes years, the farmers' practices would continue unchanged. To the dismay of environmentalists, Judge Wanger has indicated in related rulings that he may be inclined to agree with Westlands. "Westlands is saying environmental reform breaks environmental laws," fumes Tom Graff, a veteran Environmental Defense Fund attorney who has spent years sparring with Westlands over its environmental policies. "It's perverse and absurd . . . but they could win." The Westlands case is part of a flood of similar lawsuits. In courtrooms across the country, industry and landowners -- including some renowned polluters -- are trying to turn the tables on their environmental foes with the novel argument that strengthened environmental protections run afoul of environmental laws. They are using the Endangered Species Act, the National Environmental Policy Act and a host of other federal and state laws -- the very laws used by environmentalists to win their most cherished victories, such as logging curbs on behalf of the spotted owl. The Pacific Legal Foundation, a conservative public-interest law firm that has helped pioneer the tactic, says hundreds of such suits have been undertaken in the past few years, with the pace quickening in the last several months. Though most of the suits have been thrown out of court, proponents of the approach have gained several victories lately. An Idaho federal judge recently ordered that the tiny Bruneau Hot Springsnail be dropped from the endangered species list because federal regulators violated the Endangered Species Act. An Oregon federal judge last year ruled that the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service broke environmental laws by not properly evaluating various possible impacts of its owl-protection zones on people. (The case is on appeal.) In Southern California, an air-quality district recently withdrew a rule limiting toxic solvents in paint after a state court agreed with paint makers that the rule might increase air pollution because painters would have to use more coats. Just last month, the U.S. Court of Appeals in Washington, D.C., threw out a key Interior Department interpretation of wildlife law under which regulators have slapped broad restrictions on private landowners whose property is home to protected species. The court held that regulators had read meaning into the law that didn't exist. Even when the suits fail in court, simply filing them often succeeds in slowing environmental efforts in the same way that environmentalists have stalled countless dams, freeways and shopping malls. Moreover, the recent victories have muddled environmental matters so much that the Supreme Court ultimately will have to wade in, many environmental-law experts believe. "Now you've got some contradictory rulings and some real confusion" surrounding application of environmental laws, says David Price, a conservative legal expert. Many environmental lawyers fear that, given the chance, the high court would significantly water down the laws. Moreover, congressional critics of environmental laws are using the rulings as more ammunition in their fight to weaken the statutes, especially the Endangered Species Act, which is up for reauthorization this year. Environmentalists, naturally, find all this galling. Indeed, some of those involved in the suits are high on their list of alleged eco-villains: ranchers whose heavy grazing has damaged rangeland and rivers; aluminum companies whose voracious need for hydropower has contributed much to the decline of Northwestern salmon; loggers who have ravaged Western forests; giant mining concerns; dirt bikers whose motocross rallies have trashed miles of desert habitat. "These abuses are the very things these laws were designed to prevent," says Todd True, a Sierra Club Legal Defense Fund attorney who helped argue many of the spotted owl cases. "They're turning the laws on their heads." Advocates of the strategy say they are merely mimicking the tactics and tools that environmentalists mastered in recent years. "Environmental lawyers have made these laws into neutron bombs for opponents of development," asserts James Burling of the Pacific Legal Foundation. "They obstruct or destroy everything in their path and leave the environment intact. To think that you can create a weapon that powerful and have exclusive dominion over it is foolish." Simply filing such a suit permits lawyers to exploit environmental laws' elaborate procedural requirements: strict timetables for endangered-species filings, requirements for painstaking environmental reviews and the like. The result is delay. In another tactic, lawyers deconstruct the science behind the regulation, arguing that it doesn't meet the laws' strict scientific standards. The objective: to go about farming, mining, logging or other business without the added and sometimes heavy cost of complying with new environmental protections. Thus, aluminum companies have sued the National Marine Fisheries Service, alleging that federal efforts to save the endangered salmon in the Columbia River basin are scientifically lame and violate the Endangered Species Act. The nation's biggest aluminum producers -- such as Aluminum Co. of America and Kaiser Aluminum Corp. -- have plants that take advantage of the cheap power generated by the towering dams along the Columbia and its tributaries. Those dams, however, are blamed by environmentalists and federal regulators for much -- some say most -- of the dizzying decline of salmon. In their suit, the aluminum companies argue that the dams aren't so bad after all. They say their scientific studies show the real culprits to be overfishing and fish hatcheries, which introduce diseases and dilute the wild-salmon gene pool. They contend it is the companies that are endangered by the service's plan to make alternations in dam operations that would reduce power generation (and, regulators contend, fish deaths). "It would raise electric rates to intolerable levels," says John Carr, director of the aluminum companies' Portland-based trade group. He adds: "No one is more interested in saving the salmon than we are." National Marine Fisheries officials and environmentalists recoil at such talk. "We're delighted they're such admirers of the Endangered Species Act, but we're contesting their arguments," says Merritt Tuttle, senior policy analyst for the Fisheries' Northwestern region. Environmentalists, who agree that overfishing and hatcheries are contributing to the salmon problem, assert that some of the aluminum companies have had trouble complying with environmental laws themselves. Kaiser, for example, has been feuding with the Environmental Protection Agency for years over alleged repeated air-pollution violations at a Spokane, Wash., plant. (Kaiser denies any wrongdoing.) The aluminum companies lost the first legal round when federal Judge Malcolm F. Marsh of Portland ruled last year that they didn't have standing to bring the suit under environmental laws. Judge Marsh said allowing the suit to proceed "would be akin to permitting a fox to complain that the chickens have not been fed." The aluminum companies are undeterred; they have appealed, and a decision from the higher court is expected later this year. Filers of such suits have generally fared better contesting matters of procedure instead of science. Consider the case of the Bruneau Hot Springsnail, a yellowish gastropod about the size of a BB pellet. The snail lives in hot springs in high desert country in Idaho's Owyhee County. In the mid-1980s, federal scientists discovered that the springs were drying up, at least in part because of heavy irrigation pumping by cattle ranchers and alfalfa and wheat farmers. The Fish & Wildlife Service proposed protecting the snails, which probably would have meant restrictions on irrigation pumping. But the farmers and ranchers, which include several such wealthy agribusiness operators as potato and cattle baron J.R. Simplot (net worth: $950 million, by Forbes's estimate), complained to Idaho congressmen, who complained to the service, which put the brakes on its snail plan. Only after environmentalists sued, alleging that the service had broken the Endangered Species Act by dawdling, was the snail formally classified as endangered. That was January 1993. Owyhee County citizens, led by the Idaho Farm Bureau and local cattlemen's association, swiftly sued. Their argument: The service had taken too long to formally rule on the snail, just as environmentalists had been saying. Three months ago, federal Judge Harold L. Ryan ruled that the government had indeed violated the Endangered Species Act's requirement that a final decision on listing a species be made within 18 months after such a listing has been proposed. The judge also lambasted the service for its "arbitrary and capricious" behavior in failing to follow various other procedural steps. He lifted the snail's federal protection, and the snail will remain unprotected while the ruling is appealed. Environmentalists are rattled. For one thing, they believe that the main reason the feds moved at such a pace was that politically connected Idahoans got their congressmen to lean on the service. Another ominous sign for environmentalists: If applied elsewhere, Judge Ryan's ruling could conceivably reverse numerous listings or halt other pending ones; the Fish & Wildlife Service almost always takes more than 18 months to render its final ruling. "That's the scariest fallout from this," says Laird Lucas, an environmental attorney in Boise who is appealing Judge Ryan's ruling. And what of the snail? Environmentalists and federal scientists say they still believe it is headed for possible extinction without protection. In Owyhee County, they don't buy that. "There's plenty of snails," says Rayola Jacobsen of the Idaho Farm Bureau. She does concede, however, that a $500 bounty offered to anyone who can find a new snail colony hasn't turned up any yet. Meanwhile, the farm bureau is pursuing a similar suit seeking to overturn federal protections for four snails and a limpet on the Snake River. But it is the Westlands case that may have brought the turnabout approach to its highest form. Westlands provides subsidized federal water to a service area the size of Rhode Island in the arid Central Valley, mostly for cotton growers. Its acreage includes land owned or managed by some of America's wealthiest farmers, such as J.G. Boswell, whose cotton-based fortune has been estimated by Forbes at more than $500 million. In making the desert bloom, Westlands farmers sometimes made the environment reel. Runoff from Westlands farms helped poison the Kesterson Wildlife Refuge, where thousands of migratory birds have died since the late 1970s, and the heavy pumping has been a principal factor in the collapse of the populations of the federally protected Sacramento River chinook salmon and the Delta smelt. For decades, Westlands farmers disputed accusations that they were harming the environment, fought legal wars of attrition and lobbied to weaken environmental laws. Westlands officials no longer deny that they have hurt the environment, although they say their impact has been exaggerated. Westlands lobbyists continue working to weaken the Endangered Species Act during its reauthorization. All of which makes their current legal stance in Fresno noteworthy. Westlands is trying to block a government plan to use 600,000 acre feet of federal water to restore wetlands and fish habitat damaged by past irrigation and other farming practices; before Congress passed its 1992 legislation designed to curb Central Valley farmers' environmental abuses, that water would have gone to the cotton fields. In a packed Fresno courtroom recently, while 19 lawyers and a few farmers listened in, Westlands attorney Tom Birmingham made the farmers' case: The government, which wants to start using the disputed water within weeks, violated the Endangered Species Act by not following proper procedures on its salmon plan. It violated the National Environmental Policy Act, known as NEPA, by not doing an exhaustive environmental review; the law requires this because, Mr. Birmingham argued, the use of the water for environmental purposes "will have significant environmental impacts" on farmers. Ground will sink because farmers will just pump out groundwater to make up for the lost federal water. Air will fill with dust because they won't have enough water to damp down the dirt. Queried by Judge Wanger about whether the public interest is better served by protecting the farmers' environment or that of endangered species, Mr. Birmingham said, "Your honor, it is inconceivable that the public interest will not be best served by requiring the government to obey the law." Judge Wanger is expected to rule soon on Westlands' request for an injunction against transferring the water. But in an 87-page order rejecting an earlier government attempt to dismiss the case, Judge Wanger wrote that "it can not be credibly argued that the disputed water allocations . . . do not significantly affect the human environment." "We're concerned that the judge feels an environmental-impact statement will be required," observes the Environmental Defense Fund's Mr. Graff. If he makes that ruling, "it will be a travesty." It would "take years to prepare, and the wildlife doesn't have the time to spare," he says. "Westlands will grow its cotton and damage the environment some more. That's not what environmental laws were intended to achieve." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Westlands Water District is challenging the government's plan to use federal water for environmental restoration, alleging violations of the Endangered Species Act.
setting: U.S. district court case regarding environmental laws and the Endangered Species Act
characters: Westlands Water District [Villain]; environmentalists [Hero]; endangered species [Victim]
plot: Westlands Water District is challenging the government's plan to use federal water for environmental restoration, alleging violations of the Endangered Species Act, while environmentalists argue that this legal maneuvering harms endangered species.
moral: The problem is that Westlands is using legal tactics to undermine environmental protections, and the text suggests that the government must enforce environmental laws to protect endangered species and prevent further environmental damage.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial): A Species of Extremism
Text: Interior Secretary Manuel Lujan became Environmentalist Enemy Number One this month when he suggested Congress rethink the 1973 Endangered Species Act. He says the law needlessly prohibits any development whenever it could damage an endangered animal, even if it is found in other areas and is only one of dozens of subspecies. Secretary Lujan quickly retreated under a firestorm of criticism, and says he won't press for changes in the law. However, he remains "very frustrated" by the species law's byzantine requirements. This month he was told that the Animas-La Plata water project in Colorado couldn't be built because it would threaten endangered squawfish. The $582 million project would provide water for four Indian tribes. Mr. Lujan says the law allows no provision for the hardships it imposes on the Indians. He also notes that squawfish are found in many other areas. In Oregon, they are predators that account for the loss of millions of migrating steelhead and salmon smolts. To control the losses, state and federal biologists there have just begun paying fishermen $1 for every squawfish they catch. Is the squawfish perhaps more endangering than endangered? The straitjacket rules of the Endangered Species Act are also at the center of a holy war on Arizona's Mount Graham. The University of Arizona, the Smithsonian Institution and the Vatican Observatory hope to build a world-class $200 million observatory on the summit. It would put the U.S. in the forefront of astrophysics. But the area is also home to the last known examples of the Mount Graham red squirrel. Until word of the telescope project, environmentalists largely ignored the red squirrel; it is only one of 25 subspecies of red squirrel, which is common in the West. Moreover, no one has shown persuasively that the project would harm the squirrel, which has survived heavy logging on the mountain, along with the presence of campers and fishermen. Telescope backers promise to reforest the logged areas and create a 1,700-acre refuge that would be off-limits to humans. The observatory would take up only 26 acres. None of this satisfies environmental extremists who are masters at manipulating the Endangered Species Act -- not merely to protect animals, but to block any and all development. A dead squirrel has been sent to the project's director, and the University of Arizona has been vandalized. One pro-telescope biologist has received a death threat in the mail. The Rev. George Coyne, director of the Vatican Observatory, believes that if some of his environmentalist foes had been "present at the creation of the world, they would have said to God, `OK, stop now. You've got all the birds. Don't create man, because he's going to mess it all up.'" Rather than retreat, Mr. Lujan should ask that the Endangered Species Law be amended so that factors such as job losses and social costs are considered. The law shouldn't make absolute protection a form of worship if the same animals are found elsewhere, have many subspecies or can be relocated. Speaking Monday in Portland, Oregon, on the dispute over saving the spotted owl's habitat while putting loggers out of work, President Bush said, "Common sense tells us to find a needed balance." Mr. Bush's call for "balance" is an important element that has been missing for too long from the way this country thinks about the human species' place in the environment.
event_description: Discussion on amending the Endangered Species Act to consider social costs and job losses.
setting: discussion on amending the Endangered Species Act in a political context
characters: Manuel Lujan [Hero]; environmental extremists [Villain]; Indians affected by the law [Victim]; Mount Graham red squirrel [Victim]
plot: Secretary Lujan suggests rethinking the Endangered Species Act due to its prohibitive nature on development, which leads to criticism and his retreat. The law's strict requirements are shown to harm local communities and development projects, while also raising questions about the actual endangerment of certain species.
moral: The Endangered Species Act should be amended to consider social costs and job losses, promoting a balance between environmental protection and human development.

Title: Federal Offenses: Federal Police Ranks Swell to Enforce A Widening Array of Criminal Laws
Text: For years, the public face of federal law enforcement has been the Federal Bureau of Investigation. Today, for many people, the knock on the door is increasingly likely to come from a dizzying array of other police forces tucked away inside lesser-known crime-fighting agencies. They could be from the Environmental Protection Agency, the Labor or Education departments, the National Park Service, the Bureau of Land Management or the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, the agency known for its weather forecasts. Agents from NOAA, in fact, along with the Fish and Wildlife Service, raided the Miami business of Morgan Mok in 2008, seeking evidence she had broken the Endangered Species Act trading in coral. The agents had assault rifles with them, and the case documents indicated her house and business records had been under surveillance over a six-month period, says Ms. Mok. Under the 1973 law, the departments of Interior and Commerce (home to NOAA) must write regulations to define what is endangered and how it must be protected. One of those regulations specifies coral. "I felt like I was being busted for drugs, instead of coral," Ms. Mok says. "It was crazy." Ms. Mok says she showed that her coral had been properly obtained. She paid a $500 fine and served one year of probation for failing to complete paperwork for an otherwise legal transaction. Adam Fetcher of the Department of the Interior, which includes Fish and Wildlife, says the department and its bureaus "follow priorities in line with other federal law-enforcement agencies. We work within the resources appropriated by Congress to effectively follow up and investigate criminal and civil violations." Government agencies of all stripes have become the front-line enforcers for many of the laws Congress has written the past four decades. Not only do the agencies enforce these laws, they also write the voluminous regulations needed to put the laws into effect and govern federal programs. Often, Congress makes it a criminal offense to violate any part of a law it passes -- including these regulations. As a result, as more criminal laws are passed, the number of regulations that can ensnare people grows as well. It is hard to pin down precisely how many regulations could result in criminal penalties. Of dozens of federal agencies contacted by The Wall Street Journal, none could say how many of their regulations were connected to criminal statutes. Legal experts have put this number at anywhere between 10,000 and 300,000. In 1970, the Code of Federal Regulations had 54,000 pages. Today it runs to 165,000 pages and takes 27 feet of shelf space when printed and bound. The growing rule book has kept enforcement teams busy. Last year, criminal cases pursued chiefly by smaller government agencies led to 10,122 convictions, an 84% increase from 15 years ago. Also up sharply: the numbers of prosecutions and investigators. In the latest survey, by the Government Accountability Office in 2006, there were 25,000 sworn officers in the smaller government agencies (which excludes departments more commonly associated with crime fighting: Treasury, Justice, Defense and what is now Homeland Security). That number includes police, inspectors, security guards and rangers, as well as criminal investigators. Across all government agencies, there were about 138,000 federal law-enforcement officers that year, GAO figures show. The Justice Department accounted for more than 40% of that total. Among the smaller agencies, currently there are 3,812 criminal investigators, up from 507 in 1973, the first year for which records are available from the Office of Personnel Management. The EPA received its first two criminal investigators in 1977. It now has 265. Even critics of the system say it is important to uphold the law and that criminal sanctions are sometimes necessary. "There are cases that should and can be made," says Roger Marzulla, a Justice Department official in the Reagan administration who oversaw environmental-crime prosecutions. Enforcing regulations also ensures that rule-breakers don't have an unfair advantage over competitors, he says. An August raid on Gibson Guitar Corp. has drawn heavy criticism from both sides of the political aisle. In that raid, Fish and Wildlife Service agents swarmed the Nashville company to seize rosewood and ebony the agency suspected had been illegally imported from India. The company says its wood was obtained legally and that no charges have been filed. "Why is it we're treating what is essentially a violation of rules and regulations in a criminal manner?" says Tom Fitton, president of Judicial Watch, a conservative watchdog group. Steven Benjamin, president-elect of the National Association of Criminal Defense Lawyers, which tends to be more liberal, says: "Enforcing over-criminalization has put us at war with ourselves." Skeptics also say some of these smaller departments tend to wield their powers indiscriminately, even for seemingly minor infractions, in ways that seem self-justifying. "When you start making innocuous actions crimes, you multiply the number of people who are enforcing" the laws and regulations, says Ronald Gainer, a former Justice Department official for Democratic and Republican administrations who has cautioned for years against the proliferation of federal law. "You multiply the number of people who have to enforce criminal laws and they all want guns." Congress provides relatively little oversight, particularly in comparison to the FBI, whose officials are regularly brought before hearings. Committees overseeing federal departments not traditionally associated with crime-fighting pay less attention to their law-enforcement units. Often, it is the agencies' own internal inspectors-general who blow the whistle on problems with other officers in the department. In a stinging 2010 report, the inspector general for the Commerce Department focused on widespread complaints from fishermen in Massachusetts, North Carolina and Florida that criminal investigators from NOAA's National Marine Fisheries Service's Office of Law Enforcement were being heavy-handed, even in civil cases. The report detailed a 2006 case where a warrant affidavit for a fish dealer's records contained false information, and another case where an agent gained unauthorized access to a facility, then opened another door to let other officers in. "The reality is that if you hire a cadre of criminal investigators, they're going to carry out their work like criminal investigators -- and that's what they did," says inspector general Todd Zinser. NOAA officials say the agency has improved its enforcement program since the report, with new leadership and more scrutiny of charging decisions. It has also frozen hiring for criminal investigators and employed more officers to help fishermen with compliance. "I think that we agreed that there was a need for a more balanced perspective in the makeup of our work force," says Eric Schwaab, Assistant Administrator for Fisheries. Christopher Kortlander met two dozen agents from several different federal agencies when they burst into the tiny Custer Battlefield Museum he founded in Garryowen, Mont., in 2005. Mr. Kortlander has said in court filings protesting his treatment that "24 or more federal agents, armed and brandishing automatic weapons" participated in the raid. In an interview, he said he counted agents from the Bureau of Land Management, FBI, Fish and Wildlife Service, National Park Service and several other forces. One of Mr. Kortlander's interns was pushed to the ground in the raid, he says. The agents sought artifacts they said were being sold online under false pretenses as historical items found at Little Bighorn, scene of the bloody 1876 battle between Indians and the U.S. Army. No charges were brought against Mr. Kortlander. But agents returned in 2008, that time alleging Mr. Kortlander was in illegal possession of eagle feathers. Again, no charges were filed. Mr. Kortlander denies wrongdoing. In 2009, federal prosecutors took the rare step of notifying Mr. Kortlander's attorney in writing that it wouldn't be pursuing charges in connection with either raid. Mr. Kortlander is suing the government in federal court to recover some Cheyenne bonnets taken by the Bureau of Land Management during the second raid. "They raid these people like they're Scarface," says Penelope Strong, Mr. Kortlander's attorney. Using force to go after drug dealers or violent criminals is understandable, Ms. Strong says. "But somebody in a hot, sultry part of Montana, in a trading post where people are wandering around buying blankets? Come on." The agencies referred inquiries about the case to the U.S. attorney's office in Montana. A spokesman declined to comment, citing the lawsuit. Postal inspectors, customs and other revenue agents were some of the first federal law-enforcement officers in the years after the Revolution. In the 19th century, Congress created forces to guard federal buildings and to police the expanding American West. After the Civil War, the federal government set up the Department of Justice and stepped up controls of immigration, alcohol and drugs. The Department of the Interior and the Forest Service received new responsibilities under the environmental legislation sought by President Theodore Roosevelt in the early 20th century. But the foundation for the current growth of federal enforcement agencies was laid in the 1960s and 1970s when smaller departments started having inspectors general whose task was to police the government. Lawmakers writing sweeping environmental laws in that era also granted additional resources to the agencies administering those laws. That, in turn, created new enforcement teams. Lawmakers and traditional law-enforcement agencies, in particular the FBI, resisted extending the power to carry firearms, execute warrants and make arrests to other agencies, says Jeffrey Bumgarner, a professor of government at Minnesota State University who has written a history of federal law enforcement. But agencies chipped away at that opposition, he says. EPA agents were given police powers in 1988. The U.S. Marshals Service started issuing annual blanket authorizations in 1995 to some agencies that didn't have them. After the Sept. 11, 2001, attacks, when the FBI's attention shifted to terrorism matters, Congress gave permanent powers to inspectors general in more than two dozen agencies. All of the officers are trained at one of the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center facilities now run by the Department of Homeland Security. About 90 federal agencies send their personnel to the centers for training in everything from criminal investigations to cyber terrorism and land-management police training. In the mid-1990s, many agencies tried to scale back their vigor in pursuing criminal prosecutions amid concern among members of Congress about aggressive tactics. An influential 1994 memo by Earl Devaney, then head of the Environmental Protection Agency's criminal-enforcement program, suggested applying criminal sanctions to only "the most significant and egregious violators." Mr. Devaney's advice didn't stick, and the numbers are rising. In 2010 there were 12,606 prosecutions from cases investigated chiefly by agencies other than Justice, Treasury, Defense and Homeland Security. That was a 50% increase from 15 years ago. In September, former oil-refinery manager Hubert Vidrine Jr. was awarded $1.67 million from a malicious-prosecution lawsuit he filed against the EPA. The judge said she would have made it higher if not limited by federal law. Mr. Vidrine's troubles began in September 1996, when the agency's criminal-investigation division led nearly two-dozen armed federal and state authorities on Canal Refining Co., an oil refinery in Church Point, La. Mr. Vidrine described in an interview a scene of guns, police dogs, flashing lights and roadblocks. At one point, according to court documents filed in the lawsuit, agents refused to allow female employees to use the bathroom and prevented them from phoning their homes or child-care providers to make arrangements for their children. The plant, which employed about 260 people, shut down refining operations the following year and is idle now. Mr. Vidrine was charged in 1999 with "knowingly" storing hazardous waste, which later turned out to be used refined oil not covered by federal regulations. The potential penalty was five years and a $50,000 fine for each day of the violation. The case was dismissed in 2003, by which time Mr. Vidrine had spent more than $120,000 in legal fees maintaining his innocence, according to a statement by the judge. The lead EPA agent in the case pleaded guilty Oct. 3 to charges he lied under oath and obstructed justice. A few days earlier, federal judge Rebecca Doherty sided with Mr. Vidrine in his lawsuit. The EPA declined to comment, citing potential further action by the Justice Department. In recent weeks the Justice Department filed a notice that it plans to appeal the case to the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals -- promising to push a case that began in 1996 into 2012. The regulations that spurred the raid were so complex they had led to disagreements among EPA personnel, as well as state environmental quality personnel, the judge noted in her opinion. She called the regulations a "morass." Judge Doherty wrote that, "Given the egregious conduct displayed by an agent of the government and the devastation wrought on otherwise law-abiding citizens," she would have given Mr. Vidrine more than $1.67 million, if federal law allowed punitive damages against the government, "in the hope of deterring such reckless and damaging conduct and abuse of power in the future." --- Kris Maher contributed to this article. Credit: By Louise Radnofsky, Gary Fields and John R. Emshwiller

Title: Roberts Shows Some Openness; Nominee Hints at Judicial Approach, Says He Isn't an Ideologue
Text: Washington -- AFTER A WEEK of carefully parrying pointed questions from both ends of the political spectrum during closely watched hearings, Judge John Roberts concluded his Senate confirmation hearings for chief justice by declaring, "I'm not an ideologue." But Judge Roberts did flag some significant elements of his judicial approach during questioning by the Senate Judiciary Committee this week, even as he cautiously avoided discussing matters that he said could come before the Supreme Court. Among other things, Judge Roberts, currently on the U.S. appeals court here, suggested openness to a broad view of the federal power to regulate interstate commerce, some acceptance of constitutional privacy rights -- and some distance from liberal civil-rights advocates and civil libertarians. While he frustrated some Democrats who said he was avoiding answering their questions, he gave Republicans no assurance that he was sympathetic to their causes. He demurred, for instance, when Sen. Sam Brownback (R., Kan.) asked "whether the unborn child is a person or is a piece of property." Still, Republicans professed no doubts about Judge Roberts, who himself offered no objection when Sen. Lindsey Graham (R., S.C.) praised him as a "strict constructionist." And by the end of the third day of hearings yesterday, his more open demeanor appeared to mend fences with some Democrats who had complained about his cooperation earlier in the week. Here are some highlights from Judge Roberts's testimony on key areas: Federal Power and the Commerce Clause: Judge Roberts gave Democrats some hope that he backed a broad view of the Commerce Clause, the constitutional provision giving Congress power to regulate interstate commerce. For more than 70 years, the clause has been the linchpin of ambitious national legislation, including the New Deal economic plan, the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Endangered Species Act. Some conservative legal scholars reject the theory behind Wickard v. Filburn, the 1942 opinion that gave Congress virtually unfettered power under the Commerce Clause. But Judge Roberts suggested he might accept the reasoning in the case, which found that even an individual activity of minor economic impact could be regulated if many people doing the same thing cumulatively affects interstate commerce. "Under the Commerce Clause, can Congress pass a law banning even non-commercial cloning?" Sen. Charles Schumer (D., N.Y.) asked yesterday. "It would seem to me that Congress can make a determination that this is an activity, if allowed to be pursued, that is going to have effects on interstate commerce," Judge Roberts replied. "Obviously, if you were successful in cloning an animal, that's not going to be simply a local phenomenon. That's going to be something people are going to -- " "You can leave it at that," Sen. Schumer said. "That's a good answer, as far as I'm concerned." In a separate exchange with Sen. Schumer, though, Judge Roberts declined to give a blanket endorsement of the Wickard decision as beyond challenge. Sen. Schumer called that disturbing, warning that "if certain viewpoints became majority viewpoints," that could lead to "the dismantling of the entire apparatus to protect our rights, through the narrowing of the commerce clause." Privacy: Judge Roberts went further than some conservative legal scholars -- and further than conservative Justices Clarence Thomas and Antonin Scalia did in their confirmation hearings -- in asserting that the Constitution accords a right to privacy -- a crucial prerequisite, though not a guarantee, for legal support of abortion rights and gay rights. In particular, he endorsed the majority opinion in the 1965 contraception case, Griswold v. Connecticut, saying he agreed "with the Griswold court's conclusion that marital privacy extends to contraception and availability of that. The court, since Griswold, has grounded the privacy right discussed in that case in the liberty interest protected under the Due Process Clause." At a different point in the hearings, he said liberty "does cover areas such as privacy and it's not protected only in procedural terms, but it's protected substantively as well." At his 1991 hearing, Justice Thomas acknowledged the court had created a privacy precedent but notably didn't take a position approving it. When Justice Scalia was asked at his 1986 hearing whether he thought there was a right to privacy, he refused to give an opinion, saying, "I can tell you that that is what a number of Supreme Court opinions now say." But Judge Roberts did leave himself some wiggle room, refusing repeatedly to say how broadly he would interpret privacy rights, and in particular whether he felt the right applied to abortion. Civil Rights: One area in which Judge Roberts didn't significantly distance himself from the court's most conservative judges was civil rights. Despite repeated invitations by Democrats to disown his 1980s memos critical of busing and challenging congressional efforts to strengthen the Voting Rights Act, Judge Roberts didn't do so. Sen. Edward Kennedy (D., Mass.) cited a memo in which Judge Roberts, then working in the Reagan administration, said a portion of the Voting Rights Act was "constitutionally suspect" and asked the judge whether he still felt that was so. Judge Roberts said he saw "no basis" today to think so. Some civil-rights advocates said that answer could mean that he hasn't seen a smart-enough argument to overturn it. Judge Roberts did appear to take a more liberal view than Justices Thomas or Scalia when he said it was appropriate for Justice Sandra Day O'Connor to weigh real-life ramifications when ruling on Michigan law-school admissions affirmative-action programs. But it was left unclear how much weight he would give such information or how much real-life evidence he might consider. Listening to Congress: Judge Roberts took pains to distinguish himself from Justice Scalia on the value of reading legislative history in interpreting statutes, a sensitive point for many lawmakers. Justice Scalia has been critical of using legislative history, saying he reasons from the original meaning of legal texts. Critics say legislative history can help judges understand how lawmakers intended their measures to apply. Senators in both parties seemed pleased when Judge Roberts said he relied on it on his current court and would continue to do so as chief justice. "But I have quoted and looked to legislative history in the past to help determine the meaning of ambiguous terms, and I would expect to follow that same approach on the Supreme Court," he said. Death Penalty: Judge Roberts seemed to defend policies speeding executions as he discussed his reasons in past years for pushing to limit the access of condemned inmates to federal courts. Despite probing by Sen. Russell Feingold (D., Wis.), he didn't endorse concerns raised more recently that wrongfully convicted prisoners may face execution. That is a contrast to the court's senior justice John Paul Stevens. In a speech last month Justice Stevens noted that new DNA evidence demonstrates "a substantial number of death sentences have been imposed erroneously," indicating "serious flaws in our administration of criminal justice." --- Question of the Day: Did Supreme Court nominee John Roberts respond appropriately to senators' questions? Visit WSJ.com/Question to vote. Plus, WSJ.com subscribers can read the full transcripts of John Roberts's confirmation hearings, at WSJ.com/OnlineToday ---                     Judge Roberts To Chief Justice Roberts?   John Roberts is expected to win full Senate confirmation in time tosucceed the late Chief Justice William Rehnquist when the Supreme Courtbegins its next term. Below, Judge Roberts at his confirmation hearingsyesterday.   The Calendar  -- Deadline for written questions to Judge Roberts from the SenateJudiciary Committee, Sept. 16.  -- Judiciary Committee vote scheduled, Sept. 22  -- Full Senate debate opens Sept. 26  -- Full Senate vote, by Sept. 30  -- Supreme Court begins new term, Oct. 3   The Prospects  -- The Senate's 55 Republicans are all but certain to vote for JudgeRoberts. The question is how many Democrats will. Leading Democrats remainuncertain on how strongly to oppose the nominee: "If I think he's going tobe a Justice Scalia . . . I vote no . . . If I think he's going to be aRehnquist, I probably vote yes because it won'tchange anything" - Sen. Joseph Biden (D., Del.)
event_description: Discussion of the federal power to regulate interstate commerce as it relates to the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Senate confirmation hearings for chief justice
characters: Judge John Roberts [Hero]; Democrats [Victim]; Republicans [Villain]; Civil rights advocates [Victim]
plot: Judge John Roberts navigates Senate confirmation hearings, suggesting a broad view of federal power under the Commerce Clause, which gives hope to Democrats but frustrates them as he avoids clear commitments. His responses to questions about privacy rights and civil rights reveal a complex stance that leaves both parties uncertain about his future rulings.
moral: The problem is the uncertainty surrounding Judge Roberts's judicial philosophy, particularly regarding civil rights and the Commerce Clause. The text suggests that a more transparent and definitive stance from Roberts would alleviate concerns and foster trust among both parties.

Title: We Must Fight Against the Tyranny of Kelo
Text: Scott Bullock's commentary was a welcome reminder of the havoc wreaked by the Supreme Court in Kelo v. City of New London and it is to the credit of The Wall Street Journal that the anniversary of this truly outrageous injustice did not go unrecognized ("The Specter of Condemnation," Rule of Law, June 24).    Our private property rights are threatened today not just from eminent domain abuse but from regulatory takings such as the Endangered Species Act, as well as by local government telling us which legal activities, such as smoking, we can and cannot engage in while on the premises of private businesses. The House has passed not just one but two bills that would swing the pendulum back in favor of private property rights. Yet The Private Property Rights Protection Act and a reformed Endangered Species Act, which includes protections for private property owners, sit bottled up in the Senate. The joke around Washington is that the Senate is where good ideas go to die. Let's hope it doesn't become the place where our property rights go to die, too. Greg Blankenship Director Illinois Policy Institute Springfield, Ill. --- The Kelo ruling sparked widespread national outrage, but its timing (summer's end, 2005, with most state legislatures out of session) allowed developers and big government advocates to sit and wait, hoping the anger and publicity would die down. In South Carolina, voters will not weigh in on their proposed amendment until this November, more than a year after the ruling. Strict federalists may support a state-only response but Institute for Justice is right to press for federal protections as well. The hard lobbying of local governments, regional developers and NIMBY environmentalists has dulled or defeated several state bills. Others are poorly written and rely on case law for their new "tightened" definitions of public use and blight. Partial and regulatory takings, both essential to the debate, have also been glossed over by some lawmakers looking for a quick fix. Property rights and a traditional understanding of the Fifth Amendment remain essential for economic development and the realization of widespread home ownership. The Institute of Justice is doing all Americans a great favor by keeping up the fight. Neil Mellen Research Director South Carolina Policy Council Columbia, S.C.
event_description: Discussion of a reformed Endangered Species Act that includes protections for private property owners.

Title: Rural Washington County Hit by Economic Buzz Saw
Text: TONASKET, Wash. -- Okanogan County is in trouble. From the cab of his pickup truck, Dan McCarthy points to some of the evidence: fields piled high with unearthed apple trees, brown and dry as kindling, discarded by farmers facing bankruptcy. "They hung on for year after year, hoping it would get better," says Mr. McCarthy, the county's pest-control agent. "But this year, they got the rug pulled out from under them." Pretty much everyone is feeling that way in Okanogan County, where an extraordinary array of outside forces have come together to wreak havoc on the local economy. "Everybody's going broke," says Dave Schulz, chairman of the Okanogan Board of Commissioners. What's frustrating for locals is that they feel they can't defend themselves from those outside forces. Not against the Chinese apple producers flooding the market nor the weakness of the Canadian dollar that's making tourists from British Columbia stay home. Then there's the federal officials enforcing the Endangered Species Act and the environmental groups wanting to save the lynx in the Loomis Forest and the ecosystem around Buckhorn Mountain. "It's the worst I've ever seen," says County Commissioner Ed Thiele, who has lived in the county for 58 years. "It feels like you're at the bottom of a huge hole and everybody's piling this stuff on top of you. Just when you feel like you can climb out, someone piles more on." Okanogan isn't the only Washington county that is seeing hard times. Policy makers have been confounded for more than a decade by the intractability of "the two Washingtons" syndrome, which pits the interests of the flourishing high-tech-based urban economy west of the Cascade Mountains against those of the fading natural-resource-based rural economy to the east. But Okanogan -- 5,300 square miles bordered by the Cascades, the Canadian border and the Columbia River -- is facing particularly harsh times. "The nexus of things seems to be problematic for them," says William Beyers, a professor of economic geography at the University of Washington in Seattle. "I don't think there's any other part of the state that's yelling with the same intensity." The predicament brings home to policy makers an uncomfortable reality: Many local economies in Washington have little control over their own fates. "Local counties aren't going to influence currency-exchange rates or world apple prices," says John Mitchell, Western regional economist for U.S. Bancorp in Portland. It starts with apples, an important commodity for the county. China, now the world's largest apple producer, had a bumper crop this year and is sending so much cheap apple-juice concentrate onto the market that the International Trade Commission ruled in July that the imports are doing economic harm to U.S. producers. A ton of Northwest juice apples is fetching about $30 this year, compared with $66 last year, and $137 in 1997. Prices for other varieties are also under pressure from competition and slack demand. A pound of fresh apples from Washington is selling for 29% less than last year. "The money we spent raising apples this year, we didn't get any of it back," says Wayne Verbeck, a farmer in Tonasket who, after more than 30 years in the business, is losing his farm, unable to continue paying rent on the 70 acres he leases and forced to give the 30 acres he owns to the Farm Service Agency to pay off his debt. So some farmers are opting out of the apple business altogether. At least 345 acres of orchards have been abandoned. With no one to care for them, the likelihood of pest infestations increases. As a result, this year marks the first time Okanogan County has ever had to spend money to excavate abandoned orchards. So far, Mr. McCarthy, the pest-control agent, has removed trees from 90 acres on four farms, costing the county $25,000 of its annual $45,000 pest-control budget. For other farmers, and some businesses, the problem is the Endangered Species Act. The National Marine Fisheries Service prevented operators of six irrigation ditches in the Methow Valley from turning the canals on this spring. The service feared the removal of water from tributaries to the Methow River would harm salmon habitats. Instead, the man-made water shortage pinched some apple and alfalfa growers' profits and caused other problems. The water level in Patterson Lake, near a resort in the Methow Valley, dropped about eight feet because the lake depends on one of the six irrigation ditches for water. That cost Sun Mountain Lodge, the county's biggest taxpayer and third-largest employer, about $75,000 in revenue when tourists canceled their reservations fearing the low water level would make recreation questionable. And fears about the future spurred Sun Mountain Lodge to postpone indefinitely a planned $6 million expansion of a conference room and spa. "If we don't have water, we simply can't operate," says Brian Charlton, Sun Mountain's manager. The fisheries service, irrigation-ditch operators and county officials are talking about a compromise, but locals aren't optimistic: In March, when nine runs of Northwest salmon were listed under the Endangered Species Act, the upper Columbia River Spring Chinook run, which has habitats in rivers in Okanogan County, warranted endangered status. That could mean severe restrictions on logging, livestock grazing, irrigation and water usage. With the salmon listings, "it makes the future uncertain" for any economic-development effort the county might pursue, says Mr. Schulz of the commissioners' board. And in a county with a 7.4% unemployment rate and where the logging and ranching industries that once employed most people ran out of steam in the 1980s, job growth is a big worry. So when Houston-based Battle Mountain Gold Co. announced plans for an open-pit gold mine on Buckhorn Mountain, many residents were thrilled. The Crown Jewel mine would employ about 150 people, and mean $9.9 million a year in property taxes. But environmental concerns are casting a shadow on the mine, too. Conservation groups, Native American tribes and the federal government oppose Crown Jewel because, they say, it would leave behind a 118-acre pit and toxic pit-lake, along with other dangerous waste products. Challenges have been filed in federal and state agencies and courts, and the Clinton administration says under an 1872 mining law, the mine would dump too much waste on public land. "Battle Mountain Gold would mean a tremendous amount not only to our community, but to our county," says Jimmie Dale Walker, the mayor of Oroville. "If you create more than 100 jobs, you can figure you're going to hire another service person for every four or five permanent jobs. . . . So it's not just the jobs with the mine, it's the jobs those jobs will create." The economic pinch brought on by environmental interests could reach local school districts. The Loomis Forest Fund, a coalition of conservation groups headed by Bellingham-based Northwest Ecosystem Alliance, has raised about $13 million to buy logging rights on 25,000 acres in the state-owned Loomis Forest. The forest, home to wolverines, grizzly bears and the healthiest remaining population of lynx in the lower 48 states, is on land in Okanogan County managed by the state Department of Natural Resources. Portions of the forest have been logged for the past 30 years, with the money from timber sales going into a trust fund for schools. Public-school administrators say that taking the logging rights out of commercial use could cost school districts millions of dollars and have a big impact on timber-dependent economies. As it is, the county's timber industry has been decimated, with only three sawmills operating -- there were 27 in the early 1980s -- and 31 million board feet cut this year, compared with 300 million board feet 10 years ago. The state is expected to decide this fall whether the sale will go through. Adding insult to injury is the strength of the U.S. dollar. That makes agricultural commodities grown in the county less desirable compared with those produced by foreign countries. And it is keeping British Columbian shoppers at home. The Canadian dollar is worth 67 U.S. cents and county sales-tax revenue is down 5% from this time last year. Merchants near the border say they're feeling the pain. Jim Price, general manager of Price's Center, a grocery and general store in Oroville, says he had to reduce his inventory and lay off several employees, though he won't say how many. "We're not selling the Pendleton wool shirts that we used to," Mr. Price says. "We're selling more of the cheaper import shirts." Okanogan's economic predicament is so serious that the Legislature this year voted to use state funds to reimburse the county for 80% of the cost of its first-ever capital murder trial. Before lawmakers came to the rescue, the county was looking at spending 9% of its annual budget on public defender and other court costs. So what does the future hold for the county? The city of Omak is using $3 million in Federal Aviation Administration grants to renovate Omak Airport -- which hasn't been served by commercial flights for 20 years -- in hopes of building an industrial park and luring commercial airlines. The county commissioners have put together an economic-development task force to explore ways to fund the expansion of sewage systems and other infrastructure upgrades to lure new businesses. The county is also capitalizing on the one segment of the population that's moving into the county: senior citizens. An assisted-living facility housing some 58 residents opened in Omak in Feburary, and a specialty-living residence is set to open next door around October. Kathy Stevenson, owner of the Bike Shop in Okanogan, says she is seeing so many more retirees come into her store that she's looking into carrying electric, three-wheel and recumbent bicycles -- things "that appeal to older customers." Still, civic leaders are for the most part stumped, feeling solutions are beyond their control. Says Commissioner Thiele: "We seem to be the only county that can't seem to get projects to make jobs." ---                   The Lesser Washington    From the Endangered Species Act to competition from Chinese apple growers, Okanogan County faces a slew of outside forces that has left its economy barren    Facts About Okanogan County:    -- Square miles: 5,300   -- Population: 38,400   -- Three largest cities:        Omak, population 4,500        Okanogan, population 2,400        Brewster, population 2,100   -- Primary industries: agriculture, timber, tourism   -- Main agriculture commodities: apples, cattle, alfalfa,      wheat   -- Largest employers: agriculture, government   -- Unemployment rate: 7.4% in July; compared with a 4.6%      state average   -- Okanogan 1998 median household income: $27,041;      compared with the state average of $44,134   -- County's annual budget: $11 million    Sources: Okanogan County Commission; Washington Employment Security Department Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Nine runs of Northwest salmon were listed under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: NA

Title: World-Wide
Text: BATTLE LINES FORMED as Bush got set to address Congress tonight. The president is expected to lay out greater details of his Social Security plan, but not present a full-blown proposal. Top Senate Democrat Reid said the private-accounts plan has no bipartisan support and likely will fail. He said most Democrats oppose confirming Gonzales as attorney general, but won't filibuster a Senate vote expected tomorrow. He added, however, Democrats will again block votes on judicial nominees Bush vows to resubmit. Bush also plans to boost IRS enforcement funding, add $500 to Pell Grants and push a Medicaid overhaul states may resist. His "Clear Skies" plan may need some fancy footwork to get a Senate vote. Dean is favored to become the Democratic Party chairman on Feb. 12 as ex-Rep. Frost dropped out after failing to win organized labor's support. --- Iraq's president said tens of thousands in Sunni areas may have been denied votes due to ballot shortages, and said U.S. troops must remain for now. The U.S. denied a soldier was abducted and a doll maker said a figure shown in an insurgent Web video looked like one of its action figures. Iraq's U.N. envoy wants to stop paying for suspended arms inspections. An ex-Abu Ghraib guard pleaded guilty on the eve of trial for Iraq prisoner abuses. The military extended its Guantanamo inquiry by a month. --- Israel slowed its pullout from five West Bank towns in response to fresh shelling of a Gaza settlement.
