Meanwhile, the attorney general prohibited the confiscation of Palestinian lands the new security barrier has cut off. Egyptian police clashed in the Sinai mountains with Islamic militants believed tied to the October Red Sea resort bombings, killing at least one. --- Nepal's king declared martial law, put political leaders under house arrest and cut off contact with the outside world, including planes en route. --- Pope John Paul II was rushed to a Rome hospital after he suffered what an aide labeled a "breathing crisis." He has battled the flu since Sunday. --- Pakistan said the U.S. agreed to arms sales opposed by India. A U.S. commander said Pakistan is helping site Afghan-based U.S. artillery fire. --- Colombian rebels carried out their deadliest attack in some time, using missiles to kill 14 marines at a navy base located in a southern river town. --- A car bomb in Georgia killed three policemen and hurt dozens in Gori, raising fear of new clashes with Russia-backed South Ossetia separatists. --- Zimbabwe set March 31 parliamentary elections, but Mugabe has managed to crush opposition since the disputed 2002 vote extending his reign. --- The Justice Department warned judges that their sentencing decisions will be monitored after the Supreme Court upended guidelines. --- Tough ethics rules were announced by the National Institutes of Health, including broad curbs on employees' consulting and investing. --- Medicare will cover virility drugs such as Viagra under the new prescription benefit, HHS officials said, but under tight controls. Antidepressant use fell among children and adolescents since the FDA required stronger warning labels in October, Medco reported. --- A federal judge ruled the Bush administration violated the Endangered Species Act in a 2003 rule change relaxing protection of U.S. gray wolves. The EU would require a cost-benefit test for new environmental regulations under a plan to reinvigorate industry due out today. --- Canada introduced a bill to legalize same-sex marriages. Pitched battle with the opposition Conservatives and religious organizations is certain. --- Medical bills trigger half of all personal bankruptcies and insurance offers little protection, a Harvard study in the journal Health Affairs found.
event_description: A federal judge ruled the Bush administration violated the Endangered Species Act in a 2003 rule change relaxing protection of U.S. gray wolves.
setting: federal court case
characters: Bush administration [Villain]; U.S. gray wolves [Victim]; federal judge [Hero]
plot: A federal judge ruled against the Bush administration for violating the Endangered Species Act by relaxing protections for U.S. gray wolves, which resulted in harm to the species.
moral: The problem is the Bush administration's failure to protect endangered species, and the text suggests that stronger enforcement of the Endangered Species Act is necessary to ensure their protection.

Title: Congress Passes Debt-Limit Boost; Specter Flap Ends
Text: WASHINGTON -- Congress narrowly approved an $800 billion increase in the federal debt limit, as Republicans put the final touches on a year-end spending bill and ended their postelection infighting over who will next lead the Senate Judiciary Committee. Sen. Arlen Specter (R., Pa.) is all but assured of the post after being embraced by fellow Republicans on the panel and pledging yesterday that he would give President Bush's judicial nominees "quick committee hearings and early committee votes." Conservatives chafed that Mr. Specter must be kept on "a very short leash," but Republicans said the four-term 74-year-old moderate had weathered the storm he set off by suggesting that antiabortion nominees could face tough sledding in the Senate, even with gains made by his party Nov. 2. "It's a done deal," said the outgoing Judiciary chairman, Sen. Orrin Hatch (R., Utah). By comparison, the debt increase last night -- and the spending bill this weekend -- may be only the opening rounds of a larger budget battle facing the new Congress and the administration next year. The expanded borrowing authority should carry the government at least through this fiscal year ending Sept. 30, and the Treasury held out the possibility of going ahead with a delayed four-week bill auction as soon as today. The increase is the third in as many years, bringing the ceiling to $8.184 trillion, or 37% higher than when President Bush took office in 2001. The rapid escalation is a political liability for Republicans. Just as in the Senate on Wednesday, the 208-204 House vote largely broke along party lines, and Democrats accused the White House of disastrous fiscal politics and a "debt debacle" that will haunt generations to come. The fiscal pressures are a big factor in the year-end bargaining over spending, and the $388 billion-plus package taking shape provides only a 1% to 2% increase over 2004 for domestic spending. The Internal Revenue Service isn't immune, and the agency's proposed $10.32 billion budget for fiscal 2005 that started Oct. 1 could essentially cut by half the funding increase sought for improved tax enforcement in this fiscal year. Instead of a $393 million, or 9.4%, increase in this account, the IRS is promised just $227 million more than in 2004, and the picture will get worse after an additional 0.75% across-the-board cut is imposed, subtracting a further $33 million. That reduction could increase as the White House demands more for its priorities, such as the space program or the proposed nuclear- waste repository in Nevada's Yucca Mountain. At the same time, the very size of the measure makes it a magnet for extraneous legislative provisions promoted by special interests in the days before adjournment. Farm- and chemical-industry forces had sought a change in the natural-resources budget for temporary protection from new regulations meant to require more-stringent analysis of the impact of pesticides on endangered species. Thousands of pesticides have been approved by the Environmental Protection Agency as not harmful to humans, but the Endangered Species Act also requires consultation about the impact on wildlife. The issue is especially important in the Pacific Northwest because of the potential impact on the region's salmon. "These kinds of last-minute amendments are very, very dangerous," said Rep. Norman Dicks (D., Wash.), who interceded with top Republicans on the House and Senate Appropriations committees to strike the language. While other less-controversial riders are being added elsewhere, there appears to be more of an effort this year by many in the party leadership to avoid time-consuming controversies and keep the bill as clean as possible -- and on a fast track. While his fellow Texans have supported new legislation to challenge country-of-origin food-labeling laws, even Majority Leader Tom DeLay said he was uncertain if the provisions should be included in the omnibus spending bill now. "This is one case where less is more," said a Senate leadership aide.
event_description: Proposed change in natural-resources budget for temporary protection from new regulations on pesticide impact analysis.
setting: Congressional debate over year-end spending bill and federal debt limit increase
characters: Rep. Norman Dicks [Hero]; Farm- and chemical-industry forces [Villain]; endangered species [Victim]
plot: Farm- and chemical-industry forces sought a change in the natural-resources budget to protect themselves from new regulations requiring stricter pesticide impact analysis, which endangered species, particularly salmon in the Pacific Northwest, would suffer from. Rep. Norman Dicks intervened to strike this language from the bill.
moral: The problem is the potential harm to endangered species from relaxed pesticide regulations, and the text suggests that Congress should prioritize the protection of wildlife by rejecting last-minute amendments that undermine environmental safeguards.

Title: High court to decide on regulatory limits of protecting animals on private land
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Supreme Court agreed to decide how far regulators may go to protect endangered animals from industry or development on private land.    A decision, which is expected by July, could be of tremendous importance to timber interests and other businesses whose activities affect the habitats of imperiled species.    The high court announced Friday it would hear the Clinton administration's appeal of a lower court ruling that the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service had overstepped its authority under the Endangered Species Act. The service barred "modification" of endangered species' habitats that cause injury or death of animals on the endangered list.    In 1990, the government put the northern spotted owl on the list and said it was threatened by logging in the Pacific Northwest. That prompted timber interests to launch a sweeping legal challenge of the Fish and Wildlife Service's longstanding interpretation of the Endangered Species Act.    In a ruling last year, the federal appeals court here agreed with the timber industry that regulators had misinterpreted the word "harm" in the statute. Congress didn't intend the word to mean mere changes in habitat, but instead a "direct application of force against the animal," the appeals court concluded.    The administration warned in its appeal to the Supreme Court that under the lower court's ruling, regulators would be severely hampered and some threatened species could perish. Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt has relied on the Fish and Wildlife Service's more expansive interpretation of the law to pressure loggers in the Southeast and developers in California to restrict their activities on land that is home to endangered birds other than the spotted owl.    The administration further argued that the Fish and Wildlife Service's interpretation is consistent with the Endangered Species Act's text and its purpose of protecting threatened animals from development and industry. (Babbitt vs. Sweet Home Chapter of Communities for a Greater Oregon)    Separately, the Supreme Court said it would decide how quickly workers must file suits claiming they weren't given the legally required notice of plant closings.    A federal law known as the Warn Act obliges employers to notify workers 60 days in advance of a plant's being shut. Employees may sue to get money damages if they aren't notified. But the Warn Act doesn't specify the time period in which such suits must be filed.    The question before the justices is whether a six-month limit found in another federal labor law should be applied, or whether the typically longer periods found in state labor statutes should be used. Lower courts have issued conflicting rulings on the issue, prompting the justices to intervene.    The high court will consider a pair of consolidated cases involving suits against Cargill Inc.'s North Star Steel Co. and Crown Cork & Seal Co. (North Star Steel Co. vs. Thomas, Crown Cork & Seal Co. vs. United Steelworkers of America)  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Supreme Court is set to decide on the interpretation of the Endangered Species Act regarding the protection of endangered species' habitats.
setting: Supreme Court decision regarding the Endangered Species Act
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; timber interests [Villain]; endangered species [Victim]; Clinton administration [Hero]; Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt [Hero]
plot: The Supreme Court will decide on the appeal from the Clinton administration regarding the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's authority to protect endangered species' habitats, which has been challenged by timber interests claiming misinterpretation of the Endangered Species Act.
moral: The text suggests that the Supreme Court should uphold the Fish and Wildlife Service's interpretation of the Endangered Species Act to ensure the protection of endangered species from harmful industry practices.

Title: U.S. News: Wisconsin Suit: Keep Dogs From Wolf Hunt
Text: Groups opposed to animal cruelty are challenging the use of hunting dogs in Wisconsin's first-ever wolf-hunting season this fall, saying it will create bloody confrontations and dog deaths. This fall marks the first season of wolf hunting in the upper Midwest. The predators were removed from federal protection under the Endangered Species Act in Minnesota, Wisconsin and the Upper Peninsula of Michigan in January, following years of legal challenges. Wisconsin is the only state to allow dogs to be used for tracking wolves, which prompted the Wisconsin Humane Society and similar groups to file suit against the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources last week in Dane County Circuit Court. The groups say the regulations put in place by the state don't adequately protect hunting dogs and wolves, and will create situations akin to illegal dog fighting. "Mainstream hunters do not want wolves to die in bloody, grotesque ways," said Anne Reed, director of the Wisconsin Humane Society. "This is inhumane and wrong for both animals." The court challenge comes as hunters are applying for hunting permits. So far, more than 10,200 people have applied for permits. About 2,000 permits will be awarded by lottery. The Department of Natural Resources said in an email to hunters that it is continuing the hunt as planned and believes its rules are sufficient for the use of dogs. A department spokesman declined to comment further. Still, groups opposed to animal cruelty say hunting dogs are being injured by wolves even before the wolf hunting begins, pointing to the death of 192 dogs since 1985 that occurred while dogs were used to hunt other animals. Wolves were placed under federal protection in the early 1970s. As the Midwest wolf population has grown from a base in northern Minnesota, farmers and locals have increasingly complained of threats to cattle, dogs and deer. A separate population in the Rocky Mountains was removed from federal protection in Montana and Idaho in 2009, with hunting subsequently allowed in those states. The groups in the latest lawsuit say they aren't trying to end wolf hunting altogether, but instead focus on the treatment of dogs in the new hunt. The dispute is expected to come to a head in coming weeks, as lawyers look for a judge to block the use of dogs and force the rules to be rewritten by the department or the state legislature, said Jodi Habush Sinykin, a lawyer representing the anti-cruelty groups. Subscribe to WSJ: Credit: By Caroline Porter and Mark Peters
event_description: A lawsuit has been filed challenging the regulations of Wisconsin's wolf-hunting season regarding the use of hunting dogs.
setting: Dane County Circuit Court, Wisconsin
characters: Wisconsin Humane Society and similar groups [Hero]; Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources [Villain]; hunting dogs [Victim]
plot: The Wisconsin Humane Society and similar groups are challenging the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources' regulations on the use of hunting dogs in the wolf-hunting season, arguing that these regulations will lead to harm for both dogs and wolves. The Department of Natural Resources continues to support its rules despite the lawsuit.
moral: The problem is the inhumane treatment of hunting dogs and the potential for bloody confrontations during the wolf hunt; the text suggests that the regulations should be rewritten to better protect the dogs.

Title: Victory For Panhandle In Fish Fight
Text: Texas Panhandle residents have scored an important victory in their fight to keep the Arkansas River Shiner off the endangered-species list. U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service officials proposed listing the shiner -- a two-inch-long minnow found in the Canadian River -- as endangered in 1994, at the urging of agency biologists who have been tracking the animal's population. But, last month, officials in the agency's Tulsa field office, which has authority over the Panhandle, forwarded a recommendation that the fish be placed on a list of "threatened" rather than "endangered" species. Landowners and agricultural interests had feared that an endangered listing would trigger restrictions on water pumping from the Ogallala Aquifer, a source of water for streams where the shiner is found and the primary source of drinking and irrigation water for the Panhandle. The less-restrictive "threatened" designation triggers the same rules that are designed to protect a species as an endangered listing does. But it gives federal officials far more leeway to grant exceptions to those rules, and both sides of the debate expect officials to use that leeway to make sure farmers and developers never have to suffer limits on pumping from the aquifer. "It gives us some room for negotiation," says Wayne Wyatt, manager of the High Plains Underground Water Conservation District in Lubbock. Meanwhile, the proposal has infuriated environmental groups, who threaten to sue if the designation is adopted and broad exemptions are put in place. They insist that the rapid decline of the shiner over the past century -- it has disappeared from 80% of its historical range -- is evidence enough to declare the shiner as endangered. The plan "is a cop-out" that allows federal officials to water down the protections the shiner would have enjoyed as an "endangered" animal, says Scott Royder, a spokesman for the Lone Star Chapter of the Sierra Club in Austin. The plan still must be approved by a regional Fish and Wildlife office in Albuquerque, N.M., and by the department's director, Jamie Clark, in Washington. It could be thrown out, officials say, if agency lawyers determine there is not enough evidence to justify a "threatened" listing. Department officials are optimistic their recommendations will ultimately be sustained. Since their original recommendation, officials say, they have gathered new scientific information that justifies a "threatened" listing. And, they add, historically, recommendations made by field offices are almost always approved. "If we've done our homework, [the proposal] shouldn't be changed, and in this case, I think we have," says Jerry Brabander, a field supervisor in the wildlife service's Tulsa field office, which was given the responsibility of reviewing public arguments in the matter. (The High Plains Underground Water Conservation District, which has authority over the Ogallala, already has the power to regulate how much water is pumped from the aquifer. Indeed, its managers already enforce a 47-year-old rule telling farmers how much space they must maintain between wells. But its managers are loath to institute new restrictions, and are even more wary about the potential for federally imposed rules.) Originally, Fish and Wildlife Service biologists agreed that the shiner required the tougher protections of the "endangered" status. Because the shiner is only prevalent in one river, wildlife officials argued, it needed as much protection as possible, since a large-scale toxic spill or disease could wipe out the entire population. Opponents in the Panhandle disagreed from the start, crowding public hearings and submitting boxloads of testimony to Fish and Wildlife officials pleading against "endangered" status. Under the terms of the Endangered Species Act, individuals, businesses and cities are prohibited from engaging in any activity that harms an endangered species or its habitat. Lobbying against the proposal has been intense. A swarm of Texas political leaders contacted the Fish and Wildlife Service. And the Texas Parks and Wildlife Department joined numerous lobbying organizations, including the Texas Farm Bureau and the Texas Cattlefeeders Association, in campaigning against the designation. To force a decision, the Sierra Club and the Southwest Center for Biological Diversity, of Tucson, Ariz., filed a lawsuit in U.S. District Court in Albuquerque in March demanding an immediate decision on how the fish would be listed. The conclusion: Fish and Wildlife biologists say new evidence shows that a "threatened" listing is more appropriate. Data provided by the Texas Water Development Board indicate that the Ogallala Aquifer doesn't contribute as much water to the Canadian River as previously believed, reducing the risk posed to shiner habitats if water levels drop in the aquifer. Furthermore, biologists say, a more careful study of the river system indicates that the portion above Lake Meredith is isolated from segments downstream, meaning that any toxic spills or diseases would only wipe out part of the shiner's habitat. And department officials say they have more closely examined another body of water where small numbers of shiner are found -- the Cimarron River -- and concluded that there are enough fish there to re-start the species if catastrophe wipes out the shiner in the Canadian River. With a "threatened" listing, federal wildlife officials can exempt certain groups or actions that would otherwise be prohibited by an endangered listing. In the past, exceptions have been granted that allow scientists to capture and kill protected animals for scientific study and that permit fishermen to catch protected fish, so long as they throw the fish back unharmed. Broad exemptions are less common, but have also occurred: in Southern California, for example, Fish and Wildlife officials have used a "threatened" listing to allow developers to build in areas favored by the California Gnatcatcher bird, which was listed as "threatened" in 1993. In the Panhandle, a "threatened" listing would enable the federal wildlife agency to go forward with an agreement, reached last year with the Texas Parks and Wildlife Department and the Oklahoma Department of Wildlife Conservation, in which the Service promised not to "restrict or regulate groundwater use" from the Ogallala Aquifer or to adopt conservation measures that would adversely affect "existing agricultural and land-management activities in the region." The Wildlife Service didn't sign the agreement because it wanted to subject the deal to a public comment period first, and also because it wanted to make minor revisions to the plan. Officials acknowledge that if the "threatened" listing is approved, they are considering a broad exemption based on the agreement. "Our intent is to live up to the spirit of that agreement," says Mr. Brabander of the Fish and Wildlife Service. But, he adds, "anything is possible" in terms of exemptions. Environmentalists say broad exemptions are unacceptable. Protecting the species under the terms of that agreement, says Peter Galvin, a conservation biologist with the Biological Diversity center, would merely "allow the shiner to become extinct more slowly." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Proposal to list the Arkansas River Shiner as 'threatened' instead of 'endangered'.
setting: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service proposal process regarding species listing
characters: Texas Panhandle residents [Hero]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service officials [Villain]; Arkansas River Shiner [Victim]; Environmental groups [Hero]; Wayne Wyatt [Hero]; Scott Royder [Hero]; Peter Galvin [Hero]
plot: Texas Panhandle residents successfully lobby against the endangered listing of the Arkansas River Shiner, leading U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service officials to propose a less restrictive 'threatened' designation, which is expected to allow for exemptions that benefit local agricultural interests.
moral: The rapid decline of the Arkansas River Shiner necessitates stronger protections, and the proposed 'threatened' listing undermines the species' survival; therefore, the endangered status should be upheld to ensure adequate conservation measures.

Title: Environmental Groups Intend to Sue Oregon
Text: PORTLAND, Ore. -- Environmentalists and commercial fishermen filed notice they will sue the state of Oregon to block logging practices on private lands that they consider harmful to the endangered coho salmon, in a move that could eventually curtail timber operations there.    In giving the 60-day notice of intent to file suit in federal court, as required under the Endangered Species Act, the groups said state officials have failed to protect endangered coho runs by allowing logging and road building on erosion-prone slopes and logging in areas too close to streams. They said if the state doesn't move to change the practices, an injunction will be sought during July.    If granted, that could result in significant reductions of timber harvests on the lands -- but not on the same scale as when vast swaths of federally owned forests were declared off-limits a decade ago amid spotted-owl restrictions, say the critics of the forestry practices.    "We are not looking for a shutdown of the timber business, but what we do need to stop are the specific practices that are known to be harmful to fish," said David Bayles, conservation director of the Pacific Rivers Council, an environmental group in Eugene. The suit will be brought by that group and others including the Audubon Society of Portland, and Pacific Coast Federation of Fisherman's Associations in San Francisco.    Oregon officials denied allowing any practices that hurt the coho salmon. "We proactively enforce existing rules," said Ted Lorensen, forest-practices program director for the Oregon Department of Forestry in Salem.
event_description: Environmentalists and commercial fishermen filed notice to sue Oregon over logging practices harmful to endangered coho salmon.
setting: federal court case regarding logging practices in Oregon
characters: Environmentalists and commercial fishermen [Hero]; State of Oregon officials [Villain]; Endangered coho salmon [Victim]
plot: Environmentalists and commercial fishermen file a notice to sue Oregon for harmful logging practices, claiming state officials have failed to protect coho salmon, which could lead to an injunction that reduces timber harvests.
moral: The problem is the harmful logging practices that threaten the coho salmon, and the text suggests that the state must change these practices to protect the species.

Title: Seminoles' Chairman Takes Tribe's Music Out of the Big Swamp
Text: Chief Jim Billie, the Seminole Tribe of Florida chairman, has donned many hats through the years, from roadside souvenir salesman to alligator wrestler. Now, he wants to add a new hat: national folk singer. The Vietnam veteran, whose home is in rural Hendry County, retells mythical stories and folk tales on "Alligator Tales," a compact disk released this fall by Sound of America Records, in Albuquerque, N.M. The CD, which mixes elements of tribal, rock and country music, is being played on some 85 "alternative" stations nationwide, says a spokeswoman for Mr. Billie. Most important to Mr. Billie are the Seminole Indian lyrics in some songs. "I'm trying to get the young people to learn their language," he says. "We're passing legends and myths from one generation to the next. Just like they did in the old days. Only now you can buy the CD instead of making your voice hoarse." This isn't Mr. Billie's first brush with national fame. In 1983, he was prosecuted for violating the federal Endangered Species Act by killing a Florida panther on the Big Cypress Reservation. Environmentalists were outraged when he beat the charges by arguing in part that he did it in keeping with Seminole religious tradition. Under Mr. Billie's leadership, the 2,500-member Seminoles have become among the most prosperous tribes in the nation netting, by his estimates, some $150 million a year from interests in casinos alone. Even with all his business accomplishments, Mr. Billie says music has always been central to his life. He has performed his music at Indian festivals around the country for years. Some of the selections on "Alligator Tales" are personal tributes to his grandparents. One song, "The Rabbit and the Bear" is a folk tale he recalls his grandmother telling him with the moral "Trust your wit," a tenet he says has helped him in business. Meanwhile, the Seminoles presented a copy of the song "Che Hun Tah Mo," (which means "Hello, how are you?") to Florida State University President Sandy D'Alemberte during the recent Florida-Florida State game for use as a cheer at Seminoles football games. Unlike many tribes that have taken umbrage with teams using Indian names as mascots, the Seminoles approve of the FSU nickname. In fact, Mr. Billie says use of his song would be a good way for FSU to honor the tribe in return for the use of its name. Jim Melton, president of the alumni association, says the marching band will spend the next few months deciding how to incorporate the song into its repertoire. "It may take a few years for this to catch on," Mr. Melton says. "But we're excited to incorporate more of the Seminole culture." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Prosecution for killing a Florida panther.
setting: null
characters: Chief Jim Billie [Hero]; Environmentalists [Villain]; Florida panther [Victim]
plot: Chief Jim Billie, after being prosecuted for killing a Florida panther, argues that his actions were in line with Seminole religious tradition, leading to his acquittal. He now focuses on promoting Seminole culture through music.
moral: The text suggests that preserving and promoting Seminole culture, including language and traditions, is essential, and that honoring the tribe's heritage should be embraced by institutions like Florida State University.

Title: Interior, HHS Nominees Lay Out Goals of Incoming Administration
Text: WASHINGTON -- Interior Secretary-nominee Gale Norton defended her record as a conservative conservationist and states'-rights advocate in a Senate confirmation hearing that was pointed but polite.    Environmental groups have whipped up a vigorous campaign to block Ms. Norton's confirmation, based on a trove of past writings and legal arguments -- including some that challenge the constitutionality of federal laws that, if confirmed, she would be charged with enforcing.    Meanwhile, President-elect George W. Bush's nominee to head the Department of Health and Human Services, Wisconsin Gov. Tommy Thompson, told a Senate panel that the new administration would press ahead with a proposal for a Medicare prescription-drug plan for low-income elderly. He said it also would welcome congressional overtures to reach quick agreement on a comprehensive overhaul of Medicare.    During his confirmation hearing, Gov. Thompson said Mr. Bush was committed to propose his "immediate helping hand" plan, referring to Mr. Bush's plan to provide $48 billion in block grants over four years to states to help low-income seniors buy prescription drugs. But he also said the administration was ready to "plow ahead" to develop consensus with Congress on overhauling the federal health program for the elderly and disabled.    If an overhaul could occur, he said, "I am absolutely certain the president-elect would be one of the happiest guys in America, and I know I would be."    During Ms. Norton's confirmation hearing before the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee, senators pressed her about many of her contentious statements, and in most cases, the former Colorado attorney general used the opportunity to expand her position and remove the hard edges that have worried many Democrats.    She stuck to generalities on flashpoints like national monuments, water rights and wilderness areas, and stressed that she was "fully committed" to enforcing the Endangered Species Act. Ms. Norton refrained from strongly endorsing anything controversial -- including domestic oil and gas exploration, expected to be a hallmark of a Bush energy policy. But she did send a signal to business interests that she would consider rolling back Clinton-era regulations and restrictions. "We will be looking at what needs to be changed," she said.    Responding to a question about domestic energy development from Sen. Frank Murkowski (R., Alaska), Ms. Norton said, "We would hope to work with you to find the right kinds of solutions that would balance environmental protection, as well as finding ways of providing the kind of energy resources that need to be available."    The Senate Finance Committee, which is handling Mr. Thompson's nomination, praised his record in Wisconsin. But several Democrats sharply criticized the block-grant approach, saying a drug benefit should be available to all Medicare beneficiaries, not just low-income ones.    After the hearing, GOP Sen. Charles Grassley of Iowa, the panel's incoming chairman, also expressed skepticism about the block grants and said he favored quick action on a comprehensive overhaul of Medicare. He urged the new administration to appoint a task force to rapidly develop an overhaul plan. He said Congress should be prepared to act on the issue in the spring -- an ambitious time frame considering Medicare's political and technical complexities.    Gov. Thompson said Mr. Bush believes he must make the block-grant proposal on principle, because it was part of his campaign platform. The governor also said it would be a good "security blanket," should comprehensive Medicare reform fail. But he stressed that the administration would be flexible and work with Congress to develop a comprehensive reform plan quickly, if that is what lawmakers wanted.  Credit: Staff Reporters of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Norton expressed commitment to enforcing the Endangered Species Act during her confirmation hearing.
setting: Senate confirmation hearing
characters: Gale Norton [Hero]; Environmental groups [Villain]; Democrats [Victim]
plot: Gale Norton defends her record and commitment to enforcing the Endangered Species Act during her Senate confirmation hearing, while environmental groups campaign against her confirmation based on her past writings. Democrats express concern over her positions and the potential rollback of regulations.
moral: The problem is the opposition from environmental groups and the concerns of Democrats regarding Norton's past actions. The text suggests that the Senate should support Norton's confirmation to ensure enforcement of the Endangered Species Act while balancing environmental protection with energy resource development.

Title: World-Wide
Text: Blair is likely to face Arab hostility as he mediates Mideast peace. Among his biggest challenges as the "Quartet" envoy is anger over the wars in Iraq and Lebanon, and the perception that he parrots the U.S. line. In his last appearance before Parliament as Britain's prime minister, he said a two-state solution is possible. Gordon Brown succeeded him as Britain's leader. Separately, Bush said he would appoint an envoy to a coalition of Muslim countries to bolster ties to the U.S. The Israeli army killed at least 11 Palestinians in Gaza in an operation targeting Hamas militants on its border. --- The Senate subpoenaed White House and Cheney records on Bush's warrantless surveillance program. The administration gave no immediate reply, but the move holds the potential for a constitutional confrontation even as Bush's support slides, even among Republicans. --- The Senate immigration bill appears in trouble as attempts from right and left to alter elements of the bipartisan deal failed. Republicans and Democrats are pulling back from supporting the bill, and it isn't certain whether 60 votes to close debate can be mustered today. --- Bush sends his national security adviser to Capitol Hill today to try to limit Republican defections on Iraq. Iran said it is open to new Iraq talks, but the U.S. was noncommittal. A U.S. envoy in Baghdad predicted progress by fall against sectarian attacks. A car bomb killed at least 14. Iranians torched gasoline stations in Tehran and spewed abuse at Ahmadinejad after the government imposed fuel rationing. --- North Korea allowed U.N. atomic inspectors to visit its reactor. Separately, the U.S. said it was "troubled" Pyongyang test-fired a missile. --- The U.S. and EU reached deals on securing access to international bank data and information on trans-Atlantic air passengers. --- The U.S. prison population grew 2.8% in 2006 to 1.6 million federal or state inmates, the Justice Department said, biggest jump since 2000. --- Pregnant women who take certain antidepressants have an elevated but small risk of birth defects, according to two studies. --- Archaeologists said a long-overlooked mummy is Queen Hatshepsut, Egypt's most powerful female pharaoh. DNA tests are ongoing. --- The bald eagle will be taken off the protected list, a sign conservationists say that the embattled Endangered Species Act is working. --- Heavy rains hit Texas, latest in a swarm of storms that have killed 11. --- Died: Liz Claiborne, 78, fashion designer, in New York, on Tuesday.
event_description: The bald eagle will be taken off the protected list.
setting: political context surrounding the Endangered Species Act
characters: conservationists [Hero]
plot: The bald eagle is being delisted from the protected list, which conservationists interpret as a sign that the Endangered Species Act is effective.
moral: The delisting of the bald eagle indicates that the Endangered Species Act is successfully protecting species, suggesting that continued support for the Act is necessary.

Title: Nature Conservancy-Led Land-Preservation Plan Outperforms and Out-Innovates Federal Program
Text: EUDORA, Kan. -- Like jet fighters in formation, two brilliantly colored wood ducks rocket from cover and wing out over the gently flowing Kansas River. Behind them is a 54-acre fragment of another era. It's the riverbank forest as it must have looked before the white man came: Wild grape vines, thick as a man's wrist, looping between trees with trunks a yard in diameter. And almost lost among the trees are three men -- scientists doing a poet's work. They're part of a state-private conservation effort that's arguably outperforming and out-innovating the federal government. Initiated and coordinated by the nonprofit Nature Conservancy, the Natural Heritage Program now has spread to all 50 states. Its goal is to inventory, then preserve, critical habitat for the nation's most endangered plant and animal species. Or, as the Conservancy puts it, to save "the last of the least, and the best of the rest." Throughout most of the 1980s, a period of depressed federal land-acquisition budgets, the Conservancy has acquired more acreage than the federal government's Fish and Wildlife Service. The service is the lead land-acquisition agency when it comes to carrying out the federal Endangered Species Act. And at least four-fifths of this acreage was first identified through state Natural Heritage programs. In the process, the Conservancy-state effort has established a computerized data base so fine-grained that, in some states, it records the precise locations of individual eagle nests and clumps of globally endangered plants. "We've done it by going into partnership with state governments," says Hardy Wieting Jr., national coordinator of the heritage network. "The federal government has nothing like it." Washington does have the 1973 Endangered Species Act, the keystone of endangered species protection. But a January General Accounting Office report rapped the Fish and Wildlife Service and other federal agencies for moving too slowly to execute that legislation. The report estimated that one-third of the 500 plants and animals on the federal endangered list are still in decline. It cited a lack of systematic, coordinated federal species-recovery plans. And it warned that "the Earth is nearing a stage of extinction of species unequaled since that of the age of dinosaurs." The bottom line is that you can't protect endangered species unless you know where they are. Enter the state Heritage programs and the Conservancy, a national environmental group unlike any other. Founded in 1951, the Conservancy owns and manages more than 1,000 preserves, the largest private nature preserve system in the world. The Conservancy takes great pains to sound unromantic. "We're in the science business, not the pretty business," President Frank Boren likes to say. It makes a studied effort to avoid sentimentality. Thus the focus of the Arlington, Va.-based organization isn't scenery (although the acres it saves often are strikingly scenic). The unwavering focus is on preserving biological diversity. That means saving species and their habitat -- by definition, species most people have never heard of. The Neosho madtom, for example. The Neosho madtom is a rather undistinguished-looking catfish, two inches long and threatened nationally, and Kansas has it. Or Scott's riffle beetle. A Kansas lake may be its only habitat on the globe. Or the western fringed prairie orchid, which has only three known populations in this state. "What I'd really love to find is the running buffalo clover," muses Craig Freeman, a state biologist who coordinates the Kansas program. Running buffalo clover, which is on the federal endangered list, is a kind of Holy Grail of the prairie. As he speaks, Mr. Freeman is returning from an idyllic afternoon -- a hike through an upland forest of spreading oaks and nodding dogtooth violets, accentuated by white-tailed deer and red-tailed hawks. "Ground truthing," such field inventories are called. But much of his work, like that of colleagues William Busby and Chris Lauver, is far more tedious. In state after state, Natural Heritage workers poke through herbariums, doggedly study satellite photos and pore over old records. To help guide the search here in Kansas, Mr. Lauver has been consulting the handwritten field notes of 19th century surveyors. This state, hardly a hotbed of environmentalism, was the 47th to join the Heritage program. Its participation is due in good measure to the efforts of Republican Gov. Mike Hayden, a trained wildlife conservationist. "Some of these areas are so important scientifically that they must be preserved," he says. "The Conservancy deals in a very professional way, with voluntary, arms-length negotiations, that's more likely to be successful in a state like ours." This month the Conservancy signed up the last remaining state, Alaska. Even so, the nationwide program is just hitting its stride in much of the country. And, since updating the data bank is an open-ended process, the program is intended to continue as a more or less permanent partnership with the states. Pioneered in 1974 in South Carolina, the program now keeps a computerized tab on more than 55,000 species, from common to endangered. The list grows almost daily. Wyoming and Colorado have discovered a dozen species previously unknown to science. Massachusetts has rediscovered two dozen species thought to have vanished from the state a half-century ago. In one of the most ambitious state efforts, the Texas program has located 351 globally imperiled plants and animals. Reversing the normal state of affairs, federal agencies increasingly turn for help to the states and their ecological data banks -- for example, to plan U.S. Forest Service timber sales in Washington state. And private developers use the computerized data to avoid costly clashes with environmentalists. The Florida program alone handles 1,400 information requests a year. And with the U.S. program rolling, the Conservancy already has begun to go global. It has established 10 similar programs in Central and South America, the latest in Brazil, as well as one in Canada. "Our dream -- and it's already happening -- is to create an international data bank of natural resources around the world," says Conservancy President Boren. That, he says, would provide "a true overview of the state of the Earth and its species." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Natural Heritage Program aims to inventory and preserve critical habitat for endangered species.
setting: state-private conservation effort in the context of endangered species protection
characters: Nature Conservancy [Hero]; Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; endangered species [Victim]
plot: The Nature Conservancy, through its Natural Heritage Program, is successfully inventorying and preserving critical habitats for endangered species, outperforming the federal Fish and Wildlife Service, which is criticized for its slow response and lack of effective recovery plans.
moral: The problem is the federal government's inadequate action in protecting endangered species, and the text suggests that state-private partnerships like the Natural Heritage Program are essential for effective conservation efforts.

Title: U.S. Agency Offers Scaled-Back Plan to Protect Owls
Text: The U.S. Interior Department submitted a plan that would classify 6.8 million acres of Pacific Northwest forest as prime spotted-owl territory, which likely will be subject to logging restrictions.    The final federal proposal, handed over to Judge Thomas Zilly in Seattle federal court, designates about a third less land as so-called "critical owl habitat" than some previous proposals, primarily because of administration concerns over the economic impact of prohibiting logging to save the owl. The owls are in danger of extinction because of heavy logging in the forests where they nest.    The government first proposed listing more than 11 million acres as prime owl habitat, and reduced that to 8.2 million last fall. Environmentalists criticized the government for further scaling back the plan to the 6.8 million acres included in the final version, even as logging groups were still calling the final proposal economically devastating.    The spotted owl has become a symbol of environmentalists' efforts to save the remnants of the nation's virgin forests, which have been reduced by an estimated 98% in the past two centuries. And the owl issue has become a battleground in the conflict between wildlife preservation and economic growth.    Listing land as critical owl habitat means that the acreage will likely be subject to some logging restrictions, which can range from modest limits on how many trees can be cut to a total logging ban. Another Interior Department team is working on a so-called recovery plan, which will contain additional details of how the logging limits will shake out.    That plan is expected to be complete in February, but it won't put the matter to rest. The Forest Service is working on a separate owl plan that must be submitted to a federal court in Seattle, which is also hearing a separate owl-related case.    Yet another Interior Department task force, dubbed the "God Squad," is evaluating whether the government should waive protecting the owl under provisions of the Endangered Species Act because of the potential economic impact of doing so. And additional lawsuits, as well as new initiatives from Congress and the administration, are likely to keep the controversy bubbling throughout the year.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The U.S. Interior Department submitted a plan to classify 6.8 million acres of forest as critical habitat for the spotted owl.
setting: federal court case in Seattle regarding critical habitat designation
characters: U.S. Interior Department [Villain]; spotted owl [Victim]; environmentalists [Hero]; logging groups [Villain]
plot: The U.S. Interior Department submitted a plan to designate 6.8 million acres as critical habitat for the spotted owl, reducing the area from previous proposals due to economic concerns. This decision is criticized by environmentalists who advocate for more extensive protections, while logging groups argue the restrictions are economically devastating. The spotted owl is endangered due to logging activities in its habitat.
moral: The problem is the endangered status of the spotted owl due to logging, and the text suggests that more extensive habitat protections should be implemented to ensure the owl's survival.

Title: The Morning Brief: A Different Angst In the Spring of '08
Text: The Wall Street Journal Online The Morning Brief, a look at the day's biggest news, is emailed to subscribers by 7 a.m. every business day. Sign up for the e-mail here. War, inflation and economic decay; costly gas and food throwing unwanted attention on Big Oil and Big Agriculture; and the looming 40th anniversary of one of the great protest periods of the last century could make for an awfully anti-establishment trend in the month of May. Tomorrow's traditional May Day marches in France will carry the added impetus of unions trying to build toward a much bigger day of demonstrations later in the month against the government's plans for tougher retirement rules, as Le Monde reports. (Among other measures, the French government wants to lengthen the required amount of time worked to earn a full pension.) The French unions' bid for popular support may be helped by the growing economic anxiety in France, where consumer confidence hit a 21-year-low this month according to the statistical agency Insee, as the Financial Times reports. The French aren't alone in their pessimism. Consumer confidence in the U.S. fell again this month after a sharp drop in March, according to the Conference Board's monthly index, which shows an increasingly bleak view of the current situation. "This continued weakening suggests that not only has the feeble level of growth in the first quarter spilled over into the second quarter, but that economic conditions may have slowed even further," said Conference Board consumer-research chief Lynn Franco, who added that job conditions and gas prices are heightening concerns and compared inflation expectations to the all-time high that followed Hurricane Katrina. Gas prices in particular are making headlines, especially as politicians this election year line up to propose remedies for a problem that voters feel daily at the pump. These include some presidential candidates' notion of suspending gas taxes and the mounting support in Congress to halt the purchase of oil for the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. As The Wall Street Journal reports, 16 Republican senators -- including John McCain -- have joined Democrats in backing the measure, despite opposition from President Bush. This has made it all the more inconvenient for the big oil companies to be reporting big profits this earnings season. BP and Royal Dutch Shell yesterday posted results that beat analysts' expectations, as the FT reports, while Exxon Mobil and Chevron are expected to describe robust quarterly profits later this week, the Journal adds. Journal columnist George Anders notes oil companies have learned a lot about dealing with public indignation since the public ire over gas prices of the 1970s and now "all that knowledge may be put to the test." Another 1970s target of public ire again in the headlines, he points out, is Big Agriculture, with food prices soaring in the U.S. and around the world and blamed in part on the transition of corn and other commodities from food production to lucrative biofuel. Grain- processing giant Archer-Daniels-Midland yesterday reported a 42% year- to-year increase in quarterly profit, joining the likes of Monsanto, Deere and others that had similar windfalls, the Journal reports. The farming industry is also under fire at a time when food producers in general have been hit with an unprecedented skein of safety recalls. And one new study finds agriculture companies have exerted "significant influence" over research that affects regulation of their activities, the Journal reports. Potentially fueling suspicions about the food companies, the European Commission yesterday said only about two-thirds of the increase in nutritional prices on the Continent are due to the higher cost of ingredients like wheat, milk and cooking fat, as the FT reports. "Energy, transport and labor costs have risen. But it is possible that somewhere along the food chain someone may be doing well out of this," the Commission said. By definition, for-profit corporations try to earn as much as they can when demand for their products goes up, but when those products play a key role in the daily lives of most people, politics enters the fray. Driving and eating costs were among the issues forming the backdrop for Mr. Bush's news conference yesterday, where he acknowledged Americans are concerned about food prices and explained "there is no magic wand to wave right now" that would lower gas prices. While Mr. Bush faces no more elections himself, politicians who do face voters in November are undoubtedly keeping a close eye on surveys like a new Wall Street Journal/NBC poll that indicates 73% of U.S. citizens disapprove of his management of the country's economic policy. Amid the anxiety and economic concern that currently seethe from Paris to Washington, it is interesting to note that both France and the U.S. are starting to commemorate the outburst of angst in the streets of both countries in the spring of 1968. But those were eruptions of anger against authority, and against a war, and the youth in neither country looks ready for such revolution today. --- Time Warner Announces Cable Spin-Off Time Warner this morning announced its long-anticipated plan to spin off Time Warner Cable by ceding its 84% stake to shareholders. "We've decided that a complete structural separation of Time Warner Cable, under the right circumstances, is in the best interests of both companies' shareholders," Chief Executive Jeff Bewkes said in a statement, adding that the parent company is "working hard" on a deal expected to be finalized "soon." The media-and-entertainment conglomerate also reported a 36% drop in first-quarter net income. --- Also of Note . . . New York Times: A New York state appeals court unanimously ruled that the Port Authority was liable for damages caused by the 1993 World Trade Center bombing, because it knew about but chose to ignore "an extreme and potentially catastrophic vulnerability that would have been open and obvious to any terrorist who cared to investigate and exploit it." The ruling upheld a jury's verdict that the agency was 68% liable for the bombing and the terrorists 32% liable, and can thus be forced to pay all the damages to injured survivors and to relatives of those killed. Nikkei: In a unanimous vote, the Bank of Japan decided to leave its overnight lending-rate target unchanged at 0.5%, apparently concluding it needs to closely watch developments at a time when the global economic outlook is becoming increasingly uncertain due to growing concerns over a possible recession in the U.S. and rising crude oil prices. BBC: Russia warned it will retaliate if Georgia uses force against breakaway regions, with Moscow accusing Tbilisi of preparing to invade Abkhazia and using that as a justification for boosting Russian forces there and in the neighboring Russian region of South Ossetia. Georgia reacted angrily, calling the move "irresponsible," and the European Union said it would be unwise for Russia to increase troop numbers there given the current tensions. Times of London: South Africa, joined by China, Russia, Libya and Vietnam, led efforts to block the dispatch of a United Nations envoy to Zimbabwe as the U.N. Security Council met to discuss the African country's election stand-off. Diplomats said that South African opposition to a U.N. mission meant that the next step would probably be a public meeting of the 15-nation Security Council on Zimbabwe under Britain's presidency in May. San Francisco Chronicle: A federal judge ruled the Interior Department has violated a legal deadline for deciding whether to list the polar bear as an endangered species because of global warming and must issue a decision on the matter by May 15, putting species protection on a collision course with the Bush administration's push for oil drilling off Alaska. In her decision, U.S. District Judge Claudia Wilken said oil industry operations in Alaska's Chukchi Sea, where one-fifth of all polar bears live, "could jeopardize the continued existence of the species" unless it is protected under the Endangered Species Act. Associated Press: A bill introduced by Senate Democrats seeks to ban manufacturers from using a potentially unsafe chemical common in baby bottles, toys and other children's products. A government report recently concluded that the chemical bisphenol A, or BPA -- used in shatterproof plastic water bottles, canned food linings and hundreds of other common items -- could pose a risk to infants and children. Washington Post: Sen. John McCain rejected calls by his Democratic opponents for universal health coverage, instead offering a market- based solution with an approach similar to a proposal put forth by President Bush last year. Mr. McCain's belief in the power of the free market to meet the nation's health-care needs sets up a stark choice for voters this fall in terms of the care they could receive, the role the government would play and the importance they place on the issue, since Democratic Sens. Barack Obama and Hillary Clinton have vowed government action to fulfill what they cast as a moral right for Americans to have health insurance. Reuters: Greece formally signed a deal to join Russian gas-export monopoly Gazprom's $15.58 billion South Stream gas pipeline, which will be jointly built with Eni of Italy, in the latest blow to the rival Nabucco pipeline backed by the U.S. and European Union. Financial Times: Chancellor of the Exchequer Alistair Darling launched a review of the competitiveness of the U.K.'s tax system, as a leading accountant warned a "bandwagon effect" could create a flood of companies fleeing the country for tax purposes. Bloomberg: BG Group, the U.K. company planning a liquefied natural gas project in Australia, made an unsolicited offer to buy Sydney- based Origin Energy for $12 billion, as it seeks to boost sales of the fuel into Asia. Wall Street Journal: Microsoft is expected to make its next move in the three-month-old takeover standoff with Yahoo as early as today, since the two sides have failed to reach any negotiated acquisition deal. One option Microsoft has weighed is nominating a proxy slate of directors to replace Yahoo's board, while waiting to put any acquisition offer directly to Yahoo shareholders, say people familiar with the matter. Women's Wear Daily: Lord & Taylor is joining the growing ranks of U.S. retailers looking to international expansion -- eying opportunities in Asia and elsewhere in North America -- even as it seeks to beef up existing stores with better merchandising and renovations. Variety: In a development that will amp up strike fears, hopes for a Screen Actors Guild deal with the major studios and television networks this week have nearly vanished. The entertainment conglomerates have been feeling increasingly frustrated over SAG's refusal to budge significantly from its initial proposals -- particularly after the companies asked for an extra week to close the "significant gaps" in positions before turning to rival union AFTRA's prime-time deal for two weeks of bargaining set to start on Monday. Nature: Recovery of the ozone hole above Antarctica could warm the Antarctic and cause more ice to melt in coming decades, researchers say. As the ozone hole heals, wind patterns that shield the interior of the polar region from warm air may break down, causing warming in the Antarctica as well as warmer and drier conditions in Australia. --- Quote of the Day "I am outraged by the comments that were made and saddened over the spectacle that we saw yesterday. You know, I have been a member of Trinity United Church of Christ since 1992. I have known Reverend Wright for almost 20 years. The person I saw yesterday was not the person that I met 20 years ago. His comments were not only divisive and destructive, but I believe that they end up giving comfort to those who prey on hate and I believe that they do not portray accurately the perspective of the black church. They certainly don't portray accurately my values and beliefs. And if Reverend Wright thinks that that's political posturing, as he put it, then he doesn't know me very well. And based on his remarks yesterday, well, I may not know him as well as I thought either," Barack Obama said yesterday, in denouncing Jeremiah Wright Jr. and the controversial remarks of his former pastor. --- Some links in this column are to sites that require a subscription or registration.
event_description: A federal judge ruled that the Interior Department must decide whether to list the polar bear as an endangered species due to global warming.
setting: federal court case
characters: U.S. District Judge Claudia Wilken [Hero]; Interior Department [Villain]; polar bear [Victim]
plot: A federal judge ruled that the Interior Department must decide whether to list the polar bear as an endangered species due to global warming, as the oil industry operations in Alaska could jeopardize the species' existence.
moral: The problem is the threat to the polar bear's existence due to global warming and oil drilling; the text suggests that the Interior Department must act to protect the species by listing it as endangered.

Title: U.S. Agency Hit by Crossfire In Salmon War
Text: One month after the Puget Sound wild Chinook joined the list of threatened species, the long-awaited salmon war is raging. Major elements of Gov. Gary Locke's salmon-restoration plan proved too controversial to even make it to the floor before the Legislature adjourned its regular session Sunday. The National Marine Fisheries Service criticized the plan as vague and inadequate. And the fisheries service itself is under attack, accused by a new coalition of landowner interests of so poorly managing the Northwest's salmon resources that it essentially broke the law when it put the Puget Sound run on the list. The Seattle-based coalition -- called Common Sense Salmon Recovery -- last week notified the fisheries service that it intends to file a lawsuit against the agency in federal court in Washington, D.C., charging that the March 16 Chinook salmon listings that have caused such angst in the state were unnecessary and should be withdrawn. "If NMFS had done its job, we wouldn't be in the position we are today," says Dean Boyer, a spokesman for the Washington Farm Bureau, a coalition member. The other members are the Building Industry Association of Washington, the Washington Association of Realtors and the Washington Cattlemen's Association. The fisheries service rejects the coalition's contentions. "I have a high degree of confidence in the quality of the science we've used in making this decision to list," says Will Stelle, the fisheries services regional administrator in Seattle. "Anyone who is challenging that risks spending money on lawyers when the money could be better spent elsewhere." Environmentalists say the planned lawsuit is just a stalling tactic by the landowners and developers. "The battle has been joined," says Chris Zimmer, spokesman for Save Our Wild Salmon, a Seattle conservation group. The coalition's talk of a lawsuit "looks like an effort to pass the buck . . . and avoid some of the serious habitat problems that have been created by these very industries." "We're not saying NMFS has done a perfect job out here" he adds, "but part of that has been because many of these industries are so resistant to reforming their land-use practices. It's like pulling teeth." The proposed lawsuit could hardly make the salmon turmoil any messier in Olympia, where the centerpiece of Gov. Locke's recovery plan -- an overhaul of the state's water laws that would restrict use -- unraveled during the last week of the legislative session. Unveiled earlier this year under the title "Extinction is Not an Option," the plan might be facing extinction itself. Tim Ceis, a former budget adviser to Gov. Locke and now head of the King County Endangered Species Policy Office, was summoned to Olympia last month to try to propel talks on the water measure. After two weeks, he gave up. "It's like a dying tree," he says of the process of negotiating with disparate interest groups. "You cut off one limb, and then another, and soon all you have left is a stump." The Legislature never got around to debating the water measure's merits. Nor did it consider another of the plan's elements, a deal to offer a tax break to timber companies in exchange for their limiting logging in forests near salmon spawning waters. The timber provision could be resurrected in a special legislative session scheduled to begin May 10, and it has the backing of fisheries service. But the federal agency isn't enthusiastic about the current overall plan, which is due to be revised this summer. In an April 12 letter to Gov. Locke, Mr. Stelle said the strategy for restoring salmon runs is so lacking in detail that the agency can't possibly determine whether it would work. In addition, the letter said, the plan's focus on voluntary water conservation efforts in and around salmon habitats is inadequate because it doesn't suggest what might be done if voluntary efforts fail. "There's a good amount of work that needs to get done," Mr. Stelle says in an interview. If it isn't done soon, his agency may soon step in. Under the Endangered Species Act, if a state doesn't design an acceptable recovery plan for its threatened and endangered fish within 90 days of their listings, the federal government must step in to prescribe one. The landowner coalition says that would be like letting the fox into the chicken coop. "Remember the story of Nero, the emperor who fiddled while Rome burned? Well, NMFS is fiddling and spending millions of dollars on studies of fish while the runs have declined," says Jim Johnson, an Olympia lawyer working for the coalition. "But at least Nero's fiddling did not accelerate the fire. NMFS's fiddling not only substantially contributed to the decline of the runs, but also cost millions, which could've been better spent for fish." Coalition members contend that the fisheries service over the years violated several federal laws, including the Endangered Species Act, and failed to regulate commercial and tribal salmon fishing in ocean waters rigorously enough while also not controlling the activities of such salmon predators as seals and sea lions. In addition, they say, the agency failed to count hatchery-produced salmon when it made the calculations that resulted in the determination that certain runs were in danger of extinction. In the end, the coalition contends, the agency's own failures forced it to make the listings, which pressured the Locke administration to come up with a strategy focusing on habitat restoration -- something that most observers agree would create a regulatory nightmare for the agricultural and development interests in the coalition. Restricting development and water use near salmon habitats, as Gov. Locke has proposed, would "make an already expensive housing market skyrocket," says Bob Camp, a Lakewood-based home builder and member of the trade groups' salmon task force. "And we're not talking hundreds of dollars here, were talking many thousands." The coalition wants the NMFS to withdraw the listings, and focus its efforts on conserving wild salmon in ocean waters by, among other things, restricting harvests by commercial and tribal fishermen. That, says Mr. Boyer of the Farm Bureau, could lead to a more balanced approach to salvaging salmon that would be "would be less onerous, less costly and less disruptive to people's lives and less disruptive to the Washington economy." People who harvest wild salmon for a living disagree. Rob Zuanich, executive director of the Seattle-based Purse Seine Vessel Owners Association, a trade group representing some 400 fishing concerns, says that "on a short-term basis, there may be an argument" for limiting commercial and tribal harvests. "But to think that that alone will make the difference is wrong," he says. "What's pretty clear from most of the reputable science out there is that even if you restricted all forms of harvest, some of these salmon runs still wouldn't rebuild. Existing habitat and water flow just isn't there to sustain them." Tony Meyer, a spokesman for the Northwest Indian Fisheries Commission, notes that tribal harvest has been dramatically reduced over the last decade and "that hasn't fixed the problem." Most observers agree that the state will be grappling with the salmon issue for years to come. "The idea that we were going to waltz in and resolve this with some happy talk wasn't realistic," says Todd True, a lawyer with Earth Justice Legal Defense in Seattle. "This problem is not going to go away in a hurry." Credit: Staff Reporters of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A coalition intends to file a lawsuit against the National Marine Fisheries Service to challenge the recent listings of Puget Sound Chinook salmon as threatened.
setting: federal court case regarding endangered species listings
characters: National Marine Fisheries Service [Villain]; Common Sense Salmon Recovery coalition [Hero]; Puget Sound Chinook salmon [Victim]
plot: The Common Sense Salmon Recovery coalition intends to file a lawsuit against the National Marine Fisheries Service, accusing it of poor management and illegal actions regarding the listing of Puget Sound Chinook salmon as threatened, which has led to significant controversy and legislative inaction.
moral: The National Marine Fisheries Service should withdraw the listings of Puget Sound Chinook salmon and focus on more effective conservation strategies that do not disrupt local economies.

Title: Mr. Ziff Lands in Middle of Shrub Scandal
Text: CHILMARK, Mass. -- You can't even pick up stones on the beach here. Posted signs say it is a crime. So imagine the uproar on Martha's Vineyard when huckleberry bushes and viburnum shrubs vanished from a nature preserve last spring. Dozens of trees were uprooted from a sanctuary in a nearby town, too. The plants, it turns out, had been carted off to the 12-acre West Tisbury estate of Dirk Ziff, a New York investor and heir to a publishing fortune. The shrubbery's journey sparked an investigation by the state, which determined that the guardians of the preserves -- not Mr. Ziff -- were to blame. The guardians, the Sheriff's Meadow Foundation, had barter arrangements with landscapers and in this case failed to get permission from the state to move the plants. That violated state laws designed to protect the habitats of rare and endangered species. A settlement is in the works between the foundation and the state to repair the damage. Mr. Ziff, 44 years old, says he didn't know the plants were coming from the preserve until after the project was under way, according to people familiar with his thinking. When he found out, he was assured by his landscaper that the removal of the plants was helping conservation efforts. The situation has brought uncomfortable attention to the publicity- shy Mr. Ziff, whose net worth was estimated by Forbes magazine this month at $3.7 billion. He and his two brothers founded Ziff Brothers Investments in the 1990s, after the $1.4 billion sale of their family's Ziff-Davis publishing empire. They invested in various public and private-equity ventures, including hedge funds. The family fortune was estimated by Forbes at more than $10 billion. Mr. Ziff is known as a low-key philanthropist, who on Martha's Vineyard has contributed to a hospital, YMCA, museums -- and a modest amount to the Sheriff's Meadow Foundation. He is a vice chairman of New York City public broadcaster Channel 13/WNET and of the Robin Hood Foundation, a large New York philanthropy, and has been a major Democratic donor. "It's so stunning this could happen," says Sally Lasker, who lives on 235 acres next to the Ziff property. Some families of property donors complained to Sheriff's Meadow. "They wanted this to be preserved," Ms. Lasker says. "That's why they call them preserves." The fuss is an embarrassment for the Sheriff's Meadow Foundation. Founded in 1959 by the late Henry Beetle Hough, editor of the Vineyard Gazette, it oversees about 2,637 acres of conservation land, much of it donated by private landowners. For years, Sheriff's Meadow has made loose arrangements with landscapers to help care for its properties and get rid of trees and shrubs to maintain the balance of native habitats. It holds an annual "Free Tree Day," inviting the public to dig up young cedars. It has long bartered with contractors to take certain greenery in exchange for services, such as mowing. That is what it did in this case. Now, with a new director in place, the foundation's policy has been changed. Trudging through Chilmark's 61-acre Priscilla Hancock Meadow, which the foundation oversees, the new director, Adam Moore, explains that the group had been aiming to nurture native grasslands. Mr. Moore stops at a patch of bare sand where the huckleberry bushes used to be. Also missing, Mr. Moore says, is some Nantucket shadbush, a rare tree. At the 154-acre Caroline Tuthill Preserve in Edgartown, from which trees were removed, the foundation was trying to restore an open meadow. Mr. Ziff wanted to devote an area of his property strictly to native plants, re-creating what the vineyard was like before it was inhabited, according to the people close to him. It is a trend gaining momentum on Martha's Vineyard. Planning officials encourage that because the plantings prevent erosion, form habitats for wildlife and migratory birds and require less watering. But an official list of plants warns, "As a general rule, do not transplant from natural settings. Native plants are increasingly available in nurseries." John Hoff, Mr. Ziff's landscaper, and the foundation made an informal deal. Then he began removing the plants with the foundation's consent. In all, 33 trees, mostly pitch pines and some cedars, were removed from the Caroline Tuthill preserve, using huge machines that damaged the property, Mr. Moore says. At Priscilla Hancock, the shrub removal left some holes on about two acres, according to Mr. Moore. Mr. Hoff didn't return calls seeking comment. Mr. Hoff had assured Mr. Ziff that this was a "win-win," people familiar with the situation say. If he hadn't taken the trees, they would have been cut down. But the preserves, as well as part of the Ziff property, are designated "priority habitat," homes to rare species, including birds, moths and plants. Any work that may disturb the habitat, from construction to removing plants, must be approved by a state agency that enforces the Massachusetts Endangered Species Act. "Priority habitat" applies to about three-quarters of Martha's Vineyard, an 87-square-mile island off Cape Cod. The foundation failed to get permission and didn't adequately oversee the work, Mr. Moore says. "We are sorry for the distress that this has caused," Sheriff's Meadow vice president Emily Bramhall wrote to "the island community" in a letter posted on the group's Web site. The Martha's Vineyard flap came to light in the spring, when an ecologist at National Heritage, the state agency, got a tip. Mr. Hoff wasn't implicated in any wrongdoing. Nor was Mr. Ziff, though the state has a separate inquiry into whether construction work done on the Ziff property violated environmental rules.
event_description: Investigation into unauthorized removal of plants from a nature preserve.
setting: investigation by the state regarding habitat violation in a nature preserve
characters: Sheriff's Meadow Foundation [Villain]; Dirk Ziff [Victim]; the plants and trees [Victim]; the state agency [Hero]
plot: Unauthorized removal of plants from a nature preserve led to an investigation by the state, which found the Sheriff's Meadow Foundation responsible for the habitat violation, while Dirk Ziff was misled about the legality of the plant removal.
moral: The Sheriff's Meadow Foundation must improve its oversight and obtain necessary permissions to prevent future habitat violations, ensuring the protection of endangered species and their habitats.

Title: U.S. News: Group Backs Waxman for Panel
Text: WASHINGTON -- California Rep. Henry Waxman won backing from a key group of Democrats in his bid to unseat Michigan Rep. John Dingell as chairman of the powerful House Energy and Commerce Committee. At stake is the direction of crucial legislation during a period of solid Democratic control of Congress. The committee's mandate is broad, with oversight of everything from climate change to health care to telecommunications. The Democrats' Steering and Policy Committee, which helps allocate committee memberships and chairmanships, voted 25-22 Wednesday to nominate Mr. Waxman for the post. The full House Democratic membership will decide Thursday whether to heed the panel's recommendation. The starkest difference between the men may concern so-called greenhouse gases, which trap the sun's heat in the atmosphere and contribute to global warming. Mr. Waxman favors faster reductions of such emissions than does Mr. Dingell, who has been one of the auto industry's staunchest allies in Congress. His ouster from the chairmanship would be a major setback for Detroit's auto makers at a time when they are seeking assistance from Washington. Mr. Waxman's strong showing was striking given Congress's longstanding deference to seniority. Mr. Dingell, 82 years old, is the second-longest serving House member in history, having been elected 53 years ago, and he has either been chairman of the committee or its top Democrat since 1981. Those supporting Mr. Waxman, 69, say his views are more in line with those of President-elect Barack Obama. Mr. Dingell is in some ways more conservative than many in his party, and some Democrats argue that leaving him in charge of a key panel could squander a historic opportunity. Supporters of Mr. Dingell argue that his record is unmatched, and that he has helped push through such laws as the Clean Air Act and Endangered Species Act. House Speaker Nancy Pelosi has maintained a public neutrality, but her views are more aligned with those of Mr. Waxman and she has had run-ins with Mr. Dingell in the past. --- Stephen Power contributed to this article. Credit: By Naftali Bendavid
event_description: Discussion of support for the Endangered Species Act.
setting: House Energy and Commerce Committee leadership election
characters: Rep. Henry Waxman [Hero]; Rep. John Dingell [Villain]; Detroit's auto makers [Victim]
plot: Rep. Henry Waxman seeks to unseat Rep. John Dingell as chairman of the House Energy and Commerce Committee, which could lead to faster reductions in greenhouse gas emissions, impacting Detroit's auto makers who rely on Dingell's support.
moral: The problem is the potential stagnation of progressive environmental legislation under Dingell's leadership; the text suggests that Waxman should be elected to promote faster action on climate change.

Title: Conservationists Fight Feds Over Damaged Pine Trees
Text: Thousands of pine trees in Alabama, downed or damaged by Hurricane Opal, are the object of a tug-of-war between the U.S. Forest Service and environmentalists. Because of delays caused by the dispute, both the timber industry and the south Alabama counties encompassing Conecuh National Forest could lose a windfall from the sale of salvaged trees. The reason: The wood has already begun to rot, says Deborah Baker, director of the Southern Timber Purchasers Council in Atlanta, a trade group.
