By the time the timber is finally harvested, she says, "it will be devalued to the point of nothing." Indeed, once the wood does rot, it can be used only as pulpwood, which currently sells for about $8 a ton. Untainted pine, which can be used for plywood, sells for as much as $50 a ton. Seeking to sell the fallen timber quickly, the Forest Service's Montgomery office turned to a new law designed to speed logging of dead and dying trees on federal lands. The measure, enacted in November, suspends environmental laws, including the Clean Water Act and the Endangered Species Act, so that trees posing a safety or fire hazard can be harvested without the lengthy and costly public hearings required by those laws. Other states with federal forests harmed by Opal also have invoked the new salvage-timber law. In Georgia, for example, Chattahoochee National Forest was hit, but "only dead and downed trees along roadways are being cut," says Rene Voss of the state's Sierra Club. In Alabama, however, trees on federal lands remain untouched. While environmentalists want some of the damaged trees to remain, the timber industry wants to remove as many as possible. Federal officials estimate that the timber salvaged from Southeastern forests damaged by Opal will generate about $10 million. The timber industry estimates that it, too, will reap millions. Because a glut of timber harvested from private lands has halved prices, timber firms plan to store the salvaged wood until prices rise. The small Alabama counties that encompass federal forest lands also have a lot at stake; they are entitled to 25% of the revenue of timber harvested there. Covington County is hoping for as much as $1.5 million, to be used for roads and schools. The delay is a result of the first major challenge to the federal salvage-timber legislation. The Biodiversity Legal Foundation, an environmental lobbying group in Boulder, Colo., has filed a lawsuit in U.S. District Court in Montgomery, accusing the Forest Service of abusing the measure. The environmental group claims the Forest Service, under pressure from the timber industry, is using the law as an excuse to circumvent laws that normally govern the cutting of pine. The Alabama case is one of the nation's "worst instances of abuse," says Jasper Carlton, director of the foundation. The foundation is fighting the proposed salvage operation on grounds the law is unconstitutional. "Environmental laws like the Clean Water Act were created to protect our rights and our public lands," says Ray Vaughan, an attorney for the plaintiffs. "This [measure] strips us of those laws without due process. It leaves logging to the unbridled discretion of the Forest Service. As long as the word `salvage' is uttered, they can knock down any trees they want." This misuse of the salvage law, environmentalists say, is being promoted by the timber industry for its own financial gain. "These sales are about benefiting the industry in the name of salvage, and do not have anything to do with forest health or safety, which was supposed to be the intention of the bill," says Mr. Vaughan. But timber-industry officials call the suit a frivolous attempt to stop logging in national forests. "The people filing the suit want the land to be a wilderness area and not a working forest," says Ms. Baker of the timber trade group. For its part, the Forest Service won't comment on pending litigation. But since complaints surfaced, it has scaled back the salvage operation: The plan now calls for fewer than 20,000 acres to be harvested, down from 40,000. That's still too much, says Mark Bailey, a zoologist for the Alabama Natural Heritage Program, a private group seeking to protect forests in the state. "There is a real opportunity to restore the forest to a more natural state," he says. "We're not saying don't salvage. We're just saying that it needs to be scaled back some." (See related letter: "SOUTHEAST JOURNAL --- Letters to the Editor: Objectivity on Timber, Not Advocacy" -- WSJ Jan. 24, 1996) (See related letter: "SOUTHEAST JOURNAL --- Letters to the Editor: Another View of the Pines" -- WSJ March 13, 1996) Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Suspension of environmental laws, including the Endangered Species Act, to expedite timber harvesting.
setting: U.S. District Court in Montgomery, Alabama, regarding the federal salvage-timber legislation
characters: U.S. Forest Service [Villain]; environmentalists [Hero]; timber industry [Villain]; small Alabama counties [Victim]; Biodiversity Legal Foundation [Hero]; Ray Vaughan [Hero]; Mark Bailey [Hero]
plot: The U.S. Forest Service seeks to expedite timber harvesting by invoking a new law that suspends environmental protections, leading to a lawsuit from environmentalists who argue this circumvents necessary regulations. The timber industry supports the law for financial gain, while small Alabama counties stand to lose revenue due to delays in harvesting.
moral: The problem is the misuse of the salvage law by the Forest Service and timber industry, which threatens environmental protections and public rights. The text suggests that the salvage operation should be scaled back to ensure forest health and compliance with environmental laws.

Title: Golf Course Becomes Test Of Wash. Salmon Policy
Text: Willows Run, a Scottish-style golf course nestled beside the Sammamish River in Redmond, Wash., likes to think of itself as green in more ways than one. Its Web site touts the course as proof that environmentally sensitive acreage and duffers "can comfortably coexist." Well, maybe. But the Washington Department of Ecology suspects that the Willows Run irrigation system might not be legal, and environmentalists are convinced that the watering of the course imperils a run of Chinook salmon listed under the U.S. Endangered Species Act. Now the DOE is sparring with the course's owners -- high-tech billionaire Paul Allen and his brother-in-law, Brian Patton -- and environmentalists have seized on the quarrel as a test of the state's commitment to saving salmon. "This is war," says Rob Caldwell, executive director of the nonprofit Center for Environmental Law & Policy in Seattle. "Taking water from the river that they are not entitled to has a direct and immediate effect on salmon. It's outrageous." Jason Hunke, a spokesman for Mr. Allen, says the irrigation is lawful. "No one wants to pull river water, but it's a right that's valid and we have a business to run," he says. "Here you have a businessman who is trying to be strategic and environmentally sensitive, and a department with limited resources. It's an unfortunate situation, but we're between a rock and a hard place." DOE officials aren't so sure Willows Run is acting inside the law. They've asked Willows Run to provide evidence by Monday that it's not breaking Washington's water-rights statute. The roots of the squabble can be traced to August 1993, when Willows Run, which opened in 1994, applied to the DOE for permission to convert surface-water rights to groundwater rights, and expand those groundwater rights. The application put Willows Run at the end of a long queue for rights conversions and permits, and by the summer of 1998, the course's owners were still waiting for an answer. In a June 1998 letter to the agency, Willows Run's engineer, J. David Benson, threatened to start drawing water from the Sammamish if its application wasn't acted on soon. The letter noted that the course owners knew that taking such a step would be risky. "We feel this may have an adverse effect on fish within the Sammamish River," it said. Three months later, department officials documented that the course was in fact being watered, possibly in violation of state law. But the agency didn't move to enforce the law, in large part because it didn't have the staff: Spending cuts enacted by the Legislature in 1994 had slashed about 35 positions from the department. Then, in their most recent session, lawmakers approved funding for four new, full-time DOE workers to concentrate on water-use compliance. Meanwhile, the federal government in March listed runs of salmon in Washington as threatened and endangered. And state surveys of the Sammamish, an important migration corridor for Puget Sound wild Chinook, suggested that low flows, and the resulting warmer water, were killing fish. "It's certainly a watershed of interest," says Dan Swenson, manager of the DOE's Northwest regional office in Bellevue. So, with its beefed-up compliance staff, the agency earlier this summer conducted a field survey of Willows Run, during which it was noted that a portable pump was withdrawing water from the Sammamish. "It is still unclear to us how and under what authority you obtain your irrigation water," said a letter posted last month to Mr. Patton, the co-owner, from Buck Smith, a senior environmental specialist with the agency's water-resources program in Bellevue. The letter asked Mr. Patton to show by Aug. 9 that the irrigation is legal. Mr. Patton asked for and was granted an extension until Monday. The first question before the DOE is whether Willows Run even has valid surface-water rights to convert. The rights, the course claims, were granted to the site's previous owners in the 1950s. The DOE says that under Washington law, if rights aren't exercised for five years they revert to the state. And according to the recent letter to Mr. Patton, the agency has "field notes, land photos, aerial photos and {staff} knowledge" indicating that the rights weren't "beneficially exercised until recently for a period of more than five years." If Willows Run doesn't prove to the DOE's satisfaction that it is removing irrigation water from the Sammamish under a valid water-rights permit, the agency can issue what is known as a relinquishment order, taking the rights away. That can be appealed to the state Pollution Control Hearings Board. If the board upholds the order, Messrs. Patton and Allen can appeal to superior court. Willows Run doesn't necessarily dispute that the rights went unused for quite some time, but back in 1992, one of its lawyers contended that that didn't matter. In a letter to an assistant attorney general that year -- when the sale of the property was being negotiated -- Larry Martin, a lawyer then representing the prospective owners, said there was concern that the DOE "might attempt to forfeit" the rights under the five-year rule. The lawyer argued that the 1967 state law establishing the relinquishment rule exempted water rights granted before 1967. Therefore, the letter concluded, "the water rights are not subject to forfeiture under this statute." Mr. Hunke, the spokesman for Mr. Allen, says Willows Run owners still believe the surface-water right is valid because it hasn't been relinquished. "We believe it is an unrelinquishable right because it predated the 1967 law," he says. "We're very confident that the forfeiture statute doesn't apply to us, and the reason it doesn't is the time frame in which it was granted." DOE spokeswoman Mary Getchell says agency officials are not considering the 1992 letter in assessing the golf-course application, but instead are awaiting a new response from the golf course. She says the department's rules are straightforward: "If a water user hasn't used the water rights for five years for beneficial use, the rights are relinquished." She says the department is also looking at whether Willows Run is using more water than permitted on a new course on the property. "There is an area at Willows Run which all of a sudden is being irrigated," Ms. Getchell says. "We have not received a permit request for that area." The dispute might be partially resolved through an agreement being negotiated between golf-course owners and the City of Redmond. Jim Streit, a Redmond utility engineer, says his agency is trying to craft a deal in which golf-course owners would pay $75,000 for a new water pipeline in addition to paying for water used to irrigate the course. But, Mr. Streit says, the water would be used to "supplement" other water sources, not for the entire 36 holes. Mr. Hunke says it's unclear if that deal will go through. Environmentalists are eager for the state to make an example of Willows Run. "I think the evidence will show the water right is no longer valid, and I congratulate the state for taking action to enforce this aberrant use of water," says Mr. Caldwell, the water-rights lawyer. But it's not certain that the state will take action. That, says the DOE's Mr. Swenson, will depend on what proof Willows Run provides. "The ball," he says, "is now in their court." Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Washington Department of Ecology is investigating the legality of the Willows Run golf course's irrigation system in relation to water rights and its impact on endangered Chinook salmon.
setting: investigation by the Washington Department of Ecology regarding water rights and environmental impact
characters: Washington Department of Ecology [Hero]; Willows Run golf course [Villain]; Chinook salmon [Victim]; environmentalists [Hero]
plot: The Washington Department of Ecology investigates the legality of Willows Run's irrigation system, suspecting it may harm endangered Chinook salmon. Environmentalists argue that the golf course's water usage is illegal and detrimental to the salmon population, while the course's owners claim their water rights are valid. The DOE is awaiting proof from Willows Run regarding their water rights, which could lead to enforcement actions if they are found to be in violation.
moral: The state must enforce water rights regulations to protect endangered Chinook salmon from illegal water usage by Willows Run, ensuring that environmental laws are upheld.

Title: No Big Overhaul Is Seen on Endangered Species Act
Text: WASHINGTON -- A top Interior Department official predicted Congress won't approve a major overhaul of the Endangered Species Act.    Some Western lawmakers have called for big changes in the law, and they had hoped for White House backing. But Mike Hayden, assistant secretary for fish and wildlife and parks, said the Bush administration wouldn't recommend such a course, and he expressed doubt that it would succeed. "There will be some people saying radical things, but I don't think they'll prevail," he told a group of reporters.    The law, under which the government can limit development to protect species in danger of becoming extinct, has provoked a number of fights between environmentalists and industry. Mr. Hayden said he does expect Congress to approve some "fine-tuning" of the law.    On other matters, Mr. Hayden said he opposed some proposals to toughen the government's policy on dealing with companies that hold concession contracts in national parks. He said a bill that Sen. Dale Bumpers (D., Ark.) is expected to introduce might restrict the rights of concessionaires so much that the most qualified companies wouldn't be interested in the job. But he praised provisions to raise the franchise fees paid to the government and to place revenue from concessions in a special account isolated from the congressional appropriations process.
event_description: Discussion of a major overhaul of the Endangered Species Act.
setting: congressional hearing
characters: Mike Hayden [Villain]; Western lawmakers [Hero]; environmentalists [Victim]; industry [Villain]
plot: Western lawmakers advocate for a major overhaul of the Endangered Species Act, hoping for White House support, but Mike Hayden predicts Congress will not approve such changes, indicating a struggle between environmentalists and industry.
moral: The problem is the lack of support for significant reforms to the Endangered Species Act, and the text suggests that Congress should consider some adjustments to the law to better protect endangered species.

Title: Oregon Spotted Owl's Fate Is to Be Decided Today
Text: WASHINGTON -- This could be Judgment Day for some spotted owls in Oregon.    The Endangered Species Committee -- the so-called God Squad -- is to decide today if the government should sell 44 Oregon land plots for logging, despite findings that logging there would further threaten the endangered spotted owls.    The committee is made up of the secretaries of interior, agriculture and the Army; an undersecretary of commerce; the Environmental Protection Agency administrator; the chairman of the White House Council of Economic Advisers, and a representative of Oregon.    It will take five votes to exempt the land, totaling 4,500 acres, from the Endangered Species Act.    Also today, Interior Secretary Manuel Lujan, chairman of the committee, will unveil a Bush administration proposal to salvage logging jobs in the Pacific Northwest region by lifting some protections for the endangered spotted owl.    Republican Sen. Slade Gorton of Washington is expected to introduce the proposal in Congress next week as an alternative to a more restrictive plan to be announced by a group of scientists and state officials in Portland today. This plan will not consider the economic effects of protecting the owl.
event_description: The Endangered Species Committee is deciding whether to exempt 44 Oregon land plots from the Endangered Species Act for logging purposes.
setting: a decision-making process by the Endangered Species Committee regarding logging exemptions
characters: spotted owls [Victim]; Endangered Species Committee [Villain]; Interior Secretary Manuel Lujan [Hero]; Republican Sen. Slade Gorton [Hero]; scientists and state officials [Hero]
plot: The Endangered Species Committee is deciding whether to exempt 44 Oregon land plots from the Endangered Species Act for logging, which threatens the spotted owls. The committee's decision could lead to the sale of land for logging, while a proposal by Secretary Lujan aims to salvage logging jobs by lifting protections for the owls.
moral: The problem is the threat to the endangered spotted owls due to logging, and the text suggests that the government should prioritize job preservation over species protection, as indicated by the proposals being introduced.

Title: U.S. Watch
Text: TEXAS Court Upholds 2013 Law Limiting Abortion Clinics A federal appeals court in New Orleans upheld a Texas law restricting abortion clinics on Thursday, a ruling that could lead to a Supreme Court review of how far states can go in regulating the procedure. Among other provisions, the 2013 Texas law requires physicians to have admitting privileges at a hospital within 30 miles of where an abortion is performed. The effect has been to shutter several clinics in the far-flung state because local hospitals either were located beyond the 30-mile limit or refused to grant such privileges to physicians who perform abortions. Planned Parenthood and other abortion-rights advocates challenged the statute, arguing it imposed an "undue burden" on women seeking abortions, a standard the Supreme Court has used since 1992 in evaluating abortion regulations. -- Jess Bravin --- TECHNOLOGY U.S. Seeks Easier Access To Warrants for Hacking The U.S. Department of Justice is pushing to make it easier for law enforcement to get warrants to hack into the computers of criminal suspects across the country. The move, which would alter federal court rules governing search warrants, comes amid increases in cases related to computer crimes. Investigators say they need more flexibility to get warrants, especially when multiple computers are involved or the government doesn't know where the computer is physically located. The Justice Department effort is raising questions among some technology advocates, who say the government should focus on fixing the holes in computer software that allow such hacking instead of exploiting them. -- Jennifer Valentino-DeVries --- ENDANGERED SPECIES Split View Over Listing Of Lesser Prairie-Chicken The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service said Thursday that it will list the lesser prairie-chicken as a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act. The decision to protect the grayish-brown bird, a type of grouse found in parts of Colorado, Kansas, New Mexico, Oklahoma and Texas, was met with opposition from the oil industry, which said it will complicate drilling, as well as from some environmentalists, who said it doesn't go far enough. The lesser prairie-chicken's listing as threatened is a lesser form of protection than an endangered species listing, but it means that property owners and others using land on which the chicken lives will have to take special precautions to protect its habitat. -- Ana Campoy
event_description: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service will list the lesser prairie-chicken as a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal agency decision-making under the Endangered Species Act
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; oil industry [Villain]; lesser prairie-chicken [Victim]; some environmentalists [Villain]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service lists the lesser prairie-chicken as a threatened species, which leads to opposition from the oil industry and some environmentalists, affecting property owners and land use.
moral: The lesser prairie-chicken needs stronger protections to ensure its survival, and the government should take more decisive action to safeguard its habitat.

Title: U.S. News: Wolf's Return is Big and, for Some, Bad
Text: Corrections & Amplifications Agents from the U.S. Agriculture Department's Wildlife Services program have shot wolves in Idaho near the Montana border. A U.S. News article on March 22 about a dispute over wolves incorrectly said the agents were from the Interior Department's U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. (WSJ Apr. 9, 2014) Back in the mid-1990s, nearly three dozen gray wolves captured in Canada were ferried south on planes, trucks and snowmobiles to the wilderness of Idaho, where wildlife biologists set them loose as part of a federal ecological experiment. Now, many in the state say they wish the creatures had never set foot on Idaho soil. More than 650 wolves roam the state today, according to low-end estimates by the Idaho Department of Fish and Game. The growth has stirred complaints from ranchers and hunters, who say the animals are wreaking havoc on Idaho's prized elk and livestock, and prompted the governor's office to embark on an effort to wipe out three-quarters or more of the population. The reintroduction of wolves has triggered controversy in recent years throughout the northern Rockies, where states such as Wyoming and Montana have struggled to rein in booming wolf populations. The issue has now moved to Idaho, where lawmakers Thursday approved an amended version of a bill championed by Gov. Butch Otter that would set up a statewide authority for wolf management and pump $400,000 in state money a year into a lethal campaign against the animals. But the idea for a so-called wolf depredation control board has sparked an outcry by conservationists, who say the state is gripped by a resurgence of anti-wolf sentiment not seen since the early 20th century, when the animal was hunted, trapped and poisoned to near-oblivion. "Idaho has a lot of wolves, and they seem to bring out all kinds of emotion in everybody," said Jerome Hansen, a supervisor for Idaho Fish and Game. In 1995 and 1996, federal biologists at Congress's direction shipped wolves from Alberta and British Columbia to central Idaho and Yellowstone National Park with the goal of restoring the ecological balance in a region teeming with elk and other creatures lower on the food chain. After the population surged, the U.S. in 2011 and 2012 stripped wolves in Idaho and several other states in the region of protection by the Endangered Species Act. The government also gave Idaho more responsibility -- but less money -- for managing the animals. State leaders aim to reduce the wolf population to 150 and 15 breeding pairs -- a threshold they say would avoid a potential relisting under the Endangered Species Act. To help pay for the effort, the governor wants to spend an additional $400,000 a year for more monitoring, hunting and trapping. The first lump of it was approved Thursday. Mr. Otter and legislators say the wolf is hurting the state economy by plundering livestock and driving away out-of-state elk hunters. "They're a destroying animal," said Sen. Monty Pearce, among several lawmakers who work in the ranching industry. The elk population has fallen about 15% since the wolves arrived, according to state estimates, while revenues from elk-hunting permits have plummeted. Wolves have also killed 2,589 sheep, 610 cows and 72 dogs in Idaho, according to federal tallies. Last summer, state Sen. Jeff Siddoway, who owns a sheep ranch in Jefferson County, lost 176 sheep during a single attack last summer. Most of animals died trampling over each other as they fled. Conservationists say the state is treating wolves like vermin, and the plan to cut the population to as low as 150 might endanger the species all over again. "They've demonized wolves and made it a politically beneficial tool to make people afraid of them," said Suzanne Stone, a wolf conservationist in Boise. Mr. Otter's office says the concerns are overblown. "We acknowledge that wolves are here to say," said spokesman Jon Hanian. "We are trying to manage this population for sustainability and to ensure they do the least possible harm to native wildlife as well as livestock." Already, the wolf population has fallen at least 20% after peaking in 2009, according to state data. At Idaho's request, U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service agents have shot dozens of wolves near the Montana border, where the predators have been blamed for a rash of elk deaths. Hunters have killed more than 300 wolves a year since 2011. Conservationists hope the courts will intervene. Lawyers at environmental group Earthjustice filed a federal suit in January to stop the state exterminating two wolf packs in the Frank Church-River of No Return Wilderness. The request, denied by a trial judge in January, is before the Ninth U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals. Credit: By Jacob Gershman
event_description: Idaho lawmakers approved a bill to establish a statewide authority for wolf management.
setting: Idaho's legislative context regarding wolf management and conservation efforts
characters: Gov. Butch Otter [Hero]; ranchers and hunters [Villain]; wolves [Victim]; conservationists [Hero]; Sen. Monty Pearce [Villain]; environmental group Earthjustice [Hero]
plot: Idaho lawmakers approved a bill to create a statewide authority for wolf management, aiming to reduce the wolf population due to complaints from ranchers and hunters about livestock losses, while conservationists oppose the plan, fearing it will endanger the species.
moral: The text suggests that the problem is the growing anti-wolf sentiment and the potential harm to the wolf population, advocating for a balanced approach to wolf management that considers ecological sustainability and the protection of the species.

Title: Northwest Lumber Mills Pin Hopes on Trump
Text: SKAMANIA COUNTY, Wash. -- This slice of southern Washington is home to some of the most lush and productive timberland in the world, carpeted by Douglas fir and hemlock from the slopes of Mount St. Helens to the Columbia River Gorge. The local lumber industry has shriveled nonetheless.Sawmills have been closing across the Pacific Northwest over the past 30 years. There is just one left in Skamania County, down from six during its logging heyday.Limited log supply from the region's national forests has cut off their raw material, while cheap lumber from Canada has taken market share for their finished product.Owners of the remaining mills have high hopes that President Trump will deliver relief by increasing logging in national forests and raising trade protections against Canadian exports. Although the latter can be achieved with a stroke of the president's pen, meaningfully boosting the federal timber harvest could take years and be impeded by litigation and red tape."We have all the trees we need," Trump said early in his term while threatening broad tariffs on Canadian trade.Regional Forest Service supervisors have been directed to map five-year plans to contribute to a 25% increase in the overall volume of timber harvested from national forests to satisfy Trump's call for greater wood production.Last month, the Agriculture Department began the process of rescinding its "roadless rule," which prohibits road building or reconstruction on roughly 59 million federal acres. That is just one barrier to logging. Others protect rivers, old-growth forests, and habitats for northern spotted owls and marbled murrelets.Trump's push to build up domestic production capacity of raw materials has the White House considering tariffs on lumber imports in the name of national security, as it has with steel and copper. Any import taxes Trump imposes would be on top of the duties that are already collected on lumber that is imported from Canada.The antidumping and countervailing duties on Canadian softwood lumber stem from a trade dispute that has been raging for decades. The combined duty rate on Canadian lumber is expected to more than double in the coming weeks to about 34% for most producers regardless of Trump's tariff decision."This administration is very supportive of our industry," said Devin Stockfish, chief executive of Seattle's Weyerhaeuser, the country's largest private landowner and most prolific lumber producer. "If you go back in time, you very rarely heard a president talk about lumber and timber as much as President Trump."Two earlier presidents set the Pacific Northwest lumber industry on its course.Theodore Roosevelt created the U.S. Forest Service in 1905 amid concern that lumber barons like Frederick Weyerhaeuser would strip the Pacific Northwest as they had the Lake States and exhaust the country's timber supply unless the government stepped in.Temperate rainforests along the Pacific Ocean and the Cascade Range were set aside as public reserves.Overlogging again became a worry in the late 1980s. The concern was dwindling old growth, however, not running out of lumber.To turn back the chain saws, environmentalists took what was then a novel approach and used the Endangered Species Act to sue on behalf of the threatened northern spotted owl.President Bill Clinton stepped in to break the stalemate. The result was 1994's Northwest Forest Plan, which reduced logging on federal lands. In practice, there has been even less logging than the plan prescribed."It was an overcorrection," said Garret Stump, a third-generation lumberman who is vice president of log procurement at WKO, which operates the last sawmill in Skamania County. "It's been a slow bleed since then."The sting comes not just from job losses at the hundreds of Northwestern sawmills that have closed. Roughly 80% of Skamania County is federal forestland and a further 10% or so is controlled by the state. That means that the county earns tax revenue on most of its territory from timber receipts, which have slowed to a drip.Recent budget cuts forced the local school district to lay off teachers and close two of its four schools.The WKO sawmill in Carson once sourced about 70% of its logs from federal land, but that share has declined to less than 15%.The mill buys logs from industrial timberland operations as well as state and county forests and even locals clearing off a couple acres, Stump said.A computer scans each log that enters the mill and instantly determines how many boards it will yield, then tells a machine how to position it through the first set of bandsaws to achieve the optimal breakdown."Everything we do in our mill is to make it more efficient, more competitive, so that we can pay more for logs when we need to," Stump said.He said a 25% boost in the federal timber harvest sounds substantial, but if the recent output of the nearby Gifford Pinchot National Forest were to increase at that rate, the extra wood would sustain a shift at the mill for about a month.Trump's order won't denude the national forests. Nor is it likely to have much impact on the price Americans pay for lumber. A 25% increase from recent levels would add less than 1% to the total U.S. timber harvest, according to Campbell Global, JPMorgan's timberland investing unit. Still, in pockets of the Northwest, any additional logs will help.Over the past 18 months, seven sawmills in Oregon have announced closures."The No. 1 reason is they don't have enough logs, not that we have a low lumber price," said Levi Beelart, owner of B&G Logging.B&G won bidding to thin about 600 acres of Douglas fir that was planted more than 50 years ago on the surfside slopes of the Siuslaw National Forest. The Forest Service wanted about 40% of the canopy cover cleared to make room for other species and restore the monoculture to a mixed forest. The wood, about 13 million board feet, will go to B&G's sawmill in Philomath and others nearby.The other problem for the Northwest's mills is across the border. Canada's lumber producers built up more capacity than needed to supply their own market, and they ship wood to the U.S. to keep mills running.U.S. sawyers have argued successfully in trade cases that their Canadian competitors are supplied with subsidized government logs, and that they offer two-by-fours over the border for less than they sell them at home.The resulting countervailing and antidumping duties are calculated annually using sales data from two years prior and collected from Canadian exporters at the border.Canadian interests and U.S. home builders say the duties add to the cost of American housing. Although they disagree about the extent, U.S. producers don't deny that the duties lift lumber prices. They say they are necessary to level competition.Last year, about 24% of the softwood lumber consumed in the U.S. was Canadian. That is down from roughly 35% in the 1990s and a sign that the duties are working, proponents say. Big Canadian lumber producers have closed facilities at home and invested in sawmills in the U.S. South, where there is plenty of pine on private plantations.Stimson Lumber Chairman Andrew Miller, a seventh-generation lumberman, said Canadian mills are now quicker to curtail output when lumber demand drops."We don't spend very long in that negative cash flow zone, which is reassuring," said Miller, who is also chairman of the U.S. Lumber Coalition, which has fought for the duties.Stimson, which owns about 600,000 acres of timberland and six Northwest sawmills, is building a new mill to replace its existing facility outside Portland, Ore. Upgrades to other mills in Oregon and Idaho could be next if Trump imposes a lasting tariff, Miller said."We have capacity we could grow into with the right market conditions," he said.By Ryan Dezember
event_description: Discussion of plans to increase logging in national forests.
setting: discussion of plans to increase logging in national forests
characters: President Trump [Hero]; local lumber industry [Victim]; Canadian lumber producers [Villain]; environmentalists [Villain]
plot: The local lumber industry is suffering due to limited log supply from national forests and competition from Canadian lumber, leading to mill closures. President Trump aims to increase logging in national forests and impose tariffs on Canadian lumber to support the domestic industry. However, environmental regulations and litigation may hinder these efforts.
moral: The problem is the decline of the local lumber industry due to insufficient logging and competition from Canadian imports. The text suggests that increasing logging in national forests and imposing tariffs on Canadian lumber are necessary actions to revitalize the industry.

Title: Asides: Endangered Law
Text: The Environmental Defense Fund has looked back over the nearly 25-year history of the Endangered Species Act and concluded it isn't working. A spokesman said the law "is only holding the line on losses and it doesn't even do that very well." Leaving aside the law's definition of a "species," we wonder what the EDF is actually saying here. It now speaks of "incentives" for private landowners. But one interpretation might be that the EDF and its allies simply failed in using the massive legal and police powers of the federal government to make individual citizens surrender. That is, at least one interested species is thriving and still healthy.    (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: Species Act Shouldn't Endanger Landowners" -- WSJ Dec. 24, 1996)
event_description: The Environmental Defense Fund suggests changes to the Endangered Species Act to improve its effectiveness.
setting: the nearly 25-year history of the Endangered Species Act
characters: Environmental Defense Fund [Hero]; federal government [Villain]; individual citizens [Victim]
plot: The Environmental Defense Fund critiques the Endangered Species Act for failing to protect species effectively, suggesting that the federal government's legal powers have not been used to compel compliance from individual citizens, resulting in some species thriving while others are still at risk.
moral: The Endangered Species Act is ineffective and needs reform to better incentivize private landowners and protect endangered species.

Title: Little Fish May Put California in Hot Water
Text: A three-inch fish is poised to make one of the biggest splashes yet in the escalating fight over how resources are managed in this country.    The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is expected to determine soon whether to propose protecting the minnow-like delta smelt, a tiny fish that lives only in estuaries of north-central California. The smelt's numbers have plunged an estimated 90% to about 200,000 fish in the past 20 years because of heavy human use of the estuaries. Many people involved in the smelt debate say the agency will recommend that the fish be listed as threatened under the Endangered Species Act. The agency could act as early as today.    Such a recommendation, which would have to be approved by the Interior Department, would put the delta smelt smack in the middle of the growing controversy over the disruption caused humans by protecting animals. Some 20 million people -- two-thirds of California's population -- get their water from the estuaries where the smelt lives. Almost everyone agrees that saving the smelt could require drastic reductions in the amount of water drawn from the estuaries for human use by California's elaborate system of pumps, dams and tunnels. That would place more pressure on the state's water supply, already badly strained by spiraling population growth and five years of drought.    Thus, many people in the debate say the smelt issue could swiftly boil down to a stark choice: Will there be enough water for smelt, or for people? "It's going to be one hell of a fight," says William Davoren, founder of the Bay Institute, a conservation group based in Sausalito, Calif.    The smelt dispute is sure to be seized upon by critics in Congress and elsewhere who seek revisions in the Endangered Species Act and other wildlife laws. For one thing, there is considerable debate about the cause of the smelt's decline, and about how crucial smelt are to the ecosystem of the delta they inhabit, formed by the Sacramento and San Joaquin rivers as they spread out into San Francisco Bay. Moreover, protecting the smelt would have wide impacts: Recently enacted logging curbs designed to save spotted owls may eventually cost 25,000 jobs; smelt protections could affect many millions of people, from all segments of society.    "I don't think people fully grasp what kinds of changes in their lives might be required to recover this animal," says Randall Brown, chief of the state Water Resources Department's environmental division. Though the state won't discuss how it would cope with reduced water supplies, rationing could spread and become stricter, and drinking water could be severely restricted in some areas.    Smelt supporters say there is plenty of water for both people and smelt. Indeed, underlying the push to save the smelt is a long-running crusade to change the way water is allocated in California. Farmers, for example, use 80% of the water consumed in California, much of it drawn from the smelt's estuaries; much of farmers' water goes to grow subsidized, water-intensive crops such as cotton and rice. Winning protection for smelt would achieve what many environmentalists and water reformers have been attempting for years: reducing farmers' disproportionate lock on the state's water resources.    Not surprisingly, all of the state's powerful farm lobbying groups oppose any federal protection for the smelt, as do water bureaucrats from the state and the federal Bureau of Reclamation, which also uses the delta system as a source of water. Opponents of protection generally say that more study of the smelt's condition is needed and that protecting the delta smelt would disrupt water supplies and damage agriculture. There is also concern that the fish might be so far gone already that protecting it would be pointless.    Environmentalists argue that the smelt is an "indicator species," and that its troubles indicate that "the delta and bay systems are very sick," says Tryg Slettleland, a fisheries consultant. Indeed, other species that once thrived in the area now also are in deep trouble, like the winter run of the Chinook salmon up the Sacramento River, which once numbered about 150,000 fish but has fallen to 230. The polarization between the two sides means that, as in most recent environmental fights over troubled species, the smelt's fate probably will be decided in court.    The smelt seems an unlikely creature to prompt such a fuss. Finger-length, bluish, weighing about three ounces, the smelt live in the so-called entrapment zone of the Sacramento and San Joaquin rivers' delta system. In this zone, fresh water moving west toward the bay collides with salt water moving inland, producing a perfect garden for algae, plankton and other foundations of the marine food chain.    Since the early 1980s, the smelt have been dying off in droves. Though there is some debate over the exact causes of the decline, whole schools of smelt are sucked each year into massive pumps used to draw water out of the delta and send it south, where it slakes the thirst of millions of people in Los Angeles and other cities and irrigates desert cotton fields.    Moreover, heavy pumping in recent years as a result of the drought and population growth has dropped delta water levels so much that more salt water is entering the estuaries. Many environmentalists say the increased salinity is killing smelt. The heavy pumping also has caused the San Joaquin River to flow backward for about 10 miles, possibly disorienting the smelt and disrupting their breeding practices.    Biologists also have begun to find increasing levels of toxins in the San Joaquin and Sacramento rivers, primarily from pesticide runoff and discharges from oil refineries. Smelt supporters say the toxins are contributing to the fish's demise.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is expected to propose protecting the delta smelt as a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: the ongoing debate over resource management in California, particularly regarding the Endangered Species Act and water allocation
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; delta smelt [Victim]; farm lobbying groups [Villain]; water bureaucrats [Villain]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service is expected to propose protecting the delta smelt as a threatened species, which could lead to reduced water supplies for California's population due to the need to protect the fish's habitat. This situation creates a conflict between the needs of the smelt and the water demands of millions of people, particularly farmers who rely on the estuaries for irrigation.
moral: The text suggests that protecting the delta smelt is crucial for the health of the ecosystem, and advocates for a reevaluation of water allocation in California to ensure both the survival of the smelt and the needs of the human population are met.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial): Bush and Clinton Among the Owls
Text: George Bush and Bill Clinton went toe to toe this week in the Northwest woods over something real -- loggers' jobs and spotted owls. We're happy to go into the woods to find out how these two men actually think about a flesh-and-blood issue. What we take away from this encounter is that President Bush's instincts, fitfully, are with the market, and Bill Clinton's strongest instincts run toward a paternalistic government. The Northwest, of course, is the site of the great battle between loggers' jobs and the effort to protect the spotted owl under the authority of the Endangered Species Act. Judges, environmental groups and various parts of the federal wilderness bureaucracy have decided that the future, and the woods, belong to the owls. It may well be that economics will make the work in the woods disappear someday, but the idea here is that the loggers and their families should depart the animals' "habitat." Call it eco-cleansing. President Bush said some sensible things about weighing costs and benefits in deciding on species protection. "The balance has simply been lost," he said, and it has. This sounds sensible unless biology is not merely your work but your religion and you regard the premature death of any sub-human life as a moral offense. (Sometimes that isn't even good biology.) The environmental zealots who subscribe to that religion were quick to respond to the President's idea, saying it would gut the species act and is unthinkable. In most areas of public discussion, this kind of absolutism would be regarded as fringe rhetoric and relegated to the obligatory one paragraph in a news story. But when it comes to The Environment, fundamentalism is now part of the media and political culture. That is why there has been no reform of the species act, even as federal judges have written biological theory into dictum and cost thousands of people their jobs and many more an opportunity to buy or use property. Mr. Bush used the opportunity of a visit to the economically damaged Northwest to make that point. Bill Clinton, in the same neck of the woods, wasn't there to talk about species absolutism. Instead Mr. Clinton replayed what has become a core theme of his candidacy, no matter if the subject is manufacturing, free trade or the environment. What we need, he said, are retraining programs (that somebody is going to have to pay for), and a "summit" of government officials, labor representatives and business to forge a solution to the problems of the Northwest. We realize there are still people, often found in the business community, who still think these government-business partnerships can solve various problems -- from education to health care to logging in the Northwest. Our strong sense, though, is that the number of believers has dwindled the past 25 years as more managers discovered that the turnaround time on decisions by government is just too long. By the time the "summit" acts, the problem has changed. Or the summit's recommendations are so hedged that no real progress is possible. But Bill Clinton still believes in this stuff. He brings it up constantly. He talks on and on about what "we" are going to do, meaning "we" in government and "you" outside government. Our reading of the current political mood, across a wide spectrum, is that most people don't want a lot of input from "we" in government. They mainly want an economy that creates jobs, just like the economy they had seven or eight years ago. That said, we'll concede that the Bush administration's record on resource regulation has been something of a puzzle. The President tolerates an EPA administrator who seems to operate contrary to the general bent of policy, even though that appointee's equivocations have appeased virtually no one among his fellow enviros. In that community, about the only vote William Reilly has saved for George Bush is William Reilly's, and we wonder about that. Problems such as the loggers' jobs or nonsensical wetlands enforcement had to boil over before the White House acted. At least the cost-benefit concept got an airing from the chief executive. It would be reassuring to hear the words "balancing costs and benefits" emerge from Governor Clinton's verbiage, but we're not reassured. The private-sector bent of the Democratic Leadership Council was an early, hopeful sign, but as the campaign wears on, what seems to be emerging is a Democratic candidate whose real love is "we" the government. (See related letter: "Letters to the Editor: We Know a Sow's Ear When We See It" -- WSJ Oct. 12, 1992) 920916-0158
event_description: Discussion on balancing species protection with economic impacts in the Northwest.
setting: Northwest woods, discussion on species protection and economic impacts
characters: George Bush [Hero]; Bill Clinton [Villain]; loggers and their families [Victim]; environmental zealots [Villain]
plot: George Bush advocates for balancing economic impacts with species protection, while Bill Clinton promotes government intervention and retraining programs, leading to a conflict between loggers' jobs and the protection of the spotted owl.
moral: The text suggests that a balance between economic needs and species protection is necessary, criticizing the absolutism of environmentalists and advocating for a more pragmatic approach to policy.

Title: U.S. News: U.S. Watch
Text: FLORIDA Court Halts Execution Of Man Who Killed 4 The Florida Supreme Court is halting the execution of a man who killed his ex-wife, daughter and two others until the U.S. Supreme Court reviews whether a sedative used in lethal injections is effective. Tuesday's state Supreme Court ruling puts a hold on 59-year-old Jerry Correll's execution, which had been schedule for Feb. 26. The U.S. Supreme Court halted executions in Oklahoma until it can determine whether condemned inmates suffer during the use of midazolam as the first in a three-drug lethal injection mix. The state high court said Florida uses virtually the same execution process as Oklahoma and in a 5-2 decision said it shouldn't follow its current execution procedure until the U.S. Supreme Court determines if it is constitutional. -- Associated Press TEXAS Fire at Islamic Center Accidental, Man Says A homeless man who was charged with arson after a fire that damaged an Islamic center in Houston said that he didn't intend to set the facility on fire and was merely trying to stay warm. Darryl Ferguson, 55 years old, was being held without bond following the blaze Friday at a storage building for the Quba Islamic Institute, according to jail records. The site was unoccupied at the time, and no one was hurt in the fire. The Texas office of the Council on American-Islamic Relations has sought an investigation into whether the fire was a hate crime. Mr. Ferguson was arrested Monday after rolling up on a bicycle to talk to on-scene investigators. He told reporters Monday night that the fire was accidental. -- Associated Press --- WASHINGTON STATE Federal Probe Sought Of Police Shooting The leader of a Hispanic business leaders group in an agricultural city in Washington state is asking the U.S. Justice Department to investigate last week's fatal police shooting of an orchard worker who ran from officers after reportedly throwing rocks at them. In a letter to Attorney General Eric Holder, Felix Vargas, the chairman of Consejo Latino, said a probe by a regional special investigations unit will have "no credibility whatsoever," the Tri-City Herald reported. The unit has exonerated officers in reviews of three other recent police shootings in Pasco, Mr. Vargas said. The special investigations unit, made up of four law enforcement agencies, doesn't include officers from Pasco, but its members could feel pressure to exonerate local colleagues, he suggested. Antonio Zambrano-Montes, 35 years old, reportedly was throwing rocks at a busy intersection. Police said the rocks hit two officers and that Mr. Zambrano-Montes refused to put down other stones. The U.S. attorney's office in Spokane didn't immediately return a call seeking comment Tuesday. Kennewick Police Sgt. Ken Lattin, a spokesman for the special unit, said federal or state officials would be welcome to review the findings, but the homicide investigation should be left to police. -- Associated Press --- OREGON Minnow Is First Fish Off Endangered List A tiny minnow that lives only in backwaters in Oregon's Willamette Valley is the first fish to be formally removed from Endangered Species Act protection because it is no longer in danger of extinction. The action comes 22 years after the 3-inch-long fish was first listed as an endangered species, and five years after it was upgraded to threatened. Paul Henson, state director for Fish and Wildlife, said the Oregon chub shows many species can be brought back from the edge of extinction if key needs are met, such as a safe place to live. The chub had almost disappeared from the Willamette Valley as the swampy backwaters and beaver ponds it depends on were drained to control flooding and create farms and cities. Restoration has involved working with private, tribal and public landowners to restore ponds and backwaters the fish needs to thrive, transplanting fish to expand its population, and protecting them from predators. -- Associated Press
event_description: The Oregon chub is formally removed from Endangered Species Act protection.
setting: Oregon's Willamette Valley, related to the Endangered Species Act
characters: Oregon chub [Victim]; Paul Henson [Hero]; private, tribal and public landowners [Hero]
plot: The Oregon chub, once on the brink of extinction, is removed from Endangered Species Act protection due to successful restoration efforts involving collaboration among various landowners and wildlife management.
moral: The problem of species extinction can be addressed through collaborative restoration efforts, and similar actions should be taken to protect other endangered species.

Title: U.S. Agency Proposes California Bird Be Put on List of Endangered Species
Text: Federal officials proposed that a tiny California songbird be placed on the endangered species list, despite an unusually heavy lobbying campaign from business interests and their political allies. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service found that the California coastal gnatcatcher faces possible extinction because its habitat, the low scrub and sagebrush that dots the southern California coast, is being plowed under for housing tracts. The action triggers a one-year review process, which is almost certain to result in consequently listing the bird as endangered and requiring potentially drastic measures to save it. The move was a blow to a host of big-time California developers, including Irvine Cos., Baldwin Co. and Santa Margarita Co. They and other developers, who own much of what remains of the gnatcatcher habitat, have mounted a lobbying blitz in Sacramento and Washington to convince state and federal wildlife officials not to list the bird as endangered. The developers have spent more than $1 million on studies they contend refute federal and state scientists' findings that the gnatcatcher is imperiled. And they predict that granting the bird endangered status will stall construction, cost jobs and generally be an economic calamity. While environmentalists dispute those claims, developers seemed to be gaining the upper hand in the gnatcatcher debate last week, when California officials declined to list the bird as endangered under state wildlife laws, despite state scientists' strong recommendation that they do so. "Obviously, we're disappointed" (with the federal decision), said Don Steffensen, vice president of Lusk Co., a developer based in Irvine, Calif. He said it's too early to say what specific impact the agency's ruling will have, but added that the "effect on the economy could be severe." The Fish and Wildlife Service's action enhances the gnatcatcher dispute's status as an important test case in escalating battles over how far to go to protect wildlife, and in the debate over the Endangered Species Act itself. The act is up for reauthorization next year. And many environmentalists expect an attack on the law from business interests who believe the law's protections, in some cases, are causing too much economic and social dislocation for people. Environmentalists hailed the proposed listing. "We are pleased and relieved . . . and grateful for the fact that the federal government is willing to enforce the law," said Joel Reynolds, a senior attorney with the Natural Resources Defense Council, an environmental group. "Slowly but surely, the gnatcatcher is moving toward being protected." The listing also eased concerns expressed recently by some federal scientists that top agency officials might buckle under the heavy lobbying. "My faith is restored," said one federal scientist. However, the gnatcatcher clearly isn't out of the woods yet. Developers said they will push their case during the one-year review. They charged the agency with ignoring new studies they say demonstrate that there is more coastal sage habitat than federal scientists say, that there are millions of gnatcatchers in Mexico and that the coastal gnatcatcher may not be a separate species at all. "The (agency's data) is the weakest of reeds upon which to base a decision that will launch this amount of economic chaos," said Hugh Hewitt, an attorney for developers. The proposed listing has little immediate effect, other than to intitiate the review period. Theoretically, the Fish and Wildlife Service could change its recommendation after the review. But in the hundreds of cases of plants and animals that have gone through the listing procedures, there is believed to be only one instance -- involving a desert plant known as the mathis spiderling -- in which the agency reversed a proposed listing. In that case, studies done during the review period discovered thriving colonies of mathis spiderling in Mexico and other areas, leading the agency to conclude it wasn't in danger of extinction. The recommended listing of the gnatcatcher could be a boost for an embryonic state plan being pushed by California's Department of Resources that calls for developers to cooperate with the state and with environmentalists to create broad zones of protected habitat along the coast. The plan, which state officials see as a potential model for resolving other environmental disputes in the nation, seeks to balance the demands of development with those of nature and to avoid protracted battles that have erupted recently over gnatcatchers, spotted owls and other species. Environmentalists hope that with the prospect of eventual listing of the gnatcatcher now on the horizon, developers will have more incentive to cooperate with the state plan. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Federal officials proposed that the California coastal gnatcatcher be placed on the endangered species list.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; developers [Villain]; California coastal gnatcatcher [Victim]; environmentalists [Hero]
plot: Federal officials proposed listing the California coastal gnatcatcher as endangered, which is opposed by developers who lobby against the listing. The Fish and Wildlife Service's proposal indicates the bird faces extinction due to habitat destruction, triggering a review process that could lead to protective measures. Despite lobbying efforts, environmentalists support the listing as a necessary step for conservation.
moral: The problem is the threat to the California coastal gnatcatcher due to habitat destruction driven by development. The text suggests that the federal government should proceed with the endangered listing to protect the species and encourage cooperation between developers and environmentalists to create protected habitats.

Title: U.S. News: Obama's Fix for a Political Problem Stirs Legal Question
Text: President Barack Obama's health-care fix may salvage a political promise to let Americans keep cheaper health plans if they like them. Whether he has the legal authority to do so, however, could be a tougher question. While the Affordable Care Act grandfathers some existing policies as written, it also sets new requirements for policies issued on or after Jan. 1, 2014. That spurred carriers to inform millions of consumers with plans that fell short of the new rules that their policies couldn't be renewed after they expire. The provision was intended to improve health-care coverage across the board, and some of those affected, mainly older or lower-income consumers who buy coverage on their own, say they have found cheaper or better coverage through the insurance marketplaces the Affordable Care Act established. Some consumers are about to get the federal government's help in paying their premiums. But many others say they would prefer to keep their existing plans, and have reacted with outrage at what they consider a broken promise to let them do just that. Republican critics of the health law have characterized the cancellations as indicative of wholesale problems with the law -- and suggested that Mr. Obama lacks the power to delay enforcement of higher coverage standards when the statute itself sets a specific date for their application. "I am highly skeptical that they can do this administratively," House Speaker John Boehner (R., Ohio), said Thursday. "I just don't see, within the law, their ability to do that." The White House argues, however, that the chief executive has some discretion in implementing laws. Administration lawyers point to a 1985 Supreme Court decision, Heckler v. Chaney, which dismissed a lawsuit seeking to compel the Food and Drug Administration to exercise its enforcement powers. The circumstances, however, were substantially different. The suit was filed by condemned inmates who argued that the FDA was required by federal law to review the drugs state officials had chosen for their execution by lethal injection. The Reagan administration -- whose lawyers in the case included now-Justice Samuel Alito -- argued that courts shouldn't second-guess the executive's policy decisions regarding enforcement of the laws. William Rehnquist, then an associate justice, wrote the court's opinion. In general, he wrote, executive agencies were better situated to decide the best way to go about carrying out their obligations. Moreover, "when an agency refuses to act it generally does not exercise its coercive power over an individual's liberty or property rights, and thus does not infringe upon areas that courts often are called upon to protect," the late Justice Rehnquist wrote. "There's a long history of administrative agencies delaying action in implementing particular statutes when there are serious difficulties in doing so, or sometimes when they just don't like the statute," said Timothy Jost, a law professor at Washington and Lee University and an expert in the Affordable Care Act. For instance, he said, Republican administrations have sometimes put off enforcing provisions of the Endangered Species Act and other environmental laws. "It's less common when you have an administration that's really trying to implement the statute," he said. On the other hand, there have been occasions when the courts have required agencies to act against their wishes. During the George W. Bush administration, in a case brought by Massachusetts and other states that said they were threatened by global warming, the court found the Environmental Protection Agency had ignored its duty to consider whether greenhouse gases should be regulated as air pollutants. "While the President has broad authority in foreign affairs, that authority does not extend to the refusal to execute domestic laws," Justice John Paul Stevens wrote for the court. Even if critics believe the president lacks the authority to extend the life of substandard insurance policies, they may have trouble getting a court to hear their claim. The Supreme Court long has held that a plaintiff must demonstrate an injury he wants redressed in order to get his day in court. The prisoners on death row certainly met that standard when they challenged the FDA in the Chaney case. But it may be harder for a plaintiff to demonstrate an injury from being permitted, but not required, to renew an insurance policy they already hold. Credit: By Jess Bravin
event_description: Discussion of delaying enforcement of the Endangered Species Act and other environmental laws.
setting: discussion of the Affordable Care Act and its implementation in a political context
characters: President Barack Obama [Hero]; Republican critics [Villain]; older or lower-income consumers [Victim]
plot: President Obama attempts to fix health-care policy to allow Americans to keep their plans, but Republican critics argue he lacks the legal authority to do so, affecting older and lower-income consumers who prefer their existing plans.
moral: The problem is the potential loss of existing health plans for consumers, and the text suggests that the administration should be allowed to exercise discretion in implementing the law to protect these consumers.

Title: Some Indian Hunters Come Under Fire; Do They Overkill? --- Tribal Rights Permit Them To Ignore State Limits; Many Bull's-Eyes in Court
Text: MIAMI -- In the nighttime blackness of a South Florida swamp, a man fired a pistol at two glowing, green eyes, killing a lion-sized animal. Last week, almost four years later, both the federal government and Florida gave up efforts to prosecute a Seminole Indian chief on charges of killing a Florida panther, one of the world's most endangered animals. In the little Idaho town of Kamiah a few days ago, anti-Indian anger erupted after a Nez Perce Indian, apparently within his legal rights, shot a bighorn ram considered a community pet. And in Wisconsin, complaining sportsmen persuaded a congressman to introduce a bill that would revoke Chippewa tribal hunting rights bestowed by treaty 150 years ago. From Florida to Washington state, Indians' broad hunting rights are under fire. One reason: Indians are making bold new court claims to their rights under old treaties. In addition, many people consider Indian hunting privileges out of step with the popular trend toward conservation. Terming hunting rights "a volatile issue in many states," Thomas Dougherty, Rocky Mountain regional executive for the National Wildlife Federation, a sportsmen's group, comments, "What we're hearing from sportsmen's groups and affiliates is that with many game populations bouncing back as a result of sportsmen's money and efforts, they have strong feelings about uncontrolled harvest." Apart from sportsmen, few people know that many tribes' hunting rights extend far beyond reservations to ancestral hunting grounds. There the Indians often are subject only to tribal rules and can ignore state hunting seasons and bag limits. In central Idaho, the reservation for some 3,000 Nez Perce Indians spreads across about 150,000 acres of good deer, moose and bighorn-sheep country. But their ancestral hunting grounds to which they have treaty hunting rights, now mostly national forests, sweep westward into Washington and Oregon. Similarly, a 1983 decision by a federal appeals court gave six bands totaling some 9,000 Chippewas hunting rights on four million acres, roughly the northern third of Wisconsin. Compared with regular hunting restrictions, Indians' privileges on off-reservation ancestral hunting grounds often are extremely liberal. The Nez Perce in Idaho recognize no bag limits or seasons. Charles Hayes, a member of the tribal executive committee, says that although they can legally hunt year-round, "the majority follow the pattern set by ancestors. Our ancestors didn't hunt in the dead of winter or when animals were carrying young." Even so, Indian practice diverges sharply from typical fall hunting seasons, which emphasize "fair chase" -- wildlife gets a sporting chance. In contrast, Indians and non-Indians agree, tribes hunt for meat, not recreation, and the difference is reflected in their hunting techniques. One such method: hunting late in the year when winter storms have driven deer, moose and elk down from the mountains into large groups in the valleys. Some Indians' hunting tactics considered "biologically unsound" are especially criticized. "They kill {elk} cows with calves by their side in June and July, and there's no rule against it," says Dwight Kilgore, an Idaho Fish and Game officer in the Nez Perce area. Similarly, Wisconsin's regular deer season runs nine days, with the limit generally one deer and, in a few areas, two. The Chippewas, negotiating with the state, agreed to a four-month season, September through December. The Chippewa bands and the state also set the size of their deer harvest, but within that share Indians haven't any individual limits, and their spokesmen concede that some shoot 20 to 30 deer a season. Some Indians hunt all four months but supply venison to others in the bands, says James Jannetta, an attorney for the Chippewas. He scoffs at the impact on wildlife. "We're talking about the rights of a community of a few hundred Indian hunters compared with hundreds of thousands of others," he says. "The Indian harvest in Wisconsin is less than the road kill." However, George Meyer, enforcement administrator for the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources, notes, "The courts even ruled the Indians could sell game and fish to the public." The state negotiated a temporary agreement under which Indians would confine wildlife commerce to bartering within the tribes. The Indian harvest of game, often unmeasured, perplexes state officials who set seasons according to game populations as estimated by aerial surveys and other methods. Idaho's Nez Perce don't compile any game-kill figures, and "we have no way to know what's taken by Indians," says Frank Nesmith, chief of law enforcement for Fish and Game. "If there's a strong harvest by Indians, we come up with shorter seasons and less permits for non-Indians." The bighorn killed by a Nez Perce recently near Kamiah was on private land within the reservation. The ram had taken a liking to the rocky slopes around the lumbering town, and the rare animal had become a local attraction. "What upset me most was that it was an opportunity for people to see a bighorn sheep, and now it's lost forever," says Richard Bovey, a state fire warden whose home looked out on the ram's favorite territory. "Under the treaty, the Indian had the right to do it. But having the right and doing right are two different things." Under Idaho law, only two bighorn rams are permitted in a hunter's lifetime. In Wisconsin, treaties giving Indians special hunting rights are under attack. Rep. F. James Sensenbrenner Jr. introduced a bill in Congress last July stating, "Any off-reservation usufructuary right to hunt, fish and gather granted to any Indian tribe by any treaty, law or executive order which may be exercised in the State of Wisconsin is hereby abrogated." The Republican congressman says he seeks to abrogate "expanded rights handed down by federal court." Protect Americans' Rights and Resources, a Wisconsin-based organization that claims affiliates in 15 states, opposes Indian treaties generally. "We want treaties abrogated because they are outdated and have been replaced by new opportunities and expensive programs to take care of needy Indians and non-Indians alike," says Larry Greschner, its executive director. "We don't think the Indian should enjoy full benefits of citizenship and then claim to be a sovereign nation." In Washington state, tribal victories in fishing cases have prompted the state to try negotiating to get Indians to restrict their hunting seasons voluntarily. "We said we know you have the right to hunt on federal lands, but if you are willing to restrict hunting to biologically sound times and manner, we won't challenge your right to hunt on state lands," says Robert Rasmussen, Indian affairs administrator for the Department of Wildlife. Last season, five of the 20 tribes agreed. The Florida panther case illustrates the complexity of such litigation. Bruce Rogow, attorney for Seminole Chief James Billie and a Nova University law professor, conceded that his client killed the big cat in December 1983. The case was thrown out of state court in 1985 for lack of jurisdiction but was reinstated in 1986. This year, when the Justice Department prosecuted Chief Billie, Mr. Rogow argued that "the U.S. Supreme Court has never held that the federal endangered-species act applies to Indian reservations." He also contended that the endangered status applied to only one species, that there are variations of the panther in Florida and that which one the dead panther belonged to isn't known. After a federal-court mistrial due to a hung jury, a state-court jury acquitted Chief Billie on a similar charge this month. "It's clear Indians do have different privileges," Mr. Rogow says. Some experts call litigating Indian cases a losing game. Says James Vaught, chief of law enforcement for New Mexico Game and Fish, "We've been to the U.S. Supreme Court twice, been whipped both times and now we're sitting on the bench saying, 'If you want to argue with those Indians, more power to you.'" Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A bill was introduced in Congress to revoke off-reservation hunting rights granted to Indian tribes by treaties.
setting: NA
characters: NA
plot: NA
moral: NA

Title: World-Wide
Text: ANOTHER EARTHQUAKE ROCKED northern Iran as the death toll mounted. The most powerful aftershock in three days shook the devastated Caspian Sea region, triggering a landslide that blocked a road linking the shattered town of Rudbar to the coast and impeding relief efforts elsewhere. The casualty toll soared to 50,000 killed and 200,000 injured, according to a senior U.N. relief official. Sixty other tremors were reported in the region within a 24-hour period. Army mountaineers climbed peaks in the rugged area in an attempt to rescue villagers trapped in remote hamlets, according to Iran's official IRNA news agency. A plane carrying medicine and other supplies from the U.S. was among 50 relief aircraft that landed in Tehran during the day, the news agency said. --- QUEBEC INTENDS to draft its terms and conditions for remaining in Canada. After the collapse of a constitutional plan to recognize Quebec as a "distinct society," the French-speaking province's premier said a public debate will begin this fall on Quebec's role within Canada. Bourassa gave no hint of what his government would demand. The proposed Meech Lake agreement died after two provinces, Newfoundland and Manitoba, failed to ratify it by a Saturday deadline. The accord's collapse was viewed as a serious setback for Canadian Prime Minister Mulroney. The Canadian dollar was expected to drop because of the impasse, but traders said the decline would be cushioned by Canada's high short-term rates. --- Activists shouted "shame, shame" as HHS Secretary Sullivan gave a speech ending the Sixth International Conference on AIDS in San Francisco. As Sullivan defended administration efforts to fight the disease, hundreds in the audience loudly protested U.S. travel curbs on people carrying the AIDS virus. --- Israel's Sharon announced his nation won't settle Soviet Jews in occupied territories. The housing minister's move was seen as a way to head off any change in Soviet emigration policy. Meanwhile, Egypt's foreign minister flew to the U.S., hoping to avert a crisis in U.S.-Arab ties over Washington's suspension of talks with the PLO. Jordan's King Hussein said the Mideast is approaching the same state of hair-trigger tension that led to the outbreak of an Arab-Israeli war in 1967. The 54-year-old monarch called for an urgent reassessment of U.S. policy in the Middle East. He said Arabs "may have no other choice" than to fall into another war. --- Mandela arrived in Washington for talks today with President Bush and Secretary of State Baker about the direction of U.S.-South Africa relations. The black nationalist leader, who said he has been "greatly encouraged" by his rousing U.S. reception, will address a joint session of Congress tomorrow. --- The northern spotted owl was declared threatened and will be placed under the protection of the federal Endangered Species Act on July 23. The Bush administration tomorrow will announce its preliminary proposal to regulate logging on millions of acres of Pacific Northwest national forests to preserve the owl. --- Sixty-three of Walesa's colleagues broke ranks with the Polish Solidarity leader over his criticism of the current Solidarity-led government. The senior advisers and longtime allies of Walesa signed a letter that called for him to dissolve the national Citizens Committee in which they fought together to overcome Communist rule. --- European Community leaders begin a two-day summit meeting today in Dublin, divided over the issues of financial aid to the Soviet Union and sanctions against the South African government. However, Irish Prime Minister Haughey remarked, "The whole trend is toward unity and coherence and integration, and not the other way about." --- Hooded gunmen shot 19 people to death execution-style outside a nightclub near Medellin, Colombia's cocaine trafficking center, and 36 others were killed in weekend incidents in the city, according to police. Medellin, a city of about three million, has been placed under heavy army guard because of drug-related bombings and assassinations. --- Talks were slated to resume between El Salvador's insurgents and the government. Rebels said the government agreed to discuss the dismantling of paramilitary groups accused of human-rights violations.
event_description: The northern spotted owl was declared threatened and will be placed under the protection of the federal Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: northern spotted owl [Victim]; Bush administration [Hero]
plot: The northern spotted owl is declared threatened, leading the Bush administration to propose regulations on logging to protect the species.
moral: The problem is the threat to the northern spotted owl due to logging activities, and the preferred solution is to implement regulations to protect its habitat.

Title: A Special Weekly Report From The Wall Street Journal's Capital Bureau
Text: DEMOCRATS SCRAMBLE for ways to ride out the Clinton sex-scandal storm. Senate Democrats mull pushing for a quick censure vote, even if it doesn't end impeachment debate. The goal: to help senators up for re-election distance themselves from Clinton's troubles. In a Wall Street Journal/NBC News poll, for the first time in Clinton's presidency as many Americans (43%) have negative feelings about him as have positive ones (40%). In the House, Democratic strategists gripe that Clinton's new damage-control team is heavy with former Senate hands.
