Title: U.S. News: Washington Wire
Text: PRESIDENTIAL ELECTION Payment From Trump Lawyer Questioned A government-watchdog group filed complaints with the Federal Election Commission and the Justice Department asking the agencies to investigate a $130,000 payment arranged by President Donald Trump's lawyer to a former adult-film star before the 2016 presidential election. Common Cause said the payment, revealed in The Wall Street Journal this month, violated reporting requirements and may have exceeded contribution limits. The group asked the Justice Department to investigate whether Mr. Trump violated criminal law by omitting the transactions from campaign filings. The Journal said lawyer Michael Cohen arranged for the payment to Stephanie Clifford in return for her agreement not to publicly discuss an alleged sexual encounter with Mr. Trump. "The Common Cause complaint is baseless along with the allegation that President Trump filed a false report" to the FEC, said Mr. Cohen. He and the White House have denied any encounter between Mr. Trump and Ms. Clifford. -- Michael Rothfeld --- SUPREME COURT Endangered-Species Case Joins Docket The Supreme Court agreed to take up a case that tests the scope of habitat protections under the Endangered Species Act. The case centers on the 2012 U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service designation of critical habitat for the dusky gopher frog. The frog has been listed as endangered since 2001, when the known adult population was about 100, all from one pond in Mississippi. The service also chose to include in its designation private land in Louisiana that it said could serve as a breeding ground. Weyerhaeuser Co., which brought the case to the Supreme Court, owns part of the land and leases the rest to grow timber. Weyerhaeuser says the Endangered Species Act didn't authorize the designation of its land as critical habitat. -- Brent Kendall
event_description: The Supreme Court is reviewing a case regarding the designation of critical habitat for the dusky gopher frog under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: Supreme Court case regarding habitat protections under the Endangered Species Act
characters: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; Weyerhaeuser Co. [Villain]; dusky gopher frog [Victim]
plot: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service designated critical habitat for the dusky gopher frog, which is endangered, including private land owned by Weyerhaeuser Co. Weyerhaeuser challenges this designation in the Supreme Court, claiming it is unauthorized.
moral: The critical habitat designation is necessary to protect the dusky gopher frog, and the Supreme Court should uphold the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's authority under the Endangered Species Act.

Title: Top court showed sensitivity to business last term, despite spotted-owl ruling
Text: Business interests suffered a potentially significant defeat as the Supreme Court rebuffed the timber industry's effort to weaken federal protection for the spotted owl and other endangered species.    But overall, the high court's 1994-95 term, which ended yesterday with a burst of rulings, included some important victories for business on issues ranging from employee benefits to securities-fraud lawsuits. Other decisions, including one involving disputes over frequent-flier promotions, effectively split the difference between corporate and consumer interests.    "Looking over all of these cases, I'd say the court is showing greater sensitivity to the economic realities of concern to business . . . even in cases where we didn't have big wins," said Robin Conrad, a senior lawyer at the U.S. Chamber of Commerce.    In yesterday's environmental ruling, the justices, by a 6-3 vote, backed the broad reading of the federal Endangered Species Act that regulators have used to stop logging and development on millions of acres of private land.    Enacted in 1973, the act has become a potent symbol to conservatives of overreaching federal regulation. Corporate and property-rights advocates immediately vowed to redouble their support for Republican proposals in Congress to roll back the species-protection law through the legislative process.    Landowners and timber companies launched the Supreme Court case with a 1991 suit that complained that they were financially hurt by curbs on logging to protect the spotted owl in the Pacific Northwest and the red-cockaded woodpecker in the Southeast. They challenged the government's interpretation of a part of the Endangered Species Act that prohibits "harm" to an animal threatened with extinction. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has read "harm" to include changes in habitats that injure wildlife.    A lower federal court said that harm was limited to direct application of force to animals, but Justice John Paul Stevens wrote for the Supreme Court majority that "given Congress's clear expression of the Endangered Species Act's broad purpose to protect endangered and threatened wildlife, the [government's] definition of `harm' is reasonable."    Despite yesterday's ruling, business lawyers taking the long view said they were satisfied with the Supreme Court term as a whole. Ms. Conrad of the Chamber of Commerce pointed to a decision in January involving job discrimination. While the justices rejected employers' argument that bias suits should automatically be thrown out when alleged victims are shown to have broken company rules, they added that money damage awards may be sharply limited on the basis of an employee's misconduct.    Justice Anthony Kennedy, who wrote the majority opinion, "struggled for balance" by giving some weight to evidence of worker wrongdoing, Ms. Conrad noted.    Still, employee advocates saw some reason to cheer about the job-bias case. "No longer will employers be allowed to escape liability for discriminatory conduct with this convenient legal loophole" of alleging worker misbehavior, said Judith Lichtman, president of the Women's Legal Defense Fund.    The frequent-flier decision and a ruling on truck safety also allowed both business and its opponents to walk away feeling at least partially vindicated. The common question in these two cases was the degree to which federal laws regulating industry precluded consumer suits against companies, a perennial issue at the Supreme Court.    American Airlines had been sued by passengers unhappy over the carrier's cuts in its frequent-flier program. American, a unit of AMR Corp., persuaded the high court that when such claims are based on state consumer-protection laws, they ought to be pre-empted by federal statute. But the justices said frustrated travelers could proceed with claims that American effectively violated its contract to provide customers with certain benefits. (The high court didn't address the merits of the contract claims.)    In the truck-safety case, the justices said there wasn't an applicable federal standard that pre-empted a pair of suits filed in Georgia by accident victims and their relatives against Freightliner Corp. and Navistar International Corp. The plaintiffs said that under Georgia injury law, the manufacturers should be held liable for failing to equip their trucks with antilock brakes. "We were pleased by the result," says Con Hitchcock, an attorney with Public Citizen, a consumer-advocacy group in Washington.    But corporate defense lawyers said that apart from the defeat for the two truck makers, Justice Clarence Thomas included language in his opinion for the unanimous high court that will make it easier in future cases for businesses to argue for pre-emption of suits filed under state laws.    First Amendment protection for advertising or other "commercial speech" was again prominent on the business docket, and here, too, the results were mixed. Brewers persuaded the high court to strike down archaic federal alcohol-labeling requirements in a decision that will help other industries attack disclosure rules they find excessive. But in a ruling that cut in the other direction, the justices upheld a Florida restriction on attorney solicitations of accident victims and their relatives. Personal distaste on the part of some justices for crass lawyer advertising may explain that result as much as any constitutional reasoning.    Business dodged a bullet in a March ruling on pensions and other employee benefits. The justices ruled unanimously that a widely used provision for amending corporate employee benefit plans meets federal statutory requirements for making such changes. The opposite result would have subjected many companies to massive liabilities for past changes.    In February, the justices shielded companies from certain investor suits alleging securities fraud. The high court ruled 5-4 that a key provision of the federal Securities Act of 1933 applies to initial public offerings, not to subsequent market trading or privately arranged deals. The securities industry had warned that a broad reading of the 1933 act would have stifled brokers' and other professionals' communications about prospective stock transactions.    The event this term that may have attracted the most avid attention from court watchers in the corporate bar didn't even involve a decision on the merits. It was the justices' announcement that in their next term, which begins in October, they will once again entertain corporate pleas for constitutional limits on punitive damages.    While Congress continues to debate putting legislative curbs on such damages, the justices agreed to hear an appeal by BMW of a $2 million award in an Alabama suit. The German auto maker was sued by a customer who was dismayed to discover that his new $40,000 sedan had been repainted on the way from factory to showroom.    "Taking the BMW case may be the most important thing [the high court] did the whole term," said Stephen Bokat, general counsel of the Chamber of Commerce.    ---                Supreme Court by the Numbers    -- Most Likely Allies: On the right, Justices Antonin Scalia and Clarence Thomas once again voted together most frequently -- in 83% of the nonunanimous cases. On the left, Clinton-appointed Justices Ruth Bader Ginsburg and Stephen Breyer stuck together 79% of the time.    -- Least Agreeable Twosome: Justices Stevens and Thomas voted the same way less than 11% of the time in nonunanimous cases. The runners-up were Justices Stevens and Scalia, at 19%.    -- The Rookie Breyer: After Justice Ginsburg, his next most likely ally was Justice David Souter, with whom he voted 74% of the time in split cases. He was least likely to agree with Justice Thomas (43%).    -- Swing Votes: Justices Anthony Kennedy and Sandra Day O'Connor cast key votes and wrote critical opinions in most of the 16 cases decided by a 5-4 vote. Kennedy was in the majority 13 times; O'Connor, 11.    -- Lone Dissenter: Justice Stevens, renowned for his independence, filed solo objections to majority opinions nearly 10% of the time.    -- Unanimity and Splitsville: An unusually large 43% of this term's decisions were unanimous. But a relatively big share -- 20% -- came down to the wire with 5-4 rulings. Note: Voting rates are based on justices reaching the same result, but not necessarily by the same reasoning.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The Supreme Court upheld the broad interpretation of the Endangered Species Act regarding the definition of 'harm' to endangered species.
setting: Supreme Court ruling on the Endangered Species Act
characters: timber industry [Villain]; spotted owl and other endangered species [Victim]; Supreme Court [Hero]
plot: The Supreme Court upheld the broad interpretation of the Endangered Species Act, rejecting the timber industry's attempt to weaken protections for endangered species, which resulted in continued federal protection for the spotted owl and other species.
moral: The problem is the timber industry's efforts to undermine endangered species protections; the text suggests that strong enforcement of the Endangered Species Act is necessary to safeguard these species.

Title: A Special News Report on People And Their Jobs in Offices, Fields and Factories
Text: MANY CHIEF EXECUTIVES favor Perot or Bush on labor issues. Robert Weiner of Prince Street Technologies, an Atlanta carpet maker, says the Texas independent would handle soaring health costs. Martin Walker of M.A. Hanna says Perot sees the need for competitive, better-trained workers. Stuart Rose of Audio/Video Affiliates likes Perot for his health-care and free-trade policies -- even though the Texan has criticized a U.S.-Mexico free-trade pact. But Donald Moffitt of Consolidated Freightways and William Agee of Morrison Knudsen, a Bush campaign official in Idaho, think Bush can best solve health issues. Kenneth Iverson of Nucor, concerned about striker replacement restrictions and changes to workers' compensation, favors Bush or Perot. Judith Starkey, co-owner of Chamberlin Edmonds, a Georgia health-care firm, says Clinton doesn't know small business, Bush "missed the ballgame," and Perot lacks political skills. SKILL-BASED PAY boosts worker productivity and morale. Some employers hope to get more work out of fewer hands by offering raises to employees who learn new skills. In a survey of 27 companies with such programs, consultant Towers Perrin found that 70% to 88% reported higher job satisfaction, product quality or productivity. Some 70% to 75% cited lower operating costs or turnover. Nine of 22 Corning plants are on skill-based pay, and more switches are planned; at one, workers can lift base pay from $9.50 an hour to nearly $13.50. Workers at American Steel & Wire can boost annual salaries by up to $12,480 by acquiring as many as 10 skills. Cross-training also simplifies vacation scheduling, the firm says. TIMBER WORKERS fight for jobs amid environmental disputes. Lawsuits stymie logging on 13 Oregon tracts, even though a high-level panel waived Endangered Species Act requirements protecting the northern spotted owl there. The Timber Industry Labor-Management Committee lobbies for regulations and legislation to limit appeals of federal timber sales. The House Agriculture Committee, though, last week rejected one such amendment and a plan by Interior Secretary Lujan to minimize Northwest job losses. The environmentalist Wilderness Society argues that appeals affect only small amounts of national forest timber. But Tim Louck, a Camino, Calif., mill worker, says, "The very small amount left that we can harvest is being appealed." The industry group says an environmental bill passed by the House panel would cost 100,000 jobs. DRAWING THE LINE: The timber labor-industry group says a poll of 2,033 Northwest voters showed that from 51% to 68% wouldn't accept the loss of a single job to protect endangered species; only 4% to 8% would tolerate 10,000 lost jobs. But the Nature Conservancy and the National Audubon Society say their poll suggests that most western voters support the Endangered Species Act. BURDENSOME REGULATION? Unions will have to spend $192 million to make needless accounting changes proposed by the Labor Department, the AFL-CIO asserts. The Labor Department puts the cost at $58 million. The rules are designed to enforce a Supreme Court ruling that non-union workers who pay union dues needn't pay the portion used for political purposes. HIGH-LEVEL DISAGREEMENT: More than 56% of 500 large-company directors polled by Coopers & Lybrand believe stockholders should have more influence over executive compensation, but over 59% of the companies' chief executives don't think so. REPRIEVE GRANTED: The Occupational Safety and Health Administration defers a threatened takeover of the North Carolina job safety and health program, at least until early 1993. The state has made a commitment to correct program deficiencies, OSHA said. LAPTOP COMPUTERS enhance salespeople's efficiency. Since SmithKline Beecham's pharmaceutical division gave laptop computers and portable printers to all 1,800 of its sales staff, each sales person now makes, on average, one-and-a-half more calls per week. Lynch, Jones & Ryan, New York brokerage firm, says its 13 salespeople have used the computers for a year to keep up records or plan meetings during travel "down time." The 280 sales reps in Ciba-Geigy's agricultural group use laptops for word processing, maintaining customer data, linking with headquarters and faxing. At Minnesota Mining & Manufacturing, 600 salespeople use laptops to communicate faster, boosting their productivity and that of managers and marketing people, an official says. Tim McMahon, a consultant at laptop-sales firm Sales Technologies, says all companies will have them five years from now. But now, they aren't always "user friendly," he admits. THE CHECKOFF: Consultant Foster Higgins says some 53% of 334 employers with flexible benefit programs cite cost containment as the chief reason, up from 46% in 1990. . . . Staff members at the Labor Department's Women's Bureau pitch in to help build an "all-woman house," put up entirely by women, as part of a Jimmy Carter work project for Habitat for Humanity International.
event_description: Lawsuits stymie logging on 13 Oregon tracts related to Endangered Species Act requirements.
setting: timber litigation related to Endangered Species Act requirements
characters: Timber workers [Victim]; Environmentalists (Wilderness Society, Nature Conservancy, National Audubon Society) [Villain]; Timber Industry Labor-Management Committee [Hero]
plot: Timber workers are fighting for jobs as lawsuits stymie logging on Oregon tracts due to Endangered Species Act requirements. The Timber Industry Labor-Management Committee is lobbying for regulations to limit appeals of federal timber sales, while environmentalists argue that the appeals only affect small amounts of timber.
moral: The text suggests that the loss of jobs for timber workers due to environmental regulations is unacceptable, advocating for a balance between job preservation and environmental protection.

Title: Squaring Off: Question: Is It Time for Washington to Do Away With the State Spending Cap?
Text: Six years ago, Washington voters narrowly approved a ballot measure designed to -- depending on your view -- restrain or hamstring the Legislature when it comes to public spending. Initiative 601 limits state spending increases to a percentage based on the average statewide inflation rate for the preceding three years, and changes in the state's population during the previous 12 months; if more revenue flows into the general fund than the formula would allow to be spent, the extra money goes into an emergency reserve fund. Should the spending cap be eliminated? Yes, says State Rep. Jim McIntire, a Democrat from Seattle, because it risks trapping Washington's economy in a cycle of shrinking public services and growing tax cuts. No, says Bob Williams, president of the Evergreen Freedom Foundation, based in Olympia, because it has been a boon to the state's economy and enjoys widespread public support. Jim McIntire Yes -- What business could survive indefinitely by reinvesting an increasingly smaller share of its profits in new products and markets? Yet state spending has been shrinking dramatically as a share of Washington's economy ever since the general-fund spending limit took effect. Between the fiscal years 1995 and 2001, general-fund spending has fallen to 5.7% of state personal income from 6.8%, a decline of 16%. General-fund spending increases have slowed from roughly 8% a year during the late 1980s and early 1990s to about 4% a year since Initiative 601 took effect. To accommodate this slower spending growth, lawmakers have routinely cut hundreds of millions of dollars from inflation adjustments and programs within the state's Department of Social and Health Services, and provided minimal salary increases to teachers and state employees. One exception to this pattern was the current biennium, which ends in 2001, in which budget writers gave teachers and state employees a base salary increase of 3% for each of the two years -- financed with a one-time windfall of $772 million from reductions in required pension-fund contributions, welfare-caseload reductions and tobacco-settlement funds. But even if the state economy continues to perform as it has for the past few years, windfalls of this magnitude will not be repeated in the next biennium. In addition, because the I-601 growth formula is based on inflation and population growth in preceding years, spending risks falling behind what current population growth and inflation would justify. For example, state economic forecasters predict that over the next few years the combined rate of inflation and population growth will increase again. As a result, state budget writers will face an unprecedented challenge as they try to finance demands on state services based on yesterday's milder economic conditions while responding at the same time to emerging transportation-funding problems and the Endangered Species Act listings for salmon. Then there is the prospect of misguided tax cuts. Based on current economic and revenue forecasts, appropriated funds for the current biennium and long-term trends and demographic projections, I estimate that between 1999 and 2005, personal income will grow 34.5%, the cost of current services 28.7%, state general fund revenue 24.9%, and the I-601 limit 21.2%. This suggests that spending increases permitted by I-601 will be less than the rate of growth in revenue otherwise would allow, and, more important, that state spending will fail to keep up with the cost of public services and salaries. The result will be to force continued real cuts in services and salaries, and to inspire more tax-cut demagoguery as I-601 ensures that not all of the revenue collected is spent. How long can we continue to cut taxes and shrink state services? Roughly 59% of the state general fund is spent on schooling, from kindergarten to higher education. Add health-care costs and corrections spending, and we have accounted for nearly three-quarters of the general fund. Washington already ranks 48th in average class size. Must we wait until we've driven the best teachers from the schools and test scores drop precipitously? When that happens, how long will it take us to rebuild the educational institutions and roads we've allowed to deteriorate and become overcrowded? The latest comparative measures tell us that state and local taxes in Washington are near the national averages. According to the Washington Research Council, per-capita state and local tax revenues were 6% above the national average in 1996, just before roughly $1 billion of property, business and motor-vehicle excise-tax cuts during the past two years. We have reduced state government. What Washington needs now is a realistic spending limit that allows state investments in education, infrastructure and other public services to grow with the economy. Shrinking state budgets will lead our economy into serious trouble unless we change the I-601 growth formula. Bob Williams Spending restrictions appear to be necessary for most lawmaking bodies. Otherwise, long-suffering taxpayers face a deadly combination: clamoring special interests who always want more money, and lawmakers and governors who can't or won't say, "No!" Washington is one of 27 states with a statutory or constitutional provision to limit state spending. The groundswell for a spending ceiling was born in Washington state in 1991, when the House Appropriations and Revenue Committee, led by its then-chairman Gary Locke, developed a budget that spent $770 million above the predicted state revenue. That spending spree created a negative financial "bow wave" that in 1993 pushed lawmakers to pass a new budget that included tax increases of more than $900 million. Angry voters responded by qualifying two spending-limit initiatives for the November 1993 ballot-Initiatives 601 and 602. When the former initiative was approved and became law, it immediately changed the level and tone of the budget debate in Olympia. Republican and Democratic lawmakers quickly were forced to find efficiencies to stay within spending-growth caps that averaged about 4% a year -- less than half of what the increases had been during the previous six years. All the while, essential state-government services did not suffer cutbacks and our economy continued to prosper. And few knowledgeable observers believe our state would have its $1 billion surplus and excellent bond rating if I-601 were not the law of this land. In years past, legislators ignored statutorily mandated rainy-day reserves. Now a two-thirds legislative vote is needed to take funds out of the reserve. Another benefit of I-601: fewer tax and fee hikes. Nearly $3 billion in tax reductions have been granted since the initiative became law. Lawmakers still manage sometimes to get around the limits set. During this budget-writing year, they punctured the spending ceiling by taking items out of the general fund in response to pressure from a coalition of educators, labor, social-service providers, religious groups and environmentalists. This same band of allies has attempted to bend or break the initiative since its passage. They claim if its limitations are not breached, K-12 education funding will suffer, salary increases will be impossible, and social programs and higher education will go begging for financial support. Yet education has still received a high priority in recent budgets, and teachers have received a substantial pay increase. Two means of exceeding the cap were provided in I-601. Lawmakers can ask voters to approve a spending increase at a November general election. Or, if our state suffers natural disasters and the governor signs a bill declaring an emergency that has been approved by two-thirds of the Legislature, the spending limit may be exceeded for 24 months. But lawmakers are loath to ask voters for permission to spend more money. So, the opponents of spending limits are working on ways to change how the limit is calculated, even as many of our lawmakers keep tinkering with the restrictions by putting more programs "off-budget" in dedicated funds. This is an effective strategy in our state, where the spending cap only applies to the portion of our budget called the general fund. Indeed, when the initiative was passed, the state's general fund was more than 50% of the total state budget. Our general-fund budget now accounts for only 46% of all state spending. Putting certain programs or projects out of the reach of the general fund not only lifts the spending ceiling for those particular areas, it also provides more money to spend for the reduced number of budget items left in the general fund. If such sleights of hand go unchecked, they will make I-601 moot. Foes of the spending cap thought they were wearing down the public's strong support of I-601. Then, in July, half-a-million valid signatures were submitted for an initiative cutting high vehicle-excise taxes to $30 per vehicle and requiring voter approval for all future tax and fee increases. So much for voter apathy!
event_description: Discussion of Endangered Species Act listings for salmon.
setting: legislative chamber or committee debate
characters: Jim McIntire [Hero]; Bob Williams [Villain]; Washington taxpayers [Victim]
plot: Jim McIntire argues for the elimination of spending caps to prevent harm to public services, while Bob Williams defends the spending limits as beneficial to the economy. The ongoing debate leads to a situation where taxpayers face reduced services and potential economic decline due to the restrictions imposed by Initiative 601.
moral: The problem is that the I-601 spending cap is harming public services and the economy, and the text suggests that Washington needs a realistic spending limit that allows for growth in education and infrastructure to avoid serious economic trouble.

Title: U.S. News: Reptile Roils Oil Patch --- Companies Oppose Endangered-Species Tag for Lizard, but Brace for Its Listing
Text: The dunes sagebrush lizard, while just five inches long, is causing a big ruckus in the oil patch. The U.S. government is considering whether to put it on the endangered species list, with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service arguing oil and gas development in the Permian Basin, a rich oil-producing area in West Texas and Southeast New Mexico, is destroying parts of the lizard's home, a unique sand dune ecosystem. The oil industry says the listing of the Sceloporus arenicolus would bring economic ruin. "This is the most prolific oil-producing region in onshore America," said Ben Shepperd, president of the Permian Basin Petroleum Association, an industry group in Midland, Texas. "If you are to knock out a big portion of that, it clearly would drive prices up at the gasoline pump." The battle over the lizard is the latest example of the industry-versus-creature conflicts generated when species are slated for the endangered or threatened list. Northwest loggers failed to stop the northern spotted owl from being designated as threatened in 1990, and California winemakers have clashed with water managers over conservation measures to protect certain salmon. In the fight over the lizard, though, there is some rare cooperation between federal officials and industry: Some companies are paying for conservation efforts in advance to minimize the impact a listing would have on their businesses, even as they continue to oppose such a decision. Politicians suggest dire consequences if the lizard is subject to the Endangered Species Act. In New Mexico, Republican Gov. Susana Martinez warned Fish and Wildlife officials in a May letter that "the future of our state's economy and livelihood of so many employers and hardworking New Mexicans are at stake." That is overstating it, environmentalists say, pointing out the lizard's habitat takes up just a fraction of the eight counties where the species is found. For example, the lizards occupy 600,000 acres of the total 11 million acres in the four New Mexico counties that would be affected. "They are totally baseless claims that are being made by politicians who feel that there's political gain," said Noah Greenwald, endangered-species director at the Center for Biological Diversity, a nonprofit that first proposed the lizard for listing in 2002. With "reasonable precautions," said Mr. Greenwald, the lizard should be able to coexist with the oil industry. Fish and Wildlife officials say the proposal is based on the best available science and that the agency is working with companies to avoid any interruptions. An endangered-species listing would force drillers to work with the agency to avoid disturbing the habitat and could expose them to penalties if lizards were killed. A decision is expected in December. Scientists consider the lizard a "habitat specialist," an organism so adapted to its environment that it can't exist elsewhere. The creature evolved in dunes anchored by shrub-like shinnery oaks, developing larger scales over its ear openings to keep sand out and long front toes to run up steep, sandy slopes. That ecosystem has shrunk by 40% since 1982 in New Mexico, the agency calculates. The size of the lizard population isn't known. Lee Fitzgerald, a herpetology professor at Texas A&M University who has studied the lizard since 1994, said the population has declined in areas crisscrossed with roads and oil-well pads. "When you tear [the dunes] down, they are gone and so are the species that depend on them," he said. Still, he said, identifying and avoiding the dunes shouldn't be difficult, especially given the oil industry's experience working in environmentally sensitive areas. Some oil and gas companies, from giants such as Chevron Corp. to smaller independents, are pre-emptively seeking to head off any new requirements by voluntarily signing conservation agreements. Four companies have signed on and 19 others are in the process of doing so. Under such agreements, companies commit to pay for habitat restoration -- the cost is $10,000 to $20,000 for each new well -- and to use techniques such as horizontal drilling to avoid the lizards. The program, run by the federal Bureau of Land Management, is only available in New Mexico, where about half the land that would be affected is managed by the agency. Credit: By Ana Campoy
event_description: The U.S. government is considering whether to put the dunes sagebrush lizard on the endangered species list.
setting: U.S. government consideration of endangered species listing, involving the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the oil industry in the Permian Basin
characters: dunes sagebrush lizard [Victim]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; oil industry [Villain]; politicians [Villain]; environmentalists [Hero]; Noah Greenwald [Hero]
plot: The U.S. government is considering listing the dunes sagebrush lizard as endangered due to habitat destruction from oil and gas development, leading to conflict between the oil industry and conservation efforts. The oil industry argues that such a listing would harm the economy, while environmentalists and scientists advocate for the lizard's protection and suggest coexistence strategies.
moral: The problem is the potential economic impact of listing the lizard as endangered, but the text suggests that with reasonable precautions and cooperation between the oil industry and conservation efforts, both the economy and the lizard can be protected.

Title: Environmentalists Uncertain on Roberts; Review of Record Sends Mixed Signals for One of Left's Most Vocal Constituencies
Text: Washington -- THE ENVIRONMENTAL community looks at Judge John G. Roberts Jr. as something of a chameleon. As a result, it is having a hard time deciding how green he is and whether to join other liberal groups in opposing his nomination to the Supreme Court. As a private lawyer, he won a big case for environmentalists that slowed development in Lake Tahoe. As a judge, he later issued an opinion on the Endangered Species Act considered troubling to supporters of that measure. Doug Kendall of Community Rights Council, an environmental-law organization that helps to rate judicial nominees' green credentials, worked on the Lake Tahoe case that blocked development for years while a preservation plan was completed. He says Judge Roberts "wrote the best brief I've ever read." Yet he's "pretty skeptical" about whether Judge Roberts would be an ally on the court. Uncertainty about the leanings of Judge Roberts, who is President Bush's pick to fill the seat of retiring Supreme Court Justice Sandra Day O'Connor, has silenced for now one of the left's most vibrant constituencies. Women's rights groups were quick to mobilize against him with rallies and petition drives. Indeed, NARAL Pro-Choice America was widely accused last week of battling the nomination too aggressively with television ads linking Judge Roberts to abortion bombers; the group pulled the spots. Most environmental groups, in contrast, have posted material on their Web sites and urged members to lobby senators to ask Judge Roberts pointed questions on their issues. Judge Roberts poses the first big test for the environmental community's new activism on judicial nominations. Such groups never weighed in heavily on these matters before. But just as a series of adverse rulings on school prayer and other issues energized the religious right on judge selection, environmentalists decided to get involved in 2001 after a string of disappointing Supreme Court rulings, including one that exempted some ponds and lakes used by migratory birds from clean-water regulations. Their new activism reached a peak in 2004, when they launched telephone banks and petition drives to block the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals nomination of William Myers, a former Interior Department attorney who once advocated as a lawyer and lobbyist for cattlemen and miners in clashes with environmentalists over land use and other issues. Mr. Myers' nomination continues to be blocked by a Democratic filibuster in the Senate. Environmental leaders say they could still rally to oppose Judge Roberts in September, after the Senate Judiciary Committee hearings shed more light on his legal philosophy. The hearings open when Congress returns Sept. 6. In advance of that, Democrats are sparring with the White House over access to Judge Roberts' records. They want to review them to try to figure out whether there is some reason to block the nomination, or to allow the process to move smoothly so he can take his seat in time for the new court session opening in October. For environmental groups, a key issue in the Roberts hearings will be his view on the breadth of the Constitution's Commerce Clause, which gives Congress authority to regulate interstate commerce. Virtually all important environmental laws are rooted in that clause -- as are civil-rights laws -- and conservatives for years have been pressing the federal courts to adopt a narrower reading of it. For instance, they have argued that species found in only a single state -- such as red wolves of North Carolina, cave insects in Texas and the Delhi Sands Flower-Loving Fly in California -- shouldn't be protected by a federal law regulating "interstate" commerce. Thus far, they have lost every time and the Supreme Court has refused to take up the matter. But a number of conservative judges have endorsed the challenge, including several who were on Mr. Bush's short list of Supreme Court candidates. The case that puts Judge Roberts in the heart of this fight involves a small population of endangered arroyo southwestern toads that live in the sandy banks of Keys Creek in San Diego County, Calif. In 2000, the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers concluded that a 280-home development planned by real-estate developer Rancho Viejo LLC near the creek could threaten the toad population. A fence could block access to their breeding area, and a plan to dredge fill dirt from the creek would further disrupt their habitat, government officials said. U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service officials proposed a compromise that involved removing the fence and bringing fill dirt in from another location. But Rancho Viejo rejected the offer and filed a lawsuit against the Interior Department at the U.S. District Court in the District of Columbia. The complaint challenged the government's authority under the Commerce Clause to designate the toad an endangered species. Rancho Viejo was assisted in its case by such conservative legal organizations as the Claremont Institute Center for Constitutional Jurisprudence, headed by John C. Eastman, a former law clerk of Justice Clarence Thomas, and the Pacific Legal Foundation. Both aim to roll back more than a half century of high-court jurisprudence deferring to acts of Congress centered on the Commerce Clause. Two Supreme Court cases touching on the Commerce Clause fueled their argument. In 2000, the court struck down the Violence Against Women Act, in part because it didn't affect interstate commerce. In 1995, the justices overturned a federal law creating gun-free zones around public schools on similar grounds. The district court in 2001 ruled against Rancho Viejo, and its decision was upheld by a three-judge panel at the D.C. Circuit Court of Appeals. In 2003, Rancho Viejo petitioned for a rehearing before the full appellate court. On a 7-2 vote, its request was denied. Judge Roberts, who had been on the court for only a few months, was one of the dissenting judges. In a five-paragraph opinion, he said the court's approach in Rancho Viejo "seems inconsistent" with the Supreme Court holdings in the gun- free zone and violence-against-women cases. And in a passage that has, since Judge Roberts' nomination to the Supreme Court, become one of the most famous phrases of his short judicial career, he wrote: "The panel's approach in this case leads to the result that regulating the taking of a hapless toad that, for reasons of its own, lives its entire life in California constitutes regulating 'Commerce . . . among the several states.' " Judge Roberts didn't take his argument further and reveal how he might rule on the case if the full panel had taken it up. The last sentence of his dissent only deepened the mystery around him, by suggesting that he might still have sided with the "hapless toad" and its backers. A review by the full court, he said, would "afford the opportunity to consider alternative grounds for sustaining application of the [Endangered Species] Act that may be more consistent with Supreme Court precedent." But Glenn Sugameli of Earth Justice, a partner in the environmental community's judicial-vetting project, says: "No federal court has ever ruled that any federal wildlife protection is beyond Congress's Commerce Clause power. It is a position that, although he doesn't give his ultimate view, is troubling." Judge Roberts' other rulings on the court, which typically handles appeals of government-agency actions, are equally opaque. He tends to defer to government regulators, whether they are disappointing business or the greens. In one instance, he wouldn't intervene for a firm objecting to its listing as a Superfund site. In another, he rebuffed the Sierra Club in a pollution case. "His record on the D.C. court is even-handed," says David Bookbinder, the Sierra Club's lead counsel. "There is certainly no pro- or anti-environmental bias we can see. We're glad, but these are not the sort of cases that go to the Supreme Court." So they await the hearings in hopes of clarity. Meanwhile, in San Diego County, an agreement was reached between the Rancho Viejo developers and government regulators. "The toad is still alive, and the development is in," reports Jane Hendron, spokeswoman for the Carlsbad Fish and Wildlife Office.
event_description: A court ruling upheld the government's authority to designate the arroyo southwestern toad as an endangered species.
setting: U.S. District Court in the District of Columbia and the context of Supreme Court nominations
characters: Judge John G. Roberts Jr. [Villain]; Rancho Viejo LLC [Villain]; Endangered arroyo southwestern toad [Victim]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Hero]; Environmental community [Hero]
plot: Judge Roberts issued a dissenting opinion in a case involving the endangered arroyo southwestern toad, which was challenged by Rancho Viejo LLC, leading to uncertainty about his stance on environmental issues. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service proposed a compromise to protect the toad, but Rancho Viejo rejected it and filed a lawsuit against the Interior Department, questioning the government's authority under the Commerce Clause.
moral: The environmental community must remain vigilant and active in opposing judicial nominees like Judge Roberts who may threaten the protections afforded to endangered species under the Endangered Species Act.

Title: A Special Weekly Report From The Wall Street Journal's Capital Bureau
Text: DEMOCRATS AIM to surf waves of Republican woes. Party strategists dismiss as premature calls for producing a "contract" like the one Gingrich once used. Though critics say Democrats lack a clear 2006 message, House campaign chief Emanuel notes that 1994 Republicans "laid out their contract six weeks before the election." With DeLay and other Republican figures enmeshed in scandal probes, pollster Geoff Garin emphasizes "tying all these events together into a coherent theme: the arrogance of power." To embarrass foes, Democrats introduce anti-cronyism bills. The Center for American Progress proposes withdrawing 80,000 troops from Iraq in 2006, rest in 2007. LOUISIANA FIGHTS for Medicaid help from Washington. Bush administration agrees to pick up the Medicaid costs of hurricane evacuees through agreements with seven states, including Texas, Florida, Mississippi and Idaho. But Louisiana hasn't yet submitted its request. It's "not the most pressing issue," says Health and Hospitals Secretary Fred Cerise. The top priority for the cash-strapped state is extra federal funding for its Medicaid program, which would require legislation. Until then, it's responsible for some Medicaid costs of residents who've left the state. Sens. Grassley and Baucus push for a short-term increase in Medicaid funding and eligibility, but Health Secretary Leavitt calls their solution an "unnecessary" new entitlement. FED SEES economy weathering hurricanes, but data will be murky. The central bank is expected to conclude the economy remains strong enough to justify continued credit tightening. But Goldman Sachs economist Ed McKelvey says beginning next week the effects of Katrina and Rita will blur the picture of underlying trends. Outside analysts, fearing Bush will let loyalty guide his pick, urge him to follow the Roberts template and emphasize credentials in choosing Greenspan's successor. "The Fed [chairman] is as important as chief justice," says Wharton economist Jeremy Siegel. Accelerating its forecast, online exchange Intrade predicts Bush will identify his choice in October. DO AS WE SAY: While the SEC requires companies to file annual reports within three months of a completed fiscal year, the agency still hasn't published its 2004 enforcement statistics. A spokesman says the agency is no longer required to publish such information, which costs money. For now, the data are available only upon request. SWITCH-HITTERS: Pro-business group BIPAC, praised for 2004 advocacy and turnout efforts, endorses conservative Democratic Sen. Nelson of Nebraska, citing the importance of "bipartisan coalitions." At the same time, moderate Republicans' Main Street Partnership wins support from a fragmented labor movement; Teamsters and firefighters unions each donate $5,000 to the partnership's political action committee and $25,000 to its policy arm. GIMME SHELTER: Rolling Stones keyboardist Chuck Leavell, who runs a Georgia tree farm when not making music, will address the National Press Club on "Family Forest Sustainability" on Monday before joining Mick Jagger on stage at Washington's MCI Center. Leavell plans to urge policy makers to address the "invisible crisis" of creeping development, now aggravated by hurricane damage to one-third of forest acreage in Mississippi, Louisiana and Alabama. OFF MESSAGE? Though embattled ex-director Brown told Congress this week that FEMA lacked the ability to track emergency supply shipments, he rejected that same conclusion as "unacceptable" earlier this year when it was stated by Homeland Security's inspector general. A bureaucratic reorganization at DHS, already in limbo due to Katrina, now faces added problems due to the unexpected resignation announcement of Customs Commissioner Bonner. BLAME GAME: Families of 9/11 victims complain the Transportation Security Administration withholds documents. The families bypassed a victims-compensation fund in favor of going to court against screeners of flights who worked Sept. 11, 2001. But they say the TSA resists their document requests even though they've obtained clearance for access. The attorneys say TSA has labeled the unclassified documents as Sensitive Security Information that they don't "need to know." Harry Ong, whose sister Betty phoned authorities from a doomed flight to report the hijacking, calls it "ironic that . . . the government refuses to tell me what happened before her heroic phone call." A spokeswoman says the TSA has worked to give families "as much information as possible." MINOR MEMOS: Earned it: Statistician Kitty Furlong, who started at White House Council of Economic Advisers during Truman administration, has retirement party today. . . . The Academy of Evangelical Scientists, lobbying Congress not to weaken the Endangered Species Act, asks "What Would Noah Do?"
event_description: The Academy of Evangelical Scientists is lobbying Congress not to weaken the Endangered Species Act.
setting: lobbying Congress regarding the Endangered Species Act
characters: Academy of Evangelical Scientists [Hero]
plot: The Academy of Evangelical Scientists is actively lobbying Congress to prevent any weakening of the Endangered Species Act.
moral: The Endangered Species Act is crucial and should not be weakened; Congress must uphold its protections for endangered species.

Title: Wildlife Agency Seeks a Middle Ground
Text: Texas wildlife officials have joined with other prairie states in an effort to forestall federal protection of the swift fox.   On Friday, Andrew Sansom, executive director of the Texas Parks and Wildlife Department, signed a petition -- circulated among wildlife directors in nine states -- that seeks to prevent the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service from listing the fox under the federal Endangered Species Act. Instead, the states are promising the agency that they will develop a regional management plan for protecting the animal. The significance of the action, though, extends well beyond the swift fox. The move is part of a broader strategy by the state's parks and wildlife agency to defuse anger among rural landowners, who believe their property rights have been violated by federal environmental regulations. The agency hopes that its experience working with farmers and ranchers to maintain wildlife for fishing and hunting will enable it to balance the needs of conservation and agriculture. "If the goal is conservation of this species, then it's clear that the local approach is the better approach," says Larry McKinney, director of resource protection at the Parks and Wildlife department. Perhaps, but it won't be easy: This effort to find a middle ground is looked at with suspicion from both sides. Landowners, for instance, worry that the Endangered Species Act "is not going to give state wildlife officials the flexibility to be understanding," says Tom Beard, president of the Texas and Southwestern Cattle Raisers Association. On the other side, environmental groups are wary of efforts to replace federal endangered-species protections with state safeguards, which can be more vulnerable to local political pressures. "In a general sense, the Sierra Club would not want to see state wildlife agencies shy away from supporting the listing of species as endangered because of political concerns," says Ken Kramer, Texas director of the Sierra Club. Certainly, the political pressures have never been greater. Smoldering resentment among landowners exploded last month after word leaked out that the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service was considering designating as many as 800,000 acres in 33 Central Texas counties as a critical cedar woodland habitat for the golden-cheeked warbler, a migratory songbird that was listed as endangered in 1990; such a designation would mean an extra layer of protection for the songbird. The result: About 30 agricultural organizations last month formed an alliance to press for federal and state legislative changes, and a reported 3,000 property owners rallied at the state Capitol protesting the Endangered Species Act. Into this volatile mix stepped the state wildlife agency, hoping to provide a buffer between the federal government and landowners. The problem was clear: Environmental protection depends largely on the cooperation of landowners in the state, where 97% of land is privately owned. Yet the feds don't provide any consistent guidelines as to what the regulations will and won't allow; investigations are typically launched only after a landowner does something that might be a violation. That ad-hoc approach produces a level of fear and uncertainty that angers landowners. It also leads to such pre-emptive strikes as clearing cedar from their land; if there's no habitat for the warbler, landowners figure, they won't be subject to the restrictions on their property. In addition, the state lacks the authority to advise landowners about what they can and can't do. Any advice the state gives could be overruled by the feds. So the state is preparing a formal request for such authority, which could be submitted to federal wildlife officials later this month. It already has received approval for guidelines affecting 55 other endangered species. And it has prepared a brochure, which still must be approved by federal officials, that lists permitted and forbidden activities regarding the warbler. For example, the guidelines say that clearing a swath of cedar narrower than 20 feet for a fence or road is permitted; a wider cut would require creating an additional habitat to mitigate the loss. Grazing or farming near a warbler habitat is also permitted; even burning pasture lands to encourage new grass growth in some cases is permissible. Federal officials seem encouraged by the project. "We're trying to build in the greatest amount of flexibility and still protect the bird," says Sam Hamilton, state administrator for the federal Fish and Wildlife Service. With the swift fox, state officials hope to devise a program that satisfies federal officials without resorting to the restrictions of the Endangered Species Act. In June, the federal wildlife service said it intended to seek endangered-species designation for the fox, which lives in short-grass prairie -- often near prairie-dog towns -- from Texas to Canada. The announcement quickly sparked concerns among state officials about possible restrictions on agriculture -- and about the likely backlash from farmers and ranchers -- that such a decision might mean. Already one Panhandle lawmaker, Warren Chisum, a Democrat from Pampa, has sounded dire warnings about the threat to West Texas. In a letter mailed to wildlife officials in nine states, the director of the Colorado Division of Wildlife warns that listing the swift fox "will only serve to amplify existing perceptions of capricious federal interventionism" and might "actually harm populations rather than enhance them." Instead, the states are proposing to count swift-fox populations, restore and maintain habitat, and trap and transplant threatened dens. In Texas, officials plan a detailed census of the swift fox -- only two sightings have been reported in the past six months -- and intend to form a "swift-fox working group" with Panhandle landowners to develop a conservation plan for the animal. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Efforts to prevent the listing of the swift fox under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: political context surrounding the Endangered Species Act and state wildlife management efforts
characters: Texas wildlife officials [Hero]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; landowners [Victim]; environmental groups [Victim]
plot: Texas wildlife officials, in collaboration with other prairie states, are attempting to prevent the federal listing of the swift fox under the Endangered Species Act by proposing a regional management plan, while landowners express concerns about federal regulations infringing on their property rights.
moral: The problem is the tension between federal environmental regulations and landowner rights; the text suggests that a local approach to conservation, involving cooperation between state officials and landowners, is the preferred solution.

Title: U.S. News: Pombo Embarks on Fresh Path --- Ex-California Lawmaker, a Foe of Environmentalists, Seeks Seat in New District
Text: When former Rep. Richard Pombo was soundly defeated in his 2006 re-election bid after serving seven terms, many environmentalists hoped they had seen the last of one of their biggest foes. But as the GOP's fortunes improve nationally, the California Republican is seeking a path back to power. Mr. Pombo announced last month that he was running again for the House, but not for his old seat representing California's 11th Congressional District, on the outskirts of the San Francisco Bay area, where he was first elected in 1992 and still lives. Instead, he is seeking an open seat in the neighboring 19th District, which he considers still safely conservative. During Mr. Pombo's former tenure as chairman of the House Resources Committee he weighed proposals to sell off some national parks and advocated weakening the Endangered Species Act, among other controversial measures. Such proposals prompted the Sierra Club to label him "the most frightening man in Congress." Mr. Pombo, 49 years old, said his signature issue -- to ease government control over land use -- is timelier than ever amid the sluggish job market. He cited recent federal orders to protect endangered fish in the Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta, which he said have led to large water cutbacks for farms in the 19th District, and killed jobs. "When you have 29% to 30% unemployment in some of these towns, a lot of that is tied to environmental regulations," he said. Mr. Pombo boasts strong conservative credentials that endear him to many Republicans in California and nationwide. Big lobby groups like the National Rifle Association also have assisted in his fund-raising. Republican Rep. Devin Nunes of Fresno has endorsed Mr. Pombo, saying he tried to protect Central Valley residents from environmental regulations that many people in the region believe led to problems like water cutbacks to farmers. "Richard was driven from office because of this issue," said Mr. Nunes. "The people of the valley see this as a fight against radical environmental groups." Environmental groups worked for Mr. Pombo's defeat in 2006. He also faced criticism for declining to launch a committee investigation into alleged influence-peddling by lobbyist Jack Abramoff, from whom he had received campaign contributions. Mr. Abramoff pleaded guilty in 2006 to three felonies in a political-corruption scandal. Mr. Pombo denied any wrongdoing and has said the first time he was aware Mr. Abramoff had contributed to his campaign was when he read about it in a newspaper. Mr. Pombo said a major obstacle to his re-election was voters' anti-Republican mood in 2006. His new bid faces resistance beyond his foes in the environmental movement, including from some fellow Republicans. GOP Rep. George Radanovich, the current congressman from the 19th District, which stretches between Fresno and Modesto in California's Central Valley, isn't planning to run for re-election. But he backs Republican state Sen. Jeff Denham in the June 8 GOP primary to select his successor. "I would like to see [Mr. Pombo] run for Congress again -- in his own district," he said. Mr. Pombo said that Mr. Denham also resides outside the solidly Republican 19th District. Mr. Denham, 42, is an almond farmer from Merced, Calif., about 10 miles past the district line. Mr. Pombo owns a ranch near Tracy, Calif., which is also about 10 miles over the line. A spokesman for Mr. Denham said the senator's house is almost completely encircled by the 19th district. U.S. House candidates are allowed to compete in congressional districts even if they aren't residents of the district, as long as they reside in the state after the election. Mr. Denham burnished his conservative credentials by defeating a recall initiative last year after he refused to vote for a state tax increase. His state Senate district includes about 100,000 of the 600,000 residents of the 19th District, according to Dave Gilliard, a GOP consultant in Sacramento who is helping to coordinate his campaign. "Pombo has never represented a blade of grass in the district," Mr. Gilliard said. Mr. Pombo countered that his ex-district overlapped a part of the 19th District before it was redrawn in 2001, and that his family has roots in the Central Valley going back more than 100 years. Mr. Radanovich said Mr. Pombo could best help the party by trying to recapture his former 11th District seat, which was won in 2006 by Democrat Jerry McNerney, a wind-energy consultant. Mr. McNerney benefited as more Democratic-leaning voters from coastal parts of the Bay Area moved to inland cities in the 11th. Mr. Pombo said the 11th also became less conservative after it was redrawn in 2001 to include more liberal Bay Area suburbs. "When they did redistricting, they moved my district," he said. Credit: By Jim Carlton
