Unlike in the era of the Chevron ruling, he said, today "the focus is much greater on the text of the statute" itself, leaving judges less room to impose their own policy preferences. Solicitor General Elizabeth Prelogar, representing the Biden administration, said Chevron not only was workable, but in 40 years had become ingrained in the lawmaking process, with Congress relying on agencies to fill the gaps in legislation. Deferring to agency judgments, as long as they are reasonable, promotes stability and uniformity in federal law, she said, with a single approach applying nationwide rather than various decisions by federal courts across the country. Justice Brett Kavanaugh countered that the administrative leeway provided by Chevron had the opposite effect. "The reality of how this works is Chevron itself ushers in shocks to the system every four or eight years when a new administration comes in," he said. The U.S. Chamber of Commerce and other business groups have filed briefs supporting the plaintiffs. The AFL-CIO and consumer, environmental and health organizations have urged the court to leave Chevron intact. In the 1984 Chevron case, the Supreme Court reasoned that executive-branch agencies are better positioned than federal judges to apply the statutes they were assigned to implement -- and if an agency's regulations are out of line, it can be held accountable through the elected president. Conservatives argue that over decades of congressional gridlock, Chevron deference -- as the doctrine is known -- has given federal agencies an end run around the lawmaking process. The original name of the case tells the story: Natural Resources Defense Council v. Gorsuch -- Anne Gorsuch Burford, that is, the late mother of Justice Gorsuch, whom President Ronald Reagan had appointed to run the Environmental Protection Agency. The case involved the Reagan administration's appeal of a decision preventing the EPA from relaxing a Carter-era regulation requiring aggressive efforts to upgrade pollution-emitting equipment at power plants and other facilities. Three justices recused themselves, and Justice John Paul Stevens was selected to write for the 6-0 majority, which found the Clean Air Act language ambiguous and the EPA chief's reading of its requirements to be reasonable. Credit: By Jess Bravin
event_description: The Supreme Court is considering overturning the Chevron v. NRDC precedent, which affects agency interpretations of federal laws like the Endangered Species Act.
setting: NA
characters: NA
plot: NA
moral: NA

Title: My Friend the Ferret
Text: Meeteetse, Wyo. -- In the distance I could see the glow of spotlights, bouncing and flashing like the lightning of a thunderstorm on the horizon, as two biologists trekked through the gray Wyoming night. With car batteries strapped to their backs, lights in hand they were looking for the same thing as I. At 2 a.m. a light beam revealed the objects of our search: Four pairs of glowing, emerald-colored eyes shining against the prairie's brown monotony, as vivid as headlights on a moonless, deserted highway. It's not often that an amateur naturalist gets to see the round, masked faces of one of America's rarest and most mysterious animals -- the black-footed ferret. The lack of knowledge is not due to apathy. The problem lies in the reclusive, subterranean nature of the weasel-like animals. "Black-footed ferrets are born, eat, sleep, raise their young and die underground," says Dave Belitsky, the director of the black-footed ferret program of Wyoming's Game and Fish Department. "They probably spend 95% of their time below the surface, and when they do come up it's almost always at night." There's also no positive proof that the ferrets were ever plentiful across their original range, which is believed to have stretched from Alberta to Arizona -- roughly the range of their favored prey, prairie dogs. The first scientific reference to the animal as a species wasn't until 1851 by naturalist John James Audubon. Reports of ferrets, both alive and dead, remained surprisingly uncommon throughout the next century. Indeed, few people had ever heard of the species until its listing in the 1967 register of the Federal Endangered Species Act. Unfortunately the notoriety was relatively short-lived. At the time the only known, scientifically documented population was a small pocket of animals in South Dakota. The last sightings of that fragile group was in the early 1970s. Scattered ferret sightings continued to trickle in from western states, but as the years passed, more and more people feared that the species had gone the way of the passenger pigeon. Then on a September night in 1981, the species was "rediscovered," by a ranch dog who won a noisy brawl with one of the lithe creatures in a farmyard near Meeteetse. According to Mr. Belitsky, the discovery almost went unreported. After showing the little corpse to his wife, the farmer threw it away. She retrieved the unknown animal as an addition to their mantel, and the local taxidermist luckily recognized it as a blackfooted ferret. Biologists dispatched to Meeteetse soon found some of its friends. The good news spread and thousands of man-hours and dollars went into the search. More ferrets appeared every year, and they seemed well along on the come-back trail. Surveys during the summer of 1984 showed approximately 130 of the beautiful, tan and black animals to be inhabiting the 30 or so prairie dog towns scattered over the 8,000-acre Meeteetse site. But then, seemingly as fast as it had turned good, the news turned bad. Blackfooted ferret numbers began to drop at a worrisome rate. "A year after the peak population of 1984 the ferret population was down about half -- to about 85 animals," recalled Mr. Belitsky. "Two months later, in September, it had more than halved again, down to about 25 to 30 ferrets." Why? Because of the animal's secretive behavior the biologists couldn't be sure. "Something was going on because ferrets had not been seen in places where they were known to have been before," said Mr. Belitsky. "But nobody really knew what." The scientists eventually came up with the answer -- but at a very high and sorrowful price. In mid-September 1985 the decision was made to take six ferrets from the area for a captive breeding program at the Game and Fish's Sybille Wildlife Research Unit, north of Laramie. The animals taken were the cream of the crop, hand-picked for age, breeding experience and gender. The ferrets were transferred as they were caught to Sybille. Within a few days the last animal to come in, a male, started faltering. It eventually died, a victim of canine distemper, a not uncommon disease in the wild. Predictably, the other five captive animals followed suit, having been exposed to the sick ferret. In a scramble for more animals, biologists took all the remaining ferrets they could find from Meeteetse, a total of six. Their age and gender mix was hardly optimal but they were, at least, ferrets. Many outside observers predicted that with the coming of spring there would be no ferrets left at the Meeteetse prairie dog towns. Once again, the species was practically written off as extinct in the wild. But again the shy creatures proved the doomsayers wrong. Surveys this past summer indicated a population of 14, including two adult females with healthy litters of four and five young ferrets each. Yet there still remained a problem. Experts estimated that it would take at least 20 individual animals for the population to sustain itself, either in captivity or in the wild. Difficult and emotional decisions had to be made. In early August four more ferrets were taken into captivity, including a much needed and hopefully sexually experienced adult male. On Aug. 28 a board made up of members from the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and Wyoming Game and Fish biologists made the decision to take the remaining animals. On Sept. 3, what was then the last known wild black-footed ferret walked into a live trap and was shipped to Laramie. It was probably the ferret I'd studied earlier. Whether the little beasts will do what is good for them and procreate, is a big question. Neither the black-footed ferrets taken from Meeteetse last year nor any taken in South Dakota in the early 1970s have ever reproduced successfully in captivity. But there is hope. Dr. Tom Thorne, the Wyoming Game and Fish veterinarian in charge of the captive breeding program at Sybille, feels there's a good chance the animals may be able to bring forth a new generation. And he is building them a dream habitat that will painstakingly duplicate the ferrets' natural environment. Standing beside Dr. Thorne in a cement block room at the Sybille facility, both of us scrubbed clean and bedecked in sterile clothing, I pondered the prospects of the species as I stared at a caged ferret. Who knows, maybe another population will suddenly pop up as it did in Meeteetse. Mr. Belitsky says he gets "between three and four reports a week from people who say they've seen ferrets in other parts of Wyoming and a few other states." Surely they can't all be wrong. And there may well be more animals at Meeteetse. Several weeks after the last trapping, biologists began seeing another animal or animals of unknown origin. So regardless what happens with the captive breeding program science will never be totally sure of the status of the black-footed ferret. It'll remain mysterious. --- Mr. Pearce is a free-lance writer based in Manhattan, Kan.
event_description: The decision was made to take black-footed ferrets from the wild for a captive breeding program.
setting: Wyoming Game and Fish Department's captive breeding program
characters: black-footed ferrets [Victim]; biologists [Hero]; canine distemper [Villain]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and Wyoming Game and Fish biologists [Hero]
plot: Biologists discovered a small population of black-footed ferrets in Meeteetse, but their numbers began to decline due to unknown reasons. In response, biologists initiated a captive breeding program to save the species, but faced challenges including disease and low population numbers.
moral: The black-footed ferret population is critically low, and immediate action through captive breeding is necessary to prevent extinction and ensure the species can sustain itself.

Title: Oregon Plan For Salmon Is Under Fire
Text: The Pacific Rivers Council is about to fire a shot across the bow of Gov. John Kitzhaber's salmon-recovery plan, and might hit itself in the process. The influential Eugene-based environmental group, which in 1993 petitioned the federal government to list coastal coho salmon under the Endangered Species Act, will issue a report today calling the governor's plan so flawed that it won't stop at-risk runs from deteriorating, let alone revive them. The report is most critical of a key element of the plan that is still being implemented: a committee's review of Oregon logging regulations and its recommendations about how they should be revised to help replenish the salmon population. The fault-finding rankles the timber industry, the state's second-largest employer, because it comes before the committee's work is done -- and because the council itself has a representative on the panel. "I think the Pacific Rivers Council is stepping out in front of the process," says Blake Rowe, assistant vice president for timber at Longview Fibre Co. and a member of the advisory committee. He says the council's effort to create a "bigger splash" with its ideas may become more of a "distraction" than a focal point for salmon restoration. The council made its criticism public "to sharpen the focus on restoration of salmon and accelerate the work of this committee," says David Bayles, the group's conservation director. The panel's work so far, he says, makes it clear it's heading down the wrong road by focusing on proposals that are vague and toothless. Officials with the National Marine Fisheries Service, which is charged under the Endangered Species Act with ensuring that salmon runs are rebuilt, agree with some of the complaints in the report. "The criticism is apt at this point," says Will Stelle, the fisheries service's regional administrator in Seattle. Steps taken in Oregon to date "haven't been all we want," particularly in the area of keeping the banks of streams where young fish swim pristine. The state's salmon plan relies mostly on voluntary efforts by landowners, working through the state's 92 local watershed councils, to create their own restoration programs. Two years ago, the fisheries service was so impressed with the plan that it decided to delay listing coastal coho runs as threatened until it had a chance to work. But a U.S. magistrate last June ruled that the agency had "unlawfully placed the risk of failure squarely on the species" by deferring the listing, and an appeals court upheld the ruling. The coho was added to the threatened list in August. After the listing, the timber committee was appointed at Gov. Kitzhaber's request, to look at logging rules on private land -- where some 65% of the Oregon coho habitat is located -- and recommend changes. Gov. Kitzhaber's salmon plan remains the focus for restoring the salmon and so far has triggered nearly 1,500 conservation efforts that last year alone revived 5,000 acres of stream banks and opened 200 miles of streams once blocked to migrating fish. But bigger changes won't arrive in the forest until the committee decides on which of the 50 proposed changes to logging rules it will impose in the state, according to Ray Wilkeson, legislative director for the Oregon Forest Industries Council, which represents private timber owners. Mr. Wilkeson says the variety of ideas on the table reflects an effort to make sure everyone is heard. The tone of the Pacific Rivers Council study will determine whether it helps focus the work, or not. "Delivering ultimatums is not the way to reach agreement," he says. But the Pacific Rivers Council says the proposals on the table -- aside from its own -- just won't cut it. "Extinction will continue even under these forest practices" being considered, according to the report. One, for example, would have landowners themselves inspect logging roads after harvest to make sure there's no threat of erosion that would infiltrate fish habitat. Another proposal for tracts with a high risk of landslides suggests a mix of thinning the timber and rapid reforestation, rather than a logging ban. What the Pacific Rivers Council wants is for the state to set 100-foot-wide no-logging buffer zones beside most streams and rivers -- current law calls for 20-foot zones -- and to establish refuges for young salmon. It also wants the state to end logging at river junctions and on any slope where there is a high probability of landslides. The timber industry's Mr. Wilkeson says that widening the buffer zones "would be a crude way of achieving our goals," noting that the industry believes it can improve conditions for salmon by better managing the stream-side trees rather than simply letting them stand. He adds that the existing zones cost the timber owners $35 million in lost revenue every year. As for the idea of refuges, he says they haven't been proven to work, but adds, "There's nothing [the committee] wouldn't consider." Ann Hanus, Oregon's assistant state forester for resource policy, says the timing of the council's criticism is less than helpful. "It's difficult to reach consensus when parties have staked out their position already," she says. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Coastal coho salmon added to the threatened list.
setting: Oregon's salmon recovery plan and the review of logging regulations
characters: Pacific Rivers Council [Hero]; Gov. John Kitzhaber [Villain]; coastal coho salmon [Victim]; timber industry [Villain]; National Marine Fisheries Service [Hero]
plot: The Pacific Rivers Council criticizes Gov. Kitzhaber's salmon recovery plan, claiming it is flawed and will not help at-risk salmon populations. This criticism is met with resistance from the timber industry, which feels threatened by the council's actions. The National Marine Fisheries Service supports some of the council's complaints, indicating that current efforts are insufficient to protect salmon habitats.
moral: The problem is the inadequacy of the current salmon recovery plan and logging regulations, which threaten the coastal coho salmon. The text suggests that the state should implement stricter logging regulations, including wider no-logging buffer zones and the establishment of refuges for young salmon, to effectively protect the species.

Title: U.S. acts to protect steelhead trout runs along the West Coast
Text: WASHINGTON -- The federal government proposed protecting numerous steelhead trout runs along the West Coast, in another sign of the troubled condition of wild fish populations in the U.S.    The action by the National Marine Fisheries Service was expected. It could lead to restrictions on logging, fishing and other commercial activities in a wide area of the Western U.S. In addition to calling for protection under the Endangered Species Act of 10 runs of steelhead, the agency also listed the Umpqua River cutthroat trout in Oregon as endangered.    The range of the fish covered by the action is huge, spanning from the Canadian border to Southern California. In fact, the action gives the steelhead more protected habitat than any species has been granted since the passage of the Endangered Species Act in 1973.    It won't be clear for about a year what restrictions on use of that habitat will be implemented to help the fish recover. They are likely to include limits on logging, fishing, dam operation, irrigation and development -- all of which have damaged steelhead habitats and contributed to the decline of the species.    However, the government, which has been under pressure from critics of the Endangered Species Act, led by Republicans in Congress, is certain to seek compromises that allow some level of commercial activity in much of the steelhead's range.
event_description: The federal government proposed protecting steelhead trout runs and listed the Umpqua River cutthroat trout as endangered under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: National Marine Fisheries Service [Hero]; commercial activities (logging, fishing, etc.) [Villain]; steelhead trout and Umpqua River cutthroat trout [Victim]
plot: The National Marine Fisheries Service proposed protecting steelhead trout runs and listed the Umpqua River cutthroat trout as endangered, which could lead to restrictions on harmful commercial activities that have contributed to the decline of these fish populations.
moral: The problem is the decline of wild fish populations due to harmful commercial activities, and the text suggests that the government should implement restrictions to protect these species and their habitats.

Title: Lots of Spending, Not Much Stimulus to Justify Cost
Text: Regarding your editorial "A 40-Year Wish List" (Jan. 28): I'm very concerned about the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009 because, at some point, someone will have to pay back all the money that is being borrowed. When this happens, we will have a real economic problem that will make this recession look like no big deal. All we are doing is making our children and grandchildren pay for our bad decisions over the last 20 years. Eventually, if we continue down this road, just the interest on the federal debt will exceed tax revenues. This recession is primarily caused by too much debt -- too much individual debt, too much corporate debt, too much state debt and, yes, way too much federal debt. Borrowing money to fix a problem caused by too much borrowing is insane. Peter M. Blakely Reno, Nev. --- I think we're missing the big picture about the so-called stimulus package. This legislation should first be approved by the Department of the Interior for compliance with the Endangered Species Act. The reason is very simple for all to see. We are about to kill the goose that gave us centuries of golden eggs. And once that goose is gone, will we have the wisdom to acknowledge the loss or the courage to resurrect it? Jack Moran Poquoson, Va. --- Is it too soon to ask President Barack Obama to do that "line-by-line" check of the budget he promised during his campaign? If not now, then when? Andrea Economos Scarsdale, N.Y. --- While Washington is concerned with bolstering the confidence of Americans who are depleting savings in order to weather this recession, it is the confidence of Americans who have savings and capital to invest that matters for a sustainable recovery. As long as this latter group lacks confidence and expects its reward for risk taking to shrink rather than grow, it will scale back its exposure to recession (e.g., via job cuts) and hold capital on the sidelines. Until Washington announces a plan that invigorates investors and encourages risk taking, the return to prosperity in the U.S. will remain nothing more than a wish. Susan Jacobs Randolph, N.J. --- We are told that $900 billion of wealth transfers, uh, I mean stimulus, must be approved immediately without thorough discussion or debate. Those who want to take some time to discuss and/or decide the matter are being derided as footdraggers or finger-pointers. Anyone want to guess how the next scene will play out? Here is one thing you can be sure of. There will not be many in Congress who will have the guts to admit they misallocated your tax payments if, or should I say when, this program fails to achieve its desired effect. Michael Valiante Newark, Del.
event_description: Legislation approval by the Department of the Interior for compliance with the Endangered Species Act.
setting: legislative chamber or committee debate
characters: Department of the Interior [Villain]; American public (children and grandchildren) [Victim]; Jack Moran [Hero]
plot: The Department of the Interior is portrayed as potentially approving legislation that could harm future generations by failing to comply with the Endangered Species Act, while Jack Moran argues for the importance of this compliance to prevent long-term economic and environmental damage.
moral: The problem is the potential approval of legislation without considering its compliance with the Endangered Species Act, which could lead to irreversible harm. The text suggests that the Department of the Interior must prioritize this compliance to protect future generations.

Title: A Special Weekly Report From The Wall Street Journal's Capital Bureau
Text: HUSSEIN ORDERS aides and families to stay put once war begins, U.S. says.   But an administration official says some Iraqis ship out money and plot to flee. Hussein moves money, too, the official adds, and makes plans, as he did in 1991, to evacuate his family. But the U.S. assumes the dictator himself will stay.   Bush advisers scramble to create an Iraqi "consultative counsel" for possible postwar government, to bridge a military administration and state ministries. It would include expatriates and dissidents in Iraq. Pentagon forms a "contractor hotline" for help if Saddam torches oil fields.   As for Osama bin Laden, CIA's Tenet quips to reporters, "We've got him in the basement."    ON KOREAN FRONT, U.S. acts to avoid repeat of North's plane intercepts.   With the U.S. resuming reconnaissance flights off North Korea after the March 2 incident, a Pentagon official says more surveillance aircraft will be sent out so the military can spot North Korean fighter jets faster. That way, U.S. planes can pull away before they can be intercepted and possibly forced to land.   The Pentagon ruled out having U.S. fighter jets escort surveillance planes as "too provocative" to Pyongyang. A State Department official laments that United Nations Security Council members don't meet about North Korea: "They're completely consumed with Iraq."    COSTLY BOEING LEASE gets boost from Defense adviser's endorsement.   Pentagon acquisitions chief Edward Aldridge tells Secretary Rumsfeld the unprecedented $17 billion lease is "the best solution" for upgrading Air Force tanker fleet. The plan, which critics derided as a Boeing bailout, calls for leasing 100 specially modified 767 jetliners as midair-refueling aircraft.   Rumsfeld still wants to confer with White House Budget Director Daniels, a critic. To buy planes would cost about $125 million each; leasing, $146 million. But 67 new aircraft could be in use within six years if leased; 23 if they are bought.   Tankers have aged faster than expected due to heavy use in Afghanistan region.    SPEND A WHILE: Justice Department quietly drops much-publicized rules to slash the time foreigners can stay in the U.S. on visas. The travel and housing industries argued a crackdown would hurt tourism and vacation-home sales. Among foes: Florida Gov. Bush. Justice acted just as new Department of Homeland Security took over immigration and visa enforcement. Now DHS must decide whether to go along: "The issue is not dead," says a department spokesman.    YOUR SLIP IS SHOWING: Asked about scandal at the labor-owned financial-services company, Ullico, AFL-CIO President John Sweeney says on C-SPAN, "If there are problems with Enron -- er -- Ullico, they should be corrected." Organized labor has been a vocal critic of Enron and other corporate scandals. Ullico's board considers corporate-governance recommendations from an internal review next week.    GOVERNMENT LAWYERS SEETHE at Judge Lamberth's rulings in suit alleging U.S. mishandled Indian trust funds. He orders Justice lawyers to personally pay fines for "frivolous motions" that tie up Indians' lawyers. To prevent hackers from getting Interior records, Lamberth ordered the department to shut down Web operations. That forced government lawyers for the Indian case and others to work from library or home computers.    STILL NOT A-LIST: Environmental groups didn't know whether to laugh or cry on opening the fancy invitations: Join President Bush in marking the second anniversary of U.S. withdrawal from the multination Kyoto Protocol on climate change, and to honor Exxon Mobil Chairman Lee Raymond for "voluntary solutions to global warming." The White House says the invitations were a hoax.    WHO'S EXTREME? New environment-committee chiefs take on green groups.   Sen. Inhofe, who carries a card with environmentalists' epithets for him on it -- "Attila the Hun" is one -- launches "Operation End Extremism." The Senate hearings, aimed at Endangered Species Act and its green supporters, play off war fever to push to exempt military bases from the act's regulations.   But House Resources Committee Chairman Pombo wants to rewrite the law: A military exemption "doesn't do anything for private-property owners," his spokesman says. Sierra Club head Carl Pope figures administration advisers aren't fretting over the Hill Republicans' hard line.   "It makes some of their environmental positions appear more reasonable," Pope says.    MINOR MEMOS: Rate interest: Fed's Greenspan, unaware his mike is on before a satellite address to a Paris forum, tells a technician he needs to hear if "they're getting up and applauding or . . . sitting on their hands." . . . "When Bush was talking about regime change, who knew he was talking about Tony Blair?" Rep. Emanuel quips.
event_description: Senate hearings aimed at exempting military bases from Endangered Species Act regulations.
setting: Senate hearings aimed at Endangered Species Act regulations
characters: Sen. Inhofe [Villain]; House Resources Committee Chairman Pombo [Hero]; Sierra Club head Carl Pope [Victim]
plot: Sen. Inhofe uses the Senate hearings to push for a military exemption from the Endangered Species Act, while House Resources Committee Chairman Pombo argues that this exemption does not benefit private-property owners, indicating a conflict between military interests and environmental protections.
moral: The text suggests that the military exemption from the Endangered Species Act is problematic and that a more balanced approach is needed to protect both military interests and environmental concerns.

Title: Environment
Text: EPA Is Likely to Ease Curbs on a Fungicide BROAD use of a popular, but carcinogenic, group of fungicides is likely to be allowed to resume soon. When the Environmental Protection Agency proposed a ban on most uses of ethylene bisdithiocarbamate fungicides in 1989, it offered industry a way out: If a survey of grocery shelves showed that the health risk from foods made from EBDC-treated crops fell within EPA guidelines, the ban wouldn't have to take effect. The agency's analysis, which had shown that the chemicals posed an unacceptably high risk of cancer, had been based on tests on crops in the fields. Makers of the chemicals voluntarily halted most uses covered by the proposed ban as of Jan. 1, 1990. These included EBDC use on apples, pears, carrots, celery, cucumbers, squash and tobacco. Some uses -- on crops such as grapes, peanuts and asparagus -- weren't covered by the proposed ban or the voluntary withdrawal. The four makers of the chemical -- BASF, Du Pont, Elf Atochem and Rohm & Haas -- have now completed the $10 million study, which looked at samples in hundreds of cities of groceries made from treated crops. Janet Ollinger, a Rohm & Haas productregistration manager, says that "for the vast majority of samples, there was no measurable residue." The EPA may announce its decision as early as this week. Officials say many of the uses covered by the proposed ban are likely to be allowed, so long as they don't result in a cancer risk of more than one in a million in the course of a lifetime. Ms. Ollinger says the industry is especially interested in resuming use of the chemical on apples, lettuce, peppers, watermelons, honeydew melon, cucumbers, broccoli, cabbage and dried beans. Rick Hind, legislative director of Greenpeace's toxics campaign, charges that if the EPA allows these chemicals back on the market, it will "be using the population as guinea pigs. This is a very potent carcinogen." Scarce Black Bears Attract a Following THE TEDDY bear may be poised for a comeback in Louisiana. As acrimony over the spotted owl and other threatened or endangered species grabs the headlines, environmentalists and industry are quietly working together to rescue the black bear. The bear, which got its nickname in the early 1900s after Teddy Roosevelt refused to shoot one while hunting, numbered around 50,000 in the Southeastern U.S. in the early 1800s. These days only about 200 remain, mostly in Louisiana. In December, the Interior Department listed it as threatened under the Endangered Species Act. A year before that, the Black Bear Conservation Committee was born. This group, including representatives from federal and state agencies, environmental groups, universities and the timber industry, plans to complete a bear-restoration plan by June. Already, it has helped persuade the state to raise the penalty for killing a black bear to $10,000 and set up a bear newsletter and a bear coordinator's office. Jimmy Bullock, wildlife manager for Anderson-Tully, a Memphis, Tenn., timber company, says, "The bear seems compatible with good forest-management practices." Murray Lloyd, conservation chairman of the Louisiana Sierra Club, agrees, "It turned out this wasn't as much of a problem as people seemed to think." But Defenders of Wildlife, a Washington-based group, contends that the bear should be classified as endangered, rather than threatened, and that critical habitat should be set aside while a recovery plan is worked out. White House May Join War Against Warming JOHN SUNUNU'S departure raises the possibility of a shift on global-warming policy by the White House. The former chief of staff was an ardent opponent of international efforts to curb the atmospheric buildup of carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases, which many scientists believe threaten to cause a disastrous warming of the Earth. Now that Mr. Sununu is gone, there are suggestions the administration may want to move on the issue. His successor, Samuel Skinner, has had one meeting and several conversations with the EPA on the topic. While the White House remains opposed to specific targets, "there may be some kind of goal within the realm of possibility," according to an official familiar with the negotiations. Nations would agree to try to stabilize per-capita emissions by some date without promising to do so. EPA Administrator William Reilly recently asked his staff to look for new ideas that might be acceptable to the White House. The agency is working on a package of voluntary energy conservation programs modeled after the Green Lights program, under which the EPA urges companies to replace inefficient lighting. "There is, in a small way, some indication that they may be moving," says David Gardiner, Sierra Club legislative director. Whether this translates into concrete action may be more apparent next month in New York, when representatives of about 100 countries will meet to continue work on a climate-change treaty scheduled to be signed at June's Earth Summit in Rio de Janeiro. Odds and Ends PORTIONS of the stage from the halftime show of the Super Bowl will be recycled into stage props for community theaters and building insulation by the set's creator, VEE of Minneapolis. . . . A flyer from Finnegans' Wash, Gas & Go in Chevy Chase, Md., urges frequent professional car washes to guard against damage from acid rain.
event_description: The Interior Department listed the black bear as threatened under the Endangered Species Act.
setting: federal agency rulemaking
characters: Environmental Protection Agency [Villain]; Rick Hind [Hero]; black bear [Victim]; Black Bear Conservation Committee [Hero]; Defenders of Wildlife [Hero]
plot: The EPA proposed a ban on carcinogenic fungicides but offered a way for the industry to avoid it if health risks were deemed acceptable. The black bear, now listed as threatened, is being supported by conservation efforts, while the EPA's actions are criticized for potentially endangering public health.
moral: The problem is the potential health risks posed by the fungicides and the inadequate protection of the black bear. The text suggests that stricter regulations and a more protective classification for the black bear are necessary to ensure both public health and species recovery.

Title: U.S. News: Farms Are Left High and Dry Despite Rains
Text: California is awash in water after record-breaking rains vanquished years of crippling drought. That sounds like great news for farmers. But Ron McIlroy, whose shop here sells equipment for plowing fields, knows otherwise. "I'll be lucky if I survive this year," he said. Illustrating how broken California's vast water-delivery system is, many farmers in Central Valley, America's fruit and vegetable basket, will get just 40% of the federal water they are supposed to this year. Why? Endangered fish. The pumps that transport water from wet Northern California to the semiarid south have been drastically slowed to protect threatened migrating smelt, measuring up to 3 inches, and steelhead. That means growers in the U.S.'s richest farming area are having to plant fewer crops even as they are surrounded by water. The decision by the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation, and California officials, to curtail water to farmers for the silvery fish has ignited an uproar in the southern Central Valley, and threatens to upend this important agriculture region just as it was recovering. "There's no reason for it and it's dangerous for the country," said Wayne Western, farm manager of Hammonds Ranch, which plans to plant tomatoes, cotton and other crops on only 60% of its 5,000 acres this year, compared with 80% last year. "You don't know how to plan. You kind of come to a standstill." Drought has pummeled California's farm industry, the nation's largest, for much of the past decade. University of California researchers estimate drought-related farm losses totaled $7 billion and cost 40,000 jobs in 2014, 2015 and 2022 combined. The southern Central Valley, a top U.S. producer of almonds, pistachios, tomatoes and more, has been hard hit. A 2022 study by the area's Westlands Water District, the country's largest agricultural irrigator, found a strong correlation between local poverty and available water. "Every drop of water creates jobs," said John Harris, chairman of Harris Farms in the valley. Record rain and snow last year allowed farmers in the district to receive their full allotment of federal water, a contrast to the prior two years when they received none. Ron McIlroy's company, McIlroy Equipment, was among many celebrating a rare lift in sales after years of dismal business. He eagerly anticipated similar success this year following another wet winter. California was declared officially drought-free, boosting spirits. But the picture darkened for farmers when the Bureau of Reclamation initially forecast a 15% water allocation in February before raising it to 40% in April. California's water woes stem from a tricky balancing act: Most of the water is in the north and must be pumped south, where most farmers and other people live. The water must go through the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta, where fish listed under the Endangered Species Act migrate. Environmental groups have successfully fought to protect these threatened species, resulting in a series of federal opinions and court orders over the past four decades that have put the squeeze on water pumping through the delta. "It's clear to researchers that overpumping fresh water from the delta is what drove imperiled fish like the delta smelt to near-extinction, so it makes sense to enforce reasonable, sustained limits," said John Buse, senior counsel at the Center for Biological Diversity, among groups that has pushed for constraints. Farmers question the data behind the claims of harm to threatened smelt and steelhead, arguing that fish said to be at risk from the pumps include both native and hatchery-bred fish. "The Endangered Species Act has become a weapon to strip water from farmers," Harris said. Nonetheless, to comply with current state and federal regulations, officials say they must curtail pumping when the fish are present. This spring saw record-low pumping during a wet year because of a high number of fish, said Karla Nemeth, director of the California Department of Water Resources. Consequently, the San Luis Reservoir, a key water supply for southern Central Valley farmers, sits at just 55% capacity, among the few in the state now not filled to the brim. "We realize that our contractors were hoping to see a greater amount of water," Karl Stock, a Bureau of Reclamation regional director, said at the time, citing "regulatory constraints" as a motivating factor. Some, like fourth-generation pistachio farmer Rebecca Kaser, 40, stored water from last year's bounty -- for a future drought. But now, she expects to use much of it this year. "I'm unhappy about it," said Kaser, who was inspecting her 560-acre spread from behind the wheel of a Dodge Ram one recent blistering hot day. Farmers can buy water on the private market, but that can cost five times as much as federal water, said third-generation farmer Mitch Coit, who grows almonds, tomatoes and more on 5,000 acres. He said he might leave up to 80% of his land unplanted next year, given water uncertainty and low commodity prices. "Water isn't being managed properly and we are paying the price," he said. Bureau of Reclamation spokeswoman Mary Lee Knecht said, "We acknowledge the difficulties uncertain water supplies create for our irrigators, and we are in constant communication with them to ensure timely understanding." Gov. Gavin Newsom's team is using the bottleneck as Exhibit A for constructing a $20 billion tunnel to bypass the delta, in part, to allow threatened fish to swim undisturbed. The project faces fierce opposition from a coalition of landowners, tribes and environmental groups. Credit: By Jim Carlton
event_description: The U.S. Bureau of Reclamation's decision to curtail water delivery to farmers to protect endangered fish species.
setting: California's water delivery system and the regulatory context surrounding the Endangered Species Act
characters: U.S. Bureau of Reclamation [Villain]; farmers in Central Valley [Victim]; endangered fish species (delta smelt and steelhead) [Victim]; John Buse, senior counsel at the Center for Biological Diversity [Hero]; Gov. Gavin Newsom's team [Hero]
plot: The U.S. Bureau of Reclamation curtailed water delivery to farmers to protect endangered fish species, leading to significant crop reductions and economic distress for farmers in the Central Valley, while environmental advocates argue for the necessity of these protections.
moral: The text suggests that the current management of water resources is flawed, advocating for a reevaluation of water allocation policies to better balance the needs of farmers and the protection of endangered species.

Title: House Panel Approves Immigration Bill Making Biggest Cuts in Over 70 Years
Text: WASHINGTON -- The House Judiciary Committee approved legislation that would combat illegal immigration and also make the most dramatic cuts in legal immigration in more than 70 years. The committee voted 23-10. The bill would deliver the second of a one-two punch against immigration. Already, separate measures would exclude legal immigrants from federal public-assistance programs, including a variety of housing, education and welfare programs. Taken together, the bills reflect the nation's growing hostility to immigrants. Yesterday, immigration advocates tried unsuccessfully to kill provisions involving legal immigration, including scaling back the numbers of foreigners who are allowed to enter the U.S. each year. The public-assistance provisions are contained in bills to overhaul the welfare system that have passed the House and Senate, and now await action by a conference committee that will iron out differences between the two versions. Under the Senate measure, even some naturalized citizens would be denied certain benefits. GOP estimates of legal immigration under the Smith bill have been creeping up. Originally, the bill was expected to cut immigration between 1997 and 2001 by one-third to 535,000 annually. But after the vote, Rep. Lamar Smith (R., Texas), who sponsored the bill, put the figure at 780,000. The bill would bar certain categories of family members who are now eligible for immigration. "America will continue to be the most generous nation in the world under this legislation," Rep. Smith said. "It will also ensure that the world's best and brightest will come to America to help our technology companies create waves of new jobs for Americans." But critic Stephen Moore, fiscal-policy studies director for the libertarian Cato Institute said: "If enacted, we will see the largest reduction in legal immigration since the 1920s. We might as well place a `no entry' sign on the Statue of Liberty." The legislation, which now goes to the full House, would: Create a demonstration worker-verification program in five states. With a telephone call, employers could verify any job applicants' eligibility to work. Critics contend that if the program is expanded nationwide, it would involve the federal government in every hiring decision and would lead to a national identification card. Allow secret evidence in removing suspected foreign terrorists, limit judicial proceedings in the exclusion and deportation process, make entry more difficult for asylum seekers, and cut refugee admissions about 50% over two years. Increase the number of Border Patrol agents by 5,000 during the next five years, waive the Endangered Species Act to provide speedy construction of physical barriers to illegal immigration in Southern California and toughen bogus-document penalties. Increase fines to $5,000 from $1,000 for each violation of a nonimmigrant worker-visa program. Though the Judiciary Committee measure also would slightly reduce the number of employment-related visas, Rep. Smith made sufficient concessions to business groups to modify their opposition. "They've come a long, long way," said Phyllis Eisen, of the National Manufacturers Association. The real problem for business interests, however, is in the Senate where draft legislation prepared by Sen. Alan Simpson (R., Wyo.) would slash employment visas almost 50%. While some groups that represent business interests, such as the National Association of Manufacturers and the American Immigration Lawyers Association, continue to push for separating the sections of the bill that cover legal and illegal immigrants, the American Council on International Personnel didn't press that position after Mr. Smith agreed to some compromises. "The problems business had with the bill have essentially been addressed," said Austin Fragomen, a New York lawyer and executive director of the council, which represents large, multinational corporations. Among other things, Mr. Smith reduced to two years from five the length of time that certain recent college graduates can't work in the U.S. Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: The legislation would waive the Endangered Species Act to expedite the construction of physical barriers to illegal immigration.
setting: House Judiciary Committee approval of immigration legislation
characters: Rep. Lamar Smith [Hero]; Stephen Moore [Villain]; immigrants [Victim]
plot: The House Judiciary Committee approved legislation aimed at reducing illegal immigration and cutting legal immigration, which includes provisions that would harm immigrants and limit their access to public assistance. Rep. Lamar Smith supports the bill, claiming it will benefit America, while critics like Stephen Moore argue it will severely restrict legal immigration.
moral: The problem is the perceived threat of immigration, and the text suggests that the solution is to pass legislation that significantly reduces both legal and illegal immigration to protect American interests.

Title: Politics: Washington Wire --- Dispatches from the Nation's Capital
Text: CONSERVATIVE SUPER PAC Club for Growth Action will launch its largest-ever digital ad buy to support Republican candidates in five House races and Montana Sen. Steve Daines as he tries to fend off a challenge from Democratic Gov. Steve Bullock. The $3 million buy, which starts Sept. 8, will target voters on streaming TV platforms like Hulu and Sling, as well as internet radio and podcast services. The ad buy is in addition to $10 million in traditional TV ad reservations the Club has booked through Election Day. Club President David McIntosh said in an interview that digital ads for House races have the potential to be more targeted and efficient, given the high price of booking TV ads in suburban metropolitan areas where many of the most competitive House races are located. The ad buy includes races in suburban Indianapolis, Atlanta, Austin and Richmond, along with the at-large House seat in Montana. Montana's Senate and House races are receiving special attention from Club for Growth Action given Montanans' relative propensity to split their tickets, and with President Trump's polling numbers there down compared to 2016, when he won by 20 points. "Because Bullock is a popular sitting governor, he's made that a competitive race," McIntosh said of the Montana Senate contest. "TIGER KING" STAR Joe Exotic is expected to file a formal pardon application next Thursday. The Netflix reality show character and former tiger breeder, whose real name is Joseph Maldonado-Passage, is currently incarcerated on two counts of murder for hire and 17 animal abuse charges, including violations of the Endangered Species Act. The pardon application, viewed by the Journal, argues that Exotic wasn't given the opportunity to properly defend himself in court, and that the Netflix series has allowed Americans to "know more about Joe's personal life, business dealings and the cast of characters that helped him run his exotic animal park in Oklahoma." It also makes attempts to explain the circumstances of the crimes the court found him guilty of committing. Each year, hundreds of petitioners file applications for pardons. Trump has pardoned 27 people since taking office. In an exchange with reporters in April, Trump addressed the possibility of pardoning Exotic. "I know nothing about it. He has 22 years, for what? What did he do?" He then asked reporters if they were "on [Exotic's] side and if they would recommend a pardon. "I'll take a look," Trump said. Tiger King producers haven't ruled out the possibility of revisiting the story, which ranked as one of Netflix's most successful programs ever. STATES REVISIT BALLOT RULES to account for the expected widespread shift to mail-in voting this November. Pennsylvania Gov. Tom Wolf, a Democrat, asked the Republican-led legislature last week to allow counties to begin processing ballots as early as 21 days before Election Day. Currently, counties have to wait until the morning of Election Day to begin processing and counting ballots. General Assembly Republicans' opening offer on legislation was to allow three extra days of processing before the election. Both parties support a provision to mail out ballots to voters at least 28 days before the election, up from the current 14-day deadline. In Florida, Republican Gov. Ron DeSantis in June signed an emergency order granting election authorities the ability to process ballots up to 22 days ahead of Election Day. Ballot processing on the other side of election day has also come into question in some states: A federal judge in Georgia on Monday ruled that the state must process absentee ballots that are postmarked by Election Day and received up to three days later. IOWA SEN. Joni Ernst sells T-shirts for her campaign with the slogan "hands off our caucuses." Ernst has elevated the risk of losing the Iowa Caucuses as the first presidential nominating contest into a top campaign issue in her tight re-election bid. One problem: A Republican senator has little influence over whether the Democratic Party decides to keep Iowa at the front of the line. Following the counting debacle in this year's Iowa Caucuses, many Democratic officials called for abandoning the cumbersome caucus model altogether and choosing a different order for presidential primaries to better reflect the diversity of the Democratic electorate. SUPERMODEL KARLIE KLOSS hosts an education-focused event for Democrat Joe Biden, her first formal foray into the presidential race. A celebrity hosting a low-key campaign event normally wouldn't turn heads, but Kloss is married to Josh Kushner, brother of Trump senior adviser Jared Kushner and brother-in-law of Ivanka Trump. Following a January dust-up over presidential politics on Project Runway, a fashion-design television show Kloss hosts, Kloss said she hadn't supported Trump in 2016 and would again vote for the Democratic candidate in 2020. MINOR MEMOS: Following Joe Kennedy's loss to Ed Markey in the Massachusetts Democratic Senate primary, some cheekily blame an ad in the Worcester Telegram & Gazette that misspelled the city's name as "Worchester." . . . Biden wades into Pittsburgh pizza war between rivals Aiello's and Mineo's Pizza House with delivery of Mineo's pies to first responders: "I'm writing in Elizabeth Warren," says one Aiello's fan. . . . Sen. Mitch McConnell returns to small Kentucky town where 35 years ago a store owner offered him a free RC Cola and MoonPie if he visited.   Credit: By Gabriel T. Rubin
event_description: Joe Exotic is expected to file a formal pardon application related to violations of the Endangered Species Act.
setting: political context surrounding pardon applications and the Endangered Species Act
characters: Joe Exotic [Victim]; the court [Villain]; Donald Trump [Hero]
plot: Joe Exotic, currently incarcerated for serious crimes including violations of the Endangered Species Act, is expected to file a pardon application arguing he was not given a fair chance to defend himself, while Donald Trump has expressed openness to considering the pardon.
moral: The text suggests that Joe Exotic deserves a fair chance for a pardon, implying that the judicial process may have been unjust and advocating for a reevaluation of his case.

Title: U.S. News: Endangered Species Act Protections Eased
Text: WASHINGTON -- The Trump administration is easing several Endangered Species Act regulations, responding to complaints from developers and other business interests who say the landmark 1973 law designed to protect wildlife has become too onerous. The changes announced Monday won't apply to species now considered threatened or endangered, such as grizzly bears. But they will put new restrictions on how future assessments are made, which environmentalists say are likely to limit how much the risks of climate change can be considered and how many species end up listed as threatened and endangered. The revisions also end a policy that automatically gave the highest level of protection to species listed as threatened even before they were listed as endangered, and they place more restrictions on which areas can be designated as critical habitat. "The Endangered Species Act has led to important conservation efforts that have brought species back from the brink of extinction," said Margaret Everson, acting director of the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. "However, there are improvements we can make." As part of the changes, the government can also start presenting the economic costs of listing new species as threatened or endangered. Administration officials say this information won't be used in decision-making, but environmentalists say the policy opens the door to that eventually, or to using costs to build public pressure against a threatened or endangered designation. California and several other states are planning lawsuits to block the changes. The Endangered Species Act is there "not just so that our kids can go up to see a condor or grizzly bear, it's about what it means to have those species in our ecosystems," California Attorney General Xavier Becerra said in a call with reporters. "And today our ecosystems are facing an unprecedented threat." Trade groups cheered Monday's announcement. Several of them, representing a broad portion of the U.S. business community, including power-plant owners, manufacturers and pipeline companies, had pushed for an overhaul. Changes to what constitutes a critical habitat were at the top of the list of their requests, with developers saying past administrations had introduced overbroad definitions that led to protections for hundreds or even thousands of miles of major rivers. "Today's announcement is an important step toward modernizing endangered species protection, including making permitting for infrastructure more efficient while protecting our nation's threatened and endangered species," Jim Matheson, CEO of the National Rural Electric Cooperative Association, said in a statement. The new rules would prioritize protecting areas where threatened and endangered species now live over areas where they don't live but may potentially thrive. Environmentalists say overlooking areas endangered species have vacated may wipe out the type of habitat they need to expand back into to recover. "These regulations are in fact a very serious threat to how endangered species are protected and conserved, at a time when the world is facing an extinction crisis and should be committing to redoubled efforts to conserve imperiled wildlife," said Bob Dreher, senior vice president for conservation programs of Defenders of Wildlife. Interior Secretary David Bernhardt and Commerce Secretary Wilbur Ross signed the new regulations on Monday. They will go into effect 30 days after they are published in the federal register, likely within the next two weeks, an Interior spokeswoman said. The revisions are the latest move by the Trump administration to curb the way government agencies and environmentalists can use decades-old environmental laws. While scientists and environmentalists have warned of the need to address increasing threats to the planet's climate and its biodiversity, the administration and allies in business say their efforts amount to government expansion and a barrier to economic progress. The Endangered Species Act has been credited for reviving iconic animals such as the bald eagle and the humpback whale. As several hundred species have been added to the list, focus has shifted to small fish and insects. Scientists and environmentalists warn that the decline of such animals can foreshadow doom for entire ecosystems. Farmers and oil producers have argued against decisions that cordon off thousands of miles of water and land they want for irrigating crops or for finding oil and gas. The Trump administration already has made changes in how it handles the program. The Obama administration added roughly 49 species a year to the lists of the threatened and endangered. The Trump administration has never added more than 11 in one year, and just one in 2019, the trispot darter, a type of perch from the southeast listed as threatened in late January. Mr. Becerra and Massachusetts Attorney General Maura Healey repeatedly cited findings from a United Nations group this spring that showed the rate of extinctions is accelerating to unprecedented levels, with about one million plant and animal species now at risk of dying out during the next few decades. Humans depend on many species for their food and livelihoods, so animal and plant diversity also could have consequences for people around the globe, the group said.   Credit: By Timothy Puko
event_description: The Trump administration is easing several Endangered Species Act regulations.
setting: Washington, D.C. regulatory context regarding the Endangered Species Act
characters: Trump administration [Villain]; environmentalists [Hero]; endangered species [Victim]; California Attorney General Xavier Becerra [Hero]; business interests and developers [Villain]; Bob Dreher [Hero]
plot: The Trump administration eases Endangered Species Act regulations in response to business complaints, which environmentalists argue will harm endangered species and ecosystems. This regulatory rollback prioritizes economic interests over conservation efforts, leading to potential legal challenges from states like California.
moral: The text suggests that the regulatory changes pose a serious threat to endangered species and ecosystems, advocating for stronger protections and a commitment to conservation efforts in light of the extinction crisis.

Title: Citing regulatory errors, judge orders songbird off endangered species list
Text: A federal judge in Washington, D.C., ordered the government to drop a California songbird from the endangered species list, finding that federal regulators violated environmental laws in putting it there.    Judge Stanley Sporkin ruled that the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service violated provisions of the Endangered Species Act and another federal law by not providing the public enough access to data used to determine that the bird, the California gnatcatcher, was imperiled. The bird, which lives in coastal sage scrub along the Southern California coast, was granted federal protection last year.    The ruling marks the second time ever that a species has been ordered off the list because courts found that the government didn't follow procedures laid out in environmental laws. In January, a federal judge in Idaho stripped a tiny snail of federal protection, ruling similarly that the Fish and Wildlife Service hadn't followed proper procedures in listing it as endangered.    Those decisions are part of a growing trend in which landowners and industry are suing to block increased environmental safeguards, alleging that the safeguards themselves violate environmental laws. Hundreds of such cases have been filed in the past few years.    Most have failed. But in addition to the rulings on the snail and gnatcatcher, proponents of the strategy won another big victory when a federal judge in Fresno, Calif., last week blocked implementation of a legislation aimed at curbing agribusiness's damaging environmental practices in California. In that case, the judge ruled the government hadn't properly evaluated the impact of the law's environmental protections on farmers.    The gnatcatcher case is a particular blow to environmentalists and the government. Gnatcatchers live only in the scrub country along the Southern California coast. Federal scientists say about 90% of the gnatcatcher's historic habitat has been plowed under in recent decades, mainly for housing developments. What's left is almost entirely owned by private developers, including Irvine Cos. and other huge real estate concerns. A trade association for the developers was the plaintiff in the gnatcatcher case.    The gnatcatcher was listed after the government and developers entered into an unusual agreement under which developers would be allowed to wipe out some gnatcatcher habitat, as long as they set aside other territory as gnatcatcher preserves. That agreement was hailed as a model for future species listings by Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt.    Judge Sporkin's ruling calls the gnatcatcher model into question, however. The Interior Department couldn't immediately be reached to comment on the ruling; environmental attorneys said the agency is likely to appeal, and could invoke procedures that might protect the gnatcatcher temporarily while the agency figures out how to deal with the ruling.    However, these attorneys also said it's more likely the government will take months to rethink its approach to the gnatcatcher. In the meantime, Hugh Hewitt, an attorney representing developers, said his group will attack the gnatcatcher listing on other grounds in separate lawsuits and try to block re-listing of the species.    Mr. Hewitt suggested that landowners might react to the suspension of the gnatcatcher's protection by plowing under its habitat as fast as possible. "A lot of landowners will look seriously at clearing their land of coastal sage brush, to rid themselves of this problem," he said.  Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: A federal judge ordered the removal of the California gnatcatcher from the endangered species list.
setting: a federal court case in Washington, D.C.
characters: Judge Stanley Sporkin [Hero]; U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service [Villain]; California gnatcatcher [Victim]; Hugh Hewitt [Villain]; landowners and developers [Villain]; environmentalists [Victim]
plot: Judge Stanley Sporkin ordered the government to remove the California gnatcatcher from the endangered species list due to violations of environmental laws by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, which failed to provide adequate public access to data. This ruling is part of a trend where landowners and industry challenge environmental protections, leading to potential harm to the gnatcatcher and other species.
moral: The problem is the failure of the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service to follow proper procedures in protecting endangered species, which undermines environmental safeguards. The text suggests that the government must reevaluate its approach to species protection to ensure compliance with environmental laws.

Title: Bill to Be Introduced To Pay Compensation Under Species Act
Text: In recent months, pressure has been building within the Republican majority to change the law, and earlier this year, Congress passed a bill halting all new endangered-species listings through the end of 1995. Sen. Dirk Kempthorne (R., Idaho) also plans to introduce a bill to scale back the Endangered Species Act. As introduced, the House bill seems unlikely to win much support in the environmental community or from the Clinton administration. Both have criticized the idea of compensating landowners for regulatory "takings," in this case regulations that limit uses of private land in order to protect endangered plants and animals, as a costly process likely to inhibit government action severely. WASHINGTON -- A House committee chairman plans to propose changes to the Endangered Species Act to compensate businesses and landowners whose property values drop as a result of endangered-species listings. The long-awaited bill, set to be unveiled at a news conference today by Chairman Don Young (R., Alaska) of the House Resources Committee, comes after months of field hearings that focused mainly on the act's cost to property owners. It also reflects considerable input from House Speaker Newt Gingrich (R., Ga.), whose opposition to what he views as excessive regulation is balanced in this case by his long-standing support for the endangered-species law.
event_description: A House committee chairman plans to propose changes to the Endangered Species Act to compensate businesses and landowners affected by endangered-species listings.
setting: a legislative chamber debate regarding changes to the Endangered Species Act
characters: House committee chairman Don Young [Hero]; Sen. Dirk Kempthorne [Hero]; environmental community [Villain]; Clinton administration [Villain]; businesses and landowners [Victim]
plot: The House committee chairman plans to propose changes to the Endangered Species Act to compensate businesses and landowners whose property values drop due to endangered-species listings, amidst criticism from the environmental community and the Clinton administration.
moral: The problem is the financial burden on businesses and landowners due to endangered-species regulations, and the text suggests that compensating them is a necessary reform to alleviate this issue.

Title: Business News: Court Overturns EPA Approval of Bayer Weedkiller
Text: Bayer AG was dealt a setback in its efforts to provide farmers with new weapons against hard-to-kill weeds, after a federal appeals court vacated the Trump administration's approval of a controversial weedkiller made by the agribusiness giant. The U.S. Ninth U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals said Wednesday that the Environmental Protection Agency in 2018 failed to acknowledge or significantly understated risks posed by three herbicides, including Bayer's XtendiMax, whose active ingredient is dicamba, when it reapproved the sprays for use on U.S. farms. Bayer said that it would continue working to make sure farmers have access to the spray. "We strongly disagree with the ruling and are assessing our options," the company said, adding that it would work to minimize impact on its customers if the ruling stands. The ruling comes as seed-and-pesticide makers vie to offer farmers a way to stop weeds resistant to glyphosate, the world's most actively used herbicide, which is marketed under Bayer's Roundup brand and other names. It is a boost to rival Corteva Inc., which is working to hasten the rollout of its own new seed and herbicide spray combination, called Enlist, which doesn't use dicamba. Bayer shares fell 3.8% on Thursday, while Corteva shares climbed 4%. XtendiMax, marketed by Monsanto Co. before Bayer acquired the company in 2018, was designed to kill weeds that can choke out soybean and cotton plants. But farmers and academics said the herbicide damaged neighboring crops because its active ingredient is prone to evaporating off plants, drifting on wind and shriveling crops that weren't developed to resist the weedkiller. The court faulted the EPA for disregarding evidence that showed dicamba had caused substantial damage, and for failing to recognize that certain applications of the herbicides had "torn apart the social fabric of many farming communities" where farmers who used the spray have had disputes with neighbors who didn't. In 2016, an Arkansas farmer was shot and killed in what authorities said appeared to be a dispute over dicamba damage to crops. An EPA spokesman said the agency was reviewing the decision and would move promptly to address the court's directive. The EPA in 2018 said it would continue to allow farmers to spray crops with new versions of XtendiMax, while tightening restrictions on how and when it could be applied. That decision was a win for Bayer, which also sells soybean and cotton seeds genetically engineered to withstand the herbicide. Farm and environmental groups including the National Family Farm Coalition, Center for Food Safety and Center for Biological Diversity filed a lawsuit against the EPA, arguing that the agency's re-approval of three dicamba-based herbicides violated the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act and the Endangered Species Act. "Today's decision is a massive win for farmers and the environment," said George Kimbrell of the Center for Food Safety, who acted as lead counsel in the case. Steve Smith, senior director of agriculture for Indiana tomato company Red Gold Inc., welcomed the decision after seeking stricter dicamba restrictions for years. "We were always very concerned about being able to supply our customers if we lost some of our crop, so that's a big relief."   Credit: By Jesse Newman
event_description: Federal appeals court vacated the EPA's approval of dicamba herbicides.
setting: federal appeals court ruling
characters: Bayer AG [Villain]; Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [Villain]; farmers and academics [Victim]; Corteva Inc. [Hero]; National Family Farm Coalition, Center for Food Safety, Center for Biological Diversity [Hero]; George Kimbrell [Hero]; Steve Smith [Hero]
plot: The federal appeals court vacated the EPA's approval of dicamba herbicides due to failure to acknowledge risks, impacting Bayer's business while benefiting competitors like Corteva. Farmers and academics suffer from dicamba's harmful effects on crops, leading to disputes and even violence in farming communities.
moral: The problem is the harmful impact of dicamba herbicides on crops and farming communities, and the text suggests that stricter regulations and accountability for companies like Bayer and the EPA are necessary to protect farmers and the environment.

Title: Camcorders Spark Two Cable Ventures --- Media: Camnet Gains Following With Documentaries
Text: LOS ANGELES -- Cameraman Jay April sinks ankle-deep into a three-story-high compost heap, believed to be the largest in the Western world, and trains his camcorder on the heap's long-haired, bearded owner, who speaks only in rhyme. Environmental zealot Tim Dundon, better known as Zeke the Sheik from the planet Bleak, is pontificating on the pile: I mix it like pancake batter To make the ladder of matter fatter, But it doesn't matter Because everybody thinks I'm as mad as a hatter. At intervals, Zeke breaks into song. A favorite: "I Never Promised You a Rose Garden." The footage will soon air on Camnet, a five-month-old cable network that shows minidocumentaries shot with camcorders by a cadre of aging video activists and artists. Started by two women from Venice Beach, Calif., Camnet eschews anchors, reporters and hosts. Correspondents have taped homeless poets, a skinhead picnic on the Mexican border, a visit to the Nixon Museum, a "Justice for Janitors" rally in Los Angeles and a guided tour of the Barbie doll inventor's prosthetic breast factory. Once Camnet lent a camcorder to Los Angeles gang members, whose woeful footage of life in their neighborhood drew tears from one video editor. This isn't cute enough to be "America's Funniest Home Videos." And it's a far cry from NBC's "I Witness Videos," which is often grisly. It's cinema verite meets vox populi, a gentle amateur service from the handheld point of view. The stories, some sober and serious, some arcane, air the last two weeks of every month to more than 828,000 homes in 10 cable systems from Los Angeles to Philadelphia. The same two-hour loop airs 12 times a day. "Think of television as food," says Mr. April. "We've been eating the same meat and potatoes served up by the networks since the 1950s. Now people are cooking more exotic dishes at home." That attitude has drawn a cult-like following of young and old viewers. "They reveal as opposed to preach," says Victor Dinnerstein, a 46-year-old San Fernando Valley resident and Camnet fan. "It's a little off the wall, but it's pure insight." Camnet is run on a budget not much bigger than the combined savings of cofounders Nancy Cain and Judith Binder. They met while working on "The '90s," a critically acclaimed, populist camcorder-based magazine show now in its fourth and final season on PBS. They liked the concept so much that they decided it could work on a much bigger scale. Ms. Binder had been an independent director with alternative theater troupes in Los Angeles. Ms. Cain and several other Camnet contributors are the same counterculture video artists who roamed from loft to loft in New York in the late 1960s and early 1970s, watching each other's experimental videos on such topics as the trial of the Chicago Seven and Woodstock. They dubbed themselves the Videofreex and once transmitted homemade programming to a valley in upstate New York that wasn't being served by a broadcast station. Camnet personnel still haven't exactly joined the Establishment. Advertisers are few despite the cheap rates. Sponsors can buy two dozen 30-second spots a day for a full week for $504. Editorial control, what little there is, lies entirely in the hands of Ms. Cain, a longtime freelance video producer, and Ms. Binder. "We open the window a little wider, try to let things develop a little more," says Ms. Binder. "Sometimes we succeed, sometimes we fail, but we're willing to risk the process to find the truth." The two edit tapes sitting side by side before two 13-inch monitors in a small backroom of Ms. Cain's Venice Beach house, which is littered with videocassettes. On the wall are pictures of President Bush and former presidents Reagan and Nixon. "They inspire me," Ms. Cain remarks, deadpan. The pair seek a seamless tapestry of footage when they edit, and they discourage their 17 or so correspondents from ever turning the camera off during shoots. "Process is product," says Ms. Cain. "I have a high tolerance for raw tape." One of the more prolific correspondents, if not the most passionate, is Mr. April, a militant environmentalist who sleeps in a giant green tent in his living room. He drives from shoot to shoot in a 1970 Karmann Ghia, hugging the broken driver-side door so it doesn't swing open on curvy mountain roads. On a sweltering California afternoon, he dutifully follows and records Zeke the Sheik as he points out areas of interest in his compost pile. The footage isn't exactly broadcast quality. When the compost beneath his feet crumbles, Mr. April struggles for balance and the camera wobbles. He inadvertently coughs in the middle of a question, and an overhead jet muffles Zeke's voice. "It may not be technically perfect, but it's flat-out honest," he says. "This is gonzo journalism with a human touch." What the network lacks in polished professionalism, it makes up in serendipity. Mr. April once stumbled on a group of real estate agents playing softball in a park whose construction drove the last known Palos Verdes Blue butterfly into extinction, in violation of the Endangered Species Act. The cameraman approached a middleaged couple sitting on bleachers and asked them solemnly, "Are you aware this was the last known habitat of the Palos Verdes Blue butterfly?" "I'm sorry to hear that," the woman responded politely, as she continued to watch the game. Mr. April also shot a story on a coyote that was feasting on the poodles of the rich. It included interviews with the distraught pet owners and the county agent called to trap the coyote. The animal was eventually shown writhing in a trap, and minutes later, a single gunshot was heard off camera as Mr. April zoomed in on the agent's truck and its logo: Animal Control. The agent, identified on camera only as Louie, told the camera he didn't feel good about killing the coyote. "What justifies this in my heart and in my mind is the pets I save. Here I saved perhaps 10, 20 cats." It was a classic Camnet moment -- unstaged and otherwise unremarkable, a snapshot of life made more dramatic by its appearance on a TV screen. "If you point a camera and let people say what they want to say, nine times out of 10 it's more stimulating, more interesting than some reporter who's more concerned with the perfect part down his hair," says Mr. April. Mr. April hopes for the day Camnet becomes a full-fledged network, but he says it has already spawned a revolution. "It used to be that Big Brother is watching us," he says. "Now, it's Little Brother." (See related article: "Media: New York Gets A New Channel For Local News" -- WSJ July 30, 1992) Credit: Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal
event_description: Construction that led to the extinction of the last known Palos Verdes Blue butterfly.
setting: Camnet's production environment and the context of environmental activism in Los Angeles
characters: Tim Dundon (Zeke the Sheik) [Hero]; Real estate agents [Villain]; Palos Verdes Blue butterfly [Victim]; Mr. April [Hero]; Louie (Animal Control agent) [Villain]
plot: Mr. April records Zeke the Sheik discussing his compost pile, while also capturing footage of real estate agents whose construction led to the extinction of the Palos Verdes Blue butterfly. This highlights the conflict between environmentalists and those contributing to habitat destruction.
moral: The text suggests that the destruction of habitats by real estate development is a significant problem, and it advocates for greater awareness and action to protect endangered species like the Palos Verdes Blue butterfly.

Title: REVIEW & OUTLOOK (Editorial): A Snail Retreat
Text: The Bruneau Hot Springs snail is only about as big as one of the smaller letters in this sentence, but it's at the center of a giant battle over the Endangered Species Act. Although the tiny Idaho snail can only be distinguished from other snails because of its unusually large sexual organ, we've noted how it had threatened to shut down the economy of Idaho's Bruneau Valley ("The Emotional Species Act," November 2, 1993). Then this month, Federal Judge Harold Ryan removed it from the Endangered Species list. He criticized the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service for withholding scientific data from the public and said its behavior "represented self-serving and superficial responses from an agency that had already made up its mind." Even Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt, a fervent environmentalist, seems to be having second thoughts about the Bruneau snail. In a visit to Idaho last week, he said there may be no need to relist the snail if the state government will better maintain the water level in the 126 hot springs the pinhead snail is known to occupy. He said that "macho arm-wrestling" between state and federal officials led to the snail's listing. "I think there is a lot less than meets the eye," he joked to ranchers. So it looks as if the 100 or so ranchers in the Bruneau Valley may survive after all, but their battle drew our attention once again to how the Endangered Species Act is misused by extremists to halt unwanted development and restrict private property.
